TITLE: Airway management complications
RISK_BRIEF:
Airway Management Complications
SPECIALTY: Anesthesiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Airway Management

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Permanent brain damage, cardiac arrest or death
Airway management complications are significant drivers of malpractice risk and severe patient harm in anesthesiology.
Mitigating Your Risk  
With appropriate technique selection, vigilant monitoring and effective communication and documentation, anesthesiologists can mitigate the risks associated with difficult airway management and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for airway management complications and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving difficult airway complications in anesthesiology typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 71% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 29%. Any one of these—or a combination—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Airway Management Complications [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Selection of inappropriate airway management strategy, failure to escalate to alternative techniques or fixation on a single approach despite repeated failures increase the risk of adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support appropriate technique selection, timely escalation and avoidance of fixation errors.
Conduct a comprehensive preoperative airway risk assessment 
Perform and document an airway evaluation before every anesthetic, assessing multiple predictors of difficulty with direct laryngoscopy, video laryngoscopy, face-mask ventilation, supraglottic airway use and front-of-neck access. Preoperative predictors of difficult intubation are frequently identifiable in retrospective analyses of difficult airway claims; systematic assessment enables appropriate planning and reduces unanticipated difficulty.
Formulate and communicate a primary and backup airway strategy
Establish a documented plan before induction that includes the primary technique, alternative approaches and explicit triggers for escalation. A documented strategy helps ensure that the team understands an alternative approach and reduces delays in escalation when the primary approach fails.
Limit intubation attempts and avoid perseveration
Restrict the number of laryngoscopy attempts—generally no more than three optimized attempts before transitioning to alternative techniques or declaring failure—consistent with CAFG and DAS recommendations. Repeated attempts cause airway edema and bleeding, progressively worsening conditions. Limiting attempts preserves rescue options and prevents "cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate" (CICO) scenarios.
Recognize indications for awake intubation and implement when appropriate
When appropriate, perform awake intubation when the patient is suspected to be a difficult intubation and one or more of the following apply: difficult ventilation predicted, increased aspiration risk, patient unlikely to tolerate brief apnea or expected difficulty with emergency invasive airway rescue. This approach aligns with the 2022 ASA Difficult Airway Algorithm decision tree, which uses these specific criteria to guide pathway selection. Recent studies from experienced centers demonstrate high success rates and low complication rates, though success rates vary with operator experience and institutional volume. While video laryngoscopy has expanded options for managing difficult airways after induction, awake intubation remains an important technique that provides an extra margin of safety when multiple risk factors converge or when impossible intubation is predicted.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Insufficient monitoring after extubation or airway intervention increases the risk of unrecognized deterioration and delayed reintubation.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support early identification of post-extubation complications and prompt intervention.
Perform structured extubation risk assessment
Before extubation, evaluate the patient’s ability to tolerate removal of airway support and the potential difficulty of reintubation. Extubation failure occurs in 10%–20% of critically ill patients who meet weaning criteria, with rates varying by clinical context. In the perioperative setting, identifying at-risk patients allows for appropriate monitoring and rescue planning.
Maintain enhanced monitoring in the immediate post-extubation period
In at-risk patients, continue pulse oximetry, capnography (when available) and frequent clinical assessment for an appropriate period post-extubation, recognizing that upper airway obstruction from laryngeal edema typically occurs soon after extubation. The duration of enhanced monitoring should be individualized based on patient risk factors and clinical trajectory. Early detection allows intervention before respiratory failure develops.
Use airway exchange catheters for high-risk extubations
In patients with known difficult intubation or anticipated difficult reintubation, consider leaving an airway exchange catheter in place after extubation. The catheter provides a conduit for immediate reintubation if extubation fails, avoiding the need for a repeat difficult laryngoscopy.
Establish clear criteria for reintubation
Define objective postoperative thresholds, such as oxygen saturation, respiratory rate or work of breathing, that trigger reintubation rather than relying on subjective assessment. Delayed reintubation is associated with increased morbidity and mortality. Objective criteria reduce hesitation and prevent prolonged hypoxia.


CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Medication Error

Contributing action or omission: Incorrect drug selection, dosing errors or administration of look-alike medications during airway management increases the risk of adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce medication errors during high-stress airway management situations.
Standardize medication organization and labeling in the anesthesia workspace
Maintain standardized medications that are pre-labeled and color-coded according to international standards and organized for rapid access. This may include pre-prepared medication trays, designated medication drawers with standardized layout, or automated dispensing cabinet configurations with emergency airway medication sets. The specific implementation should match institutional resources while ensuring that emergency medications are immediately accessible, clearly labeled and organized to reduce time pressure and cognitive load during emergencies. Substitution errors and incorrect dosing are the most common error types in anesthesia medication management, and standardized organization reduces swap errors. 
Store high-risk medications separately
Store neuromuscular blocking agents and other high-risk medications separately from routine induction agents. Physical separation creates a forcing function that prevents inadvertent selection of paralytic agents when sedatives are intended.
Verify medications verbally before administration
Read the drug name and dose aloud before administration, particularly during emergencies. Verbal verification creates a cognitive pause that interrupts automatic behavior, allowing error detection before administration.
CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Technical errors during laryngoscopy, supraglottic airway placement or emergency surgical airway access increase the risk of failed airway management and patient harm.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure technical proficiency, appropriate device selection and timely escalation to definitive airway management when standard approaches fail.
Maintain proficiency with video laryngoscopy as a primary technique
Develop and maintain expertise in video laryngoscopy for both routine and difficult airway management. Video laryngoscopy reduces failed intubation rates (risk ratio 0.41-0.51), improves first-pass success and provides better glottic visualization compared to direct laryngoscopy across patient populations and clinical settings. Video laryngoscopy appears to provide the greatest benefit for less experienced operators and in patients with difficult airway features. Maintain competency in direct laryngoscopy as a backup technique when video laryngoscopy fails or is unavailable
Ensure competency in supraglottic airway device placement
Maintain proficiency with second-generation supraglottic airway devices as both primary airway management tools and rescue devices. Supraglottic airways provide a critical rescue option when intubation fails. Second-generation devices offer advantages over first-generation devices, including higher seal pressures and gastric access.
Maintain competency in emergency surgical airway techniques through simulation-based training
Participate in simulation-based cricothyrotomy training, preferably using the scalpel-bougie-tube technique, which is recommended by most airway societies. Initial training should include a minimum of five practice attempts, thought evidence suggests performance may continue to improve through 7-10 iterations. Cannula-based techniques may be considered by practitioners experienced in their use.
When formal simulation programs are unavailable, consider low-cost alternatives such as porcine larynx models or self-made simulators, which have demonstrated effectiveness in improving procedural speed and accuracy.
Declare CICO early and proceed immediately to surgical airway
When oxygenation is failing and both intubation and ventilation have failed, declare a CICO emergency and proceed immediately to front-of-neck access. Delay in attempting a surgical airway is a common error that contributes to claims. Early declaration and action prevent the hypoxic brain injury that occurs with prolonged failed rescue attempts.

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Provider-to-provider communication failures, including inadequate team briefings and handoff breakdowns, increase the likelihood of clinical errors.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support coordinated team response.
Conduct pre-induction team briefings for anticipated difficult airways
Before induction in patients with known or suspected difficult airways, brief the entire team on the airway plan, backup strategies and role assignments. Briefings ensure that each team member understands their role during emergencies, reducing response time and improving outcomes.
Use structured handoff tools during intraoperative care transitions
Implement handoff protocols with electronic checklists that include airway management details, current airway status and any difficulties encountered. Intraoperative transitions are associated with adverse outcomes. Structured tools improve information transfer and reduce the risk of critical information loss.
Employ closed-loop communication during airway emergencies
Verbalize instructions clearly to team members and require verbal confirmation and acknowledgement of task completion. Closed-loop communication ensures that instructions are heard, understood and executed, which is critical during high-stress CICO situations.
Communicate airway status at all care transitions
Share airway assessment findings and management difficulties during handoffs to the PACU, ICU and ward staff. Post-extubation complications may occur after operating room transfer; receiving providers must understand the patient's airway risk profile to ensure appropriate monitoring and response to deterioration.

ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Insufficient documentation of airway assessment, management attempts and complications compromises continuity of care and create liability exposure.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive, contemporaneous documentation that protects both patients and practitioners.
Systematically document preoperative airway assessment findings
Record specific airway examination findings (such as Mallampati class, mouth opening, thyromental distance, neck mobility and dentition) rather than documenting "airway normal." Detailed documentation confirms an appropriate assessment and provides critical information for future anesthetics. Inadequate airway evaluation remains the most cited judgment failure in claims.
Sample documentation language: "Airway assessment: Mallampati III, mouth opening 3 cm, thyromental distance 5 cm, limited neck extension, BMI 42, full dentition with prominent upper incisors. Short thick neck, no prior history of radiation or surgical airway changes. Prior anesthetic record reviewed — Grade 3 view with direct laryngoscopy (2019, Dr. Smith, Community Hospital). Predictors of difficult mask ventilation: BMI >40, Mallampati III, limited jaw protrusion. Predictors of difficult supraglottic airway: limited mouth opening, short thyromental distance.
Assessment: Multiple predictors of difficult intubation and difficult mask ventilation. Awake intubation indicated per ASA criteria (suspected difficult intubation with predicted difficult ventilation)."
Create a detailed record of airway management attempts
Document each intubation attempt intra- and postoperatively, including the device used, laryngoscopic view, reason for failure and complications. Detailed documentation demonstrates appropriate escalation, explains clinical decision-making and provides essential information for future airway management.
Sample documentation language: "Induction: Propofol 200 mg, rocuronium 50 mg. First attempt: C-MAC hyperangulated blade, Grade 2a view, ETT 7.0 passed with bougie, confirmed with ETCO2 and bilateral breath sounds. SpO2 remained >95% throughout."
Carefully document and communicate incidents of difficult airway management 
In cases of difficult airway management, document findings postoperatively in a standard format, notify the patient both verbally and in writing and ensure that the information is prominently flagged in the medical record. Future anesthesiologists depend on this information to plan safe airway management. Failure to communicate difficult airway status to patients and future providers is a preventable source of harm.
Document the rationale for clinical decisions
Record the reasoning behind technique selection, particularly when deviating from standard approaches or managing anticipated difficulty. Documenting clinical reasoning demonstrates thoughtful decision-making and provides context that may be critical in defending care decisions.
ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR
Failure to Recognize Responsibility 
Contributing action or omission: Failure to perform adequate preoperative airway assessment, modify the airway plan based on risk factors or recognize indications for awake intubation increases the risk of unanticipated difficult airway emergencies.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate recognition of airway risk and selection of the safest management approach.

• Perform comprehensive airway assessment before every anesthetic

Before initiating anesthetic care, ensure that an airway risk assessment is performed to identify patient, medical, surgical, environmental and anesthetic factors that may indicate potential for a difficult airway. Evaluate demographic information, clinical conditions, prior airway history and physical examination findings. No single characteristic is consistently more predictive of risk than another; assess and consider multiple factors. 

• Recognize indications for awake intubation

Perform awake intubation when the patient is suspected to be a difficult intubation and one or more of the following apply: increased aspiration risk, patient unlikely to tolerate brief apnea, or expected difficulty with emergency invasive airway rescue. Recent studies demonstrate 95%–99% success rates and low complication rates (1.6%–5.4%) with awake intubation, but awake intubation is underused when difficult airways are clearly recognizable. Video laryngoscopy can be used to perform awake intubation with success rates comparable to flexible bronchoscopy in available studies, with potentially shorter intubation times, making the technique more accessible to practitioners already familiar with VL. Proficiency with flexible bronchoscopy remains valuable, particularly for complex airway pathology. Awake intubation remains the ASA-recommended approach when appropriate and when the criteria above are met. The ASA acknowledges that clinical judgment, patient cooperation and clinical context may support proceeding with intubation after induction when benefits outweigh risks.

• Modify the airway management plan based on identified risk factors

When airway assessment identifies predictors of difficulty, adjust the management plan accordingly. This may include: ensuring that difficult airway equipment is immediately available, having a skilled assistant present, considering awake intubation or planning for video laryngoscopy as the primary technique. Document the rationale for the chosen approach.

• Recognize high-risk clinical contexts

Maintain heightened vigilance in settings associated with increased airway complications: emergency procedures, non-operating room locations, morbidly obese patients and patients with multiple comorbidities. These contexts are overrepresented in closed claims analyses and warrant additional preparation.

• Participate in regular airway management training and simulation

Maintain competency in both routine and rescue airway techniques through ongoing education and simulation. Human factors, including situational awareness, decision-making and avoidance of perseveration, play essential roles in airway management safety. Institutions should designate an airway lead to help institute protocols and ensure adequate training.

TITLE: Aspiration
RISK_BRIEF:
Pulmonary Aspiration During Anesthesia
SPECIALTY: Anesthesiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): General Anesthesia, Procedural Sedation

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Pulmonary Aspiration
Pulmonary aspiration of gastric contents during anesthesia is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk in anesthesiology.
Mitigating Your Risk 
With systematic preoperative risk assessment, adherence to evidence-based fasting guidelines, appropriate airway management techniques and vigilant postoperative monitoring, anesthesiologists can mitigate the risks associated with pulmonary aspiration and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for pulmonary aspiration and provides strategies to help prevent them.   
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns 
 
Malpractice claims involving pulmonary aspiration typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 70% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 30%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Pulmonary Aspiration During Anesthesia Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Medication Error
Contributing action or omission: Errors in medication selection, dosing or timing during induction and airway management increase aspiration risk and compromise patient safety.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize medication administration and reduce the likelihood of errors during high-stakes airway management.
Tailor RSI drug selection and dosing to the patient
Select induction agents and doses based on patient-specific factors including hemodynamic status, comorbidities and aspiration risk. Commonly used RSI regimens include propofol,  etomidate or ketamine for induction, combined with succinylcholine or rocuronium for neuromuscular blockade. Ketamine or etomidate may be preferred in hemodynamically unstable patients. Where institutional RSI protocols exist, follow them; document deviations with clinical rationale.
Ensure immediate availability of reversal agents before and during surgery

Where sugammadex is available, confirm its accessibility before using rocuronium for RSI. Where sugammadex is not on formulary, consider this limitation when choosing between rocuronium and succinylcholine, particularly in patients with anticipated difficult airways where the ability to rapidly reverse paralysis may be clinically important. Verify that atropine and other emergency medications are immediately accessible.
Verify medication identity and dose before administration

Before induction, verify medication identity and dose using available safety systems. When they’re available, use barcode scanning or two-person verification for high-risk medications. In solo practice settings without a second anesthesia provider, use standardized syringe labeling per ASTM D4774 color-coding standards, read the vial label before each drug draw, and read the syringe label before each administration. These self-verification practices reduce syringe swap and dosing errors.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate postoperative monitoring means providers can miss early signs of aspiration, delaying intervention and worsening outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure early detection of aspiration-related complications and prompt escalation of care.
Establish standardized post-aspiration monitoring protocols

Monitor oxygen saturation, respiratory rate and auscultatory findings at defined intervals postoperatively; such protocols facility early detection and management of aspiration. Patients who develop symptoms within 2 hours (cough, wheeze, SpO2 decline >10% or new infiltrate) require close monitoring and may need mechanical ventilation. Maintain heightened vigilance for at least two hours following witnessed or suspected aspiration. 
Communicate aspiration events clearly during handoff
Use structured handoff tools, such as SBAR, to communicate aspiration events, interventions and monitoring requirements to PACU staff. Communication failures contribute to patient injury in 43% of anesthesia malpractice claims. Clear handoff documentation ensures continuity of care and demonstrates appropriate transfer of responsibility.
Define escalation triggers and response protocol
Establish clear, institutionally defined criteria for escalation, such as declined SpO₂ below a predetermined threshold, new respiratory distress or fever. Ensure that PACU staff understand when to notify the anesthesiologist. Consider defining escalation triggers based on patient baseline, procedure type and clinical context.
Obtain chest imaging when clinically indicated

Consider postoperative chest radiography in patients with witnessed aspiration who develop respiratory symptoms, hypoxemia or abnormal lung examination. Radiographic documentation of pulmonary status provides objective evidence of clinical decision-making and guides treatment.


CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL
Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Technical errors in airway management, including inadequate preparation, improper positioning and failure to secure the airway rapidly, increase aspiration risk.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize airway management and minimize the window of vulnerability during induction.
Verify that suction equipment functions before every induction

To ensure that suction equipment is operating properly, test suction tubing and Yankauer catheter immediately before induction. Confirm adequate suction pressure and position equipment in close reach. Functional suction is essential for managing regurgitation, and equipment malfunction during critical events is a preventable systems failure.
Prepare backup airway equipment and personnel

Before surgery, ensure that equipment such as video laryngoscope, supraglottic airways and fiberoptic bronchoscope are immediately available and trained assistance is present for high-risk inductions. Backup equipment and personnel enable rapid airway control.
Optimize patient positioning for aspiration prevention

Position patients in reverse Trendelenburg (head-up) when feasible during induction. If regurgitation occurs, turn the patient to lateral decubitus position and apply Trendelenburg to prevent distal migration. Proper positioning reduces the risk of gastric contents reaching the lower airways and demonstrates attention to preventive measures.
Secure the airway before abolishing protective reflexes in patients at aspiration risk
Perform rapid sequence induction with endotracheal intubation for patients at aspiration risk who require general anesthesia; this remains the standard approach to minimize the window of vulnerability between loss of consciousness and airway protection. When aspiration risk coexists with anticipated difficult airway management, consider awake intubation per the 2022 ASA Difficult Airway Guidelines, which recommend awake intubation when the patient may be difficult to intubate and is at increased risk of aspiration. When planning procedural sedation rather than general anesthesia for a patient with aspiration risk factors, evaluate whether the procedure can be delayed, sedation depth minimized or the anesthetic plan modified to include airway protection. Make these decisions on a case-by-case basis and document the rationale.
Consider cricoid pressure, recognizing its limitations

Though the evidence is unclear, consider cricoid pressure (Sellick maneuver) during RSI based on institutional protocol and clinical judgment. Release pressure if it impairs laryngoscopy or ventilation. Document whether cricoid pressure was applied, modified or withheld and the rationale for the decision.
Manage witnessed aspiration immediately and systematically

Suction oropharynx and trachea promptly when aspiration occurs during surgery. Secure the patient’s airway and provide 100% oxygen. Consider bronchoscopic suction when suspecting that the patient aspirated particulate matter. Avoid routine prophylactic antibiotics or corticosteroids. Systematic management of aspiration events demonstrates competent crisis response.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Communication failures between providers, including incomplete information transfer and unclear leadership, increase the likelihood of clinical errors and adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support coordinated team response.
Implement structured handoff protocols

Use SBAR or similar frameworks for all care transitions, whether pre-, intra- or postoperative. Communication failure contributes to patient injury in 43% of anesthesia malpractice claims.
Notify the surgical team when fasting requirements have not been met

When inadequate fasting is noted before surgery, immediately notify the surgeon and jointly decide whether to proceed, delay or modify the anesthetic plan. Shared decision-making distributes accountability and ensures that all team members understand risks. Document the team’s discussion and decision.
Use closed-loop communication during intraoperative airway management

In team-based anesthesia models, verbalize each step during RSI and require verbal confirmation from assisting personnel. In solo practice settings where no anesthesia assistant is present, use a structured self-verification process: read the syringe label aloud or silently confirm each medication before administration. Regardless of practice model, document the sequence and timing of medications administered during induction. Closed-loop communication reduces errors during high-stakes procedures and creates a shared model among team members.
Establish clear leadership during intraoperative airway emergencies

Designate the anesthesiologist as airway team leader and assign roles (medication administration, cricoid pressure, equipment management) to other team members before induction in high-risk cases. Clear leadership and role assignment improve coordination during emergencies and can reduce adverse outcomes.
ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUE

Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure
Contributing action or omission: Lack of applicable processes and protocols and inadequate equipment maintenance undermine consistent, safe care delivery.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve readiness, standardize response and support coordinated escalation.

Develop EHR order sets for aspiration prophylaxis and management

Create pre-built order sets for pharmacologic prophylaxis in high-risk patients and for post-aspiration monitoring and treatment. High-risk conditions include obesity, diabetes, gastroparesis, gastroesophageal reflux, pregnancy, hiatal hernia, ileus or bowel obstruction, opioid use, and emergency surgery. Order sets standardize care, reduce cognitive load and ensure that evidence-based interventions are consistently applied. In facilities without EHR systems, maintain printed or laminated protocols and checklists for aspiration management that are readily accessible in all anesthetizing locations.
Implement standard preoperative fasting policies

Develop and disseminate fasting policies consistent with ASA guidelines. The 2023 ASA update (modular update of the 2017 guidelines) recommends that healthy adults be encouraged to drink up to 400 mL of carbohydrate-containing clear liquids until 2 hours before elective procedures to minimize harms of prolonged fasting. Minimum fasting intervals: clear liquids ≥2 hours, breast milk ≥4 hours, infant formula/nonhuman milk/light meal ≥6 hours, fatty foods ≥8 hours. These intervals apply to healthy patients without conditions that increase aspiration risk (gastrointestinal obstruction, pregnancy, uncontrolled reflux, gastroparesis, diabetes, obesity, opioid use or emergency procedures); such patients may require modified fasting strategies. Standard policies ensure consistent practice across providers and shifts.
Integrate NPO verification into pre-procedure checklists

Include NPO status verification in the surgical safety checklist with dual verification by nursing and anesthesia teams. Add electronic prompts to the pre-anesthesia assessment. Systematic verification processes create redundancy and ensure that NPO status is confirmed before every case.
Consider point-of-care gastric ultrasound when fasting status is uncertain or unreliable
Where expertise and equipment are available, consider gastric point-of-care gastric ultrasound (POCUS) to assess aspiration risk and guide anesthetic planning in cases of clinical uncertainty, such as unclear fasting history, NPO non-compliance, emergency or urgent surgery, or conditions that delay gastric emptying (gastroparesis, diabetes, opioid use, obesity, pregnancy, renal or hepatic dysfunction). Gastric POCUS is an emerging but increasingly well-validated adjunct to clinical assessment. While not yet mandated by ASA guidelines, it represents a practical tool for individualized risk stratification, particularly in emergency and urgent settings where fasting time is an unreliable predictor of gastric volume. Document the ultrasound findings and their impact on the anesthetic plan.
Ensure that suction equipment is maintained and functional

Require daily checks of suction function in all operating rooms and procedural areas. Keep preventive maintenance logs for all equipment. Equipment malfunction during aspiration events represents a preventable systems failure that exposes both the provider and institution to liability.
Conduct regular simulation training for aspiration and airway emergencies

Where institutional resources permit, implement multidisciplinary simulation training for airway emergencies to improve team performance and crisis response. For practices without access to formal simulation programs, alternative approaches include periodic review of cognitive aids and emergency algorithms, tabletop exercises and participation in continuing medical education focused on airway emergencies. The American Board of Anesthesiology's MOCA simulation component provides one structured pathway for practicing anesthesiologists. Document participation in any form of emergency preparedness training. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Insufficient documentation compromises continuity of care, accountability and defensibility in the event of adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support timely, complete documentation that reinforces clinical decision-making and clear communication.
Document preoperative NPO status verification and risk assessment

Record the time of last oral intake, type of intake, source of information (patient, family, chart), aspiration risk factors and the rationale for the anesthetic plan. Documentation demonstrates deliberate, evidence-based decision-making, which is essential for defensibility in case of complications.
Document decision rationale when deviating from guidelines before or during surgery

When proceeding despite inadequate fasting when modifying standard RSI technique, document the risk-benefit analysis and clinical reasoning. Deviation from guidelines without documented rationale is often cited as substandard care in malpractice claims, while clear documentation demonstrates thoughtful decision-making.
Record aspiration events and management contemporaneously

Using standardized terminology, document the event, timing, estimated volume and character of aspirate, interventions performed and team communication. Contemporaneous documentation is more credible than retrospective entries and demonstrates competent response.
Ensure that handoff documentation includes airway management details

Document airway management technique, complications, aspiration events, interventions and monitoring requirements for PACU staff. Complete handoff documentation ensures continuity of care and demonstrates appropriate transfer of responsibility, while gaps are often implicated in adverse outcomes.
Maintain the integrity of the medical record 

Document events contemporaneously whenever possible; contemporaneous records are more credible than retrospective entries. When corrections are necessary:
Use the amendment, addendum or correction function appropriate to the documentation system (EHR or paper). Ensure that the original entry remains visible and the correction is attributed, dated and time-stamped.
When editing automatically captured physiologic data (such as removing monitor artifact), use the system's designated annotation or invalidation function and document the reason.
Never falsify, backdate or delete entries to conceal what occurred. Undisclosed alterations are discoverable through EHR metadata and severely damage credibility in litigation, even when the underlying care was appropriate.

TITLE: Critical hypotension
RISK_BRIEF:
Critical Hypotension during Anesthesia Care
SPECIALTY: Anesthesiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Perioperative Care

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Critical Hypotension

Critical hypotension during anesthesia care is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in anesthesiology.

Mitigating Your Risk
With proactive risk identification, appropriate selection of medication and effective technique, anesthesiologists can mitigate the risks associated with critical hypotension and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for critical hypotension and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving critical hypotension during anesthesia care typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 74% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 26%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Critical Hypotension Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act on Diagnostic Finding

Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify patients at high risk for hypotension or recognize early hemodynamic deterioration increases the likelihood of delayed intervention.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to expedite identification of hypotension risk and hemodynamic deterioration, allowing for earlier intervention.
Conduct systematic preoperative risk stratification
Identify patients at elevated risk during preoperative evaluation by assessing ASA status, baseline blood pressure, cardiovascular comorbidities, chronic antihypertensive medications (particularly ACE inhibitors/ARBs and beta-blockers), emergency surgery status and anticipated blood loss. Document risk factors and communicate them to the perioperative team. For patients on ACE inhibitors/ARBs, follow institutional protocols regarding withholding them on the day of surgery. Continuation of ACE inhibitors/ARBs on day of surgery is associated with increased intraoperative hypotension risk. Systematic risk identification supports individualized hemodynamic planning and aligns with standard preoperative assessment expectations.

Establish individual baseline blood pressure targets
Document baseline awake blood pressure during preoperative evaluation of a patient. Refer to ambulatory or preoperative clinic blood pressures when available, as day-of-surgery readings may reflect situational (“white coat”) responses. Consider patient-specific thresholds informed by baseline blood pressure instead of population-based cutoffs. Patients with chronic hypertension or cerebrovascular disease may require higher intraoperative pressures to maintain organ perfusion. Individualized targets based on documented baseline values support defensible clinical decisions and avoid reliance on population-based cutoffs that may not account for patient-specific physiology.
Implement continuous arterial blood pressure monitoring for high-risk patients when available 

Use invasive arterial line or noninvasive continuous NIBP monitoring for high-risk patients and surgeries when available and feasible. When continuous monitoring is not available, increase the frequency of intermittent NIBP measurements and maintain heightened vigilance for clinical signs of hypoperfusion during induction and critical periods. Randomized trials show that continuous monitoring reduces the number and severity of hypotensive episodes compared to intermittent oscillometric monitoring by enabling earlier detection and intervention. Expert consensus supports NIBP cycling every 1–2 minutes during induction and other high-risk periods when continuous monitoring is unavailable, as standard cycling intervals may miss brief but clinically significant hypotensive episodes.
Use automated alerts for hypotension when your monitoring system supports them
Enable automated alerts for sustained hypotension (MAP below 65 mm Hg or patient-specific threshold), if available. In the absence of automated alerts, set personal vigilance thresholds and intervene promptly when MAP falls below target. Automated alerts reduce reliance on vigilance and facilitate earlier intervention. Machine learning-based early warning systems (such as the Hypotension Prediction Index) have been shown to reduce hypotension exposure, but these systems are not yet widely available.
Use advanced hemodynamic monitoring during surgery in high-risk cases when available

Monitor stroke volume, cardiac output or dynamic fluid responsiveness indices (pulse pressure variation, stroke volume variation) in high-risk patients or surgeries if possible. Advanced monitoring helps identify the underlying cause of hypotension and guides targeted therapy. When advanced monitoring is not available, use clinical assessment and basic monitoring to guide therapy. Assess heart rate, urine output, estimated blood loss and fluid balance. Evaluate response to fluid bolus, consider point-of-care ultrasound (if trained) to assess volume status and cardiac function. Use transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) or focused cardiac ultrasound (FoCUS) in patients with unexplained hemodynamic instability when expertise is available.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Medication Error

Contributing action or omission: Errors in vasopressor selection, dosing or administration contribute to inadequate treatment of hypotension and iatrogenic harm.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce medication errors and support safe, effective pharmacologic management of hypotension.
Standardize vasopressor availability and preparation

Ensure that vasopressors (phenylephrine, ephedrine, norepinephrine) are immediately available before induction in all cases. If commercially prepared prefilled syringes are available, stock them in anesthesia workstations to reduce preparation errors and medication contamination. If prefilled syringes are not available, prepare vasopressors in advance using standard concentrations and double-check calculations. Avoid bedside dilution of vasopressors whenever possible. When dilution is necessary, use standard concentrations.
Apply color-coded syringe labeling per international standards

Label all syringes immediately upon preparation using standard color-coding (ISO 26825/ASTM D4774). Verify drug name, concentration and expiration before each administration. Use double-check systems for vasopressor and anesthetic dosing when available. Syringe swaps are a leading cause of anesthesia medication errors. Standard labeling and verification at the point of administration reduces the risk of administering the wrong-drug and wrong-dose errors that can cause or worsen hemodynamic instability.
Administer vasopressors promptly when hypotension persists
Initiate vasopressor therapy without delay when hypotension is severe or persists after appropriate fluid administration. Temporary vasopressor use while fluid resuscitation is ongoing is acceptable practice. Prolonged hypotension, even brief episodes below critical thresholds, is associated with increased organ injury risk. Prompt vasopressor initiation shortens hypotensive exposure and demonstrates timely clinical decision-making. 
Select vasopressors based on the underlying hemodynamic mechanism
Choose vasopressors whose pharmacologic profile matches the identified or suspected cause of hypotension. Consider the balance of vasoconstrictive and inotropic properties when selecting an agent. Mechanism-based vasopressor selection addresses the root cause of hypotension and reduces the risk of iatrogenic harm. Agents with primarily vasoconstrictive effects may be insufficient or harmful when cardiac output is compromised, while agents with beta-agonist properties may worsen hemodynamics in conditions involving dynamic outflow obstruction. 
Titrate vasopressors to defined hemodynamic endpoints during surgery

Administer vasopressors with a clear hemodynamic target rather than empirically. Reassess response after each intervention and document the rationale for dose adjustments. Set escalation triggers for transitioning from bolus vasopressors to continuous infusion. Triggers include requiring more than 2–3 boluses within a defined time period or persistent hypotension despite repeated boluses. Goal-directed vasopressor titration reduces the risk of both under-treatment and over-treatment. Predefined escalation triggers support timely transitions from bolus to infusion therapy and reduce indecision during evolving hemodynamic instability.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failure to address the underlying cause of hypotension or to escalate care appropriately prolongs hypotensive exposure and increases organ injury risk.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that hypotension is treated based on its underlying mechanism and escalation occurs when initial interventions fail.
Identify and treat the hemodynamic endotype during surgery

Determine if hypotension is due to vasodilation, hypovolemia, myocardial depression or bradycardia using available monitoring (heart rate, stroke volume variation, cardiac output if available). Treat based on the cause: fluids for hypovolemia, vasopressors for vasodilation, inotropes for myocardial depression, chronotropic agents for bradycardia. Treat rate abnormalities when they contribute to hemodynamic compromise. Mechanism-based treatment demonstrates sound clinical reasoning and addresses the root cause of hypotension rather than treating a number. Hypotension can result from a variety of underlying circulatory problems, which may explain why uniform blood pressure targets alone have not consistently improved outcomes in clinical trials.
Implement goal-directed fluid therapy protocols for use during surgery

Administer fluids only when clinical or metabolic signs of hypovolemia or tissue hypoperfusion are present. Use dynamic indices of fluid responsiveness (pulse pressure variation, stroke volume variation) to guide fluid administration when available. Monitor for fluid overload (pulmonary edema, elevated CVP) when administering large volumes. Goal-directed fluid therapy reduces the risk of both under-resuscitation and volume overload. Fluid loading in vasodilatory hypotension may be ineffective or harmful when the primary problem is loss of vascular tone rather than intravascular volume deficit. 
Extend vigilant monitoring into the postoperative period
Continue frequent blood pressure monitoring in the post-anesthesia care unit (PACU) and consider extended monitoring for high-risk patients. Treat postoperative hypotension when blood pressure falls below patient-specific or institutional thresholds. Postoperative hypotension may go undetected without active monitoring and is independently associated with myocardial injury. Published guidelines identify MAP below 60-65 mm Hg and systolic blood pressure below 90 mm Hg as thresholds associated with increased cardiovascular, cerebrovascular and renal complications. 
CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Technical errors in monitoring, vascular access or anesthetic technique can contribute to unrecognized or prolonged hypotension.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate monitoring and procedural technique to detect and prevent hypotension.
Modify induction technique based on patient risk factors

Titrate induction agents carefully in high-risk patients (elderly, hypovolemic, ASA III-IV).  Have vasopressors prepared and immediately available before induction. Consider the hemodynamic profile of the chosen induction agent and adjust dose or agent selection based on the patient’s cardiovascular status. Post-induction hypotension is one of the most common and preventable causes of early perioperative hemodynamic instability. Dose reduction and careful titration in vulnerable patients demonstrate anticipatory risk management.
Implement pre-emptive vasopressor strategies in high-risk patients

Consider starting low-dose vasopressor infusion (norepinephrine, phenylephrine) before or at induction in high-risk patients. Anticipate that neuraxial blockade causes vasodilation and plan vasopressor support accordingly. Proactive vasopressor administration, rather than reactive treatment after hypotension develops, reduces the duration and severity of hypotension exposure. Fluid preloading alone may not prevent hypotension following neuraxial blockade in normovolemic patients.

Optimize anesthesia monitoring to titrate agents and avoid excessive depth
Monitor the depth of anesthesia and titrate anesthetic agents to the minimum effective dose. Adjust opioid selection and dosing based on the patient's hemodynamic response during induction and maintenance. Excessive anesthetic depth contributes to vasodilation and myocardial depression, both of which cause or worsen hypotension. Precise titration guided by monitoring reduces unnecessary hemodynamic compromise.
Account for patient positioning effects on blood pressure

Anticipate hemodynamic changes from position shifts (Trendelenburg, reverse Trendelenburg, lateral decubitus, beach chair) during surgery and plan monitoring and interventions accordingly. Verify proper positioning of blood pressure cuff and arterial line transducer; reposition or recalibrate if readings are inconsistent with clinical status. Position changes can cause abrupt blood pressure shifts that mimic or mask true hypotension. Artifactual hypotension readings from cuff malposition or transducer error can lead to inappropriate treatment or failure to recognize true hypotension.


ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Provider-to-provider communication failures, including handoff breakdowns and unclear leadership, increase the likelihood of clinical errors and delayed response to hypotension.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes, clarify leadership and support coordinated team response to reduce errors and the chance of perioperative morbidity and mortality.
Establish closed-loop communication protocols for critical events
Clearly verbalize medication orders and require confirmation from the administering provider to ensure that instructions are heard and understood. Announce blood pressure values and trends during resuscitation. Closed-loop communication reduces the risk of medication and dosing errors during high-acuity events by confirming that instructions are heard, understood and executed correctly.

Implement standardized intraoperative handoffs

Use standard protocols, such as SBAR, I-PASS or institution-specific procedures, for all intraoperative handoffs. Include electronic handoff checklists in the anesthesia record where available, or a printed/verbal checklist in settings without electronic records. Checklists should include current hemodynamic status, vasopressor requirements, cumulative blood loss and anticipated hemodynamic challenges. Structured handoff tools ensure that critical hemodynamic information transfers reliably between providers. Omission of vasopressor requirements or active hemodynamic instability during handoff frequently delays intervention.

Standardize PACU handoff to include intraoperative hypotensive events

Communicate intraoperative hypotensive events, treatments and current hemodynamic status to the PACU team. Include monitoring and intervention thresholds in postoperative orders.
Postoperative hypotension is common, often unrecognized and independently associated with organ injury. Explicit communication of intraoperative hemodynamic events ensures the receiving team maintains appropriate vigilance and intervention readiness.
Communicate proactively with the surgical team throughout all phases of care 

Brief the team before surgery on high-risk patients and the planned management approach for them. Provide real-time updates during surgery on significant hemodynamic events and when surgical maneuvers (insufflation, retraction, blood loss) are contributing to instability. Designate a team leader at the onset of a hemodynamic crisis to coordinate care, assign tasks and maintain awareness of the situation. Surgical maneuvers are a frequent contributor to intraoperative hypotension; real-time communication enables the surgical team to modify technique or pause when a patient requires hemodynamic stabilization. Clear leadership during crises reduces response delays and role confusion.


ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Insufficient or delayed documentation of assessments, interventions and communication can compromise continuity of care and defensibility in litigation.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support timely, accurate and comprehensive records that enhance patient safety and provide defensibility in adverse outcomes.
Document blood pressure values and interventions in real time

Record significant hypotensive episodes (MAP below 65 mm Hg or patient-specific threshold), including onset time, duration, nadir blood pressure and temporal relationship to surgical or anesthetic events. If your facility uses an anesthesia information management system (AIMS), review automated entries for accuracy before case completion, as auto-populated data errors may be attributed to the anesthesiologist of record.
If you are using a paper anesthesia record, document blood pressure values frequently throughout the case and more often during critical periods. Annotate significant hypotensive episodes with interventions and response to treatment. Record the actual measured value rather than a smoothed or estimated value, as discrepancies between the paper record and monitor download data are discoverable in litigation. ASA standards and common practice support intervals of 5 minutes or less for paper records.
Document rationale for monitoring and treatment decisions during and after surgery

Record the clinical reasoning for treatment decisions, including the choice of vasopressors, fluids or other interventions. Document the rationale for monitoring decisions (for example, why invasive arterial monitoring was or was not used). Ensure consistent, real-time documentation by all anesthesia team members. Documenting clinical reasoning demonstrates thoughtful, evidence-based decision-making and supports defensibility of adverse outcomes. Records that capture the "why" behind decisions are substantially more defensible than those that capture only the "what."

Ensure that documentation captures all ASA-required elements during and after surgery

Whether using structured EHR templates or a paper record, verify that documentation includes physiologic monitoring data, all medications (with dose, time, route and response), blood loss, significant intraoperative events and key provider communications. Where structured templates are available, use them to reduce omissions. Structured templates standardize documentation and reduce variability, making it less likely that required elements are missed during high-acuity cases. Completeness of the anesthesia record is a frequent focus of expert review in malpractice litigation.
Complete postoperative documentation promptly

Complete the anesthesia record, including a summary of significant intraoperative events and hemodynamic status at transfer, before the patient leaves the facility. Document postoperative concerns shared with the receiving team. When clinical care and documentation conflict during a rapidly evolving event, prioritize patient care, then document as soon as the situation permits, noting the reason for any delay. Delayed documentation increases the risk of omissions and inaccuracies. Records completed well after the event are more vulnerable to challenge in litigation and may not reflect the clinical reasoning at the time decisions were made.

Conduct periodic audits of hypotension documentation

In group or institutional settings, participate in quality assurance programs that audit documentation of hypotensive episodes and interventions. For independent or locum practitioners, conduct a personal review of your documentation after any case involving significant hypotension, noting whether onset, duration, nadir, interventions and response were captured. Use audit findings to identify improvement opportunities. Routine review, whether institutional or self-directed, identifies documentation gaps before they become vulnerabilities in litigation. Audit programs also demonstrate a commitment to continuous improvement, which supports both patient safety and institutional credibility.
OVERVIEW:
Critical Hypotension during Anesthesia Care
SPECIALTY: Anesthesiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Perioperative Care

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Critical Hypotension

Critical hypotension during anesthesia care is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in anesthesiology.

Mitigating Your Risk
With proactive risk identification, appropriate selection of medication and effective technique, anesthesiologists can mitigate the risks associated with critical hypotension and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can
PRESENTING_CONDITIONS: Perioperative Care
ADVERSE_OUTCOMES: Critical Hypotension

TITLE: Opioid related harm
RISK_BRIEF:
Opioid-Related Patient Harm
SPECIALTY: Anesthesiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Perioperative Pain Management

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Opioid-Related Patient Harm

Opioid-related patient harm represents a significant driver of malpractice risk and severe patient harm in anesthesiology. 
Mitigating Your Risk 
With systematic risk assessment, standardized protocols and vigilant monitoring, anesthesiologists can mitigate the risks associated with opioid therapy and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for opioid-related patient harm and provides strategies to help prevent them.
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving opioid-related patient harm typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 69% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 31%. Any one of these—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Opioid-Related Patient Harm [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Medication Error

Contributing action or omission: Failure to individualize opioid therapy based on patient risk factors increases the likelihood of adverse events.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support targeted, evidence-based opioid therapy and minimize the likelihood of harm.
Stratify patients using validated risk classification

During preoperative evaluation, systematically categorize patients based on their opioid use history. Identify modifiers that increase risk, including obstructive sleep apnea, cardiac disease, pulmonary disease, renal impairment, concurrent sedative use and psychiatric comorbidities. Document and communicate findings to the perioperative team. Systematic preoperative risk classification enables tailored opioid dosing, appropriate monitoring intensity and identification of modifiable risk factors before surgery. 
Implement multimodal analgesia as the foundation of pain management
Use individualized multimodal analgesia, selecting from regional/neuraxial techniques, acetaminophen, NSAIDs, gabapentinoids, ketamine, lidocaine infusions and other nonopioid adjuncts as clinically appropriate, to reduce reliance on opioids. Agent selection should account for patient comorbidities, contraindications, procedure type and institutional formulary. Be aware of agent-specific risks, including the FDA's caution regarding concurrent use of gabapentinoids with opioids and CNS depressants.
Use the lowest effective opioid dose for the shortest duration

When opioids are required, use immediate-release formulations and avoid modified-release formulations and transdermal patches in the immediate postoperative period. Titrate carefully in high-risk and opioid-tolerant patients. Individualize dosing decisions based on patient factors, procedure type and clinical response. 
Communicate opioid risk and intraoperative management to the discharging team
At the end of anesthesia care, communicate the following information to the surgical team responsible for discharge planning: the patient's opioid risk classification, cumulative intraoperative opioid dose, multimodal agents administered and any adverse effects. While discharge prescribing is typically the responsibility of the surgical service, anesthesiologists can influence safe prescribing by providing clear, documented recommendations based on intraoperative opioid requirements and patient risk factors.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Medication Error

Contributing action or omission: Failure to administer the correct opioid dose, prevent syringe swaps, ensure the correct route of administration or avoid preparation errors increases the risk of patient harm.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce the likelihood of opioid medication errors through standardization, verification and system safeguards.

Create systems to reduce human error
Minimize distractions during medication preparation and administration throughout all stages of surgery. Contributing factors, such as inattention, haste, communication failures and fatigue, can lead to errors. Report all medication errors and near-misses to institutional safety systems for analysis and improvement.
Implement standardized labeling and storage protocols
Use color-coded, pre-printed syringe labels consistent with national standards before and during operations. Store opioids in clearly labeled, designated compartments, separate from look-alike medications. Regularly audit medication storage to ensure compliance. Syringe swaps and incorrect ampoule selection are among the most frequently reported types of anesthesia medication errors. Standardized color-coded labeling and physical separation of opioids from look-alike medications create verification layers, while regular audits prevent drift as staff and formularies change.
Use prefilled syringes and standardized concentrations
Before and during operations, use commercially prefilled syringes when available to eliminate preparation errors. Avoid multi-dose vials when single-dose alternatives exist. Where prefilled syringes are not institutionally available, advocate for their adoption through pharmacy and patient safety committees. In the interim, use standardized dilution protocols and perform independent verification of manually prepared opioid concentrations. Incorrect dosing and dilution errors are among the more frequently reported anesthesia medication errors. Prefilled syringes and standardized concentrations substantially reduce preparation-related failures, while independent verification of manually prepared drugs provides a secondary check where prefilled options are unavailable.
Adopt verification processes at each step of medication handling
During operations, perform independent double-checks for high-alert medications, particularly opioid infusions. Verify drug identity, concentration and dose before administration. Use barcode scanning at the point of administration when available. Where barcode scanning is not available, advocate for institutional adoption and rely on manual verification processes (read-back, independent double-check) as interim safeguards. Verification processes interrupt error chains before administration, reducing the likelihood of adverse events.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failure to prioritize non-opioid alternatives, select appropriate patients for opioids and escalate care contributes to adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support appropriate, timely treatment decisions that are responsive to patient status.
Set realistic expectations and assess functional outcomes rather than relying on pain scores
Before surgery, establish realistic expectations with patients about pain and recovery timelines. In the immediate postoperative period, assess analgesic adequacy using patient-reported comfort, ability to participate in early recovery activities (deep breathing, coughing, position changes) and sedation level rather than relying solely on numeric pain scores. Communicate functional pain goals and analgesic response to the surgical and nursing teams responsible for ongoing postoperative pain management and rehabilitation. This approach prevents unnecessary opioid dose escalation, reduces the risk of respiratory depression and oversedation and aligns with current evidence-based practice.
Ensure availability of reversal agents and rescue protocols

Maintain naloxone availability in all areas where opioids are administered. Train all perioperative staff on the recognition and management of opioid-induced ventilatory impairment. Establish protocols for rapid response to opioid-related emergencies. Preparation for opioid emergencies reduces time to intervention and demonstrates appropriate systems of care.

Establish clear escalation pathways for inadequate analgesia
Develop institutional protocols for escalating care when standard multimodal approaches fail, including criteria for consultation with acute pain services. Reassess the analgesic plan and consider alternative interventions before escalating opioid doses. Document clinical reasoning when modifying the analgesic plan to support continuity of care across providers and thoughtful decision-making. Structured escalation pathways facilitate appropriate specialist involvement for complex patients and reduce the likelihood of inappropriate dose escalation.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failure to adequately monitor for opioid-induced ventilatory impairment (OIVI), including respiratory depression, oversedation and airway obstruction, is a leading contributor to preventable opioid-related deaths.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below apply to the inpatient postoperative period and work together to support early detection of deterioration and prompt intervention. While anesthesiologists may not directly perform all postoperative monitoring, they are responsible for specifying monitoring requirements based on the opioid regimen administered, communicating those requirements during handoff to the receiving team and ensuring that appropriate monitoring orders are in place before transferring care.
Standardize monitoring protocols based on opioid route and patient risk 
Monitor sedation level, respiratory status and oxygen saturation closely in patients receiving systemic opioids in the initial hours after surgery and following dose changes. 
For neuraxial opioid monitoring, ASA guidelines recommend the following minimum intervals:
Single-injection neuraxial hydrophilic opioids (such as morphine): monitor at least hourly for the first 12 hours, then every 2 hours for the next 12 hours, continuing for at least 24 hours.
Extended-release epidural morphine: monitor for at least 48 hours after administration.
Clinicians should consult current ASA guidelines and adapt monitoring frequency to individual patient risk factors, institutional protocols and clinical circumstances.
Most opioid-induced respiratory depression occurs early in the postoperative course, when timely detection through structured monitoring can prevent irreversible harm. Monitoring consistent with ASA-specified intervals supports early identification of deterioration and prompt intervention. Protocols based on opioid route and patient risk ensure that monitoring intensity matches the clinical risk profile.
Prioritize sedation assessment over respiratory rate alone
Use validated sedation scales to assess for opioid-induced ventilatory impairment (OIVI). Treat oversedation as OIVI until proven otherwise. Do not rely on respiratory rate as the sole indicator of ventilatory status. Recognize that hypoxemia may be a late sign, especially in patients on supplemental oxygen.
Respiratory rate can remain normal even as tidal volume and minute ventilation decline, making it an unreliable early indicator. Sedation typically precedes clinically significant respiratory depression, providing an earlier intervention window. Supplemental oxygen can further mask deterioration by maintaining SpO2 despite worsening hypoventilation.
Increase monitoring intensity for high-risk patients
Implement continuous pulse oximetry postoperatively for patients receiving parenteral opioids, particularly those with risk factors like obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), obesity, concurrent sedatives and extremes of age. Consider capnography monitoring in patients receiving patient-controlled analgesia or those at elevated risk. 
Patients with OSA, pulmonary disease, cardiac disease and concurrent sedative use face a significantly elevated risk for respiratory depression. Enhanced monitoring supports earlier detection of deterioration and more timely intervention.
Establish clear response protocols for detected deterioration
After surgery, define escalation triggers and response pathways for abnormal monitoring findings. Keep reversal agents immediately available and train staff in their use. Document all monitoring findings, interventions and patient responses. 
Structured response protocols reduce time to intervention when deterioration is detected. Contemporaneous documentation supports continuity of care and provides a record of clinical decision-making.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Failure to establish clear handoffs, define responsibility or coordinate care between providers increases the likelihood of opioid-related errors and adverse events.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support coordinated team response.
Establish clear leadership and role assignment
Designate responsibility for postoperative opioid management and monitoring at each transition of care. Ensure that receiving providers acknowledge and understand opioid-related concerns and monitoring requirements. Define protocols for escalation when concerns arise. Clear leadership and role assignment establish accountability and reduces the likelihood of gaps in patient monitoring.
Implement structured handoff protocols
Use standard frameworks, such as SBAR, I-PASS or institutional equivalents, for all care transitions. Include opioid-specific information in handoffs, including current opioid regimen, cumulative doses, patient risk factors and monitoring requirements. Structured handoff tools improve the relay and retention of critical patient information and reduce opportunities for error.
Emphasize closed-loop communication
Verbalize opioid orders and confirm receipt and understanding from intra- to postoperative care. Train teams on closed-loop communication during both routine care and simulations. Document critical communications in the medical record. Closed-loop communication confirms receipt and understanding of critical information, reducing the likelihood of errors.
Communicate opioid-related risk factors and intraoperative management to the surgical team responsible for discharge planning
Before transferring postoperative care, communicate the patient's opioid risk classification, preoperative opioid use history, cumulative intraoperative opioid dose, multimodal agents used, and any observed adverse effects or concerns to the surgical team. When patients are on chronic preoperative opioid therapy, flag the need for outpatient prescriber coordination in the handoff. 
Include specific recommendations for postoperative opioid management and monitoring intensity based on the patient's risk profile. While coordination with outpatient providers and discharge opioid planning is typically the responsibility of the surgical service, anesthesiologists can support safe transitions by ensuring that critical opioid-related information is clearly communicated and documented at each handoff.
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Failure to adequately document risk assessment, treatment decisions, monitoring and clinical reasoning compromises continuity of care and weakens defense against malpractice claims.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support timely, complete documentation that reinforces clinical accountability.
Document preoperative opioid risk assessment and plan 
Record the patient's opioid history, risk classification and identified risk factors, including obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), cardiac disease, pulmonary disease, renal impairment, concurrent sedative use and psychiatric comorbidities. For risk classification, use O-NET+ or an equivalent systematic categorization to classify patients as opioid-naïve, opioid-exposed or opioid-tolerant. Document the analgesic plan, including the rationale for opioid use. Record informed consent discussions regarding analgesic options, risks and alternatives. Preoperative risk documentation demonstrates a commitment to thoughtful clinical decision-making and can be critical in the event of a malpractice claim.
Maintain contemporaneous intraoperative records
In accordance with ASA guidelines, document all opioids administered during surgery, including drug, dose, time, route and patient response. Record any unusual events, complications or deviations from the planned analgesic approach. Document clinical reasoning when modifying the analgesic plan intraoperatively. Time-based documentation of medication administration and patient response provides essential evidence of appropriate care.
Ensure comprehensive postoperative documentation
Record monitoring parameters, sedation assessments and respiratory status at specified intervals. Document adverse events, interventions and patient responses. Complete records ensure continuity of care and provide essential evidence during adverse outcome reviews.
Avoid documentation pitfalls
When using templates or copy-and-paste functions, review and individualize content to ensure it reflects actual clinical observations and decisions rather than generic or carried-forward information. Do not alter documentation to misrepresent clinical events. If addenda or late entries are required, clearly label them with date and time. Inaccurate or altered documentation undermines credibility and can transform an otherwise defensible case into an indefensible one.
