Mitigating Your Risk 
With systematic risk assessment, standardized protocols and vigilant monitoring, anesthesiologists can mitigate the risks associated with opioid therapy and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for opi
PRESENTING_CONDITIONS: Perioperative Pain Management
ADVERSE_OUTCOMES: Opioid-Related Patient Harm

TITLE: Peripheral nerve injury
RISK_BRIEF:
Peripheral Nerve Injury

SPECIALTY: Anesthesiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Peripheral Regional Anesthesia, General Anesthesia with Positioning

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Perioperative Peripheral Nerve Injury

Perioperative peripheral nerve injury (PNI) is a significant driver of malpractice claims and severe patient harm in anesthesiology.
Mitigating Your Risk
With meticulous technique, careful documentation and clear communication, anesthesiologists can mitigate the risks associated with perioperative peripheral nerve injury and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for perioperative peripheral nerve injury and provides strategies to help prevent them.
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving perioperative peripheral nerve injury typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 81% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 19%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Perioperative Peripheral Nerve Injury [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Examination

Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify patients at increased risk for nerve injury limits the opportunity for risk-benefit discussion and technique modification.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve preoperative identification of at-risk patients and support informed decision-making.
Perform a focused preoperative history and physical examination

Review the patient’s history for diabetes mellitus, peripheral neuropathy, peripheral vascular disease, alcohol dependency, prior nerve injuries and extremes of body habitus. Preexisting conditions such as diabetes and neuropathy increase the risk of perioperative nerve injury. Identification allows for individualized risk-benefit discussion and technique selection. 
Evaluate surgical and anesthetic factors contributing to risk during surgical planning and consent discussion

Consider tourniquet use, anticipated surgical traction and planned patient positioning (prone, lithotomy, lateral decubitus). Certain surgical procedures (cardiac, neurosurgery, orthopedic) and positions carry a higher risk of nerve injury. Awareness enables prompt mitigation. 
Discuss individualized risks and benefits of regional anesthesia during the consent process

Consider alternative analgesic techniques for patients with preexisting neuropathy or infection at the injection site. Informed consent that specifically mentions the risk of transient or permanent nerve injury increases patient autonomy and reduces liability exposure. 
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act On Diagnostic Finding

Contributing action or omission: Failure to recognize new postoperative neurologic deficits delays diagnosis and treatment, increasing the risk of permanent injury.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support early detection and appropriate escalation of suspected nerve injury.
Consider performing and documenting postoperative neurologic evaluations

Assess motor function, sensation and any paresthesia prior to PACU transfer or discharge for all patients receiving peripheral nerve blocks. Early detection of new neurologic symptoms enables timely investigation and intervention, potentially limiting severe injuries. 
Promptly investigate and document new neurologic symptoms

If a patient reports persistent numbness, weakness or pain after expected block resolution, notify the surgical team and consider early neurology consultation. Delayed recognition of nerve injury is a common contributor to adverse outcomes and malpractice claims. Contemporaneous documentation supports continuity of care and demonstrates responsiveness. 
Educate patients on warning signs and follow-up

Provide clear discharge instructions for reporting persistent neurologic symptoms. Patient engagement in symptom monitoring helps with early detection and demonstrates a commitment to patient safety.


CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL

Technique-Related Error (Block-Related)

Contributing action or omission: Improper needle placement, failure to use multimodal monitoring and injection against high resistance increase the risk of direct nerve injury during peripheral nerve block procedures.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce the likelihood of direct nerve injury during peripheral nerve block procedures.
Select the appropriate needle type and use ultrasound guidance when possible as the primary nerve localization method.
 
Use blunt-tip needles when possible and consider employing ultrasound guidance during peripheral nerve block procedures. Real-time visualization of the needle tip, nerve and local anesthetic spread facilitates immediate detection of intraneural injection (nerve swelling) and reduces unintentional needle-nerve contact. 
Consider adjunctive monitoring techniques when available and applicable
Consider using nerve stimulation for blocks targeting mixed motor-sensory nerves as an additional safeguard against needle-nerve contact. Evidence on the incremental benefit of adding nerve stimulation to ultrasound-guided blocks is mixed; clinical judgment should guide its use. The ASRA advisory recommends monitoring injection pressure as another adjunctive safety measure that may provide additional warning of intraneural needle placement.
Stop injection immediately if warning signs occur

Discontinue injection and reposition the needle if the patient reports severe pain, paresthesia or electric shock-like sensation, or if high injection pressure is detected. These warning signs may indicate intraneural needle placement. Prompt repositioning can prevent fascicular injury. 
CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL

Technique-Related Error (Positioning-Related)

Contributing action or omission: Improper patient positioning, inadequate padding and failure to perform interval position checks increase the risk of positioning-related peripheral nerve injury.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce the likelihood of positioning-related peripheral nerve injury. 
• Collaborate with the intraoperative team to ensure proper limb positioning

Coordinate with surgeons and nursing staff to avoid high-risk positions. These include upper extremity abduction >90° in supine patients, prolonged or excessive elbow flexion, shoulder braces placed directly over the acromioclavicular joint in steep Trendelenburg, direct compression on the peroneal nerve at the fibular head in lithotomy and excessive hip flexion or thigh abduction in lithotomy. Effective communication and collaboration among all team members reduces the likelihood of injuries.
• Use appropriate protective padding

Apply padding to protect vulnerable nerves (ulnar nerve at elbow, peroneal nerve at fibular head, brachial plexus). Recognize that padding alone does not eliminate risk. 

• Perform and document interval position checks
Verify and document padding and limb placement at positioning and at regular intervals during prolonged procedures. Prolonged time in anesthesia is a predictor of positioning-related peripheral neuropathy. Interval checks reinforce accountability and enable early detection of position drift.


ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Communication failures between anesthesia, surgery and nursing providers increase the likelihood of positioning errors and delayed recognition of nerve injury.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes, clarify leadership and support coordinated team response.
Establish clear leadership and roles during regional anesthesia procedures

Designate a team leader to coordinate care, assign roles and ensure accountability during block performance and positioning. Clear leadership reduces the risk of errors and supports a coordinated response to complications.
Communicate about positioning to the surgical team

Before and during a procedure, discuss anticipated positioning, draping interference or surgical traction that could endanger nerves. Proactive communication enables collaborative problem-solving and reduces the risk of positioning-related nerve injury. 
Implement standard handoff protocols at all care transitions
Use frameworks like SBAR during intra- and postoperative transitions. Structured handoffs reduce information loss and support continuity of care.
If nerve injury is suspected postoperatively, communicate with key players in a timely manner
Notify the care team, risk management and the patient. Prompt communication expedites intervention and increases patient trust. Document contemporaneously.
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Insufficient documentation of technique, monitoring and patient response may compromise continuity of care and weakens defense in the event of a claim.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support timely, complete documentation that reinforces continuity of care and clear clinical communication.
Document preoperative risk assessment and consent discussion

Record the rationale for technique selection, discussion of risks (including nerve injury) and patient consent. Documenting informed consent reinforces patient autonomy and provides evidence of appropriate risk communication. 
Record all procedural parameters

Note needle type, imaging guidance (if used), nerve stimulator settings (if used), injection pressure and patient response (including presence or absence of paresthesia or pain) following a block. Detailed documentation provides a contemporaneous record of technique and enables continuity of care.
Document patient positioning and interval checks

Note positioning details, padding used and any adjustments made during surgery. Documenting positioning and padding demonstrates attention to nerve protection. 
Record postoperative neurologic assessment

Document the presence or absence of residual block effects and any new neurologic findings immediately after surgery and before PACU discharge. Contemporaneous documentation of neurologic status aids early detection of injury and demonstrates attention to patient safety.
ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUE

Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure

Contributing action or omission: Lack of protocols, equipment and training undermines consistent care delivery and increases the risk of preventable injury.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve readiness, standardize response and support coordinated escalation.
Maintain readily available regional anesthesia equipment

Ensure that ultrasound machines with linear probes, blunt-tip needles and nerve stimulators are available when needed. Consider adding injection pressure monitoring devices to enhance safety during peripheral nerve blocks.
Consider implementing positioning checklists and adhere to time-out procedures

Verify and document padding and limb placement by anesthesia and nursing staff during surgery, at positioning and at regular intervals during prolonged procedures. Checklists and time-outs may reduce the risk of positioning errors and reinforce accountability. 
Consider implementing postoperative neurological assessment policies

When possible, perform and document neurologic assessments for all patients receiving peripheral nerve blocks. Standardized assessment improves early detection of injury and demonstrates a commitment to patient safety.
Provide continuing education and competency validation for anesthesia staff

Conduct simulation and workshop training on ultrasound-guided nerve blocks, injection pressure monitoring and positioning safety when feasible. Review ASRA and ASA practice advisories on peripheral nerve injury prevention. Training and competency validation promote consistent, evidence-based practice and reduce the risk of technique-related injury.
Conduct regular audits and quality improvement reviews

Engage in ongoing review of near-miss reports and incident debriefs to identify technique or process improvements. Include nerve injury prevention metrics focused on processes, techniques, outcomes and systems in quality improvement programs. Continuous quality improvement identifies system vulnerabilities and drives practice improvement.

Perioperative peripheral nerve injury (PNI) is a significant driver of malpractice claims and severe patient harm in anesthesiology.
Mitigating Your Risk
With meticulous technique, careful documentation and clear communication, anesthesiologists can mitigate the risks associated with perioperative peripheral nerve injury and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical an
PRESENTING_CONDITIONS: Peripheral Regional Anesthesia, General Anesthesia with Positioning
ADVERSE_OUTCOMES: Perioperative Peripheral Nerve Injury

TITLE: Respiratory failure
RISK_BRIEF:
Postoperative Respiratory Failure
SPECIALTY: Anesthesiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Perioperative Respiratory Distress

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Postoperative Respiratory Failure / Respiratory Arrest

Postoperative respiratory failure is a significant driver of malpractice risk and severe patient harm in anesthesiology.
Mitigating Your Risk 
With appropriate medication selection, vigilant monitoring and proper technique, anesthesiologists can mitigate the risks associated with postoperative respiratory failure and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for respiratory failure and provides strategies to help prevent them.
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving postoperative respiratory failure typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 77% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 23%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Postoperative Respiratory Failure Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Medication Error
Contributing action or omission: Errors in drug selection, dosing or administration can lead to respiratory depression or airway compromise.

Mitigation Strategies
The strategies below work together to enhance medication safety, enabling prompt intervention and improved patient outcomes.
Avoid or minimize sedative combinations (benzodiazepines + opioids) in high-risk patients
During preoperative planning and intraoperative management, identify high-risk patients for respiratory depression and limit concurrent use of respiratory depressants. High-risk patients include those with obstructive sleep apnea, morbid obesity and renal/hepatic dysfunction and the very young and very old. Combining opioids and sedatives significantly increases the risk of respiratory depression and avoiding those combinations reduces adverse events and demonstrates risk-aware prescribing. 
Ensure that antagonists are immediately available whenever opioids or benzodiazepines are administered
Before administering sedatives or opioids, verify the availability of antagonists such as naloxone and flumazenil in the procedure room and PACU. Immediate access to reversal agents enables rapid intervention for respiratory depression, reducing the severity of adverse outcomes and demonstrating preparedness. 
Use standardized syringe labeling and color-coding per international standards 
During preoperative preparation and intraoperative administration, label all syringes with drug name, concentration and date/time of preparation using color-coded labels consistent with ISO 26825 standards. Multimodal interventions, including improved labeling, reduce medication errors by 21%–37% per anesthetic. Clear labeling prevents drug swaps and dosing errors that can cause respiratory depression. 
Verify drug identity and dose at the point of administration using available safety systems
Where available, use automated dispensing cabinets and barcode verification to confirm drug identity and dose before administration. Automated systems reduce medication errors by up to 73% and provide an auditable record of drug administration. In settings without automated systems or EMR integration, implement manual verification practices within individual practitioner control:
Perform a two-person verbal verification of drug name, concentration and dose with a second qualified individual (CRNA, circulating nurse) before administering high-risk medications.
Maintain a written, time-stamped log of all medications prepared and administered, including drug name, concentration, dose, route and time.
Organize the anesthesia workspace using a consistent, standard layout so that medications are stored in predictable locations, reducing the risk of drug swaps.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention
Contributing action or omission: Inadequate postoperative monitoring can delay recognition of respiratory compromise.

Mitigation Strategies
The strategies below aim to improve postoperative monitoring, enabling early detection of respiratory compromise and reducing the risk of adverse outcomes.
Adhere to ASA Standards for Basic Anesthetic Monitoring during operative and postoperative periods
Maintain continuous pulse oximetry and capnography (for intubated or deeply sedated patients) and frequently assess respiratory rate and depth. Periodic assessment of airway patency, respiratory rate and oxygen saturation is associated with early detection of hypoxemia. 
Identify and monitor high-risk patients with increased intensity and duration
Screen for OSA using validated tools such as STOP-Bang, identifying patients with morbid obesity, renal insufficiency, COPD or high cumulative opioid doses. Plan for enhanced monitoring or ICU admission as needed. Patients with OSA have a 2.5-fold greater risk of postoperative pulmonary complications. Proactive identification and monitoring reduce adverse events.
Implement continuous pulse oximetry and consider capnography for all patients receiving parenteral opioids or sedatives
Use continuous monitoring postoperatively for patients at risk of respiratory depression. Capnography detects hypoventilation earlier than pulse oximetry alone. Together, they identify respiratory depression episodes in 46% of general care floor patients receiving opioids, often before clinical deterioration is apparent, reducing the risk of unrecognized respiratory failure. 
Use standard sedation scoring systems and reassess sedation and ventilation frequently
Perform assessments postoperatively using validated sedation scales such as Ramsay or RASS and document them regularly per institutional policy. Treat oversedation as indicative of OIVI until proven otherwise, regardless of respiratory rate or oxygen saturation.
CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL
Technique-Related Error
Contributing action or omission: Airway management errors, inadequate preoxygenation or failure to anticipate and prepare for difficult airway can lead to severe hypoxemia, cardiac arrest, anoxic brain injury or death.

Mitigation Strategies
The strategies below aim to improve airway management and reduce procedural errors through preparation, proper technique and adherence to evidence-based guidelines.
Perform a comprehensive airway risk assessment before every anesthetic
Evaluate demographic information, clinical conditions and prior anesthetic records. Also conduct a physical examination, including assessment of facial features, Mallampati score, neck circumference and neck mobility. Preoperative airway assessment identifies risks for difficult intubation, ventilation and aspiration, enabling preparation and reducing the likelihood of airway emergencies. 
Ensure availability of difficult airway equipment and formulate a documented airway management strategy
Verify that a difficult airway cart or portable storage unit is immediately available. A clear strategy and readily available equipment support rapid response to airway difficulty, reducing hypoxic injury risk and ensuring adherence to ASA Difficult Airway Guidelines. 
Use video laryngoscopy as the primary intubation technique when resources allow
Employ video laryngoscopy intraoperatively with appropriate blade type especially for anticipated difficult intubations. Video laryngoscopy offers higher first attempt and overall success rates with fewer complications compared to direct laryngoscopy. 
Limit total intubation attempts and declare failure early
Restrict the number of laryngoscopy attempts – generally no more than three optimized attempts before transitioning to alternative techniques or declaring failure – consistent with CAFG and DAS recommendations. Repeated attempts cause airway edema and bleeding, progressively worsening conditions. Limiting attempts preserves rescue options and prevents "cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate" (CICO) scenarios. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Insufficient or delayed documentation of patient assessments, interventions and communications can compromise continuity of care, clinical decision-making and legal defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies
The strategies below aim to improve documentation practices, creating timely, accurate and comprehensive records that support clinical decision-making and provide defensibility in adverse events.
Use structured templates aligned with safety bundles to standardize recordkeeping
Use templates that prompt documentation of key elements, including risk assessment, monitoring parameters, interventions and handoff communications. Templates reduce omissions, improve consistency and facilitate chart review in the event of litigation. 
Document the preoperative airway risk assessment and postoperative monitoring plan
Record the airway evaluation, OSA risk assessment and plan for postoperative monitoring and disposition in the anesthesia record. Clearly documenting these elements provides a reliable record in the event of an adverse outcome. 
Record type, dose, timing and route of all opioids, sedatives and reversal agents
Use time-based documentation, intra- and postoperatively, to capture all medications administered, including response where appropriate. Accurate, time-stamped records ensure continuity of care, support clinical decisions and provide an auditable trail for risk management and legal review. 
Document intraoperative respiratory events and airway maneuvers
Record any episodes of desaturation, airway obstruction, difficult intubation or need for airway adjuncts. Comprehensive documentation of intraoperative events informs postoperative planning. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Provider-to-provider communication failures, such as handoff breakdowns and unclear leadership, can result in overlooked vital details and delayed interventions.

Mitigation Strategies
The following strategies improve provider-to-provider communication, ensuring clear, structured care transitions that reduce errors and enhance patient safety.
Designate a team leader and clarify roles during respiratory emergencies
Assign a team leader at the start of every shift or during respiratory emergencies to coordinate care, assign roles and ensure accountability. Clear leadership reduces confusion and improves response times and team function. 
Implement handoff protocols for all care transitions
Use structured frameworks, such as SBAR, I-PASS or an institutional equivalent, to communicate patient-specific respiratory risks, monitoring instructions and pending interventions at every handoff. Standardized protocols reduce miscommunication, a leading cause of preventable incidents. 
Communicate patient risk factors and monitoring plan at every handoff
Verbally highlight OSA status, cumulative opioid dose, use of reversal agents and any intraoperative respiratory events during care transitions. Communicating these risk factors alerts receiving staff about the need for heightened vigilance. 
Emphasize closed-loop communication during routine care and simulations
Train healthcare teams to verbalize instructions and confirmations. Closed-loop communication reduces errors and is a recognized best practice that improves team performance and reliability. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEM
Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure
Contributing action or omission: Lack of protocols, inadequate equipment or failure to support consistent care delivery can delay detection of respiratory compromise and lead to adverse outcomes that could have been prevented.

Mitigation Strategies
The strategies below aim to strengthen processes at the institution or practice level by standardizing postoperative monitoring protocols, ensuring the availability of functioning equipment and supporting consistent care delivery to prevent respiratory compromise.
Implement OSA screening protocols for all preoperative patients
Use validated screening tools such as the STOP-Bang questionnaire with clear guidance for preoperative monitoring and disposition. Systematic screening identifies patients at higher risk for respiratory complications, improving the ability to anticipate problems and reducing liability exposure. 
Ensure that adequate postoperative monitoring equipment is available and functioning across all care areas
Maintain readily available and functioning pulse oximeters, capnography devices and alarm systems in PACU, step-down and inpatient units. Establish routine equipment checks and maintenance protocols to prevent equipment failures that can delay recognition of respiratory compromise. Implement policies requiring confirmation that monitoring equipment is connected, powered on and displaying accurate readings before care transfers are completed, with a defined escalation pathway when equipment is unavailable or malfunctioning. 
Establish and enforce postoperative monitoring policies
Require continuous monitoring for all patients receiving parenteral opioids or sedatives, with extended observation for those reversed with naloxone or flumazenil. Standard policies ensure consistent care delivery and reduce variability that can lead to adverse outcomes. 
Conduct routine audits and case reviews of adverse respiratory events
Monitor adherence to postoperative monitoring policies through audits and facilitate interdisciplinary reviews of adverse respiratory events to identify system issues and improvement opportunities. Continuous quality improvement uncovers system failures and fosters a culture of safety that reduces patient harm and malpractice risk. 
Support ongoing staff education on safe opioid use, recognition of respiratory depression and emergency airway management
Establish a structured education program for managing postoperative respiratory emergencies and ensure that staff members understand current ASA and APSF recommendations. Scale the format to available resources: from high-fidelity simulation and scheduled retraining intervals in well-resourced settings, to low-fidelity tabletop exercises, team-based walkthroughs of emergency airway algorithms or structured case discussions in settings with limited simulation access. Regular training, regardless of format, builds competency in recognizing and responding to respiratory compromise, reducing the likelihood of intervention delays and adverse outcomes.

Mitigating Your Risk 
With appropriate medication selection, vigilant monitoring and proper technique, anesthesiologists can mitigate the risks associated with postoperative respiratory failure and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrativ

TITLE: Acute Coronary Syndrome
RISK_BRIEF:
Acute Coronary Syndrome

SPECIALTY: Cardiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Chest Pain, Dyspnea, Suspected Ischemia

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Missed Myocardial Infarction, Delayed Reperfusion, Cardiovascular Death, Recurrent Ischemic Events

Acute coronary syndrome (ACS) is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in cardiology.   

Mitigating Your Risk 
 
With timely diagnostic testing, proactive risk stratification and coordinated communication, cardiologists can mitigate the risks associated with acute coronary syndrome and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for acute coronary syndrome and provides strategies to help prevent them.    

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving acute coronary syndrome stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 87% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 13%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.  

Actions and Omissions Driving Acute Coronary Syndrome Risk [insert chart] 
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Incomplete assessment of symptoms, risk factors or prior cardiac history increases the likelihood of missed or delayed ACS diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure comprehensive initial evaluation and appropriate risk stratification for patients with suspected ACS.

• Obtain a focused cardiac history immediately upon presentation

During the initial patient evaluation, assess chest pain characteristics, including nature, onset, duration, location, radiation, precipitating and relieving factors. Also evaluate associated symptoms, such as dyspnea, diaphoresis and nausea. Obtain prior cardiac history, including known coronary artery disease (CAD), cardiomyopathy, prior myocardial infarction (MI), percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) or coronary artery bypass graft (CABG). Assess cardiovascular risk factors, such as hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, smoking, family history and cocaine/methamphetamine use. Taking a patient’s history supports accurate risk stratification and shows adherence to evaluation protocols.

• Be alert to atypical presentations in high-risk populations

Suspect ACS in older adults, women and patients with diabetes, even in the absence of classic chest pain. In the SILVER-AMI study, 44% of patients aged 75 and older did not report chest pain as their primary symptom. Women are more likely to present with unusual symptoms, such as nausea, jaw, neck or back pain, fatigue and dyspnea. Patients with these presentations should be evaluated promptly, as missed diagnoses frequently result in diagnostic errors and adverse outcomes.

• Perform a targeted physical examination to identify high-risk features

Check for hemodynamic instability (hypotension, tachycardia), signs of heart failure (jugular venous distension, pulmonary rales, S3 gallop), new murmurs indicating mechanical complications and findings that point to alternative diagnoses (pericardial friction rub, unequal pulses suggesting aortic dissection). Physical examination findings guide risk stratification and help identify conditions requiring immediate intervention.

• Document clinical reasoning for risk assessment

Record symptoms, key characteristics, relevant history and physical exam findings that support the patient evaluation. Include the rationale for the assigned risk stratification category. This level of documentation shows systematic evaluation and supports defensibility.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain timely ECG or serial troponin measurements delays diagnosis and appropriate treatment escalation.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely diagnostic testing and proper interpretation of results.

• Obtain an ECG within 10 minutes of presentation

Follow the ACC/AHA Class 1 guidelines and perform a 12-lead ECG, interpreting it immediately in all patients with suspected ACS. Review the prehospital ECG for resolved ischemic changes in patients arriving by EMS. ECG delays contribute to slower reperfusion and adverse outcomes.

• Perform serial ECGs when initial ECG is nondiagnostic

Order serial ECGs every 15 to 30 minutes during the first 1 to 2 hours in patients with suspected ACS and a nondiagnostic initial ECG. Dynamic ischemic changes may not appear on initial evaluation; serial monitoring increases sensitivity for detecting evolving ischemia. Document the timing and findings of each ECG.

• Obtain posterior leads when posterior MI is suspected

Order posterior leads V7-V9 and evaluate for posterior STEMI when the initial ECG shows ST-segment depression in leads V1-V3 with ischemic symptoms. Posterior MI may occur without anterior ST-segment elevation and may be missed on a standard 12-lead ECG. Neglecting to order posterior leads when indicated leads to missed STEMI.

• Measure high-sensitivity troponin with appropriate serial sampling

Check cardiac troponin as soon as possible, preferably using a high-sensitivity assay. Repeat testing at 1 to 2 hours for high-sensitivity troponin or at 3 to 6 hours for conventional assays in patients with nondiagnostic initial troponin. A single, high-sensitivity troponin below the detection limit may exclude MI when symptoms begin at least 3 hours before arrival. Record symptom onset and troponin timing to ensure accurate interpretation.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act On Diagnostic Finding

Contributing action or omission: Failure to recognize STEMI, STEMI equivalents or significant troponin elevation delays reperfusion therapy and increases mortality.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve recognition of high-risk ECG patterns and accurate interpretation of cardiac biomarkers.

• Recognize STEMI equivalents requiring emergent angiography

Beyond classic STEMI criteria, identify ECG patterns associated with acute coronary occlusion:
Hyperacute T waves
de Winter's sign (upsloping ST-segment depression with tall T waves in the precordial leads)
Wellens' syndrome (biphasic or deeply inverted T waves in V2-V3)
Posterior MI pattern
ST-segment elevation in aVR with diffuse ST-segment depression.
Those patterns warrant emergent coronary angiography, as outcomes are similar to those in patients with more extensive ST-segment elevation. 

• Compare with prior ECGs when they are available

Review previous ECGs to distinguish new from chronic findings in patients with suspected ACS and ST-segment or T-wave changes. This step is especially important in patients with baseline abnormalities, such as left ventricular hypertrophy, left bundle branch block or paced rhythm. Document the comparison and whether changes are new or unchanged.

• Obtain emergent expert consultation for equivocal ECGs

Request immediate review from an expert when ECG findings raise concern for ACS but do not meet clear diagnostic criteria. Consider emergent echocardiography to identify new wall motion abnormalities in patients with ongoing symptoms and equivocal ECG findings. Missed coronary occlusion increases the risk of death and other preventable harm.

• Interpret troponin in clinical context using validated pathways

Use validated clinical decision pathways, such as HEART or EDACS, to integrate troponin results with clinical presentation and ECG findings. Recognize that elevated troponin requires differentiation between Type 1 MI (plaque rupture), Type 2 MI (supply-demand mismatch) and acute or chronic myocardial injury. Document clinical reasoning for troponin interpretation and management decisions.

• Maintain a high index of suspicion in patients with known CAD

Recognize that clinical decision pathways may be less reliable in patients with established coronary artery disease (CAD). These patients require a more cautious approach, including lower thresholds for admission and further evaluation.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Handoff failures, unclear escalation pathways and inadequate communication between emergency physicians, cardiologists and interventionalists increase the risk of delayed treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and enable coordinated team responses.

• Implement handoff protocols for ACS patients

Use tools such as SBAR or I-PASS when transferring care between providers, units or facilities. Include current clinical status, ECG findings, troponin values and trends, medications administered, pending results and monitoring parameters. Handoff failures contribute to two of every three sentinel events reported to the Joint Commission. 

• Establish escalation triggers and notifications

Define protocols for activating the cardiac catheterization laboratory, notifying interventional cardiology or escalating care based on STEMI on ECG, hemodynamic instability, refractory symptoms or significant troponin elevation. Document notification and response times.  Escalation pathways reduce delays in definitive treatment.

• Use closed-loop communication during time-critical interventions

During STEMI activations and other emergent situations, give verbal orders and confirmations. Document catheterization lab activation time, door-to-balloon time target and explain any delays.

• Coordinate discharge communication with outpatient providers

Before discharge, share the diagnosis, interventions, medication changes and follow-up requirements with the patient's primary care physician and outpatient cardiologist. Complete and transmit discharge summaries promptly. Inadequate information transfer is linked to adverse outcomes and readmissions. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider	

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate patient education about warning symptoms, the importance of medication and follow-up requirements increases the risk of delayed presentation and poor adherence.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient understanding and engagement in their care.

• Provide patients instructions about warning symptoms

At patient discharge, explain symptoms requiring immediate medical attention, including recurrent chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, syncope or symptoms similar to the initial presentation. Give written instructions in the patient's primary language and at an appropriate literacy level. Document patient education and confirm their understanding.
• Explain the importance of medication adherence

Discuss the purpose of each discharge medication, particularly dual antiplatelet therapy and the risks of discontinuation without medical guidance. Emphasize that premature discontinuation of P2Y12 inhibitors significantly increases the risk of stent thrombosis and recurrent MI. Address potential barriers to adherence, including costs, side effects and regimen complexity.

• Confirm follow-up appointments before discharge

Schedule cardiology follow-up within 1 to 2 weeks of discharge and confirm with the patient. Provide contact information for questions or concerns. Patients who do not receive timely follow-up face a higher risk of adverse outcomes and readmission.

ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of clinical findings, decision-making rationale and time-critical interventions compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document ECG timing and interpretation

Record ECG time, interpretation and resulting actions. In patients with STEMI, document the time of first medical contact, ECG diagnostic, catheterization lab activation and door-to-balloon. This documentation supports adherence to time-based quality metrics and defensibility.

Sample documentation language: "12-lead ECG obtained at 14:32, interpreted at 14:35. ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, aVF with reciprocal depression in I, aVL consistent with inferior STEMI. Catheterization laboratory activated at 14:37. Interventional cardiology notified."

• Document troponin values with timing and clinical interpretation

Record troponin values, time of collection and clinical interpretation. Include management rationale based on troponin results, such as Type 1 MI, Type 2 MI or myocardial injury. Documentation demonstrates systematic evaluation and sound clinical reasoning.

• Document time-critical metrics for STEMI care

Record the time of first medical contact, symptom onset, ECG diagnostic, catheterization lab activation, arterial access and reperfusion in STEMI patients. Document delays and reasons for them, including patient factors, system factors or clinical complexity. Recording this information supports quality improvement and time-sensitive care.

• Document clinical reasoning for disposition decisions

When discharging patients from the emergency department after ACS evaluation, record low-risk findings supporting the decision, including a normal or unchanged ECG, troponin values and timing, risk score calculation and the absence of high-risk features. Include patient education and follow-up. Capturing these details supports adherence to clinical decision pathways and defensibility in case of an adverse event.

ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify and address barriers to medication adherence, lifestyle modification and follow-up increases the risk of recurrent ischemic events.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and address barriers to adherence.

• Assess barriers to medication adherence before discharge

Identify potential hurdles, including cost, insurance coverage, polypharmacy, cognitive impairment and lack of social support. Involve pharmacy services for medication reconciliation and assistance programs when needed. Address side effects proactively.

• Provide written medication lists and instructions

Ensure that patients receive an accurate medication list with instructions for each therapy. Use teach-back methods to confirm understanding. Medication errors during care transitions are a leading cause of post-discharge adverse events.

• Refer to cardiac rehabilitation

After ACS, refer patients to cardiac rehabilitation, which reduces recurrent events and deaths. Address participation barriers, including transportation, work obligations and insurance coverage. Document the referral and the patient’s response.

• Document patient non-adherence and continued outreach

Record missed follow-ups and declined testing, including outreach attempts and reasons for non-adherence. Continue engagement by phone, patient portal or mail. When patients decline recommended care, note the discussion, recommendations and risks that were reviewed.

Mitigating Your Risk 
 
With timely diagnostic testing, proactive risk stratification and coordinated communication, cardiologists can mitigate the risks associated with acute coronary syndrome and reduce malpractice exposure. This report ex

TITLE: Anticoagulation
RISK_BRIEF:
Anticoagulation Management

SPECIALTY: Cardiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Atrial Fibrillation, Venous Thromboembolism, Mechanical Heart Valves, Acute Coronary Syndrome

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Stroke, Systemic Embolism, Major Bleeding, Intracranial Hemorrhage, Death

Anticoagulation management is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in cardiology.
Mitigating Your Risk
With proactive risk identification, evidence-based dosing and anticoagulation protocols, cardiologists can mitigate the risks associated with anticoagulation management and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for anticoagulation management and provides strategies to help prevent them.  
  
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving anticoagulation management stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 78% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 22%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.   

Actions and Omissions Driving Anticoagulation Risk [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act On Diagnostic Finding

Contributing action or omission: Failure to recognize supratherapeutic or subtherapeutic anticoagulation levels, missed drug interactions or renal dysfunction increases the risk of bleeding or thromboembolic complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure accurate assessment of anticoagulation status and timely recognition of factors that alter drug effect.

• Verify renal function before initiating and during DOAC therapy

Evaluate creatinine clearance before starting direct oral anticoagulant therapy (DOAC) and reassess annually or more frequently in patients with borderline renal function or acute illness. DOAC dosing is critically dependent on renal function. Dabigatran requires dose reduction at CrCl 30-50 mL/min and is contraindicated below 30 mL/min; apixaban and rivaroxaban require dose adjustments at specific thresholds. Failure to adjust dosing for renal impairment is a common source of overdosing and underdosing errors. 

• Maintain therapeutic INR monitoring for warfarin patients

Order INR at least weekly during initiation until a patient is stable, then every 1-4 weeks during maintenance. Target an INR of 2.0-3.0 for most indications; INR above 4.0 provides no additional benefit and substantially increases bleeding risk. Time in the therapeutic range is associated with lower rates of thromboembolic events and major bleeding; aim for TTR of 70% or more. 

• Recognize signs of occult bleeding or supratherapeutic anticoagulation

Monitor for unexplained anemia, hemodynamic instability or subtle neurologic changes suggestive of bleeding. In patients receiving DOACs, standard coagulation tests (PT, aPTT) may not reflect anticoagulant effect; consider drug-specific assays when clinically indicated. Early recognition of bleeding allows timely intervention before clinical deterioration. 

CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Medication Error

Contributing action or omission: Incorrect anticoagulant selection, dosing errors or failure to adjust therapy for patient-specific factors increase the risk of stroke, systemic embolism and major bleeding.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate anticoagulant selection and evidence-based dosing.

• Screen for clinically significant drug interactions at each encounter

Review medication lists for CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein inhibitors (ketoconazole, ritonavir, dronedarone) and inducers (rifampin, phenytoin, carbamazepine, St. John's wort) that significantly alter direct oral anticoagulant levels. Concomitant use of antiplatelet agents, NSAIDs or selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors with warfarin significantly increases bleeding risk. Document interaction assessments and dose modifications. 
• Use evidence-based dosing criteria for DOACs

Apply FDA-approved dosing guidelines based on age, weight, renal function and concomitant medications. For apixaban in atrial fibrillation, reduce to 2.5 mg twice daily when at least two of the following are present: age 80 or older, weight 60 kg or less and/or creatinine of 1.5 mg/dL or greater. Avoid non-evidence-based DOAC dosing, as overdosing and underdosing are associated with significantly worse outcomes. 

• Select DOACs over warfarin for eligible patients with nonvalvular AF

Reserve warfarin for patients with mechanical valves, moderate-to-severe rheumatic mitral stenosis or those receiving strong CYP3A4 inducers that would cause unreliable DOAC levels. DOACs are preferred over warfarin in patients without mechanical heart valves or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis to reduce mortality, stroke, systemic embolism and intracranial hemorrhage risk. 

Minimize duration of triple antithrombotic therapy after PCI 
Prescribe triple therapy with an OAC, P2Y12 inhibitor and low-dose aspirin only when necessary and for the shortest duration to mitigate bleeding risk—typically 1 to 4 weeks, but no more than 30 days in patients at high ischemic and low bleeding risk undergoing PCI who need chronic oral anticoagulation. The preferred strategy is early transition to dual antithrombotic therapy with a DOAC and clopidogrel (the preferred P2Y12 inhibitor in this setting). DOACs are preferred over warfarin unless the patient has a mechanical heart valve or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis. Dual therapy reduces bleeding significantly but is associated with a small increase in stent thrombosis risk; however, the net clinical benefit favors dual therapy for most patients. Individualize therapy duration based on ischemic versus bleeding risk, documenting indication, clinical rationale and planned duration. Adhering to guideline-directed therapy and communicating the antithrombotic plan across providers are essential to reducing adverse events and supporting defensible care.
• Implement anticoagulation protocols and order sets

Adopt institution-specific, guideline-aligned protocols when starting anticoagulants, adjusting doses or monitoring therapy. Use pre-built EHR order sets that incorporate renal function-based dosing, drug interaction alerts and monitoring parameters. Adhering to protocols reduces prescribing variability and supports consistent, defensible care. 

• Engage clinical pharmacy services in anticoagulation management

Use clinical pharmacists, particularly in complex cases or when multiple interacting medications are present. Involving pharmacists in multidisciplinary rounding increases recognition of DOAC medication errors and reduces adverse drug events. Consider referring patients requiring intensive monitoring to dedicated anticoagulation clinics. 

• Avoid bridging anticoagulation in most patients with AF undergoing elective procedures

Temporarily stop warfarin or DOACs without bridging in the majority of patients with AF undergoing invasive procedures. The BRIDGE trial showed that no bridging is superior to bridging for major bleeding and noninferior for thromboembolism. Reserve bridging after individual assessment for patients with recent stroke/TIA, mechanical valves or very high thromboembolic risk. 

• Time DOAC interruption based on agent, renal function and procedural bleeding risk

Hold DOACs for 24-48 hours (2-3 half-lives) for low bleeding risk procedures and 48-96 hours (4-5 half-lives) for high bleeding risk procedures. Extend interruption intervals in patients with renal impairment, particularly for dabigatran. Resume DOACs on postoperative day 1 after low bleeding risk procedures and on days 2-3 after high bleeding risk procedures once hemostasis occurs. 

• Establish protocols for anticoagulation reversal in bleeding emergencies

Follow institutional protocols that specify reversal agents by anticoagulant class, including 4-factor PCC plus IV vitamin K for warfarin, idarucizumab for dabigatran and 4-factor PCC for factor Xa inhibitors. Ensure that reversal agents are available and that staff is trained to administer them. Failing to achieve effective hemostasis is associated with more than 3-fold higher mortality. 

• Continue anticoagulation for device procedures when thromboembolic risk is high

Continue warfarin rather than interrupting with bridging in patients with AF and an annual stroke risk of 5% or more when performing pacemaker or defibrillator placement to reduce pocket hematoma risk. For patients receiving DOACs, uninterrupted or briefly interrupted therapy is reasonable. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate handoffs, unclear communication of anticoagulation status during care transitions or failure to coordinate periprocedural management increase the risk of medication errors and adverse events.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication and ensure continuity of anticoagulation management across care settings.

• Implement handoff protocols for anticoagulated patients

Use SBAR and other tools to share current anticoagulant, dose, indication, latest INR or renal function and planned interruptions or adjustments. Document transfer details in the medical record. Clear handoff communication is critical given the narrow therapeutic window for anticoagulated patients. 

• Coordinate periprocedural management with all involved providers

Communicate the time that anticoagulation was interrupted, bridging needs and resumption plans among the prescribing physician, proceduralist and anesthesia team. Document the plan in the medical record and confirm patient understanding. Include proceduralists in resumption decisions. 

• Establish escalation pathways for anticoagulation-related concerns

Create rapid response protocols for supratherapeutic INR, suspected bleeding and urgent procedural needs. Ensure that all team members have access to anticoagulation expertise and reversal agents. Use closed-loop communication to confirm receipt and execution of critical information. 
• Communicate anticoagulation status at hospital discharge

Document the anticoagulant, dose, indication, treatment duration and follow-up monitoring in the discharge summary. Communicate directly with the outpatient provider handling anticoagulation management. Before discharge, verify that patients have access to medications and understand follow-up. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION 
Communication Failure Between Provider and Patient

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate patient education on anticoagulation therapy, warning signs or adherence requirements increases the risk of medication errors and delays  recognition of complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients understand their therapy and can participate in safe anticoagulation management.

• Provide anticoagulation education at initiation and reinforcement at follow-up visits

Explain the indication for anticoagulation, expected treatment duration, signs and symptoms of bleeding and thrombosis and when to seek emergency care, including after head trauma or severe bleeding. Document that education was provided and patient understanding was confirmed. 

• Educate patients on drug-specific administration requirements 
Emphasize that incorrect administration can result in subtherapeutic drug levels or treatment failure.
Rivaroxaban 15-20 mg: Take with food
Dabigatran: Keep capsules in original packaging; do not crush or open capsules
Apixaban VTE treatment: Reduce from 10 mg twice daily to 5 mg twice daily after one week
• Instruct patients on drug and food interactions that affect anticoagulation
Advise patients to avoid NSAIDs and notify their anticoagulation provider before starting new medications, including over-the-counter products and supplements. For warfarin patients, emphasize maintaining consistent (not restricted) vitamin K intake. Provide written materials in the patient's primary language and at an appropriate literacy level. 
• Provide guidance for missed doses 
Explain to patients what to do if a dose is missed and emphasize that they should not double the next dose. Provide contact information for anticoagulation questions. Document delivery of instructions and patient understanding.
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of anticoagulation prescribing and dosing rationale, as well as monitoring and patient education compromises care continuity and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces safe anticoagulation management.

• Document the indication, target and planned duration of anticoagulation

Record the anticoagulation indication (such as AF with CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of X, acute DVT, mechanical mitral valve), target INR or DOAC dose rationale and planned duration. Proper documentation enables continuity across providers and demonstrates adherence to evidence-based indications. 

Sample documentation language: "Apixaban 5 mg BID initiated for stroke prophylaxis in nonvalvular AF. CHA₂DS₂-VASc score 4 (HTN, DM, age 72, female). No dose reduction criteria met (age 80, weight 78 kg, Cr 0.9). Indefinite duration planned. HAS-BLED score 2."

• Document renal function assessment and dose adjustment

Record creatinine clearance at initiation and reassessment. Document the selected dose and the rationale, including justification for using standard dosing despite borderline renal function. Documentation supports monitoring and dosing decisions. 

• Document drug interaction screening and management

Record the review of medication lists, interactions and management decisions, including dose adjustment, alternative agent selection and enhanced monitoring. Consistent documentation demonstrates systematic attention to common anticoagulation errors. 

• Document patient education and understanding

Record anticoagulation education, confirmation of patient understanding of bleeding signs, medication adherence, drug interactions and emergency instructions. Document barriers, such as patient refusal or limited comprehension, and interventions to address them, including teach-back methods, visual aids, interpreter services and family involvement. 

Sample documentation language: “Anticoagulation education provided, including signs of bleeding, importance of adherence, avoidance of NSAIDs and when to seek emergency care. Patient demonstrated limited understanding and could not recall key safety information. Daughter present and involved in education. Daughter verbalized understanding of bleeding warning signs, need to take apixaban twice daily with dose reduction after one week and when to seek emergency care. Pill organizer provided. Daughter will supervise medication administration and monitor for complications."

TITLE: Aortic Dissection
RISK_BRIEF:
Aortic Dissection


SPECIALTY: Cardiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Chest Pain, Back Pain, Syncope, Acute Cardiovascular Emergency

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Aortic Rupture, Stroke, End-Organ Malperfusion, Death

Aortic dissection is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in cardiology.  

Mitigating Your Risk

With timely identification of acute aortic syndrome, rapid imaging and vigilant monitoring during the acute phase, cardiologists can mitigate the risks associated with aortic dissection and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for aortic dissection and provides strategies to help prevent them.    

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving aortic dissection stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 89% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 11%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.   
Actions and Omissions Driving Aortic Dissection Risk [insert chart] 
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain appropriate imaging in patients with clinical features suggestive of aortic dissection delays diagnosis and increases the risk of mortality.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely identification of acute aortic syndrome and appropriate escalation to definitive imaging.

• Systematically apply the Aortic Dissection Detection Risk Score (ADD-RS)

When patients present with back or abdominal pain, syncope or malperfusion, calculate the ADD-RS using three categories:
Predisposing conditions, including Marfan syndrome, family history of aortic disease, known aortic valve disease, recent aortic manipulation and known thoracic aortic aneurysm
Pain features that are abrupt onset with severe intensity or a ripping/tearing quality
Exam findings, such as pulse deficit, systolic blood pressure differential, focal neurologic deficit, new aortic regurgitation murmur and hypotension/shock 
Assign 1 point for each category with at least one risk marker. Applying this score systematically increases detection of acute aortic syndrome and demonstrates adherence to guideline-recommended risk stratification. 
• Obtain CT angiography promptly in patients with ADD-RS >1

In patients with a high probability of acute aortic dissection (ADD-RS score >1), perform CT angiography (CTA) of the chest, abdomen and pelvis immediately. Do not wait for other test results. CTA is highly sensitive and specific for diagnosis, providing key surgical planning information, including the extent of dissection, branch vessel involvement and the presence of malperfusion. Delays in definitive imaging in high-risk patients are a common failure point.

• Use D-dimer testing judiciously in low-risk patients

In patients with ADD-RS ≤1 (low- to intermediate-risk), order a highly sensitive D-dimer <500 ng/mL to help exclude acute aortic dissection without advanced imaging. Combining ADD-RS ≤1 with a negative D-dimer has a failure rate of 0.3%. However, do not use D-dimer to rule out dissection in high-risk patients (ADD-RS >1), as it lacks sufficient sensitivity. 

• Consider bedside echocardiography as an adjunct in unstable patients

In hemodynamically unstable patients who cannot be safely transported to CT, use transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) to identify direct signs of dissection (intimal flap, aortic wall thickening) and indirect signs (pericardial effusion, aortic regurgitation and aortic dilatation). In patients with high clinical suspicion and positive TTE findings, proceed directly to surgical consultation. Perform TEE in the operating room or ICU when CT is not feasible for unstable patients. 

CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act On Diagnostic Finding
Contributing action or omission: Failing to recognize atypical presentations or symptoms and misinterpreting imaging increase the risk of delayed or missed diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve recognition of aortic dissection in its varied presentations and reduce diagnostic anchoring on alternative diagnoses.

• Maintain a high index of suspicion for atypical presentations

Because aortic dissection does not always present with classic tearing pain, remain alert to unusual symptoms during diagnosis. IRAD data show that 6.4% of patients had painless dissection, presenting with syncope (33.9%), congestive heart failure (19.7%) or stroke (11.3%). Patients with isolated abdominal pain, neurologic deficits or limb ischemia may have an underlying dissection. Always consider aortic dissection in the differential for patients with unexplained hypotension, pulse deficits or new aortic regurgitation murmur. 
• Avoid using chest radiographs to rule out aortic dissection
Proceed directly to CT angiography, MRI or transesophageal echocardiography when aortic dissection is suspected. Chest x-rays lack sensitivity and specificity to reliably rule out aortic dissection, with up to 15% of confirmed cases showing normal films. Mediastinal widening appears in only 63%–64% of type A dissections and has poor specificity: nearly half (48.6%) of patients with confirmed dissection who had no high-risk clinical features still showed mediastinal widening. 
• Proceed to definitive imaging if clinical suspicion for dissection persists, regardless of troponin results

Recognize that the combination of normal ECG, normal troponins and elevated D-dimer in a patient with chest pain should raise suspicion for acute aortic syndrome rather than acute coronary syndrome. Troponin elevation does not rule out aortic dissection, because it may result from coronary artery involvement by the dissection flap or hypotension-induced ischemia. If clinical suspicion for dissection persists, proceed to definitive imaging regardless of troponin results. 
Prioritize ECG-gated CT techniques when possible
Recognize that non-gated scans frequently introduce motion artifacts, which can lead to false-positive or false-negative errors. Utilize ECG-triggered CTA whenever available to achieve superior image clarity and maximize diagnostic accuracy.
• Consider transfer of patients to a high-volume aortic center
For patients with suspected or confirmed type A aortic dissection who are hemodynamically stable, initiate transfer when feasible. High-volume centers have multidisciplinary aorta teams with expertise in cardiac surgery, vascular surgery, advanced imaging and interventional radiology, enabling rapid diagnosis and coordinated treatment. Interfacility transfer does not increase operative mortality. 
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate post-operative or post-discharge surveillance increases the risk of missed complications and aneurysms and emergent reintervention.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate monitoring during the acute phase of aortic dissection and long-term surveillance to detect complications. 

• Monitor for signs of malperfusion and hemodynamic instability

Continuously evaluate patients for end-organ malperfusion (altered mental status, oliguria, abdominal pain and limb ischemia) and hemodynamic deterioration during the acute phase. Patients with type A dissection remain at risk for rupture, tamponade and sudden death even when initially stable. Mortality in the first 48 hours is approximately 0.5% per hour for patients who have not undergone surgery. 
• Follow recommended surveillance imaging protocols

Perform CT or MRI surveillance imaging at 1 month, 6 months and 12 months post-event, then annually if aortic findings remain stable (whether treated surgically, endovascularly or medically). About 50% of patients are lost to follow-up by 28 months, and 38% of medically treated type B dissection patients require later intervention. Systematic surveillance detects threatening enlargement and identifies the need for reintervention. 
• Establish follow-up at a high-volume aortic center for long-term surveillance
Consider referral for patients initially treated at low-volume facilities. High volume centers provide structured surveillance through dedicated aortic care clinics with multidisciplinary expertise. They have demonstrated significantly better long-term outcomes, likely reflecting not only superior acute surgical care but also more systematic long-term monitoring and timely reintervention when needed.

ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of clinical reasoning, risk stratification and time-critical decision-making compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that adheres to the standard of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document ADD-RS calculation and clinical reasoning

Detail the risk factors across all three ADD-RS categories (predisposing conditions, pain features and exam findings) and the final score. Document the reasoning for the diagnostic pathway selected based on those assessed risk levels. This process ensures a systematic, defensible assessment and further evaluation and treatment. 

Sample documentation language: "ADD-RS calculated: Predisposing conditions (0)—no known connective tissue disease, aortic valve disease or family history of aortic disease. Pain features (1)—abrupt onset of severe chest pain. Examination findings (0)—no pulse deficit, BP symmetric, no neurologic deficit, no new murmur. ADD-RS = 1 (intermediate risk). Given intermediate risk, obtained D-dimer which was elevated at 1,200 ng/mL. Proceeding to CTA chest/abdomen/pelvis."

• Record the timing of key clinical events

Document the time of symptom onset, presentation, first suspicion of dissection, image ordering and completion, surgical consultation and start of treatment. Documenting these intervals is critical to demonstrating necessary urgency with a time-dependent, high-mortality condition like aortic dissection. 

• Document communication with consultants and transfer decisions

Log all interactions with cardiovascular surgery, including time of contact, clinical details, recommendations and disposition decisions. When considering transfer to a higher-level center, document the rationale for transfer or for local management. 

• Note patient education and follow-up plans at discharge

After aortic dissection (post-operative or medically managed), document education on warning signs needing immediate evaluation, blood pressure goals, medications and a follow-up plan with dates for surveillance imaging and appointments.
