TITLE: Cardiac Catheterization
RISK_BRIEF:
Cardiac Catheterization 

SPECIALTY: Cardiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Coronary Artery Disease, Acute Coronary Syndromes, Valvular Heart Disease, Cardiomyopathy

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Vascular Complications, Bleeding, Contrast-Induced Acute Kidney Injury, Stroke, Myocardial Infarction, Death
Cardiac catheterization is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in cardiology. 
Mitigating Your Risk   
   
With upfront recognition of patient risk, proactive complication identification and coordinated team action, cardiologists can mitigate the risks associated with cardiac catheterization and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for cardiac catheterization and provides strategies to help prevent them.  
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns 
Malpractice claims involving cardiac catheterization stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 84% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 16%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.    
Actions and Omissions Driving Cardiac Catheterization Risk [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.

CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain appropriate preprocedural testing delays risk assessment and prevents implementation of preventive strategies.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure comprehensive preprocedural evaluation and identification of modifiable risk factors.

• Perform preprocedural risk assessment using validated calculators

Use risk calculators such as the Society for Cardiovascular Angiography & Interventions percutaneous coronary intervention (SCAI PCI) Risk Calculator or the Mehran contrast-induced acute kidney injury (CI-AKI) score to estimate mortality, acute kidney injury and transfusion risks before procedures. Document risk scores and discuss risks with patients during informed consent. Calculators help identify high-risk patients who may benefit from additional preventive measures and support shared decision-making, aligning with American College of Cardiology (ACC)/American Heart Association (AHA)/SCAI recommendations.
• Obtain baseline laboratory evaluation before all catheterization procedures

Order a complete blood count and metabolic profile, including serum creatinine and estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR). These baseline tests help identify patients at higher risk for contrast-induced acute kidney injury and bleeding complications, enabling preventive measures and adherence to best practices. Ensure that outpatient results are available within 30 days and before the procedure day to allow rescheduling if abnormalities are found. Check eGFR and prothrombin time/international normalized ratio (PT/INR) on the procedure day in patients with baseline renal insufficiency or who take warfarin regularly. 

• Assess peripheral vascular status before determining the access site

During the preprocedural exam, document peripheral pulses and check for peripheral arterial disease (PAD), which is an independent predictor of vascular access complications (OR 2.42). Vascular assessment guides access site selection and identifies patients who may benefit from ultrasound-guided access or alternative access approaches.

• Offer pregnancy testing to women of childbearing age

Provide serum or urine pregnancy testing (beta-hCG) to women of childbearing age before the procedure. Pregnancy testing prevents fetal radiation exposure and is required per SCAI best practices.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failure to apply evidence-based strategies for vascular access, contrast minimization and nephroprotection increases the risk of preventable complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce vascular complications, bleeding and contrast-induced acute kidney injury.

• Discontinue nephrotoxic medications and correct modifiable risk factors

Hold ACE inhibitors, ARBs and NSAIDs before procedures in patients at elevated CI-AKI risk. Correct periprocedural hypovolemia, hypotension, hyperglycemia and anemia. Pretreat with high-intensity statins, which reduce CI-AKI through pleiotropic anti-inflammatory effects. 
• Administer preprocedural hydration for patients at elevated CI-AKI risk

Give intravenous isotonic saline (1–1.5 mL/kg/hour) for 3–12 hours before and 12–24 hours after the procedure in patients with eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73 m², unless heart failure or volume overload are present. Consider LVEDP-guided hydration in high-risk patients. Hydration and contrast minimization remain the primary evidence-based strategies for CI-AKI prevention. 
• Choose radial artery access as the default approach for coronary procedures

Use radial access instead of femoral access in patients undergoing PCI for acute coronary syndromes to reduce bleeding, vascular complications and death (Class I, Level A recommendation). Radial access is associated with a 24% relative risk reduction in all-cause death and a 51% reduction in major bleeding compared with femoral access. In stable ischemic heart disease, radial access lowers access-site bleeding and vascular complications.
Reserve femoral access for anatomic or clinical limitations or planned mechanical circulatory support. 

• Implement contrast minimization strategies for all patients

Track the total contrast volume during the procedure and notify the operator when the contrast volume/creatinine clearance ratio exceeds 3.0; a ratio below 2.0 is preferred. Reduce contrast by:
Limiting injections
Using biplane imaging
Replacing left ventriculography with echocardiography
Using intravascular imaging (IVUS)
Staging complex PCI procedures. 
CI-AKI risk increases by 12% for every 100 mL of contrast administered. Contrast minimization remains the most effective prevention strategy.


CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Technical errors during vascular access, catheter manipulation or hemostasis increase the risk of vascular injury, bleeding and other procedural complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize procedural technique and reduce the likelihood of technical complications.

• Use fluoroscopy/ultrasound guidance and micropuncture needle for femoral artery access

When femoral access is required, use fluoroscopic and ultrasound guidance to optimize the puncture site and reduce vascular complications, particularly in patients with obesity, prior groin surgery or peripheral arterial disease. Ultrasound guidance improves first-pass success rates and reduces the risk of high or low punctures that lead to retroperitoneal hemorrhage or pseudoaneurysm formation. Using the micropuncture technique (21 gauge needle, 0.018” wire) allows confirmation of access site/level and is potentially associated with lower bleeding/hematoma risk and reduced access-site complications, particularly in higher-risk patients. 

• Select appropriate sheath and catheter sizes

Choose the smallest sheath size needed for the procedure, as larger sizes increase vascular complications (OR 1.29 per French size increase). 5F or 6F systems usually work for diagnostic catheterization. Minimize sheath dwell time and consider early removal when anticoagulation permits.

• Ensure proper hemostasis technique and monitoring

When using manual compression or vascular closure devices (VCDs), achieve adequate hemostasis before mobilizing the patient. Failed VCD deployment is the strongest independent predictor of vascular complications (OR 7.38). Record the hemostasis method and result in the procedure report. For radial access, use patent hemostasis techniques to reduce radial artery occlusion.

• Recognize and manage procedural complications promptly

Prepare trainees and operators to use equipment and handle procedural emergencies, such as using covered stents for coronary perforation and snares for device retrieval, inserting hemodynamic support devices and performing pericardiocentesis. Recognizing and treating complications early reduces their impact and demonstrates competency in emergency management. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate handoff communications, unclear escalation pathways and insufficient coordination between procedural and post-procedural care teams can delay recognition and treatment of complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and enable coordinated team responses.

• Implement handoff protocols for post-procedure care transitions

Use handoff tools such as SBAR when transferring patients from the catheterization lab to recovery or inpatient units. Share information about the access site and hemostasis method, anticoagulation status, contrast volume, procedural findings, complications and specific monitoring parameters. Document handoff details in the medical record. Structured handoffs prevent missed information during care transitions and ensure continuous monitoring.

• Establish escalation triggers for post-procedure complications

Set criteria for escalation, such as access site bleeding, hemodynamic instability, chest pain or neurologic changes after procedures. Train recovery unit staff to spot vascular complications, such as retroperitoneal hemorrhage, which can present as back pain, hypotension or unexplained hemoglobin drop. Assign responsibility for notifications and responses.
 • Communicate procedural results to patients, families and referring providers

Before discharge, share procedural results and any complications with patients and, if appropriate, their families. Document discussions with the patient, receiving team and referring provider. Communicating results supports patient understanding and ensures smooth transitions.

• Contact patients within 24-72 hours after discharge

Ensure that a catheterization laboratory team member initiates follow-up with patients, prioritizing those discharged the same day after PCI. They should check for complications, reinforce medication adherence and address patient questions. Prompt follow-up helps identify complications and demonstrates engagement in patient care.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate informed consent discussions, failure to address patient-specific risks and insufficient discharge education increase the risk of patient harm and malpractice exposure.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients are informed participants in their care and understand post-procedure expectations.

• Conduct informed consent discussions in a neutral environment before the procedure day

Use shared decision-making to explain the procedure, risks, benefits and alternatives. Ensure that the explanation is in the patient’s native language and at an appropriate literacy level. Engage qualified medical interpreters in person, by phone or by video when the discussion is not in the patient's primary language.

• Discuss procedure- and patient-specific risks

Structure the consent discussion to systematically cover all potential complications. Outline common procedural risks, such as bleeding and access-site issues, and detail severe events, including death, stroke, myocardial infarction, transfusion or vascular injury. In addition, explain patient-specific vulnerabilities identified during their clinical assessment. For diagnostic catheterization, explain the chance of ad hoc PCI and its risks, including dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) requirements and restenosis. 

• Address do-not-resuscitate (DNR) status before the procedure

Discuss with the patient or healthcare proxy whether to temporarily suspend a DNR for the procedure, ensuring that they understand the implications of both options. Document the decision, including the terms and duration if suspension is agreed upon. Clear documentation helps avoid confusion during procedural emergencies.

• Provide comprehensive discharge instructions

Give written guidance on access site care, activity limitations, driving restrictions, medication adherence (especially DAPT duration), warning signs requiring immediate evaluation and contact details for questions or complications. Schedule serum creatinine testing within 3-5 days for patients at higher risk of CI-AKI. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUE

Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure

Contributing action or omission: Lack of standardized protocols, inadequate preprocedural checklists and absence of quality monitoring undermine consistent care delivery and complication prevention.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve readiness, standardize processes and support quality improvement.

• Develop EHR order sets for catheterization procedures

Use pre-built, evidence-based order sets for preprocedural patient evaluation, intraprocedural medications, post-procedure monitoring and discharge instructions. Include automated alerts for risks like abnormal renal function that require protocol changes. 

• Establish institutional protocols for CI-AKI prevention

Use evidence-based steps for hydration, contrast monitoring and nephrotoxic medication management to avoid CI-AKI. Define a maximum acceptable contrast dose or contrast volume/creatinine clearance ratio during timeout and alert the operator when thresholds are approached or exceeded. Standardized protocols ensure consistent practices that can prevent complications.
• Implement standardized preprocedural checklists

Use checklists to verify informed consent, allergies (including contrast reactions), anticoagulation status, renal function, pregnancy testing (when applicable), NPO status and airway assessment. Checklists reduce omissions and ensure risk evaluation is performed.

• Participate in quality registries and benchmark performance

Join the NCDR CathPCI Registry or similar quality registries to track complication rates against national benchmarks. The NCDR provides quality metrics like the PCI in-hospital risk-adjusted acute kidney injury rate. Regularly reviewing benchmarked data helps identify areas for improvement and shows a commitment to quality care.

• Conduct simulation training and debriefings for procedural emergencies

Run realistic simulation scenarios for catheterization lab emergencies, such as coronary perforation, tamponade, vascular complications or anaphylaxis. Assign team roles and conduct structured debriefings after simulations and actual events to identify system improvements.
PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failing to address barriers to medication adherence, recognition of warning signs and follow-up increases the likelihood of adverse outcomes after discharge.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and address barriers to safe post-procedure care.

• Emphasize DAPT adherence during discharge education

Stress the critical need to maintain DAPT after coronary stent placement. Explain the required duration and the risks of stopping early, such as stent thrombosis, which carries high mortality. Provide written instructions with medication names, doses and duration. Address potential adherence challenges, including costs, side effects and upcoming procedures that could interrupt DAPT.

• Provide stent identification cards 

Ensure that identification cards include stent type, location and implantation date. Provide a complete medication list with clear instructions. Those materials support continuity of care and guide antiplatelet management decisions by other providers.

• Schedule follow-up appointments before discharge

Arrange follow-ups within 2-4 weeks. Patients with baseline renal insufficiency, anemia or procedural complications need earlier follow-up and lab tests. Confirm that the patient understands the plan.

• Identify and address barriers to follow-up

Assess post-procedure challenges, including transportation, work obligations, insurance coverage and health literacy. Consider telehealth options for medication review and symptom checks when appropriate. Document barriers and mitigation steps.
 • Provide instructions for recognizing and reporting complications

Explain warning signs, such as access site bleeding or swelling, chest pain, shortness of breath, neurologic symptoms or allergic reactions that need urgent care. Give contact details for questions or complications and confirm that the patient understands the warning signs.

TITLE: Endocarditis
RISK_BRIEF:
Infective Endocarditis


SPECIALTY: Cardiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Fever with cardiac risk factors, Bacteremia, New or changing heart murmur, Prosthetic valve dysfunction

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Stroke, Heart failure, Septic emboli, Multiorgan failure, Death

Infective endocarditis is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in cardiology. 
Mitigating Your Risk 
 
With early recognition of risk factors, timely diagnostic evaluation and decisive escalation of care, cardiologists can mitigate the risks associated with infective endocarditis and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for infective endocarditis and provides strategies to help prevent them.   
 
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving infective endocarditis typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 75% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 25%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.   

Actions and Omissions Driving Infective Endocarditis Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Failing to elicit risk factors for infective endocarditis (IE) or recognize subtle clinical findings delays diagnosis and increases the likelihood of embolic complications and advanced disease at presentation.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure systematic identification of at-risk patients and recognition of clinical findings suggestive of IE.

• Obtain detailed history of recent healthcare exposures and procedures

Discuss with the patient recent hospitalizations, invasive procedures, dental work and intravascular catheter use. Over one-third of IE cases in the United States are healthcare-associated. Identifying the portal of entry helps determine the microbiologic workup and source control decisions.
• Systematically assess cardiac and non-cardiac risk factors in patients with unexplained fever or bacteremia

During the initial evaluation of patients with fever of unknown origin, new bacteremia or sepsis, ask about native valve disease, prosthetic heart valves, prior IE, congenital heart disease, intracardiac devices, injection drug use, recent dental procedures, hemodialysis and indwelling vascular catheters and immunosuppressed state. Document the presence or absence of each risk factor. This approach identifies high-risk patients early and triggers an optimal diagnostic workup. The 2020 ACC/AHA guidelines recommend blood cultures in all at-risk patients with unexplained fever. 

• Perform a comprehensive cardiovascular examination with attention to new or changing murmurs

Auscultate for new regurgitant murmurs in patients with suspected IE. Note that new murmurs are present in only 48% of cases, and classic findings, such as Janeway lesions and Osler nodes, occur in fewer than 5%. Document each murmur’s character, location, radiation and the presence or absence of peripheral stigmata. New or worsening regurgitation is a major Duke criterion and warrants immediate echocardiography. 

• Examine for embolic and immunologic phenomena

Inspect the skin, conjunctivae, nail beds, palms and soles for petechiae, splinter hemorrhages, Janeway lesions and Osler nodes. Perform fundoscopic exams for Roth spots when feasible. Although these findings occur in only 5%–10% of patients, their presence supports the diagnosis and may indicate ongoing embolic risk.

CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to order appropriate blood cultures before initiating antibiotics, perform adequate echocardiographic evaluation or use advanced imaging promptly increases the risk of missed or delayed diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely and complete diagnostic evaluation using the Modified Duke Criteria framework.

• Obtain at least 2–3 sets of blood cultures from separate venipuncture sites before initiating antibiotics

Draw cultures from peripheral sites using strict aseptic technique, ensuring adequate blood volume; avoid intravascular lines. If clinically feasible, space collections 30 minutes to 6 hours apart (though the 2023 Duke-ISCVID criteria no longer require strict timing). Starting antibiotics before completing blood cultures is a common source of culture-negative IE and diagnostic delays, while proper collection yields positive results in 90%–95% of native valve IE. Record the time, site and technique of each culture.

• Perform transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) as the initial imaging study in patients with suspected IE on native valves

Obtain TTE promptly when IE is suspected in patients without prosthetic values or cardiac devices. TTE has high specificity (>90%) but modest sensitivity (approximately 60%–75%) for detecting vegetations. If TTE is negative but clinical suspicion remains, or if image quality is suboptimal, proceed to transesophageal echocardiography (TEE).

• Proceed immediately to TEE as the first imaging study in high-risk clinical scenarios

Bypass TTE and advance directly to TEE in patients with prosthetic valves or CIEDs, Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia (especially without a known source), suspected complicated IE (abscess, fistula or severe valve dysfunction), or high clinical suspicion with suboptimal TTE windows. TTE sensitivity drops to 40%–50% in prosthetic valve IE, making it inadequate for ruling out infection in these populations. If TEE is unavailable or must be delayed, obtain TTE without delay as a temporizing measure.

• Consider advanced imaging when echocardiography is inconclusive

Leverage cardiac CT and 18F-FDG PET/CT, which are recognized as major imaging criteria in the 2023 Duke-ISCVID guidelines, when the results of echocardiography are uncertain. These modalities are especially useful in prosthetic valve IE, CIED infections and when echocardiography is equivocal. In prosthetic valve IE, a PET/CT can increase diagnostic sensitivity from 50%–70% to over 90%.
• Repeat echocardiography within 5–7 days if initial study is negative but clinical suspicion persists

Perform urgent repeat TEE if new clinical findings develop during therapy, including worsening heart failure, new conduction abnormalities or persistent fever despite appropriate antibiotics. Vegetations may grow to a detectable size during this interval, and complications, such as abscesses or fistulas, may become evident. 


CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure

Contributing action or omission: Delayed surgical consultation, failure to recognize indications for early surgery or poorly timed interventions increase the risk of heart failure, stroke and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support timely recognition of surgical indications and proper multidisciplinary decision-making.

• Engage a multidisciplinary endocarditis team early in cases of possible or confirmed IE

Include cardiology, cardiac surgery, infectious disease and imaging specialists in the core team. In facilities without cardiac surgery capability, coordinate with a heart valve center to determine whether transfer is needed. A team-based approach reduces 1-year mortality by 7.5%–10.3%, shortens time to surgery and leads to management changes in about half of patients. Document team discussions and recommendations in the medical record.

• Recognize and act on Class I indications for early surgery

Perform early surgery during initial hospitalization and before completing antibiotics for:
Valve dysfunction causing heart failure symptoms
Heart block, annular or aortic abscess or destructive penetrating lesions
Persistent infection (bacteremia or fever >5–7 days despite antibiotics)
IE caused by fungi or highly resistant organisms.
Assess embolic risk and consider surgery for large vegetations
Evaluate the risk of embolic events, which affect 22%–50% of IE cases and peak during the first two weeks of therapy. Key risk factors include vegetation size exceeding 10 mm, high vegetation mobility, S. aureus infection and mitral valve involvement, particularly the anterior leaflet. Consider early surgical intervention for patients with severe valve regurgitation and mobile vegetations larger than 10 mm, or when vegetations over 10 mm involve the anterior mitral leaflet alongside other clinical indications. 
• Evaluate for neurologic complications before surgical intervention

Perform brain imaging (CT or MRI) in patients with neurologic symptoms and in those being considered for surgery. Stroke complicates 20%–40% of IE cases and is an independent predictor of mortality. The optimal timing of surgery after stroke remains controversial; decisions should be individualized based on stroke severity, risk of hemorrhagic transformation and the urgency of cardiac indications.


ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate handoffs, unclear escalation pathways and failure to share critical findings across specialties increase the risk of delayed diagnosis and treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication and enable coordinated multidisciplinary care.

• Implement handoff protocols for patients with suspected or confirmed IE

Use standardized frameworks such as SBAR for transitions between the emergency department, inpatient units and intensive care. Communicate current Duke Criteria status, blood culture results, echocardiographic findings, antibiotic regimen and pending surgical decisions. Document the handoff in the medical record. 

• Establish escalation triggers for urgent multidisciplinary consultation

Create protocols that specify when to escalate care, such as with new hemodynamic instability, neurologic symptoms, persistent fever or bacteremia despite antibiotics, new conduction abnormalities and echocardiographic evidence of abscess or worsening valve dysfunction. Ensure that all team members understand escalation pathways and documentation requirements. 

• Coordinate transfer to a heart valve center when surgical expertise is unavailable

Initiate early referral to a center with cardiac surgery capabilities for patients at facilities without such resources. Document transfer discussions, timing considerations and any barriers. Ensure continuity of antibiotic therapy and monitoring during transfer.


ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of diagnostic criteria, clinical reasoning and treatment decisions compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces clinical decision-making and provides a defensible record.

• Document collection technique and timing of blood cultures

Record the time, site (peripheral vs. central line) and aseptic technique for each blood culture set and note whether cultures were obtained before or after antibiotic initiation. This supports the interpretation of culture results and shows adherence to diagnostic standards. 
• Record Modified Duke Criteria status at diagnosis and with clinical changes

Document which major and minor criteria are met, the classification (definite, possible or rejected IE) and the clinical reasoning supporting the diagnosis. Update this documentation when new information becomes available, such as positive blood cultures, echocardiographic findings and embolic events. 

Sample documentation language: "Modified Duke Criteria assessment: 1 major criterion (positive blood cultures with S. aureus from 2 separate sets) and 3 minor criteria (predisposing injection drug use, fever 38.5°C, splenic infarct on CT). Classification: Definite IE by clinical criteria."

• Document echocardiographic findings with vegetation characteristics

Record vegetation location, size (in millimeters), mobility and associated valve dysfunction. Document the presence or absence of abscess, fistula or prosthetic dehiscence, as well as image quality limitations and rationale for proceeding to TEE or advanced imaging. 

• Record multidisciplinary team discussions and recommendations

Note the date, participants, imaging and clinical data reviewed and specific recommendations from each endocarditis team meeting. Share outcomes with the referring provider, primary care physician and patient, and maintain a database for quality improvement and audit. 
• Document surgical indications assessment and decision-making

Record the presence or absence of each Class I and Class II surgical indication. When surgery is recommended, document the timing rationale; when it is deferred or declined, note the clinical reasoning, patient factors and the informed consent discussion. 

Sample documentation language: "Surgical indications assessment: Class I indication present (persistent bacteremia day 6 despite appropriate antibiotics). Discussed with cardiac surgery and endocarditis team. Patient is a surgical candidate. Risks, benefits and alternatives discussed with patient and family. Patient consents to proceed with valve replacement."

• Document patient education regarding IE prophylaxis and follow-up

Record all patient education provided before discharge, including guidance on antibiotic prophylaxis (when indicated), symptoms requiring urgent medical evaluation and follow-up appointment details. Confirm and record the patient's understanding of these instructions.

TITLE: Insertion or Change Pacemaker
RISK_BRIEF:
Insertion/Change of Cardiac Pacemaker 
SPECIALTY: Cardiology
PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Sinus Node Dysfunction, Acquired AV Block, Post-procedural Conduction Disturbance, Conduction System Disease, Carotid Sinus Syndrome, Neuromuscular Disease with Conduction Abnormalities, Battery Depletion, Device Malfunction, System Upgrade or Downgrade
ADVERSE OUTCOMES: Lead Dislodgment, Pneumothorax, Cardiac Perforation, Pocket Hematoma, Infection, Venous Thrombosis
Insertion/change of cardiac pacemaker is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in cardiology. 
Mitigating Your Risk 
 
With proactive risk identification, thorough procedural planning and appropriate device selection, cardiologists can mitigate the risks associated with inserting or changing a cardiac pacemaker and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for inserting or changing a cardiac pacemaker and provides strategies to help prevent them.   

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving inserting or changing a cardiac pacemaker typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 82% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 18%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Insertion/Change of Cardiac Pacemaker Risk [insert chart] 
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Incomplete pre-procedural assessment of history and physical exam increases the risk of unrecognized contraindications, suboptimal anticoagulation management and preventable complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support a comprehensive pre-procedural assessment that identifies risk factors and informs procedural planning.

• Obtain complete medication history with attention to anticoagulation and antiplatelet therapy

Document all anticoagulant and antiplatelet medications, including indications, therapy duration and thromboembolic risk (CHA₂DS₂-VASc score for atrial fibrillation patients). This information is critical for anticoagulation management. Avoid heparin bridging in most cases due to increased pocket hematoma risk; uninterrupted warfarin or minimally interrupted direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) therapy is preferred. 

• Identify patient-specific risk factors for complications

Assess and document risk factors associated with increased complication rates, including advanced age, elevated or low BMI, diabetes, chronic kidney or liver disease, heart failure, malnutrition, smoking history and low platelet count. Consider these factors when planning the procedure and determining post-procedural monitoring needs. 
• Assess for prior device-related infections or complications

Document any history of device infection, lead extraction and pocket revision or hematoma. Prior cardiovascular implantable electronic device (CIED) infection is a known risk factor for subsequent infection. Enhanced infection prevention strategies, including antimicrobial envelope placement, may benefit these patients. 

• Perform focused physical examination of the planned implant site

Examine the infraclavicular region for surgical scars, skin integrity, infection and adequacy of subcutaneous tissue. Patients with limited subcutaneous tissue or poor nutrition are at increased risk for erosion and may benefit from retropectoral pocket placement. Document findings to support procedural planning decisions. 

CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain appropriate pre-procedural testing, assess cardiac structure and function or identify conditions affecting procedural planning increases the risk of unsuitable device selection and poor outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure proper patient selection and procedural planning through comprehensive evaluations before procedures.

• Obtain baseline 12-lead ECG in all patients under consideration for pacemaker implantation

Obtain a resting 12-lead ECG to document bradyarrhythmia or conduction abnormality before implanting the device. The ECG may also reveal underlying indicators of structural heart disease (such as Q waves, QT prolongation, LV hypertrophy or low QRS voltage) that guide device selection and prognosis. This establishes the diagnostic basis for the procedure and supports clinical decisions. 

• Perform echocardiography to assess ventricular function in patients with suspected structural heart disease

Perform pre-procedure echocardiography in patients with suspected structural heart disease or heart failure symptoms to determine left ventricular ejection fraction and identify treatable conditions. LV function assessment guides device selection (single-chamber, dual-chamber or CRT) and identifies candidates for physiologic pacing strategies to avoid pacing-induced cardiomyopathy. 

• Use ambulatory monitoring to document symptom-rhythm correlation when bradycardia is intermittent

When bradycardia is suspected but not documented on a resting ECG, use ambulatory monitoring (Holter, event recorder or implantable loop recorder) based on symptom frequency. Short-term external monitors may suffice for symptoms less than 30 days apart, whereas implantable cardiac monitors provide longer surveillance for infrequent symptoms. Documenting symptom-rhythm correlation strengthens pacing indications and patient selection. 

• Assess renal function and coagulation status before the procedure

Obtain baseline labs, including serum creatinine and coagulation parameters. Renal dysfunction affects anticoagulation management and contrast use during venography, while coagulation status informs anticoagulation management decisions and hematoma risk. These evaluations support individualized procedure planning and risk mitigation. 


CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act On Diagnostic Finding

Contributing action or omission: Failing to recognize pacing contraindications, identify reversible causes of bradycardia or correctly interpret rhythm abnormalities increases the risk of inappropriate device implantation and preventable patient harm.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure accurate interpretation of diagnostic findings and optimal patient selection.

• Before proceeding with permanent pacing, ensure that bradycardia cannot be attributed to reversible causes

Evaluate for reversible causes of bradycardia, including drug toxicity (beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, digoxin, antiarrhythmics), electrolyte abnormalities, hypothyroidism, Lyme disease, sleep apnea and transient vagal episodes. Permanent pacing is not indicated if bradycardia is expected to resolve or results from nonessential drug therapy. Documenting this evaluation demonstrates sound clinical reasoning. 

• Verify that symptoms correlate with documented bradycardia

Establish a direct link between symptoms and rhythm abnormalities to satisfy Class I pacing requirements. Symptoms alone, such as syncope, presyncope, fatigue and exercise intolerance, do not meet Class I criteria without documented bradycardia. If the correlation remains unclear, classify the indication as Class II and document your clinical reasoning. 

• Recognize Class III (no benefit) symptoms that contraindicate pacing

Avoid permanent pacing in asymptomatic patients presenting with sinus bradycardia, first-degree AV block or type I second-degree AV block at the supra-His level, as well as any AV block expected to resolve. Device implantation in these scenarios provides no clinical benefit, exposes patients to unnecessary procedural risks and deviates from the standard of care. 

• Assess for conditions that may affect procedural approach or device selection

Review imaging and clinical history for conditions affecting planning for a procedure, including prior chest surgery, venous anomalies, other implanted devices, active infection or skin breakdown at the planned implant site. Identifying those factors before the procedure reduces intraoperative complications. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Failures in communication—such as inadequate handoffs, unclear documentation of pacing indication and insufficient coordination between implanting physicians and referring providers—increase the risk of inappropriate device selection, delayed recognition of complications and suboptimal follow-up.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support coordinated care across the procedural continuum.

• Confirm that the pacing indication and supporting diagnostic evidence are documented before implantation 

Before implantation, verify that the medical record contains a documented pacing indication (Class I, IIa or IIb), the supporting diagnostic evidence and the rationale for the chosen device type. If the documented indication is absent, incomplete or inconsistent with the clinical presentation, contact the referring provider to clarify before proceeding. This verification reduces the risk of implanting a device for an unsupported or unsuitable indication. 

• Coordinate anticoagulation management with the prescribing provider

Share the perioperative management strategy with the provider managing anticoagulation therapy. Document the approach and rationale for holding or continuing anticoagulation and the resumption plan. Avoid heparin bridging in most patients, as it increases hematoma risk without reducing thromboembolic events. 

• Implement handoff protocols for post-procedural care transitions

Use standardized frameworks such as SBAR when transferring care between procedural teams, inpatient units and outpatient settings. Communicate procedural details (venous access site, lead positions, pacing parameters, complications), anticoagulation status, activity restrictions and follow-up plan. Document handoff details in the medical record. 

• Establish clear follow-up communication with referring providers

Share procedure outcomes, device programming and follow-up plans with the referring physician. Include the indication, device type, lead positions, programmed parameters and complications. Clarify who is responsible for long-term device follow-up and when the patient should return for evaluation. 


ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failing to educate patients about activity restrictions, complication warning signs, device follow-up and lifestyle considerations increases the risk of lead dislodgement, delayed recognition of complications and suboptimal long-term outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient engagement and ensure timely recognition of device-related problems.

• Provide post-procedural activity restrictions and wound care instructions

Educate patients about activity restrictions to prevent lead dislodgement, including avoiding raising the arm on the implant side above shoulder level for 4–6 weeks, lifting more than 10 pounds or performing repetitive arm movements. Provide written wound care instructions and signs of infection (redness, warmth, swelling, drainage, fever). Document patient education and understanding. 

• Educate patients about warning signs requiring immediate evaluation

Instruct patients to seek immediate care for symptoms suggesting device malfunction or complications, including:
Return of pre-implant symptoms (syncope, presyncope, severe fatigue)
Chest pain
Shortness of breath
Hiccups or muscle twitching (suggesting lead perforation or phrenic nerve stimulation)
Swelling, redness, drainage, skin breakdown, device erosion or pain at the device site
Fever
Provide contact information for the device clinic and emergency evaluation. 

Sample patient education language: "Contact your doctor or go to the emergency room immediately if you experience: fainting or near-fainting, chest pain, shortness of breath, hiccups that won't stop, twitching of your chest or abdomen, swelling or redness at your pacemaker site or fever."

• Establish remote monitoring and ensure patient understanding of the system

Enroll patients in remote monitoring within 2-4 weeks of implantation. Educate them on how to use the system, the importance of regular transmissions and what to do if they receive an alert. Remote monitoring enables early detection of lead malfunction, arrhythmias and battery depletion, improving outcomes. 

• Provide written information about device identification and MRI safety

Ensure that patients receive and understand their device identification card. Educate them about the device’s MRI compatibility status and the need to inform healthcare providers about their pacemaker before any procedure. Most modern pacemakers are MRI-conditional but require specific protocols. 

• Document patient education and address barriers to follow-up

Record the education provided, topics covered and the patient's stated understanding. Identify and address follow-up barriers. Schedule the first post-implant, in-person evaluation before discharge and confirm with the patient.

TITLE: Cancer
RISK_BRIEF:
Skin Cancer


SPECIALTY: Dermatology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Suspicious Skin Lesions, Pigmented Lesions, Non-Healing Wounds

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Delayed Diagnosis, Metastatic Disease, Disfigurement, Death
Skin cancer is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in dermatology.

Mitigating Your Risk

With systematic approaches to lesion evaluation, appropriate biopsy technique, timely communication of results and structured follow-up, dermatologists can mitigate the risks associated with skin cancer and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for skin cancer and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving skin cancer typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 60% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 40%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Skin Cancer Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Failing to fully assess patient risk factors, perform comprehensive skin examinations or adequately evaluate suspicious lesions increases the likelihood of missed or delayed skin cancer diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure systematic identification of high-risk patients and thorough evaluation of suspicious lesions.

• Obtain comprehensive skin cancer risk history at initial and periodic visits

At the initial visit and subsequent checkups, document the patient’s history of skin cancer, blistering sunburns, tanning bed use, immunosuppression status and genetic syndromes. Document family history of melanoma or keratinocyte carcinoma. Risk stratification informs examination intensity and surveillance intervals. Patients with a prior keratinocyte carcinoma have approximately a 40% risk of developing a new primary keratinocyte carcinoma within five years, and this risk increases substantially with each additional skin cancer. 

• Perform complete skin examination with dermoscopy for high-risk patients

For patients with risk factors for melanoma (atypical nevi, high mole count, personal/family history), conduct a total body skin examination using dermoscopy. The NCCN recommends dermoscopy, total-body photography and sequential digital dermoscopy to detect new primary melanoma, particularly in patients with high mole counts. Document findings, including the number and location of atypical lesions.

• Apply systematic clinical criteria when evaluating pigmented lesions

Use the ABCDE criteria (asymmetry, border irregularity, color variation, diameter >6 mm, evolution) and "ugly duckling" sign to identify lesions warranting biopsy. Document the clinical features that prompted a biopsy or the rationale for observation. Maintain high clinical suspicion of amelanotic or pink lesions, as they can delay early detection. 

• Perform a comprehensive examination of anatomically challenging areas

Include scalp, ears, interdigital spaces, nail beds, soles and genital areas in examinations, as melanomas in those locations are frequently diagnosed at later stages. Subungual melanoma requires specialized biopsy of the nail matrix. Document examination of these areas.


CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to biopsy suspicious lesions, re-biopsy when needed, or use appropriate biopsy technique increases the risk of delayed diagnosis and understaging.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate tissue sampling and accurate pathologic diagnosis.

• Maintain a low threshold for biopsy of clinically suspicious lesions

Biopsy any lesion with clinical or dermoscopic features suggestive of malignancy rather than opting for observation. Failure to biopsy and diagnose is the most common cause of keratinocyte carcinoma litigation. In immunosuppressed patients, maintain an especially low threshold for biopsy, as lesions may be difficult to assess clinically. 

• Select biopsy technique appropriate to clinical suspicion and anatomic site

For lesions suspicious for melanoma, excisional/complete biopsy with 1–3 mm margins is preferred to ensure accurate Breslow thickness measurement. Avoid superficial shave biopsy, as it may compromise pathologic diagnosis and Breslow thickness assessment. An exception is suspected melanoma in situ, lentigo maligna type, for which a broad shave biopsy (extending into the deep papillary or superficial reticular dermis) may provide more thorough histologic assessment of potential focal microinvasion than multiple punch biopsies. For suspected SCC, ensure that the biopsy extends into the dermis. Document the rationale for the chosen biopsy technique.

• Rebiopsy when initial specimen is inadequate for diagnosis or microstaging

If a shave biopsy shows residual tumor or pigment at the base, perform a deeper biopsy (punch or elliptical) immediately, submitting it in a separate container and noting that the initial specimen was transected. If the pathology report indicates an inadequate specimen for staging, arrange rebiopsy before planning treatment.

• Submit complete clinical information with pathology requisition

Include patient age, anatomic location of the biopsy, clinical diameter, lesion status (primary or recurrent), immunosuppression status and history of radiation at the site. This information is essential for accurate pathologic interpretation and risk stratification.



CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Procedural errors, including inadequate biopsy depth, wrong-site procedures, failure to achieve clear margins and surgical complications, increase the risk of adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure technically appropriate procedures and minimize complications.

• Verify lesion identity and location before biopsy or excision

Take prebiopsy photographs to aid clinical/pathologic correlation and prevent wrong-site surgery. Include a regional view with anatomic landmarks. Confirm lesion identity with the patient, particularly when biopsying multiple lesions in one visit.

• Plan excisional biopsy orientation with definitive treatment in mind

For elliptical/fusiform excisional biopsy of suspected melanoma, orient the incision longitudinally (axially) and parallel to underlying lymphatics on extremities to permit accurate lymphatic mapping if needed. Avoid wider margins that could interfere with sentinel lymph node biopsy.

• Ensure adequate depth for accurate pathologic staging

For suspected melanoma, extend the biopsy to a depth sufficient to avoid transection at the deep margin, usually to the deep reticular dermis. For suspected SCC, ensure that the biopsy extends into the dermis. If the tumor appears to extend beyond the dermis, perform surgical excision instead of curettage and electrodesiccation or shave removal. 

• Confirm margin clearances before performing tissue rearrangement

For standard excision (where margins are sent for permanent section pathology), delay tissue rearrangement (flap reconstruction or extensive undermining) until clear margins are confirmed on final pathology. Use second intention healing, linear repair or skin grafting pending margin confirmation. If tissue rearrangement is required for closure, Mohs micrographic surgery or other forms of PDEMA is preferred to prevent the need for a staged procedure. For Mohs or other forms of PDEMA, margins are confirmed intraoperatively through real-time histologic assessment of the entire peripheral and deep margin surface. Once clear margins are confirmed—typically within the same operative session—reconstruction including flap repair and tissue rearrangement can proceed immediately. 

 


ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Failing to promptly communicate biopsy results, adequately explain diagnosis and treatment options or ensure patient understanding of follow-up requirements increases the risk of delayed treatment and harm.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely, clear communication that supports informed decision-making and adherence to treatment.

• Establish and follow a systematic process for communicating biopsy results

Develop a standardized workflow for result notification, including tracking pending pathology results and documenting patient contact. Up to one-third of physicians fail to notify patients of abnormal results. Ask patients about their preferred contact method during biopsy consent.

• Communicate malignant diagnoses directly to patients 

For melanoma or other malignant diagnoses, speak directly with patients in person or by phone rather than by voicemail or patient portals. Allow time for questions and document the conversation.

• Clearly explain diagnosis, prognosis and treatment options

When delivering a skin cancer diagnosis, explain the type of cancer, stage if known, treatment options, expected outcomes, and timeline. For melanoma, discuss the need for wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy when indicated. Document the informed consent discussion and stated patient understanding.

• Ensure that patients understand follow-up surveillance requirements

Educate patients about the risk of recurrence and new primary skin cancers. Patients with prior keratinocyte carcinoma have a 40% risk of developing a new primary keratinocyte carcinoma within five years. Provide written instructions on self-examination, sun protection and follow-up intervals. 




ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of clinical findings, biopsy rationale, pathology results, treatment discussions and follow-up plans compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces clinical decision-making and provides a defensible record.

• Document clinical description and rationale for biopsy or observation

Record the location of each suspicious lesion using anatomic landmarks, size and clinical features (ABCDE criteria, dermoscopic findings). Note the clinical rationale for biopsy or continued observation. When choosing observation, document the follow-up plan and criteria for biopsy.

Sample documentation language: "3 mm pigmented macule on right posterior shoulder with irregular border and color variation on dermoscopy. Clinical features concerning for atypical nevus vs. early melanoma. Shave biopsy performed to deep reticular dermis. Patient to return for results in 7–10 days."

• Document informed consent discussions for procedures

Before biopsy or excision, document discussion of the procedure, risks (scarring, infection, bleeding, incomplete removal), benefits and alternatives. For Mohs surgery or wide local excision, include expected cosmetic outcomes and reconstruction options. Failure to obtain informed consent is a recurring theme in keratinocyte carcinoma litigation cases. 
• Document pathology results and communication with patient

Record the date pathology results were received, diagnosis, key pathologic features (Breslow thickness, ulceration, margins for melanoma, differentiation, depth, perineural invasion for SCC) and the date and method of patient notification. Note the patient's stated understanding and questions.

• Document the follow-up plan with specific intervals and surveillance strategy

Record the recommended follow-up schedule based on diagnosis and risk stratification. For melanoma, include stage-appropriate surveillance intervals. For keratinocyte carcinoma, document risk category and corresponding follow-up frequency. Include patient education provided regarding self-examination and sun protection.

Sample documentation language: "Discussed diagnosis of pT1b melanoma, stage IB. Wide local excision with 1 cm margins and sentinel lymph node biopsy recommended. Patient understands diagnosis and treatment plan. Follow-up every 6–12 months for 5 years, then annually. Self-skin examination and sun protection counseling provided. Patient verbalized understanding."




PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify and address barriers to patient follow-up, delays in seeking evaluation for changing lesions or non-adherence to surveillance recommendations increase the likelihood of advanced disease.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and address barriers to timely evaluation and follow-up.

• Educate patients about warning signs requiring prompt evaluation

Provide clear verbal and written instructions about signs and symptoms that warrant immediate evaluation: new or changing moles, non-healing wounds, lesions that bleed or crust and any rapidly growing skin lesions. Patient delay remains a leading contributor to late-stage melanoma diagnosis, and studies show that patient awareness of melanoma severity is low. 

• Address barriers to patient follow-up

Identify and attempt to mitigate obstacles such as transportation, work schedules, insurance and health literacy. Teledermatology may serve as a triage or supplemental monitoring tool between in-person visits for select patients, though it is not a substitute for in-person total body skin examination with regional lymph node assessment, particularly in higher-risk patients. Recognize that patients with limited English proficiency and those from communities with lower baseline skin cancer awareness may face additional barriers to timely follow-up.

• Provide written follow-up instructions in patient's primary language

Clearly state the recommended follow-up interval and signs to monitor between visits. Provide office contact information in case the patient has concerns. Ensure that materials are provided at the appropriate literacy level and in the patient's primary language. 

• Document patient non-adherence and continued outreach efforts

When a patient misses a follow-up appointment or declines a recommended biopsy, document the recommendation, risks explained and the person’s stated reasons for declining. Continue contacting the patient by phone, patient portal or mail and document all outreach attempts.

Sample documentation language: "Patient declined biopsy of 8 mm irregularly pigmented lesion on left calf. Discussed concern for possible melanoma and risks of delayed diagnosis including metastatic disease. Patient states preference to 'watch it' and return if it changes. Risks and recommendations documented. Patient to return in 3 months for re-evaluation or sooner if lesion changes. Written instructions provided."

TITLE: Abscess
RISK_BRIEF:
Abscess 
SPECIALTY: Diagnostic Radiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Suspected Infection, Postoperative Fever, Soft Tissue Swelling

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Missed or Delayed Diagnosis, Unnecessary Intervention, Sepsis
Abscesses are a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in diagnostic radiology.

Mitigating Your Risk

With attention to diagnostic accuracy, timely communication and clear reporting, diagnostic radiologists can mitigate the risks associated with abscesses and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for abscesses and provides strategies to help prevent them.    

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving abscesses stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 96% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 4%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.   
Actions and Omissions Driving Abscess Risk [insert chart]  
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act On Diagnostic Finding

Contributing action or omission: Failing to recognize imaging features of abscess, misinterpretation of fluid collections or confusion with abscess mimics increases the risk of delayed treatment and patient harm.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve diagnostic accuracy and reduce the likelihood of missed or misinterpreted abscess findings.

• Apply appropriate imaging protocols 
Use the following guidelines when selecting imaging modalities for suspected abscesses based on clinical context: 
Intra-abdominal abscess: CT with IV contrast is preferred (88% sensitivity, 93% specificity) for collection detection vs. ultrasound (75% sensitivity, 91% specificity). However, CT specificity for distinguishing infected from noninfected collections is only 39%. When findings are unclear, integrate clinical and laboratory data before recommending intervention.
Perianal disease: Choose MRI pelvis with and without IV contrast, or CT pelvis with IV contrast, as first-line options. 
Soft tissue infection: US, MRI (with and without IV contrast or without IV contrast), and CT with IV contrast are all rated "Usually Appropriate" with normal or nonspecific initial radiographs. Select the modality based on clinical context: use ultrasound for superficial collections and real-time guidance; MRI for deep soft tissue characterization and extent of disease; and CT with IV contrast for rapid evaluation, particularly in the emergency setting. When necrotizing fasciitis is suspected, imaging should not delay surgical exploration.
• Evaluate imaging features that differentiate abscesses from noninfected collections
During image interpretation, assess findings associated with infection, including rim enhancement (>50% circumference), wall thickness (>3 mm), fluid attenuation ≥20 HU, entrapped gas and adjacent fat stranding. Extraluminal gas is the most specific CT sign of abscess (present in about 38% of cases), but its absence does not rule out infection. Document these features in the report to guide clinical decision-making. 
• Recognize entities that mimic drainable abscesses

Before recommending drainage, consider alternative diagnoses, including:
Neoplasms: lymphoma, necrotic tumors, ovarian cancer
Ischemia/infarction: liquefactive splenic infarct
Diverticula: calyceal, Meckel, giant colonic
Congenital variants: obstructed duplicated collecting system
Postoperative changes: expected anatomy after urinary diversion or Roux-en-Y
Non-pyogenic infections: tuberculosis, echinococcal cysts
Misdiagnosis can lead to unnecessary percutaneous drainage, complications and delayed treatment. 
• Integrate clinical and laboratory data when imaging findings are unclear

When imaging features are nonspecific, incorporate clinical information, such as fever, elevated C-reactive protein (≥100 mg/L), leukocytosis and diabetes status. A validated scoring system combining CT attenuation ≥20 HU, entrapped gas, diabetes and CRP ≥100 mg/L achieved an AUC of 0.96 for distinguishing infected versus noninfected postoperative collections. Document the clinical context guiding the interpretation.
• Account for reduced sensitivity in specific populations

Recognize decreased CT sensitivity for abscess detection in immunocompromised patients. Use a lower threshold for follow-up imaging or intervention when clinical suspicion remains high despite equivocal findings. Document the clinical context and exam limitations.
• Use systematic search patterns to reduce perceptual errors

Recognize that perceptual errors are common. In a national study of radiology adverse event claims, false-negative findings (missing visible abnormalities) accounted for 48.7% of claims, satisfaction of search (stopping after finding one abnormality) for 12%, and misinterpretation for 13.1%. To mitigate these errors: 
Review all available series and reconstructions (axial, coronal, sagittal), not just a single plane
Use a checklist-based approach to ensure systematic coverage of all anatomic regions on every study
Scroll through the entire dataset rather than relying on key images or scout views
After identifying one abnormality, deliberately continue the search for additional findings
For complex cases, overnight or high-volume shifts, or when clinical suspicion remains high despite a negative initial interpretation, consider double-reading or peer consultation 
Cognitive debiasing strategies, such as pausing to consider alternative diagnoses before finalizing a report, can help counteract anchoring bias and premature closure, which are common contributors to interpretive errors.
• Evaluate emerging AI-assisted detection tools with caution
Ensure ongoing validation of AI-based detection tools. Any deployed tool should be FDA-cleared for the intended use and used as an adjunct to, not replacement for, radiologist interpretation. Automation bias, where incorrect AI outputs negatively influence radiologist performance, is a documented risk; institutions should provide training on algorithm limitations and radiologists should independently verify AI-flagged findings before acting on them.  


CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to recommend appropriate imaging, contrast administration or follow-up imaging when initial studies are nondiagnostic increases the risk of missed abscess.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that appropriate imaging is recommended and performed when clinical suspicion warrants further evaluation.

• Recommend contrast-enhanced imaging for suspected abscess

Ensure that IV contrast is administered unless contraindicated when protocoling or interpreting studies for suspected abscess. Rim enhancement—only visible with IV contrast—is the key feature differentiating abscesses from noninfected collections. Unenhanced CT is approximately 30% less accurate than contrast-enhanced CT for acute abdominal pain evaluation, with false-negative and false-positive rates of 14%–19% for unenhanced studies. Document the rationale if contrast is not administered.

• Advise ultrasound for superficial soft tissue infections with clinical suspicion

Recommend point-of-care or formal ultrasound for patients with cellulitis when the physical exam cannot confirm an underlying abscess. Ultrasound detects hidden abscesses, prevents unnecessary invasive procedures and guides further imaging. Systematic reviews show ultrasound sensitivity for cutaneous abscess detection ranges from 89% to 98%, compared to 75% to 90% for clinical assessment alone.
• Propose image-guided drainage when appropriate

When imaging reveals a drainable abscess, advise image-guided drainage in the report. The ACR Appropriateness Criteria (2020) discuss size thresholds in the context of specific clinical variants. No single size cutoff applies universally; drainage decisions should integrate collection size, clinical status, accessibility, collection complexity (unilocular vs. multiloculated), and cause. CT-guided and ultrasound-guided drainage are effective alternatives to surgical drainage for many abscesses. Note the preferred guidance modality based on collection location and visibility.
• Recommend follow-up imaging when the initial study is limited or nondiagnostic

Advise follow-up imaging with appropriate modalities when initial studies have technical limitations, such as motion artifact or suboptimal contrast timing, or when findings are equivocal. Consider an MRI for deep pelvic collections where CT spatial resolution is limited. For superficial collections, ultrasound may better characterize internal structure. Record the recommended modality, timing and clinical indication.

• Include follow-up recommendations in the report impression

When advising additional imaging, include it in the report impression with notes on modality, time frame and clinical indication. Studies show recommendations for additional imaging appear in over 10% of radiology reports, but adherence ranges from 29% to 77%. Adherence is significantly higher when recommendations include a specific time frame and are placed in the impression section of the report—the section most consistently read by referring clinicians. Recommendations lacking time frames or impression placement have lower completion rates. To maximize follow-up adherence, include the recommended modality, specific time frame (follow-up CT with IV contrast in 6–8 weeks), and clinical indication in the impression.




CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failure to recommend or perform appropriate follow-up imaging after abscess drainage or treatment increases the risk of unrecognized treatment failure, recurrence or complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate post-treatment surveillance and timely recognition of treatment failure.

• Use evidence-based criteria for drain removal decisions

Remove drains when infection signs resolve, catheter output falls below 10–20 cc/day and repeat imaging confirms abscess resolution. The 10–20 cc/day output threshold is based on expert consensus and common practice rather than prospective comparative evidence; clinical judgment should prevail when output trends, symptoms or collection complexity suggest otherwise. Whether routine pre-removal imaging is necessary remains unsettled; consider selective use of pre-removal imaging based on clinical trajectory, abscess complexity and institutional protocol. Document the rationale for the removal decision.
• Recommend follow-up imaging after drainage procedures
Advise follow-up imaging after image-guided abscess drainage to evaluate treatment response. Perform imaging within 48-72 hours if clinical improvement is absent or at 1-2 weeks to confirm resolution. Specify the modality (typically the same as used for diagnosis or drainage guidance) and clinical indication. Include the recommendation in the procedure report. 

• Assess for residual or recurrent collection on follow-up imaging

Review follow-up imaging after drainage for residual undrained collections, new or enlarging collections, catheter position and function and complications such as fistula formation or hemorrhage. Compare with prior imaging to identify interval changes. Document signs of treatment failure or the need for reintervention.

• Report persistent or worsening findings to the clinical team

Notify the referring provider directly when follow-up imaging shows treatment failure, such as enlarging or new collections or clinical deterioration. These findings may require urgent action. Document communication in the report. 



ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Failing to promptly communicate critical findings, document communication adequately or ensure effective handoff processes increase the risk of delayed treatment and patient harm.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely, documented communication of critical findings and support coordinated care.

•  Make clear recommendations in reports

When identifying an abscess or drainable collection, include clear recommendations on drainage, specialty consults (interventional radiology, surgery) and the urgency of intervention. Avoid vague language that could delay treatment. Reference ACR Appropriateness Criteria when applicable to support evidence-based guidance. 
• Communicate abscess findings directly to the responsible provider when possible

Report abscess findings, especially in cases of sepsis or clinical deterioration, directly to the referring provider or their designee via telephone or another synchronous method. The Joint Commission identifies timely reporting of critical test results as a National Patient Safety Goal, with communication failures being a leading cause of malpractice claims in radiology. Match communication urgency to clinical acuityusing the ACR Actionable Reporting Work Group's three-tier framework: Category 1 findings require communication and clinical decision within minutes (for example, abscess in a septic or clinically deteriorating patient); Category 2 findings require action within hours (for example, newly identified abscess in a stable patient); and Category 3 findings require action within days (for example, incidental small, stable collection warranting outpatient follow-up). Follow institutional policy for specific timeframes. 
• Use closed-loop communication for critical findings
Implement systems that require referring providers to actively acknowledge receipt of critical results. Electronic alert systems with acknowledgment tracking improve adherence to communication policies and reduce the risk of failures. EHR-integrated closed-loop programs have achieved near-complete success in notifying providers and patients while facilitating next steps in findings management. Ensure that audit trails include time, date, and acknowledgment status.
• Follow escalation protocols when responsible providers are unreachable

Amplify communication efforts when referring providers cannot be reached immediately, according to institutional protocols. Continue escalation until the appropriate provider acknowledges receipt of the critical finding. Document all communication attempts and final acknowledgment in the report. 

• Document communication in the radiology report

Record communications about critical results in the final radiology report. Note when communication occurred, who received the findings and how the report was delivered, such as by telephone or in person. Reporting templates can improve compliance with documentation requirements. 
Sample documentation: "Critical finding of hepatic abscess communicated to Dr. Smith via telephone at 14:32 on 04/03/2026."
• Be available for consultation after the final report

Stay accessible to the ordering clinician to discuss imaging findings after finalizing the report. Offer a process for reviewing new clinical information and creating an agenda when needed. This supports ongoing clinical decision-making and reduces the risk of misunderstandings.
