TITLE: Bowel perforation
RISK_BRIEF:
Bowel Perforation 

SPECIALTY: Diagnostic Radiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Acute Abdomen, Trauma, Post-procedural Imaging

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Missed or Delayed Diagnosis of Bowel Perforation, Sepsis, Peritonitis, Death
Bowel perforation is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in diagnostic radiology.
Mitigating Your Risk

With systematic image interpretation, timely communication of critical findings and thorough documentation, diagnostic radiologists can mitigate the risks associated with bowel perforation and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for bowel perforation and provides strategies to help prevent them.
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving bowel perforation stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 93% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 7%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.   
Actions and Omissions Driving Bowel Perforation Risk [insert chart]  

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act On Diagnostic Finding
Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify or correctly interpret CT findings of bowel perforation—including extraluminal air, bowel wall discontinuity and indirect signs—increases the risk of delayed surgical intervention and adverse patient outcomes.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve detection of perforation findings and reduce interpretation errors.

• Evaluate for direct signs of perforation on every abdominal CT

During image interpretation, systematically search for CT findings of perforation, including extraluminal air (pneumoperitoneum), bowel wall discontinuity, extraluminal oral contrast and focal bowel wall defects. Use lung windows to detect subtle free air on soft tissue windows. Careful evaluation of high-specificity findings reduces perceptual errors, which cause a majority of radiology errors. 
• Recognize indirect signs that suggest occult perforation

Evaluate CT imaging for indirect findings that may indicate perforation even when direct signs are absent, including unexplained free fluid without solid organ injury, bowel wall thickening, mesenteric fat stranding, abnormal bowel wall enhancement and focal fluid collections. CTs can miss up to 20% of bowel injuries in blunt trauma and up to 28% in penetrating trauma, making recognition of indirect signs critical. When multiple indirect signs are present, the probability of surgical bowel injury increases substantially; four or more findings are strongly predictive of injury requiring surgery. 

• Apply validated scoring for equivocal cases

Implement standardized systems, such as Faget's and McNutt's, when CT findings are indeterminate. The Faget scoring system grades CT signs by relevance (e.g., small hemoperitoneum = 1 point, pneumoperitoneum = 5 points); a score of 5 or higher confers an 11-fold increase in bowel injury requiring surgical exploration. The McNutt grading scale evaluates mesenteric injury on CT; a grade of 4 or higher combined with clinical findings (abdominal tenderness, elevated WBC) confers higher risk of bowel injury. Scoring systems that combine radiologic, biochemical and clinical signs improve diagnostic performance beyond individual CT findings.   

• Maintain awareness of false-positive and false-negative pitfalls

Recognize that free intra-abdominal air can result from non-perforation causes, such as air tracking through the diaphragmatic crura from a pneumothorax, leading to false-positive results. Conversely, contained perforations may show minimal or no pneumoperitoneum. Findings like pneumatosis intestinalis with pneumoperitoneum in chronic conditions (such as pseudo-obstruction) may not require surgery and should be correlated with clinical status. 

• Consider AI-assisted detection tools as complementary aids 

Consider AI-based pneumoperitoneum detection tools as complementary triage and detection aids. Radiologists must independently verify AI findings, as sensitivity decreases for small amounts of free air, and AI cannot replace clinical correlation. AI-assisted detection tools are emerging and no major radiology society currently recommends AI-assisted detection as standard practice. Institutions deploying these tools should establish governance, acceptance testing and monitoring protocols consistent with the multi-society statement on AI in radiology (ACR/CAR/ESR/RANZCR/RSNA, 2024), including documentation protocols that capture AI use, human verification and clinical correlation. The ACR ARCH-AI program provides a framework for institutions to attest to minimum criteria in these areas, with formal ACR AI practice accreditation anticipated in 2027.

CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing
Contributing action or omission: Failing to recommend appropriate contrast protocols or order repeat imaging when an initial CT is negative, but clinical suspicion remains high, increases the risk of missed perforation.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate imaging recommendations and follow-up.

• Recognize limitations of FAST for bowel injury evaluation
In the trauma setting, recognize that focused assessment with sonography for trauma (FAST) is not well suited for evaluation of bowel injury; significant fluid trapped in the mesentery may not be detected. A negative FAST does not exclude bowel or mesenteric injury and should not replace CT when clinical suspicion for perforation exists.
• Ensure appropriate contrast protocols for suspected perforation

Use IV contrast-enhanced CT as the standard modality for suspected bowel perforation. In time-sensitive situations, single IV contrast is preferred, as it has accuracy comparable to triple contrast (IV, oral, rectal). When oral contrast is used and extravasation is identified, this finding has high specificity for perforation. 
• Recommend CT when plain radiographs are negative but clinical concern persists

Recognize that free air under the diaphragm on an upright chest radiograph suggests hollow organ perforation, but absence of pneumoperitoneum on plain films does not exclude perforation. Recommend a CT for patients with clinical signs of perforation (peritonitis, severe abdominal pain) even if initial radiographs are negative. Document this guidance.
• Consider CT for patients with blunt trauma and negative initial imaging

In the blunt trauma setting, consider recommending repeat CT after 6–8 hours for patients with high clinical suspicion of bowel injury (high-energy mechanism, persistent abdominal tenderness, elevated WBC) and equivocal or subtle findings on initial CT. Delayed imaging can improve sensitivity by allowing time for enteric fluid and intraluminal gas to leak into the peritoneal cavity. However, this practice has mixed evidence and must be weighed against increased radiation exposure and contrast-related risks. Document the recommendation and rationale in the radiology report.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Medication Error

Contributing action or omission: Failure to recognize imaging findings that require discontinuing high-risk medications or to communicate findings needing immediate clinical intervention leads to increased patient harm and delayed treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate recognition and communication of findings relevant to medication management.

• Identify perforation in patients on high-risk medications

Maintain heightened awareness for perforation in patients on high-risk medications, such as bevacizumab (anti-VEGF therapy), immune checkpoint inhibitors or chronic corticosteroids or chronic NSAID use and communicate this finding urgently when detected.
• Document clinical context that affects interpretation

When the patient’s history includes immunosuppression, recent chemotherapy or other conditions that may impact imaging findings, note the clinical context of high-risk medications in the report and recommend clinical correlation to facilitate appropriate management.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Failure to communicate critical bowel perforation findings to the referring provider, inadequate documentation of communication attempts and lack of escalation protocols can delay treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and ensure timely notification of critical findings.

• Communicate critical findings directly to responsible providers

Use direct, synchronous communication by telephone with the responsible provider or designee to report a bowel perforation with pneumoperitoneum or other critical (Category 1) findings requiring immediate intervention. Do not rely solely on report availability in the EHR for critical findings. The ACR recommends communicating tests in a manner matching the urgency of the situation. 

• Establish escalation protocols when providers cannot be reached

When the responsible provider cannot be reached in a timely manner, continue communication efforts per institutional protocols. This may include contacting covering physicians, nursing supervisors or hospital operators. Document all escalation attempts. Communication should end only when an appropriate provider acknowledges receipt of the critical finding.
• Use structured reporting to ensure completeness of critical finding documentation

Implement contextual reporting templates for critical findings that automatically capture required communication elements, such as when, to whom and how. This approach improves guideline adherence in communicating critical findings from 28.6% to 90.4% in quality improvement studies. 

• Document communication in the radiology report

Note communication of critical findings in the final radiology report, including:
What was communicated
To whom (name and role)
When (date and time)
How (telephone, in person) 
This documentation creates an audit trail that demonstrates adherence to communication standards and supports defensibility.
Sample documentation language: "Critical finding of pneumoperitoneum communicated by telephone to Dr. [Name], Emergency Medicine attending, at [time] on [date]. Dr. [Name] acknowledged receipt and stated patient will proceed to surgical consultation."
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of imaging findings, clinical correlation, differential diagnosis and communication of results compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support documentation that reinforces clinical decision-making and provides a defensible record.

• Document pertinent positive and negative findings systematically

Include in the report critical information such as presence or absence of key findings for bowel perforation: pneumoperitoneum (location and estimated volume), bowel wall integrity, free fluid, mesenteric stranding, bowel wall thickening and enhancement pattern. Documenting pertinent negatives shows evaluation and supports defensibility when findings are subtle or absent.

Sample documentation language: "No pneumoperitoneum identified on lung or soft tissue windows. No extraluminal contrast. No focal bowel wall discontinuity. Trace free fluid in the pelvis without solid organ injury—recommend clinical correlation and consideration of repeat imaging if clinical concern persists."

• Document comparison with prior imaging when available

Review and record comparison with relevant previous imaging when available. Failure to consult prior studies is a known source of diagnostic error. Documenting interval changes can help confirm or rule out acute perforation.
• Document limitations that may affect interpretation

When technical factors, such as motion artifact, suboptimal contrast timing or limited oral contrast opacification) impede evaluation for perforation, record those limitations. Documentation demonstrates awareness of diagnostic uncertainty and supports recommendations for additional evaluation.
• Provide a clear differential diagnosis and recommendations
When findings are inconclusive, document the differential diagnosis and offer recommendations for clinical correlation, additional imaging or surgical consultation. Avoid vague or noncommittal language lacking actionable recommendations, as this increases risk of delayed follow-up. Recommendations should be actionable, and, when appropriate, include a time frame.

TITLE: Breast cancer
RISK_BRIEF:
Breast Cancer

SPECIALTY: Diagnostic Radiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Breast Imaging (Screening and Diagnostic)

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Missed or Delayed Breast Cancer Diagnosis

Breast cancer is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in diagnostic radiology.
Mitigating Your Risk

With accurate interpretation, appropriate follow-through and thorough documentation, diagnostic radiologists can mitigate the risks associated with breast cancer in diagnostic radiology and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for breast cancer in diagnostic radiology and provides strategies to help prevent them.
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving breast cancer in diagnostic radiology stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 92% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 8%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.   
Actions and Omissions Driving Breast Cancer Risk in Diagnostic Radiology [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act On Diagnostic Finding
Contributing action or omission: Failure to detect or correctly interpret mammographic findings—including masses, calcifications, architectural distortion and asymmetries—is the leading cause of breast imaging malpractice claims. 

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce perception errors, improve lesion detection and support accurate BI-RADS categorization.

• Systematically review all prior imaging 

Look for subtle interval changes, developing asymmetries or slowly growing lesions when evaluating prior exams during mammogram interpretation. Prior image comparison is particularly important for BI-RADS category 3 assessments, where 2–3 years of demonstrated stability is typically needed before assessment can be downgraded to benign (BI-RADS category 2). This practice aligns with ACR BI-RADS guidance and reflects the standard of care.

• Remain alert for multiple abnormalities to prevent satisfaction bias

Adopt a systematic approach to image review, examining all breast regions, including the axilla and posterior tissue. Avoid the cognitive bias known as "satisfaction of search," where review stops after identifying one abnormality, leading to missed cancers. Pay particular attention to architectural distortions and asymmetries, as those are frequently overlooked. 
• Recognize high-risk mammographic presentations

Focus on mammographic findings linked to higher malignancy rates, such as new or enlarging asymmetries, architectural distortion without prior surgery, grouped amorphous or fine pleomorphic calcifications and masses with indistinct or spiculated margins. Dense breast tissue limits mammographic sensitivity. Document breast density in every report and consider supplemental imaging recommendations per ACR and NCCN guidelines. 
• Be aware of emerging AI-assisted detection tools 

If using AI-assisted detection tools, use those that are FDA-cleared and implement them as a second reader or decision support tool, not as a substitute for radiologist interpretation. 
Prospective trials show AI-supported screening can increase cancer detection rates, with reported improvements ranging from 4%-29% depending on the integration strategy and screening setting. However, results vary across studies and integration strategies, and some trials have reported higher recall rates alongside better detection. AI may under detect certain cancer subtypes, including triple-negative cancers and cancers in dense breast tissue or non-mammary zones. AI-assisted detection is an evolving area and providers should stay informed about AI limitations and participate in quality monitoring. 
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing
Contributing action or omission: Failing to recommend biopsy for suspicious findings or supplemental imaging for high-risk patients or those with dense breast tissue increases the risk of delayed diagnosis.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate imaging recommendations based on patient risk factors and imaging findings.

• Advise supplemental screening for high-risk patients

Recommend an annual breast MRI with and without contrast for patients with a residual lifetime breast cancer risk of ≥ 20% as defined by models that include a comprehensive family history (e.g., Tyrer-Cuzick, BRCAPRO, BOADICEA/CanRisk). Begin no later than age 40 or 10 years before the youngest affected family member's diagnosis, but not before age 25. Annual mammography with tomosynthesis should begin no later than age 40 or 10 years before the youngest affected family member’s diagnosis, but not before age 30. For patients unable to undergo an MRI, choose contrast-enhanced mammography (CEM) or molecular breast imaging (MBI) as alternatives. Document the basis for the risk assessment and supplemental imaging in the report.
• Address dense breast tissue with appropriate recommendations

Advise supplemental screening for patients with dense breasts per NCCN guidelines. For extremely dense breasts, breast MRI with and without contrast is recommended (beginning at age 50, with consideration at age 40). For those who qualify for but cannot undergo MRI, consider CEM or MBI; whole breast ultrasound may be done if CEM or MBI is not available. For heterogeneously dense breasts, the recommendation is to "consider supplemental screening" and use shared decision-making. Dense breasts limit mammographic sensitivity and increase cancer risk. Federal regulations mandate breast density notification; ensure that reports include a density assessment and supplemental imaging recommendations. 

• Advise biopsy for BI-RADS 4 and 5 lesions

Recommend tissue sampling through image-guided core needle biopsies for lesions categorized as BI-RADS 4 (suspicious) or BI-RADS 5 (highly suggestive of malignancy). BI-RADS 4 lesions carry a >2% to < 95% probability of malignancy (subcategorized as 4A, 4B and 4C with increasing suspicion), while BI-RADS 5 lesions carry a probability of ≥ 95%. Document biopsy recommendation and implement tracking systems to confirm biopsy completion. 


• Avoid inappropriate BI-RADS 3 assessments

Reserve BI-RADS category 3 (probably benign) only for validated findings specific to each modality. For mammography, validated findings include a noncalcified circumscribed solid mass, focal asymmetry or solitary group of punctate calcifications. For ultrasound, validated findings include a circumscribed oval parallel solid mass, an isolated complicated cyst and clustered microcysts. Do not assign BI-RADS 3 from screening without a diagnostic workup. Avoid BI-RADS 3 for new findings, palpable lesions or those that have increased in size or extent. Inappropriate BI-RADS 3 assignments can delay cancer diagnoses, leading to detection at advanced stages.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention
Contributing action or omission: Failing to establish imaging-pathology concordance after biopsy, track patients with BI-RADS 3 findings requiring follow-up or ensure completion of diagnostic workup increases the risk of missed or delayed cancer diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure proper post-biopsy management and systematic follow-up of imaging findings.

• Establish imaging-pathology concordance

After an image-guided breast biopsy, review pathology results alongside imaging findings to determine concordance. Document the assessment after reviewing the core needle biopsy pathology report, which may require discussion with the pathologist. For BI-RADS 5 lesions, any non-malignant percutaneous tissue diagnosis is automatically considered discordant and requires a repeat (usually surgical) biopsy. 

• Manage discordant biopsy results with surgical excision

Recommend surgical excision or repeat biopsy for lesions with imaging-pathology discordance, where benign pathology does not explain the findings. Malignancy rates in discordant lesions vary widely (approximately 7%-24%) depending on the study population, biopsy technique and BI-RADs category. Record the concordance assessment and recommended action in the medical record and use tracking systems to confirm completion of excision.

• Track BI-RADS 3 and BI-RADS 0 assessments

Implement reliable monitoring systems to ensure appropriate follow-up. Patients with BI-RADS 0 assessments should receive timely diagnostic resolution. When prior imaging is needed for comparison, obtain and review prior exams promptly and issue an addendum with a final BI-RADS assessment. For BI-RADS 0 assessments requiring additional imaging, timely diagnostic follow-up is recommended; while no universal mandatory timeline exists, attention to minimizing delays is important. Patients with BI-RADS 3 should complete follow-ups at 6, 12 and 24 months. Create an addendum in the record when obtaining previous exams for comparison and assigning a BI-RADS 0 assessment. Document tracking activities and confirm follow-up completion.
• Ensure timely diagnostic follow-up after abnormal screening

Monitor and address delays in diagnostic follow-up after an abnormal breast screening. Implement tracking systems to ensure completion of recommended diagnostic workups and biopsies. Contact patients who miss follow-up and document outreach attempts.

ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR
Inappropriate Professional Conduct
Contributing action or omission: Disagreements between radiologists and referring clinicians or pathologists—particularly regarding biopsy recommendations, concordance assessments or management of discordant results—can lead to communication breakdowns, delayed care and increased liability exposure when not handled professionally.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support professional collaboration and constructive resolution of clinical disagreements.

• Engage in direct collegial communication for clinical differences

Address disagreements about imaging interpretation, biopsy recommendations or concordance assessments by contacting the referring clinician or pathologist directly by phone or secure messaging. Avoid confrontational language in reports or electronic communications. Frame discussions around patient safety and shared decision-making rather than blame.

• Participate in multidisciplinary breast conferences

Attend and present cases at interprofessional breast tumor boards or radiology-pathology correlation conferences. Use these forums to discuss discordant cases, resolve diagnostic uncertainty and establish consensus management plans. Document conference discussions and recommendations in the medical record.

• Document clinical reasoning when recommendations differ
Record the imaging rationale in the report when imaging findings or recommendations differ from clinical expectations, such as recommending a biopsy for a clinically benign-appearing lesion or recommending against a biopsy when the clinician suspects malignancy. Offer to discuss the case directly with the referring clinician. This approach demonstrates professionalism and supports defensibility. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Incomplete or ambiguous radiology reports—such as failing to document breast density, provide clear BI-RADS assessments and management recommendations or record concordance assessments—compromise continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support standardized documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Use standardized BI-RADS reporting structure for all breast imaging

Structure mammography, ultrasound and MRI reports using the ACR BI-RADS format, including indication, breast composition/density, findings described with BI-RADS lexicon, final assessment category (0-6) and management recommendations. FDA Quality Mammography Standards require that mammography results be reported using the Final Assessment categories. Consistent use of BI-RADS terminology helps radiologists effectively communicate findings, estimate malignancy risk and provide clear management recommendations.

• Include breast density in every mammography report

Document breast density assessment, including fatty, scattered fibroglandular, heterogeneously dense or extremely dense, in mammography reports. Federal regulations require notifying patients about breast density. Include supplemental imaging recommendations based on density and risk factors. Documentation demonstrates compliance with regulatory requirements and supports appropriate patient counseling.

• Document concordance after every biopsy

After reviewing biopsy pathology results, record the concordance assessment in the medical record, including imaging findings, pathology diagnosis, concordance determination (concordant or discordant) and recommended action. 
• Follow institutional policies regarding documentation of AI tool use 

Be aware of policies in your institution or practice that govern documentation regarding AI. Documentation and disclosure requirements may vary by clinical setting and regulatory jurisdiction.

TITLE: CVA
RISK_BRIEF:
Cerebrovascular Accident
SPECIALTY: Diagnostic Radiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Suspected Acute Stroke, TIA, Neurological Deficit

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Cerebrovascular Accident (Missed or Delayed Diagnosis)
Cerebrovascular accidents are a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in diagnostic radiology.

Mitigating Your Risk

With systematic image interpretation, timely communication of critical findings and complete, defensible documentation, diagnostic radiologists can mitigate the risks associated with cerebrovascular accident and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures associated with malpractice claims for cerebrovascular accidents and provides strategies to help prevent them.
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns 
Malpractice claims involving cerebrovascular accidents stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 93% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 7%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.  
Actions and Omissions Driving Cerebrovascular Accident Risk [insert chart] 
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act on Diagnostic Finding
Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify early ischemic changes, large vessel occlusion, intracranial hemorrhage or posterior circulation infarcts increases the risk of delayed treatment and adverse patient outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve detection of acute cerebrovascular pathology and reduce interpretive errors.

• Evaluate for large vessel occlusion on all stroke protocol imaging

Systematically evaluate the internal carotid arteries, M1 and M2 segments of the middle cerebral arteries, basilar artery and vertebral arteries. Document the presence or absence of LVO in the report, as this finding directly impacts treatment decisions. Rapid identification is critical for determining whether thrombectomy is appropriate. 
• Acknowledge non-contrast CT limitations for early ischemic stroke

Understand that non-contrast head CT within 3 hours of symptom onset has a sensitivity of approximately 47%-53% for acute ischemic stroke but remains highly sensitive for intracranial hemorrhage. Include sensitivity limitations in reports for patients with stroke symptoms, emphasizing that a negative NCCT does not rule out acute ischemic stroke. Highlighting these limitations supports defensible clinical decisions.

• Apply systematic search patterns for commonly missed findings

Use a structured search to review high-risk anatomic regions, including the insular ribbon, basal ganglia, posterior fossa and cerebral convexities. In neuroradiology, commonly missed cerebrovascular findings include acute/subacute infarcts, aneurysms and subdural hematomas. Diagnostic errors occur more frequently during high-volume shifts, and most are perceptual not interpretive. A systematic interpretation lowers perceptual errors and reflects diligent practice.
• Stay alert for posterior circulation stroke

Recommend an MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging if clinical history suggests posterior circulation symptoms (vertigo, ataxia, diplopia, dysarthria) and CT findings are negative. Recognize CT’s limited sensitivity in the posterior fossa due to beam-hardening artifacts from the temporal bones. Posterior circulation strokes, which account for 20%-25% of all ischemic strokes, are often overlooked on CT but reliably detected by MRI with DWI. 
• Be aware of emerging AI-assisted detection tools 

Consider leveraging FDA-approved AI tools (such as RapidAI, Viz.ai, Aidoc) for automated ASPECTS scoring, hemorrhage detection, LVO identification and core/penumbra quantification, which often precede formal radiology reports. Note that these are emerging tools that may help support prompt triage but do not replace formal radiologist interpretation. Follow institutional policies regarding documentation of AI use.

CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing
Contributing action or omission: Failing to recommend an appropriate or additional imaging modality when initial studies are insufficient increases the risk of a missed stroke diagnosis.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that imaging recommendations align with current guidelines and clinical context.

• Recommend MRI with DWI when CT is negative in patients with stroke symptoms

Proceed with a brain MRI when no infarct is detected on the initial head CT with, given CT’s low sensitivity. AHA/ASA guidelines recommend that patients with suspected TIA be evaluated within 24 hours of symptom onset and prefer MRI over CT. When interpreting a negative NCCT in a patient with stroke symptoms, include a recommendation for MRI with DWI. This demonstrates awareness of modality limitations and supports appropriate clinical decision-making.
• Ensure that vascular imaging is obtained for suspected large vessel occlusion

Get a CTA or MRA of the head and neck for patients with severe deficits possibly caused by large vessel occlusions. These imaging modalities help identify occlusion location and determine eligibility for mechanical thrombectomy within 24 hours of symptom onset. If vascular imaging was not included in the initial order but clinical indication suggests LVO, recommend a CTA head and neck in the report. Rapid vascular imaging is essential for guiding interventional workup. 

• Recommend advanced imaging when appropriate for extended window treatment decisions

Do not wait: Advanced imaging is essential for patient selection beyond the initial 6-hour window. The DEFUSE-3 trial established perfusion mismatch-based selection for the 6- to 16-hour window, while the DAWN trial used clinical-core mismatch criteria for the 6- to 24-hour window. For patients presenting beyond 6 hours with suspected LVO who may be thrombectomy candidates, recommend perfusion imaging or other advanced imaging (MRI with DWI) if not already obtained. Document the clinical rationale for extended-window intervention.
• Address imaging limitations in the report when a clinical-radiological mismatch exists

Recognize when imaging findings and clinical presentations do not match, such as a negative CT in a patient with dense hemiplegia. In these cases, recommend additional imaging. A clinical-radiological mismatch may indicate early stroke, posterior circulation pathology or stroke mimic. Documenting awareness and suggesting follow-up imaging demonstrates clinical reasoning and supports defensibility.

CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Medication Error
Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify and report imaging findings relevant to thrombolysis or thrombectomy eligibility increases the risk that treatment decisions are based on incomplete information.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that imaging interpretation supports appropriate treatment decisions.

• Report the presence or absence of hemorrhage 

State in the impression whether hemorrhage is present or absent, with clear, unequivocal language, such as "No evidence of intracranial hemorrhage.” Intracranial hemorrhage is an absolute contraindication to thrombolytic therapy, and because current AHA/ASA guidelines support thrombolysis eligibility extending up to 9 hours from onset in selected patients with perfusion-based evidence of salvageable tissue, the radiologist’s role in excluding hemorrhage applies across all eligible treatment windows. This explicit statement supports rapid treatment decisions and demonstrates awareness of clinical implications.

• Report ASPECTS score and early ischemic changes for thrombectomy eligibility

Include the ASPECTS score when interpreting a stroke protocol CT, particularly when early ischemic changes are present, and document the regions involved. The 2026 AHA/ASA guidelines recommend EVT as follows: for patients with ASPECTS 6–10 presenting within 6 hours with NIHSS ≥6 and prestroke mRS 0–1 (Class I); for patients with ASPECTS 3–5 within 6 hours meeting the same criteria (Class I, based on large-core trial evidence); and for selected patients with ASPECTS 3–5 in the 6- to 24-hour window who are age <80, NIHSS ≥6, prestroke mRS 0–1, and without significant mass effect on imaging (Class I). EVT may also be reasonable for ASPECTS 0–2 within 6 hours in selected patients (Class 2a). Accurate ASPECTS reporting across the full range (0-10) is critical, as treatment eligibility now extends well beyond the traditional threshold of ASPECTS > 5. 

• Quantify core and penumbra volumes when perfusion imaging is performed

Report automated software measurements of core volume, penumbra volume and mismatch ratio when interpreting perfusion studies. The DEFUSE-3 trial used perfusion mismatch criteria (core volume <70 mL, mismatch ratio ≥1.8, and absolute mismatch volume ≥15 mL) to select patients for thrombectomy in the 6- to 16-hour window. The DAWN trial used clinical-core mismatch criteria stratified by age, NIHSS severity and core volume thresholds (ranging from <21 mL to <51 mL) for the 6- to 24-hour window. 2026 AHA/ASA guidelines incorporate these criteria for extended-window treatment decisions. Automated perfusion software can misclassify core and penumbra volumes due to patient motion, poor contrast bolus or incomplete brain coverage; document any technical limitations that could affect measurement accuracy. Quantitative reporting supports evidence-based treatment decisions and demonstrates adherence to trial-based selection criteria.

• Identify findings that may alter treatment risk-benefit calculations

Report imaging findings, such as large established infarcts, extensive white matter disease, microhemorrhages and mass lesions that may influence treatment decisions even when not absolute contraindications. Note their potential clinical significance when appropriate. Reporting supports informed clinical decision-making and demonstrates image evaluation.

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Failure to communicate critical stroke findings in a timely manner, unclear escalation pathways or inadequate handoff of urgent findings increase the risk of treatment delays and adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely, reliable communication of critical stroke imaging findings.

• Establish and follow tiered communication protocols based on finding urgency

Apply a three-tier stratification system (critical, urgent and incidental) based on time sensitivity and severity to address actionable findings. The ACR Actionable Reporting Work Group recommends that communication timelines be customized based on institutional protocols and clinical context. As a general framework, critical findings such as LVO and acute hemorrhage warrant communication within minutes; urgent findings like new infarcts without LVO within hours; and significant incidental findings within days. Adhere to institutional protocols for each tier.
• Communicate critical stroke findings directly to the responsible provider

Deliver acute stroke findings requiring rapid intervention through direct, synchronous physician-to-physician communication. Do not rely solely on report availability in the EHR for critical findings. Escalate per institutional protocols when the responsible provider is not reachable. Sharing critical test results is a Joint Commission National Patient Safety Goal and a major contributor to radiology malpractice claims. Direct communication ensures that the treating team can act promptly on time-sensitive information.
• Be aware of emerging AI-enabled notification systems as adjuncts to direct communication
Recognize that AI platforms can expedite communication of critical findings but should supplement, not replace, direct interaction by the radiologist. When AI-generated alerts are sent, ensure that formal follow-up communication from the radiologist occurs, as AI notifications do not fulfill the radiologist's responsibility for direct personal communication of critical findings.
• Document communication in the radiology report

Create an audit trail in the finalized radiology report, as part of the critical test result communication. Record key details, including when communication occurred, who received the findings, the method used and acknowledgment of receipt. 
Sample documentation: "Critical findings communicated to Dr. [Name], ED attending, via telephone at [time]. Dr. [Name] acknowledged receipt and understanding of findings."



ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of imaging findings, clinical reasoning, modality limitations or communication of results compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support documentation that reinforces clinical decision-making and provides a defensible record.

• Use reporting templates for stroke imaging

Implement structured reporting to enhance the completeness and consistency of radiology reports. For stroke protocol imaging, templates should include the presence or absence of hemorrhage, early ischemic changes with ASPECTS score, presence or absence of LVO with location, perfusion parameters (if applicable), comparison with prior imaging and recommendations. These templates ensure that critical elements are not omitted and help treating clinicians to quickly extract essential information. 
• Document the clinical indication and relevant history

Add key details in the report, such as symptom onset time, last known well and presenting symptoms. This information adds context the interpretation and shows that findings were clinically correlated. Document whether clinical information is limited or unavailable and how that may affect interpretation.
• Document modality limitations when relevant

Acknowledge the reduced sensitivity of CT for early ischemic changes, particularly in the posterior fossa, when interpreting NCCT in patients with stroke symptoms. 
Sample documentation: "Note: Noncontrast CT has limited sensitivity for hyperacute ischemic stroke. If clinical suspicion remains high despite a negative CT, MRI with DWI is recommended."
• Provide clear, actionable recommendations

Include recommendations for additional imaging, clinical correlation or follow-up when appropriate. Avoid vague language; instead of "clinical correlation recommended," specify what clinical information would be helpful or what additional imaging should be considered. Clear recommendations support appropriate clinical action and demonstrate clinical reasoning.

• Follow institutional policies regarding documentation of AI tool use
Be aware of policies in your institution or practice that govern documentation regarding AI. Documentation and disclosure requirements may vary by clinical setting and regulatory jurisdiction.

TITLE: Lung cancer
RISK_BRIEF:
Lung Cancer

SPECIALTY: Diagnostic Radiology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Pulmonary Nodules, Lung Cancer Screening, Incidental Chest Findings

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Delayed Lung Cancer Diagnosis, Missed Malignancy, Advanced-Stage Disease at Presentation

Lung cancer is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in diagnostic radiology.   

Mitigating Your Risk

With systematic image review, guideline concordant recommendations and thorough documentation, diagnostic radiologists can mitigate the risks associated with lung cancer and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for lung cancer in diagnostic radiology and provides strategies to help prevent them.  
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving lung cancer in diagnostic radiology typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 67% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 33%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate. 
Actions and Omissions Driving Lung Cancer Risk in Diagnostic Radiology [insert chart] 
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act On Diagnostic Finding
Contributing action or omission: Failure to detect pulmonary nodules or interpret nodule characteristics accurately drives lung cancer-related malpractice claims in radiology.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve nodule detection, reduce interpretation errors and support accurate characterization of suspicious findings.

• Use systematic search patterns and review all lung windows

Systematically review images on lung windows, as nodule size is underestimated on soft-tissue windows, and some nodules—particularly nonsolid (ground-glass) and small nodules—may not appear on mediastinal windows. This approach reduces detection errors caused by satisfaction of search or incomplete image review.
• Scrutinize high-risk anatomic locations

Focus on areas where lung cancers are commonly missed, such as the lower lobes, endobronchial locations, perihilar regions and areas obscured by overlapping structures like pulmonary vessels, the diaphragm and mediastinum. In patients with interstitial lung disease, fibrotic changes can mask nodules, leading to diagnostic delays averaging over 3 years. Documenting the review of these challenging areas strengthens defensibility.

• Recognize atypical lung cancer presentations

Be aware that lung cancer can mimic benign conditions like pneumonia, lung abscesses, postinfectious scarring, atelectasis and emphysema. Pneumonic-type adenocarcinoma often resembles infectious pneumonia and requires careful CT evaluation. Lung cancers with air lucency may appear as cysts, cavitary lesions or bullous changes. Recommend follow-up imaging or tissue sampling, when consolidation or ground-glass opacity persists despite treatment or lacks typical infectious features. This vigilance reduces the chance of misclassifying a malignancy as a benign disease.
• Compare with prior imaging when available
Request and review previous studies, as nodule change or stability is the most important radiologic factor in evaluation. Document comparisons and interval changes in nodule size, density or morphology. For screen-detected nodules, Lung-RADS v2022 and the NCCN Lung Cancer Screening Guidelines define growth as an increase of 1.5 mm in mean diameter for solid nodules, or >1.5 mm in the solid component for part-solid nodules. For incidentally detected nodules, the Mazzone & Lam JAMA 2022 review defines a meaningful change in size as an increase of ≥2 mm in mean diameter. Note that Fleischner Society 2017 guidelines do not specify a numeric growth threshold for incidental nodules. Failing to compare prior imaging with current studies deviates from standard practices and increases the risk of missing interval growth.

• Be aware of emerging AI-assisted detection tools

Consider leveraging FDA-cleared AI-based computer-aided detection (CAD) systems as second readers, when available, to improve nodule detection sensitivity. AI systems should serve as a second opinion, not replace a radiologist’s judgment, as prospective validation data are still limited and most models have not undergone multi-institutional validation in diverse clinical populations. Radiologists should review AI results, ensure clinical correlation and document findings. When AI identifies a finding that the radiologist determines is a false positive, document the rationale for the override.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing
Contributing action or omission: Failing to recommend further evaluation for detected nodules or apply guideline-concordant management recommendations increases the risk of delayed lung cancer diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that appropriate follow-up recommendations are provided and documented for all actionable findings.

• Recognize the limitations of chest radiographs for lung cancer detection
Recommend a CT when clinical suspicion for lung cancer exists or when a patient meets screening criteria, rather than relying on negative chest radiograph findings. Sensitivity for detecting lung nodules under 1 cm is poor, and a substantial number of lung cancers, particularly small nodules, may be missed on chest radiography. Document modality limitations when appropriate.
• Implement reporting systems for lung cancer screening

Use Lung-RADS categories for all lung cancer screening LDCT exams to classify nodules and guide management recommendations. Adhere to established protocols in reporting follow-up recommendations for Lung-RADS findings. 
• Apply Fleischner Society guidelines for incidental nodules

Use the 2017 guidelines to manage incidentally detected nodules outside of screening programs.  Recommendations vary based on nodule size, number, density (solid vs. subsolid) and patient risk factors. Part-solid nodules carry higher malignancy rates than solid nodules of the same size and require distinct follow-up intervals. Radiologists should apply the appropriate Fleischner pathway and document the specific guideline category and rationale for the recommended management.
Note: Fleischner Society guidelines do not apply to patients with a known primary cancer, immunocompromised patients or patients younger than 35 years, who may require more aggressive evaluation.
• Provide actionable follow-up recommendations

Include guidance in the report impression for managing nodules requiring surveillance or further evaluation, such as imaging type, timing and clinical indication. Avoid vague language like "clinical correlation recommended.” Clear recommendations support appropriate follow-up and show the radiologist communicated findings requiring action. 

CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care

Contributing action or omission: Failing to recommend multidisciplinary evaluation for suspicious nodules or arrange for specialist referral delays diagnosis and treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate escalation of care for nodules with concerning features.

• Recommend multidisciplinary evaluation for suspicious nodules

Request evaluation of nodules with a high probability of malignancy or complex clinical features by specialists in thoracic radiology, pulmonary medicine and thoracic surgery. Document this recommendation in the radiology report. Multidisciplinary nodule programs improve diagnostic accuracy and ensure that patients receive guideline-concordant care. Risk calculators (Mayo, Brock, Herder models) can assist with probability determination, but they do not replace multidisciplinary evaluation. 

• Recommend appropriate evaluation for highly suspicious lesions

For nodules classified as Lung-RADS 4A, 4B or 4X, recommend the category-appropriate level of diagnostic evaluation per Lung-RADS v2022 management recommendations, ranging from short-interval follow-up to tissue sampling or surgical evaluation. For nodules with a high pretest probability of malignancy based on validated risk models, the CHEST guideline and recent reviews support proceeding directly to definitive management rather than continued surveillance in appropriate candidates. Document the findings supporting the recommendation, including size, morphology, growth and FDG avidity. Ensure that tissue samples, when obtained, are sufficient for both histology and molecular testing. 

• Communicate directly for findings requiring immediate action
Contact the ordering provider or their designee when imaging reveals findings highly suspicious for malignancy or requiring urgent intervention, such as airway compromise or superior vena cava syndrome. Generate a report and document the communication, including the time, recipient and method. This closed-loop process enables prompt action on critical findings.

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Communication failures—including inadequate reporting of findings, failure to directly communicate critical or actionable results and unclear follow-up recommendations—increase the risk of delayed diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and ensure that actionable findings reach the appropriate providers.

• Provide clear recommendations in the report impression

Place key findings and follow-up actions in the report impression, not the body. Use unambiguous language to specify actions and timelines and avoid hedging that hides the clinical significance of findings. Clear guidance is more likely to improve follow-up and strengthen defensibility in litigation. 
• Implement tiered critical findings and communication protocols

Define urgency levels with specific communication requirements for radiology findings. Use direct synchronous communication for critical findings (such as new masses highly suspicious for malignancy), documenting the recipient and time. Urgent findings (such as nodules requiring short-interval follow-up) may allow asynchronous communication with acknowledgment tracking. Record all communications in the radiology report or addendum.
• Establish escalation pathways when providers cannot be reached

Define and follow escalation procedures when the ordering provider cannot be reached for critical findings. This may include contacting covering physicians, department supervisors or hospital operators. Document escalation attempts and outcomes. The communication process should not end until an appropriate provider acknowledges receipt of the information.
• Use structured reporting to ensure completeness

Develop templates for lung cancer screening and nodule evaluation that include location, size (mean diameter), density classification, Lung-RADS or Fleischner category, follow-up recommendations and comparison with prior imaging. This approach reduces omissions and improves guideline adherence. Refer to the ACR-STR Practice Parameter for required reporting elements. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUE
Failure of Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure
Contributing action or omission: Lack of systematic nodule tracking and follow-up processes and protocols undermines consistent care delivery and increases the risk of patients being lost to follow-up.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize processes and ensure that patients with actionable findings complete follow-up.
• Ensure technical adequacy of imaging protocols

Create CT acquisition and reconstruction protocols to improve nodule detection and characterization. Use contiguous thin sections (≤1.5 mm) and reconstructed multiplanar images. For lung cancer screening, maintain CT dose index volume (CTDIvol) ≤3 mGy for average-sized patients. Standardization reduces measurement errors and improves comparison accuracy.
• Develop protocols and order sets for lung nodule management

Create institutional standards for lung nodule management based on current guidelines (Lung-RADS, Fleischner Society, NCCN). Implement EHR-based order sets to facilitate timely, appropriate follow-up imaging with correct technique specifications. Consider automated alerts to flag patients with nodules requiring follow-up who have not completed recommended imaging.
• Implement nodule tracking and navigation systems

Establish tracking tools to ensure that patients with screen-detected or incidental lung nodules complete guideline-based follow-up. These systems should capture all patients with actionable nodules, monitor completion of recommended follow-up imaging and generate alerts for overdue exams. Include patient outreach for missed appointments. NCCN recommends combining tracking tools with navigation processes for all lung cancer screening programs. 
• Conduct regular quality improvement reviews

Assess nodule management outcomes, such as guideline-based follow-up rates, time to diagnosis for screen-detected cancers and delayed diagnosis cases. Use findings to identify system failures and make improvements. Participation in quality registries like the ACR Lung Cancer Screening Registry supports benchmarking and ongoing progress.


ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR
Failure to Recognize Responsibility
Contributing action or omission: Failing to take ownership of follow-up or inadequate documentation of clinical reasoning increases the risk of patient harm and liability exposure.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that radiologists fulfill their professional responsibilities for findings requiring action.

• Maintain competency in current guidelines and imaging techniques

Keep up with evolving policies for lung nodule management, lung cancer screening criteria and imaging techniques. Today’s recommendations have changed significantly, favoring longer surveillance intervals and higher intervention thresholds than older guidelines. 
• Document clinical reasoning for management recommendations

Record factors like size, morphology, stability, patient risks and guideline category when recommending surveillance over immediate nodule evaluation. When advising against routine follow-up for small nodules, note that the decision aligns with current guidelines. Documenting these details shows sound clinical judgment and supports defensibility.
• Address discrepancies between preliminary and final interpretations

Establish processes to identify and communicate significant differences between preliminary and final attending interpretations. When a discrepancy could affect patient management, directly contact the ordering provider and document the communication. This is especially critical in teleradiology and after-hours settings where preliminary reads may guide initial management.
• Take responsibility for ensuring that critical findings are communicated

Do not rely solely on written reports to fulfill the duty to communicate critical or urgent findings. For actionable findings, confirm that the appropriate provider has received and acknowledged the information. If confirmation is not obtained, escalate through defined pathways and document all attempts.
