TITLE: Acute myocardial infarction
RISK_BRIEF:
Acute Myocardial Infarction
SPECIALTY: Emergency Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Chest Pain, Dyspnea, Suspected Acute Coronary Syndrome

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Acute Myocardial Infarction

Acute myocardial infarction is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in emergency medicine. 
 
Mitigating Your Risk
With vigilant diagnostic assessment, timely reperfusion, standardized communication protocols and thorough documentation, emergency medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with acute myocardial infarction and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for acute myocardial infarction and provides strategies to help prevent them.
 
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
 
Malpractice claims involving acute myocardial infarction typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 83% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 17%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Acute MI Risk [insert chart] 

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Incomplete history-taking or physical examination leads to failure to recognize high-risk features or atypical presentations of AMI.

Mitigation Strategies
The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate triage and initial assessment, recognition of high-risk populations and identification of atypical presentations that may otherwise be missed.
Identify high-risk patient populations requiring heightened vigilance

During triage and initial assessment, flag patients at increased risk for atypical presentations: women (especially those 55 years and older), older adults, patients with diabetes, chronic kidney disease or prior stroke/heart failure. Women report chest pain and diaphoresis less often, with back pain, jaw pain, epigastric pain and lightheadedness being more common. Documentation of risk factors and symptom assessment in these populations demonstrates adherence to the standard of care.
Systematically elicit ischemic symptoms beyond chest pain

Obtain a focused cardiac history at initial presentation that includes not only chest discomfort but also dyspnea, diaphoresis, nausea, jaw/neck/arm pain and unexplained fatigue. About 40% of men and 48% of women with ACS present with nonspecific symptoms such as dyspnea, either alone or with chest pain. Recognizing these presentations prevents anchoring on noncardiac diagnoses and supports defensibility.
Compare current presentation to prior cardiac history

Obtain and document previous cardiac history, including previous MI, revascularization (PCI/CABG), known coronary artery disease and prior ECGs when available. Comparing the current ECG to baseline is essential for identifying new ischemic changes, particularly in patients with chronic ST-T abnormalities. This practice reduces missed diagnoses and demonstrates diligent evaluation.

Perform targeted cardiovascular physical examination

At initial evaluation, assess vital signs (including bilateral blood pressures if aortic dissection is considered), jugular venous distension, lung auscultation for rales, cardiac auscultation for murmurs or S3 and peripheral perfusion. Physical examination findings inform risk stratification (for example, Killip class) and help identify cardiogenic shock or mechanical complications requiring immediate intervention. Documenting pertinent positives and negatives supports clinical reasoning.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain timely ECG, serial troponins or supplemental leads results in missed or delayed AMI diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies
The strategies below work together to ensure timely acquisition and appropriate interpretation of diagnostic studies, reducing the risk of missed or delayed AMI diagnosis.
Obtain and interpret ECG within 10 minutes of presentation

Acquire a 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of first medical contact for all patients with suspected ACS (Class I recommendation). An initial nondiagnostic ECG does not exclude ACS; 11% of patients ultimately diagnosed with STEMI have an initial nondiagnostic ECG. Timely ECG acquisition is a core quality measure, and failure to meet this standard is difficult to defend.
Perform serial ECGs when initial ECG is nondiagnostic

Repeat ECG at 15-minute to 30-minute intervals during the initial 1–2 hours for patients with persistent symptoms, high clinical suspicion of MI or clinical deterioration. Dynamic ECG changes may reveal evolving STEMI or ischemia not seen on the initial tracing. Document the rationale for serial ECGs and findings to demonstrate ongoing reassessment.
Obtain posterior and right-sided leads when clinically indicated

Acquire posterior leads (V7–V9) in patients with isolated ST-segment depression in V1–V3 or suspected left circumflex occlusion. Obtain right-sided leads (V3R, V4R) in patients with inferior STEMI to evaluate for right ventricular involvement. Posterior MI is often missed because the posterior wall is not visualized on standard 12-lead ECG; up to 20% of patients initially diagnosed with NSTEMI are later found to have posterior transmural infarction. Failure to obtain supplemental leads when indicated represents a deviation from the standard of care.
Order serial high-sensitivity cardiac troponin measurements

Measure hs-cTn at presentation and again at 1–2 hours (or 3–6 hours for conventional assays) to detect or exclude myocardial injury. A single hs-cTn below the limit of detection (5 ng/L for many assays) in patients presenting ≥3 hours after symptom onset can reliably rule out MI with sensitivity >99%. Facilities that have not yet transitioned to hs-cTn assays should use conventional troponin with 0/3–6 hour serial measurement protocols and validated risk scores (e.g., HEART) for risk stratification. Document troponin timing relative to symptom onset and the rationale for serial testing or early rule-out.
Obtain urgent echocardiography for hemodynamically unstable patients

Order urgent echocardiography (including point-of-care ultrasound by trained clinicians) for patients with cardiogenic shock, hemodynamic instability or suspected mechanical complications. Echocardiography identifies wall motion abnormalities, mechanical complications and alternative diagnoses. Document findings and their impact on management decisions.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Delays in reperfusion therapy or failure to activate appropriate resources result in preventable myocardial damage and adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies
The strategies below work together to ensure timely reperfusion, appropriate escalation and adherence to time-based quality metrics that directly impact patient outcomes.
Activate cardiac catheterization laboratory immediately upon STEMI diagnosis

For patients with STEMI, activate the catheterization laboratory immediately upon ECG diagnosis with a system goal of first medical contact–to-device time ≤90 minutes. Early advance notification by EMS and immediate activation upon ED arrival reduces time to reperfusion and improves survival. Document time of ECG acquisition, STEMI recognition and catheterization laboratory activation.
Administer fibrinolytic therapy when PCI is not available within 120 minutes

If primary PCI cannot be achieved within 120 minutes of first medical contact, administer fibrinolytic therapy (tenecteplase, alteplase or reteplase) within 30 minutes of arrival for eligible patients, followed by transfer to a PCI-capable facility. Document contraindication assessment and rationale for fibrinolytic administration or deferral.
Recognize and treat STEMI equivalents with the same urgency as classic STEMI
Treat patients with posterior MI (ST depression V1–V3 with ST elevation on posterior leads), de Winter's sign (upsloping ST depression with tall T waves in precordial leads) and hyperacute T waves as STEMI equivalents requiring emergent angiography. Delayed recognition of these patterns leads to delayed reperfusion and worse outcomes.
Apply validated clinical decision pathways for risk stratification
Use validated tools, such as the HEART Pathway, EDACS or ESC 0/1-hour algorithm, to stratify patients into low, intermediate or high-risk categories. A HEART score ≤3, combined with nonischemic ECG and serial troponins 99th percentile, identifies approximately 30% of patients as low risk with 30-day death/MI rate of 0.4%. Structured risk stratification supports safe discharge decisions and provides documentation of clinical reasoning.




ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR
Failure to Recognize Responsibility

Contributing action or omission: Cognitive biases, premature closure and failure to reconsider the diagnosis contribute to missed AMI.

Mitigation Strategies
The strategies below work together to reduce cognitive bias, ensure equitable care and promote a culture of diagnostic humility.
Avoid premature diagnostic closure

Maintain AMI on the differential diagnosis throughout the ED encounter, particularly when initial ECG and troponin are nondiagnostic. Approximately 41% of NSTE-ACS patients have neither ST-segment depression nor T-wave inversion on initial ECG. Document consideration of ACS and rationale for alternative diagnoses to demonstrate ongoing clinical vigilance.
Recognize and mitigate implicit bias in diagnostic evaluation

Apply standardized diagnostic protocols uniformly regardless of patient demographics. Black patients have consistently higher odds of missed AMI diagnosis (OR 1.18–4.5 across studies), and women age 55 years and older are more likely to be discharged with undiagnosed AMI. 
Reassess patients with persistent or recurrent symptoms

Re-evaluate patients who report ongoing or worsening symptoms during an ED stay, including repeat ECG and troponin measurement. Clinical deterioration should prompt immediate reassessment and escalation. Document reassessment findings and clinical decision-making.




ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUES
Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure

Contributing action or omission: Lack of standardized protocols, inadequate resources or system delays undermine timely AMI diagnosis and treatment.

Mitigation Strategies
The strategies below work together to standardize processes, reduce variability, and ensure consistent delivery of time-sensitive care.
Implement standardized chest pain protocols with embedded clinical decision pathways

Develop and maintain ED protocols that incorporate validated clinical decision pathways (HEART, EDACS, ESC 0/1-hour algorithm) with clear criteria for ECG timing, troponin measurement intervals and disposition decisions. Protocolization reduces ED length of stay by 20–45% and standardizes care delivery. Ensure that protocols are accessible and regularly updated.
Establish EHR-integrated order sets and alerts

Create pre-built order sets for suspected ACS that include ECG, serial troponins at appropriate intervals and risk score calculation. EHR integration reduces cognitive burden and ensures that critical tests are not omitted. Implement automated alerts for abnormal troponin results or ECG findings suggestive of ischemia.
Establish risk-stratified outpatient follow-up protocols
For patients discharged from the ED after evaluation for possible ACS, arrange outpatient follow-up based on risk stratification. Low-risk patients ruled out for MI by a clinical decision pathway (HEART score ≤3 with negative serial troponins) should receive follow-up within 30 days and, if feasible, within 14 days. Intermediate-risk patients (HEART score ≥4) discharged after further observation or negative noninvasive testing should receive cardiology or PCP follow-up within 7 days. Notify the patient's PCP and/or cardiologist at the time of ED discharge to facilitate timely follow-up. Develop standard discharge order sets that embed follow-up referral based on risk category to ensure that this step is not omitted. Where local follow-up access is limited, consider establishing an acute care follow-up clinic or utilizing telehealth-based cardiology visits for post-discharge symptom assessment and risk factor management.
Maintain 24/7 access to cardiac catheterization laboratory activation

Ensure that systems are in place for immediate catheterization laboratory activation, including clear communication pathways between ED, cardiology and interventional teams. For non-PCI-capable facilities, establish transfer agreements and protocols with PCI-capable centers. Track and review door-to-balloon times as a quality metric.
Participate in regional or national AMI registries

Participate in at least one regional or national registry (such as NCDR ACTION Registry) to track outcomes, complications and quality of care for AMI patients. Registry participation supports quality improvement and benchmarking against peer institutions.


ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Provider-to-provider communication failures—including incomplete handoffs, unclear leadership and fragmented information transfer across care transitions—increase the likelihood of diagnostic delays, treatment errors and missed escalation opportunities in AMI care.

Mitigation Strategies
The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes, ensure critical information transfer and support coordinated team response across the AMI care continuum.
• Implement structured handoff protocols at every care transition

Use standardized frameworks such as SBAR or I-PASS for all handoffs involving AMI patients, including EMS-to-ED, ED-to-catheterization laboratory and inpatient transitions. At the EMS-to-ED handoff, verify key details upon arrival, including prehospital medications administered, ECG findings and symptom onset time. Relay confirmed prehospital data immediately to the catheterization laboratory team to facilitate preparation and prevent medication errors (such as duplicate anticoagulation dosing). Structured handoffs reduce miscommunication and care failures by ensuring consistent transfer of critical information. The Joint Commission requires hospitals to maintain a handoff communication process that provides opportunity for discussion between the giver and receiver of patient information. 

• Establish clear leadership and role assignment during AMI emergencies

Designate a team leader at the start of each shift and when an AMI emergency occurs to coordinate care, assign roles and ensure accountability. Define protocols that clearly outline leadership responsibilities during STEMI activations. This should be established at shift start and reinforced when activations occur. Clear leadership reduces confusion during time-critical interventions and ensures that escalation decisions (proceeding to catheterization laboratory, initiating fibrinolysis) are made decisively. 

• Minimize ED dwell time through proactive catheterization laboratory coordination

When immediate transfer to the catheterization laboratory is not feasible, minimize the dwell time in the ED and monitor it as part of quality outcome measures. Direct communication between the ED physician and interventional cardiologist should occur for all STEMI patients. This coordination should begin upon STEMI identification and continue until catheterization laboratory transfer. Reducing ED dwell time through coordinated communication directly impacts door-to-balloon times and patient outcomes. 
• Deliver structured feedback to EMS and referring facilities
Follow up with all EMS agencies transporting patients to the STEMI receiving center within 24 to 48 hours of patient arrival. Invite them to attend multidisciplinary meetings and participate in discussions aimed at improving outcomes. Feedback loops enhance system performance, strengthen communication practices, and uncover opportunities for process improvement.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate patient communication—including failure to use shared decision-making, insufficient discharge education and lack of confirmation of patient understanding—increases the risk of treatment non-adherence, delayed recognition of recurrent symptoms and preventable readmissions.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients and caregivers understand their diagnosis, treatment plan and self-care responsibilities, supporting informed decision-making and reducing post-discharge adverse events.

• Use patient decision aids and shared decision-making for risk communication

For patients with acute chest pain and suspected ACS who are deemed low or intermediate risk by a clinical decision pathway, use patient decision aids such as Chest Pain Choice to facilitate risk communication and shared decision-making regarding the need for admission, observation or discharge with outpatient follow-up. This should occur during ED evaluation once risk stratification is complete. Decision aids increase patient knowledge, engagement and satisfaction while reducing low-value testing and observation unit admissions without increasing adverse events.
 
• Provide comprehensive discharge education covering essential components

Ensure that discharge education addresses: reason for hospitalization (diagnosis, tests, procedural results); tailored lifestyle modifications; medications (purpose, dose, frequency, adverse effects, refill instructions, importance of adherence); symptom management (what to monitor, actions to take if symptoms recur, who to call); return to daily activities (physical activity, sexual activity, work, travel); psychosocial considerations (depression, anxiety); and follow-up care (cardiology appointments, cardiac rehabilitation, additional testing). This should occur before discharge and include written materials. Comprehensive education improves disease-related knowledge, healthy behaviors and medication adherence. 

• Address social determinants of health and barriers to care

Assess and address barriers to obtaining and taking prescribed medications, including referral to pharmacy assistance programs or social work as appropriate. This should occur during discharge planning. Addressing social determinants reduces disparities in post-discharge outcomes and supports medication adherence. 
• Deliver patient-centered discharge communication in preferred language

Provide discharge instructions verbally and in writing in the patient's or caregiver's preferred language and at the appropriate literacy level. Engage in shared decision-making regarding assessment of goals and preferences. This should occur before discharge and be documented in the medical record. Patient-centered communication that respects language preferences and incorporates patient values improves comprehension and adherence to the care plan. 

• Use the teach-back method to confirm understanding of self-care and medications

Assess patient and caregiver capacity for self-care, including secondary prevention, symptom monitoring and medication adherence. Use the teach-back method to confirm understanding of self-care instructions and the treatment regimen. This should occur during discharge education and be documented. Teach-back identifies gaps in understanding before discharge, allowing for targeted re-education and reducing the risk of medication errors and missed warning signs. 



ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Insufficient or delayed documentation compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies
The strategies below work together to create a clear, contemporaneous record that enables continuity of care and demonstrates adherence to the standard of care.
Use standard templates aligned with quality measures

Employ documentation templates that capture required elements for AMI quality measures, including ECG timing, troponin measurement, medication administration and discharge instructions. Templates ensure completeness and facilitate quality reporting.

Example documentation language: "52-year-old woman with diabetes presenting with 2 hours of epigastric discomfort and dyspnea. Given atypical presentation and diabetes, maintained high suspicion for ACS. Initial ECG obtained at 08:12 (within 10 minutes of arrival) showed nonspecific ST-T changes; compared to prior ECG from 2024, no acute changes. Initial hs-cTnI 4 ng/L at 08:15. Serial ECG at 08:45 unchanged. Repeat hs-cTnI at 10:15 was 5 ng/L (delta 3 ng/L). HEART score 3 (low risk). Patient asymptomatic, hemodynamically stable. Discharged with PCP follow-up arranged within 14 days per low-risk protocol; return precautions reviewed using teach-back."
Maintain a clear, auditable timeline of key clinical details 

Record the time of key events: symptom onset (as reported by patient), ECG acquisition and interpretation, troponin collection and results, catheterization laboratory activation and reperfusion therapy. Time-stamped documentation demonstrates adherence to guideline-recommended intervals and supports defensibility.
Document patient-specific factors that influenced evaluation

Record patient-specific factors that may affect presentation or interpretation, such as previous cardiac history, baseline ECG abnormalities, chronic kidney disease and diabetes. Documentation of those factors demonstrates individualized assessment and supports clinical reasoning.
Document clinical reasoning for disposition decisions

Document the rationale for admission, observation or discharge, including risk stratification score (if used), interpretation of ECG and troponin results and consideration of alternative diagnoses. For patients discharged after ACS evaluation, document why the patient was deemed low risk, the specific risk stratification tool and score used, and the follow-up interval arranged based on risk category (for example, 14–30 days for low risk, within 7 days for intermediate risk).

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Chest Pain, Dyspnea, Suspected Acute Coronary Syndrome

Acute myocardial infarction is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in emergency medicine. 
 
Mitigating Your Risk
With vigilant diagnostic assessment, timely reperfusion, standardized communication protocols and thorough documentation, emergency medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with acute myocardial infarction and reduce malpractice exposure. This report

ADVERSE_OUTCOMES: Acute Myocardial Infarction

TITLE: Aortic dissection
RISK_BRIEF:
Aortic Dissection

SPECIALTY: Emergency Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Chest Pain, Back Pain, Syncope, Perfusion Deficit

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Missed or Delayed Diagnosis of Aortic Dissection

Acute aortic dissection is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in emergency medicine.
Mitigating Your Risk
With systematic risk stratification, timely diagnostic imaging and vigilant hemodynamic management, emergency medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with aortic dissection and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for aortic dissection and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving aortic dissection typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 66% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 34%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.

Actions and Omissions Driving Aortic Dissection Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Incomplete history-taking and physical examination lead to failure to recognize high-risk features of aortic dissection. 
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure systematic identification of high-risk features and reduce the likelihood of anchoring on alternative diagnoses.
Apply the Aortic Dissection Detection Risk Score (ADD-RS) systematically at triage and initial evaluation

At initial assessment, screen all patients with acute chest, back or abdominal pain, syncope or perfusion deficit for the 12 high-risk features across three ADD-RS categories:
Predisposing conditions (Marfan syndrome, known aortic disease, family history of aortic disease, recent aortic manipulation, known aortic valve disease)
Pain features (abrupt onset, severe intensity, ripping/tearing quality)
Examination findings (pulse deficit, blood pressure differential >20 mmHg between arms, new aortic regurgitation murmur, hypotension/shock).
Systematic use of the ADD-RS is a Class I recommendation in the 2022 ACC/AHA guidelines and improves detection of acute aortic syndrome (AAS). Documenting the ADD-RS score demonstrates a structured, guideline-concordant approach to risk assessment.
Obtain a targeted vascular and family history

During the initial encounter, ask about history of aortic aneurysm, prior aortic or cardiac surgery, connective tissue disorders, family history of aortic dissection or sudden death and recent cocaine or amphetamine use. These predisposing conditions are core elements of the ADD-RS and directly affect pretest probability. Failure to elicit this history is a common malpractice issue.
Perform bilateral blood pressure measurement and a focused vascular examination

At initial assessment, measure blood pressure in both arms and perform a focused vascular examination, including assessment of peripheral pulses, auscultation for aortic regurgitation and evaluation for signs of malperfusion (limb ischemia, neurologic deficit). A blood pressure differential >20 mmHg between arms or pulse deficit is a high-risk ADD-RS finding. These findings are often absent from documentation in malpractice cases but should significantly raise clinical suspicion when present.
Avoid premature diagnostic closure on more common diagnoses

When evaluating chest or back pain, include aortic dissection in the differential diagnosis before anchoring on acute coronary syndrome, musculoskeletal pain or pulmonary embolism. Acute coronary syndrome is the most common misdiagnosis in patients with AAS. The combination of aortic pain, normal ECG, normal troponins and elevated D-dimer is a warning pattern that should prompt urgent aortic imaging. Maintaining aortic dissection in the differential can help with defensibility in a malpractice case.




CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to order appropriate diagnostic imaging or laboratory testing when aortic dissection is in the differential diagnosis is a leading contributor to malpractice claims. 

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate and timely diagnostic testing when a physician suspects aortic dissection.
Order CTA of the chest, abdomen and pelvis when clinical suspicion warrants definitive imaging

In any patient with an ADD-RS >1 (high risk) or direct signs of AAS on point-of-care ultrasound, proceed directly to CTA without delay. CTA is the diagnostic test of choice for hemodynamically stable patients, with near 100% sensitivity and specificity. The 2021 AHA/ACC Chest Pain Guideline gives a Class I recommendation for CTA when aortic dissection is suspected. Promptly ordering definitive imaging is the most defensible action in evaluating aortic dissection. Facilities without 24/7 CTA capability should maintain a low threshold for emergent transfer when AAS is clinically suspected.
Use the ADD-RS plus D-dimer strategy to safely rule out AAS in low-risk patients

In patients with an ADD-RS of ≤1 (low risk), obtain a highly sensitive D-dimer assay. If the D-dimer is ≤500 ng/mL FEU, AAS can be excluded without advanced imaging per the 2025 ACEP Level B recommendation. This approach has a failure rate of 0.3% in prospective multicenter data (ADvISED study). Applying a validated rule-out strategy reduces unnecessary imaging while providing a defensible, evidence-based framework for decision-making. (Note: This strategy requires a quantitative, highly sensitive D-dimer assay such as ELISA-based or immunoturbidimetric; qualitative or point-of-care assays with different sensitivity profiles may not be interchangeable).
Consider focused cardiac point-of-care ultrasound (FoCUS) as a secondary risk stratification tool

Use FoCUS to identify direct signs of AAS (intimal flap, intramural hematoma, penetrating ulcer) or indirect signs (aortic root dilation ≥4 cm, pericardial effusion, aortic regurgitation) in patients with ADD-RS ≤1. When finding direct signs, bypass D-dimer testing and proceed directly to advanced imaging per the 2025 ACEP Level C recommendation. FoCUS adds diagnostic value and expedites definitive imaging in patients with sonographic findings.
Do not rely on chest X-ray to exclude aortic dissection

Avoid using chest X-ray as a tool to diagnose or exclude AAS due to its lack of sensitivity and specificity. A widened mediastinum is often absent in confirmed dissection cases. While chest X-ray may raise suspicion or suggest alternative diagnoses, a normal result should never rule out aortic dissection. Relying on a normal chest X-ray is a documented source of false reassurance in malpractice cases.

CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Insufficient hemodynamic monitoring and delayed initiation of anti-impulse therapy after diagnosis or during the diagnostic workup increase the risk of dissection propagation, rupture and end-organ malperfusion.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support prompt hemodynamic stabilization, sustained monitoring and timely escalation to definitive care.
Initiate anti-impulse therapy immediately upon clinical suspicion

Upon suspicion of aortic dissection, begin IV beta-blocker therapy (e.g., esmolol, labetalol) targeting a heart rate of 60–80 bpm and systolic blood pressure 120 mmHg, or the lowest BP that maintains adequate end-organ perfusion. Administer beta-blockers before vasodilators to prevent reflex tachycardia. This is a Class I recommendation in the 2022 ACC/AHA guidelines. Early anti-impulse therapy reduces aortic wall stress, limits dissection propagation and demonstrates standard-of-care management even before definitive diagnosis.

Ensure continuous hemodynamic monitoring appropriate to the clinical setting 

Place an arterial line for continuous BP monitoring in an ICU or monitored setting when AAS is diagnosed or strongly suspected. Invasive BP monitoring is a Class I recommendation per 2022 ACC/AHA guidelines, as it allows precise titration and early detection of hemodynamic deterioration. In ED settings where arterial line placement is not immediately feasible, this procedure should not delay initiation of anti-impulse therapy or emergent transfer to a higher level of care for definitive management. 2010 ACCF/AHA guidelines emphasize that interfacility transfer must be carried out efficiently and that the transferring institution is responsible for stabilizing and maintaining blood pressure control throughout transport. When invasive monitoring is unavailable, frequent non-invasive blood pressure measurement with continuous cardiac monitoring should be used to guide therapy titration until arterial line placement can be established at the receiving facility. (Note: No guideline specifies a non-invasive BP cycling interval for AAS; this bridging strategy reflects pragmatic clinical practice).
Perform serial reassessments for signs of malperfusion

After starting medical therapy, perform serial neurovascular exams to assess for new or worsening pulse deficits, neurologic changes or abdominal pain indicating mesenteric or limb ischemia. Malperfusion syndromes can develop or worsen after presentation and significantly increase mortality. Serial reassessments enable early detection of complications that may require surgical or endovascular intervention.
Activate surgical consultation and transfer protocols without delay

Immediately consult cardiovascular surgery upon diagnosis or strong suspicion of type A aortic dissection. If on-site surgical capability is unavailable, initiate emergent transfer to an aortic center while continuing medical management. Pre-arranged transfer agreements with aortic centers minimize transfer time. Surgical mortality for type A dissection has decreased to 18%, while medical management alone carries 57% mortality. Delays between diagnosis and surgery reduce survival.

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Provider-to-provider communication failures—including handoff breakdowns, unclear escalation of clinical concern and inadequate transfer communication—are identified as common contributors to diagnostic delays in aortic dissection cases.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support coordinated team response across care transitions.
Implement closed-loop communication during resuscitation and stabilization

During management of a patient with suspected or confirmed AAS, use closed-loop communication for medication orders, blood pressure targets and procedural interventions. Designate a team leader to coordinate care. Closed-loop communication reduces medication errors and ensures situational awareness in high-acuity, time-sensitive clinical scenario.
Use structured handoff frameworks for all care transitions involving suspected AAS

When transferring care between providers (shift change, ED-to-ICU, ED-to-OR, interfacility transfer), use a standardized handoff framework such as SBAR to communicate the clinical concern for aortic dissection, ADD-RS score, imaging results, current hemodynamic parameters, ongoing anti-impulse therapy and pending surgical consultation status. Communication breakdowns are common in medico-legal cases involving aortic dissection, and structured handoffs promote clear communication of clinical concerns and management.
Communicate clinical urgency when activating surgical consultation or transfer

When contacting cardiovascular surgery or arranging interfacility transfer, clearly state the suspected diagnosis, Stanford classification (if known), hemodynamic status and any signs of malperfusion or complications. Include key imaging findings and current medical management. Explicit communication of urgency helps ensure proper triage by the receiving team and reduces time to definitive intervention.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate communication with patients about the diagnostic evaluation, the significance of symptoms and the importance of return precautions increases the risk of delayed re-presentation and compromises defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients understand their evaluation and know when to seek care.
Communicate diagnostic uncertainty when it exists

If the evaluation is inconclusive or if the patient is being discharged with a low probability of AAS, communicate this uncertainty in plain language. For example: "We have done testing that makes a serious aortic condition unlikely, but if your symptoms return or worsen, come back to the emergency department immediately." Transparent communication about diagnostic uncertainty supports shared decision-making and provides a defensible record of patient education.
Provide explicit return precautions when discharging patients evaluated for chest or back pain

When discharging patients where aortic dissection was considered but ruled out, provide clear verbal and written instructions to return immediately if symptoms recur, worsen or change, especially in the case of new tearing or ripping pain, sudden severe abdominal pain, syncope, weakness or numbness in an extremity. Patients who understand warning signs are more likely to seek timely re-evaluation. Documenting return precautions demonstrates appropriate discharge communication.
Ensure that patients understand the need for follow-up imaging or specialist evaluation

When discharging patients with outpatient follow-up instructions (repeat imaging for indeterminate findings, cardiology referral), clearly explain the timeframe and importance of follow-up. Document the plan in writing. Patients who understand the rationale for follow-up are more likely to complete recommended evaluations.



ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of clinical reasoning, risk stratification, diagnostic decision-making and hemodynamic management compromises continuity of care and defensibility. 

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces clinical reasoning and provides a defensible record.
Document the ADD-RS score and clinical reasoning for the diagnostic approach

Record the ADD-RS score, high-risk features assessed and rationale for the chosen diagnostic pathway. If aortic dissection is considered but advanced imaging is not pursued, document the clinical reasoning for this decision. Explicit documentation of structured risk assessment demonstrates guideline-concordant care and provides a defensible record.

Sample documentation language: "Patient presents with acute-onset severe chest pain radiating to the back. ADD-RS calculated: predisposing conditions = 0, pain features = 1 (abrupt onset, severe intensity), examination findings = 0. ADD-RS = 1 (low risk). D-dimer obtained: 220 ng/mL FEU (500). Per 2025 ACEP clinical policy, AAS excluded without advanced imaging. Patient counseled on return precautions."
Document hemodynamic parameters, interventions and response to treatment

Record serial vital signs, including bilateral blood pressures, heart rate targets, medications (drug, dose, route, time) and patient response. Document the rationale for hemodynamic targets and any therapy adjustments. Comprehensive documentation of hemodynamic management demonstrates standard-of-care treatment and supports continuity during care transitions.
Document all consultations, communications and transfer arrangements

Record the time, content and outcome of all consultant communications, including the consultant’s name, clinical information shared, recommendations received and actions taken. For interfacility transfers, document the accepting physician, facility, mode of transport and clinical status at time of transfer. Documenting consultant communication demonstrates appropriate escalation and care coordination.
Document patient notification, education and discharge instructions

Record the content of patient education, including return precautions, follow-up instructions and the patient's stated understanding of the information. If the patient declines recommended evaluation or leaves against medical advice, document the discussion and risks explained. Documenting patient communication supports defensibility and demonstrates appropriate discharge planning.



ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Patient-related factors, including delayed presentation, atypical symptom perception, leaving before evaluation is complete and failure to follow up on recommended testing, can contribute to diagnostic delays.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient engagement and address barriers to timely diagnosis and follow-up.
Identify and address barriers to completing the diagnostic evaluation

Assess patients for factors that may lead to premature departure from the ED, including long wait times, competing obligations, fear of diagnosis or financial concerns. Communicate the urgency and rationale for the evaluation in progress. Patients who understand the potential severity of their condition are more likely to remain for completion of the workup.

Document patient decisions to decline recommended evaluation

If a patient declines recommended imaging or leaves before evaluation is complete, document the clinical concern communicated, the specific risks discussed (including the possibility of a life-threatening aortic condition), the patient's reasons for declining and any alternative plan offered. Thorough documentation of informed refusal demonstrates appropriate effort to ensure that the patient understood risks.
Provide clear follow-up instructions for patients with uncontrolled hypertension or known aortic disease

For patients with known risk factors for aortic dissection (uncontrolled hypertension, known aortic aneurysm, connective tissue disorder) who present with non-diagnostic evaluations, ensure that they have a clear follow-up plan with their primary care provider or cardiologist. Emphasize the importance of blood pressure control and adherence to surveillance imaging schedules. Connecting patients with longitudinal care reduces the risk of future acute presentations with advanced disease.
OVERVIEW:
Aortic Dissection

SPECIALTY: Emergency Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Chest Pain, Back Pain, Syncope, Perfusion Deficit

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Missed or Delayed Diagnosis of Aortic Dissection

Acute aortic dissection is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in emergency medicine.
Mitigating Your Risk
With systematic risk stratification, timely diagnostic imaging and vigilant hemodynamic management, emergency medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with aortic dissection and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and
PRESENTING_CONDITIONS: Chest Pain, Back Pain, Syncope, Perfusion Deficit
ADVERSE_OUTCOMES: Missed or Delayed Diagnosis of Aortic Dissection

TITLE: Appendicitis
RISK_BRIEF:
Appendicitis

SPECIALTY: Emergency Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Acute Abdominal Pain, Right Lower Quadrant Pain

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Missed or Delayed Diagnosis of Appendicitis
Missed or delayed diagnosis of appendicitis is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in emergency medicine.
Mitigating Your Risk 
With evidence-based diagnostic pathways, appropriate treatment selection, timely surgical consultation and structured documentation and follow-up systems, emergency medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with missed or delayed diagnosis of appendicitis and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for missed or delayed diagnosis of appendicitis and provides strategies to help prevent them. 
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving appendicitis typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 77% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 23%. Any one of these—or a combination—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Missed or Delayed Diagnosis of Appendicitis Risk [insert chart] 

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failure to obtain appropriate laboratory studies or imaging when clinical suspicion for appendicitis exists increases the risk of missed diagnosis and delayed treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate diagnostic evaluation of patients with suspected appendicitis.
Obtain complete laboratory evaluation, including inflammatory markers
During the initial ED evaluation, order a CBC with differential, a CRP and a urinalysis in all patients with suspected appendicitis. Also, obtain pregnancy testing in reproductive-age women. Pregnancy testing is essential to guide imaging selection and exclude ectopic pregnancy. Leukocytosis occurs in 67%–90% of patients with appendicitis. CRP achieves 96% sensitivity for appendiceal perforation.
Apply clinical decision rules to guide imaging decisions
Use validated clinical scoring systems, including AIR score, Alvarado score and Pediatric Appendicitis Score, during initial clinical assessment to risk-stratify patients and guide imaging decisions. Low-risk scores (AIR 4, Alvarado 5) show high sensitivity for ruling out appendicitis. However, clinical scores alone are insufficient to exclude appendicitis in intermediate-risk patients without imaging. The ACEP 2023 Clinical Policy notes that the Alvarado score has insufficient diagnostic accuracy to identify patients who do not need imaging.
Obtain CT imaging in adults with moderate-to-high clinical suspicion
In adults with right lower quadrant pain and clinical features suggestive of appendicitis, obtain a CT of the abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast as the first-line imaging study during the initial ED evaluation. Recommended by the American College of Radiology and ACEP for adults with suspected appendicitis, CT has 91%–96% sensitivity and 90%–94% specificity, reduces negative appendectomy rates and identifies alternative diagnoses.
Use ultrasound as first-line imaging in children and pregnant patients
During the initial ED evaluation, first perform an abdominal ultrasound in pediatric patients and pregnant women with suspected appendicitis. Ultrasound avoids ionizing radiation and delivers reasonable diagnostic accuracy. If the ultrasound yields equivocal or non-diagnostic results, proceed to MRI (preferred in pregnancy) or CT. In pregnancy, MRI achieves 95%–100% sensitivity and 97%–98% specificity for appendicitis. 
Implement serial examinations for equivocal presentations
When the initial evaluation is inconclusive and appendicitis cannot be excluded, implement an observation period with serial abdominal examinations rather than immediate discharge. Use this strategy when clinical suspicion persists despite a negative or equivocal initial workup. Serial examinations achieve 97% sensitivity and 91% specificity when paired with repeat imaging as indicated. Observation and reassessment may be reasonable alternatives to an immediate CT in cases of non-diagnostic ultrasound. 
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failure to select an appropriate treatment pathway, obtain timely surgical consultation, recognize high-risk imaging features or plan discharge appropriately increase the risk of perforation and treatment failure.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate treatment pathway selection, timely surgical intervention when indicated and safe transitions of care. The operative versus non-operative decision framework below assumes on-site or readily available surgical consultation. Facilities without surgical services should prioritize early transfer once appendicitis is diagnosed or strongly suspected (see "Arrange transfer when surgical capability is unavailable" below), initiating antibiotics per institutional protocol and remote surgical guidance when available.
Apply evidence-based criteria when selecting operative versus non-operative management
During the initial ED evaluation, assess CT imaging for high-risk features including appendicolith, appendiceal diameter ≥13 mm and mass effect. Patients with these findings have approximately 40% treatment failure rates with antibiotics alone and should be recommended for surgical management if fit for surgery. In patients without high-risk CT findings and who are fit for surgery, either appendectomy or antibiotics-first management is an acceptable treatment; shared decision-making should guide the choice. This approach matches patients to appropriate interventions and aligns with current guidelines from the World Society of Emergency Surgery and American College of Surgeons. 

Assess surgical fitness and perioperative risk before treatment selection
Before recommending appendectomy, evaluate the patient's fitness for surgery, particularly in elderly patients or those with multiple comorbidities, in collaboration with the internal medicine/hospitalist and surgical teams. Use validated risk assessment tools such as the NSQIP surgical risk calculator or frailty indices to estimate perioperative morbidity and mortality. In patients who are not fit for surgery and lack high-risk CT findings, an antibiotics-first approach should be recommended. In patients who are not fit for surgery, but whose CT findings indicate they are at high risk, weigh the calculated surgical risk against the risk of antibiotic treatment failure and incorporate patient preferences into the decision. Documenting this risk assessment supports informed consent and demonstrates thoughtful clinical decision-making.
Recognize treatment failure in patients returning after antibiotic initiation
Treat patients returning to the ED within 24–72 hours of antibiotic initiation for appendicitis with persistent or worsening symptoms as probable treatment failure, not with extended or restarted antibiotics. Early failure occurs in 8–12% of nonoperatively managed patients. Criteria for conversion to surgery include clinical deterioration, lack of improvement after 24–48 hours, diffuse peritonitis or sepsis. Risk is higher in patients whose index visit showed an appendicolith, fever >38°C, appendiceal diameter ≥15 mm, or CRP ≥100 mg/L. Obtain surgical consultation promptly and document the antibiotic timeline, symptom trajectory and rationale for escalation.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care

Contributing action or omission: Failure to obtain timely surgical consultation or transfer to a facility with surgical capability when appendicitis is suspected increases the risk of delayed definitive management.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that appropriate surgical expertise is engaged.

• Establish clear criteria for surgical consultation
Obtain surgical consultation as soon as appendicitis is identified through imaging. Also, consult with a surgeon when strongly suspecting appendicitis despite inconclusive imaging, when observing clinical deterioration, or when a patient returns with persistent or worsening symptoms after antibiotic treatment. Early surgical involvement supports optimal timing of intervention and shared decision-making regarding operative versus non-operative management. 
• Arrange transfer when surgical capability is unavailable
Immediately arrange transfer to a facility with surgical services when appendicitis is diagnosed or strongly suspected and the presenting facility lacks surgical capability. Also, consider transfer when clinical suspicion persists despite inconclusive imaging; surgical evaluation, including diagnostic laparoscopy, may be both diagnostic and therapeutic. Delays in definitive management increases the risk of perforation and associated morbidity. 
• Communicate clinical uncertainty to consultants
When requesting surgical consultation, clearly communicate the degree of clinical certainty, imaging findings, patient risk factors and concerns about an atypical presentation. Use structured communication frameworks such as SBAR to ensure complete information transfer. Clear communication supports appropriate surgical decision-making and establishes a shared understanding of clinical uncertainty.


ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Failure to accurately or sufficiently document clinical findings, risk stratification and medical decision-making compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to create a comprehensive medical record that demonstrates thorough evaluation, supports safe transition of care and provides defensible documentation of clinical reasoning.

• Document complete abdominal examination findings including pertinent negatives

Record specific examination maneuvers (McBurney sign, Rovsing sign, psoas sign, obturator sign) and their results, including negative findings. Record the presence or absence of rebound tenderness, guarding and peritoneal signs. Pertinent negatives are as important as positive findings in demonstrating a thorough evaluation and supporting clinical decisions.

• Record risk score calculations and clinical reasoning

Document the specific risk stratification score (AIR, AAS or Alvarado), the calculated score and the risk category (low, intermediate, high). Clearly document the clinical reasoning behind the disposition decision, including why appendicitis was considered less likely if the patient is being discharged. 
Example documentation: "AIR score 3 (low risk). Given low-risk stratification, normal inflammatory markers and benign examination, appendicitis is unlikely. Patient discharged with return precautions."

• Document communication with consultants and primary care providers

Record all communications with surgical consultants, including consultation time, clinical details provided and recommendations received. For discharged patients, document communication with or plans for follow-up with primary care. This creates a clear record of care coordination and can help ensure continuity.

• Provide and document explicit discharge instructions with specific return precautions

Document that the patient received written and verbal instructions to return for worsening pain, migration of pain to the right lower quadrant, fever, inability to tolerate oral intake or any clinical deterioration. Specify a follow-up timeframe (24–48 hours) and ensure that instructions are provided in the patient's preferred language. Clear documentation of return precautions demonstrates proper patient education and shared decision-making.


ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUES
Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure
Contributing action or omission: Failure to implement standardized diagnostic pathways and risk stratification protocols leads to inconsistent evaluation and increases the likelihood of missed diagnoses.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize the diagnostic approach, ensure appropriate use of imaging and reduce variability in clinical decision-making across providers and shifts.
• Create EHR-based clinical decision support and order sets

Implement EHR alerts to calculate risk scores when abdominal pain is documented. Create pre-built order sets for appendicitis workup, including appropriate laboratory tests (CBC with differential, CRP, urinalysis, pregnancy test) and imaging. Automated prompts reduce cognitive burden and ensure consistent evaluation across providers.

• Implement validated clinical scoring systems for risk stratification

Use the Appendicitis Inflammatory Response (AIR) score or Adult Appendicitis Score (AAS) for initial evaluation of patients with right lower quadrant pain. Applying these tools standardizes the diagnostic approach, provides defensible documentation of clinical reasoning and helps identify:
Low-risk patients who may not require imaging
Intermediate-risk patients who warrant imaging
High-risk patients who may proceed directly to surgical consultation 
AIR and AAS scores are more accurate than the Alvarado score, which has reduced sensitivity in HIV-positive patients and cannot reliably differentiate complicated from uncomplicated appendicitis in elderly patients. In pregnant patients, physiologic leukocytosis and nausea/vomiting may falsely elevate the score. 

• Develop imaging protocols tailored to patient populations

Establish department protocols for imaging based on patient population:
CT with IV contrast for most adults
Ultrasound as first-line for children and young adults
Ultrasound followed by MRI for pregnant patients when ultrasound is inconclusive
In pregnant patients, ultrasound visualizes the appendix in only 7% of cases, with 18% sensitivity. Standardized imaging pathways reduce diagnostic delays and ensure appropriate modality selection.

• Establish protocols for scheduled re-evaluation of discharged patients

Implement a system for scheduled return visits within 24–30 hours for patients discharged with undifferentiated abdominal pain. Re-evaluation changes diagnosis or treatment in about 22% of patients with non-specific abdominal pain. Elevated CRP (>27 mg/L) at the index visit predicts patients more likely to need follow-up treatment. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS
Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care
Contributing action or omission: Patients who do not return for scheduled re-evaluation or fail to follow discharge instructions increase their risk of delayed diagnosis and adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve patient understanding of warning signs, ensure appropriate follow-up and reduce barriers to return visits.

• Use teach-back method to confirm patient understanding of return precautions

Before discharge, ask patients to explain in their own words the symptoms that should prompt an immediate return to the ED. Confirm understanding of warning signs, such as pain migration to the right lower quadrant, fever and inability to tolerate oral intake. This method identifies gaps in understanding and improves adherence to return instructions.
Patients with language barriers, low health literacy or cognitive impairment need extra attention to ensure comprehension. Use trained medical interpreters, not family members, for non-English speaking patients. 

• Provide written discharge instructions in patient's preferred language

Provide discharge instructions at an appropriate reading level in the patient’s primary language. Include symptoms warranting return, follow-up timeframe, and contact information for questions. Written instructions reinforce verbal education and serve as a post-discharge reference.

• Address barriers to return visits during discharge planning

Identify and document barriers to return (such as transportation, childcare, work obligations) and provide resources or alternatives when possible. For patients with significant follow-up barriers, consider observation or admission. Proactively addressing barriers reduces the risk of delayed return if symptoms worsen.
• Implement callback systems for high-risk discharged patients

For patients discharged with undifferentiated abdominal pain and higher risk for appendicitis (intermediate risk scores, elevated CRP, atypical presentations), implement a nursing callback within 12–24 hours to assess symptom progression. Patients with comorbidities, elderly patients and women of reproductive age have higher rates of missed appendicitis and may benefit from more intensive follow-up protocols. Children also are vulnerable due to communication challenges, atypical presentations and high rates of discharge without laboratory or imaging evaluation. Proactive follow-up identifies patients who are deteriorating but have not yet returned for care.

With evidence-based diagnostic pathways, appropriate treatment selection, timely surgical consultation and structured documentation and follow-up systems, emergency medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with missed or delayed diagnosis of
