TITLE: CVA
RISK_BRIEF:
Cerebrovascular Accident

SPECIALTY: Emergency Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Acute Neurological Deficit, Altered Mental Status, Dizziness/Vertigo, Headache

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Cerebrovascular Accident (Stroke)

Cerebrovascular accident is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in emergency medicine.
Mitigating Your Risk

With timely recognition, guideline-concordant treatment and thorough documentation, emergency medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with cerebrovascular accidents (CVAs, or strokes) and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for strokes and provides strategies to help prevent them.
These strategies are based on AHA/ASA guidelines applicable across all levels of stroke center certification. Where specific resources are unavailable (MRI, CT perfusion, on-site neurology), the corresponding recommendation should be adapted to local capability, with emphasis on telestroke consultation and rapid transfer protocols to higher-level centers.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving strokes typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 83% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 17%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.

Actions and Omissions Driving Stroke Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Incomplete history-taking or physical examination leads to failure to recognize high-risk features, atypical presentations or posterior circulation stroke.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure comprehensive initial assessment, recognition of high-risk populations and identification of atypical presentations that may otherwise be missed.

• Establish precise time of symptom onset or last known well

At initial presentation, determine and document the exact time the patient was last known to be at neurological baseline. That time, not symptom onset, defines eligibility for thrombolysis (within 4.5 hours) and thrombectomy (within 24 hours). Accurate time documentation is essential for treatment decisions and demonstrates adherence to the standard of care.

• Systematically elicit stroke symptoms beyond classic FAST criteria

Obtain a focused neurological history that includes facial droop, arm weakness and speech difficulty as well as dizziness, vertigo, diplopia, visual field changes, ataxia, dysphagia, nausea/vomiting and altered mental status. Patients with stroke misdiagnosis are often FAST-negative (78% vs 22% of correctly diagnosed patients) and present with nonspecific symptoms. Recognizing these presentations prevents anchoring on non-stroke diagnoses.

• Obtain and document vascular risk factors

Obtain and document history of hypertension, diabetes, atrial fibrillation, prior stroke/TIA, coronary artery disease, smoking and hyperlipidemia. Patients with multiple vascular risk factors presenting with atypical symptoms have significantly higher rates of DWI-positive ischemia on MRI. Documentation of risk factors demonstrates individualized assessment and supports clinical reasoning.
• Perform a complete neurological examination including posterior circulation assessment

At initial evaluation, assess cranial nerve function, visual fields, extraocular movements, gait and truncal ataxia, coordination and sensory function in addition to standard motor and speech assessment. The NIHSS has limitations in assessing posterior circulation and nondominant hemisphere strokes. Patients with posterior circulation large vessel occlusion can have an NIHSS score of 0 with symptoms limited to headache, vertigo and nausea.

• Apply the HINTS examination for patients presenting with acute vestibular syndrome

For patients presenting with acute-onset vertigo, nystagmus and gait instability, perform the HINTS examination (Head-Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew). HINTS is more sensitive for cerebellar stroke than early MRI. A central pattern (normal head impulse, direction-changing nystagmus or skew deviation) should prompt urgent stroke evaluation.





CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failure to obtain timely neuroimaging, vascular imaging or appropriate laboratory studies can result in missed or delayed stroke diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely acquisition and appropriate interpretation of diagnostic studies, reducing the risk of missed or delayed stroke diagnosis.
• Obtain and interpret non-contrast CT within 25 minutes of arrival

Acquire non-contrast head CT within 25 minutes of patient presentation for all patients with suspected stroke (AHA/ASA guideline recommendation). The only assessment required before thrombolytic initiation is blood glucose level. Delayed CT imaging (median 4.1 hours vs 1.5 hours) is strongly associated with stroke misdiagnosis.

• Obtain CT angiography for patients with suspected large vessel occlusion

For patients presenting with severe deficits possibly caused by large vessel occlusions, obtain CT angiography of the head and neck to identify occlusion location and determine eligibility for mechanical thrombectomy within 24 hours. CTA is 92%–100% sensitive and 82%–100% specific for large proximal cerebral artery occlusions.

• Pursue MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging when CT is nondiagnostic

Given the insensitivity of CT imaging (47%–53% sensitivity within 3 hours), absence of infarct on initial head CT should be followed up with brain MRI in patients with high clinical suspicion. DWI-MRI is 88% sensitive and 95% specific for acute infarction within 24 hours. A meta-analysis showed that 34% of patients with transient neurologic symptoms had acute infarction on brain MRI. When on-site MRI is unavailable, arrange transfer for emergent MRI if the patient requires acute intervention decisions. Arrange outpatient MRI within 24 hours for stable patients with suspected TIA or minor stroke, consistent with the 2023 AHA Scientific Statement on TIA in the ED.

• Obtain ECG and cardiac monitoring

Acquire a 12-lead ECG to identify atrial fibrillation or other arrhythmias as the cause of embolic stroke, though ECG should not delay CT imaging. Cardiac monitoring during the first 24 hours is recommended to detect atrial fibrillation and potentially life-threatening arrhythmias.




CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care

Contributing action or omission: Delays in activating stroke expertise, initiating reperfusion therapy or transferring to a higher level of care can result in preventable neurological damage and adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely reperfusion, appropriate escalation and adherence to time-based quality metrics that directly impact patient outcomes.

• Activate stroke code immediately upon recognition of suspected stroke

Initiate stroke code activation within 5 minutes of symptom recognition, with neurological expertise available within 10 minutes. Stroke team activation may be initiated before patient arrival by EMS or at arrival by triage nurses. The currently accepted activation window is 24 hours from the time the patient was last known to be at baseline. However, patients presenting 6–24 hours after last known well require advanced imaging (CT perfusion or MRI diffusion/perfusion) demonstrating salvageable tissue to establish thrombectomy eligibility, per DAWN/DEFUSE 3 criteria.
• Recognize that intracranial hemorrhage on CT changes the management pathway
Recognize that intracerebral hemorrhage on non-contrast CT excludes the patient from thrombolysis and requires a fundamentally different management approach, including blood pressure control, reversal of anticoagulation if applicable and neurosurgical consultation. Document the CT finding and the shift in management.
• Administer intravenous thrombolysis within 60 minutes of arrival for eligible patients

For patients with disabling deficits presenting within 4.5 hours of symptom onset, administer tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg (max 25 mg, single IV bolus) or alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg, 10% bolus followed by 1-hour infusion) within 60 minutes of arrival, with a goal of less than 30 minutes. The 2026 AHA/ASA guidelines recommend tenecteplase as the thrombolytic agent of choice based on five large RCTs demonstrating noninferiority to alteplase with the practical advantage of single-bolus administration. Tenecteplase at 0.4 mg/kg is not recommended due to potential for harm without additional benefit. Earlier administration correlates to higher treatment effect. 

• Activate thrombectomy-capable center for patients with suspected large vessel occlusion

For patients with suspected large vessel occlusion, immediately notify the thrombectomy-capable center and initiate transfer protocols. For eligible patients, administer IV tenecteplase before transfer rather than withholding thrombolysis pending thrombectomy; IV tenecteplase before thrombectomy is associated with better functional outcomes than thrombectomy alone without increased hemorrhagic risk. Patients with anterior circulation large vessel occlusions treated with mechanical thrombectomy within six hours are more likely to be functionally independent (46.0% vs 26.5%). Door-in-door-out time should be minimized for patients requiring transfer.

• Utilize telestroke consultation when on-site neurology is unavailable

Activate telestroke consultation within 20 minutes of request for facilities without on-site neurology expertise. Studies have shown that telestroke care increases rates of alteplase administration with shorter door-to-needle times and risks similar to in-person neurology care.



ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Communication failures during handoffs, between services or with consulting neurologists increase the risk of diagnostic errors and adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure accurate information transfer during critical transitions and support coordinated team response.
• Standardize communication with receiving facilities for interfacility transfer

When transferring patients for thrombectomy, communicate directly with the receiving stroke team and provide: time last known well, NIHSS score, imaging findings (including CTA results), thrombolytic administration time and any complications. Early notification of the regional partner enables rapid escalation of care.
• Ensure closed-loop communication during stroke code activations

During stroke code activations and thrombolytic administration, use closed-loop communication to confirm orders, medication administration and procedural steps. Designate a team leader to coordinate care and ensure accountability. Closed-loop communication reduces errors during high-acuity encounters.

• Use structured handoff communication for patients with suspected stroke

Employ standardized handoff frameworks (such as SBAR, I-PASS) when transferring care of patients with suspected or confirmed stroke between providers, services or facilities. Include key information: time last known well, NIHSS score, imaging findings, interventions administered and pending results. Document handoff content in the medical record.





ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate communication with patients and families about stroke symptoms, treatment decisions and return precautions increases the risk of adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure informed decision-making and appropriate follow-up care.
• Engage in shared decision-making for thrombolytic therapy

Conduct a risk/benefit discussion with either the patient or a legally authorized patient representative, with summary documentation of the discussion evident in the medical record. If the patient is unable to consent and no legally authorized representative is available, it is reasonable to proceed with interventions under the premise of emergency consent.
• Address language barriers and health literacy

Utilize interpreter services for patients with limited English proficiency. Historically disenfranchised individuals may have limited awareness of stroke preparedness and concerns about cost that delay presentation. Provide culturally appropriate education materials.

• Provide explicit verbal and written discharge instructions

For patients discharged after stroke/TIA evaluation, discuss specific symptoms that should prompt immediate return (recurrent weakness, speech difficulty, visual changes, severe headache, altered mental status) and instructions for follow-up. Use teach-back methods to confirm patient understanding. Document discharge education provided.

ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inaccurate documentation of symptom onset time, clinical reasoning, risk stratification or disposition decisions compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to create an accurate, contemporaneous record that enables continuity of care and demonstrates adherence to the standard of care.

• Use standardized templates aligned with stroke quality measures

Use documentation templates that capture required elements for stroke quality measures, including time metrics (door-to-CT, door-to-needle), NIHSS score, imaging interpretation and discharge instructions. Standardized templates ensure completeness and facilitate quality reporting.
• Document time last known well, not symptom onset

Record the time the patient was last known to be at neurological baseline, which defines treatment eligibility. Distinguish between witnessed symptom onset and time last known well. For wake-up strokes, document the time the patient went to sleep and the time symptoms were discovered.

• Document NIHSS score with individual component scores

Record the total NIHSS score and individual component scores at initial evaluation and serially during the ED stay. Use templated documentation requiring NIHSS score before initiating thrombolysis. For patients with posterior circulation symptoms, document additional findings not captured by NIHSS (truncal ataxia, dysphagia, gait abnormalities).

• Document patient-specific factors that influenced evaluation

Record patient-specific factors that may affect presentation or interpretation, such as stroke history, baseline neurological deficits, atrial fibrillation and diabetes. Documentation of these factors demonstrates individualized assessment.
• Document pertinent negatives in the neurological examination

Record the absence of focal findings when the examination is normal. Documentation of pertinent negatives demonstrates thorough evaluation and supports clinical reasoning when stroke is excluded from the differential.
Document the reason(s) a patient was deemed ineligible for thrombolytic therapy
Record the specific exclusion criterion met, such as presentation outside the treatment window, intracranial hemorrhage on CT, blood pressure exceeding 185/110 mmHg despite treatment, coagulopathy, recent stroke or head trauma, history of intracranial hemorrhage, anticoagulant use, or symptoms too mild to warrant treatment risk. When blood pressure is the basis for exclusion, document specific BP values and antihypertensive agents administered, as emerging evidence suggests intensive BP lowering before thrombolysis may not improve outcomes. For relative contraindications, document the risk-benefit analysis. For patients who decline treatment, document the informed refusal discussion. Failure to administer tPA is alleged in approximately 29% of stroke malpractice cases, making this documentation critical for defensibility.
Sample documentation language: "74-year-old woman with history of atrial fibrillation on apixaban presenting with acute left hemiparesis. Last known well 22:00 the prior evening; found with symptoms at 06:30 (>8 hours). NIHSS 14. Noncontrast CT showed no hemorrhage. CTA showed right MCA M1 occlusion. Patient ineligible for IV thrombolysis: outside 4.5-hour treatment window and on therapeutic anticoagulation. Thrombectomy team activated given LVO within 24-hour window. Neurology consulted; transfer to thrombectomy-capable center initiated."
• Document clinical reasoning for disposition decisions

Document the rationale for admission, observation or discharge, including risk stratification (ABCD2 score for TIA), interpretation of imaging results and consideration of alternative diagnoses. For patients discharged after stroke/TIA evaluation, document why the patient was deemed low risk and what follow-up was arranged.

Sample documentation language: "68-year-old man with history of hypertension and diabetes presenting with 90 minutes of dizziness, nausea and gait unsteadiness. Given posterior circulation symptoms and vascular risk factors, maintained high suspicion for stroke despite FAST-negative examination. Last known well at 08:00. Stroke code activated at 09:35 (within 5 minutes of arrival). NIHSS 2 (gaze 1, ataxia 1). Noncontrast CT at 09:42 (7 minutes from arrival) showed no hemorrhage. CTA showed no large vessel occlusion. Given NIHSS 2 and symptom onset 4.5 hours, discussed thrombolysis risks/benefits with patient; patient declined. MRI obtained showing acute left cerebellar infarct. Neurology consulted; admission recommended for stroke workup and secondary prevention."



ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS
Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Patient delays in seeking care, refusal of recommended treatment or failure to follow up contribute to adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to address patient-level barriers to timely stroke care and appropriate follow-up.

• Document informed refusal of recommended treatment

When patients decline thrombolysis or other recommended interventions, document the discussion of risks and benefits, the patient's stated reasons for refusal and their understanding of potential consequences. Obtain witnessed informed refusal when possible.

• Document patient understanding using teach-back

Use teach-back methods to confirm patient understanding of discharge instructions, warning signs and follow-up plans. Document the teach-back process and patient responses.

• Address barriers to timely presentation

Recognize that historically disenfranchised individuals are less likely to present within 3 hours or to use EMS. Potential barriers include difficulties accessing care, concerns about cost, cultural beliefs and lack of trust. Provide education about stroke symptoms and the importance of calling 911.
• Arrange appropriate follow-up for patients discharged after TIA evaluation

For patients with TIA, arrange follow-up within 24–48 hours given the high early stroke risk (7.5%–17.4% at three months). Document follow-up arrangements and provide written instructions. Consider admission for high-risk TIA (ABCD2 ≥4) when outpatient follow-up cannot be ensured.

Cerebrovascular accident is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in emergency medicine.
Mitigating Your Risk

With timely recognition, guideline-concordant treatment and thorough documentation, emergency medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with cerebrovascular accidents (CVAs, or strokes) and reduce malpractice exposure. This repo

TITLE: Cardiac arrest
RISK_BRIEF:
Cardiac Arrest

SPECIALTY: Emergency Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Cardiac Arrest Out-of-Hospital and In-Hospital

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Death, Neurological Injury, Delayed Recognition
Cardiac arrest is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in emergency medicine.
Mitigating Your Risk  
With accurate diagnosis, timely treatment and effective communication, emergency medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with cardiac arrest and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for cardiac arrest and provides strategies to help prevent them. These strategies should be adapted to local resources and institutional capabilities, consistent with AHA guidelines.  

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving cardiac arrest typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for the 80% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 20%. Any of these—or a combination—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Cardiac Arrest Risk [insert chart] 
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSIS

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Failing to rapidly obtain critical history or to perform a targeted physical examination delays identification of reversible causes and appropriate treatment. 

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure rapid acquisition of clinically relevant information during resuscitation.

● Assign a team member to obtain collateral history during resuscitation
During resuscitation, designate a team member (nurse, technician or social worker) to obtain a history from EMS, family or bystanders. Key information includes witnessed vs. unwitnessed arrest, initial rhythm, bystander CPR, medications (especially anticoagulants, insulin and opioids) and prior cardiac history. This information guides treatment decisions and may identify reversible causes.
● Rapidly assess for physical findings suggesting reversible causes
During resuscitation, assess for tracheal deviation (tension pneumothorax), distended neck veins (tamponade, tension pneumothorax), track marks or toxidrome findings (overdose) and hypothermia. Allow physical examination findings to direct immediate interventions such as needle decompression or naloxone administration.
● Clarify code status and care goals early
Clarify code status and advance directives for patients with known medical history or presenting from skilled nursing facilities. If documentation is unavailable, initiate resuscitation while obtaining information. A clear understanding of patients’ wishes supports appropriate care and reduces the risk of unwanted interventions.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSIS

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain diagnostic studies that could identify reversible causes of cardiac arrest or guide post-arrest management increases the risk of missed treatable conditions and adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support early identification of reversible causes and appropriate diagnostic evaluation during and after resuscitation.

● Systematically evaluate for reversible causes (H’s and T's) during resuscitation
During active resuscitation, verbally review the H's and T's with the resuscitation team: hypoxia, hypovolemia, hydrogen ion/acidosis, hypo/hyperkalemia, hypothermia, tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins and thrombosis. Begin this review within the first few minutes of resuscitation and repeat if ROSC does not occur. Considering reversible causes enables targeted interventions that may restore circulation and aligns with AHA guidelines, supporting defensibility.
● Utilize point-of-care ultrasound where available to identify treatable pathology
Perform focused cardiac ultrasound, if available, during rhythm checks to assess for pericardial effusion/tamponade, right ventricular dilation (suggesting pulmonary embolism) and cardiac activity. Conduct POCUS during pulse checks to minimize interruptions in chest compressions. Identifying reversible causes enables targeted treatment; however, POCUS must not delay or interrupt high-quality CPR.
● Obtain 12-lead ECG promptly after ROSC
Perform a 12-lead ECG as soon as feasible after return of spontaneous circulation to identify STEMI or other actionable findings. ECGs performed within the first eight minutes after ROSC may have higher false-positive rates for STEMI. Consider repeating the ECG if initial findings are equivocal. Early ECG guides decisions about emergent coronary angiography and aligns with post-arrest care guidelines.
● Consider CT imaging for post-arrest patients when etiology is unclear
In patients with ROSC and unclear arrest etiology, consider CT imaging (head, chest and abdomen/pelvis) to identify intracranial hemorrhage, pulmonary embolism, aortic dissection or other treatable causes. Diagnostic yield ranges 8% to 54% for clinically significant diagnoses. Weigh CT imaging against the risks of transport and contrast administration in critically ill patients.

CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care

Contributing action or omission: Failing to engage appropriate specialists or transfer to a higher level of care when indicated delays definitive treatment and increases the risk of a patient’s death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely engagement of appropriate expertise and resources.

● Activate the cardiac catheterization laboratory for suspected ACS
At facilities with onsite catheterization capability, activate the cardiac catheterization laboratory promptly for patients with ROSC and suspected acute coronary syndrome (STEMI or high clinical suspicion). Early coronary angiography combined with targeted temperature management can achieve favorable neurological outcomes in appropriately selected patients. At facilities without onsite catheterization capacity, initiate transfer to a PCI-capable center per regional STEMI protocols as soon as the patient is stabilized. Document the clinical reasoning when initiating or deferring catheterization in the laboratory.
● Consult toxicology or poison control for suspected overdose
Consult toxicology or poison control during or immediately after resuscitation for patients in cardiac arrest with suspected toxic cause (opioid overdose, medication ingestion or environmental exposure). Specific antidotes or treatments may be indicated. Document the consultation and recommendations.
● Consider ECMO/ECPR consultation where available for refractory arrest
At institutions with established ECMO programs, engage ECPR teams promptly for patients with refractory cardiac arrest who meet institutional selection criteria. Patient characteristics associated with survival include witnessed arrest, bystander CPR, shockable rhythm, intermittent signs of life during CPR and short low-flow times. ECPR requires a highly trained team, specialized equipment and multidisciplinary support; it should only be considered within an appropriately trained and equipped system of care. At facilities without onsite ECMO programs, focus resuscitation efforts on high-quality CPR, early defibrillation and systematic treatment of reversible causes. Interfacility transfer of patients with refractory cardiac arrest for ECPR is not well-established and poses significant risks.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Failing to establish clear communication channels and assign roles during resuscitation increases the risk of errors and intervention delays, undermining team coordination.
Mitigation Strategies
The strategies below work together to clarify team roles, standardize information transfer and support a coordinated response to cardiac emergencies.

● Designate a team leader at the start of every resuscitation
After recognizing a cardiac arrest, assign a team leader to coordinate care, direct interventions and ensure accountability. This reduces role ambiguity and enables a rapid, organized response, aligning with AHA recommendations for code team structure and reducing the likelihood of errors caused by unclear commands. Early leadership demonstrates adherence to standard resuscitation protocols and supports defensibility.
● Implement closed-loop communication during resuscitation
All code team members should be trained to verbalize orders clearly and confirm receipt by repeating instructions back to the team leader. This practice minimizes medication and intervention errors in the high-stress resuscitation environment. Closed-loop communication is a recognized standard in crisis resource management and demonstrates team adherence to best practices.
● Use structured handoff frameworks during transitions of care
When transferring care between providers, including during shift changes, transport or ICU admission, employ standardized handoff tools such as SBAR or I-PASS. Structured handoffs minimize information loss during transitions and document communication of essential clinical information. Include critical details during handoffs, such as arrest etiology, initial rhythm, interventions performed, medications administered with timing, CPR quality metrics and current hemodynamic status.
● Conduct structured debriefings after every cardiac arrest event
Perform both "hot" debriefings (immediately post-event) and "cold" debriefings (with data review) to identify performance gaps, reinforce effective practices and support team well-being. Debriefing improves CPR quality metrics (such as compression depth, rate and fraction) and is linked with better ROSC and survival. Documenting debriefings demonstrates institutional commitment to quality improvement.

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider
Contributing action or omission: Neglecting to clarify code status and communicate effectively with patients and families can result in inappropriate resuscitation attempts and increased distress.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support patient-centered care, ensure informed decision-making and reduce post-event distress.

● Clarify code status and goals of care early in the ED encounter
For patients at risk of deterioration, document discussions about resuscitation preferences as soon as clinically feasible. Patients transferred from skilled nursing facilities, those with advanced directives on file elsewhere or those with cognitive impairment require explicit verification of code status with available surrogates. Unclear goals of care represent a unique patient safety concern during resuscitations. Early clarification prevents inappropriate resuscitation attempts and supports patient autonomy.
● Allow family to be present during resuscitation when feasible
Develop institutional policies supporting family presence during resuscitation (FPDR), with a dedicated team member, such as a social worker, chaplain or nurse assigned to support the family, explain interventions and answer questions. FPDR can reduce complicated grief and improve long-term psychological outcomes for family members. Document whether family presence was offered and the outcome of that discussion.
● Provide clear, compassionate communication after resuscitation
Communicate with family members using plain language regardless of whether the outcome of resuscitation is ROSC or death. In the case of a surviving patient, explain that attempting to predict neurological recovery is inappropriate immediately post-arrest. When a patient dies, offer bereavement support resources to the family. Clear communication reduces family distress and potential allegations of inadequate information sharing.
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Incomplete or inaccurate documentation of cardiac arrest events can hinder quality review and compromise legal defensibility.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure an accurate resuscitation record and reduce discrepancies that can cause liability issues.
● Assign a dedicated recorder during resuscitation
Assign a team member whose sole responsibility is real-time documentation of interventions, medications, rhythms and timing. This reduces reliance on retrospective recall, which is often inaccurate, and ensures that the medical record reflects actual events and supports quality review.
● Use standardized resuscitation documentation templates
Implement EHR-based resuscitation narrator modules or paper-based code sheets aligned with Utstein-style data elements, including the date and time of arrest, location, witnessed status, initial rhythm, interventions (such as CPR, defibrillation and medications with doses and times), airway management and ROSC timing. Standardized templates reduce omissions and ensure that core data elements are captured for required quality reporting and legal defensibility.
● Capture CPR quality metrics when available
When CPR feedback devices are used, document compression rate, depth and fraction, as well as end-tidal CO2 values. Such metrics support quality improvement and demonstrate adherence to high-quality CPR standards.
● Record clinical reasoning for key decisions
Document the reasoning behind key decisions, such as stopping resuscitation, not pursuing ECPR or deviating from standard protocols. Note relevant clinical factors like prolonged downtime, terminal illness and the patient’s or family’s wishes. Detailed documentation of those decisions demonstrates sound clinical judgment and aligns with the standard of care.
● Document post-ROSC care and handoff communications
Record vital signs, neurological status, interventions (such as targeted temperature management or vasopressors) and communication with receiving teams, including ICU, cardiology and interventional services. Post-arrest documentation gaps can obscure the quality of care provided during this critical phase.
For patients with prolonged resuscitation (>20 minutes) or who do not achieve ROSC, document all interventions and the clinical rationale for termination decisions.
● Reconcile documentation discrepancies promptly
After each event, review the resuscitation record for accuracy and completeness before finalizing. Document any corrections with appropriate addenda rather than altering original entries.

TITLE: Sepsis
RISK_BRIEF:
Sepsis

SPECIALTY: Emergency Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Undifferentiated Infection, Systemic Inflammatory Response

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Missed or Delayed Sepsis Diagnosis and Treatment
Sepsis is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in emergency medicine.
Mitigating Your Risk 
With early recognition, timely treatment and escalation and thorough documentation, emergency medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with sepsis diagnosis and treatment and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for missed or delayed sepsis diagnosis and treatment and provides strategies to help prevent them. 

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving sepsis typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 84% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 16%. Any of these—or a combination—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Sepsis Risk [insert chart] 

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain a thorough history and perform a targeted physical examination delays recognition of infection and organ dysfunction, which are the foundational elements of sepsis identification.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that systematic clinical assessment supports early sepsis recognition, reducing the likelihood of missed or delayed diagnosis.
● Obtain a focused medical history, including comorbidities, medications and recent healthcare exposures
During the initial encounter, gather a history of chronic organ dysfunction (such as cirrhosis, chronic kidney disease and heart failure), immunosuppressive conditions or medications, recent hospitalizations, indwelling devices, recent antibiotic use and prior infections. This information determines the likelihood of sepsis, its probable source and the expected clinical trajectory. Patients with chronic organ dysfunction may present with acute decompensation (such as diabetic ketoacidosis or decompensated cirrhosis), where sepsis is the precipitant but not immediately apparent. Documenting this history supports appropriate diagnostic escalation and demonstrates thoroughness of evaluation.
● Perform a structured assessment for infection and organ dysfunction at initial evaluation
At triage and during the initial physician evaluation, systematically check for signs of infection (fever or hypothermia, cough, dysuria, erythema or abdominal pain) and organ dysfunction (altered mentation, hypotension, tachypnea or oliguria). Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2026 (SSC) recommend incorporating sepsis screening into performance-improvement programs for acutely ill, high-risk patients, with a strong recommendation to use NEWS, NEWS2, MEWS or SIRS over qSOFA as a screening tool. Structured assessment ensures that sepsis is included early in the differential diagnosis, when time-sensitive interventions have the greatest impact on outcomes.

● Perform a targeted physical examination based on the suspected source
When an infection is suspected, conduct a focused examination directed at the most likely source. If no source is identified, reassess areas prone to cryptic infection, including the genitourinary region and perianal regions, as well as medical device and indwelling catheter sites. A thorough, documented examination supports clinical decision-making and defensibility.
● Reassess patients with persistent or worsening clinical status
Reconsider sepsis in the differential diagnosis when there is no clear diagnosis after the initial evaluation and the patient's condition fails to improve or worsens. At least one-third of patients initially diagnosed with sepsis are later found to have noninfectious conditions, while some patients with seemingly benign presentations may progress to sepsis. Serial reassessment with documented clinical reasoning shows vigilance and leads to timely escalation.

CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to order timely diagnostic testing—including lactate measurement, blood cultures and source-directed imaging—delays sepsis recognition and treatment bundle initiation. 

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that appropriate diagnostic testing is ordered promptly when sepsis is suspected, supporting early recognition and guideline-concordant care.
● Measure serum lactate early in all patients with suspected sepsis
Order a serum lactate level (venous or arterial) during the initial evaluation for any patient with suspected infection and clinical instability. The ACEP task force supports initially measuring blood lactate in the ED and repeating measurement after initial resuscitation if levels are elevated or if clinical deterioration is suspected. The SSC 2026 guidelines note that intermediate lactate elevation (>2 to <4 mmol/L) is common in sepsis, associated with increased mortality, and may also warrant fluid resuscitation to prevent progression to overt shock. Patients with liver disease or those receiving metformin or epinephrine infusions may have elevated lactate from non-sepsis causes. Interpret lactate in the clinical context for these patients, but do not withhold measurement based on potential confounders. Early lactate measurement identifies patients at higher risk and prompts more rapid intervention.
● Obtain blood cultures before antibiotic administration whenever feasible
Collect at least two sets of blood cultures (one aerobic and one anaerobic bottle per set) from separate sites before administering antibiotics, unless doing so would delay antibiotic administration. Cultures taken after antibiotics have substantially lower yields, reducing the ability to tailor therapy and confirm a diagnosis. 
● Order source-directed diagnostic testing based on clinical assessment

In patients without a clear infection source, order a chest x-ray and urinalysis (with subsequent urine culture if infection is suspected) as baseline studies, since pneumonia and urinary tract infections are the most common sources of sepsis. Perform targeted CT imaging, rather than routine whole-body imaging, based on clinical suspicion. Targeted testing is critical for identifying the source and achieving source control, both of which are essential to sepsis management. Sample additional potential infection sources (such as cerebrospinal fluid, peritoneal fluid, wound cultures and joint aspirate) based on history and examination findings.
● Implement automated sepsis screening and laboratory alerts
Use EHR-based sepsis screening tools and automated alerts for abnormal vital signs or laboratory values (such as elevated lactate, leukocytosis or bandemia) to prompt clinician reassessment. Align EHR-based screening tools with the SSC 2026 recommendation favoring NEWS, NEWS2, MEWS or SIRS over qSOFA, which has poor sensitivity for early sepsis identification. Sepsis alert systems in the ED are associated with improved adherence to the SSC sepsis bundle and serve as a safety net when clinical recognition is delayed, particularly during high-volume periods.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Consult or Transfer Care

Contributing action or omission: Failing to escalate care through timely consultation, ICU admission or interhospital transfer increases the risk of adverse outcomes and legal challenges. 

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate and timely escalation of care for patients with sepsis and septic shock.
● Initiate early consultation for patients with septic shock or complex infections requiring source control
Start the consult immediately (rather than completing the initial workup) upon recognizing patients presenting with septic shock, complex infections requiring surgical source control (such as intra-abdominal abscess, intestinal perforation, necrotizing soft tissue infection or infected prosthesis) or infections requiring subspecialty expertise, such as endocarditis or meningitis. SSC 2026 guidelines conditionally recommend early source control, ideally within 6 hours of diagnosis, as delays are associated with increased mortality. 
● Prioritize timely ICU admission for patients with sepsis requiring critical care
Admit patients with sepsis or septic shock requiring ICU care within 6 hours of ED presentation so they receive necessary monitoring, nursing care and interventions. Observational data demonstrate that mortality increases when ED stays exceed 6 hours. 
● Establish protocols for care of sepsis patients during ED boarding

If patients with sepsis must remain in the ED, develop institutional protocols that define accountability for ongoing care, including who is responsible for subsequent antibiotic doses, vital sign monitoring and reassessment. ED boarding is linked to increased mortality; clear accountability prevents patients from being overlooked during transitions.
● Transfer patients promptly to higher-level facilities when local capabilities are insufficient
When ICU capabilities or subspecialty resources are unavailable, immediately initiate transfer to facilities that can provide appropriate care for patients with sepsis or septic shock. Administer antibiotics, IV fluids and vasopressors before and during transfer. Delayed transfers from under-resourced facilities increase the risk of adverse outcomes and create defensibility challenges.
ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR

Failure to Recognize Responsibility

Contributing action or omission: Neglecting to assume responsibility for a sepsis patient's care—including failing to initiate timely, guideline-concordant treatment, reassess after initial interventions, address abnormal results or ensure continuity during transitions—heightens the risk of poor outcomes and legal liability.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reinforce individual clinician accountability and team-based ownership of sepsis care and care transitions throughout the ED encounter.

● Administer antibiotics within recommended timeframes based on diagnostic certainty
For patients with septic shock (possible, probable or definite) or probable/definite sepsis without shock, administer antimicrobial therapy immediately, ideally within 1 hour of recognition. This is a strong recommendation from the 2026 SSC. For patients with possible sepsis without shock, perform a rapid assessment of infectious versus noninfectious causes and administer antibiotics within 3 hours if concern for infection persists. Antibiotic timing is the most consistently evidence-supported intervention for reducing sepsis mortality, with the strongest association observed in patients with septic shock.
● Do not add empiric anaerobic coverage unless the infection source carries anaerobic risk
When selecting empiric antibiotics for sepsis, assess whether the suspected source warrants anaerobic coverage. Sources warranting anaerobic coverage include the following infections: intra-abdominal, deep-seated gynecologic/obstetric, necrotizing soft tissue, head and neck, and CNS. For pulmonary or urinary sources, which are the two most common, anaerobic coverage is unlikely to benefit. Observational data associate unnecessary anti-anaerobic antibiotics (for example, piperacillin-tazobactam when cefepime would suffice) with increased 90-day mortality, acute kidney injury and decreased organ failure-free days. Use of agents with inherent anaerobic activity remains appropriate when needed for MDR coverage. Document the rationale for antibiotic selection, including the basis for including or withholding anaerobic coverage.
● Initiate fluid resuscitation promptly using weight-appropriate volume calculations
For patients with sepsis-induced hypoperfusion or septic shock, administer at least 30 mL/kg IV balanced crystalloid within 3 hours, delivered in serial boluses with reassessment after each bolus. For patients with BMI >30 kg/m², use adjusted or ideal body weight rather than actual body weight. Use actual body weight in cachectic or underweight patients. Guide fluid administration beyond the initial bolus with dynamic measures (passive leg raise, stroke volume variation) rather than static measures (CVP). Failure to receive 30 mL/kg within 3 hours is associated with increased mortality, including in patients with heart failure and ESRD. When deviating from the 30 mL/kg target, document the rationale, volume administered and clinical response.
● Maintain responsibility for the sepsis patient's clinical trajectory
After initiating the sepsis bundle, reassess the patient's response to interventions at defined intervals, such as after initial fluid boluses and antibiotic administration and at 1- and 3-hour marks. Regular reassessment identifies non-responders early and ensures that care is escalated appropriately. Failing to reassess is a common error that undermines defensibility.
● Use closed-loop communication for critical orders and results
Use closed-loop communication to verbally confirm critical orders for antibiotics, vasopressors and fluid boluses with nursing staff. Verify that blood cultures were drawn before antibiotics were administered and confirm repeat lactate orders for elevated initial values. Closed-loop communication minimizes errors and establishes a defensible chain of actions.
● Use structured handoffs during care transitions
Follow structured handoff processes when transferring sepsis patients to ensure that critical information, such as current hemodynamic status, administered and pending interventions, culture and laboratory results and outstanding clinical concerns, is passed along to other providers. Care transitions are prone to communication errors and have been identified as barriers to timely sepsis detection and management.
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: In sepsis cases, failing to document clinical reasoning, timing of interventions and reassessment findings compromises continuity of care and creates significant liability exposure.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support timely, comprehensive documentation that demonstrates thoughtful evaluation, reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record of clinical decision-making.
● Document clinical reasoning for a sepsis differential diagnosis
Record the differential diagnosis, clinical findings supporting or arguing against sepsis and the rationale for the diagnosis and treatment plan. If sepsis is suspected but not confirmed, document the basis for clinical uncertainty and the plan for ongoing reassessment. If sepsis is initially considered but ultimately excluded, document the reasoning for the alternative diagnosis. Documenting clinical reasoning demonstrates thoughtful evaluation and supports defensibility regardless of outcome.
Sample documentation language: "Patient is a 72-year-old male with history of COPD presenting with altered mental status, tachypnea (RR 28) and temperature 38.9°C. Differential includes sepsis (pulmonary source vs. urinary source), COPD exacerbation and acute decompensated heart failure. Given concern for sepsis, lactate ordered, blood cultures drawn x2, Cefepime selected given pulmonary/urinary sources most likely — anaerobic coverage not indicated. Antibiotics administered within 45 minutes of arrival. Chest x-ray and urinalysis obtained. LR bolus initiated. Will reassess after initial fluid resuscitation."
● Timestamp all critical interventions and reassessments
Record the time of sepsis recognition ("time zero"), blood culture collection, antibiotic administration, fluid resuscitation (with volume) and reassessments. Use EHR templates or sepsis-specific documentation tools to standardize documentation. Accurate timestamps demonstrate compliance with sepsis bundle elements and provide a defensible timeline. 
● Document rationale for deviation from standard sepsis bundle elements
When clinical judgment dictates deviation from guideline-recommended interventions, document the clinical reasoning at the time of the decision. Record the patient-specific factors driving the modification, the alternative approach taken and the patient's response.
Sample documentation language: "Full 30 mL/kg IVF bolus not administered as patient has a history of CHF with known EF of 15%. Concern for fluid overload with full bolus. Administered 500 mL LR bolus with reassessment of hemodynamics after each bolus. MAP improved to 68 after 1L total; holding further fluids given bilateral crackles on exam."
● Document responses to interventions and escalation decisions
After reassessments, record the patient's hemodynamic response (such as vital signs, lactate trend, urine output and mental status), adequacy of response and next steps. Serially documenting responses and decision-making creates a contemporaneous record that is far more defensible than retrospective reconstruction.
If escalation is required, document the clinical rationale (such as additional fluid boluses, vasopressor initiation or ICU consultation). If the patient is responding appropriately, document the basis for continued management. 
● Document patient and family communication
Record discussions with patients and families about the sepsis diagnosis, severity of illness, treatment plan and prognosis. If patients or families decline recommended interventions, such as ICU admission or intubation, document the discussion, including risks explained and the patient or surrogate's stated preferences. Documenting communication demonstrates informed consent and shared decision-making.
ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Patients who delay visiting the ED, have difficulty communicating symptoms or don’t follow physician recommendations increase the risk of adverse sepsis outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement, remove obstacles to timely sepsis care and increase defensibility in cases of non-adherence.
● Identify and address barriers to presentation and care
Assess patients for factors delaying presentation, such as symptom minimization, lack of access to care, language barriers and cognitive impairment. Address modifiable barriers during the ED encounter to inform discharge planning and follow-up strategies. Elderly individuals living alone, patients with cognitive impairment or dementia, non-English-speakers and those with limited health literacy are at increased risk for delayed presentation and may require additional post-discharge follow-up.
● Provide clear discharge instructions for patients evaluated for possible infection
When discharging patients with possible early infection, such as a suspected viral illness or a bacterial infection not meeting sepsis criteria, inform them of symptoms that warrant immediate return to the ED, including worsening fever, confusion, inability to keep fluids down, lightheadedness or decreased urine output. Document discharge instructions and the patient’s stated understanding of them. Clear return precautions can encourage patients to seek care early if their condition progresses toward sepsis.
● Document patient decisions to leave AMA
When patients with suspected or confirmed sepsis leave AMA, thoroughly document the conversation. Record the clinical situation explained to the patient, specific risks discussed (including death), the patient's reasons for leaving and their ability to make decisions. Offer alternatives like continued observation or return within a set timeframe. Detailed documentation of AMA cases is essential for legal protection.

TITLE: Spinal epidural abscess
RISK_BRIEF:
Spinal Epidural Abscess

SPECIALTY: Emergency Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Back Pain, Neck Pain, Neurologic Complaints

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Spinal Epidural Abscess, Neurologic Injury
Spinal epidural abscess is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in emergency medicine. This document focuses on spinal epidural abscess as the primary infectious cause of spinal cord compression in the ED setting. Cauda equina syndrome is addressed here as a complication of epidural compression. Cauda equina syndrome from non-infectious etiologies (for example, lumbar disc herniation) and traumatic spinal cord injury involve distinct clinical pathways and risk profiles and are outside the scope of this report.
 
Mitigating Your Risk  
With timely diagnostic evaluation, rapid treatment escalation and thorough documentation, emergency medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with spinal epidural abscess and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for spinal epidural abscess and provides strategies to help prevent them. 
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving spinal epidural abscess typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 86% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 14%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate. 

Actions and Omissions Driving Spinal Epidural Abscess Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing
Contributing action or omission: Failure to obtain appropriate MRI and laboratory studies in patients with possible spinal epidural abscess or cord compression is a primary driver of delayed diagnosis and adverse outcomes.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients at risk for SEA receive timely and appropriate diagnostic evaluation, reducing the likelihood of missed or delayed diagnosis.

● Adopt a risk-factor-based screening approach rather than relying on the classic triad
When evaluating patients with back or neck pain in the ED, assess for SEA risk factors (such as diabetes, IV drug use, immunosuppression, recent spinal procedure, indwelling vascular catheter, concurrent infection and recent bacteremia) rather than waiting for the classic triad of back pain, fever and neurologic deficit, which present in fewer than 10% of SEA cases. A risk-factor–based approach shifts the evaluation from pattern recognition of a rare presentation to systematic identification of at-risk patients, aligning with current emergency medicine literature and reducing the probability of anchoring on a benign diagnosis. Give extra consideration to patients who use IV drugs, as they may present with atypical complaints, have longer imaging times and face implicit bias that delays workup. Consider prompt evaluation of patients with diabetes and concurrent skin or soft tissue infections, as those are the most reported comorbidities in SEA. 
● Perform and document a thorough neurologic examination
At initial evaluation, perform and document a focused neurologic exam, including motor strength testing in all extremities, a sensory examination (including perianal sensation), deep tendon reflexes, a rectal tone assessment when indicated and gait evaluation. Up to 50% of SEA patients have a neurologic abnormality at presentation, but 40% present without one. A documented neurologic examination establishes a baseline, identifies patients with early deficits who require emergent intervention and demonstrates the thoroughness of the evaluation. Failure to perform a neurologic examination was identified as a key breakdown in 81.8% of diagnostic error cases. 
● Perform a bedside bladder scan to assess post-void residual volume in patients with suspected cauda equina syndrome or spinal cord compression
In patients with back pain and features common to cauda equina syndrome or spinal cord compression (bowel or bladder complaints, saddle anesthesia, bilateral lower extremity symptoms or new motor weakness), perform a bedside bladder scan to measure post-void residual (PVR) volume. A PVR ≥200 mL has the highest diagnostic accuracy for predicting MRI-confirmed cauda equina compression, with sensitivity of 82%–94% and negative predictive value of 97%–99%. An elevated PVR should prompt emergent MRI and surgical consultation.
Important caveat: A normal PVR does not exclude cauda equina syndrome, particularly incomplete CES, and should not be used in isolation to defer MRI when clinical suspicion is present. PVR is an adjunct to, not a substitute for, clinical assessment and definitive imaging.
● Obtain inflammatory markers (ESR and CRP) in patients with back pain and any SEA risk factor
When a patient presents with back or neck pain and one or more risk factors for SEA, order ESR and CRP during the initial ED evaluation. Inflammatory markers serve as a high-sensitivity screening tool that can identify patients who require definitive imaging. Normal inflammatory markers in a patient with risk factors provide meaningful (though not absolute) reassurance, while elevated markers should prompt an MRI.
● Order MRI with gadolinium contrast as the definitive imaging study when SEA is suspected
When clinical suspicion for SEA exists based on risk factors, symptoms or elevated inflammatory markers, immediately obtain an MRI with gadolinium contrast of the relevant spinal region. MRI is the gold standard for SEA diagnosis, with sensitivity exceeding 90%. CT imaging is insufficient to exclude SEA and is a primary contributor of missed diagnoses in malpractice cases. If MRI is unavailable, arrange emergent transfer of any patient at higher risk for missed SEA, especially those with back pain and who have been evaluated with CT only, to a facility with MRI capability.


CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention
Contributing action or omission: Failure to initiate timely empiric antibiotics, delay in surgical decompression and inadequate post-diagnosis monitoring can contribute to poor neurologic outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that once SEA is diagnosed or strongly suspected, treatment is initiated rapidly and escalated appropriately.

● Initiate empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics immediately after obtaining cultures
Once SEA is diagnosed or strongly suspected (based on imaging or high clinical suspicion with elevated inflammatory markers), obtain blood cultures and initiate empiric IV antibiotics without delay. Recommended empiric coverage includes vancomycin (for MRSA) plus a third- or fourth-generation cephalosporin (such as ceftriaxone or cefepime) for gram-negative coverage. Those with concurrent endocarditis, bacteremia or distant-site infections may require broader coverage and infectious disease consultation. Patients who have undergone recent spinal procedures may have multidrug-resistant organisms. Prompt antibiotic initiation reduces the risk of sepsis progression and further neurologic deterioration. 
● Recognize indications for emergent surgical decompression
When SEA is confirmed on imaging, immediately assess for emergent surgical decompression indications, including progressive or acute neurologic deficits, motor weakness, cauda equina syndrome, urinary retention or failure to improve with antibiotics. Pay extra attention to patients with rapidly progressive deficits (measured in hours, rather than days) that may require most urgent intervention. Patients with cervical or thoracic SEA have worse outcomes than those with lumbosacral involvement and may warrant more aggressive surgical management. Clearly communicate the degree and progression of neurologic deficits to the consulting spine surgeon.
● Monitor neurologic status frequently after diagnosis and during treatment
After diagnosing SEA and starting treatment, perform and document serial neurologic examinations at defined intervals, such as every 1–2 hours in the ED, with a clear handoff to the admitting team. Neurologic deterioration can occur rapidly even after antibiotics are started, and close monitoring enables early detection in patients who are declining and may require a management change from conservative to surgical. Serial examinations also create a documented timeline that supports defensibility.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care
Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain timely spine surgery or neurosurgery consultation or to transfer patients to a facility with appropriate surgical and MRI capabilities contributes to diagnostic and treatment delays that worsen outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate specialist involvement and access to definitive care.

● Consult spine surgery or neurosurgery immediately upon diagnosis or strong suspicion of SEA
When MRI confirms SEA or clinical suspicion is high and the MRI is pending, immediately contact spine surgery or neurosurgery service. Do not wait for final radiology reads if preliminary imaging is concerning. Time from diagnosis to surgery is a critical determinant of outcome, and consultation delays directly contribute to malpractice exposure. Early consultation allows the surgical team to assess the patient, plan the intervention and mobilize resources.
● Arrange emergent transfer when definitive imaging or surgical capability is unavailable
If MRI is unavailable at the treating facility and clinical suspicion for SEA is present, arrange emergent transfer to a facility with MRI and spine surgery capability. Do not substitute CT for MRI when SEA is in the differential. Start empiric antibiotics before transfer. Patients with progressive neurologic deficits need the most urgent transfer arrangements. In facilities without on-call spine surgery coverage, establish pre-arranged transfer agreements with tertiary centers. Document the clinical rationale for transfer, the accepting physician and facility and the patient's neurologic status at the time of transfer. Delays in transferring patients with evolving neurologic deficits represent a significant source of malpractice risk.
● Obtain infectious disease consultation for complex or atypical cases
Consult an infectious disease specialist during the ED visit or include it in the admission plan for patients with culture-negative SEA, polymicrobial infections, concurrent endocarditis, immunocompromise or uncertainty regarding antibiotic selection and duration. Involving infectious disease specialists optimizes antimicrobial management and supports a multidisciplinary approach to care. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of risk factors, neurologic exams, clinical reasoning and discharge instructions compromises continuity of care and weakens defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support thorough, real-time documentation that reinforces clinical decision-making and provides a defensible record.

● Document structured risk factor assessments for back pain patients
Document the presence or absence of SEA risk factors for every patient with back or neck pain. Factors include IV drug use, diabetes, immunosuppression, recent spinal procedure, indwelling vascular catheter, concurrent infection and fever. Use a structured template or checklist when available. Systematic documentation of risk factor assessment shows the clinician considered serious pathology, which supports defensibility even if the diagnosis is not made at the initial visit.
Sample documentation language: "Risk factor assessment for serious spinal pathology: No history of IV drug use. No diabetes. No immunosuppression. No recent spinal procedure or indwelling catheter. No concurrent infection identified. No fever. Low pre-test probability for spinal epidural abscess."
● Document a complete neurologic examination with specific findings
Record specific neurologic exam findings instead of using shorthand like "neuro intact." Document motor strength by muscle group, sensory exam (including perianal sensation when relevant), deep tendon reflexes, gait assessment and rectal tone when indicated. Detailed documentation establishes a baseline, allows comparison on return visits and demonstrates thoroughness. In malpractice litigation, vague neurologic documentation is often cited as evidence of an inadequate exam. 
Sample documentation language: "Neurologic exam: Motor strength 5/5 bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sensation intact to light touch in all dermatomes. DTRs 2+ and symmetric. Gait steady, heel-toe walk normal. No saddle anesthesia. Rectal tone normal. Post-void residual by bladder scan: 45 mL."
● Document clinical reasoning when SEA is considered and rejected
Clearly document why SEA was considered in the differential diagnosis and why advanced workup was not performed. Include the risk factors assessed, rationale for determining low pre-test probability and return precautions provided. This type of documentation demonstrates that the clinician actively considered the diagnosis and made a reasoned clinical decision, which is the strongest defense in malpractice litigation.
Sample documentation language: "Considered spinal epidural abscess given back pain presentation. Patient has no identified risk factors (no IVDU, no diabetes, no immunosuppression, no recent spinal procedure, no fever, no concurrent infection). Neurologic examination is normal. ESR and CRP within normal limits. Pre-test probability for SEA is low. Patient counseled on return precautions, including fever, weakness, numbness, bowel/bladder changes."
● Provide and document specific return precautions at discharge
When discharging a patient with back pain, provide clear written and verbal return precautions about symptoms of neurologic deterioration. These should include new or worsening weakness, numbness or tingling extremities, difficulty walking, loss of bowel or bladder control, saddle-area numbness and fever. Document that precautions were communicated to and understood by the patient. Clear instructions help patients recognize evolving symptoms and return for re-evaluation, reducing the risk of diagnostic delay on future visits.


ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS
Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care
Contributing action or omission: Leaving the ED before complete evaluation, declining recommended imaging, or failing to recognize worsening symptoms or return for follow-up contribute to delay in diagnosing SEA.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement, address barriers to adherence and create a documented record of communication efforts.

● Communicate the seriousness of the diagnostic concern in plain language
When recommending an MRI or laboratory testing for possible SEA, explain its importance to the patient in clear, non-technical terms. Describe potential consequences if the condition is missed, using phrases like "spinal infection" instead of medical terminology. Patients who understand the severity of a missed diagnosis are more likely to complete testing and follow-up. Clear communication also strengthens defensibility if the patient declines care.
● Document informed refusal when patients decline recommended workup
If a patient declines a recommended MRI, laboratory testing or admission, thoroughly document the informed refusal discussion. Include the suspected diagnosis, risks of declining evaluation (including paralysis and death), recommended next steps and the patient's reasons for refusal. When possible, have the patient sign a refusal-of-care form. Proper documentation of informed refusal shows that the clinician met the standard of care.
Sample documentation language: "Patient informed that back pain with [risk factor] raises concern for possible spinal infection that could lead to permanent paralysis if not diagnosed and treated promptly. MRI recommended. Patient declines MRI, stating [reason]. Risks of declining—including paralysis, permanent neurologic injury and death—discussed. Patient verbalizes understanding and still declines. Advised to return immediately for fever, weakness, numbness or bowel/bladder changes. Refusal-of-care form signed."
● Implement follow-up systems for high-risk patients who leave before evaluation is complete
Establish ED protocols to identify and contact patients who leave before completing an MRI against medical advice or have pending critical results. Assign responsibility for follow-up calls within 24 hours. Patients with SEA risk factors who leave the ED without a definitive evaluation are at high risk for delayed diagnosis. Patients with substance use disorders, housing instability or psychiatric comorbidities may benefit from social work involvement, patient navigation services or direct scheduling of outpatient MRI before discharge. Systematic follow-up reduces the likelihood of these patients being lost to care and demonstrates institutional commitment to patient safety.
