Acute myocardial infarction is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in family medicine. 
Mitigating Your Risk
With rapid recognition of ischemic symptoms, appropriate initial management, timely EMS transfer and structured post-event follow-up, family medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with acute myocardial infarction and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for acute myocardial infarction and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving acute myocardial infarction typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 85% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 15%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.    
Actions and Omissions Driving Acute Myocardial Infarction Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing
Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain electrocardiography or arrange troponin testing in patients with symptoms suggestive of acute coronary syndrome (ACS) delays diagnosis and increases the risk of adverse outcomes.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely identification of ACS and appropriate escalation of care.

• Obtain ECG immediately when suspecting ACS

When a patient presents with chest pain or symptoms concerning for myocardial ischemia, obtain a 12-lead ECG as soon as possible if available in the office setting. Compare results with prior ECGs to identify new changes. If ECG cannot be obtained promptly, transfer the patient immediately to the emergency department rather than delaying for an office ECG. In the emergency department, perform ECG within 10 minutes of arrival. This approach aligns with ACC/AHA guidelines and demonstrates urgency in evaluation.

• Transfer patients to the ED when ACS is a clinical concern

Send patients to the ED for serial troponin testing and observation as per the standard of care. While risk stratification tools, such as the Marburg Heart Score and HEART score, can help identify low-risk patients, no tool has been validated to definitively rule out ACS in outpatient settings. Attempting to rule out ACS in the office without high-sensitivity troponin testing exposes patients to risk and creates defensibility challenges.
• Do not hesitate to transfer patients with cardiovascular risk factors to the ED

Maintain a low threshold for emergency department evaluation in patients with multiple cardiovascular risk factors and symptoms suggestive of ischemia. Risk factors include age ≥65, diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, smoking, previous abnormal cardiac stress test results and family history of premature CAD. Document the risk factor assessment and clinical reasoning behind disposition decisions. 
• Avoid anchoring on non-cardiac diagnoses without excluding ACS

Maintain appropriate suspicion for ACS even when initial findings suggest alternative diagnoses, particularly in patients with cardiovascular risk factors. Chest pain that is reproducible with palpation or varies with breathing or position is less likely to signify ACS but does not exclude it. Approximately 41% of patients with NSTE-ACS have neither ST-segment depression nor T-wave inversions on initial ECG. 

CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Medication Error
Contributing action or omission: Errors in initial medication administration—including failure to give aspirin, inappropriate use of nitroglycerin or administering contraindicated medications—can worsen outcomes and increase liability exposure.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure proper initial pharmacologic management while avoiding harmful interventions.

• Administer aspirin immediately when ACS is suspected

Give 162 to 325 mg of non-enteric-coated aspirin as soon as ACS is suspected unless a true contraindication exists, such as aspirin allergy with anaphylaxis, active bleeding, severe untreated hypertension or active peptic ulcer. Ensure that the patient chews the aspirin for rapid absorption. Aspirin administration is a Class I recommendation with Level A evidence for reducing mortality in ACS. Document its use or the specific contraindication that precluded it. 

• Recognize contraindications to nitroglycerin before administration

Administer sublingual nitroglycerin (0.4 mg) for chest pain relief if no contraindications are present. Contraindications include:
Systolic blood pressure <90 mm Hg or ≥30 mm Hg below baseline
Severe bradycardia (<50 bpm)
Tachycardia (>100 bpm) in the absence of heart failure
Suspected right ventricular infarction (inferior STEMI with right-sided involvement)
Use of phosphodiesterase inhibitors for erectile dysfunction within 24 hours of sildenafil or 48 hours of tadalafil.
Administering nitroglycerin in the presence of those contraindications can cause severe hypotension. 

• Avoid NSAIDs in patients with suspected ACS

Do not give nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (except aspirin), whether nonselective or COX-2 selective, to patients with suspected ACS due to increased risks of mortality, reinfarction, hypertension, heart failure and myocardial rupture. For patients regularly taking NSAIDs, document counseling to discontinue during the acute event. 

• Administer supplemental oxygen only when indicated

Provide oxygen only to patients with a pulse oximetry reading <90% or with signs of respiratory distress. Routine oxygen supplementation in normoxic patients is not supported by current evidence and is no longer recommended. 

CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care
Contributing action or omission: Delayed transfer to the emergency department or failure to activate emergency medical services when ACS is suspected increases time to reperfusion and risk of adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure rapid and appropriate transfer of patients with suspected ACS.

• Transfer immediately by ambulance when ACS is suspected

Arrange ambulance transport for patients in outpatient settings. Personal automobile use is associated with increased risk and should be avoided. EMS transport allows for cardiac monitoring, prehospital ECG acquisition and direct communication with receiving facilities. Document the time the transfer was initiated and the method used.
• Communicate critical information to EMS and receiving facility

Provide EMS with essential clinical details, including symptom onset time, ECG findings (if obtained), medications administered (such as aspirin or nitroglycerin), vital signs and relevant medical history. If STEMI was identified on the prehospital ECG, EMS can activate the cardiac catheterization laboratory from the field, reducing door-to-balloon time. Clear communication ensures coordinated care and minimizes delays. 

• Understand reperfusion time targets

Ensure that primary percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) occurs within 120 minutes of first medical contact for patients with STEMI. If PCI cannot be achieved within this timeframe and symptoms have been present for less than 12 hours, fibrinolytic therapy is recommended at non-PCI-capable facilities. Awareness of these time targets reinforces the urgency of rapid transfer and helps family physicians communicate appropriately with patients and EMS. 

• Do not delay transfer for additional testing

When ACS is suspected, don’t wait to obtain additional diagnostic testing in the office setting. Serial troponin measurements, advanced imaging and risk stratification should take place in the ED, which has appropriate monitoring and intervention capabilities. ACC/AHA guidelines explicitly discourage delaying transfer for office-based testing.


ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR
Failure to Recognize Responsibility
Contributing action or omission: Failing to recognize the family physician's roles in post-MI care coordination, cardiac rehabilitation referral and secondary prevention increases the risk of recurrent events and medication non-adherence.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate longitudinal care and secondary prevention.

• Ensure timely post-discharge follow-up

Schedule post-MI follow-up within 1–2 weeks for higher-risk patients (e.g., age ≥65, reduced LVEF, incomplete revascularization) and within 2–4 weeks for lower-risk patients, but no later than 6 weeks for any patient, as delays beyond this threshold are associated with significantly lower medication adherence. Coordinate follow-up with both cardiology and primary care before discharge, as collaborative follow-up is associated with higher rates of evidence-based medication use. Share discharge summaries—including diagnosis, procedures, prescribed medications and pending issues—with all follow-up providers and confirm patient understanding of the follow-up plan using teach-back methods.
• Confirm cardiac rehabilitation referral and enrollment

Refer all patients with ACS to an outpatient cardiac rehabilitation program before hospital discharge or during the first follow-up office visit. Cardiac rehabilitation reduces death, MI, hospital readmissions and improves functional status and quality of life (Class I, Level A recommendation). Home-based cardiac rehabilitation is a reasonable alternative for patients facing barriers to center-based programs.
• Perform medication reconciliation at each visit

Review all cardiovascular medications at each post-MI visit, including aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitor, statin, beta-blocker, ACE inhibitor/ARB and SGLT2 inhibitor as indicated. Check for adherence, side effects and barriers to obtaining medications. Dual antiplatelet therapy is typically continued for at least 1 year; coordinate duration with the patient's cardiologist. 

• Systematically address modifiable risk factors

Encourage smoking cessation, annual influenza vaccination and cardiac rehabilitation to improve mortality in patients with recent MI (SORT B recommendation). Screen for depression, which is an underappreciated predictor of medication non-adherence. Address lifestyle factors, including diet, exercise and weight management, at each visit. 



ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Inadequately documenting symptom assessment, clinical reasoning, interventions and patient education disrupts continuity of care and weakens defensibility.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support documentation that reinforces clinical decision-making and provides a defensible record.

• Document symptom characteristics and cardiovascular risk factors

Record the details of presenting symptoms, including location, quality, duration, radiation, associated symptoms (such as dyspnea, diaphoresis and nausea) and timing of onset. Include relevant cardiovascular risk factors and prior cardiac history. This level of documentation demonstrates a thorough evaluation and supports clinical decision-making. 

Sample documentation language: "Patient presents with substernal chest pressure radiating to left arm, onset 45 minutes ago at rest, associated with diaphoresis. Risk factors include HTN, DM2, hyperlipidemia, former smoker. No prior CAD. Symptoms concerning for ACS."

• Document ECG findings and interpretation

Record the time of ECG acquisition, findings and interpretation. Note any comparison with prior ECGs if available. If ECG ST-segment elevation is present, document the immediate activation of transfer. If an ECG is not obtained due to prioritizing transfer, include the clinical reasoning. 

Sample documentation language: "ECG obtained at 10:42 shows ST depression in leads V4-V6, no prior ECG available for comparison. Findings concerning for NSTE-ACS. EMS activated immediately."

• Document medication administration and contraindication assessment

Record all medications administered, including dose and time. Note assessment for contraindications before giving nitroglycerin, including blood pressure, heart rate, PDE5 inhibitor use and suspected RV infarction. When choosing not to administer a medication, document the reason.

Sample documentation language: "Aspirin 325 mg chewed at 10:45. Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL at 10:48 after confirming BP 142/88, HR 78, no PDE5 inhibitor use. Patient denies sildenafil/tadalafil use in past 48 hours."

• Document transfer communication and patient education

Record the time EMS was activated, information communicated to EMS and the receiving facility and patient/family education provided. Note the patient’s understanding of the urgency of the transfer and instructions given to them. 

Sample documentation language: "EMS activated at 10:50. Communicated to EMS: suspected NSTE-ACS, aspirin and NTG administered, symptom onset 45 minutes prior. Patient and spouse educated regarding suspected heart attack and need for emergency evaluation. Patient verbalized understanding."
• Document informed refusal when a patient declines EMS transport
When a patient with suspected ACS refuses ambulance transport and opts for self-transport to the ED, document the informed refusal conversation, including the following:
Clinical concern: Suspected ACS/heart attack
Risks of self-transport: Cardiac arrest en route, delayed reperfusion and death
Recommendation for EMS transport and the patient's stated reason for refusal
Confirmation that the patient understood the risks
Alternative safety measures: Having someone else drive and proceeding directly to the nearest ED without delay
Name of any witness present
Documentation transforms potential liability exposure into evidence of sound clinical judgment and patient communication. 
Sample documentation language: “Suspected ACS discussed with patient. Strongly recommended EMS transport to ED given risk of cardiac arrest during self-transport (cited as 1 in 300). Patient declines ambulance, states he prefers to drive himself. Risks of self-transport explained, including cardiac arrest, delayed treatment, disability and death. Patient verbalized understanding of risks and continues to decline EMS stating a preference to go in personal vehicle. Spouse present for discussion. Advised patient to proceed directly to [Hospital Name] ED without stopping, instructed spouse to call 911 immediately if symptoms worsen en route. Patient agrees to these precautions.”

ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS
Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care
Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify and address barriers to medication adherence, follow-up attendance and lifestyle modification increases the risk of recurrent cardiovascular events.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient engagement and address barriers to secondary prevention.

• Assess barriers to medication adherence at each visit

Identify hurdles, such as cost, polypharmacy, side effects, lack of understanding of benefits and forgetfulness. Medication adherence after MI decreases over time, with only 29% of patients maintaining sufficient adherence to all drug classes in the first year. Patients with lower incomes, higher copayments and no caregiver support are more likely to discontinue therapy. Consider referral to pharmacy assistance programs or social work as indicated. 

• Use teach-back method to confirm understanding

Ensure that patients understand their medication regimen, warning signs requiring emergency evaluation and follow-up plan using teach-back methodology. Provide verbal and written educational information in the patient's preferred language. Patients who understand the benefits of long-term therapy demonstrate better adherence. 

• Address social determinants of health

Evaluate barriers to attending cardiac rehabilitation, such as transportation, work obligations, childcare and insurance coverage. Home-based or hybrid cardiac rehabilitation programs may improve access for patients with barriers to center-based programs. Document identified challenges and attempted interventions. 

• Document non-adherence and continued outreach

Record efforts to contact patients and their responses when they miss follow-up appointments, decline medications or fail to enroll in cardiac rehabilitation. If they refuse recommended care, especially EMS transport during acute events, include details of the conversation, such as recommendations, risks discussed and stated reasons for the refusal. Continue outreach by phone, patient portal or mail. 

Sample documentation language: "Patient reports stopping clopidogrel 2 months ago due to cost. Discussed importance of dual antiplatelet therapy for stent patency and MI prevention. Referred to pharmacy assistance program. Patient agrees to restart medication once assistance obtained. Follow-up call scheduled in 1 week to confirm."

Acute myocardial infarction is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in family medicine. 
Mitigating Your Risk
With rapid recognition of ischemic symptoms, appropriate initial management, timely EMS transfer and structured post-event follow-up, family medicine physicians can mitigate the risks

TITLE: Cancer
RISK_BRIEF:
Cancer Diagnoses

SPECIALTY: Family Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Symptomatic Presentations, Screening Encounters, Abnormal Test Results

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Advanced-Stage Disease, Morbidity, Mortality
Complications of cancer diagnosis and management are a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in family medicine.
Mitigating Your Risk
With guideline-adherent screening, timely evaluation and referral and systematic follow-up, family medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with cancer diagnosie and management and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for cancer diagnoses and management and provides strategies to help prevent them. 
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving cancers in family medicine typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 76% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 24%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.   

Actions and Omissions Driving Complications related to Cancer Diagnoses and Management [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.

CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam
Contributing action or omission: Incomplete assessment of cancer risk factors, family history and presenting symptoms increases the likelihood of missed or delayed cancer diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure comprehensive risk assessment and proper clinical evaluation of patients presenting with potential cancer symptoms.

• Obtain and review previous medical records for new and transferring patients
Request medical records from prior providers and review promptly, as they may contain previously documented genetic testing, abnormal findings, pending evaluations or cancer history not reported by the patient. Do not rely solely on patient-reported history; studies show significant rates of inaccurate or incomplete self-reporting, with false-negative rates for cancer history as high as 39% when compared to registry data. Assign responsibility for tracking record requests and reviewing incoming documents.
• Obtain and document family cancer history 

At new patient visits and annual checkups, assess family cancer history, include first-, second- and third-degree relatives, age at diagnosis and cancer type. Use validated risk assessment tools, such as Tyrer-Cuzick for breast cancer and family history criteria for colorectal cancer, to identify patients needing enhanced screening or a genetic counseling referral. Documenting the full history shows adherence to care standards and supports optimal risk stratification.

• Perform a focused physical exam for potential cancer symptoms

Examine relevant organ systems when a patient’s symptoms could indicate malignancy, such as a breast lump, rectal bleeding, unexplained weight loss or persistent cough. Document findings including pertinent negatives. Failure to perform an adequate physical exam is a contributing factor in 42% of diagnostic error claims. 
• Assess for alarm symptoms that warrant urgent evaluation

Recognize and act on alarm symptoms with a high positive predictive value for cancer. Examples include breast lump (PPV 14.8%), postmenopausal bleeding (PPV 3.9%), hemoptysis (PPV 2.7%), rectal bleeding (PPV 2.6%), hematuria (PPV 2.2%) and change in bowel habits (PPV 1.8%). Patients with those symptoms should receive expedited evaluation, not watchful waiting. Document the presence or absence of alarm symptoms, clinical reasoning and patient counseling on the importance of follow-up testing.

• Consider cancer in the differential for patients with nonspecific symptoms

Patients presenting with fatigue, weight loss or abdominal pain, are at higher risk for diagnostic delay. If symptoms persist despite initial treatment or do not fit a clear benign diagnosis, expand the differential to include malignancy. Document clinical reasoning when attributing symptoms to benign causes.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing
Contributing action or omission: Failing to order correct screening tests, diagnostic imaging or lab evaluations when indicated by symptoms, risk factors or guidelines increases the risk of delayed cancer diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely and appropriate diagnostic testing for cancer detection.

• Adhere to evidence-based cancer screening guidelines 

Draw from multiple authoritative guideline sources when screening for cancer. For average-risk screening, USPSTF recommendations provide the primary evidence-based framework. Consider AAFP positions when they differ. Specialty society guidelines, such as those from ACOG, AUA, NCCN and ACS, are valuable for high-risk populations, managing abnormal results and clinical scenarios not fully addressed by USPSTF. Document screening discussions, patient decisions and any deviations from guidelines with clinical rationale.

• Apply specialty society guidelines for patients with elevated risk

Use guidelines such as NCCN, ACG and ACS for high-risk patients based on family history, genetic syndromes or personal history. These guidelines provide more detailed recommendations than USPSTF. Document the rationale for using enhanced screening protocols.

• Order diagnostic mammography for symptomatic breast complaints
Do not use screening mammography for patients presenting with a breast lump or other breast symptoms, as it is intended for asymptomatic patients and may miss cancers in symptomatic cases. Document the indication for imaging and notify the imaging facility of the clinical concern.

• Order follow-up tests when indicated
Promptly address abnormal or inconclusive screening or diagnostic tests with further testing. Failure to follow up is a leading factor in diagnostic error claims. Establish systems to track pending test results and ensure that recommended patient follow-up is completed.

• Use fecal immunochemical testing (FIT) judiciously for symptomatic patients

Understand that FIT is validated for colorectal cancer screening in asymptomatic patients. However, colonoscopy is the appropriate diagnostic test, regardless of FIT results, in patients with symptoms suggesting colorectal cancer, such as rectal bleeding, change in bowel habits or iron deficiency anemia. Document clinical reasoning when selecting a diagnostic test.

• Expedite diagnosis with direct-access testing 

When available, consider using pathways like direct-access colonoscopy or CT imaging to reduce time to diagnosis.
• Advocate for radiology-led communication and scheduling after abnormal mammograms
Establish protocols for interpreting radiologists to communicate abnormal mammographic results directly to the referring physician and the patient and to schedule recommended follow-up imaging or biopsy without waiting for a PCP referral. This approach has been shown to increase timely follow-up from 73% to 90% and reduce the median time to diagnostic evaluation from 28 to 9 days. Without such protocols, failure to follow up abnormal mammograms within 6 months ranges from 27%–72% in primary care settings.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care
Contributing action or omission: Delays in referring patients with suspected cancer to appropriate specialists or escalating care for persistent symptoms increase the risk of advanced-stage diagnosis and adverse outcomes.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely specialist involvement and proper escalation of care.

• Send patients with alarm symptoms for urgent specialist evaluation

Promptly refer for signs such as a breast lump, unexplained rectal bleeding in patients ≥40, iron deficiency anemia or persistent unexplained symptoms. Communicate directly with the specialist when clinical concern is high and document urgency.
• Establish referral pathways 

Monitor and confirm attendance at specialist appointments. Contact patients who do not complete referrals and address their barriers to care. Document outreach attempts and patient responses.
• Re-evaluate and escalate care for persistent symptoms 
Reconsider the differential diagnosis and pursue additional testing or specialist referral if patients return with persistent or worsening symptoms after an initial negative evaluation.  Document the clinical reasoning for continued evaluation. Approximately 24% of cancer patients experience avoidable delays, often due to initial treatment for presumed benign conditions.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31810885
• Communicate clinical concern directly to specialists

When referring patients with suspected malignancy, reach out to the specialist through a referral letter or direct contact. Include relevant symptom history, exam findings, test results and reason for referral. Clear communication supports triage and reduces delays.

• Consider referral for genetic counseling 

Refer patients with family history suggestive of hereditary cancer syndromes, such as multiple affected relatives, early-onset cancers or specific cancer patterns, for genetic counseling and possible testing. Document the family history and referral decisions.

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers 
Contributing action or omission: Communication failures between primary care and specialists—such as inadequate referral information or failure to receive or act on consultant recommendations—increase the risk of diagnostic delays. 

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure effective communication across the care continuum.

• Include essential clinical information in referral communications

Provide presenting symptoms with duration and characteristics, relevant exam findings, test results, family history, reason for referral and level of clinical concern. Missing details can delay triage and evaluation.

• Act promptly on specialist recommendations

Develop processes to receive, review and act on specialist consultation reports. Document recommendations and follow-up actions. Failing to act on specialist recommendations is a known contributor to diagnostic errors. 
• Use handoff protocols during care transitions
Apply frameworks such as SBAR during care transitions to communicate pending diagnostic evaluations, abnormal test results requiring follow-up and clinical concerns. Record handoff details in the medical record.

• Close the loop on diagnostic testing ordered by other providers

Review and share diagnostic test results ordered by covering physicians, urgent care providers and specialists with patients. Ensure that follow-up is arranged and responsibility for results is assigned across providers.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider
Contributing action or omission: Failing to effectively communicate cancer risk, screening recommendations, test results and the importance of follow-up increases the likelihood of missed diagnoses and patient non-adherence.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support clear patient communication and shared decision-making.

• Engage in shared decision-making for cancer screening

Discuss benefits, risks and patient preferences for screening decisions where guidelines recommend individualized approaches, such as for prostate cancer screening (ages 55–69), colorectal cancer screening (ages 76–85) and breast cancer screening in older adults. Acknowledge guideline differences when they exist and incorporate patient values into decisions. Document the shared decision-making conversation and the patient's informed choice.

• Communicate test results and ensure patient understanding

Use plain language, not medical jargon, to explain cancer-related test results and confirm patient understanding with teach-back methods. For abnormal results, explain the significance, next steps and timeline for follow-up. Document communication of results and the patient’s stated understanding.
• Provide written guidance for follow-up and warning signs

Include details on follow-up appointments, additional testing and symptoms that require immediate evaluation. Ensure that materials are tailored to the patient's language and literacy level.

• Discuss the importance of completing recommended diagnostic evaluations

Emphasize the need for diagnostic testing or specialist evaluations, when referring patients, including the potential consequences of non-compliance.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38902082 Address patient concerns and barriers to follow-up. Document the conversation and the patient's stated understanding.

ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of clinical reasoning, screening discussions, test results and follow-up plans compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defense against liability.

• Document cancer screening status and discussions at each visit

Record screening status for age- and risk-appropriate cancers, including the date of the last screening, results and next recommended screening. If screening is discussed but declined, document the conversation, information provided and the patient's stated reasons for refusal. When applying guidelines from different organizations, document which guideline is being followed and the rationale. 
Sample documentation language: "Discussed colorectal cancer screening options. Per USPSTF, screening is recommended starting at age 45; per AAFP, age 50. Patient is 47 years old with no family history. Discussed options, including colonoscopy and FIT. Patient prefers FIT; ordered annual FIT. Colonoscopy recommended if FIT positive. Patient verbalized understanding."

• Document clinical reasoning when cancer is considered or excluded from the differential

When evaluating symptoms that could mean malignancy, record the differential diagnosis, clinical reasoning and rationale for management decisions. When symptoms are attributed to benign causes, document the basis for this conclusion and plan for reassessment if symptoms persist. 

Sample documentation language: "45 y/o female with 6-week history of change in bowel habits. No rectal bleeding, weight loss or family history of CRC. Exam: abdomen soft, nontender, no masses. Given persistent symptoms and age, referred for diagnostic colonoscopy to evaluate for colorectal pathology."

• Document review of test results and patient notification

Include normal results, date reviewed and patient notification method. For abnormal results, document communication with the patient, their understanding and the follow-up plan. 

• Document follow-up plans and safety-netting instructions

Include reassessment timeline, indications for earlier return and symptoms prompting immediate evaluation. Safety-netting documentation demonstrates proper clinical vigilance and supports defensibility if symptoms progress. 
Sample documentation language: "Discussed that if symptoms persist beyond 4 weeks or worsen, patient should return for further evaluation. Patient instructed to seek immediate care if develops rectal bleeding, unintentional weight loss or severe abdominal pain."

ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS
Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care
Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify and address barriers to screening completion, diagnostic testing and specialist follow-up increases the likelihood of delayed cancer diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and address barriers to timely evaluation.

• Identify and address barriers to cancer screening

Evaluate challenges patients may face in completing screenings, including transportation, cost, time constraints, fear and cultural factors. Offer multiple screening methods when available, such as FIT vs. colonoscopy for colorectal cancer screening, to accommodate patient preferences and increase adherence.

• Implement systems for overdue screening

Leverage EHR-based reminders, patient portals and outreach programs to identify patients behind on cancer screenings and encourage them to follow through. Organized outreach programs have been shown to increase screening rates and reduce disparities.

• Track incomplete diagnostic evaluations

Establish systems to monitor patients who have not completed recommended follow-up for referred diagnostic testing or specialist evaluations. Contact those  who miss appointments and document outreach attempts and responses.
• Document patient non-adherence and continued outreach

Record when patients decline recommended screening, diagnostic testing or specialist referrals. Include the recommendation, information provided, patient's stated reasons and plan for future discussions. Continue to address screening and follow-up at subsequent visits.

Sample documentation language: "Patient declined colonoscopy referral for evaluation of rectal bleeding, citing fear of procedure. Discussed importance of evaluation given symptoms. Offered FIT as alternative; patient declined. Will revisit at next visit. Patient instructed to return immediately if bleeding worsens or new symptoms develop."

TITLE: Cerebrovascular Accident
RISK_BRIEF:
Cerebrovascular Accident

SPECIALTY: Family Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Acute Neurologic Deficits, Transient Ischemic Attack, Stroke Warning Signs

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Permanent Neurologic Disability, Missed Thrombolytic Window, Preventable Stroke Recurrence, Death
Cerebrovascular accident is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in family medicine.
Mitigating Your Risk

With early identification, timely management and effective care transitions, family medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with cerebrovascular accident and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for a cerebrovascular accident and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving cerebrovascular accidents typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 74% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 26%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.

Actions and Omissions Driving Cerebrovascular Accidents [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Failing to elicit stroke-specific history, recognize atypical presentations or perform a focused neurologic examination increases the risk of missed or delayed stroke diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve early stroke identification and reduce diagnostic errors.

• Screen for stroke risk factors during routine primary care visits

Assess patients for modifiable stroke risk factors such as hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidemia, atrial fibrillation, smoking, physical inactivity, obesity and sleep-disordered breathing. The AHA recommends screening for traditional risk factors every 4–6 years in adults ages 20–79, with more frequent screening for those with borderline values. Use validated cardiovascular risk prediction tools to guide shared decision-making about preventive therapy. Documenting risk factor assessments supports prevention and defensibility.
• Establish precise symptom onset timing at initial presentation

For any acute neurologic complaint, determine the exact time of symptom onset or last known well (when the patient was last at neurological baseline). This timing is critical for determining eligibility for thrombolytic therapy (within 4.5 hours) and mechanical thrombectomy (up to 24 hours in select patients). Document who reported the timing of symptom onset, such as the patient, family member or witness. Failing to establish accurate timing may preclude time-sensitive interventions and demonstrates incomplete evaluation. 

• Recognize high-risk presentations for missed stroke diagnosis

Maintain vigilance for stroke in patients with dizziness, vertigo, nausea, vomiting, altered mental status or symptom resolution. These symptoms are associated with significantly higher misdiagnosis rates. Posterior circulation strokes are missed at more than twice the rate of anterior circulation strokes. Patients who are FAST-negative (no face, arm or speech abnormalities) remain at substantial risk for stroke and require careful evaluation. 

• Perform and document a focused neurologic examination

Assess the patient’s mental status, cranial nerves, motor function, sensation, coordination and gait in those with acute neurologic symptoms. The National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS) provides a standardized assessment tool that facilitates provider communication and monitors clinical changes. Document both positive and negative findings to demonstrate a thorough evaluation.

• Use the HINTS examination for patients with acute vestibular syndrome

Perform the HINTS examination (Head-Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew) on patients presenting with acute dizziness, vertigo, nystagmus and gait instability (acute vestibular syndrome). HINTS is more sensitive for cerebellar stroke than early MRI and helps differentiate between central and peripheral causes. A dangerous HINTS pattern (normal head impulse, direction-changing nystagmus, skew deviation) should prompt urgent stroke evaluation.

CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention 
Contributing action or omission: Delays in initiating stroke evaluation, failure to recognize thrombolytic eligibility or inappropriate management of transient ischemic attack (TIA) increase the risk of preventable disability and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely and proper treatment of acute stroke and TIA.

• Activate emergency response immediately for suspected stroke

When a patient presents with acute neurologic deficits consistent with stroke, mobilize emergency medical services and call 911 rather than attempting an outpatient workup. Current guidelines recommend performing head imaging within 25 minutes of the patient’s arrival to enable rapid decision-making regarding thrombolytic therapy. Outpatient evaluation delays time-sensitive treatment and is inconsistent with the standard of care for acute stroke.

• Recognize the expanded treatment windows for acute ischemic stroke

Understand that acute ischemic stroke treatment eligibility extends beyond the traditional 3-hour window. Intravenous alteplase can be administered within 4.5 hours of symptom onset; mechanical thrombectomy may benefit select patients with large vessel occlusion up to 24 hours from last known well (the last time patient was known to be at neurological baseline) based on advanced imaging criteria. Patients presenting with wake-up strokes or unclear onset times may still be treatment candidates. This knowledge emphasizes the importance of making an urgent referral.
• Treat TIA as a medical emergency requiring urgent evaluation

Follow current AHA/ASA guidelines, which recommend evaluating patients with suspected TIA within 24 hours of symptom onset, with MRI preferred over CT. Patients experiencing a TIA face a 90-day stroke risk of 3%–18%, depending on risk factors, with the highest risk occurring in the first 48 hours. For high-risk TIA or minor stroke, initiate dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin plus clopidogrel) within 24 hours of onset and continue for 21 days to reduce early recurrence by 25%.

• Initiate suitable secondary prevention promptly after hospital discharge

Implement interventions for patients with confirmed stroke or TIA, including antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy (tailored to the cause of the stroke), statin therapy, blood pressure control, diabetes management and lifestyle counseling. If carotid revascularization is indicated, aim to perform it within 2 weeks for most patients. Ensure that all measures and relevant referrals are documented.

CLINICAL: TREATMENT 
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care

Contributing action or omission: Failing to arrange timely transfer to stroke-capable facilities or consult appropriate specialists delays definitive treatment and increases the risk of adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies for Hospital-Based Family Medicine Providers

The following strategies apply to family physicians working in emergency departments, as hospitalists or in facilities without stroke capability. 

• Understand the regional stroke care system and transfer protocols

Establish relationships with primary stroke centers (PSC), comprehensive stroke centers (CSC) and thrombectomy-capable stroke centers (TSC) in the region. Hospitals lacking neurologic or radiologic personnel should implement protocols for rapid assessment and transfer. For patients with suspected large vessel occlusion, prioritize direct transport to a thrombectomy-capable center if it adds no more than 15 minutes travel time compared to the nearest PSC. 
• Utilize telestroke services when available

Activate telestroke services early to mitigate delays, ideally before patient arrival. Telestroke enables hospitals without on-site neurology coverage to access stroke expertise and is endorsed by the AHA as a Class I intervention. Consultations aid in administering thrombolytics, identifying patients who need transfer for thrombectomy and supporting clinical decision-making.
• Coordinate post-stroke follow-up before discharge

Schedule an appointment for the patient with primary care within 7–14 days of hospital discharge. Ensure that prolonged cardiac monitoring (≥30 days) is completed if not done during hospitalization; monitoring detects atrial fibrillation in about 9% of patients. Confirm that carotid imaging has been completed when indicated. Document the follow-up plan and communicate it to the patient and receiving providers.
Mitigation Strategies for Office-Based Family Medicine Providers
The following strategies apply to family physicians in outpatient practice.
• Recognize stroke and TIA requiring emergency evaluation
Immediately transfer patients who present with acute focal neurologic symptoms to the emergency department via EMS for evaluation. The American Heart Association recommends that patients with suspected TIA be evaluated within 24 hours of symptom onset. Do not delay emergency referral to perform office-based imaging or testing.
• Arrange urgent neurology consultation for complex presentations

Refer patients with atypical presentations, posterior circulation symptoms or diagnostic uncertainty. Early collaboration between primary care physicians and neurologists after a stroke is critical for making complex, cause-specific treatment decisions. Document consultation requests and recommendations.
• Ensure completion of deferred diagnostic workup
At the first post-stroke visit (recommended within 1–3 weeks of discharge), verify that the stroke evaluation is complete. Key deferred evaluations include:
Prolonged cardiac monitoring (≥30 days) to detect occult atrial fibrillation, which is found in approximately 9% of patients with cryptogenic stroke
Carotid imaging if not completed during hospitalization 
Echocardiography if a cardioembolic source is suspected but not yet identified
Follow-up brain MRI if initial CT was negative but clinical suspicion for stroke remains high (CT has only 39% sensitivity compared to 99% for MRI) 
• Coordinate time-sensitive interventions and transitions
Certain post-stroke interventions are time-sensitive and require primary care coordination: 
Carotid revascularization should be performed within 2 weeks if indicated 
Dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin plus clopidogrel or ticagrelor) should be transitioned to single antiplatelet therapy after 21–30 days in most patients to reduce bleeding risk 
Neurology follow-up should occur within 1 week for patients with TIA or minor stroke
Document all interventions, follow-up plans and communicate them to patients and specialists. 
• Assess for residual deficits and screen for poststroke depression at follow-up visits
At the first post-stroke visit and periodically during the first year, evaluate for residual motor, speech, cognitive and swallowing deficits, referring patients with unmet needs to physical, occupational or speech therapy. Screen for depression using the PHQ-9; depression affects up to 30% of stroke survivors and is associated with worse functional recovery and increased mortality if untreated. Initiate treatment with antidepressants and/or psychotherapy referral if screening is positive. Document functional status, screening results, referrals and management decisions.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Communication failures during care transitions—including inadequate handoffs, unclear escalation pathways and incomplete information transfer—can delay recognition and treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support coordinated care.

• Implement handoff protocols for stroke patients

Use frameworks such as SBAR when transferring care from outpatient to emergency settings, between shifts and from hospital to post-acute care. Communicate symptom onset time, neurologic status, imaging findings, treatments and pending evaluations. Document handoff information in the medical record. 
• Provide complete clinical information during emergency referral

Communicate directly with the receiving team when referring a patient with suspected stroke to the emergency department. Share critical details, such as time of symptom onset or last known well (when the patient was last at baseline), presenting symptoms, relevant medical history (including anticoagulant use), current medications and baseline functional status. This information is critical for determining treatment eligibility and should be documented.

• Establish communication pathways with stroke centers

Develop protocols for direct communication with regional stroke centers for patients requiring transfer. Notify the receiving facility early to allow preparation for immediate evaluation and treatment. For patients with suspected large vessel occlusion, share imaging findings and clinical severity to facilitate triage decisions.

• Communicate stroke history and prevention plan to all providers

After a patient has a stroke, share the diagnosis and its implications with all providers involved in the person’s care. Document the stroke event, including presumed pathogenesis, treatments and the secondary prevention plan. This document serves as a reference point for stroke-specific care at future visits and ensures consistent management across providers. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION 
Communication Failure Between Provider and Patient

Contributing action or omission: Failing to educate patients about warning signs, modifiable risks and indications that they should seek emergency care can delay presentation and increase recurrence of stroke. 
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient engagement and timely care-seeking.

• Educate patients about stroke warning signs using FAST

Teach patients about stroke warning signs using the FAST mnemonic (Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 911). Stress that stroke is a medical emergency requiring them to immediately contact emergency services rather than calling the physician or driving to the hospital. Document education and confirm patient understanding. 

• Counsel high-risk patients on stroke prevention

Advise patients with modifiable risk factors on stroke prevention strategies, including:
Controlling blood pressure
Controlling diabetes
Quitting smoking
Increasing physical activity
Following a healthy diet such as the Mediterranean diet, which reduces stroke risk
Managing their weight
Taking medications as directed.
The AHA’s Life's Essential 8 framework offers additional guidelines for reducing stroke risk. 

• Address barriers to follow-up and medication adherence

Identify challenges to follow-up, such as transportation, costs and health literacy. Consider offering telehealth options for follow-up visits when suitable. For patients who miss appointments or decline recommended monitoring, document outreach attempts and responses.
ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUE

Failure or Lack of Process, Policy or Procedure

Contributing action or omission: Lack of protocols for stroke recognition, triage and transfer undermines consistent care delivery and timely treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve readiness and response to stroke emergencies.

• Develop office protocols for recognizing and managing acute neurologic emergencies

Include criteria for immediate EMS activation, key information to communicate to emergency services and documentation requirements for patients with acute neurologic symptoms in outpatient settings. Train all clinical staff on these protocols. 
• Maintain current knowledge of regional stroke resources

Keep updated information on regional stroke center locations, transfer protocols and capabilities, including the distinctions between PSC, CSC and TSC. Identify facilities with 24/7 thrombectomy services and expected transport times from practice location. This information supports appropriate triage decisions. 
• Implement stroke risk factor screening

Develop EHR-based tools or checklists to prompt systematic screening for stroke risk factors during preventive care visits. Include prompts for blood pressure measurement, atrial fibrillation screening, diabetes screening, lipid assessment and lifestyle counseling. Automated reminders can improve screening rates and documentation. 

• Participate in quality improvement activities

Engage in initiatives focused on stroke prevention and recognition. Track metrics such as blood pressure control rates, anticoagulation rates for atrial fibrillation and statin prescribing for eligible patients. Use data to identify improvement opportunities and implement evidence-based interventions. 


ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Insufficient documentation of clinical findings, decision-making rationale, patient education and care coordination compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to help create comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document symptom onset time and source of information

Record the time of symptom onset or last known well baseline for any patient presenting with neurologic symptoms. Identify the source of this information, such as a patient report, family member or witness. If timing is uncertain, include that fact and the basis for estimates. This documentation is essential for determining treatment eligibility and demonstrates thorough evaluation.

• Document neurologic examination findings 

Use a systematic approach to record neurologic examination findings, including both positive and negative results. When using the NIHSS, document the total score and individual component scores. Document serial exams to track clinical change. Documenting these details demonstrates proper evaluation and monitoring. 

Sample documentation language: "Neurologic examination: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation intact to light touch. Coordination intact with finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin. Gait steady. NIHSS 0."

• Document clinical reasoning for management decisions

Capture the factors considered when making decisions about stroke evaluation, treatment or referral. Include symptom characteristics, timing, risk factors, exam findings and the differential diagnosis. If symptoms resolve or are atypical, record the reasoning behind the chosen management approach. This level of documentation shows thoughtful clinical judgment. 

Sample documentation language: "Patient presents with 20-minute episode of right arm weakness and word-finding difficulty that resolved prior to arrival. Given transient focal neurologic symptoms, concerning for TIA, patient counseled on need for urgent evaluation. EMS activated for transport to [Hospital Name] for emergent imaging and stroke workup. Patient and family educated on stroke warning signs."

• Document patient education and understanding

Before discharge or at the end of a visit, record patient education about stroke warning signs, when to seek emergency care, medication instructions and follow-up plans. Note the patient's stated understanding and any barriers to adherence. 

• Document care coordination and communication

Record communication with consultants, emergency departments and other providers regarding stroke patients. Include handoff details, recommendations and actions. For patients who miss follow-up appointments, document outreach attempts and patient response.

TITLE: Diabetes
RISK_BRIEF:
Diabetes Diagnosis and Management

SPECIALTY: Family Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus, Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Prediabetes, Diabetes Complications

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Microvascular Complications (Retinopathy, Nephropathy, Neuropathy), Macrovascular Complications (Cardiovascular Disease, Stroke), Hypoglycemia, Diabetic Ketoacidosis, Amputations, End-stage Renal Disease, Death
Diabetes diagnosis and management is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in family medicine.
Mitigating Your Risk

With systematic screening, evidence-based medication management, timely specialist referrals and thorough documentation, family medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with diabetes diagnosis and management and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for diabetes management and provides strategies to help prevent them.
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving diabetes management typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 83% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 17%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Diabetes Diagnosis and Management Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing
Contributing action or omission: Failure to screen appropriate patients for diabetes, obtain confirmatory testing or conduct recommended surveillance for disease progression and complications increases the risk of delayed diagnosis and preventable harm.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely diagnosis of diabetes and recognition of disease progression.

• Screen all adults aged 35 years and older for prediabetes and type 2 diabetes

During routine health visits, test asymptomatic adults using HbA1c, fasting plasma glucose or a 2-hour oral glucose tolerance test. Include individuals with BMI ≥25 kg/m² (≥23 kg/m² for Asian Americans) with one or more additional risk factors regardless of age. If results are normal, repeat every 3 years; test annually if prediabetes is found. Systematic screening identifies the estimated 25% of U.S. adults with undiagnosed diabetes, enabling earlier intervention to prevent complications.
• Confirm diabetes diagnosis with repeat testing

Perform a second confirmatory test if a single result meets diagnostic criteria (HbA1c ≥6.5%, fasting glucose ≥126 mg/dL or 2-hour glucose ≥200 mg/dL), either by repeating the initial test or using a different one, unless the patient has unequivocal hyperglycemia with classic symptoms. Confirmatory testing reduces diagnostic errors and establishes a defensible diagnosis.

• Assess HbA1c at regular intervals based on glycemic control

Measure HbA1c at least every 6 months for patients meeting glycemic targets and every 3 months for those not meeting them or with recent medication adjustments. Regular monitoring allows timely treatment intensification and demonstrates adherence to standard of care.

• Perform annual screening for diabetes complications

Obtain urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio and serum creatinine with eGFR at least annually to screen for diabetic kidney disease. Ensure that patients receive yearly dilated eye examinations with a referral to ophthalmology or optometry. Conduct comprehensive foot exams, including 10-g monofilament testing, pedal pulse assessment and visual inspection, at least annually. Screen for peripheral neuropathy using pinprick, temperature or vibration sensation testing. Systematic screening for complications identifies early-stage disease when intervention is most effective and demonstrates adherence to ADA Standards of Care. 
• Consider alternative diagnoses when clinical course is atypical

Screen for type 1 diabetes with islet autoantibodies, such as glutamic acid decarboxylase antibodies, in patients with new-onset diabetes who adhere to oral therapy but have persistent hyperglycemia, unexpected weight loss or rapid progression to insulin requirement. Consider other diabetes subtypes when presentation is atypical. Misclassifying the diabetes type delays appropriate treatment and increases the risk of diabetic ketoacidosis.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Medication Error
Contributing action or omission: Failure to select appropriate medication, prescribe cardioprotective agents in eligible patients, administer accurate dosing and monitor adequately for adverse effects increases the risk of hypoglycemia and other complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure evidence-based selection of medication and safe prescribing practices.

• Assess hypoglycemia risk before prescribing insulin or sulfonylureas

Check patients for prior hypoglycemic events, impaired hypoglycemia awareness, chronic kidney disease, cognitive impairment, older age and polypharmacy before starting insulin, sulfonylureas or meglitinides. When glycemic targets can’t be met with oral agents, GLP-1 receptor agonists or dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonists are preferred over insulin as the initial injectable therapy, unless severe hyperglycemia (HbA1c >10% or glucose >300 mg/dL) or symptomatic hyperglycemia is present. Deprioritize sulfonylureas in patients 65 years and older.
• Individualize glycemic targets based on patient characteristics

Set HbA1c goals at <7% for most nonpregnant adults with diabetes. Consider stricter targets (<6.5%) for younger patients with short disease duration if achievable without hypoglycemia. Apply less stringent targets (7.5%–8.5%) for older adults and patients with limited life expectancy, extensive comorbidities, history of severe hypoglycemia or impaired hypoglycemia awareness. Individualized targets reduce hypoglycemia risk while maintaining optimal glycemic control. Document recommended glycemic target and supporting rationale.
• Adjust medications appropriately for renal function

Review eGFR before starting diabetes medications and periodically during treatment. Adjust metformin dosing for eGFR 30–45 mL/min/1.73 m² and discontinue if eGFR is <30 mL/min/1.73 m². SGLT2 inhibitors may be initiated or continued for cardiorenal protection at lower eGFR thresholds in accordance with current labeling. Dose-adjust or avoid sulfonylureas in patients with significant renal impairment. Medication adjustment based on renal function prevents accumulation and reduces hypoglycemia risk. 
• Educate patients on hypoglycemia recognition and treatment

Help patients at risk for hypoglycemia understand hypoglycemia definitions, precipitating factors (fasting, delayed meals, exercise and illness), symptoms and treatment steps (15–20 g fast-acting carbohydrate, recheck glucose in 15 minutes). Prescribe glucagon for patients at risk of severe hypoglycemia and train caregivers in its use. Document education provided to patients and caregivers. 

• Prescribe SGLT2 inhibitors or GLP-1 receptor agonists for eligible patients 
Initiate an SGLT2 inhibitor (canagliflozin, dapagliflozin or empagliflozin) or GLP-1 receptor agonist (dulaglutide, liraglutide or semaglutide) with proven cardiovascular or kidney benefits in patients with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, heart failure or chronic kidney disease, regardless of HbA1c level. SGLT2 inhibitors are preferred in patients with heart failure. Tirzepatide, a dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist, is an effective glucose-lowering and weight-reduction option but does not yet carry an FDA-approved cardiovascular indication. This recommendation reflects current ADA, AACE and ACP guidelines and represents the standard of care for cardiorenal protection. 
• Prescribe statin therapy for adults with diabetes 40–75 years
Initiate at least moderate-intensity statin therapy, regardless of baseline LDL cholesterol or estimated cardiovascular risk. Prescribe high-intensity statin therapy for patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease or multiple ASCVD risk factors. If a patient cannot tolerate the intended statin intensity, use the maximum tolerated dose. When statin therapy is not prescribed for an eligible patient, document the clinical reasoning, such as contraindication, patient refusal or adverse effects and the alternatives considered. Failure to prescribe statin therapy in this population represents deviation from Class 1 recommendations across ADA, ACC/AHA and AACE guidelines. 
• Prescribe ACE inhibitors or ARBs for eligible patients
Initiate an ACE inhibitor or ARB as first-line antihypertensive therapy in patients with diabetes who have albuminuria (UACR ≥30 mg/g creatinine) or coronary artery disease; titrate to the highest approved dose that is tolerated. For patients with severely increased albuminuria (UACR ≥300 mg/g) or eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73 m², ACEi/ARB therapy is strongly recommended to slow kidney disease progression and reduce cardiovascular events. Monitor serum creatinine and potassium within 2–4 weeks of initiation or dose increase. Avoid ACE inhibitors and ARBs in individuals of childbearing potential who are not using reliable contraception. Document the rationale when ACEi/ARB therapy is not initiated in eligible patients. 
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care
Contributing action or omission: Failure to refer patients for diabetes self-management education, specialist consultation or complication management increases the risk of suboptimal outcomes and delayed treatment of complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure proper care coordination and timely specialist involvement.

• Refer patients to diabetes self-management education and support (DSMES)
At diagnosis, connect patients with accredited DSMES programs or certified diabetes care and education specialists. Also, refer patients to diabetes support when they aren’t meeting control targets, when complications develop or during life transitions. DSMES improves glycemic control, reduces complications and is recommended by ADA for all individuals with type 2 diabetes. Document referral and patient participation. 

• Refer to a registered dietitian 

Connect all patients with diabetes to a registered dietitian for personalized medical nutrition therapy. This approach improves glycemic control and cardiovascular risk factors. Document the referral and include the dietitian’s recommendations in the care plan. 

• Consult endocrinology for complex diabetes management

Refer patients to endocrinology when they present with:
Suspected type 1 diabetes
Recurrent severe hypoglycemia
Multiple diabetes complications
Requirements for complex insulin regimens (multiple daily injections or insulin pump therapy), high-dose insulin (>200 units/day) and/or diabetes technology (continuous glucose monitors or automated insulin delivery)
Lack of expected response to treatment despite adherence to it
Document clinical reasoning for a referral or the decision to manage diabetes at the primary care level. 

• Ensure timely referrals to specialists

Refer patients to:
Ophthalmology for a dilated eye examination at diagnosis and annually thereafter
Nephrology when eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73 m² or when kidney function is rapidly declining
Podiatry for foot deformities, prior ulcers or amputations
Cardiology for cardiovascular disease or heart failure requiring subspecialty management
Document referrals and confirm that the patient has received and is following the specialists’ recommendations. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Failure to coordinate adequate care, complete handoffs and act on specialist recommendations increases the risk of fragmented care and missed opportunities for intervention.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support coordinated team responses.

• Ensure adequate care coordination for patients with diabetes

Establish processes to track referrals, receive specialist reports and incorporate recommendations into care plans. Use care coordinators, patient navigators or case managers for patients with complex needs, multiple comorbidities or social barriers. Team-based care improves glycemic control, blood pressure and lipid outcomes. 

• Document and act on specialist recommendations

After specialist consultations, record recommendations and outline an implementation plan. When choosing not to follow specialist recommendations, document the clinical reasoning behind the decision. Ensure closed-loop communication with specialists for shared patients. 

• Communicate diabetes diagnosis and management plan during care transitions

Ensure that receiving providers have current information on diabetes diagnosis, medications, most recent HbA1c, complication status and follow-up needs during care transitions, such as hospital discharge, provider transfers or changes in insurance. Use handoff tools when available. 


ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider
Contributing action or omission: Failure to communicate treatment goals, medication instructions, warning signs and follow-up plans to patients raises the risk of non-adherence, delays recognition of complications and increases adverse outcomes.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and shared decision-making.

• Collaborate with patients to set goals and make decisions about care

Engage patients in setting goals for glycemic targets, weight management and lifestyle modifications. Identify their preferences, values and barriers to care. Use active listening, open-ended questions and nonjudgmental language, avoiding terms like "noncompliant," which may discourage engagement. Document shared decisions and patient-stated goals. 
• Educate patients on medication use and warning signs
When prescribing diabetes medications, provide both written and verbal guidance on dosing, timing, potential side effects and situations requiring medical attention. Instruct patients at risk for hypoglycemia on symptoms, treatment and when to seek emergency care. Explain the signs of diabetic ketoacidosis and genital infections to patients on SGLT2 inhibitors. Document all patient education. 

• Confirm understanding with teach-back methods

Ask patients to explain medications, self-monitoring and warning signs in their own words. Address gaps and document their understanding. 


ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Failure to document clinical decision-making, patient education, medication changes and follow-up plans compromises care continuity and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and establishes a defensible record of treatment.

• Document glycemic targets and rationale for individualization

Record the patient's HbA1c goal and the clinical reasoning, such as age, comorbidities, hypoglycemia risk, life expectancy and patient preferences. Explain the rationale when targets differ from standard recommendations. Including these details demonstrates thoughtful clinical judgment and shared decision-making. 

Sample documentation language: "HbA1c target set at <8% given patient's age (78 years), history of level 2 hypoglycemia and limited life expectancy due to advanced heart failure. Patient agrees with this approach."

• Document the rationale for medication selection, especially for cardiorenal-protective agents

Document clinical reasoning for prescribing or not prescribing SGLT2 inhibitors or GLP-1 receptor agonists for patients with cardiovascular disease, heart failure or chronic kidney disease. If cost, access or contraindications prevent patients from using guideline-recommended agents, document the barriers and alternative plans. 

Sample documentation language: "Patient has established ASCVD and type 2 diabetes. Initiated empagliflozin 10 mg daily for cardiovascular risk reduction per ADA guidelines. Discussed benefits and potential side effects, including genital infections and volume depletion."

• Document screenings

Record completion of annual complication screening, including dilated eye exam (date and findings or referral), foot exam (monofilament testing, pedal pulses and visual inspection), urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio and eGFR. Include abnormal findings and follow-up plans. If screening is deferred, document the reason. 

• Document hypoglycemia history and risk assessment

Record hypoglycemia frequency, severity, precipitants and symptoms during all visits with patients at risk for the condition. Include hypoglycemia awareness assessment and medication adjustments in response to hypoglycemic events. 

Sample documentation language: "Patient reports two episodes of symptomatic hypoglycemia (glucose 55–60 mg/dL) in past month, both occurring before lunch. Reduced glipizide from 10 mg BID to 5 mg BID. Reviewed hypoglycemia treatment with patient. Will reassess at next visit."
• Document follow-ups and clinical reasoning 
At each diabetes encounter, record the next interval based on glycemic control; schedule 3-month follow-ups for patients not meeting individualized glycemic targets or with recent medication changes and 6 months for patients with stable glycemic control at target. Include the rationale for chosen intervals. Consistently capturing follow-up timing demonstrates adherence to ADA and AACE monitoring recommendations and creates a defensible record of ongoing surveillance.
ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS
Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care
Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify and address barriers to medication adherence, self-monitoring and follow-up increases the likelihood of uncontrolled diabetes and complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and address barriers to care.

• Assess medication-taking behavior and barriers at each visit

Using nonjudgmental language, ask patients about medication adherence and hurdles, including costs, side effects, regimen complexity, health literacy and competing priorities. Address issues like medication rationing, which is common, and document all assessments and interventions. 

• Address cost and access barriers to medications

When cost hinders patient adherence to medication routines, explore alternatives such as generic formulations, manufacturer patient assistance programs, co-payment cards and Medicare low-income subsidies. Consider lower-cost therapeutic alternatives when clinically suitable. Document identified barriers and attempted interventions. 

• Simplify medication regimens when possible

Address complex regimens or polypharmacy by using once-daily dosing, combination products or by deprioritizing medications with high hypoglycemia risk (sulfonylureas, insulin) when safer alternatives are available. Simplified regimens improve adherence and reduce medication errors. 

• Document non-adherence and outreach efforts
Record missed appointments, declined testing or failure to fill prescriptions, along with outreach attempts and patient responses. Document refusals of recommended care, including conversation details about recommendations, risks and the patient's stated reasons for refusal. Continue outreach by phone, patient portal or mail. 
Sample documentation language: "Patient has not filled metformin prescription for 3 months per pharmacy records. Called patient today; reports stopping due to GI side effects. Discussed extended-release formulation as alternative. Patient agrees to trial. Prescription sent to pharmacy."
