TITLE: Pneumonia
RISK_BRIEF:
Pneumonia

SPECIALTY: Family Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Respiratory Infection, Cough, Fever

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Sepsis, Respiratory Failure, Death
Pneumonia is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in family medicine. 
Mitigating Your Risk

With accurate severity assessment, guideline-concordant treatment and vigilant post-treatment monitoring, family medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with pneumonia and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for pneumonia and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving pneumonia typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 76% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 24%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.   

Actions and Omissions Driving Pneumonia Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing
Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain appropriate diagnostic testing, such as chest imaging and viral testing, delays pneumonia diagnosis and can lead to inappropriate treatment or missed early intervention.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely and accurate diagnosis of pneumonia and identification of its cause.

• Obtain chest radiography when clinical findings suggest pneumonia
During the initial evaluation, order chest radiography when patients have abnormal vital signs (fever ≥38°C, tachypnea, tachycardia or hypoxemia) and clinical signs of lower respiratory tract infection, including cough, dyspnea, pleuritic pain, crackles or diminished breath sounds. Chest radiography confirms the diagnosis and shows complications like pleural effusion or multilobar involvement. Radiographic confirmation supports accurate treatment decisions and demonstrates adherence to diagnostic standards. 

• Test for COVID-19 and influenza when respiratory viruses are circulating

Check patients during periods of community viral transmission, regardless of disease severity. Viral identification affects treatment decisions (antiviral therapy) and infection prevention strategies and may reduce unnecessary antibiotic use. Do not delay empirical antibiotic initiation due to testing if bacterial infection is suspected. 
• Use validated severity scores to guide diagnostic intensity

Apply the CRB-65 (confusion, respiratory rate ≥30, blood pressure <90/60, age ≥65) or CURB-65 score at initial presentation to classify patients by mortality risk. CRB-65 is practical in outpatient settings as it does not require laboratory testing. Patients scoring 0 can usually be managed as outpatients; scores of 1–2 may require hospitalization or close observation; and scores of 3–4 indicate high risk, requiring hospitalization. Severity scoring supports site-of-care decisions and identifies patients needing more intensive diagnostics.
• Reserve blood and sputum cultures for severe CAP or resistant pathogen risk factors 

Take blood and respiratory cultures before starting antibiotics in patients with severe community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) who need ICU care, meet ATS/IDSA severity criteria or have risk factors for MRSA or Pseudomonas (such as prior infections or recent hospitalization with IV antibiotics). In non-severe CAP, routine cultures rarely affect treatment decisions and can lead to false positives. Obtaining cultures selectively improves diagnosis and helps reduce unnecessary antibiotic use.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care
Contributing action or omission: Failing to recognize indications for hospitalization, ICU admission or specialist consultation delays necessary care and increases the risk of preventable deterioration.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely escalation of care for patients with severe or complicated pneumonia.

• Apply ATS/IDSA severity criteria to identify patients requiring ICU-level care

At initial presentation, assess patients for major criteria, such as septic shock requiring vasopressors or respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation. Additionally evaluate for minor indicators, including a respiratory rate ≥30, PaO2/FiO2 ≤250, multilobar infiltrates, confusion, uremia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, hypothermia or hypotension requiring aggressive fluid resuscitation. The presence of one major criterion or three or more minor criteria indicates CAP, which should trigger ICU admission. Systematic assessment prevents underestimating disease severity. 
• Hospitalize patients with moderate-to-high severity scores or concerning clinical features

Admit patients with CURB-65 scores of 3–5 or CRB-65 scores of 3–4. Consider short-stay observation or hospitalization for patients with scores of 2. Use clinical judgment for patients with hypoxemia (SpO2 <90%), hemodynamic instability, inability to maintain oral intake, severe comorbidities or inadequate home support. Document the rationale for site-of-care decisions. 

• Consult infectious disease or pulmonology for treatment failure or unusual pathogens

Engage a specialist for patients who don’t respond to appropriate empirical therapy, develop complications (empyema, lung abscess or necrotizing pneumonia) or have a confirmed infection with resistant organisms (MRSA or Pseudomonas). Early consultation ensures effective antibiotic selection and procedural intervention when indicated. 

• Transfer to higher-level care when resources are insufficient

Move patients in outpatient settings with severe CAP features requiring ICU-level monitoring, vasopressor support or mechanical ventilation to a suitable facility. If clinical deterioration is evident, do not delay transfer for additional diagnostic workup. Document the clinical rationale for transfer and communicate directly with receiving providers. 
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention
Contributing action or omission: Insufficient post-treatment monitoring and failure to reassess clinical response can lead to unrecognized treatment failure, disease progression and preventable complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support early identification of treatment failure and ensure proper escalation of care.

• Establish clinical stability criteria and reassessment intervals

Communicate clinical stability standards to patients at the start of treatment and document expected response timelines. Clinical stability includes resolution of vital sign abnormalities (heart rate, respiratory rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation and temperature), ability to eat and normal mentation. Patients should show improvement within 48-72 hours; failure to achieve stability within 5 days is associated with higher mortality and should prompt reassessment. 

• Schedule follow-up for outpatients with moderate-risk pneumonia

Arrange an in-person or telehealth clinical reassessment within 48–72 hours of initiating treatment in patients with CRB-65 scores of 1–2 or significant comorbidities. This follow-up should include evaluation of vital signs, symptom progression and ability to maintain oral intake. Early reassessment identifies patients whose outpatient therapy is failing and requires hospitalization. 
• Provide return precautions and escalation instructions

Before discharging patients from the initial encounter, give written and verbal guidance on symptoms warranting immediate reevaluation, such as worsening dyspnea, persistent high fever despite 48 hours of antibiotics, inability to tolerate oral medications or fluids, confusion, altered mental status and chest pain. Document education and the patient’s stated understanding of the instructions.

• Reassess patients with a slow clinical response for complications 
Evaluate patients who fail to achieve clinical stability within 5 days for resistant pathogens and pneumonia complications (empyema or lung abscess) or alternative diagnoses. Consider repeat imaging, additional microbiologic testing and specialist consultation. A patient’s failure to improve should prompt reassessment rather than extending antibiotics. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Communication failures—including inadequate handoffs, unclear follow-up arrangements and insufficient coordination with specialists or receiving facilities—can delay recognition and treatment of clinical deterioration.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support coordinated care across settings.

• Use handoff protocols when transferring care

Apply handoff frameworks such as SBAR when transferring patients between outpatient and inpatient settings, between shifts or to specialists. Communicate the patient’s current clinical status, severity score, antibiotic regimen/timing of first dose, pending test results and concerns requiring monitoring. Document handoff details in the medical record. 

• Communicate directly with receiving providers for urgent transfers

Don’t rely solely on written referrals; engage with the accepting physician when patients require hospitalization or transfer to higher-level care. Share severity indicators, interventions and concerns. Document the provider’s name and time of contact. 

• Coordinate follow-up after hospitalization

Assess patients within 7–10 days of discharge from the hospital or emergency department. Review the discharge diagnosis, antibiotic regimen and duration, pending results and monitoring parameters. Patients with severe CAP or significant comorbidities may benefit from earlier follow-up. 

• Establish escalation pathways within the practice

Develop protocols for escalating care to supervising physicians, arranging same-day specialist consultations and sending patients to the emergency department. Ensure that all clinical staff understand escalation triggers and documentation requirements. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of clinical findings, severity assessment, treatment rationale and patient education compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a record that can aid in a liability defense.

• Document clinical findings supporting the diagnosis

Record symptoms (cough characteristics, dyspnea, fever duration and pleuritic pain), vital signs (temperature, respiratory rate, heart rate, blood pressure and oxygen saturation) and physical exam findings (lung auscultation findings and signs of respiratory distress). Include radiographic findings with interpretation. Documentation supports the clinical basis for the diagnosis. 

Sample documentation language: "68-year-old male with 3 days of productive cough, fever to 38.9°C, dyspnea on exertion. Vital signs: T 38.6°C, RR 24, HR 102, BP 128/78, SpO2 94% on room air. Lung exam: crackles right lower lobe. CXR: right lower lobe consolidation. CRB-65 score 1 (age ≥65). Diagnosis: community-acquired pneumonia."

• Document severity assessment and site-of-care rationale

Record the severity score (CRB-65, CURB-65 or PSI), the calculated score and the clinical reasoning supporting the site-of-care decision. When clinical judgment overrides the score, such as hospitalizing a low-score patient due to comorbidities or social factors, document the reasoning. Capturing these details demonstrates thoughtful clinical decision-making. 

Sample documentation language: "CRB-65 score 1 (age ≥65). However, patient has COPD with baseline dyspnea, lives alone and reports difficulty maintaining oral intake. Given these factors, recommending short-stay observation rather than outpatient management."

• Provide rationale for antibiotic choice and treatment plan 
Record the antibiotic regimen, reason for its use (severity, comorbidities, allergy status and local resistance patterns) and planned duration. Note any changes based on culture results or clinical response. This level of documentation demonstrates proper antibiotic stewardship and guideline adherence. 

• Document patient education and return precautions

Include details about the clinical course, warning signs requiring immediate reevaluation (worsening dyspnea, persistent fever, inability to eat or drink and confusion) and follow-up plans. Record the patient's stated understanding and ability to follow the treatment plan. 

Sample documentation language: "Discussed expected improvement within 48-72 hours. Instructed patient to return immediately or call 911 if experiencing worsening shortness of breath, fever >48 hours despite antibiotics, inability to keep down medications or confusion. Patient verbalized understanding. Follow-up scheduled in 3 days."

ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS
Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care
Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify and address barriers to medication adherence, follow-up attendance and recognition of warning signs increases the likelihood of treatment failure and delayed presentation with complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and address barriers to successful outpatient treatment.

• Assess ability to comply with outpatient treatment before discharge

Evaluate the patient's capacity to maintain oral intake, obtain and take prescribed medications, monitor symptoms and return for follow-up before starting outpatient therapy. Identify barriers, such as cognitive impairment, substance use, homelessness, lack of transportation and inadequate home support. Significant challenges may require hospitalization or observation despite low severity scores. 

• Simplify antibiotic regimens

When clinically appropriate, select once- or twice-daily dosing to improve adherence. Ensure that the patient can afford the prescribed antibiotic; consider generic alternatives or patient assistance programs when cost is a barrier. Confirm that the patient understands the dosing schedule and importance of completing the full course. 

• Provide written instructions in the patient's primary language

Tailor discharge materials to the patient’s language and literacy level. Avoid generic templates that do not address pneumonia-specific risks. Ensure that instructions name the symptoms requiring emergency evaluation. Use teach-back methods to confirm understanding. 

• Document non-adherence and continued outreach

Record missed follow-ups, medication non-adherence, outreach attempts and patient responses. Note when patients decline recommended hospitalization or evaluation despite concerning symptoms. Include conversation details, such as recommendations, risks and the patient's stated reasons for declining. Continue outreach by phone, patient portal or certified letter.

TITLE: Bowel perforation
RISK_BRIEF:
Bowel Perforation from Colonoscopy

SPECIALTY: Gastroenterology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Colonoscopy (Diagnostic, Screening, Therapeutic)

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Bowel Perforation

Bowel perforation from colonoscopy is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in gastroenterology.   
   
Mitigating Your Risk   
   
With rigorous procedural technique, systematic post-procedure surveillance, and coordinated multidisciplinary response, gastroenterologists can mitigate the risks associated with bowel perforation from colonoscopy and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for bowel perforation from colonoscopy and provides strategies to help prevent them.       
  
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns   
Malpractice claims involving bowel perforation from colonoscopy stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 81% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 19%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.      
Actions and Omissions Driving Bowel Perforation from Colonoscopy Risk [insert chart]   

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failure to promptly recognize perforation, delayed initiation of appropriate management or inadequate post-perforation monitoring increase the risk of peritonitis, sepsis and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure rapid recognition of perforation, appropriate initial management and timely escalation when needed.

• Be alert for signs of perforation during the procedure

During colonoscopy, monitor for sudden loss of luminal distension, visualization of peritoneal fat or mesentery, inability to maintain insufflation and unexplained bleeding. Immediate recognition enables endoscopic closure attempts and improves outcomes. Document findings in real time, including perforation size, location and any closure attempted.

• Initiate immediate management for perforations recognized during the procedure
Alert the endoscopy team immediately. Confirm that carbon dioxide insufflation is in use (as recommended for routine colonoscopy) and adjust to a low flow setting to minimize pneumoperitoneum. Suction luminal contents to reduce peritoneal contamination. Administer intravenous antibiotics with gram-negative and anaerobic coverage. These steps should occur concurrently with assessment for endoscopic closure.
• Consider attempting endoscopic closure for intraprocedural perforations
When perforations are recognized during the procedure in stable patients with clean sites, consider endoscopic closure using through-the-scope or over-the-scope clips (OTSC). This decision should take perforation type, size and endoscopist expertise into account. Document the technique, number of clips and the adequacy of closure. 

• Obtain CT imaging promptly when perforation is suspected
When perforation is suspected, whether during or after colonoscopy, obtain a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast as the primary diagnostic study to guide further treatment. CT is the most accurate imaging modality and is approximately 86% accurate in predicting the perforation site. Plain radiographs are less reliable, particularly after therapeutic procedures, and should not be relied upon to exclude perforation. For patients presenting with post-procedure abdominal pain, fever or distension, obtain CT urgently to guide management decisions, including the need for surgical intervention.
• Institute conservative supportive care when managing perforations non-operatively
For patients managed conservatively, whether after successful endoscopic closure or for delayed perforations not requiring immediate surgery, initiate bowel rest, intravenous fluid resuscitation and broad-spectrum antibiotics targeting gram-negative and anaerobic organisms. Fasting, antibiotic therapy and IV hydration form the basis of non-operative management and should continue until pain resolves, inflammatory markers normalize and bowel function returns. 
• Monitor patients closely after perforation

Conduct frequent clinical and imaging assessments for conservatively managed patients or those with endoscopic closure. Watch for worsening symptoms, such as increasing abdominal pain, fever, tachycardia, hypotension or peritoneal signs. Repeat CT imaging should be performed if clinical deterioration occurs or if initial conservative management does not result in expected improvement within 24–48 hours. Even if early treatment succeeds, surgery may still be required if the patient deteriorates.

CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care

Contributing action or omission: Failure to obtain timely access to surgical care, failure to transfer from non-surgical settings or delayed surgical consultation after perforation increase morbidity and mortality from colonoscopy perforation.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely multidisciplinary involvement and  escalation of care.

• Schedule high-risk therapeutic procedures at facilities with multidisciplinary support

Perform high-risk endoscopic procedures—including ESD, complex EMR, stricture dilation and stent placement—at facilities with the capacity to admit patients and with timely access to surgical consultation and intervention. For procedures with the highest perforation risk (ESD, colonic stenting), a hospital-based setting with on-site surgical, anesthesiology and radiology support is strongly preferred. When clinical circumstances require that these procedures be performed at ambulatory surgery centers, transfer agreements, an accepting surgeon and transport logistics must be established in advance.

• Involve surgery for confirmed perforations
 
Engage a surgeon for all perforation cases regardless of the initial management strategy. Early surgical involvement allows for rapid escalation of care if conservative or endoscopic methods fail. Document the consult, recommendations and monitoring plan.
• Identify patients requiring immediate surgical intervention

Act promptly for patients with signs of peritonitis, hemodynamic instability, large perforations, failed endoscopic closure, immunosuppression, transplant status or concomitant colonic pathology requiring surgery. Patients undergoing surgery within 24 hours of perforation have better outcomes; primary closure is feasible in 55% of patients operated on within 24 hours, versus 0% after 24 hours. 
• Develop transfer protocols for non-surgical facilities

Establish guidelines for rapid transfer to surgical facilities when perforation is suspected or confirmed. Ambulatory endoscopy centers must document the transfer process, receiving facility, accepting surgeon and clinical status at transfer. Perforations at outside institutions are strongly associated with requiring surgery (OR 11.35).
CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Technical errors during colonoscopy—including excessive force, inadequate loop reduction, over-insufflation and inappropriate polypectomy technique—are primary contributors to iatrogenic perforation.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to minimize perforation risk through proper technique during all phases of colonoscopy.

• Avoid pushing through fixed resistance and reduce loops

During difficult scope advancement, avoid exerting force with the endoscope against fixed resistance. Instead, use alternative maneuvers, such as abdominal compression, position changes and loop reduction before proceeding. For patients with narrowed or angulated sigmoid colons, consider using a pediatric or slim colonoscope or switching to water-only filling without gas insufflation. Stop the procedure if safe advancement is not possible. 

• Use carbon dioxide insufflation and avoid over-distension

Reduce bowel distension, abdominal discomfort and perforation risk by using carbon dioxide insufflation instead of air. Insufflate cautiously, particularly when bowel obstruction is suspected, to avoid barotrauma. Barotrauma-related perforations are most common in the cecum, where the thinner muscular layer and larger lumen diameter increase susceptibility to pressure-related injury. 

• Apply appropriate polypectomy technique based on polyp characteristics

Use cold snare polypectomy for polyps <10 mm to reduce perforation and bleeding risk compared to electrocautery. For non-pedunculated polyps 10–19 mm, the optimal resection technique remains an area of evolving evidence; cold or hot snare polypectomy, with or without submucosal injection, is acceptable depending on polyp morphology, bulkiness, histology and location. Consider submucosal injection or underwater EMR for sessile polyps in this size range. For polyps ≥20 mm, submucosal injection is standard practice when performing EMR to reduce electrocautery damage to the muscularis propria. En bloc resection by conventional EMR is generally limited to lesions <20 mm; consider ESD for larger lesions requiring en bloc resection. Carefully inspect the resection base for a target sign indicating muscularis propria injury.
• Recognize high-risk clinical scenarios and adjust technique accordingly

Exercise additional caution in patients with risk factors, such as advanced age, diverticular disease, inflammatory bowel disease, previous abdominal surgery, low BMI and colonic obstruction. Consider stopping or limiting the procedure when multiple risk factors are present. Since low-volume endoscopists and non-gastroenterologists have higher perforation rates, ensure appropriate supervision during training and consider referring high-risk patients to experienced endoscopists. 

• Maintain competency through adequate procedure volume and quality monitoring

Track individual and facility perforation rates to continuously improve quality, as higher rates are linked to low-volume endoscopists and endoscopy centers. Review perforations through a quality improvement committee to identify contributing factors and implement corrective actions.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Failure to communicate effectively among endoscopists, surgeons and other care team members regarding perforation details, patient status and management plans increases the risk of delayed or inappropriate treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication and ensure a coordinated team response.

• Provide perforation documentation to surgical consultants

Include a description of the perforation, detailing size, location, mechanism (mechanical vs. thermal), bowel preparation quality, endoscopic closure attempts and the patient's status. The endoscopist should communicate this clearly to help guide surgical decision-making and document it in the medical record.

• Implement handoff protocols for post-perforation care

Use frameworks such as SBAR to transfer patients between endoscopy, emergency department, surgical and intensive care teams. Communicate clinical status, imaging findings, interventions, antibiotic regimen and monitoring parameters. Document the handoff in the medical record.

• Establish escalation triggers and team roles

Define protocols for when to escalate care and assign responsibility for initiating surgical intervention. Examples of triggers include clinical deterioration, failure of conservative management, and development of peritoneal signs. Endoscopy units should have 24/7 availability of surgical consultation for procedures with perforation risk.

• Communicate with referring physicians and primary care providers

Notify referring physicians in a timely manner when perforation occurs. Share documentation of the complication, management and follow-up plan. This helps maintain continuity of care and supports effective outpatient monitoring after discharge.




ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION 
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Failure to obtain informed consent regarding perforation risk and provide clear post-procedure instructions increases both patient harm and malpractice exposure.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients understand procedural risks and know when to seek care.

• Conduct a thorough informed consent discussion that includes perforation risk

Inform patients that perforation is a potentially serious adverse event. Explain its estimated frequency (approximately 1 in 1,000–2,000 for diagnostic procedures, higher for therapeutic procedures) and possible consequences like surgery, hospitalization or, in rare cases, death. Clinically appropriate alternatives to colonoscopy, such as stool-based testing (fecal immunochemical test, multitarget stool DNA) for colon cancer screening, should be reviewed along with their respective risks and benefits. Documentation should reflect that perforation risk was addressed, as quality of informed consent is a key medicolegal issue in endoscopy litigation. 

• Tailor risk discussion to patient- and procedure-specific factors

Individualize the consent discussion based on patient risk factors (advanced age, comorbidities, diverticular disease) and planned interventions (polypectomy, endoscopic mucosal resection, stricture dilation). Patients with multiple risk factors or those undergoing high-risk therapeutic procedures should understand their elevated risk. The individualized risk discussion, including any alternatives considered, should be clearly documented.

• Provide written post-procedure instructions

Give patients discharge instructions outlining symptoms requiring urgent evaluation, including severe or worsening abdominal pain, abdominal distension, fever, chills, nausea, vomiting and rectal bleeding. Provide instructions in the patient's primary language and at a suitable literacy level. Include endoscopy unit contact details and after-hours guidance. Patients should be advised to call 911 or seek immediate emergency care if they experience signs of a serious emergency, such as severe unrelenting abdominal pain, lightheadedness, fainting or heavy rectal bleeding, particularly if they are not able to reach the endoscopy team. 

• Ensure patient understanding before discharge

Verify that patients and accompanying responsible adults can verbalize warning signs and when to seek care. Document the patient's stated understanding in the medical record. Do not discharge patients until sedation effects have resolved and they can understand instructions.

ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUE

Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure

Contributing action or omission: Failure to implement protocols for perforation prevention, recognition and management undermines consistent care delivery and delays appropriate treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve readiness, standardize responses and support coordinated escalation.

• Develop and maintain perforation management policies

Create a written policy for managing iatrogenic perforations at each endoscopy center. It should outline high-risk procedures, necessary equipment, escalation pathways and documentation requirements. Share this policy with radiologists and surgeons at the facility and ensure that it is reviewed and updated annually.

• Ensure availability of perforation closure equipment

Maintain readily accessible supplies for endoscopic perforation closure, including through-the-scope and over-the-scope clips (OTSC) in all endoscopy procedure rooms. Regularly inventory and verify equipment functionality. Ensure that endoscopists are trained in clip deployment techniques.

• Establish post-procedure surveillance systems

Set up systems, including post-procedure phone calls, to identify delayed adverse events. The ACG/AGA recommends that endoscopy units aim to contact 98% of patients within 72 hours of colonoscopy regardless of sedation type. In addition, continue to contact patients up to 14 days post-procedure, as 15%–45% of adverse events may go unrecognized or unreported without systematic follow-up. Ambulatory surgery centers should maintain policies for post-procedure documentation and follow-up consistent with applicable accreditation and regulatory requirements.
• Conduct quality improvement review of all perforations

Monitor all perforations under the oversight of the endoscopy unit medical director and review them through a quality improvement committee. Perform root cause analyses to identify contributing factors, develop corrective actions and implement system improvements or education. Track perforation rates by individual endoscopist and facility to identify performance gaps.


• Consider running simulation training for perforation management

Consider conducting periodic multidisciplinary simulations involving endoscopists, nurses, surgeons and anesthesiologists to practice perforation recognition and response. Debrief after simulations to identify improvement opportunities and ensure that all team members understand their roles during a perforation emergency.





PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify and address barriers to post-procedure monitoring and timely return for evaluation of concerning symptoms increases the likelihood of delayed perforation diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and ensure timely evaluation of post-procedure complications.

• Identify and address barriers before discharge

Assess whether patients have the resources to recognize warning symptoms and access emergency care. Identify barriers, including transportation, language, health literacy and social support. Patients without a responsible adult to monitor them post-procedure may require extended observation or alternative discharge planning.

• Emphasize the importance of timely evaluation for delayed symptoms

Educate patients about the fact that perforation symptoms may present in a delayed fashion. Delayed perforation from thermal injuries typically manifests 24–72 hours post-procedure, though rare later cases have been reported. Post-polypectomy electrocoagulation syndrome (transmural burns causing localized peritonitis without perforation) may present within 1 to 5 days with fever, localized pain and leukocytosis. Delayed post-polypectomy bleeding can occur up to several weeks post-procedure. Explain that any new or worsening abdominal symptoms in the days following a colonoscopy requires prompt evaluation.

• Offer accessible channels for reporting post-procedure symptoms
Provide written discharge materials to patients that include contact information for the endoscopy unit and after-hours answering service and instructions for when to go directly to the emergency department. Consider providing written materials in multiple languages based on the patient population served. 
• Record patient non-adherence and continued outreach

Log missed follow-ups or refusals of recommended evaluation despite concerning symptoms. Note discussions, advice given, risks explained and patient responses. Persist in outreach via phone, patient portal and mail, documenting each attempt.

TITLE: Colon cancer
RISK_BRIEF:
Colon Cancer


SPECIALTY: Gastroenterology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Colorectal Cancer Screening, Surveillance Colonoscopy, Symptomatic Evaluation

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Delayed Diagnosis, Advanced-Stage Cancer, Preventable Cancer Death, Procedural Complications
Colon cancer is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in gastroenterology. 
 
Mitigating Your Risk 
 
With timely screening and follow-up, effective communication with patients and referring providers and rigorous documentation, gastroenterologists can mitigate the risks associated with the diagnosis and management of colon cancer and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for colon cancer and provides strategies to help prevent them.     

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving colon cancer stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 79% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 21%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.    
Actions and Omissions Driving Colon Cancer Risk [insert chart]  

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to initiate colorectal cancer screening at the recommended age, order a diagnostic colonoscopy for symptomatic patients or follow up on positive stool-based screening tests increases the risk of delayed diagnosis and advanced-stage cancer.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely initiation of screening and appropriate diagnostic evaluation for symptomatic patients.

• Initiate colorectal cancer screening at age 45 for average-risk individuals

Discuss screening options and document shared decision-making at the first visit for patients reaching screening age. The NCCN, ACG and AGA recommend starting at 45 for average-risk adults with a 10+ year life expectancy. Offering varied screening options (colonoscopy, FIT, mt-sDNA) improves uptake and aligns with guidelines. Document this discussion to meet the standard of care.

• Apply appropriate risk stratification for family history

Apply appropriate risk stratification based on family history aligned with the latest NCCN guidelines. Screening initiation age, interval and modality differ based on the relative's diagnosis (CRC vs. advanced adenoma), degree of relation and age at diagnosis. Document family history assessment, including age of diagnosis and relationship to the patient, and the rationale for screening intervals.
• Perform colonoscopy within 9 months for all positive stool-based screening tests

Schedule colonoscopies as soon as possible and no later than 9 months after a positive result for patients with positive FIT, mt-sDNA, mt-sRNA or other stool-based tests. Establish tracking systems to ensure that all patients with positive results receive outreach within 2 weeks and at least 80% are offered a colonoscopy within 3 months.

• Evaluate patients with alarm symptoms using a timely, evidence-based approach

Promptly assess patients presenting with hematochezia, iron deficiency anemia, unexplained weight loss, changes in bowel habits or abdominal pain, including performing a colonoscopy, regardless of age or recent negative screening. The most common early-onset CRC symptoms are hematochezia (45%), abdominal pain (40%) and altered bowel habits (27%). Failure to evaluate these symptoms contributes to diagnostic delays averaging 6.2 months in younger patients. 

CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failure to ensure complete polypectomy, manage polyp findings appropriately or adhere to guideline-recommended surveillance intervals increase the risk of interval cancers and missed diagnoses.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate surveillance intervals and complete lesion management.

• Ensure complete polypectomy and refer when endoscopic resection is not achievable 

Record whether each polypectomy was complete (en bloc vs. piecemeal), including the resection technique. Include margin status and whether the specimen was fragmented. If a polypectomy cannot be performed, refer the patient to a provider capable of performing an endoscopy with polypectomy. For large colorectal polyps that cannot be completely resected endoscopically, or for lesions with high-risk endoscopic features for invasive cancer, refer to centers with appropriate expertise for advanced endoscopic management or surgical evaluation. Document the referral and ensure follow-up. Schedule repeat colonoscopies in accordance with NCCN guidelines. 
• Follow evidence-based surveillance intervals after polypectomy

Determine a patient's surveillance interval after a colonoscopy with polypectomy based on polyp histology, size, number and resection technique. Surveillance intervals range from 3 to 10 years for polyps <1 cm and from 6 months to 3 years for polyps ≥1 cm, depending on risk category and resection method. Follow the NCCN post-polypectomy surveillance and large polyp management pathways (CSCR-8 through CSCR-10) to determine the appropriate interval for each patient. Surveillance intervals assume complete resection, adequate bowel preparation and complete examination. When findings do not fit neatly into a single risk category, document the clinical reasoning for the chosen interval. 

• Repeat colonoscopy within 1 year for inadequate bowel preparation

If a colonoscopy is incomplete, or prep is suboptimal (Boston Bowel Preparation Scale score <2 in any segment), repeat promptly, no later than 1 year. Perform colonoscopies in shorter intervals if advanced neoplasia is present with inadequate bowel preparation. Document preparation quality using standardized scales.


CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Failing to meet colonoscopy quality indicators—including adenoma detection rate, cecal intubation rate and adequate bowel preparation—increases the risk of missed lesions and interval cancers.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure a high-quality colonoscopy that maximizes lesion detection and minimizes procedural complications.

• Use split-dose bowel preparation as the standard strategy

Recommend split-dose preparation, the superior choice, for all patients. Start the second dose 4 to 6 hours before the colonoscopy and finish at least 2 hours before the procedure. Bowel preparation adequacy rate should be 90% or more, defined as Boston Bowel Preparation Scale score ≥2 in each of the 3 colon segments. 
• Maintain cecal intubation rate ≥ 95% with photo documentation

Aim for cecal intubation rates of at least 95% in patients with intact colons. Include photos of cecal landmarks (appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve) in all colonoscopy reports. When colonoscopies are incomplete, consider CT colonography, balloon-assisted colonoscopy or capsule colonoscopy. 

• Maintain mean withdrawal time ≥6 minutes

Aim for a mean withdrawal time of 6 or more minutes in normal colonoscopies (aspirational target 9 minutes or more). Perform a second look at the right colon in retroflexed or forward view to improve polyp detection. 
• Achieve and monitor adenoma detection rates ≥35%

Ensure that adenoma detection rates (ADR), the key quality indicator for colonoscopy effectiveness, meet performance targets. The NCCN and ACG/AGA Quality Indicators set the performance target at ≥35% overall (40% in men, 30% in women). The AGA Clinical Practice Update sets the goal ADR at ≥30% (aspirational target ≥35%). Endoscopy units should measure and provide feedback on ADR at both the endoscopist and unit level at least annually or after 250 screening colonoscopies. Endoscopists below thresholds should consider extending withdrawal times, doing self-learning on mucosal inspection and seeking peer feedback. 

• Improve detection of sessile serrated lesions

Target a sessile serrated lesion detection rate of ≥ 6%. Note that the AGA Clinical Practice Update (2021) recommends a serrated lesion detection rate (SLDR) of ≥7% (aspirational target ≥10%) — a related but distinct metric that includes all serrated lesions, not only SSLs. SSLs are precursors to 10–30% of CRC and are often missed due to their flat shape and indistinct borders. When rates are low, focus improvement on colonoscopists and pathologists. 


ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate communication between gastroenterologists, primary care providers and specialists about screening results, surveillance recommendations and follow-up responsibilities increases the risk of missed or delayed diagnoses.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and ensure coordinated care across the screening and surveillance continuum.

• Ensure follow-up and measure completion of positive screening tests 
Monitor all positive stool-based screening tests to confirm colonoscopy completion. Measure the percentage of colonoscopies performed and the number recommended, with a programmatic quality target of achieving ≥ 95% completion within 6 months of the positive noninvasive test. Use strategies such as patient navigation, reminder systems and practice champions to increase follow-up.
• Include specific surveillance recommendations in colonoscopy reports

Document a patient’s next surveillance recommendation interval in each report. If findings  affect first-degree relatives' screening recommendations, such as advanced adenomas, add those recommendations or provide a letter for relatives. This keeps patients and referring providers informed of the follow-up plan.

• Communicate clearly with primary care regarding surveillance responsibilities

Define roles between gastroenterology and primary care for ongoing surveillance. Document in the medical record and inform the patient which provider is responsible for tracking surveillance intervals and scheduling future procedures. Communicate the timing and rationale for patients transitioning from active surveillance to routine screening.

• Coordinate care for patients with hereditary cancer syndromes

Refer patients with suspected or confirmed hereditary colorectal cancer syndromes (Lynch syndrome, FAP) to genetic counseling and share surveillance recommendations with all involved providers. Document genetic testing results and their implications for screening intervals in the medical record.

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Failure to adequately communicate screening recommendations, test results, surveillance intervals and warning signs to patients increases the risk of non-adherence and delayed presentation.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients understand their screening and surveillance responsibilities and recognize symptoms requiring evaluation.

• Conduct shared decision-making conversations about screening options

Discuss the benefits, harms, costs, availability and frequency of different screening modalities with patients. Document the discussion and the patient's stated preference. Offering multiple screening options improves uptake and aligns with guideline recommendations.
• Communicate positive screening test results promptly

Attempt to reach all patients within 2 weeks of a positive stool-based test result. Explain that a positive result requires a colonoscopy for further evaluation. Document the conversation and the patient's understanding.

• Provide clear written instructions for surveillance intervals

After each colonoscopy, provide patients with written documentation of the procedure, recommended surveillance interval and next procedure date. Explain the rationale in terms the patient can understand. Document that this information was given.

• Educate patients about symptoms requiring evaluation

Inform patients about symptoms requiring immediate evaluation, regardless of recent screening, including rectal bleeding, blood mixed with stool, unexplained weight loss, persistent change in bowel habits, abdominal pain or unexplained iron deficiency anemia. Emphasize that hemorrhoids do not exclude additional pathology, and unresolved bleeding with treatment requires a colonoscopy.
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of colonoscopy quality indicators, clinical decision-making, patient education and surveillance recommendations compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document family history and its impact on recommendations

Record the patient’s family history of colorectal cancer and advanced adenomas, including age of diagnosis and their relationship to the patient. Document how this influenced the screening or surveillance recommendation. 
• Record all colonoscopy quality indicators

Include the following in colonoscopy reports:
Indications for procedure 
Bowel preparation quality: using Boston Bowel Preparation Scale or equivalent
Cecal intubation with photo documentation of landmarks
Withdrawal time
Polyps identified: location, size and morphology
Resection technique and completeness
Complications 
Documentation shows adherence to quality standards and supports surveillance interval decisions.

Sample documentation language: "Colonoscopy performed for average-risk screening. Bowel preparation excellent (BBPS 9). Cecum intubated with photo documentation of appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve. Withdrawal time 8 minutes. 6mm sessile polyp in ascending colon removed en bloc with cold snare; specimen submitted for pathology. No complications. Recommend surveillance colonoscopy pending pathology; will finalize interval based on histology per NCCN guidelines."

• Record clinical reasoning for surveillance interval recommendations

After pathology results are available, note the findings (polyp histology, size, number, presence of dysplasia) and the rationale for the recommended surveillance interval based on guidelines. Document reasoning when deviating from standard intervals due to individual risk factors.

• Document patient education and understanding

Note that the patient received education regarding findings, recommended surveillance interval and symptoms requiring early evaluation. Include the patient's stated understanding and any barriers to follow-up. 


PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify and address barriers to screening completion, colonoscopy follow-up and adherence to surveillance increases the likelihood of delayed diagnosis and advanced-stage cancer.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and address barriers to timely screening and follow-up.

• Reach out to screening-eligible patients

Increase patient screening rates through mailed stool test outreach, patient education and reminders and clinician-directed feedback and alerts. Organized outreach programs increase CRC screening rates, reduce mortality and minimize disparities by race and ethnicity. 

• Address barriers to colonoscopy completion after positive screening tests

Identify challenges to follow-up colonoscopy, including transportation, childcare, work obligations, cost and fear of the procedure. Offer patient navigation assistance and support for social barriers. Establish relationships with colonoscopy facilities to facilitate handoffs and prioritize a follow-up colonoscopy over routine screening. 

• Use tracking systems to identify patients overdue for surveillance

Monitor patient populations to identify those overdue for surveillance colonoscopy based on prior findings. Use reminders and outreach to re-engage these patients. Document outreach attempts and patient responses.

• Document patient non-adherence and continued outreach

When patients miss scheduled colonoscopies or decline recommended follow-up, note the conversation, recommendations, risks explained and the patient's stated reasons for declining. Continue outreach using phone, patient portal or mail. If a patient declines evaluation despite concerning symptoms, document their informed refusal.

Mitigating Your Risk 
 
With timely screening and follow-up, effective communication with patients and referring providers and rigorous documentation, gastroenterologists can mitigate the risks associated with the diagnosis an

TITLE: Sedation airway management
RISK_BRIEF:
Sedation and Airway Management
SPECIALTY: Gastroenterology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Patients Undergoing Sedated Endoscopic Procedures

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Cardiopulmonary Complications, Aspiration, Hypoxia, Respiratory Arrest, Death

Sedation and airway management is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in gastroenterology. 
 
Mitigating Your Risk 
 
With proactive risk assessment, vigilant intra- and post-procedural monitoring and thorough documentation, gastroenterologists can mitigate the risks associated with sedation and airway management and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for sedation and airway management and provides strategies to help prevent them.     

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving sedation and airway management stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 84% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 16%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.    
Actions and Omissions Driving Sedation and Airway Management Risk [insert chart] 
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify patients at elevated risk for sedation-related adverse events—including those with difficult airways, significant cardiopulmonary disease or conditions predisposing to aspiration—increases the likelihood of preventable complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure systematic identification of high-risk patients before sedation is administered.

• Perform a pre-procedure sedation risk assessment 

Before sedated endoscopic procedures, screen for sedation-related risk factors. When possible, obtain this history before the day of the procedure to allow time for sedation planning, anesthesia coordination, specialist consultation or pre-procedure optimization as needed. Risk factors for sedation complications include:
Snoring, stridor or sleep apnea
Previous adverse reactions to sedation or anesthesia
Current medications and potential drug interactions, including GLP-1 receptor agonists
Tobacco, alcohol or substance use
Time and contents of last oral intake (on day of procedure)
• Perform an appropriate physical exam prior to administering sedation 
Before sedation, complete a baseline assessment of vital signs, oxygen saturation, neurologic status, auscultation of the heart and lungs, abdominal exam and airway evaluation. For abnormal baseline vital signs (oxygen saturation <95%), address the abnormality before initiating sedation. A pre-procedure exam provides a comparison point for evaluating for potential complications following the procedure.  

• Assess and document airway anatomy 

Evaluate the patient’s airway anatomy as part of the pre-sedation assessment. The ASGE recommends documenting features associated with difficult airway management, including Mallampati classification, mouth opening, neck mobility, body habitus and cervicofacial obesity.
• Stratify patients using ASA classification

Assign and document ASA physical status classification for all patients. Studies confirm ASA class predicts adverse events during an endoscopy, with odds ratios of 1.54, 3.90 and 12.02 for ASA II, III and IV/V, respectively. 
• Adjust the plan when risk factors for sedation complications are identified 

If concerning findings are identified on history or physical exam, modify the sedation plan. Options include adjusting the target sedation level, selecting alternative agents, arranging for anesthesia provider involvement, scheduling the procedure at a facility with appropriate resources (a hospital-based setting for patients requiring advanced resuscitation capability), or deferring the procedure. The ASA states that if the practitioner is not trained in rescue from the next deeper level of sedation, an anesthesia professional should be consulted. Document the assessment and plan, including the rationale for clinical decision-making.

CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain appropriate pre-procedure testing or adequately monitor during sedation increases the risk of sedation-related adverse events.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate evaluation and monitoring.

• Obtain pregnancy testing in women of childbearing age

Ensure that all women of childbearing age have pregnancy testing before sedation, as some sedatives may be teratogenic. Document the result before proceeding. 

• Consider gastric ultrasound in patients at risk for retained gastric contents

When clinical expertise and equipment are available, consider transabdominal ultrasonography to assess gastric volume in patients on GLP-1 receptor agonists with gastrointestinal symptoms suggesting retained gastric contents. The AGA Rapid Clinical Practice Update (2024) acknowledges point-of-care gastric ultrasound as a potential tool to guide decisions to proceed, delay or modify sedation, though evidence to support this modality in standard endoscopic practice is lacking. 
• Monitor continuously during sedation 

Monitor all sedated patients with continuous pulse oximetry, non-invasive blood pressure and heart rate. At minimum, assess and document level of consciousness and vital signs at key intervals:
Before the procedure begins
After administration of sedative-analgesic agents
At least every 5 minutes during the procedure 
During initial recovery
Before discharge
Use continuous ECG monitoring for patients with significant cardiovascular disease, dysrhythmia, pulmonary disease or during prolonged procedures. Consider supplemental oxygen for all patients undergoing moderate sedation; require it for procedures targeting deep sedation or when hypoxemia is anticipated. Consider capnography for procedures targeting deep sedation, as it can detect respiratory depression before pulse oximetry registers desaturation. Assign dedicated monitoring personnel during sedation; for deep sedation, monitoring staff must focus solely on continuous observation and not perform other functions during the procedure. 
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failure to adequately monitor patients during recovery or ensure standard discharge criteria are met before releasing the patient increases the risk of unrecognized respiratory depression, hypoxemia and delayed cardiopulmonary complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure continuous appropriate monitoring and timely recognition of deterioration.

• Monitor patients continuously during the recovery period
After the procedure, continue pulse oximetry and vital sign monitoring in a staffed recovery area. Maintain monitoring until the patient meets discharge criteria. Patients who received deep sedation, had prolonged procedures or have significant cardiopulmonary comorbidities may require extended observation. Do not leave sedated patients unattended during recovery.
• Apply standardized discharge criteria

Use standardized discharge criteria to evaluate respiration, oxygen saturation, blood pressure, consciousness and activity. Validated tools such as the Aldrete scoring system or the modified post-anesthesia discharge scoring system (PADSS) may be used for this purpose. Extend monitoring up to 2 hours for patients receiving naloxone or flumazenil due to re-sedation risk. Document fulfillment of discharge criteria before releasing the patient.
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of pre-procedure assessment, sedation plan, intra-procedural monitoring and discharge status compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces patient safety and provides a defensible record.

• Document pre-procedure sedation assessment

Establish a sedation plan before endoscopic procedures, including a risk assessment for sedation-related adverse events. Specify intended sedation level (no sedation, minimal, moderate, deep sedation/MAC or general anesthesia). Document the patient’s ASA classification, Mallampati score, relevant history (sleep apnea, prior sedation reactions, substance use) and NPO status. The Joint Commission mandates documentation of pre-procedural assessment. 
• Obtain and document informed consent for sedation
Before administering sedation, discuss the benefits, risks, limitations and alternatives to the planned sedation approach with the patient. Document that informed consent for sedation was obtained. The ASGE recommends that patients provide informed consent for sedation through a process that includes discussion of the sedation plan and its alternatives. This is distinct from consent for the endoscopic procedure itself.
Sample documentation: "Informed consent for moderate sedation obtained; risks including respiratory depression, aspiration and allergic reaction discussed; patient verbalized understanding and agreed to proceed."
• Perform and document procedural time-out

Conduct a procedural pause ("time out") before initiating sedation. During the time out, the patient and team stop other activities to verify the patient identification and planned outcomes, including the sedation plan. Document completion of the time-out. 

• Document intra-procedural monitoring at required intervals

Record the patient’s vital signs, oxygen saturation, level of consciousness and medications administered at the required intervals: before procedure, after sedative administration, at least every 5 minutes during procedure, during recovery and before discharge. If automated recording is used, set device alarms to alert the team to critical changes. 

• Document discharge criteria and instructions

Confirm and record that the patient met standardized discharge criteria before release. Include verbal and written discharge instructions, such as diet, activity, medication and follow-up information. Give the patient a 24-hour contact number for complications and document that a responsible individual accompanied the patient home. 

Sample documentation language: "Pre-procedure assessment: ASA II, Mallampati II, no history of sleep apnea or prior sedation complications, NPO >8 hours for solids, >2 hours for clear liquids. Sedation plan: moderate sedation with midazolam/fentanyl. Time-out completed. Intra-procedure: VS q5min documented, SpO2 maintained >95% on 2L NC. Recovery: Aldrete score 10, patient alert and oriented, ambulating independently. Discharge criteria met. Written instructions provided, patient verbalized understanding, accompanied by spouse."


ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify and address patient non-adherence with pre-procedure fasting instructions or medication management increases aspiration risk.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient preparation and address barriers to safe sedation.

• Provide NPO instructions and verify compliance

Give clear instructions to patients for when to stop eating and drinking prior to the procedure. The ASGE notes that no universally accepted pre-procedural fasting standard exists. However, ASA guidelines include fasting at least 2 hours after clear liquids and 6 hours after light meals before sedation. For fried or fatty meals, 8 hours of fasting is recommended. These intervals are endorsed by the ACG/AGA as quality indicators. If gastric emptying is impaired or in emergencies, consider delaying the procedure, modifying the sedation level or protecting the airway with endotracheal intubation. 

• Implement individualized protocols for patients on GLP-1 receptor agonists

Proceed with endoscopy for patients on GLP-1 RAs who have followed standard fasting and have no gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea, vomiting, dyspepsia, abdominal distention). For symptomatic or higher risk patients, consider a 24-hour clear liquid diet before the procedure. Tailor decisions to the patient’s indication, dose, symptoms and procedure type. Document the approach and rationale. 

• Document non-adherence and clinical decision-making

Record instances of patient non-adherence to fasting instructions, including discussion, clinical assessment of aspiration risk and decision to proceed, delay or adjust sedation. If proceeding despite non-compliance, document the rationale and any precautions taken. For patients at elevated aspiration risk, consider anesthesia involvement for airway protection, which may include rapid-sequence intubation. The AGA notes that rapid-sequence intubation may not be feasible in most ambulatory or office-based endoscopy settings and typically requires anesthesia provider involvement.

TITLE: Spleen injury
RISK_BRIEF:
Spleen Injury from Colonoscopy

SPECIALTY: Gastroenterology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Colonoscopy (Screening, Diagnostic, Therapeutic)

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Splenic Laceration, Splenic Rupture, Hemorrhage, Splenectomy, Death
Spleen injury from colonoscopy is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in gastroenterology. 
 
Mitigating Your Risk 
 
With rigorous procedural technique, systematic post-procedure surveillance and coordinated multidisciplinary response, gastroenterologists can mitigate the risks associated with splenic injury from colonoscopy and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for spleen injury from colonoscopy and provides strategies to help prevent them.     

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns 
Malpractice claims involving splenic injury from colonoscopy stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 71% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 29%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.    
Actions and Omissions Driving Spleen Injury Risk [insert chart] 

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Missed or Misinterpreted Result or Diagnostic Finding

Contributing action or omission: Failure to recognize splenic injury in patients with post-colonoscopy abdominal pain increases the risk of delayed diagnosis, hemorrhagic shock and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely recognition of splenic injury and proper escalation of care.

• Maintain a high index of suspicion for splenic injury in patients with post-colonoscopy abdominal pain

Include splenic injury in the differential diagnosis when patients present with left upper quadrant pain, left shoulder pain (Kehr sign), abdominal distension or hemodynamic instability after colonoscopy, especially without rectal bleeding. Most cases present within 24-48 hours, though delayed presentations beyond 7 days have been reported. Early recognition is critical; delayed diagnosis is associated with increased mortality.

• Recognize the clinical presentation of post-colonoscopy splenic injury

Look for left upper quadrant pain, the most common presenting symptom (58%) often accompanied by diffuse abdominal pain, nausea, lightheadedness or syncope. Left shoulder pain caused by diaphragmatic irritation (Kehr sign) is classic but not a universal finding. Be alert for hemodynamic instability, which can develop rapidly, and consider a symptom-free period followed by progressive pain as a potential indicator of splenic injury. 

• Order CT imaging promptly when splenic injury is suspected

Use a contrast-enhanced abdominal CT, the diagnostic modality of choice to reliably identify splenic laceration, subcapsular hematoma, hemoperitoneum and active extravasation. Obtain an emergent CT in patients with hemodynamic instability, clinical features of acute abdomen, leukocytosis or acute anemia after colonoscopy. Also consider intestinal perforation in the differential diagnosis. 

• Identify clinical triggers mandating urgent evaluation

Recognize hemodynamic instability, clinical features of acute abdomen, leukocytosis and acute anemia (hemoglobin drop >3 g/dL) as critical factors needing further evaluation. A hemoglobin drop exceeding 3 g/dL has been identified as a predictor of operative intervention in one systematic review of case reports. The largest registry-based study identified transfusion requirements and intensive care treatment as predictors for the need for surgery. These findings, while not validated in prospective studies, support early laboratory assessment and close monitoring in patients with suspected splenic injury. 



CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Medication Error

Contributing action or omission: Delayed or inappropriate resuscitation and suboptimal treatment selection increases the risk of hemorrhagic complications and adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient stabilization and treatment selection.

• Initiate resuscitation immediately in hemodynamically unstable patients

Provide aggressive fluid resuscitation and close hemodynamic monitoring for patients with suspected splenic injury and hemodynamic instability. Provide blood products if indicated. Early surgical consultation is warranted in all cases.

• Select treatment approach based on hemodynamic status and injury grade

Consider conservative approaches for hemodynamically stable patients with contained injuries. Hemodynamically unstable patients or those with high-grade (AAST Grade III-V) injuries typically require surgery, most often splenectomy. Given the benefits of splenic preservation, incorporate splenic artery embolization into treatment pathways for stable patients if available.

• Ensure that post-splenectomy vaccination is initiated and coordinated
Confirm that the surgical team has initiated the CDC-ACIP recommended vaccination series for asplenic adults prior to discharge. Communicate the vaccination plan to the patient's primary care physician for long-term booster management. Educate the patient on lifelong infection risks, including the need for prompt medical attention for febrile illness. Document the vaccination planning, handoff to surgery and primary care and the discussion with the patient.
CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Excessive force, loop formation in the proximal colon and inadequate technique at the splenic flexure increase the risk of splenic injury.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to minimize mechanical stress on the splenocolic ligament and reduce the risk of splenic trauma.

• Avoid pushing through fixed resistance and minimize loop formation

Consider using a pediatric or slim colonoscope in patients with a known history of adhesions, multiple surgeries, or tortuous colon or when having difficulty in the sigmoid colon. When resistance is met, the endoscopist should withdraw, straighten the scope and readvance rather than push through fixed resistance, as doing so risks perforation and splenic rupture. Loop formation, particularly in the proximal colon, should be minimized through active loop reduction techniques to ensure safe progression.

• Use the appropriate technique at the splenic flexure

Handle the splenic flexure carefully; it is a high-risk area due to the splenocolic ligament attachment. Use gentle manipulation, adequate insufflation and position changes to facilitate passage without excessive traction. Exercise caution in patients with known adhesions or difficult anatomy. Document looping, adhesions or anatomic variants in the procedure note. Difficult colonoscopies carry a higher risk of splenic injury, and documentation supports clinical decision-making and medicolegal defensibility. 

• Recognize limits of personal experience and seek support for high-risk cases 

For cases with identified risk factors for technical difficulty (prior abdominal surgery, known adhesions, difficult anatomy), consider consulting a more experienced colleague or referring to a higher-volume center. An endoscopist's procedure volume and experience are independently associated with the risk of severe adverse events, including perforation, bleeding and splenic injury. When overall quality metrics fall below established ACG benchmarks, proactively seek proctoring, mentorship or additional training.  


ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of procedure details, patient risk factors and post-procedure instructions compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Record patient risk factors for complications

Record pre-procedure risk factors, such as prior abdominal or pelvic surgery, adhesions, splenomegaly and anticoagulation status. This demonstrates awareness of elevated risks and supports appropriate technique selection.

• Document the informed consent discussion

Ensure that the informed consent process includes disclosure of the most common and most serious adverse events, including rare but potentially life-threatening complications, along with estimates of frequency and severity, consistent with the ASGE guideline on informed consent. It is best practice to explicitly discuss the possibility of splenic injury in patients who are at high risk of this complication (prior abdominal surgery, splenomegaly, known adhesions).
• Detail procedure specifics, including technical difficulty and technique

Note in the colonoscopy report the anatomic extent of exam, visualization quality, procedural limitations and challenges such as a difficult passage or excessive looping. 

Sample documentation language: "Splenic flexure traversed with moderate difficulty due to acute angulation; scope straightened and readvanced without excessive force. No external pressure applied."

• Document discharge instructions and patient understanding

Record in the procedure note or discharge summary that the patient received instruction on recognizing warning signs of complications, such as splenic injury symptoms, and was given emergency contact information. Ensure that the documentation reflects the patient’s stated understanding.

Sample documentation language: "Patient instructed to seek immediate medical attention for severe abdominal pain, left shoulder pain, lightheadedness or fainting. Patient verbalized understanding of warning signs and received written discharge instructions."

ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUE

Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure

Contributing action or omission: Lack of protocols for post-procedure monitoring and follow-up, multidisciplinary escalation, patient discharge education and adverse event tracking increases the risk of delayed recognition and suboptimal outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve readiness, standardize responses and support coordinated escalation.

• Establish post-procedure surveillance systems

Set up systems, including post-procedure phone calls, to identify delayed adverse events. The ACG/AGA recommends that endoscopy units aim to contact 98% of patients within 72 hours of colonoscopy regardless of sedation type. In addition, continue to contact patients up to 14 days post-procedure, as 15%–45% of adverse events may go unrecognized or unreported without systematic follow-up. Ambulatory surgery centers should maintain policies for post-procedure documentation and follow-up consistent with applicable accreditation and regulatory requirements.
• Establish protocols for multidisciplinary care for splenic injury management
Ensure that endoscopy units have predefined pathways for engaging surgery and interventional radiology (if available) when splenic injury is suspected. Protocols should include clear criteria for escalation, such as transfusion requirements and intensive care needs, which are predictors of surgical intervention. Standardized communication pathways among specialties support timely escalation and appropriate treatment selection.

• Provide discharge instructions with guidance on warning signs

Give patients standardized written and verbal instructions before discharge, highlighting warning signs of splenic injury, such as severe or worsening abdominal pain (especially left-sided), left shoulder pain, lightheadedness, weakness or fainting. Include emergency contact information and guidance on seeking immediate evaluation if symptoms arise.

• Implement adverse event tracking and review

Create systems to identify, track and document severe adverse events related to colonoscopy. When splenic injury occurs, endoscopy units should conduct a root cause analysis to identify contributing factors and implement corrective actions. Ensure that adverse event tracking systems capture events occurring up to 14 days after the procedure, consistent with the ACG/AGA definition of post-procedure adverse events. Late adverse events occurring after 14 days should also be documented when identified.

With rigorous procedural technique, systematic post-procedure surveillance and coordinated multidisciplinary response, gastroenterologists can mitigate the risks associated with splenic injury from colonoscopy and reduce
