TITLE: Common bile duct injury
RISK_BRIEF:
Common Bile Duct Injury During Cholecystectomy 


SPECIALTY: General Surgery

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Cholelithiasis, Cholecystitis, Biliary Disease

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Common Bile Duct Injury

Common bile duct injury during cholecystectomy is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in general surgery.
Mitigating Your Risk 
 
With proactive risk identification, involvement of specialist expertise when needed and precise surgical technique, general surgeons can mitigate the risks associated with common bile duct injury during cholecystectomy and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for common bile duct injury during cholecystectomy and provides strategies to help prevent them. These recommendations address not only best practices under favorable conditions but also the critical decisions surgeons must make when faced with intraoperative or postoperative challenges.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving common bile duct injury during cholecystectomy typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 83% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 17%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate. 

Actions and Omissions Driving Common Bile Duct Injury [insert chart] 

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act On Diagnostic Finding

Contributing action or omission: Failing to recognize anatomic variations, biliary structures or postoperative bile duct injury (BDI) increases the risk of severe patient harm and adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve preoperative risk stratification, intraoperative anatomic recognition and early postoperative detection of injury.

• Perform comprehensive preoperative imaging assessment

Review imaging before a cholecystectomy to identify findings that predict difficult dissection, such as gallbladder wall thickening ≥4 mm, pericholecystic fluid, impacted stones and common bile duct dilation. In patients with at-risk conditions like scleroatrophic cholecystitis or Mirizzi syndrome, obtain additional imaging such as MRCP to define biliary anatomy before surgery. Identifying high-risk features preoperatively allows for appropriate surgical planning, resource allocation and an informed consent discussion. 

• Identify anatomic landmarks systematically before dissection

At the start of every cholecystectomy, identify the B-SAFE landmarks: Bile duct, sulcus of Rouvière, left hepatic artery, umbilical fissure and enteric structure (duodenum). The imaginary plane passing through the sulcus of Rouvière and base of the umbilical fissure divides a generally safe area (anterior) from a potentially dangerous one (posterior). Systematic landmarking prevents tunnel vision errors and supports orientation throughout the procedure. 

• Recognize clinical signs of postoperative bile duct injury

Be highly suspicious of BDI in patients with persistent abdominal pain, fever, nausea, vomiting, abdominal distension or jaundice after a cholecystectomy. Bile leaks typically present within two weeks with fever, abdominal pain and biloma, while biliary strictures present later with cholestatic jaundice and cholangitis. Laboratory findings include leukocytosis and elevated alkaline phosphatase, GGT and bilirubin. Early recognition enables timely intervention and referral. 

• Obtain appropriate imaging when postoperative BDI is suspected

When clinical signs suggest BDI, obtain a CT or ultrasound to identify fluid collections. In stable patients, a HIDA scan can identify an ongoing bile leak. MRCP or ERCP defines biliary anatomy and injury classification. Early imaging prevents progression to sepsis and multiorgan failure. 


CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Consult or Transfer Care

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain hepatobiliary surgery consultation or transfer to a specialized center when bile duct injury (BDI) is recognized or suspected increases the risk of failed repair, recurrent strictures and long-term morbidity.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that appropriate expertise is engaged when BDI occurs and to optimize the timing of definitive repair.

• Obtain immediate hepatopancreatobiliary (HPB) surgery consultation when recognizing BDI intraoperatively

When identifying BDI during cholecystectomy without an experienced hepatobiliary surgeon available on-site, place one or two large closed-suction drains in the operative bed and transfer the patient to a center with HPB expertise. Immediate repair attempts by inexpert surgeons are associated with worse outcomes and may compromise later revisions. Early referral to an HPB center significantly decreases postoperative complications (OR 0.24) and biliary strictures (OR 0.28). 

• Avoid on-table repair by non-HPB surgeons for major injuries

Do not attempt primary repair for major BDIs (Strasberg E injuries) with tissue loss or suspected ischemic injury unless HPB expertise is available. Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy is the recommended reconstruction method. Avoid primary choledochocholedochostomy due to poor outcomes from loss of length and devascularization. 

• Recognize that conversion to open surgery does not reduce BDI risk

When considering conversion to open surgery for such issues as significant bleeding, cholecystoenteric fistula, Mirizzi syndrome or suspected malignancy, keep in mind that evidence does not show conversion alone avoids or reduces BDI risk. The most severe biliary and vascular injuries often occur after conversion. Conversion should address a specific surgical challenge, not serve as a substitute for critical view of safety or careful dissection technique. 
• Ensure definitive repair within 72 hours when expertise is available

When HPB expertise is available, pursue early repair (defined as on-table within 72 hours) of BDI. Early, aggressive surgical repair avoids sepsis and offers advantages in reduced costs and hospital readmissions. Keep in mind that between 48 and 72 hours, inflammation decreases but proliferation and healing begin, which can complicate surgical repair. 
CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Failing to achieve the Critical View of Safety (CVS), applying excessive traction, liberal use of electrocautery, dissection in dangerous zones or continuing hazardous dissection when a bailout procedure is indicated are the primary technical factors contributing to bile duct injury (BDI).

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize safe dissection techniques, ensure proper anatomic identification and provide bailout options when safe dissection isn’t possible.

• Optimize timing of surgery for acute cholecystitis

Understand that the optimal timing for a cholecystectomy is within 48 hours and no more than 10 days from symptom appearance (GRADE 1A) for patients with acute cholecystitis. Early surgery reduces inflammation-related technical difficulty. Delaying surgery beyond 10 days increases the risk of dense adhesions and difficult dissection. 
• Achieve and document the CVS before clipping any structure

CVS requires: (1) clearance of Calot’s triangle (hepatocystic triangle) of fat and fibrous tissue, (2) visualization of the lower third of the gallbladder separated from the liver bed (cystic plate clearance) and (3) only two structures (cystic duct and cystic artery) entering the gallbladder. Do not clip or divide any structure until all three criteria are met. Routine application of CVS reduces BDI to 0.09%. 
In many cases, particularly acute cholecystitis, the anatomic landmarks required for safe dissection are obscured, and achieving a critical view of safety may be difficult or impossible. Recognizing when to stop pursuing a standard laparoscopic cholecystectomy and transition to an alternative approach is one of the most consequential decisions a surgeon can make during this procedure.

• Use intraoperative biliary imaging when anatomy is uncertain

The Multi-Society Practice Guideline strongly recommends using intraoperative biliary imaging, such as IOC, intraoperative ultrasound or ICG fluorescent cholangiography, when anatomy is unclear or biliary injury is suspected. ICG cholangiography improves visualization of biliary structures and may reduce conversion rates and decrease BDI. 

• Employ bailout procedures when CVS cannot be safely achieved

Stop dissection in Calot’s triangle and transition to a bailout procedure when the CVS isn’t possible due to severe inflammation or unclear anatomy. Options include subtotal cholecystectomy (fenestrating or reconstituting), fundus-first (top-down) approach or cholecystostomy tube placement. Subtotal cholecystectomy has a BDI rate of 0.3–0.63% compared to 4.4% for conversion to an open procedure. A subtotal cholecystectomy or cholecystostomy tube is not a failure; it is a decision to prioritize patient safety over completing the originally planned procedure.

• Avoid dissection posterior to the plane of safety

Avoid dissection posterior to the plane passing through the sulcus of Rouvière and base of the umbilical fissure, which defines the boundary between safe and dangerous zones. Excessive cephalad traction narrows the angle between the cystic duct and common bile duct, bringing them into alignment and increasing the risk of misidentifying the CBD as the cystic duct. Frequently reorient by backing the camera out and "waving the flag" (moving the gallbladder back and forth).



ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Communication failures—including inadequate intraoperative consultation, unclear escalation pathways and insufficient postoperative handoffs—increase the risk of delayed recognition and suboptimal management of bile duct injury (BDI).

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support coordinated team responses when difficult cases or complications arise.

• Request intraoperative consultation when encountering a difficult gallbladder

When anatomy is unclear or dissection becomes hazardous, consider asking another surgeon for an opinion, physically or virtually, before proceeding. This is an easy, effective and inexpensive alternative to proceeding with uncertain anatomy. 

• Communicate clearly during handoff when BDI is suspected or confirmed

Use structured handoff frameworks such as SBAR when transferring care from the operating room to the postoperative unit or to another facility. Communicate operative findings,  confirmation or suspicion of BDI, drain placement and output, imaging and monitoring parameters. Document handoff content in the medical record.

• Establish clear escalation triggers for postoperative concerns

Create protocols that guide escalation of postoperative care. Triggers include increasing abdominal pain, fever, tachycardia, bilious drain output, jaundice or abnormal liver function tests. Ensure that nursing staff and covering physicians understand the triggers and can easily reach the on-call surgeon.

• Communicate surgeon-to-surgeon when transferring a patient to an HPB center 

When transferring a patient with confirmed or suspected BDI to a specialized center, communicate directly with the receiving hepatobiliary (HPB) surgeon. Provide operative notes, imaging and drain output data. Direct communication means that critical information is passed along and ensures that the receiving facility is prepared for the patient.



ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Failing to adequately document operative technique, anatomic findings or clinical decision-making compromises continuity of care and increases liability risk.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document achievement of the Critical View of Safety (CVS) with photographic evidence

Capture intraoperative photographs or video demonstrating the CVS before clipping any structure. Store images in the patient's medical record. Providing photo documentation of satisfactory CVS is associated with lower rates of BDI (0.3% vs. 2.3% without photos) and postoperative complications and also provides objective evidence of safe technique.[17] 

• Include essential elements in the operative report

Ensure that the operative report includes the clinical context and indication for cholecystectomy, intraoperative findings (degree of inflammation, adhesions and anatomic variations) and a description of the CVS and how it was achieved. The report also should detail cholangiography findings, operative data (time, blood loss, energy device used, need for conversion), complications and their management, and drain placement. 

Sample documentation language: "Calot’s triangle was dissected and cleared of fat and fibrous tissue. Two structures only—the cystic duct and cystic artery—were visualized entering the gallbladder. The lower third of the gallbladder was separated from the cystic plate. The critical view of safety was achieved and documented photographically. The cystic duct and artery were then clipped and divided."

• Document clinical reasoning when performing bailout procedures

Record factors precluding safe achievement of CVS when performing a subtotal cholecystectomy or other bailout procedure, such as the degree of inflammation, inability to identify structures or bleeding obscuring the field. Document the type of bailout (fenestrating vs. reconstituting), management of the gallbladder remnant and drain placement. This documentation demonstrates appropriate surgical judgment. 

• Document postoperative instructions and patient understanding
Record postoperative instructions, follow-up plans and surgical team contact information and document their delivery to patients, including warning signs communicated to them that require immediate evaluation: fever, increasing abdominal pain, jaundice, nausea or vomiting.

ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR

Failure to Recognize Responsibility

Contributing action or omission: Failing to recognize when operative conditions exceed expertise or when a change in surgical strategy is required increases the risk of preventable bile duct injury (BDI).

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to promote a culture of safety and appropriate recognition of operative limitations.

• Recognize and respond to intraoperative warning signs

Stop dissection when:
Identifying the cystic duct and artery isn’t possible
Dense inflammation obscures Calot’s triangle
Bleeding obscures the operative field
Anatomy is unclear
These are signals to pause, reassess and consider alternative strategies, as persisting with standard cholecystectomy under these conditions significantly increases BDI risk. 

• Adopt a low threshold for bailout procedures

Recognize that subtotal cholecystectomy is not a failure but a deliberate safety strategy. In up to 15% of operations for acute cholecystitis, achieving CVS safely is not possible. The rate of subtotal cholecystectomy increased from 0.2% in 2012 to 1.4% in 2021, reflecting growing recognition of its role in preventing BDI. The morbidity associated with BDI far exceeds the morbidity associated with subtotal cholecystectomy. 

• Disclose complications promptly and transparently

When BDI occurs, promptly and transparently disclose the complication to the patient and family. Document the disclosure conversation and arrange appropriate follow-up and specialist consultation. Prompt disclosure and appropriate management reduce both patient harm and litigation risk.
• Participate in simulation training and case review

Engage in multidisciplinary simulations of difficult cholecystectomy scenarios. Use structured debriefing frameworks after challenging cases to identify opportunities for improvement. Track and measure performance on safe cholecystectomy metrics.

TITLE: Anticoagulation
RISK_BRIEF:
Perioperative Bleeding and Thromboembolism in Anticoagulated Surgical Patients

SPECIALTY: General Surgery

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Elective and Urgent Surgical Procedures in Anticoagulated Patients

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Perioperative Hemorrhage, Thromboembolic Events (Stroke, Pulmonary Embolism, Deep Vein Thrombosis)
Hemorrhage and thromboembolic events in anticoagulated surgical patients are significant drivers of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in general surgery.
Mitigating Your Risk

With proactive risk identification, appropriate perioperative medication management and comprehensive documentation, general surgeons can mitigate the risks associated with perioperative bleeding and thromboembolism in anticoagulated patients and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for perioperative anticoagulation errors and provides strategies to help prevent them. These recommendations address not only best practices under favorable conditions but also the critical decisions surgeons must make when faced with intraoperative or postoperative challenges.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving serious bleeding or thromboembolic complications in anticoagulated patients typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 83% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 17%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.

Actions and Omissions Driving Bleeding and Thromboembolism Risk in Anticoagulated Surgical Patients [insert chart] 

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify all antithrombotic medications, assess thromboembolic risk or recognize patient-specific bleeding risk factors can result in preventable hemorrhagic or thromboembolic complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure comprehensive preoperative assessment and accurate risk stratification.

• Obtain a complete antithrombotic medication history at the initial surgical consultation

During preoperative evaluation, document all anticoagulants (warfarin, DOACs), antiplatelet agents (aspirin, clopidogrel, prasugrel and ticagrelor) and supplements affecting hemostasis. Verify the specific agent, dose, indication and timing of the last dose. Conducting this assessment at the initial surgical consultation allows adequate time for planning. Incomplete medication histories are a common contributor to perioperative bleeding complications and reflect a failure to meet the standard of care.

• Stratify thromboembolic risk using validated scoring systems

For patients with atrial fibrillation, calculate the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score to quantify stroke risk. Stratify risk in patients with mechanical heart valves according to valve type, location and individual thromboembolic risk factors. For patients with recent VTE, assess the time elapsed since diagnosis and individual risk factors such as cancer and thrombophilia. Defer elective surgery for ≥3 months after VTE diagnosis and ≥9 months after ischemic stroke. Validated risk stratification shows defensible clinical decision-making and identifies patients who may require bridging anticoagulation.

• Assess patient-specific bleeding risk factors

Evaluate for factors that increase perioperative bleeding risk, including:
A history of prior bleeding events (particularly within three months)
Bleeding with similar procedures or prior bridging
Platelet dysfunction (uremia) or thrombocytopenia
Concomitant antiplatelet therapy
Supratherapeutic INR (above target range)
Advanced age
Consider using the HAS-BLED score to quantify patient-related bleeding risk. Identifying modifiable risk factors enables optimal preoperative preparation.

• Classify procedural bleeding risk

Categorize the planned procedure as minimal, low-to-moderate or high bleeding risk based on expected 30-day postoperative major bleeding rates. Minimal-risk procedures, such as minor dental or skin procedures, may proceed without interruption of anticoagulation. Low-to-moderate-risk procedures, such as cholecystectomy and inguinal hernia repair, require shorter interruption intervals. High-risk procedures, such as major cancer surgery and procedures with neuraxial anesthesia, require complete anticoagulant elimination. Accurate procedural risk classification determines the appropriate interruption strategy.



CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain appropriate laboratory testing to assess anticoagulation status or renal function increases the risk of proceeding to surgery with residual anticoagulant effect or inappropriate dosing adjustments.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure adequate laboratory assessment before surgical intervention.

• Obtain INR for patients on warfarin before elective surgery

Check INR within 24-48 hours of a planned procedure to confirm adequate reversal (target INR ≤1.5) in patients on vitamin K antagonists. If INR remains elevated, consider delaying surgery or administering low-dose oral vitamin K. Proceeding to surgery with supratherapeutic INR significantly increases bleeding risk and represents a deviation from standard practice.

• Assess renal function to guide DOAC interruption timing

Obtain serum creatinine and calculate creatinine clearance before surgery in all patients on DOACs. Dabigatran has high renal dependence; patients with CrCl <50 mL/min require extended interruption (3-5 days for high-bleeding-risk procedures vs. two days with normal renal function). Factor Xa inhibitors, such as apixaban, rivaroxaban and edoxaban, also require extended interruption with significant renal impairment. Failing to adjust interruption timing for renal function is a common source of perioperative bleeding.

• Consider DOAC level testing for urgent/emergent procedures

Use laboratory testing to measure DOAC levels in patients who have recently taken a DOAC and require urgent (<24 hours) or emergent (<6 hours) surgery to help determine whether reversal agents are indicated. Standard coagulation tests (PT, aPTT) have limited sensitivity for detecting DOAC effect. Drug-specific assays (anti-Xa for factor Xa inhibitors, dilute thrombin time for dabigatran) provide a more accurate assessment when available.

• Obtain baseline coagulation studies for high-risk procedures

Obtain baseline PT/INR, aPTT and platelet count before surgery for patients with high bleeding risk undergoing major surgical procedures. These values provide a reference for intraoperative and postoperative monitoring and may reveal previously undiagnosed coagulopathies.

CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Medication Error

Contributing action or omission: Errors in anticoagulation interruption timing, inappropriate bridging decisions, premature or delayed resumption of anticoagulation or failure to use reversal agents when indicated increase the risk of hemorrhagic or thromboembolic complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate perioperative anticoagulation management and reduce medication-related errors.

• Apply standardized DOAC interruption protocols based on bleeding risk and renal function

Implement time-based interruption without routine DOAC level testing or heparin bridging in patients undergoing elective procedures. For low-to-moderate bleeding risk procedures, such as cholecystectomy or hernia repair, discontinue DOACs 1 day before surgery and restart one day after. In high bleeding risk procedures, such as major cancer surgery or neuraxial anesthesia, discontinue DOACs 2 days before surgery and restart 2-3 days after. Extend interruption intervals for dabigatran in patients with CrCl <50 mL/min. The PAUSE study demonstrated that this standardized approach results in low rates of major bleeding (0.9%-1.85%) and thromboembolism (<1%).

• Avoid routine bridging anticoagulation in most patients

In patients with atrial fibrillation on warfarin or DOACs and undergoing elective surgery, avoid routine perioperative bridging with heparin due to increased bleeding risk without reducing thromboembolic events. The BRIDGE trial demonstrated that bridging increased major bleeding (3.2% vs. 1.3%) without reducing arterial thromboembolism. Reserve bridging for patients at very high thromboembolic risk, such as those with mechanical mitral valves, those who have had a recent stroke/TIA (<3 months) or those with a CHA₂DS₂-VASc score >4. When bridging is indicated, discontinue therapeutic LMWH 24 hours before surgery and UFH 4-6 hours before surgery.

• Resume anticoagulation based on procedural bleeding risk and hemostasis

For procedures with low risk of bleeding, resume anticoagulation on postoperative day 1 after confirming hemostasis. For procedures with high bleeding risk, delay therapeutic anticoagulation until postoperative day 2-3. Warfarin can often be restarted the evening of surgery, given its delayed onset of action. DOACs have a rapid onset (peak effect 2-3 hours) and should be delayed in cases of ongoing bleeding or uncertainty about surgical-site hemostasis. Document the rationale for resumption timing.

• Use reversal agents appropriately for urgent/emergent surgery

Administer vitamin K and 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) to patients on warfarin with elevated INR requiring urgent surgery. For patients on dabigatran, administer idarucizumab (5 g IV). For patients on factor Xa inhibitors requiring emergent surgery, use 4-factor PCC (50 U/kg). Andexanet alfa has been withdrawn from the US market due to unresolved safety concerns, including excess thrombotic events, making 4-factor PCC the primary reversal option. Allowing sufficient time for drug clearance is always preferred when feasible.

• Coordinate anticoagulation management through multidisciplinary communication

Perioperative anticoagulation management may involve surgeons, anesthesiologists, internists, cardiologists and pharmacists. Use standard protocols integrated into electronic medical records as clinical decision support tools. Communicate the anticoagulation plan at preoperative visits, surgical time-outs and postoperative handoffs. Document the plan for interruption, bridging (if applicable) and resumption in the medical record.


ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of anticoagulation status, risk stratification, management plan and patient education compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document the complete anticoagulation assessment at preoperative evaluation

Record all antithrombotic medications with specific agent, dose, indication and date/time of last dose. Document the calculated thromboembolic risk score (CHA₂DS₂-VASc, valve risk category) and bleeding risk assessment (HAS-BLED, patient-specific factors). Include the procedural bleeding risk classification. This documentation demonstrates systematic risk assessment and supports the rationale for the management plan.

Sample documentation language: "Patient on apixaban 5 mg BID for atrial fibrillation (CHA₂DS₂-VASc 4, HAS-BLED 2). Planned laparoscopic cholecystectomy classified as low-to-moderate bleeding risk. Plan: Hold apixaban 1 day preoperatively (last dose 3/24 PM), resume POD 1 if hemostasis achieved. No bridging indicated per current guidelines."

• Document the anticoagulation interruption and resumption plan

Record the exact dates and times for anticoagulation interruption and planned resumption. If bridging is indicated, document the specific bridging agent, dose and timing. If bridging is not used, document the rationale (such as, "Bridging not indicated per ACC/AHA guidelines given low-to-moderate thromboembolic risk and increased bleeding risk with bridging"). This documentation demonstrates adherence to evidence-based protocols.

• Document intraoperative and postoperative anticoagulation-related events

Record intraoperative bleeding complications, blood product administration or hemostatic interventions. Document the assessment of surgical-site hemostasis before anticoagulation resumption. If anticoagulation resumption is delayed beyond the planned timeline, document the clinical rationale. This documentation supports defensibility if complications occur.

• Document patient education regarding anticoagulation management

Before discharge, record patient education regarding anticoagulation resumption, including which medication to restart, at what dose, on what date, and any signs of bleeding or thromboembolism requiring immediate evaluation. Note the patient's stated understanding of the instructions and any barriers to adherence.

Sample documentation language: "Discussed postoperative anticoagulation plan with patient. Instructed to resume apixaban 5 mg BID starting tomorrow (3/27) AM. Reviewed warning signs of bleeding (blood in stool, excessive bruising, prolonged bleeding from incision) and thromboembolism (leg swelling, shortness of breath, neurologic symptoms). Patient verbalized understanding. Follow-up appointment scheduled for 3/31."

Mitigating Your Risk

With proactive risk identification, appropriate perioperative medication management and c

TITLE: Bowel injury
RISK_BRIEF:
Bowel Injury During Cholecystectomy

SPECIALTY: General Surgery

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Cholelithiasis, Cholecystitis, Biliary Colic

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Bowel Injury
Bowel injury during cholecystectomy is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in general surgery. 
Mitigating Your Risk
With proactive risk identification, vigilant monitoring and coordinated response, general surgeons can mitigate the risks associated with bowel injury during cholecystectomy and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for bowel injury during cholecystectomy and provides strategies to help prevent them. These recommendations address not only best practices under favorable conditions but also the critical decisions surgeons must make when faced with intraoperative or postoperative challenges.
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving bowel injury during cholecystectomy typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 89% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 11%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Bowel Injury [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act On Diagnostic Finding
Contributing action or omission: Failing to recognize intraoperative bowel injury or delaying diagnosis of postoperative bowel perforation increases the risk of sepsis, multiorgan failure and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve early identification of bowel injury during and after cholecystectomy, supporting timely intervention and reducing the risk of catastrophic outcomes.

• Perform systematic inspection of all trocar sites and dissection areas before closure

Before completing a procedure, methodically inspect all trocar entry sites, areas of adhesiolysis and any bowel that was manipulated or retracted during the case. Conduct the inspection at the end of every laparoscopic cholecystectomy, regardless of perceived difficulty. Unrecognized bowel injury at the time of surgery is the primary driver of delayed diagnosis and poor outcomes; up to 69% of injuries are not recognized intraoperatively. Systematic inspection demonstrates adherence to standard surgical practice.
• Maintain high clinical suspicion for bowel injury in patients with prior abdominal surgery

When operating on patients with previous abdominal surgery, anticipate adhesions at trocar sites and consider modified access techniques, such as epigastric trocar-first approach or open (Hasson) entry away from prior incisions. Evidence shows that 68.9% of laparoscopic bowel injuries occur in patients with adhesions or prior laparotomy. 

• Recognize the clinical presentation of delayed bowel injury

Until proven otherwise, presume bowel injury in a patient presenting with the following symptoms after laparoscopic cholecystectomy: severe focal trocar site pain, abdominal distention, diarrhea and leukopenia (not leukocytosis), followed by rapid cardiopulmonary collapse from sepsis. Patients with unrecognized bowel perforation typically present within 96 hours with such characteristic symptoms. Early recognition relies on maintaining a high index of suspicion and performing a vigilant physical examination, as laboratory and radiographic findings are often nonspecific.

• Pursue early surgical exploration when bowel injury is suspected

Do not delay intervention for additional diagnostic studies. Suspected bowel injury following laparoscopy mandates early laparotomy or diagnostic laparoscopy to avoid life-threatening complications and malpractice claims. 

CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention
Contributing action or omission: Insufficient postoperative monitoring and delayed response to clinical deterioration increase the risk of missed bowel injury and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support earlier identification of complications, prompt escalation of care and appropriate postoperative surveillance.

• Establish clear postoperative monitoring parameters and escalation triggers

Define and communicate vital sign thresholds and clinical findings that require immediate physician notification, such as persistent tachycardia, fever, increasing abdominal pain, abdominal distention or signs of peritonitis. Document those parameters in postoperative orders and communicate them during nursing handoffs. Early recognition of deterioration is critical, as most patients with unrecognized bowel injury develop sepsis within 96 hours.

• Use clinical judgment rather than routine laboratory testing to guide evaluation

When clinical findings such as unexplained abdominal pain, fever, tachycardia or failure to progress as expected raise concern for complications, promptly order targeted imaging and laboratory evaluations, including CBC, CRP and liver function tests. Routine postoperative laboratory testing has low sensitivity and specificity for detecting bowel injury and other complications after laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Clinical assessment should guide the decision to investigate. 
• Provide explicit discharge instructions with warning signs

Before discharge, whether same-day or after overnight observation, give both written and verbal instructions detailing symptoms that require immediate evaluation, such as severe or worsening abdominal pain, fever, nausea, vomiting, abdominal distention or inability to tolerate food or liquids. Document that the patient received and verbalized understanding of these instructions. Clear discharge education encourages patient to return for evaluation earlier rather than later and demonstrates appropriate patient communication.

• Establish clear pathways for patient follow-up on postoperative concerns

Make sure that patients understand how to get in touch with the surgical team or seek emergency care if concerning symptoms develop and ensure that physicians are readily available for patients to contact. Consider telephone follow-up within 24–48 hours for same-day discharges, especially for patients at risk of complications. Providing options for patients to follow up on postoperative concerns reduces delays in seeking care.
CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL
Technique-Related Error
Contributing action or omission: Technical errors during trocar insertion, adhesiolysis or electrocautery dissection, or from continuing dissection when a bailout procedure is indicated, are the primary causes of iatrogenic bowel injury.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce the likelihood of bowel injury through standardized techniques, appropriate use of bailout procedures and intraoperative vigilance.

• Use safe access techniques, particularly in patients with previous abdominal surgery

In patients with prior periumbilical incisions, consider using modified access techniques, such as open (Hasson) entry at an alternative site, epigastric trocar-first approach with adhesiolysis under direct vision, or optical trocar entry at Palmer's point. Trocar or Veress needle insertion causes 41.8% of laparoscopic bowel injuries, while the epigastric trocar-first approach can significantly reduce small bowel injury rates and conversion to open surgery in patients with previous abdominal surgery.

• Achieve the Critical View of Safety (CVS) before clipping or dividing any structures

Ensure the CVS by (1) clearing Calot’s triangle (hepatocystic triangle) of fat and fibrous tissue, (2) visualizing only two structures entering the gallbladder (cystic duct and cystic artery), and (3) separating the lower third of the gallbladder from the liver bed (cystic plate). While CVS primarily addresses biliary injury, the disciplined dissection required also reduces the risk of adjacent bowel injury and vascular injuries (GRADE 1C). In many cases, particularly acute cholecystitis, the anatomic landmarks required for safe dissection are obscured, and achieving a critical view of safety may be difficult or impossible. Recognizing when to stop pursuing a standard laparoscopic cholecystectomy and transition to an alternative approach is one of the most consequential decisions a surgeon can make during this procedure.
• Maintain meticulous technique during electrocautery dissection

During electrocautery dissection, use the lowest effective energy settings, avoid prolonged activation and stay aware of the instrument tip position relative to the bowel. Electrocautery or laser causes 25.6% of laparoscopic bowel injuries, often through thermal spread to adjacent structures. Thermal injuries may not be immediately apparent and can result in delayed perforation days after surgery.
• Employ bailout procedures when anatomy cannot be safely defined 

When severe inflammation, dense adhesions or unclear anatomy prevent safe achievement of CVS, transition to a bailout procedure instead of persisting with hazardous dissection. Options include subtotal cholecystectomy (fenestrating or reconstituting), fundus-first dissection or cholecystostomy tube placement. Subtotal cholecystectomy is associated with a bile duct injury rate of 0.3%–0.8% and avoids the risks of dissection in a hostile operative field. Conversion to open surgery, while sometimes necessary, is itself associated with higher rates of intestinal injury compared to laparoscopic subtotal cholecystectomy, reinforcing the value of laparoscopic bailout options when feasible. A subtotal cholecystectomy or cholecystostomy tube is not a failure; it is a deliberate decision to prioritize patient safety over completing the originally planned procedure.
• Ensure adequate neuromuscular relaxation throughout the procedure

Communicate with anesthesia throughout the case to ensure adequate relaxation, including during port removal and fascial closure. Inadequate muscle relaxation, particularly during fascial closure, is associated with bowel injury during trocar manipulation and closure.




ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of intraoperative findings, surgical techniques and postoperative instructions compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document achievement of Critical View of Safety (CVS) with photographic evidence

Capture intraoperative photographs or video demonstrating all three components of CVS before clipping the cystic duct and artery. Photographic documentation provides objective evidence of safe dissection technique and is increasingly recognized as standard practice.

• Use operative note templates that capture key safety elements

Utilize procedure-specific operative note templates that prompt documentation of patient positioning, trocar placement technique, presence or absence of adhesions and achievement (or lack thereof) of CVS. Templates should also prompt recording of trocar sites, bailout procedures, bowel inspection and intraoperative complications or concerns. Templates significantly improve the completeness of documentation compared to free-text dictation.

Sample documentation language: "Critical view of safety achieved with clear visualization of cystic duct and cystic artery entering gallbladder and clearance of Calot’s triangle. Photographic documentation obtained. Systematic inspection of all trocar sites and visualized bowel performed at conclusion of case—no evidence of injury."

• Document clinical reasoning for bailout procedures

When subtotal cholecystectomy, conversion to open surgery or other bailout procedures are necessary, document the findings that prompted the decision: degree of inflammation, inability to identify anatomy, bleeding or other factors. Documentation demonstrates appropriate surgical judgment and adherence to guidelines recommending bailout when CVS cannot be safely achieved.

• Document postoperative instructions and patient understanding

Record delivery of postoperative instructions and follow-up plans to patients, including warning signs communicated to them that require immediate evaluation: severe abdominal pain, fever, abdominal distention and nausea/vomiting. Note the patient's stated understanding of the instructions. Documentation supports continuity of care and demonstrates appropriate patient communication.

Bowel Injury During Cholecystectomy

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Cholelithiasis, Cholecystitis, Biliary Colic

Mitigating Your Risk
With proactive risk identification, vigilant monitoring and coordinated response, general surgeons can mitigate the risks associated with bowel injury during cholecystectomy and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that

TITLE: Anticoagulation
RISK_BRIEF:
Anticoagulant Management
SPECIALTY: Hospital Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Venous Thromboembolism, Atrial Fibrillation, Acute Medical Illness

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Major Bleeding, Intracranial Hemorrhage, Thromboembolic Events, Death

Anticoagulant management is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in hospital medicine.
Mitigating Your Risk
With systematic risk assessment, standardized monitoring protocols and coordinated care transitions, hospital medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with anticoagulant management and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for anticoagulant management and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving anticoagulant management typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 81% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 19%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Anticoagulant Management Risk [insert chart] 

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain appropriate baseline and ongoing laboratory testing to guide anticoagulant dosing and identify patients at elevated bleeding risk increases the likelihood of adverse drug events.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate patient selection, dosing and monitoring of anticoagulant therapy.

• Obtain baseline laboratory evaluation before therapeutic anticoagulation

Before starting therapy, obtain a CBC with platelets, serum creatinine, hepatic function tests (AST, ALT) and coagulation studies (PT/INR, aPTT). Establish a baseline INR for warfarin patients and calculate creatinine clearance to guide DOAC dosing. This testing ensures appropriate dose selection, identifies contraindications such as severe thrombocytopenia or hepatic failure, and meets Joint Commission requirements for defensible prescribing. 

• Monitor renal function for hospitalized patients on DOACs

Reassess renal function when clinical status changes (such as acute kidney injury, sepsis, or volume depletion) and consider at least every 48–72 hours for critically ill patients, as DOAC dosing heavily depends on renal function. The 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS AF Guideline recommends regular renal function monitoring. Use clinical judgment and institutional protocols to guide specific intervals. 

• Use validated tools to assess bleeding risk when patients are admitted and when clinical status changes

Use context-appropriate tools to assess bleeding risk. For acutely ill medical patients being considered for VTE chemoprophylaxis, use the IMPROVE Bleeding Risk Score to guide the decision between pharmacological and mechanical prophylaxis. For patients with atrial fibrillation being considered for therapeutic anticoagulation, use the HAS-BLED score to identify modifiable bleeding risk factors and inform shared decision-making about oral anticoagulant therapy. Conducting and documenting risk assessments supports individualized prophylaxis decisions and demonstrates adherence to evidence-based practice.

• Screen for drug-drug interactions at admission and with medication changes

Review patient medication lists for CYP3A4/P-glycoprotein inhibitors (ketoconazole, ritonavir, itraconazole) and inducers (rifampin, phenytoin, carbamazepine) that significantly alter DOAC levels. Be aware that combining antiplatelet agents, NSAIDs or selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors with warfarin increases bleeding risk. In addition, commonly prescribed antibiotics—including trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin), macrolides (azithromycin, clarithromycin), metronidazole, and fluconazole—can significantly increase warfarin’s anticoagulant effect. Monitor INR closely when starting or stopping any antibiotic in a warfarin-treated patient.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Insufficient monitoring of anticoagulation therapy and failure to recognize early signs of bleeding or thrombosis increase the risk of adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support early identification of complications and prompt escalation of care.

• Standardize INR monitoring protocols for patients on warfarin

For hospitalized patients on warfarin, monitor INR frequently until their INR stabilizes within the therapeutic range (typically 2.0–3.0). Daily monitoring is advisable. Maximizing time in the therapeutic range reduces the risk of thromboembolism and major bleeding. Increase INR monitoring frequency when interacting medications, particularly antibiotics, antifungals or amiodarone, are started or discontinued, as these agents can cause rapid and clinically significant INR fluctuations.

• Implement structured surveillance for bleeding complications

Establish protocols for monitoring hemoglobin, hematocrit and clinical signs of bleeding (melena, hematuria, ecchymoses, altered mental status). While gastrointestinal bleeding occurs most frequently, intracranial hemorrhage carries the worst prognosis. Use early warning systems to detect vital sign shifts suggesting hemorrhage. Prompt recognition enables timely intervention before hemodynamic compromise.

• Monitor for treatment failure and recurrent thrombosis

Train clinical staff to recognize signs of recurrent VTE (new leg swelling, dyspnea, chest pain) and stroke (focal neurologic deficits). Patients with subtherapeutic anticoagulation or those receiving CYP3A4 inducers face significantly higher thromboembolic risks. Document clinical assessments and escalate concerns promptly.

• Reassess anticoagulation appropriateness when clinical status changes

Re-evaluate the indication, dose and agent if a patient develops acute kidney injury, experiences bleeding, requires invasive procedures or develops new contraindications. Critical illness alters LMWH absorption and distribution, potentially resulting in subtherapeutic anti-Xa levels. Consider transitioning from DOAC to parenteral anticoagulation (UFH or LMWH) in critically ill patients who are unable to take oral medications, have significant acute kidney injury, require urgent invasive procedures, or have rapidly changing clinical status that precludes reliable oral drug absorption. Routine conversion of DOACs to heparin-based products is not recommended, as heparin bridging has been associated with increased bleeding risk without reduction in thromboembolic events. Ongoing reassessment prevents both bleeding and thrombotic complications.

CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Consult or Transfer Care

Contributing action or omission: Failing to involve appropriate specialists or transfer patients to higher levels of care when they experience anticoagulation-related complications increases the risk of adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely access to specialized expertise and appropriate escalation.

• Establish clear escalation pathways for major bleeding

Define institutional protocols for rapid response activation, hematology consultation and ICU transfer. Protocols should include criteria for reversal agent administration and massive transfusion to expedite life-saving interventions.

• Involve pharmacy in complex anticoagulation management

Consider engaging clinical pharmacists for patients navigating renal or hepatic impairment, multiple drug interactions, agent transitions or bridging therapy. Pharmacist involvement in anticoagulation management improves outcomes and reduces medication errors. Document pharmacy consultations and recommendations.

• Consult hematology for patients with coagulopathies or unusual bleeding patterns

Consider obtaining hematology consultation for patients with unexplained bleeding despite appropriate anticoagulation levels, suspected heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, acquired coagulopathies or need for specialized reversal strategies. Expert consultation supports optimal management and demonstrates appropriate use of available resources.

• Ensure availability of reversal agents and establish protocols for their use

Maintain institutional protocols for anticoagulant reversal. Specify 4-factor PCC with IV vitamin K for VKAs, idarucizumab for dabigatran, and 4-factor PCC or aPCC for factor Xa inhibitors. Institutions are encouraged to stock reversal agents in emergency departments and critical care areas for immediate management of life-threatening bleeding. 



ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Provider-to-provider communication failures—including inadequate handoffs, unclear documentation of anticoagulation status and insufficient coordination during care transitions—increase the likelihood of medication errors.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and enable coordinated team response.

• Implement structured handoff protocols for anticoagulated patients

Use standardized handoff frameworks such as SBAR and I-PASS to ensure that clinical reasoning, active bleeding concerns, pending management decisions and escalation triggers for anticoagulated patients are communicated between providers. Document handoff details in the medical record. Structured handoffs reduce information loss during transitions and support continuity of care. 

• Communicate anticoagulation status clearly during perioperative transitions

Ensure that the surgical, anesthesia and nursing teams are aware of the patient's anticoagulation status, timing of last dose and plan for perioperative management. Variable practice patterns for perioperative anticoagulation may expose patients to excess stroke risk  or serious bleeding risk. Perioperative anticoagulation order sets and checklists enable standardized management.

• Establish closed-loop communication for anticoagulation orders

Verify that anticoagulation orders are received, understood and administered as intended. Confirm that critical laboratory results (supratherapeutic INR, significant hemoglobin drop) are communicated to the ordering provider and acknowledged. Closed-loop communication minimizes errors during both routine care and emergencies.

• Coordinate with outpatient providers before discharge

Communicate discharge anticoagulation plans to the patient's primary care provider, cardiologist or anticoagulation clinic before or at discharge. Include indication, agent, dose, duration of therapy, follow-up laboratory requirements and any dose adjustments made during hospitalization. Clear communication prevents gaps in monitoring and reduces readmission risk.


ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Failure to educate patients about anticoagulant therapy, warning signs of bleeding and thrombosis and the importance of adherence to medication and monitoring routines increases the risk of adverse events after discharge.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and ensure understanding of anticoagulation therapy.

• Provide comprehensive anticoagulation education before discharge

Educate patients on their specific anticoagulant, including indication, dose, timing of administration, expected duration of therapy and what to do if they miss a dose. For DOACs, emphasize the importance of strict medication adherence given the short half-life. For warfarin, discuss dietary vitamin K consistency and the need for INR monitoring. Document education provided and stated patient understanding.

• Discuss drug and food interactions

Educate patients to avoid NSAIDs and to notify providers before starting new medications, including over-the-counter products and supplements. For warfarin patients, discuss consistent vitamin K intake. For rivaroxaban, emphasize administration with food. Awareness of interactions prevents inadvertent supratherapeutic or subtherapeutic anticoagulation.

• Teach patients to recognize signs and symptoms of bleeding and thrombosis

Instruct patients on warning signs requiring immediate medical attention: severe headache, vision changes, blood in urine or stool, black tarry stools, excessive bruising, prolonged bleeding from cuts, new leg swelling, chest pain or shortness of breath. Ensure that they understand when to seek emergency care (in the event of head trauma or severe bleeding, for example). Provide written information to reinforce verbal instructions.

• Confirm follow-up arrangements and provide contact information

Ensure that patients have scheduled follow-up appointments (anticoagulation clinic, primary care, INR monitoring) and understand how to contact their care team with questions or concerns. Provide written discharge instructions in the patient's primary language and at an appropriate literacy level. Confirmed follow-up reduces gaps in care and aids in early identification of complications. 




ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of anticoagulation indication, dosing rationale, monitoring results, patient education and clinical decision-making compromises continuity of care.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.
Document the rationale for VTE prophylaxis decisions
Record the VTE risk assessment (using validated tools such as the Padua Prediction Score or IMPROVE VTE score), the prophylaxis strategy selected (pharmacologic, mechanical or none) and the clinical rationale, particularly when prophylaxis is withheld or when choosing a non-standard approach. For patients at high bleeding risk, document the contraindication, the use of mechanical prophylaxis and the plan for daily reassessment. Thorough documentation of prophylaxis decision-making demonstrates individualized risk-benefit analysis and supports defensibility.
• Document the indication for and intended duration of anticoagulation therapy

Record the specific indication for anticoagulation (such as acute PE, atrial fibrillation with CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of 4, mechanical mitral valve) and the planned duration of therapy. For VTE, document whether treatment is for a provoked versus unprovoked event and anticipated duration (3 months, 6 months, indefinite). Clear documentation of indication supports decisions for continuation or discontinuation of therapy by subsequent providers.

• Document dosing rationale, particularly for non-standard doses

When prescribing reduced DOAC doses, document the clinical rationale (renal function, age, weight, drug interactions). A significant number of patients receive DOAC doses that deviate from FDA-approved guidelines.

Sample documentation language: "Apixaban 2.5 mg BID selected per labeling criteria: age 82, weight 58 kg, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (2 of 3 dose reduction criteria met). CrCl 38 mL/min by Cockcroft-Gault."

• Document laboratory monitoring and clinical response

Record INR values with corresponding warfarin doses and dose adjustments, if any. For patients on DOACs, document renal function monitoring and dose modifications. Document clinical assessments for bleeding (hemoglobin trends, physical exam findings) and thrombosis.

• Document clinical reasoning for management decisions

When holding anticoagulation for procedures, document the bleeding risk assessment, timing of last dose, plan for resumption and any bridging decisions. When continuing anticoagulation despite bleeding risk factors, document the risk-benefit analysis. This documentation demonstrates thoughtful clinical judgment and supports defensibility in the event of adverse outcomes.

• Document patient education and understanding

Record patient education on their anticoagulant, including warning signs of bleeding and thrombosis, drug interactions, adherence importance and follow-up requirements. Document the patient's stated understanding or any barriers to comprehension. Education documentation is required by the Joint Commission and demonstrates patient engagement. 

Sample documentation language: "Discussed apixaban therapy with patient including importance of twice-daily dosing, signs of bleeding (blood in stool, excessive bruising, severe headache), avoiding NSAIDs, and need for follow-up with PCP in 1 week. Patient verbalized understanding and demonstrated teach-back of warning signs."



ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR

Failure to Recognize Responsibility

Contributing action or omission: Failing to establish clear ownership for anticoagulation management decisions—particularly during transitions between services, shifts or care settings—increases the risk of missed or inappropriate doses and delayed response to complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to establish accountability and ensure consistent anticoagulation management.

• Maintain current knowledge of anticoagulation management principles
Maintain and periodically refresh knowledge of anticoagulation dosing, monitoring, drug interactions and reversal strategies. The Joint Commission requires provider education and competency assessment for anticoagulation therapy. Staying current with evolving guidelines and institutional protocols helps reduce errors and supports safe prescribing practices.
• Designate clear responsibility for anticoagulation management

Identify a specific provider or service responsible for anticoagulation decisions for each patient. This may be the primary team, a consulting service or an anticoagulation management service. Document the responsible provider in the medical record. Clear ownership prevents assumptions that "someone else" is managing anticoagulation. 
• Ensure 24/7 availability of anticoagulation expertise where feasible

Establish protocols for after-hours anticoagulation questions, including access to clinical pharmacists when available, on-call hematology or institutional guidelines. Bleeding emergencies and urgent procedural decisions occur at all hours. 
• Advocate for institutional anticoagulation stewardship programs

Develop coordinated anticoagulation management systems with defined roles for physicians, pharmacists and nurses. Anticoagulation clinics and stewardship programs improve patient outcomes and reduce costs compared with usual medical care. Institutional programs standardize care and provide backup systems to catch errors.
OVERVIEW:
Anticoagulant Management
SPECIALTY: Hospital Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Venous Thromboembolism, Atrial Fibrillation, Acute Medical Illness

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Major Bleeding, Intracranial Hemorrhage, Thromboembolic Events, Death

Anticoagulant management is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in hospital medicine.
Mitigating Your Risk
With systematic risk assessment, standardized monitoring protocols and coordinated care transitions, hospital medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with anticoagulant management and reduce malpractice
