TITLE: Falls
RISK_BRIEF:
Hospital Falls


SPECIALTY: Hospital Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Hospitalized Adults

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Patient Falls, Fall-Related Injuries

Hospital falls are a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in hospital medicine. 

Mitigating Your Risk

With systematic risk assessment, evidence-based prevention and robust documentation practices, hospital medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with hospital falls and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for hospital falls and provides strategies to help prevent them. 

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving hospital falls typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 66% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 34%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Hospital Fall Risk [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failure to adequately assess fall risk or obtain diagnostic testing to identify modifiable risk factors increases the likelihood of preventable falls.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure systematic identification of fall risk factors and appropriate diagnostic evaluation.

• Perform standardized fall risk screening on all patients at admission in accordance with institutional policy

Initiate a fall risk evaluation on admission following the institutional fall risk screening protocol. Many hospitals use validated tools, such as the Morse Fall Scale or the Johns Hopkins Fall Risk Assessment Tool, to meet Joint Commission requirements. The 2026 Hospital Program addresses fall prevention under National Performance Goal NPG.11.02.01, which requires hospitals to implement fall risk reduction interventions based on the patient population, setting and individual patient's assessed risks. Current evidence indicates that scored screening tools alone have not been shown to reduce hospital fall rates; they should be viewed as one component of a broader fall prevention strategy rather than a substitute for individualized, multifactorial risk assessment. 

• Conduct a comprehensive multifactorial assessment for all hospitalized adults aged 65 and older and for younger patients identified as high-risk

Perform a multifactorial falls risk assessment for all hospitalized adults aged 65 and older, regardless of screening score, as recommended by the World Falls Guidelines and the American Geriatrics Society. For patients under 65, use initial screening results to identify those who warrant further assessment. The assessment should include a gait and balance evaluation (Timed Up and Go test or other validated gait and balance measure), medication review for fall-risk-increasing drugs (FRIDs), cognitive evaluation, vision screening where appropriate, orthostatic vital signs and continence assessment. This process reveals modifiable risk factors and informs individual care plans. 

• Reassess fall risk after clinical status changes

Evaluate patients following changes in their condition, such as new medications, procedures, acute illness, delirium or mobility shifts. Address potential fall risks promptly. The Johns Hopkins Fall Risk Assessment Tool more accurately tracks changes when used during hospitalization. Regular checks spot new risks for quick intervention.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failing to implement proper fall precautions or provide safe mobility assistance increases the risk of falls and injuries.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate intervention and safe patient mobility.

• Implement fall prevention interventions based on assessed risk

Customize interventions based on each patient’s risks. Fit assistive devices for gait or balance issues. For cognitive problems, enhance observation. Use scheduled toileting rounds for toileting needs. These tailored steps target each patient’s unique risks and meet Joint Commission requirements. 

• Educate patient and family on fall prevention

Teach patients and families about individual fall risks, the importance of calling for assistance before moving, how to use call lights and assistive devices and how to avoid environmental hazards. Document education and patient understanding. 

• Ensure safe mobility assistance during ambulation and transfers

Write activity orders specifying the level of help needed. Patients whose activity level is "up with assistance" have a significantly higher fall risk compared to those with bathroom privileges. Consider using gait belts during ambulation and ensuring that sufficient staff is available to provide timely help, as higher patient-to-nurse ratios are linked to increased fall risk. 

• Review and consider adjusting medications that increase fall risk

Use validated tools like Beers Criteria, STOPPFall or other validated tools to regularly review medications and identify FRIDs. Medications strongly associated with in-hospital falls include:
Antidepressants (OR 2.25)
Benzodiazepines (OR 1.97)
Hypnotics-sedatives (OR 1.90)
Antipsychotics (OR 1.61)
When possible, consider discontinuing or reducing doses. Document the rationale if these medications must be continued. 
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Insufficient monitoring of patients after falls or interventions that raise fall risk increases the risk of repeat falls and delayed recognition of injury.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate post-fall evaluation and ongoing surveillance.

• Conduct immediate post-fall assessment for injuries

After a fall, check for injuries by looking at vital signs, neurological function, musculoskeletal health and skin condition. Determine how long the patient was down to assess risk forrhabdomyolysis, dehydration or pressure injuries. Physical exams alone have high false-negative rates, so maintain a low threshold for imaging in older adults. 
• Consider implementing post-fall huddles to identify contributing factors

Meet with a multidisciplinary team briefly after falls when possible to find the cause, such as new illnesses, delirium, medications, environmental contributors and gaps in the prevention plan. Post-fall huddles have been linked to reductions in repeat falls. Use clear guidelines to ensure that evaluations are consistent. 

• Reassess and adjust fall prevention plans after each fall

Update the patient’s fall risk score after a fall and modify interventions. Document the reassessment and plan changes. These steps align with the World Falls Guidelines, which recommend revising strategies after any fall to prevent recurrence. 

• Implement enhanced monitoring for patients at highest risk

Increase vigilance for patients with multiple falls, severe cognitive impairment or high-risk medication profiles. Examples include increased rounding frequency, room placement near the nursing station or one-to-one observation where institutional resources and policy support it. Document the rationale for the selected monitoring level.


ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of fall risk assessments, interventions, patient education and post-fall evaluations compromises care delivery.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document fall risk assessment

Record the fall risk assessment tool used, score, risk factors and risk level after evaluation. Update documentation when the patient’s condition changes. Complete records ensure correct risk identification and intervention.

Sample documentation language: "Morse Fall Scale score 55 (high risk). Risk factors: history of fall within past 3 months (+25), secondary diagnosis present (+15), ambulatory with assistance (+15). Fall prevention interventions initiated per high-risk protocol."

• Document individualized interventions and patient/family education

Describe actions for each risk, such as lowering the bed, keeping the call light close, providing non-skid footwear, establishing a toileting schedule and specifying required mobility assistance. Document patient and family education, topics covered and confirmation of the patient's stated understanding. Documenting education aligns with Joint Commission best practice. 

Sample documentation language: "Fall prevention education provided to patient and spouse including: call for assistance before getting out of bed, use of call light, non-skid footwear, bed alarm in place. Patient verbalized understanding and agreement with plan."

• Document clinical reasoning when continuing medications that can increase fall risk

Note the need for the medication, alternatives considered, risks and benefits and any mitigating interventions. This shows careful clinical judgment and supports defensibility. 
• Document post-fall assessment

After a fall, record whether it was witnessed, where it occurred and what the patient was doing at the time of the fall. Note immediate assessment findings, injuries discovered or ruled out, test results, who was notified and care plan updates. If known, note how long the patient was on the floor. Full documentation helps providers identify injuries and shows that the incident was handled properly. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUES

Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate staff training and absence of protocols for identifying fall risks and interventions undermine consistent care delivery.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve organizational readiness and standardize fall prevention processes.

• Establish evidence-based fall prevention policies and protocols

Develop institutional policies using guidelines from organizations like the Joint Commission (NPG.11.02.01 for hospitals), AHRQ and the World Falls Guidelines. The Nursing Care Center Program's more detailed EPs under NPSG.09.02.01—covering assessment, interventions, staff education, patient education and effectiveness evaluation—provide a useful operational framework that hospitals may adopt as best practice even though the Hospital Program's single EP is written more broadly. Include standardized risk assessments, tailored intervention bundles based on patient risk levels, post-fall assessment procedures and reporting requirements. Multicomponent programs can reduce relative fall risk by approximately 16%-30%, depending on the components and implementation. 

• Implement EHR-based fall risk tools and alerts

Create electronic fall risk assessment tools that automatically calculate risk scores and generate tailored intervention recommendations. Add EHR alerts for high-risk medication combinations as resources allow. Automated tools support consistent assessment and reduce reliance on memory. 

• Provide comprehensive staff education and training

Educate all clinical staff, not just nurses, on fall risks, assessment tools, prevention strategies and post-fall procedures. Include fall prevention in new employee orientation. Regular training reinforces best practices and supports culture change.

• Establish interdisciplinary falls committees with physician participation

Create multidisciplinary committees to oversee fall prevention programs, review fall data, identify trends and drive improvements. While a majority of hospitals have fall committees, physician membership is rare. Include hospitalists, nurses, pharmacists, physical therapists and quality improvement staff. Committee oversight ensures accountability and continuous improvement.
 

• Optimize the physical environment for fall prevention

Make environmental changes to reduce fall risks. This includes improving lighting, decluttering pathways, adding handrails, using non-slip flooring, adjusting bed heights, ensuring that call lights are accessible and maintaining clear pathways to bathrooms. Thoughtful hospital design shifts the focus from fall prevention to promotion of safe mobility. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failing to address patient-specific barriers to fall prevention, including cognitive impairment, impulsivity and reluctance to call for assistance, increases the likelihood of falls.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient engagement and address barriers to safe mobility.

• Assess patient's understanding and ability to participate in fall prevention

Evaluate the patient’s cognitive status, health literacy and ability to use the call light and follow safety instructions. For patients with cognitive impairment, adapt your approach with simplified instructions, visual cues, family involvement and enhanced observation to increase their awareness and engagement in safe mobility. Confusion alone is strongly linked to a higher fall risk (aOR 3.6). 

• Engage family members and caregivers in fall prevention

Educate family members on fall risk factors and prevention strategies. Encourage them to be present during high-risk times to help reinforce safety instructions and notify staff if the patient attempts to move independently. Be sure to document the education provided and their involvement. 

• Address patient reluctance to call for assistance

Explore reasons why patients hesitate to ask for assistance, such as not wanting to bother staff, embarrassment about toileting needs or underestimating their fall risk. Normalize calling for assistance and emphasize that preventing falls is a priority. Use proactive rounding to anticipate needs before patients attempt to move on their own. 

• Document patient non-adherence and continued education efforts

Record repeated patient attempts to move without assistance despite education. Include their behavior, repeated education, alternative interventions such as increased rounding, room changes or family involvement and any ongoing risks. Check if cognitive impairment or delirium may be a factor. Complete records demonstrate continued efforts to prevent harm.
OVERVIEW:
Hospital Falls


SPECIALTY: Hospital Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Hospitalized Adults

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Patient Falls, Fall-Related Injuries

Hospital falls are a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in hospital medicine. 

Mitigating Your Risk

With systematic risk assessment, evidence-based prevention and robust documentation practices, hospital medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with hospital falls and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims
PRESENTING_CONDITIONS: Hospitalized Adults
ADVERSE_OUTCOMES: Patient Falls, Fall-Related Injuries

TITLE: GI hemorrhage
RISK_BRIEF:
Gastrointestinal Hemorrhage 

SPECIALTY: Hospital Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Hematemesis, Melena, Hematochezia, Acute Blood Loss Anemia

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Rebleeding, Hemorrhagic Shock, Multiorgan Failure, Death

Gastrointestinal hemorrhage is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in hospital medicine.

Mitigating Your Risk 
 
With prompt risk stratification, appropriate resuscitation and medication management, and timely escalation of care, hospital medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with gastrointestinal hemorrhage and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for gastrointestinal hemorrhage and provides strategies to help prevent them.  
 
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving gastrointestinal hemorrhage typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 81% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 19%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate. 
Actions and Omissions Driving Gastrointestinal Hemorrhage Risk [insert chart] 

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain appropriate laboratory testing, risk stratification or imaging to identify the source and severity of gastrointestinal bleeding increases the risk of delayed intervention.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely identification of bleeding source, accurate risk assessment and appropriate triage decisions.

• Obtain comprehensive initial laboratory evaluation at presentation

When a patient presents with suspected GI hemorrhage, obtain complete blood count, basic metabolic panel, coagulation studies (PT/INR, PTT), liver function tests and type and crossmatch. Recognize that initial hemoglobin may not reflect true blood loss until volume equilibration occurs. This baseline evaluation establishes severity, identifies coagulopathy requiring correction and supports appropriate transfusion decisions.

• Evaluate for upper GI source in patients with severe hematochezia

In patients presenting with severe hematochezia and hemodynamic instability, consider a brisk upper GI source. Assess for history of peptic ulcer disease, liver disease, epigastric discomfort and stigmata of cirrhosis. Consider upper endoscopy before colonoscopy when clinical suspicion for UGIB is high. Failure to exclude an upper source can result in delayed diagnosis and preventable deterioration.

• Ensure that Helicobacter pylori testing is performed in patients with peptic ulcer bleeding

Confirm that H. pylori testing (biopsy-based rapid urease test, histology or stool antigen) is performed and that results are addressed prior to discharge. False-negatives can occur due to acute bleeding in the stomach and PPI exposure. If initial testing is negative, arrange repeat testing (stool antigen) 2 weeks after stopping PPIs and 4 weeks after stopping antibiotics. H. pylori eradication reduces the chance of rebleeding. 
• Apply validated risk stratification scores at presentation

Use the Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS) for patients with suspected UGIB to determine need for hospital-based intervention. A GBS ≤1 identifies patients at very low risk who may be safely managed as outpatients with sensitivity of 98.6%. For LGIB, apply the Oakland Score; a score ≤8 predicts 95% probability of safe discharge. Risk stratification supports defensible triage decisions and appropriate resource allocation.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Medication Error

Contributing action or omission: Errors in medication selection, dosing or timing—including inappropriate transfusion thresholds, incorrect PPI dosing and mismanagement of antithrombotic agents—increase the risk of rebleeding, thrombotic events and mortality.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support appropriate pharmacologic management across pre-endoscopic, endoscopic and post-endoscopic phases of care.

• Administer high-dose PPI therapy appropriately based on endoscopic findings

For patients with high-risk ulcer stigmata (active bleeding, visible vessel, adherent clot) who receive endoscopic therapy, administer high-dose PPI therapy to reduce the risk of rebleeding, surgery and mortality. Patients with low-risk stigmata (flat pigmented spot, clean ulcer base) do not require intensive PPI therapy and can receive standard oral dosing. 

• Manage antithrombotic agents according to guideline recommendations

Follow the ACG-CAG guideline, which conditionally suggests continuing aspirin during acute GI bleeding for patients on aspirin for secondary cardiovascular prevention (very low certainty of evidence). Temporary discontinuation may be considered in patients with severe and/or ongoing bleeding, particularly in LGIB. If interrupted, resume aspirin on the day hemostasis is endoscopically confirmed. Avoid platelet transfusions for patients on antiplatelet agents without thrombocytopenia. Use prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) rather than fresh frozen plasma for warfarin reversal. Avoid routine use of reversal agents for DOACs unless life-threatening hemorrhage is present. Note that the availability of specific DOAC reversal agents may change; institutional policies should reflect currently available agents.

• Administer vasoactive drugs and antibiotics for suspected variceal bleeding

In patients with cirrhosis and suspected variceal bleeding, initiate vasoactive medications (octreotide, terlipressin) and prophylactic antibiotics at presentation, before endoscopy. Continue antibiotics and vasoactive drugs after endoscopy for variceal bleeding. Prophylactic antibiotics reduce mortality and rebleeding in cirrhotic patients with GI bleeding. 

• Avoid tranexamic acid in acute GI bleeding

Follow the current evidence that demonstrates no benefit and potential harm in administering tranexamic acid for acute GI bleeding. This is a change from previous practice and should be reflected in institutional protocols.

CLINICAL: TREATMENT 
Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Delays in endoscopy, failure to perform appropriate endoscopic hemostasis or inadequate escalation to interventional radiology or surgery increase the risk of rebleeding and mortality.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely endoscopic intervention, appropriate hemostatic therapy and coordinated escalation when needed.

• Follow restrictive transfusion thresholds for hemodynamically stable patients unless contraindicated

Adopt a restrictive transfusion strategy for hemodynamically stable patients with GI bleeding. The ACG recommends a threshold of hemoglobin 7 g/dL, with a threshold of 8 g/dL considered reasonable for patients with pre-existing cardiovascular disease. Evidence in patients with acute coronary syndrome is very limited, and transfusion decisions in this population should be individualized. Patients with hemorrhagic shock require emergent transfusion regardless of hemoglobin level. Establish serial hemoglobin monitoring at appropriate intervals in actively bleeding patients to ensure timely identification of transfusion needs, recognizing that initial hemoglobin may not reflect true blood loss until volume equilibration occurs.

• Perform endoscopy within 24 hours of presentation after hemodynamic resuscitation

For patients admitted with overt UGIB, aim to perform upper endoscopy within 24 hours of presentation. Prioritize hemodynamic resuscitation and management of active comorbidities before endoscopy. Very early endoscopy (6 hours after presentation) in high-risk patients does not improve mortality and may cause harm if performed before adequate resuscitation. For hemodynamically stable patients, endoscopy during routine hours may allow early discharge if low-risk findings are identified. 

• Perform colonoscopy for LGIB after adequate bowel preparation

Schedule colonoscopy at the next available nonurgent opportunity after adequate bowel preparation, as current evidence does not support routine urgent colonoscopy (within 24 hours) for LGIB. Consider urgent colonoscopy in select high-risk situations, such as postpolypectomy bleeding, where stigmata amenable to endoscopic therapy are likely. Obtain CT angiography as the first diagnostic study for hemodynamically unstable patients with severe hematochezia to localize bleeding and guide intervention. 
• Apply appropriate endoscopic hemostasis for high-risk stigmata

For actively bleeding ulcers (Forrest Ia, Ib), use combination therapy with epinephrine injection plus a second hemostasis modality (thermal coagulation, clips or sclerosant). For non-bleeding visible vessels (Forrest IIa), monotherapy with thermal, mechanical or sclerosant injection is acceptable. Document the Forrest classification and endoscopic findings to support clinical decision-making and defensibility.

• Establish clear escalation pathways for refractory bleeding

For recurrent bleeding after initial endoscopic therapy, attempt repeat endoscopic hemostasis. If bleeding persists after second endoscopic attempt, proceed to transcatheter arterial embolization (TAE) when available. Surgery is indicated when TAE is not locally available or after failed TAE. Institutions should have established protocols defining escalation triggers and responsible services.


ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUES

Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure

Contributing action or omission: Lack of protocols, inadequate monitoring systems and absence of multidisciplinary coordination undermine consistent care delivery and timely escalation.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve readiness, standardize response and support coordinated escalation.

• Implement institutional GI bleeding protocols with clear escalation triggers

Develop and disseminate evidence-based protocols for UGIB and LGIB management that include risk stratification criteria, transfusion thresholds, medication algorithms and escalation triggers. Implementation of institutional GI bleeding protocols has been shown to decrease transfusion requirements, reduce length of stay and decrease hospital readmissions. Protocols should specify when to activate gastroenterology, interventional radiology and surgical services.

• Develop EHR order sets for GI hemorrhage management

Create pre-built order sets that include appropriate laboratory monitoring, medication dosing (PPI infusion protocols, vasoactive agents), transfusion parameters and nursing monitoring frequency. Implement automated alerts for hemodynamic instability (hypotension, tachycardia) or significant hemoglobin decline. Order sets reduce variability and show adherence to evidence-based care.

• Ensure timely access to endoscopy for patients with acute GI bleeding 

Define on-call coverage, response time expectations and backup procedures consistent with institutional capacity. The ACG recommends endoscopy within 24 hours for acute upper GI bleeding; for suspected acute variceal hemorrhage, the AASLD recommends endoscopy within 12 hours, though prospective data supporting this specific timeframe over endoscopy within 24 hours is lacking. Resuscitation and management of active comorbidities should be undertaken before endoscopy. Facilities without 24-hour endoscopy capability should establish transfer agreements with endoscopy-capable centers, initiate pre-endoscopic management (resuscitation, IV PPI therapy, and vasoactive drugs and antibiotics for suspected variceal bleeding) before transfer, and use validated risk stratification tools (e.g., Glasgow-Blatchford Score ≤1) to identify very low-risk patients who may not require urgent endoscopy or transfer.

• Conduct multidisciplinary simulations and quality review

Perform periodic simulations of GI hemorrhage scenarios involving hospitalists, gastroenterologists, interventional radiologists, surgeons and nursing staff. Use structured debriefing after simulations and after significant bleeding events. Track and review outcomes including time to endoscopy, rebleeding rates and mortality to identify opportunities for improvement. 

PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify and address barriers to medication adherence, follow-up care and lifestyle modification increases the likelihood of recurrent bleeding.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and reduce recurrent bleeding risk.

• Provide clear discharge instructions regarding warning signs and medication adherence

Before discharge, provide written and verbal instructions about warning signs requiring immediate evaluation (recurrent hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, lightheadedness, syncope). Ensure that patients understand PPI dosing, duration and importance of completing H. pylori eradication therapy if prescribed. Document patient education and stated understanding.

• Provide clear guidance for NSAID and antiplatelet use

Instruct patients with NSAID-related bleeding to discontinue NSAIDs. However, if NSAIDs must be resumed, prescribe a COX-2 selective agent plus PPI. For patients on aspirin for secondary cardiovascular prevention, continue aspirin with PPI gastroprotection. Discuss the risks and benefits of recommended treatment and document the education and the patient's stated understanding.

• Coordinate H. pylori biopsy follow-up and eradication confirmation

Ensure that H. pylori biopsy results from the index endoscopy are reviewed and acted upon, as histopathology results are frequently pending at discharge. Establish a process for who will review the results, initiate eradication therapy (if needed) and communicate findings to the patient and outpatient providers. Schedule follow-up testing to confirm H. pylori eradication 2 weeks after stopping PPIs and 4 weeks after stopping antibiotics. Document the follow-up plan and communicate with outpatient providers.

• Document patient non-adherence and continued outreach

When patients miss follow-up appointments or decline recommended testing, document the conversation, recommendations made and patient's stated reasons for declining. Continue outreach by phone, patient portal or mail. If a patient declines evaluation despite concerning symptoms, discuss risks and document the conversation.

TITLE: Pressure ulcer
RISK_BRIEF:
Hospital-Acquired Pressure Ulcer


SPECIALTY: Hospital Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Hospitalized patients with limited mobility, critical illness, prolonged surgical procedures

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Skin breakdown, wound infection, sepsis, prolonged hospitalization, patient suffering

Hospital-acquired pressure ulcer is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in hospital medicine.  

Mitigating Your Risk

With risk assessment, evidence-based prevention, and ongoing monitoring, hospital medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with hospital-acquired pressure ulcers and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for hospital-acquired pressure ulcers and provides strategies to help prevent them. 
Pressure injury prevention is a multidisciplinary effort. While the hospitalist’s role is to ensure appropriate orders, respond to findings, guide escalation and maintain oversight of the prevention plan, this guidance covers the full prevention process, including nursing-performed interventions, since physician liability can arise from failures at any point in the process.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving hospital-acquired pressure ulcers typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 70% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 30%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Hospital-Acquired Pressure Ulcer [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify patients at risk for pressure injury development through assessment at admission and throughout hospitalization increases the likelihood of preventable skin breakdown.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely identification of at-risk patients and early recognition of skin changes before progression to more severe injury.

• Ensure that a comprehensive skin assessment is performed at admission and with each change in condition

Verify that nursing staff performs a head-to-toe skin assessment at hospital admission, focusing on bony areas like the sacrum, heels, occiput and ischial tuberosities, along with areas in contact with medical devices. The assessment should document skin condition, including the presence of blanchable or nonblanchable erythema, firmness, moisture, pain and temperature changes, and should be repeated with changes in patient’s condition, unit transfer or mobility status. Abnormal findings should be escalated to the physician promptly. The physician should examine areas of concern identified by nursing, evaluate findings in the context of the patient's overall clinical picture and confirm or adjust the prevention plan accordingly. This process helps identify existing injuries, establishes a baseline and enables early intervention. 

• Assess skin beneath and around medical devices

Carefully check the skin in contact with respiratory devices (endotracheal tubes, non-invasive ventilation masks, nasal cannulas), tubes and drains, splints, cervical collars, IV catheters and monitoring equipment. Device-related pressure injuries occur in about 10%-12% of hospitalized patients. Record these assessments separately from routine skin checks. 
• Use a validated risk assessment tool alongside clinical judgment

Employ the Braden Scale or Norton Scale at admission and regularly, as per institutional policy, to complement clinical evaluation. Although these tools have limited sensitivity and specificity, they offer a structured way to assess sensory perception, moisture, activity, mobility, nutrition and friction/shear. They are especially helpful for less experienced clinicians in pressure injury prevention and ensure consistent evaluations across providers. 

• Identify and document risk factors that increase HAPI likelihood

Beyond standardized risk scores, document the patient’s high-risk conditions for developing a hospital-acquired pressure injury (HAPI), such as preexisting pressure injuries or scars, diabetes mellitus, peripheral vascular disease, incontinence, malnutrition, use of vasopressors, prolonged surgical procedures, forced positioning and medical devices. Patients with preexisting pressure injuries face a significantly higher risk, making it essential to record this history. 
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failing to implement appropriate prevention strategies—including repositioning patients, using effective support surfaces, providing proper skin care and  optimizing nutrition—increases the risk that pressure injuries will develop and progress.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce mechanical loading on vulnerable tissues and optimize the patient's ability to tolerate pressure.

• Implement individualized repositioning schedules based on patient risk and support surface

Create repositioning plans based on each patient's activity level, mobility and type of support surface. While the optimal repositioning frequency is uncertain, EPUAP/NPIAP/PPPIA guidelines suggest frequent position changes guided by clinical judgment. Use 30-degree lateral tilt positions instead of 90-degree lateral positions when possible. Keep the head of the bed as flat as clinically appropriate to reduce shear forces. Use manual handling techniques or equipment to minimize friction during repositioning. 

• Provide appropriate support surfaces for patients at elevated risk

Use advanced static mattresses or overlays instead of standard hospital mattresses for patients at increased risk. Evidence-based guidelines strongly recommend this based on moderate-quality evidence. More recent evidence suggests that alternating pressure air surfaces may reduce pressure ulcer incidence compared with foam and may be more cost-effective, though direct comparisons with advanced static surfaces remain uncertain. Support surface selection should be guided by patient risk level, clinical context and institutional resources. 

• Prevent medical device-related pressure injuries through proactive management

Adjust or rotate medical devices whenever possible and consider protective dressings under devices that cannot be repositioned. Ensure that devices are properly sized and fitted and remove them promptly when clinically indicated. Keep in mind that extended device use increases the risk of pressure injuries. 
• Consider prophylactic dressings for high-risk patients as an adjunct to other prevention strategies
In accordance with EPUAP/NPIAP/PPPIA guidelines, consider prophylactic dressings, particularly multilayered silicone foam dressings, on bony prominences (sacrum, heels) as an adjunct to other measures. Prophylactic dressings should be considered particularly for ICU patients and those with multiple risk factors. Dressings should not replace repositioning, support surface optimization or skin assessment.
• Implement a comprehensive skin care regimen

Keep the patient’s skin clean and dry, especially after incontinence events. Use pH-balanced cleansers (pH 5.5) rather than alkaline hospital soaps and apply moisture barriers to protect the skin from urine and stool. Avoid excessive friction during cleaning. These steps maintain skin integrity and reduce maceration, which increases pressure injury risk. 

• Address nutritional deficits that impair tissue tolerance

Use validated tools to screen for malnutrition and ensure that patients at risk receive adequate calories and protein. While low albumin levels are associated with higher pressure injury risk, albumin primarily reflects inflammation and illness severity rather than nutritional status; it should be interpreted as a risk marker, not a therapeutic target. Although direct evidence that nutritional interventions prevent pressure injuries remains limited, malnutrition impairs wound healing and tissue resilience.


CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate surveillance of skin status and failure to act on early signs of pressure injury allows progression from reversible changes to severe tissue damage.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure early detection of skin changes and prompt prevention and treatment.

• Reassess pressure injury risk at defined intervals and with clinical changes

Conduct scheduled reassessments based on institutional policy and whenever the patient’s condition changes, such as during an ICU transfer, the initiation of vasopressors, new immobility or changes in continence. The Joint Commission requires reassessment "at intervals defined by the organization or as ordered by a physician." (Note: The Joint Commission's NPSG Goal 14 on pressure injury prevention is currently part of the Nursing Care Center accreditation program. These elements of performance are applied here as evidence-based best practice benchmarks for acute care settings.) 

• Initiate immediate treatment when early pressure injury is identified

When a Stage 1 pressure injury (nonblanchable erythema) is identified, take prompt action to prevent progression. Increase repositioning frequency, optimize the support surface, ensure complete off-loading of affected area and address modifiable risk factors. With timely intervention at Stage 1, most pressure injuries can be prevented from worsening.
• Be alert for unstageable and deep tissue pressure injuries
Recognize that these injuries may be present on admission but not immediately apparent, and they can evolve rapidly to reveal extensive tissue damage. Document these findings carefully, as distinguishing hospital-acquired from pre-existing injuries is critical for both clinical management and medicolegal purposes.

• Monitor for signs of infection and wound progression

Check all pressure injuries for signs of local infection, such as increased erythema, warmth, purulent drainage or odor. Watch for wound progression and spreading infection like cellulitis or systemic symptoms. At each assessment, document wound measurements, base characteristics, exudate quality and staging. Photos can help objectively track wound status over time.[2][9]

• Communicate skin status changes during handoffs and transitions of care

Share details about the patient’s pressure injury risk status, current skin findings and the prevention plan at handoffs. Ensure that preventive measures continue during transfers between units, procedural areas and shift changes. Communication failures during transitions can lead to missed repositioning or monitoring.
• Ensure appropriate wound care orders and follow-up at discharge
When discharging a patient with an active pressure injury or elevated ongoing risk, provide detailed wound care orders that include dressing type and change frequency, offloading or repositioning instructions, signs of complications requiring medical attention and any needed referrals, such as wound care, nutrition or home health. Communicate the wound status, staging and current treatment plan to the outpatient provider. Schedule or arrange follow-up appropriate to the severity of the injury. Incomplete discharge planning for patients with pressure injuries can lead to wound progression, readmission and liability exposure.


ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Insufficient documentation of risk assessment, skin status, preventive interventions and clinical decision-making compromises continuity of care.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to create a comprehensive medical record that demonstrates adherence to standard of care and supports continuity across providers.

• Document admission skin assessment with specific findings

Record the patient’s baseline skin status, noting all areas examined, the presence or absence of existing injuries and findings like skin integrity, erythema characteristics, moisture, firmness or temperature changes. If pressure injuries are present on admission, include details such as location, stage, measurements, wound base characteristics, exudate and photos. This helps distinguish between hospital-acquired and pre-existing injuries. 

Sample documentation language: "Complete skin assessment performed on admission. Skin intact over sacrum, bilateral heels, bilateral trochanters, occiput. No nonblanchable erythema identified. Braden Score 14 (moderate risk). Prevention plan initiated including high-specification foam mattress and repositioning schedule [specify frequency based on patient's mobility, activity level and support surface]."

• Document risk assessment scores and clinical reasoning

Record Braden Scale or other risk assessment scores, including subscale values. Include any clinical factors that raise risk beyond the standardized score, such as vasopressor use, planned long surgeries or preexisting injuries. This demonstrates thorough evaluation and supports tailored prevention plans. 
• Document preventive interventions and patient response

Detail preventive interventions, such as support surfaces, repositioning schedules, skin care regimens, nutritional support and device-related precautions. Including the patient’s adherence to repositioning plans, responses to skin changes and any escalation of care is encouraged. This provides evidence of proactive prevention efforts. 

• Document patient and family education

Record education on pressure injury prevention, including what was discussed and the patient’s and/or family’s stated understanding. The Joint Commission requires this education for patients, residents and families. 

Sample documentation language: "Discussed pressure injury prevention with patient and family including importance of repositioning, reporting skin discomfort, and maintaining nutrition. Patient verbalized understanding and agreed to notify staff of any skin concerns."

• Document discharge wound status, care plan and follow-up arrangements
For patients discharged with an active pressure injury or ongoing elevated risk, document the wound status at discharge, including location, stage, measurements, wound base characteristics and any changes from admission. Record the discharge wound care plan, including dressing orders, offloading or repositioning instructions, nutritional recommendations and patient/family education provided. Document referrals made (wound care, home health, nutrition) and scheduled follow-up appointments. This documentation establishes continuity between inpatient and outpatient care and demonstrates that appropriate transition planning occurred.
Sample documentation language: "Patient discharged with Stage 2 pressure injury to sacrum, 3.2 x 2.1 cm, clean granulating base, minimal serous drainage. Wound care orders provided: [specify dressing type and change frequency]. Patient and family educated on offloading, repositioning, nutrition and signs of infection requiring medical attention. Referral placed to wound care clinic with follow-up in [specify timeframe]. Home health nursing ordered for wound assessment and dressing changes [specify frequency]. Primary care provider notified of wound status and discharge plan.”
ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUE
Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure

Contributing action or omission: Lack of protocols, inadequate staff education and absence of systematic quality monitoring undermine consistent pressure injury prevention across the organization.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to create reliable systems that support consistent prevention practices regardless of individual provider differences.

• Develop and implement evidence-based pressure injury prevention protocols

Create written institutional protocols on pressure injury prevention following EPUAP/NPIAP/PPPIA guidelines and Joint Commission requirements. Include standardized timing for risk assessments, tailored prevention steps based on risk levels, escalation triggers and documentation guidelines. Protocols should cover both traditional pressure injuries and medical device-related injuries.
• Standardize discharge processes for patients with active pressure injuries
Develop institutional protocols for discharging patients with active pressure injuries or ongoing elevated risk. Include standard wound care order sets with dressing specifications and change frequency, established referral pathways for wound care, home health and nutrition services, and communication templates for notifying outpatient providers of wound status, staging and the current treatment plan. Standardizing these processes reduces variability in discharge planning and helps ensure that pressure injury management continues seamlessly in the outpatient setting.
• Develop EHR-based tools to support consistent practice where resources allow

Create templates for documenting a patient’s skin assessments, risk scores and prevention plans. Set up automated alerts for patients with high risk scores or identified pressure injuries where possible. Also include order sets for prevention, such as support surface selection, repositioning orders and nutritional consultations. 
• Provide initial and ongoing staff education on pressure injury prevention

Train all clinical staff on identifying risks, skin assessment techniques, staging criteria, prevention methods and documentation requirements. The Joint Commission requires initial and ongoing staff education, including training on recognizing new pressure injuries and taking immediate action. Physicians and licensed practitioners should be included in training on risk identification, prevention, staging and documentation.
 
• Implement audit and feedback systems to monitor compliance and outcomes

Track new and existing pressure injuries and ensure compliance with prevention protocols. Regularly share performance feedback with clinical units. Key components of a successful implementation include simplifying and standardizing interventions, engaging multidisciplinary teams, appointing skin champions, offering ongoing staff education and conducting regular audits and feedback.

TITLE: Sepsis
RISK_BRIEF:
Sepsis

SPECIALTY: Hospital Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Suspected or Confirmed Infection, Acute Illness

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Septic Shock, Multi-Organ Dysfunction, Death

Sepsis is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in hospital medicine. 

Mitigating Your Risk 
 
With timely recognition and treatment, ongoing monitoring and reassessment and clear communication and escalation, hospital medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with sepsis and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for sepsis and provides strategies to help prevent them.  

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns 
Malpractice claims involving sepsis typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 81% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 19%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate. 
Actions and Omissions Driving Sepsis Risk [insert chart]  
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failure to order appropriate laboratory tests to identify sepsis or assess disease severity delays recognition and treatment, increasing the risk of septic shock and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely recognition of sepsis and disease severity.

• Obtain serum lactate at initial presentation of suspected sepsis

Measure serum lactate during the initial evaluation for suspected sepsis. Elevated lactate over 2 mmol/L has a positive likelihood ratio of 5 for sepsis and is linked to higher mortality. Lactate testing is part of the SEP-1 bundle and aids in risk stratification and resuscitation guidance. Venous lactate is acceptable and allows faster sampling. 

• Obtain blood cultures before antimicrobial administration

Whenever possible, collect two sets of blood cultures (aerobic and anaerobic) before starting antimicrobial therapy without delaying treatment. As per guidance from the Surviving Sepsis Campaign, cultures taken after antibiotics are less effective at identifying pathogens and guiding treatment adjustments. 

• Repeat lactate measurement if initial value is elevated

Consider repeating lactate measurement within 2–6 hours for patients with an initial lactate level above 2 mmol/L, particularly those with levels ≥4 mmol/L, to assess response to resuscitation. A declining lactate trend is generally favorable, though no specific clearance target has been validated as a definitive threshold. Serial lactate is one of several tools, including capillary refill time and clinical reassessment, that can guide resuscitation intensity. Document the rationale for the monitoring approach chosen. 

• Perform source-directed diagnostic evaluation

Order imaging studies and site-specific cultures based on the patient’s symptoms to identify the infection source. Common sources include respiratory, genitourinary, intra-abdominal and skin/soft tissue infections. Pneumonia is the most common cause of sepsis, and respiratory source infections are linked to higher mortality. Identifying the source helps guide antibiotic and treatment choices. 
• Consider sepsis in patients with atypical presentations

Suspect sepsis in patients experiencing altered mental status, hypotension, dyspnea or sudden worsening of chronic conditions (like diabetic ketoacidosis or decompensated cirrhosis), even when fever or localizing signs of infection are absent. Check for sepsis at initial presentation and with any unexplained deterioration. Sepsis presentations are heterogeneous and may be masked by medications, such as beta-blockers and antipyretics, or a weakened immune system. Failing to consider sepsis in atypical cases has been proven to delay diagnosis. 
• Continuously re-evaluate for alternative diagnoses

For patients with suspected sepsis, continuously reassess and consider other possible diagnoses. Approximately 20%–40% of patients with suspected sepsis in the ED are later found to have noninfectious sepsis mimics, such as pulmonary embolism, cardiogenic shock or overdose. If another cause is confirmed or strongly suspected, discontinue empiric antimicrobials.

CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Insufficient monitoring after initial resuscitation increases the risk of unrecognized deterioration and delayed escalation of care.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support earlier identification of deterioration, prompt escalation and sustained monitoring following initial treatment.

• Implement standardized vital sign monitoring protocols for sepsis patients

Establish standards for regular vital sign checks based on illness severity. Use a validated early warning score, such as NEWS, MEWS or an institutionally adopted alternative, to identify early deterioration. The SSC 2021 guidelines recommend against using qSOFA alone as a screening tool. The specific scoring system should align with institutional protocols and available resources. 

• Reassess perfusion status after initial fluid resuscitation

After initiating crystalloid fluid resuscitation for sepsis-induced hypoperfusion, reassess volume status and perfusion. The SSC 2026 guidelines maintain a conditional recommendation for at least 30 mL/kg of IV crystalloid within 3 hours, though recent RCTs (CLOVERS, CLASSIC) found no mortality difference between restrictive and liberal strategies, and ESCIM guidelines suggest "up to 30 mL/kg" rather than "at least.” For patients with BMI >30 kg/m², use adjusted or ideal body weight. Administer fluid in serial boluses, reassessing volume status and perfusion between each bolus using available tools, such as vital signs, capillary refill time, urine output, mental status and, where available, dynamic measures such as passive leg raise or pulse pressure variation. Individualize total volume based on comorbidities, hemodynamic response and signs of fluid overload. Document the clinical reasoning for the resuscitation approach and volume administered. 

• Recognize when to stop fluid administration 

Discontinue fluid resuscitation when hypoperfusion resolves, the patient is no longer fluid-responsive or signs of overload develop (rising filling pressures, pulmonary edema, worsening oxygenation). Document the clinical rationale for continuing, withholding or removing fluids at each reassessment.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care

Contributing action or omission: Failing to escalate to intensive care or specialty consultation when indicated delays definitive treatment and increases the risk of patient death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate and timely escalation of care.

• Establish clear escalation triggers and prioritize timely ICU transfer

Define escalation criteria for ICU consultation and transfer, including persistent hypotension despite fluids, need for vasopressors, respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation, or worsening organ dysfunction. Prioritize ICU admission within 6 hours when possible, as observational data suggest that prolonged delays in ICU admission are associated with worse outcomes. When ICU beds are not immediately available, initiate ICU-level interventions (vasopressors, close monitoring) at the bedside, continue resuscitation and document the time of escalation request, clinical status and any system-level barriers to transfer.
• Obtain infectious disease or surgical consultation for source control

Consult appropriate specialists, such as infectious disease or surgical teams, for interventions such as draining an abscess, debriding infected tissue or removing an infected device. Source control should be achieved as soon as medically and logistically practical, as delays are associated with higher mortality.

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION 

Communication Failure Between Providers 

Contributing action or omission: Provider-to-provider communication failures, including inadequate handoffs and unclear escalation pathways, increase the likelihood of delayed recognition and treatment of sepsis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and enable coordinated team responses.

• Establish clear escalation triggers and team roles

Define protocols for activating care escalation based on patient symptoms, such as persistent hypotension, rising lactate levels, new organ dysfunction and failure to respond to initial resuscitation. Clearly assign responsibility for initiating escalation to ensure accountability. During high-acuity sepsis resuscitation, clear communication practices, such as confirming verbal orders and verifying medication administration, can help reduce errors. 
• Implement structured handoff protocols for sepsis patients

During care transitions, use the institution’s standardized handoff tools, such as SBAR, I-PASS or equivalent, to communicate sepsis-related details, including current sepsis status, lactate trends, fluid balance, timing of antibiotics, vasopressor needs and monitoring requirements. Document handoff information in the medical record. 

• Ensure timely provider-to-provider communication during interhospital transfer

Facilitate direct communication between providers when transferring sepsis patients to higher-level facilities. This ensures that the receiving team is informed of the patient’s current resuscitation status, treatments and ongoing care needs. Start essential treatments, including antibiotics, IV fluids and vasopressors, before the transfer. 

• Communicate with ICU team before and during transfer

Notify the ICU team when sepsis patients require a transfer. Share anticipated needs, such as vasopressor support, mechanical ventilation and invasive monitoring, to help the team prepare. Document all communication and confirm acknowledgment from the receiving provider. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Failing to communicate diagnosis, treatment plan and warning signs to patients and families compromises shared decision-making and may delay recognition of deterioration.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support patient and family engagement in sepsis care.

• Discuss the diagnosis and treatment plan with patient and family

Describe the sepsis diagnosis, its severity and the treatment plan in simple, understandable terms and address questions and concerns. For patients with septic shock or a high risk of mortality, initiate goals-of-care discussions early in the clinical course, taking into account the patient's clinical trajectory, decision-making capacity and family availability. These discussions are particularly important when the prognosis is uncertain or when the patient's condition is not improving with initial treatment. 

• Provide education about post-sepsis syndrome before discharge

When feasible, provide sepsis survivors and families with information about potential post-sepsis complications, including physical, cognitive and psychological impairments. Ensure that discharge plans include follow-up with clinicians able to assess for and manage new impairments. Written and verbal education should be offered based on the patient's and family's readiness to process information. 

• Screen for social support needs and make appropriate referrals

Where institutional resources are available, ensure that sepsis survivors are connected with social work or care management teams that can screen for health-related social needs and facilitate appropriate referrals. This is typically a multidisciplinary function rather than an individual hospitalist responsibility. 



ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of sepsis recognition and treatment times, clinical decision-making and patient reassessment compromises continuity of care.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document time of sepsis recognition and bundle initiation

Record the time when sepsis is first recognized (time zero) and when each treatment step is initiated. The SEP-1 measure requires documenting the timing of lactate measurement, blood cultures, antibiotic administration and fluid resuscitation. The evidence base for SEP-1 is evolving and there is ongoing debate regarding its clinical impact. However, recording the timing of recognition and treatment remains a current regulatory requirement and a defensible medicolegal practice.

Sample documentation language: "Sepsis recognized at 14:32 based on suspected pneumonia with new hypotension (BP 88/54) and lactate 3.2 mmol/L. Blood cultures obtained at 14:38. Ceftriaxone and azithromycin administered at 14:45. Crystalloid resuscitation initiated at 14:40 with 500 mL bolus; reassessed after each bolus. Total 2.1L administered by 15:25 with improvement in BP to 94/62. Clinical rationale: volume individualized based on hemodynamic response and absence of signs of fluid overload."

• Document clinical reasoning for antimicrobial selection and timing

Record the suspected infection source, rationale for antibiotic choices and patient-specific factors influencing treatment, such as allergies, recent antimicrobial exposure and risk of resistant organisms. If antibiotics are delayed for diagnostic reasons in patients with possible sepsis who are not in shock, include the clinical reasoning. 

• Document reassessment of volume status and perfusion

Record reassessment findings after initial fluid resuscitation, including vital signs, lactate trends, urine output and physical exam results (capillary refill, skin mottling). Include the patient’s response to treatment and the rationale for administering additional fluids or vasopressors. The SEP-1 measure requires documenting when volume and perfusion assessments are repeated within 6 hours for patients with septic shock. 

Sample documentation language: "Reassessment at 16:00 after 2.1L crystalloid: BP 94/62, HR 98, lactate 2.8 (decreased from 3.2). Capillary refill 3 seconds. Urine output 40 mL/hr. Patient fluid responsive, will continue resuscitation with additional 500 mL bolus and reassess."

• Document communication with consultants and receiving providers

Record discussions with ICU teams, infectious disease specialists and surgical consultants, including their recommendations and plans. Also, document key clinical details shared during handoffs to ensure smooth care transitions. 


ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR

Failure to Recognize Responsibility

Contributing action or omission: Failing to provide timely care or recognize clinical responsibility for sepsis patients, particularly during care transitions or when patients are boarding in non-traditional locations, can lead to adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely, appropriate care and clear accountability for sepsis care across settings.

• Administer antimicrobials promptly based on clinical certainty and severity
For patients with septic shock or a high likelihood of sepsis, administer antimicrobials immediately, ideally within 1 hour of recognition. For patients with possible sepsis without shock, conduct a rapid assessment of infectious versus noninfectious causes; if concern for infection persists, administer antimicrobials within 3 hours. If antibiotics are deferred for diagnostic evaluation in patients without shock, document the clinical reasoning and ensure close monitoring. This risk-stratified approach is supported by SSC 2026 guidelines and reflects the evolving understanding that not all patients with possible sepsis require immediate antibiotics.
• Avoid empirical anaerobic antibiotic coverage unless risk factors are present
Withhold empirical anaerobic coverage in septic patients without specific risk factors, as suggested by SSC 2026 guidelines; observational data associate empiric anti-anaerobic antibiotics with increased mortality. Risk factors warranting anaerobic coverage include intra-abdominal, deep-seated gynecologic or obstetric, necrotizing soft tissue, head and neck, CNS abscess, or empyema sources of infection. Agents with anaerobic activity (piperacillin-tazobactam, carbapenems) remain appropriate for multidrug-resistant pathogen coverage. Document the rationale for including or withholding anaerobic coverage.
• Deescalate antimicrobial therapy based on culture data and clinical trajectory
Narrow or discontinue antimicrobials once culture and susceptibility data are available, as recommended by SSC 2026 guidelines. For clinically improving patients with negative final cultures, deescalation is also suggested. Meta-analysis of 26 studies suggests deescalation may reduce short-term mortality, length of stay and antimicrobial resistance. Where optimal antibiotic duration is unclear and source control is adequate, procalcitonin combined with clinical evaluation may guide discontinuation. Document the rationale for deescalation decisions, including culture results, clinical trajectory and procalcitonin trends when used.
• Be aware of selective digestive decontamination for mechanically ventilated sepsis patients
When coordinating ICU-level care or participating in antimicrobial stewardship discussions, keep in mind that SSC 2026 guidelines suggest selective decontamination of the digestive tract (SDD) for mechanically ventilated patients with sepsis or septic shock in ICUs with low antimicrobial resistance prevalence. Hospitalists should be aware of this intervention and communicate with ICU teams regarding its applicability.
• Initiate vasopressors promptly for refractory hypotension

For patients with persistent hypotension during or after initial fluid resuscitation, start vasopressor therapy to maintain a MAP of ≥65 mm Hg. SSC 2026 guidelines suggest initial IV crystalloid fluid bolus resuscitation followed by vasopressor support if hypotension persists. In patients with unstable septic shock—identified by severely reduced blood pressure, mottled skin, ashen appearance, cyanosis, tachycardia or altered mentation—immediate concurrent administration of vasopressors together with IV crystalloid may be warranted. Do not delay vasopressor initiation to complete a specific fluid volume. Peripheral vasopressor administration is feasible and safe when central access is not immediately available; do not delay initiation while awaiting central line placement. The need for vasopressors signals that the patient is in septic shock and requires ICU-level monitoring. Do not delay treatment while waiting for an ICU bed. 
• Consider vasopressor selection in patients with concomitant cardiac dysfunction
For patients with septic shock and concomitant cardiac dysfunction, use either norepinephrine or epinephrine as the first-line vasopressor, as suggested by SSC 2026 guidelines. Norepinephrine may be preferred in patients with tachyarrhythmia or significant sinus tachycardia, while epinephrine may be preferred in patients with bradyarrhythmia or significant sinus bradycardia. For patients with persistent hypoperfusion and cardiac dysfunction despite adequate fluid resuscitation and arterial blood pressure, adding dobutamine to norepinephrine or using epinephrine alone is suggested. Document the clinical rationale for vasopressor selection.
• Clarify and follow accountability protocols during ED boarding

Adhere to hospital protocols that define the responsible care team when sepsis patients are boarding in the ED while awaiting an inpatient bed. When assuming responsibility for a boarded patient, document the time of care assumption and ensure continuity of the resuscitation plan, including ongoing monitoring, fluid resuscitation and antimicrobial therapy. Where institutional protocols do not clearly define accountability, hospitalists should work with institutional leadership to clarify roles. 

• Maintain vigilance for hospital-onset sepsis

Maintain a high index of suspicion for hospital-onset sepsis in patients who develop new signs of infection or clinical deterioration on the wards. Hospital-onset sepsis may present differently from community-onset sepsis and can be more difficult to recognize. Participate in institutional screening protocols and respond promptly to screening alerts. Hospital-onset sepsis demands the same urgency in recognition and treatment as community-onset sepsis.
