TITLE: Acute MI
RISK_BRIEF:
Acute Myocardial Infarction

SPECIALTY: Internal Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Chest Pain, Dyspnea, Acute Coronary Syndrome

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Acute Myocardial Infarction, Arrhythmia/Dysrhythmia, Stroke, Heart Failure, Cardiogenic Shock, Cardiac Arrest, Disability, Death 

Acute myocardial infarction is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in internal medicine. 
Mitigating Your Risk

With timely diagnosis, evidence-based treatment, coordinated communication and thorough documentation, internal medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with acute myocardial infarction and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for acute myocardial infarction and provides strategies to help prevent them.
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving acute myocardial infarction typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 84% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 16%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate. 
Actions and Omissions Driving Acute Myocardial Infarction Risk [insert chart]  

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing
Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain timely ECG or serial troponin measurements in patients with possible ACS increases the risk of delayed or missed diagnosis of myocardial infarction.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely identification of acute coronary syndromes and proper risk stratification.

• Obtain and interpret ECG within 10 minutes of presentation

Order a 12-lead ECG immediately upon arrival for patients presenting with symptoms suggestive of ACS, including chest discomfort, dyspnea, diaphoresis, nausea or unexplained weakness. A trained clinician should acquire and interpret an ECG within 10 minutes of first medical contact. Rapid ECG interpretation enables immediate identification of STEMI and activation of reperfusion guidelines. This timeline is a Class I recommendation in ACC/AHA guidelines and represents the standard of care for ACS evaluation. 

• Order serial high-sensitivity troponin measurements using validated algorithms

Measure troponin levels at specified intervals (0/1–hour or 0/3–hour schedules) based on your  institution’s assay specifications and rapid-rule out pathways. High-sensitivity troponin (hs-cTn) is the preferred biomarker for detecting myocardial injury. A single normal hs-cTn does not exclude MI if ordered early after symptom onset; serial measurements are required. Absolute change thresholds offer better diagnostic accuracy than relative change criteria. Serial testing is especially important for patients with chronic comorbidities such as advanced age, chronic kidney disease or heart failure, where baseline troponin may be elevated. 

• Obtain posterior leads (V7–V9) when posterior MI is suspected

Order posterior leads in cases of high clinical suspicion for circumflex occlusion, especially when the initial ECG is non-diagnostic or shows anterior ST depression. Isolated ST-segment depression in leads V1–V3 may indicate acute posterior transmural MI. ST-elevation ≥0.5 mm in posterior leads supports the diagnosis. Failing to order posterior leads can result in missed STEMI-equivalent presentations requiring emergent revascularization. 
• Repeat ECG during clinical course and with recurrent symptoms

Perform the ECG every 15–30 minutes with symptom changes during evaluation and before discharge if symptoms recur. Serial ECGs detect dynamic ischemic changes that may not appear initially. In the NCDR ACTION registry, 11% of STEMI patients had a normal first ECG, but 72.4% developed diagnostic changes within 90 minutes. 

CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention
Contributing action or omission: Delays in reperfusion therapy, failure to recognize STEMI-equivalent ECG patterns and inadequate risk stratification of NSTE-ACS patients increase the risk of myocardial damage, cardiogenic shock and death.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely and appropriate treatment of acute coronary syndromes.

• Activate STEMI protocol immediately upon ECG diagnosis

Notify the cardiac catheterization laboratory as soon as STEMI criteria are identified on the ECG. Aim for a first medical contact-to-device time of ≤90 minutes for patients at PCI-capable hospitals. Every 30-minute delay in reperfusion is associated with a 7.5% increase in relative 1–year mortality risk. Do not delay reperfusion therapy to await troponin results if the ECG confirms STEMI. 
• Recognize STEMI-equivalent ECG patterns that require emergent angiography
Initiate emergent coronary angiography in patients with high-risk ECG patterns suggestive of acute coronary occlusion. Standard STEMI criteria fail to identify a meaningful subset of these patients, despite their risk of outcomes comparable to classic STEMI. High-risk STEMI-equivalent patterns include: 
Hyperacute T waves
De Winter sign (upsloping ST-segment depression with tall T waves in the precordial leads)
Wellens’ syndrome (biphasic or deeply inverted T waves in leads V2–V3)
New left bundle branch block accompanied by ischemic symptoms
Isolated posterior myocardial infarction
• Administer fibrinolytic therapy when PCI is not available within 120 minutes

If PCI cannot be performed within 120 minutes of first medical contact, administer fibrinolytic therapy, such as tenecteplase, alteplase or reteplase, for patients without contraindications. Use half-dose of tenecteplase for those 75 or older to reduce intracranial hemorrhage risk. After fibrinolysis, transfer immediately to a PCI-capable facility for angiography within 2–24 hours. Document the rationale for fibrinolytic therapy over transfer for primary PCI. 
• Risk-stratify NSTE-ACS patients and determine timing of invasive evaluation

Use validated risk stratification tools to determine the timing of coronary angiography in NSTE-ACS patients. High-risk features, such as refractory angina, hemodynamic instability, ventricular arrhythmias or new wall motion abnormalities, warrant immediate or early invasive evaluation within 24 hours. In high-risk patients, prompt invasive angiography with revascularization within 24–48 hours reduces mortality from 6.5% to 4.9%.
• Assess for mechanical complications in patients with cardiogenic shock

Perform bedside echocardiography immediately in patients with cardiogenic shock, a new murmur or acute hemodynamic deterioration following AMI. Mechanical complications, such as ventricular septal rupture, papillary muscle rupture and free wall rupture, occur in fewer than 0.1% of AMI patients but result in more than 40% in-hospital mortality. Patients with mechanical complications should be treated at facilities with cardiac surgical expertise. 

CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate post-procedure monitoring and discharge planning increase the risk of recurrent ischemic events, readmission and preventable mortality.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support early identification of complications and optimize care transitions.

• Maintain continuous ECG monitoring during hospitalization

Monitor AMI patients with continuous telemetry to detect arrhythmias, recurrent ischemia and conduction abnormalities. Tailor monitoring duration to infarct size, revascularization status and complications. Document rhythm and any significant events. 

• Obtain echocardiography to assess ventricular function

Perform echocardiography during hospitalization to assess left ventricular ejection fraction, regional wall motion abnormalities and valvular function. Use this information to guide medical therapy decisions (such as ACE inhibitor/ARB, aldosterone antagonist and ICD evaluation) and risk stratification. Document results and their management implications. 

• Implement discharge planning with medication reconciliation

Reconcile medications before discharge, including aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitors, high-intensity statins, beta-blockers (particularly in patients with reduced ejection fraction), ACE inhibitors/ARBs and SGLT2 inhibitors as indicated. Prescribe sublingual nitroglycerin unless contraindicated. Use discharge checklists to address all guideline-directed therapies and document the reasons for any omitted therapies. 

• Refer to cardiac rehabilitation before discharge

Confirm referral to a cardiac rehab program and provide educational materials with contact details. Address barriers like transportation, work obligations and insurance coverage. Cardiac rehabilitation reduces mortality and recurrent cardiovascular events after AMI. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Communication failures—including inadequate handoffs, unclear escalation pathways and insufficient coordination between emergency, cardiology and primary care teams—raise the risk of delayed treatment and gaps in follow-up care.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication and enable a coordinated team response.

• Implement handoff protocols for ACS patients

Apply handoff frameworks such as SBAR during care transitions between the emergency department, catheterization laboratory, CCU and step-down units. Communicate ECG findings, troponin trajectory, interventions, complications, current medications and pending issues. Document handoff details in the medical record. 

• Establish escalation triggers for clinical deterioration

Follow guidelines specifying when to escalate care (such as recurrent chest pain, hemodynamic instability, new arrhythmias or rising troponin) and who is responsible for notification. Provide nurses with clear parameters for contacting physicians, using closed-loop communication to confirm orders and verify understanding. 

• Coordinate follow-up with cardiology and primary care before discharge

Schedule post-MI follow-up within 1–2 weeks for higher-risk patients (e.g., age ≥65, reduced LVEF, incomplete revascularization) and within 2–4 weeks for lower-risk patients, but no later than 6 weeks for any patient, as delays beyond this threshold are associated with significantly lower medication adherence. Coordinate follow-up with both cardiology and primary care before discharge, as collaborative follow-up is associated with higher rates of evidence-based medication use. Share discharge summaries—including diagnosis, procedures, prescribed medications and pending issues—with all follow-up providers and confirm patient understanding of the follow-up plan using teach-back methods.

• Communicate with primary care regarding long-term secondary prevention

Understand that patients with AMI need lifelong secondary prevention, including lipid management, blood pressure control, diabetes management and lifestyle changes. Provide primary care providers with complete details about the index event and ongoing medication requirements. Stress the importance of medication adherence and risk factor modification. 


ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider 
Contributing action or omission: Failure to educate patients about warning symptoms, medication adherence and lifestyle changes increases the risk of recurrent cardiac events and poor outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient engagement and self-management.

• Provide discharge education

Before patients leave the hospital, give them verbal and written education covering the reason they were hospitalized, medications (purpose, dose, frequency, potential adverse effects), symptom monitoring (what to watch for and when to seek care), activity restrictions, return to daily routine and follow-up appointments. Use the teach-back method to confirm their understanding. Provide materials in the patient's preferred language and appropriate literacy level. 

• Educate patients about warning symptoms 

Ensure that patients understand symptoms requiring emergency evaluation, such as chest pain or pressure, shortness of breath, diaphoresis, pain radiating to the arm, jaw or back or warning signs similar to their initial event. Explain when to call 911 versus contact the office. Document patient education and stated understanding. 
• Address barriers to medication adherence

Identify challenges patients are having with medication use, including costs, regimen complexity, side effects and health literacy. Refer patients to pharmacy assistance programs or social workers as needed. Simplify regimens when possible and schedule follow-ups to check adherence and address concerns.
• Provide individualized lifestyle guidance and intervention
Counsel patients on lifestyle modifications essential for secondary prevention, including dietary changes, physical activity and smoking cessation. Recommend a heart-healthy dietary pattern emphasizing vegetables, fruits, legumes, nuts, whole grains and lean protein while minimizing saturated fats, trans fats and ultra processed foods. Refer patients to a registered dietitian or nutritionist for individualized dietary counseling, particularly those with diabetes, obesity or dyslipidemia. Encourage patients without contraindications to engage in at least 150 minutes per week of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise (such as brisk walking) or 75 minutes per week of vigorous-intensity aerobic exercise, supplemented by resistance training at least 2 days per week. For current smokers, initiate smoking cessation counseling and offer pharmacotherapy (such as varenicline or nicotine replacement therapy). Document lifestyle recommendations, referrals and barriers.
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Insufficient documentation of clinical findings, decision-making, time-sensitive interventions and patient education compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to enable documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document timing of key diagnostic and therapeutic interventions

Record exact times of symptom onset (patient reported), ECG acquisition and interpretation, troponin collection and results, catheterization lab activation, arterial access and reperfusion. This shows adherence to time-based quality metrics and supports defensibility. 
Sample documentation language: "ECG obtained at 14:02, interpreted at 14:05 showing ST elevation in leads II, III, aVF with reciprocal depression in V1–V3. STEMI protocol activated at 14:06. Catheterization laboratory arrival 14:32. First device time 14:48."

• Document clinical reasoning for management decisions

When deciding between invasive and conservative management, timing of intervention or deviation from protocols, document the factors considered, such as clinical presentation, risk stratification, patient preferences, comorbidities and contraindications. This practice demonstrates sound clinical judgment. 
Sample documentation language: "Patient with NSTEMI, HEART score 7 (high risk). Discussed with interventional cardiology at 11:45. Given refractory chest discomfort despite medical therapy and rising troponin trajectory, decision made to proceed with early invasive angiography within 24 hours rather than conservative management. Patient is not on anticoagulation and has no contraindications to dual antiplatelet therapy. Risks, benefits and alternatives of invasive strategy discussed with patient, who expressed understanding and agreement. Plan documented and communicated to CCU team at handoff."
• Document recognition and evaluation of atypical presentations

Record the clinical reasoning that led to testing for patients who present with atypical symptoms (dyspnea, fatigue, nausea, back pain) and are later diagnosed with ACS. When ACS is ruled out, note the rationale, including a negative troponin trajectory, a non-ischemic ECG or alternative diagnosis. 
Sample documentation language:  “68-year-old woman with diabetes and hypertension presenting with acute onset nausea, diaphoresis and epigastric discomfort without chest pain. Given cardiovascular risk factors and atypical symptom pattern consistent with ACS in women and patients with diabetes, obtained ECG at 09:14 showing nonspecific ST-T changes in V4–V6. Initial hs-cTnI 8 ng/L at 09:18. Serial ECG at 09:45 showed new T-wave inversions in V3–V5. Repeat hs-cTnI at 11:18 rose to 48 ng/L (delta 40 ng/L). Findings consistent with NSTEMI. Cardiology consulted at 11:22 for invasive evaluation.”

• Document patient education and discharge instructions

Record patient instructions on warning symptoms, medications, activity restrictions and follow-up appointments. Note the patient's stated understanding and any identified barriers. Include written discharge guidance in the medical record. 

Sample documentation language: "Discussed warning symptoms requiring emergency evaluation, including chest pain, shortness of breath, diaphoresis. Patient instructed to call 911 for any symptoms similar to initial presentation. Reviewed all discharge medications, including purpose and dosing. Patient verbalized understanding. Written instructions provided in English."


ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS
Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care
Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify and address barriers to medication adherence, lifestyle changes and follow-up care increases the likelihood of recurrent cardiovascular events.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient engagement and address barriers to care.

• Assess and address social determinants of health

Before discharge, identify barriers patients may face in obtaining medications, attending follow-up appointments and participating in cardiac rehab. Consider factors like transportation, work obligations, childcare, insurance coverage and health literacy. Refer patients to social workers, pharmacy assistance programs or community resources as needed. 

• Simplify medication regimens when possible

Use once-daily formulations, pill organizers or medication lists to reduce complexity and improve patient adherence. Consider combination products to reduce pill burden. Review and adjust regimen at follow-ups, discontinuing unnecessary medications. 
• Implement structured follow-up systems

Establish systems for post-discharge follow-ups, including calls within the first week to reinforce education, assess symptoms and address medication concerns. Use patient portals, text reminders and other technology to support engagement. Track missed appointments and conduct outreach. 
• Document patient non-adherence and continued outreach

Record efforts to contact patients and their responses when they miss follow-up appointments, decline testing or report medication non-adherence. If they refuse care, include details of the conversation, such as recommendations, risks explained and stated reasons for refusal. Continue outreach by phone, patient portal or mail.

TITLE: Anticoagulation
RISK_BRIEF:
Anticoagulation
SPECIALTY: Internal Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Atrial Fibrillation, Venous Thromboembolism, Mechanical Heart Valves

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Bleeding Complications, Thromboembolic Events, Medication Errors
Anticoagulation is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in internal medicine.  
Mitigating Your Risk 
 
With evidence-based prescribing and monitoring, structured perioperative management and systematic patient education, internal medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with anticoagulation and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for anticoagulation and provides strategies to help prevent them.   

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving anticoagulation typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 94% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 6%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Anticoagulation Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain suitable baseline and ongoing laboratory monitoring increases the risk of both bleeding and thromboembolic complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure proper laboratory assessment before and during anticoagulation therapy, supporting safe dosing and early detection of complications.

• Obtain baseline laboratory assessment before initiating anticoagulation

Perform a complete blood count, metabolic panel (including renal and hepatic function) and baseline coagulation studies (PT/INR, aPTT) before starting anticoagulants. These tests identify contraindications, such as severe thrombocytopenia and significant renal/hepatic impairment, establish reference values and guide drug and dose selection. Renal function is especially critical for DOACs, as dabigatran is contraindicated with CrCl ≤30 mL/min and factor Xa inhibitors require dose adjustments or avoidance in severe renal impairment. 
• Implement practices for ongoing laboratory monitoring

Create written policies for baseline and ongoing lab tests for anticoagulated patients. For warfarin, monitor INR weekly during initiation and monthly once stable, with a target INR of 2.0–3.0 for most indications. For DOACs, check renal function at least annually or every 3–6 months for patients with CrCl 30–60 mL/min or those at risk for renal decline. Monitoring protocols ensures consistent care delivery, timely dose adjustments and adherence to standards of care.

• Assess bleeding risk using validated tools at each clinical encounter

Evaluate patients with tools like HAS-BLED at initiation and follow-up visits and when clinical status changes. Regular assessment during routine coagulation visits identifies modifiable risk factors, such as uncontrolled blood pressure, labile INR, concomitant antiplatelet or NSAID use and alcohol excess, that can be addressed to reduce bleeding risk while maintaining anticoagulation benefits. 


CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Medication Error

Contributing action or omission: Incorrect dosing and drug selection and failure to account for drug interactions are leading causes of anticoagulation-related adverse events.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce prescribing errors, ensure appropriate drug selection and prevent drug-drug interactions.

• Select anticoagulant according to patient-specific factors and guidelines

When initiating therapy, chose an anticoagulant based on clinical indication, patient characteristics and potential contraindications. DOACs are preferred over warfarin for most patients with nonvalvular atrial fibrillation and VTE, while warfarin is indicated for mechanical heart valves, moderate-severe mitral stenosis and triple-positive antiphospholipid syndrome. Reassess when significant clinical changes occur. Following guidelines reduces thromboembolic and bleeding risks and demonstrates adherence to evidence-based care.

• Verify correct dosing with renal considerations 

At each prescription and refill, account for renal function, body weight and age.
For apixaban in atrial fibrillation, reduce to 2.5 mg twice daily if ≥2 of the following are present: age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg or creatinine ≥1.5 mg/dL.
For rivaroxaban in VTE, prescribe 15 mg twice daily for 21 days, then 20 mg daily with food. 
For dabigatran, avoid if CrCl <30 mL/min and reduce dose with concurrent P-glycoprotein inhibitors. 
Correct dosing prevents underdosing (risk of thromboembolism) and overdosing (risk of bleeding), both common causes of adverse outcomes.

• Screen for and manage drug-drug interactions at every medication change

Review patient medication lists for significant interactions when starting anticoagulation, at each visit and when medications are added or changed. Strong CYP3A4/P-glycoprotein inhibitors (ketoconazole, ritonavir, itraconazole) significantly increase DOAC levels and bleeding risk, while strong inducers (rifampin, phenytoin, carbamazepine, St. John's wort) reduce DOAC efficacy and increase thromboembolic risk. Concurrent antiplatelet agents, NSAIDs and SSRIs increase bleeding risk with all anticoagulants. Proactive interaction management prevents adverse drug events and increases patient safety.
• Develop EHR-based clinical decision support tools
Implement pre-built, evidence-based order sets for anticoagulation with automatic dose calculations based on renal function, drug interaction alerts and required monitoring reminders. Ensure that these tools are active at the point of prescribing. Clinical decision support reduces prescribing errors, promotes guideline-based dosing and provides real-time safety checks that support defensible clinical decision-making. 

CLINICAL: TREATMENT 
Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention 
Contributing action or omission: Inappropriate perioperative management and failure to recognize or respond to bleeding complications increase the risk of adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure safe perioperative anticoagulation management and proper response to bleeding events.

• Apply perioperative anticoagulation protocols

Use evidence-based guidelines for managing perioperative DOAC and warfarin based on procedure bleeding risk and renal function. For DOACs with low-moderate bleeding risk procedures, stop 1 day before and resume 1 day after; for high bleeding risk procedures, stop 2 days before and resume 2-3 days after, with interval adjustments for renal impairment. Protocols reduce care variability, minimize bleeding and thromboembolic complications and provide documentation of management rationale.
• Avoid heparin bridging for most patients

Do not routinely bridge anticoagulation with heparin for patients with atrial fibrillation undergoing elective procedures. The BRIDGE trial showed that bridging increased major bleeding without reducing thromboembolic events in patients with moderate thromboembolic risk. Reserve bridging for very high-risk cases (such as mechanical mitral valves or recent stroke/TIA within 3 months) after careful risk-benefit evaluation. Avoiding unnecessary bridging reduces bleeding complications while maintaining thromboembolic protection.

• Ensure immediate availability of reversal agents and protocols

Maintain institutional guidelines and readily available reversal agents for anticoagulation-related bleeding emergencies, with access to hematology consultation for complex cases. Use 4-factor PCC plus IV vitamin K for warfarin, idarucizumab for dabigatran and 4-factor PCC or activated PCC for factor Xa inhibitors (andexanet alfa has been withdrawn from the US market). Clear frameworks, rapid access to reversal agents and available hematology expertise enable timely treatment of life-threatening bleeding and show that institutions are prepared to address foreseeable complications.

ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Poor adherence to medication routines leads to treatment failure (thromboembolism) or erratic anticoagulation (bleeding risk).

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve patient understanding, engagement and adherence to anticoagulation therapy.

• Provide drug-specific patient education

Teach anticoagulated patients about the signs of bleeding and thrombosis, the importance of adherence (especially for DOACs without routine monitoring), drug and food interactions, how to handle missed doses and when to seek emergency care. Provide patients with a written list of common medications and supplements that interact with their specific anticoagulant and a phone number for questions. Highlight the importance of maintaining consistent vitamin K intake and lifestyle factors that affect INR with warfarin, taking rivaroxaban with food and storing dabigatran in its original container. Patient education improves adherence, supports early recognition of complications and promotes informed engagement.
• Involve patients in shared decision-making for drug selection

Engage patients in discussions about anticoagulant options, including their preferences for monitoring, dosing, cost and lifestyle. Shared decision-making improves adherence and satisfaction and shows respect for patient autonomy.

• Systematically monitor adherence and follow-up

Establish follow-up intervals, medication refill tracking and proactive outreach for missed appointments. Consider pharmacy-based monitoring programs, telephone follow-up and electronic reminders. Tracking over 12 months is associated with improved adherence outcomes. Regular follow-up identifies non-adherence early, enables timely intervention and reinforces patient safety.

With evidence-based prescribing and monitoring, structured perioperative management and systematic patient education, internal medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with anticoagulation and reduce malpractice exposure. Th

TITLE: Cancer
RISK_BRIEF:
Cancer Diagnosis

SPECIALTY: Internal Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Symptomatic Presentations, Abnormal Test Results, Cancer Screening

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Delayed Diagnosis, Advanced-Stage Cancer, Preventable Mortality
Complications of cancer diagnosis and management are a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in internal medicine. 

Mitigating Your Risk

With guideline adherent screening, timely evaluation and referral and systematic follow-up, internal medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with cancer diagnosis and management and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for cancer diagnoses and management and provides strategies to help prevent them. 

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving cancer diagnosis and management typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 78% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 22%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Complications Related to Cancer Diagnosis and Management [insert chart]
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam
Contributing action or omission: Failure to gather a complete symptom history, assess cancer risk factors or perform targeted physical examination increases the risk of missed or delayed cancer diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure comprehensive initial assessment and appropriate risk stratification for patients presenting with potential cancer symptoms.

• Obtain and review previous medical records for new and transferring patients
Request medical records from prior providers and review promptly, as they may contain previously documented genetic testing, abnormal findings, pending evaluations or cancer history not reported by the patient. Do not rely solely on patient-reported history; studies show significant rates of inaccurate or incomplete self-reporting, with false-negative rates for cancer history as high as 39% when compared to registry data. Assign clear responsibility for tracking record requests and reviewing incoming documents. 
• Systematically assess cancer risk factors at initial presentation

When evaluating patients for potential cancer-related symptoms, gather a complete personal and family history of cancer, smoking (pack-years), prior radiation exposure and relevant occupational exposures. Document risk factors that justify heightened surveillance or early referral. Assessing risk factors ensures appropriate patient triage and adherence to evidence-based evaluation standards. Overlooking patient risk factors is among the most common diagnostic pitfalls.

• Characterize symptoms with sufficient detail to guide diagnostic strategy

Document the patient’s symptom duration, progression, associated features and response to initial treatment. For common cancer-associated symptoms, such as persistent cough, night sweats, rectal bleeding, hematuria, bruising, breast mass or unexplained weight loss, include specific characteristics that indicate the probability of cancer. Symptom documentation helps guide test selection and provides a defensible rationale for clinical decisions. Referral letters with clearly described symptoms optimize diagnostic outcomes. 
• Perform targeted physical exam based on presenting symptoms

Examine the patient’s relevant organ system thoroughly, including lymph node assessment when indicated. A clinical breast exam is essential for breast symptoms, even if imaging is planned. Gastrointestinal symptoms require abdominal and rectal exams. Document both positive and pertinent negative findings. An inadequate physical exam contributes to 42% of diagnostic errors in ambulatory malpractice claims.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing
Contributing action or omission: Failing to order proper diagnostic tests—including screening for eligible patients and diagnostics for symptomatic patients—is the most common breakdown in cancer diagnostic errors.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that proper testing is ordered and completed for patients at risk for or presenting with cancer symptoms.

• Implement systematic cancer screening for eligible patients
Ensure that all eligible patients receive cancer screenings recommended by evidence-based guidelines from organizations such as the USPSTF, ACS, NCCN and relevant specialty societies. Since screening recommendations may vary, internal medicine physicians should understand these differences to support shared decision-making with patients.
Use EHR-based reminders and population health tools to identify screening gaps and track test completion. Lack of guideline-directed screening contributes to 6%–17% of advanced-stage cancer diagnoses.
Establish strong follow-up systems to ensure timely diagnostic testing after a positive screening, such as scheduling a colonoscopy promptly after a positive stool-based test. Delays longer than 6–10 months are associated with increased cancer risk and advanced-stage disease.  

• Order diagnostic testing promptly (not screening) for alarm symptoms

Initiate a diagnostic workup immediately for symptoms that meet urgent referral criteria, such as rectal bleeding, hematuria, breast lump, dysphagia, iron deficiency anemia or post-menopausal bleeding. Only 40% of patients with alarm symptoms receive urgent referral within 14 days, yet 3.6% of those not referred are diagnosed with cancer within a year. Note that fecal immunochemical testing (FIT) is validated for colorectal cancer screening in asymptomatic patients. For patients with symptoms suggesting colorectal cancer, such as rectal bleeding, change in bowel habits or iron deficiency anemia, colonoscopy is the appropriate diagnostic test regardless of FIT results. Prompt investigation aligns with the standard of care and reduces diagnostic delays. Document clinical reasoning when selecting a diagnostic test.
Sample documentation language: "Patient presents with 6 weeks of rectal bleeding and iron deficiency anemia. Given alarm features, ordering colonoscopy on urgent basis. Discussed with patient the need for expedited evaluation to exclude colorectal malignancy."

• Pursue diagnostic evaluation when clinical suspicion persists despite negative initial testing

Order additional or alternative testing for patients with persistent symptoms or high clinical suspicion. Negative or inconclusive initial tests do not rule out cancer, and failing to recognize test limitations is a leading diagnostic pitfall. Document clinical reasoning when ordering additional workup.

• Advocate for radiology-led communication and scheduling after abnormal mammograms
Establish protocols for interpreting radiologists to communicate abnormal mammographic results directly to the referring physician and the patient and to schedule recommended follow-up imaging or biopsy without waiting for a PCP referral. This approach has been shown to increase timely follow-up from 73% to 90% and reduce median time to diagnostic evaluation from 28 to 9 days. Without such protocols, failure to follow up abnormal mammograms within 6 months ranges from 27% to 72% in primary care settings.
• Avoid attributing new symptoms to pre-existing chronic conditions without proper evaluation

Evaluate patients for cancer if they have chronic diseases, such as COPD, inflammatory bowel disease or chronic pain, present with new or changing symptoms. Don’t presume those symptoms are connected to the chronic condition. Such assumptions are diagnostic pitfalls that delay recognizing lung cancer in patients with COPD and colorectal cancer in patients with gastrointestinal conditions. 

CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care
Contributing action or omission: Delays in specialist referrals, failure to use urgent referral pathways when indicated or inadequate coordination of diagnostic workup increase the risk of advanced-stage cancer diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely specialist involvement and coordinated diagnostic evaluations.

• Use urgent referral pathways for patients with high-risk symptoms

Pursue expedited routes, such as two-week wait pathways and rapid diagnostic clinics, instead of routine referrals. Urgent suspected cancer referral pathways are associated with higher rates of early-stage diagnosis (52% early-stage vs. 16% for emergency presentations) and better survival outcomes. Document referral urgency and communicate directly with the specialist when necessary.

• Consider referral for genetic counseling 
Refer patients with family history suggestive of hereditary cancer syndromes, such as multiple affected relatives, early-onset cancers or specific cancer patterns, for genetic counseling and possible testing. Document the family history and referral decisions.
• Provide comprehensive referral information to maximize diagnostic efficiency

Include detailed descriptions of symptoms (duration, progression, characteristics), relevant exam findings, test results with dates, medical history and clinical concerns in referral communications. Referrals that lack complete symptoms and exam findings delay diagnosis. Consensus guidelines recommend including medical facts, symptom summaries, abnormal findings and reasons for referral. 
• Establish follow-up responsibility when referring patients for diagnostic evaluation

When sending patients for cancer workups, document and communicate who will track results and ensure diagnostic completion. Follow-up failures contribute to 28% of missed diagnoses in primary care and 26% in specialist care. Use closed-loop communication to confirm referral receipt and appointment scheduling.
• Explore multidisciplinary diagnostic pathways for patients with non-specific symptoms

For patients with concerning but vague symptoms, such as unexplained weight loss, fatigue or elevated inflammatory markers, consider referring to diagnostic centers or suspected cancer clinics offering coordinated multidisciplinary evaluations. Rapid diagnostic clinics reduce diagnosis time by 57%–70% compared to standard pathways. These channels are especially useful when the primary cancer site is unclear.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Communication failures between providers—including inadequate handoffs, unclear responsibility for test follow-up and insufficient coordination between primary care and specialists—contribute to a significant number of errors in cancer diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure reliable communication throughout the diagnostic process.

• Implement handoff protocols for patients undergoing cancer evaluation

When transferring care for patients with pending cancer workups, use standardized frameworks to communicate diagnostic status, outstanding tests with time frames, follow-up actions and provider responsibilities. Document handoff details in the medical record. Handoff failures contribute to 20% of diagnostic errors in ambulatory settings. 

• Establish closed-loop communication for critical test results

Implement systems to ensure that the ordering provider directly receives abnormal results suggestive of cancer and acknowledges receipt of those test results. Avoid relying solely on EHR notification. For highly suspicious findings, such as imaging indicating cancer or pathology confirming it, use verbal or secure message communications with acknowledgment. Electronic trigger-based interventions can identify patients at risk of delayed follow-up and speed up diagnostic evaluation.https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26304875
• Coordinate care between primary care and oncology

During active treatment, communicate with the oncology team to understand the treatment plan, expected side effects and which provider is managing non-oncology conditions. At treatment completion, request a survivorship care plan, including a treatment summary, surveillance schedule, expected late effects and a clear delineation of responsibilities between oncologist and PCP. For new patients with a cancer history, request oncology records and survivorship care plans and document cancer-related care needs. Ensure that every aspect of follow-up, including cancer surveillance, treatment-related toxicity management, new primary screening and chronic disease care, is assigned to a specific provider and documented to prevent gaps or duplication.
• Document communication with specialists and consultants

Record verbal communications with specialists, including recommendations and agreed follow-up plans. If specialist recommendations differ from the initial management plan, include how the discrepancies were resolved. This demonstrates care coordination and supports defensibility.

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider
Contributing action or omission: Failing to share test results, clearly explain diagnostic uncertainty or adequately educate patients about warning signs increases the risk of delayed diagnosis and patient harm.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients are informed partners in the diagnostic process.

• Engage in shared decision-making for cancer screening

Discuss benefits, risks and patient preferences for screening decisions where guidelines recommend individualized approaches. Acknowledge guideline differences when they exist and incorporate patient values into decisions. Document the shared decision-making conversation and the patient's informed choice.
• Communicate test results expeditiously

Establish systems to ensure that patients promptly receive all test results—normal, abnormal and inconclusive. Do not assume patients will follow up if they don’t hear back. Document communication method, details shared and the result. For abnormal results, confirm that the patient understands next steps and timelines.
• Use clear, non-technical language when discussing cancer-related concerns

When discussing a possible cancer diagnosis or need for further testing, use plain language suited to the patient's health literacy level. Avoid jargon—for example, say "the cancer has spread" instead of "metastasized." Use teach-back methods to confirm understanding. ASCO guidelines recommend breaking information into small doses and stopping frequently to check comprehension. 
• Educate patients about symptoms requiring prompt re-evaluation

If cancer is not found after the initial evaluation but clinical suspicion remains, clearly explain which symptoms warrant a return visit. Document this safety-netting conversation. Patients who understand warning signs are more likely to seek timely care if symptoms progress or new ones develop. 
Sample documentation language: "Discussed with patient that while initial workup is reassuring, she should return promptly if symptoms persist beyond 4 weeks, worsen, or if she develops new symptoms such as unintentional weight loss, blood in stool or abdominal mass. Patient verbalized understanding."

• Schedule follow-up when diagnostic uncertainty exists

Arrange a subsequent appointment for patients with concerning symptoms and pending workup or inconclusive initial tests before they leave. Do not rely on patients to call back. Proactive scheduling reduces the risk of losing patients during the diagnostic process. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUE
Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure
Contributing action or omission: Lack of systematic tracking for abnormal results, standardized referral pathways and population health tools to monitor screening and follow-up undermines timely cancer diagnosis.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to create reliable systems for cancer detection and diagnosis.

• Implement EHR-based tracking systems for abnormal cancer-related test results

Develop or use electronic systems to track patients with abnormal findings, including imaging, laboratory or pathology results, who have not completed recommended follow-up. Electronic trigger tools can identify patients at risk of delayed evaluation, reducing follow-up time and increasing the number of patients who receive suitable assessment. Assign responsibility for monitoring and responding to system alerts. 
• Establish referral pathways with defined timelines

Create institutional protocols that define referral pathways, timeframes and escalation procedures for patients with suspected cancer. Suspected cancer initiatives using EMR-based referral flags, embedded guidelines and tracking dashboards have improved diagnosis time by 57%–70%. Include mechanisms to track completion of referral.

• Develop population health systems for cancer screening

Implement systematic methods for identifying patients eligible for cancer screening, administering screening at indicated intervals and following up on positive results. Include robust processes for completing colonoscopies after positive stool-based tests. Organized screening programs with direct patient outreach, navigation support and clinician-directed alerts increase screening rates and reduce disparities. 

• Conduct regular case reviews and quality improvement activities

Establish processes to review delayed cancer diagnoses, uncover system vulnerabilities and drive improvements. Use structured, blame-free debriefing to analyze contributing factors. Track metrics such as time from symptom presentation to diagnosis, time from abnormal result to follow-up and the proportion of cancers diagnosed at advanced stage. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS
Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care
Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify and address barriers to diagnostic testing, specialist appointments and follow-up care increases the likelihood of delayed cancer diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and address barriers to timely diagnosis.

• Assess and address barriers to diagnostic testing and follow-up

Identify patient-specific challenges, such as transportation, work obligations, childcare, insurance coverage, fear of diagnosis and health literacy. Offer patients solutions, such as navigation services, flexible scheduling and telehealth options. Patient-related factors, including access and affordability issues as well as a documented hesitancy to undergo invasive tests, are among the most common factors associated with missed colorectal cancer diagnoses.

• Use patient navigation services for high-risk patients

Engage nurse or patient navigators to guide diagnostic procedures, overcome care barriers and ensure that patients complete the diagnostic process. Multidisciplinary collaboration and nurse navigator involvement are key to improving timely cancer diagnosis. Navigation services are especially valuable for patients with complex social needs or multiple comorbidities.
• Document patient decisions regarding recommended testing

When patients decline recommended diagnostic testing or fail to complete scheduled procedures, document the recommendations, risks explained, reasons for declining and plans for ongoing monitoring. Continue outreach by phone, patient portal or mail. Documenting those details ensures that care standards are met while respecting patient autonomy.

• Provide written instructions about the importance of completing diagnostic workup

Give patients written materials explaining the importance of recommended tests, what to expect and how to schedule appointments. Ensure that materials are in the patient's primary language and at a proper literacy level. Effectively communicating the urgency of appointments significantly influences patient follow-through.

TITLE: Diabetes
RISK_BRIEF:
Diabetes 
SPECIALTY: Internal Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Type 1 Diabetes, Type 2 Diabetes, Prediabetes

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Hypoglycemia, Diabetic Ketoacidosis, Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State, Cardiovascular Events, End-Stage Renal Disease, Lower Extremity Amputation
Diabetes is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in internal medicine. 
Mitigating Your Risk
With systematic screening, evidence-based medication management, timely specialist referrals and thorough documentation, internal medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with diabetes diagnosis and management and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for diabetes diagnosis and management and provides strategies to help prevent them.
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving diabetes diagnosis and management typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 79% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 21%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.  

Actions and Omissions Driving Diabetes Diagnosis and Management Risk [insert chart]  
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.

CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to perform appropriate screening, diagnostic testing or ongoing laboratory surveillance increases the risk of undiagnosed diabetes, missed disease progression and delayed recognition of complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support timely detection of diabetes and prompt recognition of disease progression and complications.

• Screen high-risk individuals using diagnostic tests

At routine visits, screen adults aged 35 and older and younger adults with overweight or obesity (BMI ≥25 kg/m² or ≥23 kg/m² for Asian individuals) who have at least one risk factor:
First-degree relative with diabetes
High-risk race/ethnicity
History of cardiovascular disease, hypertension, dyslipidemia, polycystic ovary syndrome, gestational diabetes
Physical inactivity
Use HbA1c, fasting plasma glucose or a 2-hour oral glucose tolerance test. Repeat testing every 3 years if normal or annually for those with prediabetes. Systematic screening identifies the 25% of U.S. adults with undiagnosed diabetes, allowing earlier intervention to prevent complications. 
• Obtain baseline and periodic laboratory evaluation for complications

At diagnosis and annually, order HbA1c, lipid panel, serum creatinine with estimated GFR, urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio and liver enzymes. Increase monitoring frequency as needed based on clinical status, medication changes or presence of chronic kidney disease. Baseline and periodic testing help identify end-organ involvement and demonstrate adherence to ADA-recommended evaluation. 
• Confirm diagnosis with repeat testing when results conflict

If screening results are near diagnostic thresholds or if HbA1c and glucose values conflict, confirm the diagnosis with a second method or repeat the test. HbA1c may be unreliable in patients with hemoglobinopathies, recent blood transfusions, HIV infection or conditions affecting red blood cell turnover. Confirmatory testing reduces diagnostic errors and ensures accurate classification. 
• Evaluate for severe features and complications at each visit

Check for symptoms of hyperglycemia (polyuria, polydipsia and weight loss), hypoglycemia, cardiovascular disease, neuropathy, nephropathy and retinopathy. Perform a comprehensive foot examination annually or more often for patients with sensory loss, previous ulcers or amputations. Refer patients for timely dilated eye exams. Systematic assessments identify complications that may necessitate management changes and also uphold the standard of care. 
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention
Contributing action or omission: Insufficient monitoring of glycemic control, medication effects and hypoglycemia risk increases the likelihood of preventable complications and adverse drug events.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support optimal monitoring and timely detection of treatment-related complications.

• Measure HbA1c at intervals based on glycemic stability

Check HbA1c every 3 months until individualized, stable targets are achieved, then at least every 6 months. More frequent testing may be needed when therapy changes or glycemic control is suboptimal. HbA1c monitoring documents the degree of glycemic control and supports timely treatment intensification when targets are not met. 

• Reassess medication regimen for guideline concordance at monitoring visits
When reviewing HbA1c results or when new cardiovascular disease, heart failure or chronic kidney disease is identified, evaluate whether the current regimen includes agents with proven cardiorenal benefit (SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists) as recommended by ADA and AACE guidelines. Failure to intensify or update therapy in response to changing clinical status—sometimes called "clinical inertia"—is a well-documented contributor to preventable complications.
• Assess hypoglycemia risk at every clinical encounter

Evaluate patients for prior hypoglycemic events (frequency, severity, precipitants, symptoms), impaired hypoglycemia awareness and clinical risk factors, such as older age, cognitive impairment, chronic kidney disease, cardiovascular disease, depression, neuropathy and gastroparesis. Review concurrent medications for interactions that potentiate hypoglycemia, particularly antibiotics (fluoroquinolones, sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim), NSAIDs and fluconazole in patients on sulfonylureas or meglitinides. Screen at least annually for impaired hypoglycemia awareness using validated tools. Prior hypoglycemia is the strongest predictor of recurrence; systematic assessment supports risk stratification and treatment modification. 

• Individualize glycemic targets based on patient characteristics

Set HbA1c goals based on age, diabetes duration, life expectancy, comorbidities, hypoglycemia risk and patient preferences. A target of 7% suits most nonpregnant adults without severe hypoglycemia. Less stringent goals (up to 8%) may be suitable for those with limited life expectancy, extensive comorbidities, history of severe hypoglycemia or advanced complications. More stringent goals (6.5%) may suit patients with short diabetes duration and low hypoglycemia risk. Individual targets balance glycemic control benefits against the risks of hypoglycemia. 
• Deintensify therapy when hypoglycemia risk exceeds benefit

Reduce or discontinue insulin, sulfonylureas or meglitinides in patients at high risk of hypoglycemia while maintaining individualized glycemic goals. When discontinuing these agents, substitute with medication classes that have lower hypoglycemia risk and proven cardiorenal benefit when indicated, such as SGLT2 inhibitors or GLP-1 receptor agonists, rather than simply removing the medication. DPP-4 inhibitors are an alternative when SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists are not suitable. Deintensification is crucial for older adults, those with cognitive impairment and patients with HbA1c below target. Severe hypoglycemia is an absolute indication for treatment modification. 
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care

Contributing action or omission: Failure to refer a patient to specialists or diabetes care and education professionals when needed delays optimal management of disease and increases the risk of complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure optimal specialist involvement and coordinated care.

• Refer to diabetes self-management education and support at critical times

Connect newly diagnosed patients to diabetes self-management education and support (DSMES) services. Provide additional referrals annually and/or when treatment goals are unmet, complicating factors develop (medical, functional, psychosocial) or during life and care transitions. DSMES improves glycemic control, lowers hospitalizations and mortality and is cost-effective. 
• Refer to endocrinology or diabetes specialists for complex management

Consider referral for patients with type 1 diabetes requiring intensive insulin therapy (multiple daily injections or insulin pump), experiencing recurrent severe hypoglycemia or hypoglycemia unawareness, failing to meet glycemic targets despite optimized therapy or managing complex comorbidities. If unfamiliar with insulin initiation or intensification, refer to a specialist. 
• Coordinate care with appropriate specialists 
Refer patients to ophthalmology for dilated eye exams, nephrology for advanced chronic kidney disease (eGFR 30 mL/min/1.73m² or rapidly declining), cardiology for cardiovascular disease, podiatry for foot complications and behavioral health for distress, depression or disordered eating. Document referrals and follow up on recommendations. 

• Refer to medical nutrition therapy
Refer patients to a registered dietitian nutritionist for personalized nutrition therapy at diagnosis and as needed. Nutrition therapy is key in diabetes management and should fit each person’s needs, preferences and culture. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Communication failures—including inadequate handoffs, unclear care plans and insufficient care coordination—increase the risk of medication errors, missed follow-ups and delays in identifying complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communications and support coordinated care.

• Implement handoff protocols during care transitions

Standardize patient transfers between settings, including hospitals, outpatient clinics, primary care and specialists. Communicate diabetes regimen, recent HbA1c and glucose levels, hypoglycemia history, diet, self-care abilities and monitoring parameters. Document this information in the medical record. Patients are at higher risk for medication errors and blood sugar issues during care transitions. 
• Communicate DSMES participation and recommendations with the care team

Diabetes care and education specialists should share patient assessments, education and recommendations with the referring clinician and team. Document DSMES referrals, participation and outcomes in the medical record. 
• Reconcile medication at every transition

Review medications during hospital admission, discharge and other transitions. Confirm insulin type, dose and timing, oral medication doses and recent changes. Medication errors during transitions often cause preventable harm. 
• Prepare patients for ongoing care after transitions 
Create detailed plans prior to discharge or transition, including follow-ups, monitoring requirements and care team contact information. Confirm that patients discharged on insulin or medications with hypoglycemia risk have necessary supplies, understand self-monitoring and know when to seek care. Document the plan and patient understanding. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate patient education on self-management, warning signs and when to seek care increases the risk of complications and delays in presentation.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient engagement and support effective self-management.

• Provide comprehensive education on hypoglycemia recognition and treatment

Instruct patients and caregivers on hypoglycemia symptoms (shakiness, sweating, confusion and palpitations), glucose treatment thresholds (70 mg/dL), proper treatment (15–20 grams fast-acting carbs) and when to seek emergency care. Ensure that patients on insulin or sulfonylureas have glucagon and caregivers know how to use it. Document education and confirm patient understanding. 

• Educate patients on warning signs requiring immediate evaluation

Teach patients to recognize symptoms that need urgent evaluation, such as severe hypoglycemia, diabetic ketoacidosis (nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, fruity breath and altered mental status), hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state (extreme thirst, confusion and weakness) and symptoms of cardiovascular events. Document education and confirm patient understanding. 

• Use teach-back methods to confirm understanding

Ask patients to repeat information in their own words. Address any gaps in their comprehension and document the process. Teach-back improves retention and identifies misunderstandings. 
• Provide clear written instructions

Give patients discharge and self-management guidance in their primary language and tailored to their literacy level. Include medication use, monitoring parameters, diet guidance and contact information for questions. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of clinical decisions, patient education, treatment changes and follow-up plans compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document individualized glycemic targets and rationale

Record each patient’s HbA1c and glucose targets and clinical reasoning for them. When goals differ from guidelines, include factors considered, such as age, comorbidities, hypoglycemia risk, life expectancy and patient preferences. This level of documentation demonstrates thoughtful, individualized care. 

Sample documentation language: "HbA1c target of 8% established given patient's age (78 years), history of severe hypoglycemia requiring ED visit, moderate cognitive impairment and limited life expectancy. Discussed risks and benefits of tighter control with patient and daughter; they agree with less intensive approach to minimize hypoglycemia risk."

• Document hypoglycemia assessment and risk factors

At each visit, record assessment of hypoglycemia history, including frequency, severity, precipitants, symptoms and treatment. Include screening for impaired hypoglycemia awareness and identified risk factors. If treatment changes due to hypoglycemia risk, note the specific events and clinical reasoning. 

• Document patient education and self-management support

Record instructions on medication use, self-monitoring, diet recommendations, hypoglycemia recognition and treatment and warning signs. Note patient's stated understanding, self-management barriers, DSMES referrals and participation status. 

Sample documentation language: "Reviewed insulin injection technique with patient; patient demonstrated correct technique with teach-back. Discussed hypoglycemia symptoms and treatment; patient verbalized understanding of 15-15 rule. Provided written instructions in Spanish. Referred to DSMES for comprehensive education."
• Document medication changes with clinical rationale
When initiating, adjusting or discontinuing diabetes medications, record the reasons, expected benefits, potential risks discussed with the patient and monitoring plan. When deintensifying therapy, note concerns prompting the change and the patient’s understanding. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify and address barriers to medication adherence, self-monitoring, dietary recommendations and follow-up increases the likelihood of poor glycemic control and preventable complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and address barriers to effective self-management.

• Assess social determinants of health affecting diabetes management

Screen patients for food insecurity, housing instability, cost-related barriers to medications and supplies, transportation challenges and health literacy limitations. Food insecurity is linked to increased hypoglycemia-related ER visits and hospitalizations. Provide support through social work referrals, patient assistance programs and community resources. 

• Identify and address barriers to medication adherence

Assess patients for medication adherence challenges like costs, regimen complexity, side effects, injection anxiety, health beliefs and competing priorities. Simplify regimens when possible, address costs with generic alternatives or assistance programs and provide targeted education. Document identified barriers and attempted interventions. 

• Provide self-monitoring equipment and education

Ensure that patients have glucose monitoring supplies suited to their treatment plan and educate them on proper technique, monitoring frequency and result interpretation. Offer training and follow-up for continuous glucose monitoring. Note education and type of equipment provided. 

• Document non-adherence and continued outreach

Record follow-up efforts and responses when patients miss appointments, decline monitoring or fail to follow treatment plans. If patients refuse care, document discussion of recommendations and risks and their stated reasons for declining. Continue outreach by phone, patient portal or mail and record every attempt. 

Sample documentation language: "Patient reports not taking metformin for past 2 weeks due to GI side effects. Discussed importance of medication, strategies to minimize side effects (taking with food, gradual dose titration). Patient agrees to restart at lower dose. If intolerance persists, will consider alternative agent. Patient verbalized understanding of risks of uncontrolled diabetes."
