TITLE: Sepsis
RISK_BRIEF:
Sepsis

SPECIALTY: Internal Medicine

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Infection, Acute Illness, Hospitalized Patients

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Septic Shock, Multi-Organ Failure, Death
Sepsis is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in internal medicine.  

Mitigating Your Risk

With timely recognition and treatment, ongoing monitoring and clear communication and escalation, internal medicine physicians can mitigate the risks associated with sepsis and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for sepsis and provides strategies to help prevent them.
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving sepsis typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 85% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 15%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.    

Actions and Omissions Driving Sepsis Risk [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing
Contributing action or omission: Failure to promptly order blood cultures, lactate levels or imaging to identify the source of infection increases the risk of delayed diagnosis and inappropriate antimicrobial therapy.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely sepsis identification and proper diagnostic evaluation.

• Measure serum lactate when a patient presents with suspected sepsis

Check serum lactate within the first hour of sepsis recognition. If levels are over 2 mmol/L, recheck to assess resuscitation response. Elevated lactate (≥4 mmol/L) signals tissue hypoperfusion and requires immediate fluid resuscitation. Intermediate lactate elevation (>2 to <4 mmol/L) is also common in patients with sepsis; it is linked to increased mortality and may indicate the need for fluid resuscitation to avoid progression to overt hypoperfusion or shock. Consider fluid resuscitation in patients with intermediate lactate elevation, particularly if there are no contraindications to fluid administration. Tracking lactate trends helps guide resuscitation adequacy.   
• Obtain blood cultures before antimicrobial administration

Draw blood cultures promptly once sepsis is recognized, ideally prior to antimicrobial administration, but do not delay therapy for culture collection, particularly in patients with hypotension. Cultures are generally collected from two different sites using 10 mL blood volume per tube. However, emerging evidence suggests single-site collection may improve pathogen detection and reduce contamination, though multi-site collection remains standard practice. Post-antimicrobial cultures have significantly reduced yield, reinforcing the importance of pre-antimicrobial cultures when feasible.
• Order imaging to identify the source of infection based on clinical presentation

Start imaging during the initial evaluation and complete it promptly. Identifying the source enables effective interventions and guides antimicrobial selection. Missing drainable sources, such as an abscess, empyema or an infected device, is a common cause of preventable sepsis deaths. 

• Use validated screening tools rather than qSOFA alone

Screen for sepsis systematically using NEWS, MEWS or SIRS criteria rather than qSOFA as a single screening tool. Among available tools, NEWS2 has shown the best combination of sensitivity and specificity in a large cohort of over 221,000 patients. However, when paired with electronic alerts that trigger bedside clinical assessment, qSOFA has been shown to improve 90-day-in-hospital mortality and therefore can still add value as part of a broader screening and alert system. Perform screening at triage and during any clinical deterioration.  
• Consider sepsis in patients with atypical presentations

Stay alert for sepsis in patients with altered mental status, hypotension, dyspnea or acute decompensation of chronic disease (diabetic ketoacidosis, decompensated cirrhosis) even in the absence of fever or localizing signs of infection. Assess for sepsis at initial presentation and with any unexplained clinical deterioration. Sepsis is heterogeneous and can be masked by immunocompromised states or by medications like beta-blockers or antipyretics. Missing a sepsis diagnosis in atypical presentations is a recognized cause of diagnostic delay. 
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention
Contributing action or omission: Failure to initiate timely fluid resuscitation, start vasopressors for refractory hypotension or achieve adequate source control increases the risk of sepsis progression to refractory shock and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely and appropriate resuscitation and source control.

• Initiate crystalloid fluid resuscitation within 3 hours for hypoperfusion

Administer at least 30 mL/kg of IV balanced crystalloid fluid (Lactated Ringer’s solution) within 3 hours of recognizing sepsis-induced hypoperfusion or septic shock. Use actual body weight or adjusted/ideal body weight for patients with BMI >30 kg/m². Balanced crystalloids are preferred over 0.9% saline based on evidence of reduced mortality and need for renal replacement therapy. However, 0.9% saline is recommended for patients with concurrent traumatic brain injury. Guide ongoing fluid management with dynamic measures such as pulse pressure variation or passive leg raises rather than static parameters alone.   
• Initiate vasopressors promptly for fluid-refractory hypotension

When hypotension persists during or after initial fluid resuscitation, start vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥65 mm Hg. In patients with unstable septic shock, including severely reduced blood pressure, mottled skin, ashen appearance, cyanosis, tachycardia or altered mentation, consider starting vasopressors concurrently with fluids. Use norepinephrine as the first-line agent. Do not delay vasopressors for central venous access, as peripheral administration is safe and feasible. Observational data associate delayed vasopressor initiation with increased mortality, though the multicenter CLOVERS trial found no difference in 90-day mortality between early and delayed strategies.   
• Pursue source control within 6 hours when indicated

Address anatomic sources of infection, such as abscess drainage, debridement of infected tissue or removal of infected devices, as soon as possible—ideally within 6 hours of diagnosis. Begin fluids, vasopressors and antimicrobials immediately, but do not delay source control solely to achieve complete hemodynamic optimization, as prolonged efforts at medical stabilization without source control for severely ill patients are unlikely to succeed. Delayed source control is a significant contributor to preventable sepsis deaths. 

• Reassess perfusion using lactate clearance and clinical parameters

Use serial lactate measurements in conjunction with clinical assessment to guide resuscitation in patients with elevated lactate or septic shock. After the initial bolus, individualize further fluid administration based on lactate clearance ( ≥10% reduction every 2 hours) rather than continuing fluids until lactate normalizes. Where lactate measurement is unavailable, capillary refill time and urinary output may serve as alternative guides.   
• Weigh IV corticosteroids for patients with septic shock requiring ongoing vasopressor therapy
Consider IV hydrocortisone 200 mg/day (50 mg every 6 hours or continuous infusion) with or without enteral fludrocortisone 50 μg daily for adults. Corticosteroids result in greater shock reversal at 7 days and may result in a small reduction in 28-day mortality. Timing of initiation varies in practice; most SSC panelists initiate at norepinephrine doses of 0.2–0.3 μg/kg/min. 
• Facilitate ICU admission within 6 hours when indicated

Do not wait to transfer patients requiring critical care for sepsis or septic shock. Delayed ICU admission is associated with decreased bundle compliance, increased mortality and longer hospital stays. Prioritize patients with septic shock for early inpatient bed availability.   
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care
Contributing action or omission: Failing to escalate to critical care or infectious disease specialists or transfer patients to higher-level facilities when indicated increases the risk of adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate escalation and specialist involvement.

• Establish escalation triggers for critical care consultation

Define institutional protocols for escalating care to critical care services, including vasopressor requirements, need for mechanical ventilation, multi-organ dysfunction or failure to respond to initial resuscitation. Evaluate escalation needs within 1–3 hours of sepsis recognition and with any clinical deterioration. Clear escalation criteria reduce ambiguity and support timely specialist involvement.     
• Obtain infectious disease consultation for complex infections

Seek specialty input for healthcare-associated infections, immunocompromised patients, unusual pathogens, persistent bacteremia or failure to respond to empiric therapy. Request a consultation within 24–48 hours for complex cases. Infectious disease involvement supports optimal antimicrobial selection, de-escalation and duration decisions. 
• Transfer patients when local capabilities are insufficient

Arrange care at an appropriate facility if local resources cannot manage critically ill patients due to a lack of ICU capacity, surgical source control or specialized interventions. Make transfer decisions early and initiate antimicrobials, fluids and vasopressors before moving a patient. Delayed transfers from facilities without adequate resources contributes to preventable mortality.  
• Document clinical reasoning for escalation decisions

Record factors influencing escalation, consultation or transfer decisions, including severity of illness, response to initial therapy, available resources and patient/family preferences. Document decisions at the time they are made. This level of documentation demonstrates thoughtful clinical judgment and supports defensibility. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Communication failures—such as inadequate handoffs, unclear escalation pathways and incomplete information transfers during care transfers—increase the risk of delayed recognition and treatment of clinical deterioration.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support coordinated team responses.

• Implement handoff protocols at all care transitions

Use frameworks such as SBAR or I-PASS during care transitions between providers, units or facilities. Perform handoffs during shift changes, unit transfers and discharges. Communicate sepsis status, antimicrobial regimen and timing, fluid balance, vasopressor needs, pending cultures and lab trends. Communication errors are common during care transitions and delay sepsis management.  

• Establish accountability during ED boarding

Assign responsibility for patient care when admitting sepsis patients awaiting inpatient beds. Specify whether the ED or inpatient team will handle monitoring, medication administration and escalation decisions. ED boarding is linked to higher mortality in patients with severe infection.  
• Use closed-loop communication for critical orders

Verbally confirm the receipt and execution of orders for time-sensitive interventions like antimicrobials, fluid boluses and vasopressors. Perform this process in real time during acute resuscitation. Closed-loop communication reduces errors and ensures that critical interventions are completed. 

• Communicate sepsis diagnosis and treatment to primary care at discharge

Share key details with primary care, including the sepsis diagnosis, treatment, antimicrobial duration and follow-up needs. Sepsis survivors have high readmission rates (40% within 3 months) and benefit from coordinated post-discharge care. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Provider and Patient
Contributing action or omission: Failing to educate patients and families about sepsis diagnosis, warning signs and post-discharge care increases the risk of delayed recognition of complications and preventable readmissions.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients and families understand the sepsis diagnosis and follow-up needs.

• Provide sepsis education before hospital discharge

Give written and verbal education on sepsis diagnosis, treatment received and post-ICU/post-sepsis syndrome. Deliver information when patients and families are ready to process it, which may require multiple sessions. Many sepsis survivors and families report dissatisfaction with discharge education. Detailed information encourages patients to seek care for complications sooner rather than later. 

• Discuss warning signs requiring immediate medical attention

Teach patients and families to recognize symptoms such as fever, altered mental status, worsening shortness of breath, decreased urine output or new signs of infection. Discuss warning signs before discharge and reinforce them at follow-up. Early recognition of recurrent sepsis or related complications enables timely intervention. 

• Include patients and families in post-ICU and hospital discharge planning

Before discharge, engage patients and families in shared decision-making to ensure that plans are understandable and feasible. Identify barriers to follow-up such as transportation, medication access and social support needs. 

• Screen for economic and social support needs

Assess needs related to housing, nutrition, finances and spiritual support and provide referrals to patients and families. Complete this screening before discharge. Unmet social needs can impair the ability to complete antimicrobial courses, attend follow-up appointments or recognize warning signs. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of the time sepsis was recognized, bundle element completion, clinical decision-making and patient response to treatment compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and creates a defensible record.

• Document time of sepsis recognition and bundle element completion

Record the exact time sepsis or septic shock was identified and note completion times for each bundle element, including lactate measurement, blood culture collection, antimicrobial administration, initiation of fluid resuscitation and vasopressor start (if applicable). Document in real time or as soon as possible. Time-stamped records demonstrates adherence to recommended timelines and support quality reporting.      
Sample documentation language: "Sepsis recognized at 14:32 based on suspected pneumonia with lactate 4.2 mmol/L and hypotension (BP 82/54). Blood cultures obtained 14:35. Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV initiated 14:48. 30 ml/kg balanced crystalloid (lactated Ringer's) initiated, completed by 15:45. Repeat lactate 2.8 mmol/L at 16:30."

• Document clinical reasoning for antimicrobial selection

Record the suspected source of infection, likely pathogens and rationale for empiric antimicrobial choice. Document the time antimicrobials were administered. Capturing these details demonstrates clinical judgment and supports defensibility if culture results differ from empiric coverage.  
• Document response to resuscitation and reassessment findings

Record vital signs, lactate trends, urine output and clinical exam findings at regular intervals during resuscitation. Note reassessments every 1–2 hours during active resuscitation. Serial documentation demonstrates ongoing monitoring and informs escalation decisions.   

• Document goals of care discussions

Record treatment plans and end-of-life conversations for patients with sepsis or septic shock within 72 hours of ICU admission, as appropriate. Include participants in the discussion, information shared and patient and family preferences in the documentation. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign recommends early goal-of-care discussions, which demonstrate patient-centered care.  
• Include sepsis-specific information in discharge summaries

Ensure that hospital discharge summaries include the sepsis diagnosis, infection source, identified pathogens, antimicrobial regimen and duration, ICU course (if applicable) and common post-sepsis impairments. Complete discharge summaries at or before discharge. Comprehensive discharge documentation supports continuity of care and reduces readmission risk. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS
Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care
Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify and address barriers to medication adherence, follow-up appointments and recognition of warning signs increases the likelihood of preventable readmissions and delayed treatment of recurrent sepsis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient engagement and address barriers to post-discharge care.

• Reconcile medications at ICU and hospital discharge

Explain to patients medications to continue, stop or start. Perform medication reconciliation at every transition, as sepsis survivors often undergo medication changes during hospitalization, and errors during transitions contribute to adverse events. 

• Arrange timely follow-up

Schedule post-discharge appointments with primary care or specialists before patients leave the hospital. Follow up with high-risk patients within 7–14 days of discharge. Nearly 40% of sepsis survivors are rehospitalized within 3 months, often for preventable conditions. Prompt post-discharge care helps identify new impairments and allows for medication adjustments. 

• Connect patients to post-critical illness programs when available
Refer sepsis survivors with ICU stays longer than 72 hours or mechanical ventilation longer than 48 hours to post-critical illness follow-up or post-hospital rehabilitation programs before discharge. These programs screen for and address physical, cognitive and emotional problems common after sepsis. 

• Explore telehealth-based transition support for high-risk patients

Consider offering proactive telehealth-based transition and recovery support that provides regular contact, medication review and complication surveillance for high-risk sepsis patients. Initiate support within 48 hours of discharge and continue for 30–90 days. Early trial evidence suggests that the STAR telehealth nurse navigation program reduces readmission and mortality after sepsis, though a larger multicenter trial did not confirm a reduction in the composite of readmission and mortality at 90 days. 
• Document patient non-adherence and outreach efforts

Record instances when patients miss follow-up appointments or decline recommended care and include all outreach attempts and their response. If patients decline evaluation despite concerning symptoms, document the refusal and discussion of recommendations and risks.

TITLE: Anticoagulation
RISK_BRIEF:
Anticoagulant Management

SPECIALTY: Neurology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Ischemic Stroke, Transient Ischemic Attack, Atrial Fibrillation, Cerebral Venous Sinus Thrombosis

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Intracranial Hemorrhage, Recurrent Stroke, Medication Error, Patient Harm

Anticoagulant management is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in neurology.
Mitigating Your Risk
With accurate stoke etiology identification, evidence-based anticoagulant selection and vigilant post-treatment monitoring, neurologists can mitigate the risks associated with anticoagulant management and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for anticoagulant management and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving anticoagulant management typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 83% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 17%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Anticoagulant Management Risk [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act On Diagnostic Finding

Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify atrial fibrillation as the cause of stroke, correctly interpret infarct size affecting anticoagulation timing or recognize contraindications to anticoagulation increases the risk of recurrent stroke or hemorrhagic complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure accurate diagnosis of the cause of stroke and appropriate risk stratification for anticoagulation decisions.

• Pursue prolonged cardiac monitoring in cryptogenic stroke

For patients with ischemic stroke of undetermined source, use prolonged external or implantable cardiac monitoring instead of 24-hour monitoring. Prolonged monitoring detects atrial fibrillation in a significant proportion of patients, enabling a shift from antiplatelet to anticoagulant therapy and reducing recurrent stroke risk. Document monitoring duration and results to support defensible decision-making.

• Assess infarct size using neuroimaging to guide anticoagulation timing

Evaluate stroke severity using clinical scales (NIHSS) and neuroimaging findings before initiating anticoagulation. Larger infarcts carry a higher hemorrhagic risk. Document the rationale for timing decisions based on infarct characteristics.

• Calculate and document stroke and bleeding risk scores

Use validated risk stratification tools (CHA₂DS₂-VASc for stroke risk, HAS-BLED for bleeding risk) when making decisions about anticoagulation. Anticoagulation is recommended regardless of other factors for patients who have experienced a previous stroke or TIA. Document scores and the clinical reasoning supporting the treatment decision to demonstrate adherence to evidence-based practice.

• Identify cerebral small vessel disease markers that modify bleeding risk

Review neuroimaging for small vessel disease markers like leukoaraiosis, cerebral microbleeds and atrophy. Moderate-to-severe small vessel disease is independently associated with increased intracranial hemorrhage risk in patients taking anticoagulants. Use those findings to guide shared decision-making and consider closer monitoring or alternatives such as left atrial appendage closure.

CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Medication Error

Contributing action or omission: Incorrect anticoagulant selection, inappropriate dosing for renal function or body weight, failure to recognize drug interactions or prescribing DOACs when warfarin is indicated increase the risk of thromboembolic or hemorrhagic complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate anticoagulant selection and dosing while minimizing medication errors.

• Implement standardized order sets and clinical decision support

Use pre-built, evidence-based order sets with renal function checks, weight-based dosing calculations and drug interaction alerts. Prescribing errors, often due to overlooked renal function, account for a majority of all DOAC medication errors. Automated alerts help prevent those errors.

• Verify anticoagulant reconciliation at care transitions

Confirm anticoagulant name, dose and timing at admission, transfer and discharge. Incorrect administration rates or frequency is the most common anticoagulation-associated medication error. Use structured medication reconciliation processes and document verification in the medical record.

• Select anticoagulant based on valve status and renal function

Prioritize DOACs over warfarin for nonvalvular atrial fibrillation; they are associated with lower rates of stroke, systemic embolism and hemorrhagic stroke compared with warfarin. Reserve warfarin for patients with moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis (including rheumatic) or mechanical heart valves, as DOACs are contraindicated in these populations. Document the indication and rationale for anticoagulation selection.

• Adjust DOAC dosing based on renal function and patient characteristics

Verify creatinine clearance before prescribing and at regular intervals. Each DOAC has agent-specific dose reduction criteria based on renal function, age and body weight. Consult the current FDA-approved prescribing information for the selected agent to confirm the appropriate dose before prescribing. Use clinical decision support tools and standardized order sets that incorporate renal dosing checks to reduce the risk of error. Incorrect dosing is the most common prescribing error with DOACs.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Poor anticoagulation monitoring, missing signs of bleeding complications or lack of surveillance for recurrent thromboembolic events increases the risk of adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate monitoring and early detection of complications.

• Establish monitoring protocols based on anticoagulant type

For warfarin, maintain INR 2.0-3.0 with regular monitoring. Subtherapeutic anticoagulation (INR <2.0) at stroke onset is associated with more severe strokes and worse functional outcomes compared with therapeutic INR levels. Consistent INR monitoring is essential to minimize time spent below the therapeutic range, during which stroke protection is sharply reduced. While DOACs do not require routine coagulation monitoring, regularly assess renal and liver function. Document monitoring frequency and results.

• Implement structured follow-up after anticoagulation initiation

Arrange follow-up within 1–2 weeks to evaluate tolerance, adherence and early complications. For patients on warfarin, schedule INR monitoring before discharge. Document the follow-up plan and confirm patient understanding of when to seek care.

• Educate patients on warning signs of bleeding complications

Provide clear verbal and written instructions on symptoms needing immediate attention: severe headache, vision changes, unusual bruising or bleeding, blood in urine or stool, and prolonged bleeding from cuts. Document patient education and stated understanding. Urgently evaluate any new neurological symptoms in anticoagulation patients.

• Monitor for early signs of hemorrhagic transformation after stroke

After starting patients on anticoagulation post-ischemic stroke, closely monitor them for neurological decline, which may signal bleeding. While the risk of symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage with early DOAC use is low, it remains clinically significant and warrants vigilance. Establish protocols for repeat neuroimaging if clinical status changes.

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Poor handoffs, unclear anticoagulation timing or lack of coordination with cardiology and primary care for long-term management of anticoagulation increase the risk of treatment gaps or errors.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and enable coordinated care across the continuum.

• Use structured handoffs for anticoagulated patients

Use standardized handoff frameworks such as SBAR when transferring care between settings or providers. Communicate current anticoagulant, dose and timing, indication, target INR (if applicable), recent laboratory values and any bleeding or thromboembolic events. Document handoff details in the medical record.

• Define escalation triggers for anticoagulation emergencies

Define and communicate signs of potential intracranial hemorrhage, including new severe headache, acute neurological deficit or altered mental status, in anticoagulated patients. Ensure that all team members know the location of reversal agents and the process for treating emergencies. 

• Coordinate with cardiology for atrial fibrillation management

Communicate with cardiology regarding rhythm control strategies, rate control and consideration of left atrial appendage closure for patients with high bleeding risk. The Watchman device may be appropriate for patients with contraindications to long-term anticoagulation. Document interdisciplinary discussions and shared decision-making.

• Notify primary care providers when patients require long-term anticoagulation

Communicate the anticoagulation plan, monitoring requirements and follow-up needs to the patient's primary care provider before discharge. Include specific instructions for INR monitoring (warfarin) or renal function surveillance (DOACs). Document communication in the discharge summary. 


ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Poor documentation of anticoagulation rationale, patient education or monitoring and dose adjustments undermines continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document the rationale for anticoagulant selection and dose

Record the indication for anticoagulation, chosen medication, dose and clinical factors (renal function, body weight, valve status, drug interactions) considered. This documentation shows adherence to evidence-based prescribing and supports defensibility if complications occur.

• Document clinical reasoning for anticoagulation timing decisions

Record the rationale for timing of anticoagulation initiation, including stroke severity assessment (NIHSS score), infarct size on imaging, presence or absence of hemorrhagic transformation and patient-specific risk factors. Documentation should reflect individualized assessment and the clinical reasoning supporting the chosen timing. 

Sample documentation language: "Patient with moderate ischemic stroke (NIHSS 8) and newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation. CT head shows no hemorrhagic transformation. CHA₂DS₂-VASc score 5. Given moderate infarct size without hemorrhage, plan to initiate apixaban 5 mg BID on hospital day 4, consistent with ELAN trial protocol and recent CATALYST meta-analysis supporting early initiation."

• Document patient education about anticoagulation therapy

Record patient education about the purpose of anticoagulation, importance of adherence, signs of bleeding requiring immediate evaluation, dietary considerations (with warfarin) and drug interactions to avoid. Document the patient's stated understanding and any barriers to adherence. 

• Document the monitoring plan and follow-up arrangements

Record the plan for anticoagulation monitoring, including INR frequency (warfarin), renal function surveillance (DOACs), scheduled follow-up appointments and contact information for questions or concerns. Document dose adjustments and the rationale for them.


PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate patient education or failure to follow up or address barriers with patients who may not follow medication routines increases the likelihood of recurrent stroke or unmonitored bleeding complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and address barriers to safe anticoagulation therapy.

• Assess and address barriers to medication adherence

Identify barriers including cost, cognitive impairment, polypharmacy, lack of understanding and difficulty with dosing schedules. DOACs may improve adherence compared to warfarin due to fixed dosing and lack of dietary restrictions, but twice-daily dosing (apixaban, dabigatran) may be challenging for some patients. Consider once-daily options (rivaroxaban, edoxaban) when adherence is a concern.

• Provide written instructions in the patient's primary language

Name the anticoagulant, dose, timing and purpose in discharge instructions. Include information on symptoms requiring emergency evaluation and contact information for questions. Ensure that instructions are provided at an appropriate literacy level and in the patient's primary language.

• Implement reminder systems and support

Consider pill organizers, medication reminder apps, or pharmacy synchronization programs for patients at risk of non-adherence. Ensure reliable transportation to INR monitoring appointments for warfarin patients or arrange home monitoring when available.

• Document non-adherence and continued outreach efforts

When patients miss follow-up appointments or report non-adherence, document the conversation, recommendations made, risks explained and patient's stated reasons for non-adherence. Continue outreach by phone, patient portal or mail. If a patient declines anticoagulation despite clear reasons for it, document the discussion, which should include information about the risks of recurrent stroke.
OVERVIEW:
Anticoagulant Management

SPECIALTY: Neurology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Ischemic Stroke, Transient Ischemic Attack, Atrial Fibrillation, Cerebral Venous Sinus Thrombosis

TITLE: Brain tumors
RISK_BRIEF:
Brain Tumors


SPECIALTY: Neurology/Neuro-Oncology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Primary Brain Tumors, Brain Metastases, Leptomeningeal Disease

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Delayed Diagnosis, Neurologic Deterioration, Treatment-Related Complications, Death

Brain tumors are a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in neurology.
Mitigating Your Risk
With timely neuroimaging, safe medication management, prompt specialist referral and coordinated multidisciplinary care, neurologists and neuro-oncologists can mitigate the risks associated with brain tumors and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for brain tumors and provides strategies to help prevent them.
This document addresses risk mitigation for neurologists involved in the diagnosis and initial evaluation of brain tumors, as well as neuro-oncologists and neurologists who participate in ongoing treatment and surveillance.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving brain tumors typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 87% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 13%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Brain Tumor Risk [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failure to obtain appropriate neuroimaging in patients with concerning symptoms delays diagnosis and increases the risk of neurologic deterioration.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely identification of brain tumors and appropriate escalation to specialist care.

• Obtain contrast-enhanced brain MRI for patients with red-flag headache features

For patients presenting with headache features concerning for secondary causes, perform contrast-enhanced brain MRI. The SNNOOP10 mnemonic provides a systematic framework for identifying red flags warranting neuroimaging, including: new onset headaches in patients aged ≥50 (concern for neoplasm or giant cell arteritis), sudden/thunderclap onset, positional headaches, headaches worsening with exertion, progressive or atypical presentations, papilledema, neurologic deficit or history of neoplasm. MRI with gadolinium-based contrast is the standard imaging method for evaluating suspected brain tumors and should be performed following a standardized brain tumor imaging protocol. Document the clinical reasoning for the decision to image or not image.
 • Ensure appropriate neuroimaging following new-onset seizures

For adult patients presenting with new-onset seizures, particularly those over age 25 without a clear precipitant, obtain brain MRI. CT is typically the first imaging modality in the emergency setting, but CT may miss lesions such as low-grade gliomas, hippocampal sclerosis and cortical dysplasia. An epilepsy protocol-specific brain MRI (thin slices, coronal FLAIR sequences) should follow as part of the standard outpatient workup. When there is clinical suspicion for a brain tumor—such as focal neurologic findings, progressive symptoms or abnormalities on initial imaging—obtain contrast-enhanced MRI with gadolinium.

• Recognize atypical presentations that warrant imaging

Consider neuroimaging in patients presenting with subtle cognitive changes, behavioral alterations, personality changes or progressive memory complaints, as these may be harbingers of brain tumors, particularly primary CNS lymphoma or frontal lobe tumors. Note that memory and headache-only complaints are associated with a higher number of avoidable diagnostic and referral delays. 
• Ensure appropriate imaging technique and interpretation

When ordering brain MRI for suspected tumor, specify contrast-enhanced study with and without gadolinium. Review imaging results promptly and ensure that radiologist interpretation addresses the clinical question. If initial imaging is equivocal or symptoms persist despite negative imaging, consider repeat imaging or advanced techniques (MR perfusion, MR spectroscopy). Document review of imaging results and communication with the patient. 

CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Medication Error

Contributing action or omission: Errors in neuro-oncology medication management, including inappropriate timing of corticosteroid administration and chemotherapy dosing errors, can result in compromised diagnostic yield, fatal myelosuppression or other serious adverse events.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to prevent medication errors and ensure safe administration of neuro-oncology therapies.

• Avoid corticosteroids before biopsy when primary CNS lymphoma is suspected
When imaging findings suggest primary CNS lymphoma, hold corticosteroids prior to biopsy whenever clinically safe, as recommended by NCCN. Corticosteroids can cause rapid tumor regression and render biopsy non-diagnostic. If steroids are clinically necessary (such as in cases of impending herniation), document the reasoning and expedite biopsy. If biopsy is non-diagnostic after steroid exposure, consider rebiopsy after steroid washout when disease progresses on follow-up imaging.
• Confirm temozolomide dosing and monitoring for each treatment phase

Verify the treatment phase (concomitant vs. maintenance) and calculate the correct dose with each prescription. During the concomitant phase, obtain CBC weekly and provide PCP prophylaxis; continue PCP prophylaxis in patients who develop lymphopenia until resolution to Grade 1 or less. During the maintenance phase, obtain CBC on day 1 and day 22 of each cycle. Perform liver function tests at baseline, midway through cycle 1, before each subsequent cycle, and 2–4 weeks after the last dose. Implement pharmacy verification protocols and educate patients on proper dosing.
• Monitor patients for chemotherapy-induced neurotoxicity
Track CNS toxicity, including cognitive dysfunction, encephalopathy and leukoencephalopathy, in those receiving high-dose methotrexate, cytarabine, PCV chemotherapy or temozolomide. Methotrexate may cause acute encephalopathy with seizures and confusion or chronic leukoencephalopathy mimicking radiation injury. Document baseline cognitive status and watch for changes during treatment, recognizing that cognitive dysfunction can resemble tumor progression.

• Educate patients on medication administration and warning signs

Provide written and verbal instructions on correct medication administration, including timing, food interactions and handling a missed dose. Ensure that patients understand warning signs requiring immediate evaluation, including fever, unusual bleeding or bruising, severe fatigue or neurologic changes. Document patient education and stated understanding. 

• Implement systems safeguards for high-risk neuro-oncology medications

Use pre-built EHR order sets that include appropriate dosing, monitoring parameters and supportive care.  Consider requiring pharmacist verification of all chemotherapy orders. Consider using a single pharmacy for chemotherapy dispensing to reduce fragmentation errors.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Consult or Transfer Care

Contributing action or omission: Failure to refer patients to experienced neuro-oncology centers or obtain appropriate subspecialty consultation delays optimal treatment and increases the risk of poor outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients receive appropriate multidisciplinary care from experienced practitioners.

• Refer patients with suspected or confirmed CNS tumors to experienced practitioners

Patients with a tumor of the brain, spinal cord or related structures should be referred to practitioners experienced in the diagnosis and management of these lesions, as recommended by NCCN. Make referrals promptly after imaging suggests or confirms a brain tumor and before making definitive treatment decisions. Document the referral, clinical urgency, and communication with the receiving team.
• Ensure timely neurosurgical evaluation for symptomatic patients

Arrange for urgent neurosurgical evaluation for patients with signs of increased intracranial pressure, progressive neurologic deficits or large tumors with mass effect. Do not delay referral for additional outpatient workup when acute intervention may be needed. Document the urgency of referral and communication with the receiving team.

• Implement multidisciplinary care models for brain tumors

Promote collaboration among cancer-related specialties through brain tumor boards or multidisciplinary clinic models, as strongly recommended by NCCN. Specialties to involve include surgery, pathology, radiology, radiation oncology and medical oncology. Document tumor board recommendations and the rationale behind treatment decisions.

• Ensure that patients are informed about clinical trial availability

Ensure that patients are informed about the potential availability of clinical trials as part of their treatment planning. For neurologists referring patients to neuro-oncology centers, communicate that clinical trial options should be discussed with the treating team. For neuro-oncologists managing treatment, discuss specific local, regional, and national trial options with eligible patients. Document the discussion of clinical trials with the patient. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate communication about diagnosis, prognosis, treatment options and warning signs increases the risk of patient harm and contributes to malpractice claims.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients and families receive clear, comprehensive information and understand their care plan.

• Discuss diagnosis and prognosis in a patient-centered manner

Provide patients with resources and discuss care options in a clear, culturally, and educationally sensitive way. Involve the patient’s chosen support person(s) when possible. Avoid vague promises; be concrete about what is known and unknown. Document patient education and stated understanding and individuals present during the discussion.

• Educate patients about treatment side effects and warning signs

Provide clear information about expected treatment effects, potential complications and symptoms requiring immediate evaluation. For patients receiving radiation therapy, explain the possibility of pseudoprogression and the potential need for additional testing to interpret follow-up scans. Document patient education.

• Establish a clear point of contact for follow-up questions

As treatment proceeds, identify one practitioner as the main point of contact for follow-up questions and referrals to specialists. This helps prevent the communication breakdowns that can occur with multiple providers. Document the designated contact’s name and communicate that information to the patient.

• Provide information to patients and families throughout the illness
Proactively assess patient preferences for the amount and detail of prognostic information they wish to receive, as many patients want more specific information about survival, disease trajectory, and expected functional decline than clinicians typically offer. Create opportunities for patients to ask questions and express their information needs. Tailor discussions to match individual preferences rather than assuming a standard level of disclosure is appropriate for all patients. Document ongoing communication and patient preferences. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUES

Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure

Contributing action or omission: Lack of protocols for imaging surveillance, treatment pathways and care coordination undermines consistent care delivery and increases the risk of missed diagnoses or delayed treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve readiness, standardize response and enable coordinated care.

• Follow imaging surveillance protocols

Implement imaging surveillance protocols based on tumor type, grade and treatment received. NCCN provides specific surveillance intervals but also states that the treating physician be given the flexibility to determine the frequency of imaging that is appropriate for specific patients. Document adherence to surveillance protocols and ensure that systems are in place to track scheduled imaging and follow-up.

• Create EHR-based clinical pathways and order sets

Develop pre-built, evidence-based order sets for brain tumor management, including imaging protocols, chemotherapy regimens, supportive care and monitoring parameters. Implement automated alerts for critical imaging results or laboratory abnormalities. Ensure that pathways align with NCCN guidelines and are regularly updated. 

• Establish reliable systems for result notification and follow-up

Implement systems to ensure that imaging results are promptly reviewed and discussed with patients. Track pending results to reduce the chance that critical finding will be missed. Document imaging results and patient notification. 

• Conduct regular case reviews and quality improvement activities

Review cases with diagnostic delays, treatment complications or unexpected outcomes to identify system vulnerabilities. Use structured debriefing frameworks to analyze contributing factors and implement improvements. Track performance metrics related to timely diagnosis, treatment initiation and adherence to surveillance.

ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR

Failure to Recognize Responsibility

Contributing action or omission: Failing to take ownership of diagnostic evaluation, treatment coordination or follow-up care increases the risk of fragmented care and missed opportunities for intervention.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure clear accountability and prevent gaps in care.

• Clarify responsibility for diagnostic workup and referral

When evaluating patients with symptoms potentially attributable to brain tumors, take responsibility for obtaining appropriate imaging and acting on results. Do not assume another provider will order indicated testing or follow up on abnormal results. Document the plan for workup and who is responsible for each component.

• Ensure successful handoffs when transferring care

When referring patients to specialists or transferring care between settings, use structured handoff communication that includes the clinical question, relevant history, imaging findings, pending results and follow-up needs. Communication failures, including handoff breakdowns, are a common contributor to adverse outcomes. Document handoff details and confirm that the new provider received them. 

• Maintain engagement during multidisciplinary care

Even when patients are receiving care from multiple specialists, maintain awareness of the overall treatment plan and clinical status. For example, attend or review tumor board discussions when possible. Do not assume that another provider is addressing a clinical concern. Ask for confirmation. Communicate the role of each team member to patients and provide a clear point of contact.
• Document clinical reasoning for management decisions

For complex decisions such as observation versus treatment, intervention timing or therapy choice, document the clinical reasoning, including factors and alternatives considered and the rationale for the chosen approach. This documentation demonstrates thoughtful clinical judgment and supports defensibility in case of adverse outcomes.

TITLE: CVA
RISK_BRIEF:
Cerebrovascular Accident (CVA)

SPECIALTY: Neurology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Acute Neurologic Deficit, Suspected Stroke

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Permanent Neurologic Disability, Missed Treatment Window, Death
Cerebrovascular accident (CVA) is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in neurology.
Mitigating Your Risk
With rapid neuroimaging, timely reperfusion therapy, prompt escalation and coordinated team communication, neurologists can mitigate the risks associated with CVAs and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for CVAs and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving CVAs typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 90% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 10%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving CVA Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain timely neuroimaging or vascular imaging delays diagnosis and precludes time-sensitive interventions, increasing the risk of permanent disability and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure rapid completion and interpretation of appropriate imaging studies to support treatment decisions.

• Obtain non-contrast head CT as rapidly as possible

Immediately perform a non-contrast head CT for suspected stroke to exclude intracranial hemorrhage. The target time should be ≤20 minutes of patient arrival; the maximum time should be ≤25 minutes. 2026 AHA/ASA guideline confirms that door-to-imaging times of ≤25 minutes are achievable across hospital settings. AHA Target: Stroke Phase III recommends targeting ≤20 minutes to support a 45-minute DTN goal. Non-contrast CT is sufficient neuroimaging for decisions about IV thrombolysis in most patients.

• Obtain CT angiography for patients with suspected large vessel occlusion

For patients with severe deficits (NIHSS ≥6) or symptoms suggesting large vessel occlusion, perform CT angiography of the head and neck to locate occlusions and determine eligibility for mechanical thrombectomy. CTA is 92%–100% sensitive and 82%–100% specific for large proximal cerebral artery occlusions. For patients with relatively minor symptoms (NIHSS <6), early vascular imaging is still reasonable, as a proportion of these patients may have an LVO with subsequent clinical deterioration. In patients without known renal dysfunction, CTA can be performed before serum creatinine results. Do not delay thrombolysis for vascular imaging if the patient is otherwise eligible.

• Use MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging for suspected TIA or minor stroke

For transient or resolving symptoms, perform MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging within 24 hours of symptom onset. DWI is 88% sensitive and 95% specific for acute infarction, identifying lesions in about 40% of TIA cases. DWI positivity is associated with a more than 6-fold increased risk of recurrent stroke at 1 year. If MRI is unavailable, perform CT first and arrange timely outpatient MRI.

• Obtain advanced imaging for extended window thrombectomy candidates

For patients presenting 6–24 hours after symptom onset with suspected large vessel occlusion, obtain imaging to assess eligibility for mechanical thrombectomy. Multiple imaging selection approaches are supported by the 2026 AHA/ASA guideline, including CT perfusion, MRI perfusion, NCCT with CTA (including collateral assessment) and MRI DWI-FLAIR mismatch. Perfusion imaging with automated postprocessing software identifies salvageable tissue and supports patient selection but is not the only acceptable approach. Document the rationale for imaging selection and interpretation.



CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Delays in initiating thrombolysis, failure to consider mechanical thrombectomy and inappropriate patient selection increase the risk of preventable disability and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely and appropriate administration of reperfusion therapies.

• Administer intravenous thrombolysis as rapidly as possible 

For eligible patients presenting within 4.5 hours of symptom onset, administer tenecteplase (0.25 mg/kg, maximum 25 mg) or alteplase (0.9 mg/kg, maximum 90 mg) as quickly as possible. The target for door-to-needle time is ≤ 45 minutes of arrival, with a maximum of ≤ 60 minutes. Tenecteplase offers the practical advantage of single-bolus administration and is FDA-approved for acute ischemic stroke (AIS). Document the time the patient was last known to be well, time of imaging and time of treatment initiation.
• Consider IV thrombolysis for patients presenting beyond 4.5 hours or with wake-up stroke
Consider IV thrombolysis as reasonable (Class 2a) for 1) patients presenting 4.5–9 hours from last known well or 2) presenting with a wake-up stroke within 9 hours from sleep midpoint with salvageable tissue on perfusion imaging or DWI-FLAIR mismatch. This requires automated perfusion-imaging capability. For patients with LVO at 4.5–24 hours who cannot receive or will have delayed thrombectomy, extended-window thrombolysis may also be considered based on TRACE-III and HOPE trial data, though evidence is more limited when thrombectomy is readily available. Document imaging selection criteria and clinical rationale.
• Do not delay thrombolysis to observe for clinical improvement

For patients who remain moderately impaired or potentially disabled, administer thrombolysis promptly; delaying treatment to monitor for further improvement is not recommended. If symptoms have fully resolved to a nondisabling state, thrombolysis may be reasonably withheld. Document the clinical rationale for any decision to treat or defer.
• Initiate mechanical thrombectomy within 6 hours for eligible patients with large vessel occlusion

For eligible patients with anterior circulation LVO (ICA or M1), target groin puncture within 6 hours of symptom onset. Administer IV thrombolysis first for patients eligible for both treatments; do not delay transfer for thrombectomy. Document time of last known well, imaging findings, NIHSS, ASPECTS and pre-stroke functional status.
• Apply extended window criteria for patients presenting 6–24 hours after onset

For patients with anterior circulation LVO presenting 6–24 hours after symptom onset or last known well, use advanced imaging to identify candidates for mechanical thrombectomy. Multiple imaging selection approaches are supported, including perfusion imaging, NCCT with CTA (including collateral assessment), and MRI DWI-FLAIR mismatch; apply current AHA/ASA guideline criteria for patient selection. Eligibility extends to patients with large ischemic cores in appropriate clinical context. Document imaging findings and the clinical rationale supporting or excluding eligibility.

• Consider mechanical thrombectomy for eligible patients with basilar artery occlusion
For patients with acute ischemic stroke due to basilar artery occlusion, NIHSS ≥10, pre-stroke mRS 0–1, and PC-ASPECTS ≥6, perform EVT within 24 hours of symptom onset. The strength of evidence is strongest within 12 hours of onset. The benefit of EVT remains uncertain for patients presenting with NIHSS <10; document the clinical rationale for treatment decisions in these cases.
• Initiate dual antiplatelet therapy for minor stroke or high-risk TIA

For patients with nondisabling, non-cardioembolic acute ischemic stroke (NIHSS ≤5) or high-risk TIA (ABCD2 ≥4) who are not candidates for thrombolysis, thrombectomy or carotid revascularization, initiate DAPT within 24 hours of symptom onset. Use aspirin plus clopidogrel for 21 days, or aspirin plus ticagrelor for 21–30 days, followed by antiplatelet monotherapy. For patients with known CYP2C19 loss-of-function alleles, ticagrelor-based DAPT is preferred over clopidogrel-based DAPT. Confirm the absence of atrial fibrillation before initiating DAPT. 
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Consult or Transfer Care

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain timely neurology consultation or transfer patients to a higher level of care delays definitive treatment and increases the risk of adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate consultation and transfer for patients requiring specialized stroke care.

• Activate stroke code and neurology consultation immediately upon suspected stroke

Activate the stroke team immediately upon EMS prenotification or triage identification of suspected stroke. Stroke team activation should precede the full history and physical exam. Document the times of stroke code activation and neurology notification.

• Use telestroke consultation when on-site neurology is unavailable

For hospitals without 24/7 neurology coverage, establish telestroke relationships with comprehensive stroke centers to guide treatment decisions. Track time to telestroke consultation as a quality metric; a benchmark of 20 minutes from request to consultation has been proposed. Document consultation findings, recommendations and treatment decisions in the medical record.

• Transfer patients with large vessel occlusion to thrombectomy-capable centers

Develop protocols for rapid identification and transfer of patients with suspected or confirmed large vessel occlusion to thrombectomy-capable or comprehensive stroke centers. The 2026 AHA/ASA guideline recommends a DIDO time of no more than 90 minutes; shorter times are associated with progressively better functional outcomes and higher likelihood of receiving EVT. Notify the receiving facility before transfer and share imaging findings, treatments and clinical status.

• Transfer complex stroke patients to higher-level care

Transfer patients with large-territory ischemic stroke, hemorrhagic stroke, rare etiologies or complex comorbidities to comprehensive stroke centers with neurocritical care, neurosurgical services and advanced diagnostics. Document the clinical rationale for the required services and transfer.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Communication failures—including inadequate handoffs, unclear escalation pathways and delayed notification of receiving facilities—increase the risk of treatment delays and clinical errors.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and enable coordinated team response.

• Implement structured handoff protocols for stroke patients

Use standardized handoff frameworks such as SBAR for transitions between EMS and emergency department, emergency department and stroke team, and sending and receiving facilities. Communicate key details, including the time the patient was last known to be well, current NIHSS score, imaging findings, treatments and pending interventions. Document handoff details in the medical record.

• Ensure EMS prenotification for suspected stroke

Establish protocols requiring EMS to notify receiving hospitals of suspected stroke patients. Such notification allows early mobilization of stroke resources and reduces door-to-imaging and door-to-treatment times. Track prenotification rates as a quality metric.

• Establish clear escalation triggers and team roles

Define protocols for escalating care during neurologic deterioration, hemorrhagic conversion or failed recanalization and determining who is responsible for initiating escalation. Assign a team leader during stroke codes to coordinate care and ensure accountability. Use closed-loop communication to confirm orders and verify medication administration.

• Communicate imaging findings and treatment decisions in real time

Directly and promptly communicate imaging interpretations to the treating physician and document them in the medical record. For telestroke consultations, record AI notifications and messaging communications in the EHR. Document the time of imaging interpretation and clinical decisions based on findings. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify and address barriers to timely presentation, treatment acceptance and adherence to secondary prevention measures increases the likelihood of adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and address barriers to timely care.

• Initiate secondary prevention before discharge

Ensure that patients with ischemic stroke or TIA receive appropriate secondary prevention therapy before discharge, including: antiplatelet agents (or anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation), high-intensity statin therapy targeting LDL cholesterol <70 mg/dL, and antihypertensive therapy targeting blood pressure <130/80 mmHg. Provide clear written instructions about medications, follow-up appointments and warning signs requiring emergency evaluation. Document the discharge plan and patient understanding.

• Educate patients and families about stroke warning signs

Provide information about stroke symptoms (sudden numbness or weakness, confusion, trouble speaking, vision changes, severe headache, dizziness, loss of balance) and the importance of calling 911 immediately. Emphasize that time-sensitive treatments are only available within narrow windows. Document patient education in the medical record.

• Address barriers to treatment acceptance

When patients or families express hesitation about thrombolysis or thrombectomy, provide clear information about the risks and benefits of treatment and the consequences of non-treatment. Use shared decision-making and document the discussion, including the patient's stated understanding and decision. If a legally authorized representative is not available and the patient cannot consent, proceed under emergency consent for interventions consistent with the standard of care.

• Document patient non-adherence and continued outreach

When patients decline recommended treatment, document the conversation, recommendations made, risks explained and patient's stated reasons for declining. If patients fail to follow up, continue outreach by phone, patient portal or mail.If a patient declines evaluation despite concerning symptoms, document their informed refusal.
