TITLE: Bowel Related Injuries
RISK_BRIEF:
Bowel Injuries in OB/GYN
SPECIALTY:  OB/GYN

PRESENTING CONDITION(S):  Gynecologic Surgery

ADVERSE OUTCOME:  Bowel Injury

Bowel injuries are a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in OB/GYN. 
Mitigating Your Risk
With careful preoperative planning, meticulous surgical technique, vigilant postoperative monitoring and timely escalation to appropriate surgical expertise, OB/GYNs can mitigate the risks associated with bowel injuries and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for bowel injuries in OB/GYN and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving bowel injuries in OB/GYN typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 85% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 15%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.   

Actions and Omissions Driving Bowel Injuries in OB/GYN Risk [insert chart] 
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act on Diagnostic Finding
Contributing action or omission: Failure to recognize bowel injury during surgery or to identify postoperative signs of it increases the risk of complications and adverse outcomes.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve intraoperative recognition of bowel injury and postoperative detection if injury is missed during surgery.
● Perform systematic inspection of the bowel before closure
Inspect all bowel surfaces near the operative field, including areas of adhesiolysis, trocar insertion sites and tissue adjacent to electrosurgical dissection, before concluding any gynecologic procedure. Perform this inspection at the end of every case, regardless of perceived complexity. Systematic inspection is the primary mechanism for converting unrecognized injuries into intraoperatively managed ones, eliminating the mortality risk associated with delayed diagnosis. Delayed diagnosis beyond 24 hours occurs in 41% of laparoscopic bowel injuries and is associated with all reported mortalities.
When there is any suspicion of rectal or sigmoid injury, perform an air-leak test (insufflation of air via a rigid proctoscope with the pelvis submerged in irrigation fluid) to confirm or exclude full-thickness injury. 
● Maintain a high index of suspicion for delayed bowel injury in the postoperative period
Evaluate any patient for bowel injury within the first 72–96 hours after gynecologic surgery if they present with persistent or worsening abdominal pain (particularly focal trocar-site pain), abdominal distention, fever, tachycardia, nausea, vomiting or diarrhea. Thermal injuries from electrosurgery may not appear for 3–7 days postoperatively as tissue necrosis evolves. Keeping a high index of suspicion prevents the most common failure mode in bowel injury claims: dismissing early warning signs as expected postoperative discomfort.
● Use imaging judiciously, but do not rely on it exclusively in high-suspicion cases

When clinical suspicion for postoperative bowel injury is present, obtain CT imaging of the abdomen and pelvis with oral and IV contrast. CT sensitivity is imperfect; in high-risk patients, CT results were negative in 3 out of 13 who were subsequently confirmed to have serious bowel complications (anastomotic leak, bowel perforation). If clinical suspicion is high despite negative imaging, strongly consider proceeding with surgical exploration, as observation alone may delay diagnosis of a life-threatening complication.
● Educate patients on warning signs before discharge
Give patients written and verbal instructions on symptoms that require immediate evaluation after any gynecologic procedure. These include worsening abdominal pain (especially focal pain at a trocar site), fever, inability to tolerate oral intake, abdominal distention and diarrhea with leukopenia. Early patient presentation allows for earlier diagnosis. Documenting discharge education also supports defensibility.


CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Consult or Transfer Care
Contributing action or omission: Failing to involve appropriate surgical consultants when bowel injury is identified, or when the primary surgeon lacks expertise to perform repairs, increases the risk of inadequate repair, postoperative complications and adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate and timely specialist involvement when bowel injury occurs or is suspected.
● Establish a preoperative plan for consultant availability in high-risk cases

Confirm the availability of a general or colorectal surgeon who can be called during surgery if needed before operating on patients with known risk factors for bowel injury (such as endometriosis, extensive adhesive disease, prior bowel surgery or gynecologic malignancy). 
Facilities without on-site general surgery coverage, including ambulatory surgery centers, critical access hospitals or community hospitals without surgical call panels, should establish a written transfer agreement with a facility that has general or colorectal surgery capability before scheduling high-risk cases. The agreement should include a direct physician-to-physician communication pathway and a plan for emergent patient transfer. For patients with the highest risk profiles (deep infiltrating endometriosis, extensive adhesive disease), consider whether the case should be performed at a facility with on-site surgical backup or with a prearranged multidisciplinary team.
Document the plan. Preoperative planning ensures that response to a bowel injury is not delayed by logistical gaps and demonstrates proactive risk management. 
● Involve a consultant when injury complexity exceeds the primary surgeon's expertise

Assess the location, extent and mechanism of injury if a bowel injury is identified during surgery. Request an intraoperative consultation with a general or colorectal surgeon if the injury involves the colon or rectum, is a full-thickness injury larger than a simple serosal defect, involves thermal injury with uncertain margins or if the gynecologic surgeon lacks the training, demonstrated competence or institutional privileges to perform the repair safely. Subjective confidence alone is not sufficient; the decision to repair should be based on the gynecologist’s documented training, case experience and scope of privileges.
In facilities without on-site general surgery coverage, if the injury exceeds the gynecologist’s expertise and privileges, perform temporary control measures to stabilize the patient, minimize contamination and then arrange emergent transfer to a facility with adequate surgical capability. Direct physician-to-physician communication with the receiving surgeon should occur before transfer to ensure continuity of care.
ACOG has emphasized that obstetrician-gynecologists should have the requisite surgical skills and experience with procedures offered to patients, and that institutions should delineate requirements for different types and levels of procedures based on competency assessment. Repair by a surgeon with relevant expertise reduces the risk of anastomotic leak, abscess and fistula formation.
● Act promptly with surgical re-exploration when delayed bowel injury is suspected

Proceed to surgical exploration without delay when a patient presents postoperatively with clinical signs that indicate possible bowel injury (such as peritonitis, sepsis or worsening abdominal pain with hemodynamic instability) even if imaging is equivocal. Obtain surgical consultation emergently if the primary gynecologic surgeon is not trained, competent and privileged to manage the anticipated findings. Delayed re-exploration in bowel perforation or leak is a primary cause of mortality and malpractice exposure.
● Ensure structured postoperative follow-up after bowel repair

Create a plan to monitor the patient's recovery after intraoperative bowel repair, including serial abdominal examinations to watch for signs of a repair-site leak (such as fever, rising WBC, worsening pain or ileus). If warning signs develop, obtain necessary imaging (CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast) to evaluate for complications such as anastomotic leak or abscess. If a consulting surgeon was involved, coordinate follow-up care with them. Structured surveillance after repair helps catch complications early and demonstrates appropriate continuity of care.
CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL
Technique-Related Error
Contributing action or omission: Technical errors during abdominal entry, dissection and use of energy devices are the primary reasons for bowel injuries that increase the risk of poor outcomes. 

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce the incidence of bowel injury through safe entry technique, meticulous dissection and appropriate use of energy devices.
● Use safe abdominal entry techniques suited to the patient's risk profile

Select the entry technique based on patient-specific factors, such as BMI, prior abdominal surgery and suspected adhesive disease. For suspected periumbilical adhesions or prior midline incisions, consider left upper quadrant (Palmer's point) entry or open (Hasson) technique. Adjust Veress needle insertion angle based on BMI (45 degrees in non-obese, 90 degrees in obese patients). Confirm intraperitoneal placement using entry pressure (≤10 mmHg). Fifty percent of laparoscopic bowel and vascular injuries occur during entry; technique selection tailored to patient risk factors is a key modifiable strategy for reducing entry-related injuries.
● Employ meticulous dissection technique where adhesions exist

During adhesiolysis, use sharp dissection close to the bowel wall, maintain clear visualization of tissue planes and avoid excessive traction on adherent bowel. Inspect all adhesiolysis areas before concluding the procedure. Adhesive disease is the major risk factor for bowel injury; careful technique is the cornerstone of prevention.
● Minimize thermal spread when using electrosurgery near bowel

When operating near the bowel, bipolar electrosurgery or ultrasonic energy devices are preferred over monopolar instruments. If monopolar instruments are used, apply the lowest effective power setting, use short activation times and allow at least 15 seconds for cooling before contacting adjacent tissue. Inspect all instruments for insulation defects before use. Thermal injuries from monopolar electrosurgery may cause transmural bowel damage that is not immediately visible on the serosal surface and may present 3–7 days postoperatively.
● Perform intraoperative integrity testing when injury is suspected

When there is concern for rectal or sigmoid injury during pelvic dissection, perform an air-leak or dye test before concluding the procedure. For the small bowel, inspect the full circumference of any area of concern. Intraoperative testing converts occult injuries into recognized and repairable ones, eliminating the morbidity and mortality associated with delayed diagnosis.


ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Communication failures—including inadequate intraoperative team communication, incomplete handoffs and insufficient postoperative communication with patients—increase the risk of missed or delayed bowel injury diagnosis and compromise coordinated response.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communications and support coordinated team responses during care.
● Use the surgical safety checklist at every gynecologic procedure

Adhere to all three phases of the WHO Surgical Safety Checklist (Sign In, Time Out and Sign Out) for all gynecologic procedures. During Time Out, verbally confirm patient-specific risk factors for bowel injury, such as prior surgery, adhesive disease or endometriosis. During Sign Out, confirm whether intraoperative complications occurred and communicate postoperative concerns. The SOGC endorses adopting the surgical safety checklist in obstetrics and gynecology, and its implementation has been associated with reduced complications and mortality.
● Communicate intraoperative events during handoff to recovery and floor teams

Use a framework such as SBAR when transferring care from the OR to the PACU or inpatient unit. Explicitly address suspected or confirmed bowel injury, difficult dissection areas, energy devices used near the bowel and postoperative monitoring parameters. Document the handoff in the medical record. Structured handoffs ensure that postoperative teams are aware of risks and can maintain necessary vigilance.
● Communicate with consulting surgeons using full clinical context

When requesting intraoperative or postoperative surgical consultation for suspected bowel injury, provide the consultant with complete information, including operative indication, surgical approach, dissection areas, energy devices used, findings and clinical concerns. Comprehensive communication facilitates effective consultant triage and response.

ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Insufficient documentation of preoperative risk assessment, intraoperative findings, surgical technique and postoperative monitoring compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.
● Document preoperative risk assessment and informed consent discussion
Before surgery, document the patient's risk factors for bowel injury (such as prior surgery, adhesive disease, endometriosis and BMI). Record the informed consent discussion. Explicitly note that bowel injury and its management was discussed as a potential complication. For high-risk patients, outline a plan for consultant availability. FIGO best practices emphasize that valid consent requires four essential elements: competence, disclosure, understanding and voluntariness. Documenting each element is critical. A record of risk-specific consent shows that the patient was fully informed and the physician anticipated potential risks.
Sample documentation language: "Risks discussed include but are not limited to bowel injury, requiring repair or possible colostomy. Patient has history of two prior cesarean deliveries and known endometriosis, which increase the risk of adhesive disease and bowel injury. General surgery backup has been arranged. Patient verbalized understanding and consented to proceed."
● Document operative technique with specificity regarding bowel-relevant steps

 Capture detailed information in the operative report, including entry technique and rationale, presence and extent of adhesions, method of adhesiolysis, energy devices used and their proximity to the bowel and bowel inspection findings (if it was inspected at the end of the case). If integrity testing was performed (such as an air-leak or dye test) document the method and result. Detailed operative documentation provides critical context if an injury is identified later.

Sample documentation language: "Adhesions between the sigmoid colon and left pelvic sidewall were lysed sharply under direct visualization. Bipolar energy was used for hemostasis with care to avoid thermal spread to adjacent bowel. At the conclusion of the case, the bowel was inspected from the rectosigmoid to the cecum; no injury was identified. Air-leak test of the rectosigmoid was negative."
● Document postoperative assessments and clinical reasoning

Record serial postoperative evaluations, including abdominal exam findings, return of bowel function, vital signs and patient-reported symptoms. When patients present with postoperative symptoms that could indicate bowel injury, document the differential diagnosis, workup and clinical reasoning. If bowel injury is excluded, note the basis for that conclusion. Documenting clinical reasoning demonstrates thoughtful evaluation and supports defensibility if symptoms later indicate a missed injury.
● Document patient communication, including discharge instructions and follow-up

Record that the patient received specific discharge guidance on bowel injury warning signs, the communication method and the patient's stated understanding. For postoperative calls, document the symptoms reported, the assessment and the plan. Documenting communication demonstrates proper patient education and follow-up.

ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS
Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care
Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify and address patient-specific barriers to postoperative follow-up, or to engage patients in recognizing warning signs, increases the likelihood of delayed diagnosis when bowel injury occurs.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient engagement and remove obstacles to timely postoperative evaluation.
● Provide clear and specific postoperative instructions at discharge

Give patients written guidance that names the symptoms requiring emergency evaluation, such as worsening abdominal pain (especially at a trocar site), fever above 100.4°F, inability to keep food or liquids down, abdominal swelling and new-onset diarrhea. Avoid generic discharge instruction templates that do not address procedure-specific risks. Specific instructions improve patient recognition of concerning symptoms and outreach for follow-up care.
● Establish communication pathways for postoperative patient concerns

Confirm that patients have a direct and reliable way to contact the surgical team or a covering provider with access to the operative record during the first 72–96 hours after surgery. The receiving provider should be familiar with operative details and aware of risk factors for bowel injury when patients call with postoperative symptoms. The ability to quickly and easily reach providers encourages patients to seek care sooner, leading to a faster diagnosis.
● Assess and address barriers to emergency follow-up

Before discharge, confirm that the patient has reliable transportation, knows where to seek emergency care and has a phone number for the surgical team. For patients with language barriers, ensure that discharge instructions are provided in their primary language. Identifying challenges enables targeted interventions that reduce the interval between symptom onset and evaluation.
● Document patient non-adherence and outreach efforts

Record contact attempts and responses when postoperative follow-ups are missed. If a patient reports concerning symptoms but refuses evaluation, document the discussion, including recommendations, potential risks and reasons for declining. Continue outreach by phone, patient portal or mail. Capturing these details demonstrates appropriate effort and supports defensibility.

This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice c

ADVERSE_OUTCOMES: Bowel Injury

TITLE: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy
RISK_BRIEF:
Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy
SPECIALTY: OB/GYN

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Pregnancy, Labor and Delivery, Postpartum Care

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Severe Maternal Morbidity (Stroke, Eclampsia, Pulmonary Edema, Maternal Death)

Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy are a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in OB/GYN.
Mitigating Your Risk
With timely diagnosis, appropriate treatment decisions and coordinated communication across the care continuum, OB/GYNs can mitigate the risks associated with hypertensive disorders of pregnancy and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for hypertensive disorders of pregnancy and provides strategies to help prevent them. 
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving hypertensive disorders of pregnancy typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 75% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 25%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate. 

Actions and Omissions Driving Postpartum Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy [insert chart] 
 
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability. 
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSIS
Failure to Order Indicated Testing
Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain necessary laboratory testing to diagnose preeclampsia or identify disease progression increases the risk of missing severe features and delaying intervention.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure the timely identification of hypertensive disorders and recognition of disease progression.

• Obtain baseline laboratory evaluation at initial presentation of hypertension

Order a CBC with platelet count, serum creatinine, AST, ALT, LDH and urine protein/creatinine ratio at new-onset hypertension after 20 weeks of gestation. Complete this initial evaluation at diagnosis to establish baseline values and identify severe features that would change management. Baseline testing reveals end-organ involvement that may not be clinically apparent and shows adherence to ACOG recommendations.
• Perform weekly laboratory surveillance during expectant management

Obtain weekly platelet count, serum creatinine and liver enzyme levels for patients with gestational hypertension or preeclampsia without severe features. For gestational hypertension, also assess proteinuria weekly. Repeat testing sooner if clinical deterioration is suspected. Serial monitoring detects disease progression before clinical decompensation and aligns with ACOG recommendations.
• Use quantitative proteinuria assessment rather than dipstick alone

Evaluate for preeclampsia using a protein-to-creatinine ratio (≥0.3 mg/dL) or a 24-hour urine collection (≥300 mg) instead of dipstick urinalysis when possible. Dipstick testing has a 71% false-positive rate at 1+ and a 9% false-negative rate overall. Quantitative assessment reduces diagnostic error and supports accurate classification of disease severity. 

• Assess for severe features in all patients with gestational hypertension

Recognize that gestational hypertension without proteinuria may still represent preeclampsia if severe features are present. Check for thrombocytopenia, elevated liver enzymes, renal insufficiency, pulmonary edema, new-onset headache unresponsive to acetaminophen or visual disturbances. Up to 50% of patients with gestational hypertension develop preeclampsia, particularly when diagnosed before 32 weeks.

CLINICAL: DIAGNOSIS
Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act On Diagnostic Finding
Contributing action or omission: Failing to recognize severe preeclampsia features, misinterpreting blood pressure measurements or delaying recognition of postpartum hypertensive emergencies increase the risk of stroke, eclampsia and maternal death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve recognition of severe disease and ensure proper escalation of care.

• Confirm severe hypertension within 15 minutes and initiate treatment promptly

Treat severe hypertension (systolic ≥160 mm Hg or diastolic ≥110 mm Hg) lasting 15 minutes or more as an obstetric emergency, requiring antihypertensive treatment within 30–60 minutes. Measure blood pressure accurately, using the correct cuff size and proper patient positioning. Delayed treatment of severe hypertension is the leading modifiable factor in preventable maternal stroke.
• Recognize atypical presentations of preeclampsia

Maintain a high index of suspicion in patients with new-onset hypertension and any concerning symptoms, as preeclampsia can present without proteinuria if other severe features are present. Symptoms like headache and right upper quadrant pain are nonspecific and may not correlate with lab abnormalities. Consider alternative diagnoses, including TTP, HUS and autoimmune disease, when presentation occurs before 20 weeks. 

• Maintain heightened vigilance for postpartum hypertensive emergencies

Monitor blood pressure closely, as it typically decreases within 48 hours postpartum but increases again between days 3–6. Preeclampsia can develop de novo postpartum, often with headaches or visual changes. Most patients who present with postpartum eclampsia or stroke experience warning symptoms for hours or days beforehand. Educate postpartum patients about warning signs and provide pathways to seek care. 
• Recognize HELLP syndrome as a distinct clinical entity

Identify HELLP syndrome by its key features: hemolysis with LDH ≥600 U/L, elevated AST/ALT ≥twice normal, platelets <100,000/μL. The condition carries the highest risk of cardiovascular severe maternal morbidity (adjusted RR 17.55) and can occur without severe hypertension or proteinuria. Delivery after maternal stabilization is recommended regardless of gestational age. 

CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention
Contributing action or omission: Delays in initiating indicated therapies, failure to manage fluid balance, inappropriate timing of delivery or failure to ensure that delivery occurs at a proper level of care increase the risk of maternal stroke, eclampsia, pulmonary edema and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely and appropriate treatment of hypertensive emergencies and disease complications.

• Treat severe hypertension as an obstetric emergency

Do not delay initiation of antihypertensive therapy. When severe hypertension (≥160/110 mm Hg) persists for 15 minutes or more, initiate antihypertensive therapy within 30–60 minutes using IV labetalol, IV hydralazine or oral immediate-release nifedipine. Target blood pressure is 140–150/90–100 mm Hg. Delayed treatment of severe hypertension, not the choice of agent, is the leading modifiable risk factor in preventable maternal stroke. Institutions should have mechanisms in place to initiate prompt administration when a patient presents with a hypertensive emergency.  

• Initiate magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis in preeclampsia with severe features

Administer IV magnesium sulfate (4–6 g loading dose, then 1–2 g/hour maintenance) to patients with preeclampsia with severe features, HELLP syndrome or eclampsia. Magnesium sulfate reduces eclamptic seizures by 58% (NNT = 100). Continue treatment for 24 hours postpartum and monitor for toxicity, including loss of deep tendon reflexes and respiratory depression. 
• Restrict intravenous fluid administration to reduce the risk of iatrogenic pulmonary edema
Limit total intravenous fluid intake to approximately 80 mL/hour during labor and delivery in patients with preeclampsia. Capillary leak and decreased colloid oncotic pressure in preeclampsia make these patients vulnerable to pulmonary edema from iatrogenic volume overload—a complication that, though rare, carries significant morbidity and mortality. Tolerate urine output as low as 10 mL/hour as long as serum creatinine remains stable, rather than reflexively increasing fluid rates for oliguria.
• Follow gestational age-based delivery timing recommendations
For Gestational Hypertension or Preeclampsia WITHOUT Severe Features:
Deliver at 37 weeks (or immediately if diagnosed at ≥37 weeks)
For Preeclampsia WITH Severe Features:
≥34 weeks: Deliver after maternal stabilization
<34 weeks: Expectant management may be considered in highly selected patients at tertiary centers with suitable maternal-fetal resources
Any gestational age: Deliver immediately if maternal or fetal condition deteriorates
When severe features are present in the late preterm period (34–37 weeks), do not delay delivery to give steroids; proceed with delivery after stabilization. 
Clear delivery timing criteria reduce ambiguity and support defensible decisions.
• Recognize conditions that preclude expectant management
Do not delay delivery when the following are present: uncontrolled severe-range blood pressures despite antihypertensive therapy, persistent headaches that do not improve with treatment, visual disturbances, HELLP syndrome, eclampsia, pulmonary edema, new or worsening renal dysfunction, suspected placental abruption or abnormal fetal testing. These conditions are absolute contraindications to continued expectant management. 

• Make certain that delivery occurs at an appropriate level of maternal and neonatal care
Ensure that expectant management of preeclampsia with severe features before 34 weeks occurs in a setting with suitable maternal and neonatal resources, as recommended by ACOG. For HELLP syndrome, deliver at a tertiary care center. If the facility lacks necessary resources, including ICU-level care, blood bank support, maternal-fetal medicine consultation and neonatal intensive care, initiate transfer after stabilizing blood pressure and administering magnesium sulfate. Failure to transfer when clinical complexity exceeds local resources is a recurring contributor to adverse outcomes and malpractice claims.
• Prioritize maternal and fetal safety over completing antenatal corticosteroid courses when clinical deterioration occurs 
Administer antenatal corticosteroids for preterm preeclampsia with severe features < 34 weeks but do not delay delivery to complete it if the clinical situation is worsening. For gestational hypertension or preeclampsia without severe features, consider late preterm steroids (34 0/7–36 6/7 weeks) if delivery is expected within 7 days. The treatment error in claims is often not the steroid itself but the decision to postpone delivery for steroid completion when it is no longer safe to do so.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Failures in communication—including inadequate handoffs, unclear escalation pathways and insufficient coordination of postpartum follow-up—increase the risk of delayed recognition and treatment of hypertensive emergencies.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support coordinated team responses across the continuum of care.
• Establish escalation triggers and team roles for hypertensive emergencies

Define protocols specifying when to escalate care (such as for severe-range blood pressures, neurologic symptoms and laboratory abnormalities) and who is responsible for initiating treatment. Assign team leadership during hypertensive emergencies and use closed-loop communication to confirm orders and verify medication administration.

• Consult maternal-fetal medicine early for complex or evolving hypertensive disorders
Obtain MFM consultation for preeclampsia with severe features before 34 weeks, as well as for HELLP syndrome, diagnostic uncertainty (such as differentiating chronic hypertension exacerbation from superimposed preeclampsia), atypical presentations and cases with significant maternal comorbidities. Early consultation supports shared decision-making on delivery timing, facilitates transfer planning when needed and reduces the risk of delayed escalation—a recurring contributor to adverse outcomes in malpractice claims.
• Implement handoff protocols for patients with hypertensive disorders

Use frameworks like SBAR during care transitions, including outpatient to inpatient settings, shift changes and transfers from labor and delivery to postpartum units. Communicate key details, including current blood pressure status, presence of severe features, medications administered, laboratory trends and specific monitoring parameters. Document handoff information in the medical record. 

• Coordinate postpartum follow-up before discharge

Schedule blood pressure evaluation within 72 hours for patients with severe hypertension and within 7–10 days for all patients with hypertensive disorders. Confirm that the patient has a working blood pressure cuff and knows how to use it. Provide contact information for the care team and document the follow-up plan and patient understanding. 

• Communicate with primary care and cardiology for long-term cardiovascular risk management

Inform primary care providers of the diagnosis and its implications, as patients with a history of preeclampsia face an increased lifetime risk of chronic hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Emphasize the importance of monitoring blood pressure annually and reducing cardiovascular risk factors. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Insufficient documentation of blood pressure measurements, clinical decision-making, patient education and postpartum instructions undermines continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document timeliness of recognition and response to severe hypertension
When identifying severe-range blood pressures (≥160/110 mm Hg), record the time of notification, bedside assessment, treatment ordered and blood pressure response. This creates a defensible record demonstrating adherence to the 30–60 minute treatment window and appropriate clinical response to an obstetric emergency.
Sample documentation language: "Notified of BP 168/112 mm Hg at 14:32, confirmed persistent at 14:47. Assessed patient at bedside—no neurologic symptoms, FHR tracing category I. Ordered IV labetalol 20 mg, administered at 14:52. Repeat BP 148/96 mm Hg at 15:12—will continue to monitor per protocol."
• Document clinical reasoning for ongoing management decisions

Record the factors influencing the choice between expectant management and delivery, including gestational age, presence or absence of severe features, lab results, fetal status and patient preferences. If expectant management is chosen, note that the patient meets the criteria and has no contraindications. If the decision is to proceed with delivery, document the indication. When management deviates from standard protocols, explain the rationale. This shows thoughtful clinical judgment. 
Sample documentation language: “At 30 2/7 weeks with preeclampsia with severe features, now with blood pressures controlled on oral nifedipine 30 mg q8h. Labs stable—platelets 142K, AST 28/ALT 22, LDH 180, creatinine 0.7. Category I tracing, AFI normal. No contraindications to expectant management: no uncontrolled severe-range blood pressures, no persistent headache or visual disturbances, no HELLP, no pulmonary edema, no renal dysfunction, no evidence of abruption. Betamethasone course completed. Plan is to continue inpatient observation with twice-weekly labs, daily NST and growth ultrasound in 2 weeks. Rationale, risks and benefits of expectant management versus delivery discussed with patient, who is in agreement.”

• Document patient education on warning signs

Before discharge (antepartum or postpartum), record that the patient was instructed on warning signs requiring immediate evaluation, including severe headache, visual changes, right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, shortness of breath and decreased fetal movement, and guidance on blood pressure thresholds for seeking care. Note the patient's stated understanding. 
Sample documentation language: "Discussed warning signs of preeclampsia with patient, including severe headache, vision changes, RUQ pain, shortness of breath. Patient instructed to call the office or return to the hospital if BP ≥160 systolic or ≥110 diastolic. Patient verbalized understanding and demonstrated correct use of home BP cuff."

• Document postpartum blood pressure monitoring plan and follow-up

Record the plan for postpartum blood pressure monitoring, including frequency of home measurements, threshold for calling or seeking care and scheduled follow-up appointments. Include medications prescribed at discharge and titration instructions. These records support continuity of care and demonstrates necessary postpartum surveillance. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS
Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care
Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify and address barriers to blood pressure monitoring, medication adherence or postpartum follow-up increases the likelihood of unrecognized hypertensive emergencies.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and address barriers to timely evaluation.

• Offer home blood pressure monitoring equipment and education

Verify that patients with hypertensive disorders have access to a validated home blood pressure monitor with a correctly sized cuff before discharge. Provide written and verbal instructions on proper technique and monitoring frequency. Home blood pressure monitoring increases the likelihood of adherence to recommended measurement frequency and may reduce hospital readmissions for hypertension. 

• Provide written guidance in the patient's primary language

Discharge instructions should specify symptoms that require emergency evaluation and outline blood pressure thresholds for seeking care. Avoid generic templates that do not address the risks of hypertensive disorder. Make sure that instructions are provided in the patient's primary language and tailored to an appropriate literacy level. 
• Identify and address barriers to postpartum follow-up

Help patients resolve challenges such as transportation, childcare, work obligations and insurance coverage. Consider offering telehealth options for blood pressure review and medication titration. Remote monitoring strategies can help compensate for racial disparities in office-based follow-up. Schedule follow-up appointments before discharge. 

• Document patient non-adherence and continued outreach

Record contact attempts and responses when follow-ups are missed or monitoring is declined. If a patient refuses evaluation despite concerning symptoms, make sure to document the discussion, including recommendations, potential risks and reasons for refusal. Continue outreach by phone, patient portal or mail.

Mitigating Your Risk
With timely diagnosis, appropriate treatment decisions and coordinated communication across the care continuum, OB/GYNs can mitigate the risks associated with hypertensive disorders of pregnancy and reduce malpractice exposure.

TITLE: NRFHT
RISK_BRIEF:
Non-Reassuring Fetal Heart Tones
SPECIALTY: OB/GYN

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Labor, Intrapartum Care

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Hypoxic-Ischemic Encephalopathy (HIE), Cerebral Palsy, Neonatal Death
Non-reassuring fetal heart tones are a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in OB/GYN.
Mitigating Your Risk
With accurate interpretation, timely, evidence-based intervention and team-based communication, OB/GYNs can mitigate the risks associated with non-reassuring fetal heart tones and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for non-reassuring fetal heart tones and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving non-reassuring fetal heart tones typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 68% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 32%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.  

Actions and Omissions Driving Non-Reassuring Fetal Heart Tones Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability. 

CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failing to Recognize, Interpret or Act on Diagnostic Finding

Contributing action or omission: Failure to recognize Category II high-risk features or Category III patterns, misinterpretation of fetal heart rate (FHR) tracings or confusion between maternal and fetal heart rates increase the risk of delayed intervention and neonatal injury.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure accurate FHR tracing interpretation and timely recognition of fetal compromise.

• Use the standardized NICHD three-tier classification for all FHR interpretations

At each FHR assessment, classify the tracing using the NICHD three-tier system: Category I (normal), Category II (indeterminate) or Category III (abnormal). Category I tracings include a baseline of 110–160 bpm, moderate variability, absence of late or variable decelerations and presence or absence of accelerations or early decelerations. Category III includes absent baseline variability with recurrent late or variable decelerations, bradycardia or sinusoidal pattern. Standardized terminology ensures consistent communication across the care team and demonstrates adherence to national guidelines. 

• Identify high-risk features within Category II tracings

When evaluating Category II tracings, assess for high-risk features, including absent variability, prolonged unexplained minimal variability, unexplained change from normal baseline to tachycardia, recurrent late or variable decelerations or more than one prolonged deceleration. Conversely, recognize protective features, such as moderate variability and accelerations that provide reassurance. High-risk Category II features warrant closer surveillance and consideration of earlier intervention. 

• Verify fetal versus maternal heart rate when clinically indicated

If FHR patterns appear inconsistent, such as FHR matching maternal pulse during tachycardia or bradycardia, determine whether the monitor is detecting the fetal or the maternal heart rate. Use simultaneous maternal heart rate monitoring when available. Misidentifying maternal heart rate as fetal heart rate can lead to false reassurance or unnecessary intervention. 

• Recognize non-hypoxic causes of abnormal FHR patterns

Keep in mind that not all FHR abnormalities are caused by acidemia. Conditions such as fetal infection or inflammation, anemia, congenital heart disease and central nervous system injury can produce significant Category II or III patterns before or without fetal acidemia. Use the clinical context, such as the presence of chorioamnionitis, meconium or known fetal anomalies, to guide interpretation.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention
Contributing action or omission: Delaying intrauterine resuscitation, failing to escalate from Category II to operative delivery when indicated or delaying response to Category III tracings increases the risk of hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy and neonatal death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely and appropriate intervention for non-reassuring FHR patterns.

• Initiate intrauterine resuscitation promptly for Category II tracings

When a Category II tracing is identified, initiate one or more resuscitation measures, such as maternal position changes, an IV fluid bolus, reducing or stopping oxytocin, amnioinfusion for recurrent variable decelerations or correcting maternal pathophysiology such as hypotension or fever. ACOG recommends attempting intrauterine resuscitation prior to cesarean delivery for Category II tracings. Nearly two-thirds of Category II tracings improve to Category I within 60 minutes of intervention. 

• Act immediately in the case of Category III tracings

Begin intrauterine resuscitation while preparing for expedited delivery. If the tracing remains Category III after initial interventions, proceed with expedited delivery either vaginally (including operative vaginal delivery if appropriate) or by cesarean. Let the clinical context determine urgency. For example, a suspected uterine rupture with bradycardia requires emergent operative delivery as quickly and safely as possible. 

• Treat uterine tachysystole associated with FHR changes
If uterine tachysystole (>5 contractions in 10 minutes averaged over 30 minutes) is associated with Category II high-risk features or Category III tracings, first reduce or stop oxytocin. If tachysystole persists, administer a rapid-acting uterine relaxation agent such as terbutaline, 0.25 mg subcutaneously. Tocolysis improves placental perfusion and allows time for fetal recovery. 

• Avoid routine maternal oxygen administration for FHR abnormalities

Follow ACOG guidance against the routine administration of maternal oxygen for Category II or III tracings in the absence of maternal hypoxia. A 2021 meta-analysis of 16 randomized trials found no improvement in umbilical artery pH, Apgar scores or NICU admissions with intrapartum maternal oxygen. Reserve oxygen for patients with documented maternal hypoxia. 

• Obtain umbilical cord blood gases after delivery for high-risk cases

Collect paired umbilical artery and vein blood samples for pH and blood gas analysis in situations such as cesarean delivery for fetal compromise, low 5-minute Apgar score, abnormal FHR tracing or other high-risk conditions. Cord blood gases offer an objective assessment of fetal metabolic status at birth and are valuable for quality assurance and medicolegal documentation.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Communication failures—including inadequate handoffs, unclear escalation pathways and a lack of shared mental models for FHR interpretation—increase the risk of delayed recognition and treatment for fetal compromise.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support a coordinated team response.

• Make sure that nurses and physicians use the same FHR classification system and terminology

Adopt NICHD nomenclature for FHR interpretation unit-wide. Nursing staff and obstetric clinicians should use the same terminology (such as Category I, II, III; baseline, variability, accelerations and decelerations) when discussing tracings. Standardized language reduces ambiguity and supports a rapid, coordinated response. 

• Implement handoff protocols that include FHR status

Use frameworks such as SBAR when transferring care between shifts or between nursing and physician teams. Communicate the current FHR category, presence of high-risk features, interventions, response to interventions and monitoring parameters. Document handoff details in the medical record. 

• Establish escalation triggers and notification expectations

Define unit protocols specifying when nursing staff should notify the physician, such as in cases Category II with high-risk features, Category III, prolonged deceleration >2 minutes or failure to respond to resuscitation. Develop expected response times and escalation pathways if the primary provider is unavailable. Use closed-loop communication to confirm receipt of critical information. 

• Conduct regular multidisciplinary FHR tracing reviews

Use these sessions to allow obstetric care teams to informally discuss indeterminate tracings and associated outcomes while building consensus for management options. These reviews promote a shared understanding and ensure consistent interpretation across the team. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider 

Contributing action or omission: Failing to inform patients about monitoring limitations, changes in fetal status or the rationale for interventions can compromise shared decision-making and patient trust.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support informed patient participation in care decisions.

• Counsel patients about fetal monitoring options and limitations

Discuss the benefits and drawbacks of continuous EFM versus intermittent auscultation with low-risk patients. Explain that EFM has a high false-positive rate and is associated with increased cesarean delivery rates without proven reduction in cerebral palsy. Document the discussion and patient preferences. 

• Communicate changes in fetal status and planned interventions

When FHR abnormalities are identified, inform the patient and support person of the findings, planned interventions and the possible need for expedited delivery. Communication helps patients understand evolving situations and can reduce their anxiety. Document that the patient was informed about fetal status and interventions. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR
Failure to Recognize Responsibility

Contributing Action or Omission: Failure to maintain competency in FHR interpretation, reluctance to escalate concerns or lack of clear responsibility among team members increase the risk of missed or delayed interventions.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure individual accountability and team competency.

• Assign leadership during FHR emergencies

Designate a team leader when a Category III tracing or prolonged deceleration occurs. The leader coordinates care, assigns roles and ensures accountability for time-sensitive interventions. Clear leadership prevents diffusion of responsibility during critical situations. 
• Empower all team members to escalate concerns

Foster a culture in which any team member, including a nurse, resident, midwife or attending, can report issues about FHR tracings without fear of reprisal. Establish escalation pathways for disagreements over tracing interpretation or management. This type of culture helps ensure timely and effective intervention. 
• Maintain current EFM certification 
Pursue continuing education that emphasizes NICHD nomenclature and maintain EFM certification. Obstetric units should provide EFM continuing education and certification that uses NICHD nomenclature. While evidence linking EFM certification to improved neonatal outcomes is limited, certification provides an objective measure of knowledge and encourages the use of standardized terminology.
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Insufficient documentation of FHR assessments, clinical decision-making, interventions and patient communication compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document FHR assessments 

Record FHR tracing assessments periodically, following frequencies established by hospital protocols. Include baseline rate, variability, presence or absence of accelerations and decelerations and overall category classification. The FHR tracing should be included in the medical record and available for review. 
Sample documentation language: "FHR tracing reviewed at 14:30. Baseline 140 bpm, moderate variability, accelerations present, no decelerations. Category I tracing. Continue routine monitoring."

• Document clinical reasoning for management decisions

When determining whether to intervene or continue to observe, record the rationale for decisions, including FHR category, presence of high-risk or protective features, response to resuscitation, labor progress and proximity to delivery. When expedited delivery is indicated, include the indication and timing. Documentation demonstrates thoughtful clinical judgment. 

Sample documentation language: "Category II tracing with recurrent variable decelerations and minimal variability. Intrauterine resuscitation initiated: position change to left lateral, oxytocin discontinued, IV fluid bolus administered. Tracing improved to moderate variability within 15 minutes. Continue close surveillance."

• Document interventions

Record interventions, initiation times and FHR responses during intrauterine resuscitation. When attempting multiple interventions, include the sequence and cumulative effect. This supports defensibility by demonstrating timely and appropriate action. 
• Document patient and care team communication
Record discussions with the patient about FHR status, planned interventions and delivery decisions. Include provider-to-provider communications, such as notifications and handoffs. Documenting patient and care team communications demonstrates coordinated care and informed consent. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS
Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Patient refusal of recommended monitoring or intervention can delay recognition of fetal compromise.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient engagement while respecting autonomy.

• Address barriers to continuous monitoring when clinically indicated

Discuss wireless monitoring options or structured intermittent auscultation for patients who desire mobility in labor if clinically appropriate. For patients with obesity, where external monitoring is challenging, explain limitations and consider early fetal scalp electrode placement. Addressing practical barriers supports patient preferences while ensuring adequate surveillance. 
• Document informed refusal of recommended monitoring or intervention

When a patient declines recommended continuous monitoring or intervention for non-reassuring FHR, record the discussion, including the recommendation, risks explained (such as potential for undetected fetal compromise), the patient's reasons for refusal and the alternative plan. Continue to offer recommended care and document ongoing conversations. 

• Engage patients as partners in monitoring

Explain to patients what the FHR monitor is measuring and what normal patterns look like. Encourage patients to alert staff if they notice changes in the tracing or if the monitor becomes displaced. Patient engagement can help identify monitoring gaps earlier.

Mitigating Your Risk

TITLE: Postpartum Hemorrhage
RISK_BRIEF:
Postpartum Hemorrhage
SPECIALTY: OB/GYN

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Labor, Vaginal Delivery, Cesarean Delivery

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Postpartum Hemorrhage
Postpartum hemorrhage is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in OB/GYN. 
Mitigating Your Risk

With proactive risk identification, prompt escalation, vigilant monitoring and coordinated response, OB/GYNs can mitigate the risks associated with postpartum hemorrhage and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for postpartum hemorrhage and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving postpartum hemorrhage typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 64% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 36% Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.  

Actions and Omissions Driving Postpartum Hemorrhage Risk [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act on Diagnostic Finding
Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify risk factors, recognize ongoing bleeding, correctly interpret blood loss volume or promptly identify PPH etiology increases the risk of severe maternal morbidity and mortality.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve early identification of hemorrhage and its underlying cause.

• Perform hemorrhage risk assessment prenatally, on admission and throughout labor
Use a validated risk stratification tool (such as those from CMQCC, AWHONN or the ACOG Safe Motherhood Initiative) to classify patients as low, medium or high risk at each stage. These tools identify 60%–85% of patients who will experience significant hemorrhage. Tailor preparedness to risk level: for moderate-risk patients, ensure active type and screen and IV access; for high-risk patients, type and crossmatch at least 2 units of PRBCs, notify anesthesia and the blood bank and confirm hemorrhage cart and surgical team availability. Embed risk assessment in the EHR; integrated tools have achieved >93% screening adherence. Note that approximately 40% of PPH events occur in low-risk patients, so risk stratification should enhance preparedness, not reduce vigilance. Document the risk assessment, assigned risk level and preparedness actions taken.
• Evaluate for placenta accreta spectrum in patients with prior cesarean delivery
Perform targeted ultrasonography with color flow Doppler in the second trimester for patients with PAS risk factors, particularly prior cesarean delivery with placenta previa or anterior low-lying placenta. Ultrasonography identifies PAS with ~90% sensitivity and specificity when performed by experienced providers. Prenatal detection reduces intraoperative blood loss and transfusion requirements and enables planned delivery at a facility with necessary expertise; however, ~35% of PAS cases remain undiagnosed until delivery, with significantly worse outcomes. When PAS is suspected, refer early to a center with multidisciplinary expertise (maternal-fetal medicine, experienced pelvic surgeons, interventional radiology, anesthesiology, blood bank with massive transfusion capacity) and plan delivery at 34+0 to 35+6 weeks before labor or bleeding onset. Do not attempt manual placental removal at delivery when PAS is suspected. Document screening assessment, findings, referral discussions and delivery plan.
• Implement quantitative blood loss measurement at every delivery

Use calibrated blood-collection drapes, weigh blood-soaked materials and monitor irrigation fluids to objectively assess blood loss during and after delivery. Visual estimation of blood loss is inaccurate and leads to underdiagnosis. Quantitative assessment supports earlier recognition and intervention, aligning with ACOG recommendations and demonstrating adherence to current care standards. 
• Use the "4Ts" framework to identify PPH etiology

When excessive bleeding occurs, rapidly evaluate tone (uterine atony), trauma (lacerations, hematomas and uterine rupture), tissue (retained placenta or clots) and thrombin (coagulopathy). Perform a rapid physical examination of the uterus, cervix, vagina, vulva and perineum. Multiple etiologies may coexist. Systematic evaluation ensures that necessary treatment is initiated and demonstrates thorough clinical assessment.

• Recognize that vital sign changes are late indicators of significant hemorrhage

Do not rely on vital sign changes to diagnose PPH. Typical clinical signs of hypovolemia, such as hypotension and tachycardia, may not appear until blood loss exceeds 25% of total blood volume (>1,500 mL in late pregnancy). ACOG recommends using quantitative blood loss measurement to guide management and emphasizes that earlier recognition—before vital sign deterioration—improves outcomes.
	Escalation thresholds:
≥500 mL (vaginal delivery): Investigate cause, even if vital signs are normal (ACOG) 
≥300 mL + any abnormal vital sign (pulse >100, SBP <100, DBP <60, or shock index >1.0): Consider early escalation—this combination identifies women at high risk for severe morbidity
≥1,000 mL or signs of hypovolemia: Meets ACOG definition of PPH; activate hemorrhage protocol 
 • Maintain heightened vigilance for secondary PPH

Be aware that secondary PPH (24 hours to 12 weeks postpartum) may present with retained  products of conception, infection (endometritis), inherited coagulopathies or subinvolution of the placental site. Make certain that patients are educated about warning signs before discharge and understand how to seek care. Document patient education and follow-up instructions.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention
Contributing action or omission: Delays in initiating uterotonic therapy, failure to escalate treatment when initial interventions fail or inappropriate timing of surgical intervention increase the risk of severe maternal morbidity and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely and appropriate treatment of PPH and support rapid escalation when needed.

• Administer prophylactic uterotonics immediately after every delivery

Give prophylactic oxytocin (10 units IM or 10–40 units as a dilute IV infusion) after all births for PPH prevention. This is supported by Level A evidence from ACOG and WHO recommendations. Active management of the third stage of labor reduces the risk of PPH by approximately 66% compared with expectant management. Ensure that institutional guidelines mandate routine uterotonic administration.

• Initiate first-line uterotonics  without delay upon recognizing PPH

Administer oxytocin, and if bleeding persists, proceed to multiple uterotonic agents in rapid succession, including methylergonovine, carboprost and misoprostol, provided there are no contraindications. Methylergonovine is contraindicated in hypertension and cardiovascular disease, and carboprost is contraindicated in asthma, cardiovascular disease (including pulmonary hypertension), hepatic disease and renal disease. No single agent has demonstrated greater efficacy than others; selection should be guided by contraindications and availability. 

• Administer tranexamic acid early when PPH is diagnosed

Give patients tranexamic acid (1g IV over 10 minutes) as early as possible when PPH is recognized. A second dose may be given if bleeding persists after 30 minutes or restarts within 24 hours of the first dose. In the WOMAN trial, TXA reduced death due to bleeding by approximately 19% overall; in women treated within 3 hours of birth, the reduction was 31%, with no benefit after 3 hours. Note: Guidance on TXA timing differs across societies; WHO and FIGO recommend giving TXA alongside uterotonics as soon as PPH is recognized; ACOG recommends TXA when initial medical therapy fails, though it acknowledges that earlier use is likely to be superior to delayed treatment. Institutions should establish guidance specifying when TXA is administered relative to uterotonics.
• Escalate promptly to mechanical and surgical interventions when pharmacotherapy fails

When uterotonics fail to control bleeding, advance immediately to intrauterine hemorrhage-control devices, uterine compression sutures (>90% success rate), uterine artery embolization where available (95%–98% success rate) or hysterectomy. Intrauterine devices include balloon tamponade and vacuum-induced devices; both have >85% success rates with similar outcomes. Earlier placement (QBL 1000–1,499 mL vs. ≥1,500 mL) has been shown to significantly reduce failure and transfusion rates regardless of device type. Institutions should ensure that at least one device type is available with trained staff. Delays in escalation from medication to other treatment options are a primary contributing factor to adverse outcomes. Establish escalation triggers and ensure readiness for surgical intervention.  

• Implement bundled treatment guidelines for PPH response

Use evidence-based treatment bundles that combine multiple interventions simultaneously rather than sequentially. The E-MOTIVE bundle (uterine massage, oxytocics, tranexamic acid, IV fluids, examination, escalation) provides the strongest trial-level evidence, with a 60% reduction in the composite of severe PPH, laparotomy or maternal death in a cluster-RCT of 210,132 deliveries in low- and middle-income settings. Bundle use ensures consistent, comprehensive treatment and demonstrates adherence to best practices.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention
Contributing action or omission: Insufficient post-hemorrhage monitoring and premature discontinuation of surveillance increase the risk of recurrent bleeding and delayed recognition of deterioration.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support sustained monitoring and early identification of recurrent bleeding or clinical deterioration.

• Establish protocols for post-PPH monitoring

Develop and follow guidelines for regularly monitoring vital signs, assessing blood loss and conducting clinical evaluations after initial bleeding control. Check vital signs at defined intervals (every 15 minutes initially, then every 30 minutes as the patient stabilizes). Frequently reassess uterine tone and bleeding status. Use early warning systems such as MEOWS to identify early signs of deterioration.

• Continue quantitative blood loss trending after initial control

Monitor and document cumulative blood loss after initial hemorrhage control. Recurrent bleeding may occur; trending QBL supports early recognition of ongoing hemorrhage. Record blood loss measurements, interventions and clinical responses in real time.

• Monitor for signs of coagulopathy and intervene as needed

Obtain laboratory studies, including CBC, coagulation studies (such as PT/INR and PTT) and fibrinogen levels. Thromboelastography may guide blood product selection. Watch for clinical signs of coagulopathy, such as oozing from IV sites and mucosal bleeding, and intervene with suitable factor replacement. Fibrinogen levels <200 mg/dL warrant consideration of cryoprecipitate administration. 

• Ensure appropriate level of care and transfer capability

Initiate stabilization measures immediately, including hemodynamic monitoring, IV access, fluid resuscitation and escalating interventions per protocol. Contact facilities with more resources early when bleeding does not resolve with available interventions. Maternal status must be stabilized before transfer; if transfer is unsafe or impossible due to patient instability, continue escalating care locally, including tamponade, surgical interventions or hysterectomy as needed and consider postpartum transfer once stabilized. PPH, particularly when severe, is best managed with a multidisciplinary team, including obstetricians, anesthesiologists, hematologists and critical care specialists. Document all transfer discussions, decisions and reasons if transfer is delayed or deemed unsafe.

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Provider-to-provider communication failures—including inadequate handoffs and unclear leadership during emergencies—increase the likelihood of clinical errors and delayed intervention.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes, clarify leadership and support coordinated team responses.

• Activate multidisciplinary response teams early for PPH emergencies

Include obstetrics, nursing, anesthesia and transfusion medicine personnel on the team, if available. Early activation enables parallel rather than sequential responses, reducing time to definitive treatment.
• Establish leadership and roles during PPH emergencies

Assign a team leader at the start of every shift or when a PPH emergency occurs to coordinate care, assign roles and ensure accountability. Define guidelines that outline leadership responsibilities during obstetric emergencies. The team leader should direct the response, assign tasks and maintain situational awareness.

• Emphasize closed-loop communication during emergencies

Regularly train healthcare teams to verbalize instructions and confirmations to minimize errors. When giving orders, make sure that the recipient repeats the order and confirms completion. This practice reduces medication errors and ensures that all team members understand the current plan. 

• Implement handoff protocols for patients with PPH risk factors or active hemorrhage

Use frameworks such as SBAR when transferring care between shifts, from labor and delivery to postpartum units and during emergencies. Communicate current blood loss, vital signs, administered interventions, laboratory values and monitoring parameters. Document handoff details in the medical record.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider
Contributing action or omission: Failure to debrief patients after events or educate them about PPH warning signs and when to seek care increases the risk of patient dissatisfaction, psychological harm and delayed presentation for secondary PPH.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients are informed about their care, understand warning signs and have pathways to seek care.

• Conduct a post-event discussion with patients 
After PPH, speak with the patient and support network before discharge, acknowledging the event and its physical and emotional effects. Explain the clinical events and care provided, answer questions and avoid language that minimizes the experience. Engage a risk manager to help prepare for the discussion. PPH is associated with a 3.4-fold increased risk of PTSD in the first year after delivery and increased risk of postnatal depression. Arrange follow-up at 1–2 weeks and screen for depression, PTSD and anxiety. Document the discussion, attendees and follow-up plan.
• Provide information on warning signs before discharge

Explain to patients about warning signs requiring immediate evaluation, such as heavy vaginal bleeding (soaking more than one pad per hour), passing large clots, dizziness or lightheadedness, rapid heartbeat and fever or chills. Write instructions in the patient's primary language and at their literacy level. Document that education was provided and confirm patient understanding. 

• Make sure that patients have pathways to seek care

Provide patients with contact information for the care team and instructions on when to call and when to go directly to the emergency department. Confirm that the patient understands how to access care if warning signs develop. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUES
Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure
Contributing action or omission: Lack of standard evidence-based protocols and EHR order sets undermines consistent care delivery and increases the risk of delayed or inadequate treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve readiness, standardize responses and support coordinated escalation.

• Create and communicate PPH protocols

Develop guidelines based on safety bundles from organizations such as ACOG, WHO and the National Partnership for Maternal Safety. Include step-by-step instructions for risk assessment, active management of the third stage of labor, early recognition of PPH and suitable interventions, including uterotonics, tranexamic acid, fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion and surgical options. Establish systems for PAS screening in patients with prior cesarean delivery, with referral pathways to centers with multidisciplinary PAS expertise. Ensure that protocols are posted in labor and delivery units and accessible to all staff.

• Develop EHR order sets for PPH management

Create pre-built, evidence-based order sets, including medications (such as oxytocin, methylergonovine, carboprost and tranexamic acid), laboratory tests (such as CBC, coagulation studies, type and screen) and blood product orders. Implement automated EHR alerts for abnormal vital signs or lab results that may indicate PPH or deterioration.

• Establish and maintain PPH carts or kits

Make certain that hemorrhage carts or kits are available in all clinical areas where hemorrhaging patients may be treated. Stock with uterotonics, tranexamic acid, IV supplies, blood-collection drapes and balloon tamponade devices. Regularly inventory and restock carts, ensuring that they are organized in a consistent layout with clear labeling.

• Implement a functioning massive transfusion protocol

Ensure that these guidelines can be rapidly activated. Institutional protocols may vary in ratios of packed red blood cells, fresh frozen plasma and platelets (1:1:1); having a defined protocol is more important than the specific ratio. Coordinate with the blood bank to guarantee rapid availability of blood products.

• Run multidisciplinary simulations and debriefings

Create realistic PPH scenarios with varying severity and complication levels, assigning roles for all relevant team members. Use a structured debriefing framework such as Plus/Delta or PEARLS after each simulation and track performance metrics. Regular simulation training improves team coordination and reduces time to intervention.

ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Insufficient documentation of blood loss, interventions, clinical assessments and key communications compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support timely, complete documentation that reinforces continuity of care and clear clinical communication.

• Document quantitative blood loss in real time

Record cumulative blood loss at defined intervals during delivery and postpartum. Include the method of measurement (such as calibrated drape or weighing of materials) and the timing. Documenting these details demonstrates adherence to current standards and supports accurate assessment of hemorrhage severity. 

Sample documentation language: "QBL at delivery: 450 mL. QBL at 15 minutes postpartum: 650 mL. QBL at 30 minutes postpartum: 850 mL. Calibrated drape used for measurement."

• Record interventions with timing and response

Document the time of each intervention, such as uterotonic administration, tranexamic acid, bimanual compression or balloon placement, along with the patient's response. Include vital signs before and after interventions. Capturing these details demonstrates timely escalation and clinical decision-making. 

Sample documentation language: "Oxytocin 40 units in 1L LR initiated at 14:32 for uterine atony. Bimanual massage performed. Uterine tone improved at 14:38. Tranexamic acid 1g IV administered at 14:35. QBL at 14:45: 950 mL. Bleeding controlled."

• Document clinical reasoning for management decisions

When making decisions about escalation to surgical intervention, record factors considered, such as blood loss, vital signs, response to medical management, laboratory values and clinical assessment. This demonstrates thoughtful clinical judgment and supports defensibility. 

• Use templates aligned with safety bundles to standardize record-keeping

Ensure that documentation captures all elements of the PPH response, including QBL, vital signs, interventions, laboratory results, clinical assessments and key communications among providers. Standardized templates reduce documentation gaps and ensure completeness.
