TITLE: Shoulder Dystocia
RISK_BRIEF:
Shoulder Dystocia with Brachial Plexus Injury

SPECIALTY: OB/GYN

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Labor and Vaginal Delivery

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Shoulder Dystocia with Brachial Plexus Injury
Shoulder dystocia with brachial plexus injury is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm.
Mitigating Your Risk
With thorough risk assessment and counseling, proper delivery technique and coordinated communication and documentation, OB/GYNs can mitigate the risks associated with shoulder dystocia with brachial plexus injury and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for shoulder dystocia with brachial plexus injury and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns 
Malpractice claims involving shoulder dystocia with brachial plexus injury typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 51% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 49%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Shoulder Dystocia with Brachial Plexus Injury Risk [insert chart] 
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act on Diagnostic Finding
Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify and communicate shoulder dystocia risk factors during prenatal care and labor or failing to recognize the "turtle sign" and promptly diagnose shoulder dystocia increases the risk of delayed intervention and neonatal injury.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve identification of at-risk patients and ensure prompt recognition of shoulder dystocia.

• Perform risk factor assessment during prenatal care and on admission to labor

Assess for established risk factors during prenatal visits and at admission to labor and delivery, such as prior shoulder dystocia, maternal diabetes, suspected fetal macrosomia, maternal obesity (BMI ≥25) and advanced maternal age. Document evaluation and communicate elevated risk to the delivery team. Be aware that risk factors have poor predictive value, with 50%–70% of shoulder dystocia cases occurring without identifiable risk factors. Nevertheless, systematic assessment supports informed counseling and delivery planning. 

• Obtain ultrasound estimation of fetal weight when macrosomia is suspected

Order ultrasound biometry within 2 weeks of expected delivery when clinical assessment suggests macrosomia. Assess estimated fetal weight and abdominal-head circumference difference (≥2.5 cm is an independent risk factor). Recognize the limitations of ultrasound accuracy, especially at higher birth weights, and communicate uncertainty to patients during counseling. 

• Exercise heightened caution with operative vaginal delivery when macrosomia is suspected
Weigh the elevated risk of shoulder dystocia when considering operative vaginal delivery with suspected macrosomia. Vacuum-assisted delivery of a macrosomic fetus carries a threefold to fivefold increased odds of shoulder dystocia based on two population-based cohort studies each exceeding 1 million births. Risk compounds when multiple factors are present: the combination of an LGA newborn, diabetes and vacuum delivery yields an OR of 33 for shoulder dystocia. Additionally, history of prior shoulder dystocia is the strongest independent predictor of shoulder dystocia following operative vaginal delivery.
Before proceeding with operative delivery, assess estimated fetal weight, the adequacy of the maternal pelvis, fetal station and position and second-stage labor progress. Counsel the patient regarding delivery options and risks, engage in shared decision-making and document the discussion. When operative vaginal delivery is planned in the setting of suspected macrosomia, particularly with concurrent diabetes or prior shoulder dystocia, assemble the shoulder dystocia team in advance and confirm that personnel trained in internal maneuvers are immediately available.
When estimated fetal weight exceeds 4,500 g and there is a prolonged second stage or arrest of descent above +2 station, cesarean delivery is indicated rather than operative vaginal delivery.
• Recognize the "turtle sign" and diagnose shoulder dystocia without delay

Train all delivery attendants to recognize the "turtle sign"—defined as retraction of the fetal head against the perineum after delivery—as the hallmark of shoulder dystocia. Shoulder dystocia is formally diagnosed when gentle downward axial traction fails to deliver the anterior shoulder. Unequivocally announce "shoulder dystocia" to the team to initiate emergency response protocols.

• Assess for brachial plexus injury and skeletal fractures immediately after delivery

Perform an immediate neonatal exam after shoulder dystocia to assess for brachial plexus injury, such as asymmetric arm movement or Moro reflex abnormality, as well as clavicle or humerus fracture. Document findings and notify pediatrics immediately. Early identification allows for necessary referral and accurate prognostication.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention 
Contributing action or omission: Improper technique during delivery maneuvers—including excessive traction, fundal pressure or failure to escalate to internal maneuvers—increases the risk of brachial plexus injury and other neonatal complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure proper technique during shoulder dystocia management and reduce the risk of iatrogenic injury.

• Avoid excessive or laterally directed traction on the fetal head

After identifying shoulder dystocia, refrain from excessive downward or lateral traction on the fetal head, as this increases strain on the brachial plexus. Guidelines recommend applying only gentle axial traction no greater than the force used during routine delivery and never applying fundal pressure, which can worsen impaction and increase injury risk. Some experts recommend a 60-second "hands-off" period after head delivery to allow spontaneous shoulder rotation before applying traction. 

• Initiate McRoberts maneuver with suprapubic pressure as first-line intervention

Position the patient with hyperflexion of the thighs against the abdomen (McRoberts maneuver) with an assistant applying suprapubic pressure to dislodge the anterior shoulder. This approach resolves approximately 56%–75% of shoulder dystocia cases. If unsuccessful after 30–60 seconds, escalate promptly to internal maneuvers.  

• Perform internal maneuvers when external ones fail

Proceed to internal maneuvers, such as rotational maneuvers (Wood's screw) or posterior arm delivery, if McRoberts and suprapubic pressure are unsuccessful. Posterior arm delivery has the highest success rate (86.1%) compared to rotational methods (62.4%). Insert the correct hand (based on fetal face direction) through the sacro-posterior region to reach the posterior forearm. All providers should be proficient in at least two maneuvers beyond McRoberts. 

• Avoid repetition of unsuccessful maneuvers
When a maneuver fails, proceed to a different maneuver rather than repeating the unsuccessful one. Repeating failed maneuvers has been associated with brachial plexus injury, and persisting with multiple maneuvers does not reduce brachial plexus injury but increases the risk of severe maternal lacerations. It’s important to escalate to a different approach rather than repeat ineffective techniques. 
• Maintain awareness of time and escalate to rescue maneuvers when indicated

Track the time from head delivery to full delivery of the neonate. If standard maneuvers fail, consider last-resort interventions, such as intentional clavicle fracture, Zavanelli maneuver (cephalic replacement) or symphysiotomy. Each institution should know the time required to prepare for abdominal rescue and practice this during simulation exercises. 


ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Failure to communicate risk factors during handoffs, clearly define team roles during emergencies or coordinate care effectively increases the likelihood of delayed or suboptimal intervention in cases of shoulder dystocia.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support coordinated team responses during shoulder dystocia emergencies.

• Communicate shoulder dystocia risk factors during shift handoffs and care transitions

Use handoff frameworks such as SBAR to communicate identified risk factors for shoulder dystocia during care transitions between providers. Include estimated fetal weight, diabetes status, prior shoulder dystocia and any concerns about labor progress. Document handoff information in the medical record. 
• Designate a team leader and assign clear roles during a shoulder dystocia emergency

Establish protocols for team leadership and role assignments during incidents of shoulder dystocia. The team leader coordinates maneuvers, tracks time and directs escalation. Assign roles for McRoberts positioning, suprapubic pressure, timekeeping and documentation. Use closed-loop communication to confirm orders and actions. 
• Announce shoulder dystocia clearly and summon additional assistance immediately

When shoulder dystocia is recognized, announce "shoulder dystocia" unequivocally to activate the emergency response and summon additional personnel, including nursing support, anesthesia and pediatrics. This should also prompt the nurse to mark the time of head delivery. A clear announcement confirms that all team members understand the emergency and can assume their roles.

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider
Contributing action or omission: Failing to counsel patients about shoulder dystocia risk or conduct adequate informed consent discussions regarding mode of delivery undermines shared decision-making and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate patient counseling and informed consent when shoulder dystocia risk is elevated.

• Counsel patients with risk factors about shoulder dystocia and delivery options

Discuss shoulder dystocia risk and potential complications, including brachial plexus injury, when significant risk factors are present (such as prior shoulder dystocia with complications, estimated fetal weight >4500 g with diabetes or >5000 g without diabetes). Present delivery options, including the risks of vaginal delivery versus cesarean delivery. Document the discussion and the patient's informed decision. 

• Acknowledge the unpredictability of shoulder dystocia during prenatal counseling

When planning delivery, explain that 40%–70% of shoulder dystocia cases occur without identifiable risk factors, and the ability to predict it is limited. This honest communication sets appropriate expectations and shows that the provider has considered the risk. 

• Acknowledge that brachial plexus injury can occur without shoulder dystocia or clinician error
When counseling patients about delivery risks, explain that brachial plexus injury can result from maternal forces of labor alone. The ACOG Task Force on Neonatal Brachial Plexus Palsy concluded that neonatal brachial plexus palsy can be caused by endogenous (maternal) forces, exogenous (clinician-applied) forces or a combination, and that even properly applied traction can result in injury. In one institutional cohort, 42% of brachial plexus injuries occurred without shoulder dystocia and 9% followed cesarean delivery. Incorporating this point into prenatal counseling sets appropriate expectations, supports informed decision-making and strengthens defensibility by demonstrating that the provider communicated the inherent and unavoidable risks of delivery. Document that this discussion occurred.
• Communicate with the patient and family promptly after shoulder dystocia with injury

Following shoulder dystocia complicated by brachial plexus injury or other neonatal complications, speak to the patient and family as soon as the clinical situation permits. Explain what happened, the interventions attempted and the status of the neonate. Document the conversation. Timely, compassionate communication supports the family and demonstrates professionalism. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR
Provider's Failure to Recognize Responsibility
Contributing action or omission: Failure to maintain competency in shoulder dystocia management, escalate to internal maneuvers in a timely manner or conduct post-event debriefings undermines patient safety and team learning.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure provider competency and promote continuous improvement in shoulder dystocia management.

• Maintain competency through regular simulation training

Confirm that all providers who attend vaginal deliveries participate in regular shoulder dystocia simulation training, including both external and internal maneuvers. Simulation training is associated with a significant reduction in neonatal injury rates and improved adherence to management protocols. Use mannequin-based training supplemented by video instruction to reinforce proper technique. Evidence-based training programs that include dedicated shoulder dystocia scenarios include:
PROMPT (PRactical Obstetric Multi-Professional Training): A multi-professional obstetric emergency program with moderate-certainty evidence of reducing neonatal trauma due to shoulder dystocia
AHRQ Safety Program for Perinatal Care: Uses in situ simulation and TeamSTEPPS principles, with 89% of participating units implementing shoulder dystocia-focused simulation
PRONTO: A comprehensive obstetric and neonatal emergency program using the low-cost PartoPants birth simulator, validated in low-resource settings across multiple countries
Interprofessional blended learning: E-learning modules specifically designed for shoulder dystocia diagnostics and emergency treatment, combined with hands-on practice, achieved adequate or better performance in 95.9% of trainees 
In situ simulation, conducting drills on the labor ward itself, eliminates the need for a dedicated simulation center and allows training within the team's actual clinical environment. Where resources are limited, a "low-dose, high-frequency" model—brief, regularly repeated drills rather than infrequent full-day courses—can maintain skills with minimal disruption to clinical operations.
• Conduct a structured debriefing after every shoulder dystocia event

After any shoulder dystocia, hold a team debriefing to review the event, identify successes and discuss areas for improvement. Use a standardized debriefing framework. Debriefing supports team learning, identifies system issues and demonstrates commitment to quality improvement. 

• Report shoulder dystocia events through institutional quality review processes

Verify that shoulder dystocia events are reported through appropriate institutional channels for quality review. Aggregated data helps identify trends, evaluate training effectiveness and drive system-level improvements.

ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Incomplete documentation of shoulder dystocia events—including maneuvers performed, timing and neonatal assessment—compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document informed consent discussions regarding mode of delivery

When cesarean delivery is offered due to shoulder dystocia risk factors, record discussion of risks, alternatives and the patient's decision. If the patient declines cesarean delivery, document the risk discussion, the patient's understanding and their informed choice to proceed with vaginal delivery. 

Sample informed consent documentation language:
“Date: [Date] | Gestational Age: [xx weeks, x days] | Provider: Dr. [Name]
Risk factors identified: [List patient-specific risk factors, such as gestational diabetes (diet-controlled), estimated fetal weight 4,600 g by ultrasound at 37w2d, prior shoulder dystocia (2022, uncomplicated)]
Discussion of shoulder dystocia risk: Discussed with the patient that the above risk factors are associated with an increased risk of shoulder dystocia during vaginal delivery. Explained that shoulder dystocia is an emergency in which the baby's shoulder becomes impacted behind the pelvic bone after delivery of the head, requiring additional maneuvers to complete delivery. Potential complications discussed include:
Brachial plexus injury (nerve injury to the baby's arm); most cases resolve, but permanent injury can occur
Fracture of the clavicle or humerus
Fetal hypoxia possibly resulting in brain injury or death in severe cases
Maternal perineal lacerations and postpartum hemorrhage
Limitations of prediction discussed: Explained that ultrasound estimation of fetal weight has a significant margin of error, especially at higher birth weights and that 40% –70% of shoulder dystocia cases occur without identifiable risk factors. Discussed that most macrosomic newborns deliver vaginally without shoulder dystocia. 
Brachial plexus injury discussed: Explained that brachial plexus injury can result from maternal forces of labor alone, without shoulder dystocia or any error in clinical management, and that cesarean delivery reduces but does not eliminate the risk of brachial plexus injury.
Delivery options discussed:
Vaginal delivery: Risks include shoulder dystocia and associated neonatal and maternal complications as described above. The delivery team is trained in emergency maneuvers to manage shoulder dystocia if it occurs.
Cesarean delivery: Reduces but does not eliminate the risk of shoulder dystocia and brachial plexus injury. Risks include surgical complications, longer recovery, infection, hemorrhage and implications for future pregnancies.
Induction of labor: Earlier delivery may reduce birth weight and shoulder dystocia risk. Discussed that induction does not increase the rate of cesarean delivery but has not been shown to reduce brachial plexus palsy specifically.
Patient's decision: After discussion of the risks, benefits, alternatives and limitations in predicting shoulder dystocia, the patient [elected trial of vaginal delivery / elected scheduled cesarean delivery / elected induction of labor at [xx] weeks / requests additional time to decide]. The patient verbalized understanding of the risks discussed, including that shoulder dystocia cannot be reliably predicted or entirely prevented. Questions were answered. [If applicable: Patient declined recommended cesarean delivery. Risks of vaginal delivery with current risk factors were re-reviewed. Patient understands and accepts these risks. Informed refusal documented.]
Shared decision-making confirmed. The patient was an active participant in the decision-making process, and the plan reflects her informed preferences after review of the available evidence and clinical considerations.
• Use a standardized checklist or template for documenting delivery complicated by shoulder dystocia

Implement an electronic checklist or  template to document shoulder dystocia deliveries. Standardized checklists significantly improve documentation of critical elements, such as maneuvers performed, head-to-body interval, which shoulder was anterior and neonatal arm movement after delivery. Debrief with the delivery team to review documentation details and confirm consistency across EHR entries.

• Document the head-to-body delivery interval

Record the time from delivery of the fetal head to complete delivery of the body. This interval is critical for understanding the emergency duration and potential hypoxic exposure. Assign a team member to track time during the event. 

• Document all maneuvers performed in sequence

Record each maneuver, the order in which they were performed and the response to each intervention. Include McRoberts positioning, suprapubic pressure, rotational maneuvers, posterior arm delivery and any additional interventions. Document avoidance of fundal pressure. 

• Document neonatal assessment findings immediately after delivery

Include Apgar scores, arm movement symmetry, Moro reflex and evidence of injury (for example, brachial plexus palsy or clavicle fracture). Record whether and when pediatrics was notified. If brachial plexus injury is identified, include specific findings and planned follow-up. 

• Document communication with the patient and family

Record discussions about the shoulder dystocia event, any injuries and the follow-up plan. Include the patient's questions and information provided. Such records demonstrate proper communication and support continuity of care.

Sample documentation of delivery complicated by shoulder dystocia:
 Prenatal Risk Factors Identified: Gestational diabetes (diet-controlled). Estimated fetal weight 4,200 g by ultrasound at 37w4d. Risk factors for shoulder dystocia discussed with patient at 36-week visit; risks of vaginal delivery versus cesarean delivery reviewed. Patient elected trial of vaginal delivery after informed discussion. Decision and counseling documented in prenatal record on [date].
Labor Summary: Admitted at 39w2d in spontaneous labor. Shoulder dystocia risk factors communicated to delivery team during shift handoff using SBAR format, including gestational diabetes and estimated fetal weight. Labor progressed spontaneously. Second stage: 1 hour 45 minutes.
Shoulder Dystocia Event: Spontaneous vaginal delivery of fetal head at 14:31. Turtle sign observed—fetal head retracted against perineum. "Shoulder dystocia" announced to team at 14:32. Additional nursing, anesthesia and pediatrics summoned immediately.
Team Roles Assigned
Team leader/delivering physician: Dr. [Name]
McRoberts positioning: RN [Name] and RN [Name]
Suprapubic pressure: RN [Name]
Timekeeper/documentation: RN [Name]
Pediatrics: Dr. [Name] (present at bedside by 14:33)
Anterior Shoulder: Right
Traction: Only gentle axial traction applied. No lateral traction applied. No fundal pressure applied at any point during delivery.
Maneuvers Performed (in sequence)
McRoberts maneuver initiated at 14:32 with simultaneous suprapubic pressure applied by RN [Name] over the right (anterior) shoulder. Unsuccessful after approximately 45 seconds.
Posterior arm delivery performed at 14:33. Right hand inserted posteriorly (fetal face directed to patient's right). Posterior arm successfully swept across fetal chest and delivered.
Body delivered at 14:33.
Head-to-Body Delivery Interval: 75 seconds (14:31 to 14:33).
No episiotomy performed. [Or: "Episiotomy performed to facilitate access to posterior shoulder."]
Maternal Assessment Perineum: Second-degree laceration repaired with [suture type]. No third- or fourth-degree laceration. Estimated blood loss: 350 mL. No cervical or vaginal sidewall lacerations identified on inspection. Patient hemodynamically stable postdelivery.
Neonatal Assessment
Birth weight: 4,310 g
Apgar scores: 7 at 1 minute, 9 at 5 minutes
Bilateral arm movement: Symmetric, spontaneous movement of both upper extremities observed immediately after delivery
Moro reflex: Normal and symmetric bilaterally
Clavicle palpation: No crepitus bilaterally
Humerus: No deformity or crepitus bilaterally
Skin: No bruising or petechiae over shoulders or arms
Cord gases: Arterial pH 7.22, base excess -6
Pediatric Notification: Dr. [Name] present at delivery. Neonatal exam reviewed with pediatrics. No evidence of brachial plexus injury or skeletal fracture at initial assessment. Follow-up neonatal exam planned at 24 hours of life.
Communication with Patient and Family: Discussed shoulder dystocia event with patient and [support person] at 15:00. Explained that shoulder dystocia occurred during delivery, described the maneuvers performed and reviewed the neonatal assessment findings. Informed the family that the neonate is moving both arms normally and no injury was identified on initial exam. Explained that a follow-up exam will be performed. Patient's questions answered. Patient expressed understanding.
Attending Physician: Dr. [Name]
Date/Time of Note: [Date] 15:15
ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS
Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care
Contributing action or omission: Failure to address patient non-adherence to diabetes management, weight management recommendations or follow-up for suspected macrosomia may increase shoulder dystocia risk. 

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement with modifiable risk factors and ensure appropriate documentation.

• Counsel patients with gestational diabetes on the importance of glycemic control

Explain that diabetes care, including diet, glucose monitoring and insulin if needed, reduces the risk of fetal macrosomia and shoulder dystocia. Provide diabetes education resources and confirm the patient understands the connection between glycemic control and delivery outcomes. Document counseling provided. 

• Recommend physical activity and dietary measures for patients at risk of macrosomia

Advise patients with obesity or excessive gestational weight gain to adopt physical activity and dietary modifications to reduce the risk of fetal macrosomia. Document the recommendations and patient response. Physical activity coupled with dietary measures reduces fetal macrosomia in obese women. 

• Document patient decisions to decline recommendations

If a patient declines recommended interventions, such as cesarean delivery for high-risk macrosomia, labor induction or diabetes management, document discussion about risks, alternatives and the patient's informed decision. Continue to provide necessary care and record ongoing counseling.

• Ensure follow-up for neonates with brachial plexus injury

Provide necessary referrals for pediatric neurology, physical therapy and occupational therapy evaluation when brachial plexus injury is confirmed. Emphasize the importance of follow-up to the family and document the referral plan. Most brachial plexus injuries resolve, but early identification of severe injury allows for timely surgical evaluation.

With thorough risk assessment and counseling, proper delivery technique and coordinated communication and documentation, OB/GYNs can mitigate the risks associated with shoulder dystocia with brachial plexus injury and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurr

TITLE: Urologic Injury
RISK_BRIEF:
Urologic Injuries
SPECIALTY: OB/GYN

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Gynecologic Surgery, Cesarean Delivery, Peripartum Hysterectomy

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Bladder Injury, Ureteral Injury, Genitourinary Fistula, Renal Compromise
Urologic injuries are a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in OB/GYN.
Mitigating Your Risk
With meticulous surgical technique, timely injury recognition and repair and clear communication and documentation, OB/GYNs can mitigate the risks associated with urologic injuries and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for urologic injuries and provides strategies to help prevent them. 
Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving urologic injuries typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 76% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 24%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Urologic Injuries Risk [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSIS
Failure to Order Indicated Testing
Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain suitable preoperative imaging or postoperative evaluation when urologic injury is suspected delays diagnosis and increases the risk of severe complications, including fistula, sepsis and renal loss.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely identification of urologic injury risk factors and prompt postoperative diagnosis when injury is suspected.

• Obtain preoperative imaging when anatomic distortion is anticipated

Before a complex gynecologic surgery in patients with prior cesarean deliveries, endometriosis, large fibroids or suspected placenta accreta spectrum, consider preoperative imaging, including ultrasound or MRI, to assess bladder involvement or ureteral displacement. Perform this evaluation during surgical planning to inform consent discussions and operative approach. Identifying anatomic risk factors preoperatively enables proper surgical preparation and demonstrates adherence to individualized risk assessment. 
• Promptly order postoperative imaging when symptoms suggest injury

Obtain imaging when postoperative patients present with flank pain, fever, hematuria, abdominal distension, decreased urine output or peritonitis. The median time to diagnosis for complex lower urinary tract injury is 10 days postoperatively, with ureteral obstruction identified at 6 days and fistula at 12–14 days. CT urography with delayed-phase images is the gold standard for suspected ureteral injury, enabling detection of contrast extravasation or ureteral obstruction. For suspected bladder injury, CT cystography (retrograde filling with contrast via catheter) is the preferred modality. Delayed/excretory CT alone is inferior to retrograde CT cystography for bladder injury evaluation. Delayed imaging in symptomatic patients increases sepsis risk and renal compromise.

• Order serum creatinine when ureteral injury is suspected

Obtain serum creatinine for patients with postoperative symptoms suggestive of ureteral injury, such as flank pain, fever or ileus, to assess for obstructive uropathy. Rising creatinine levels postoperatively should trigger urgent imaging. Early detection of ureteral obstruction allows intervention before permanent renal damage occurs. 


CLINICAL: DIAGNOSIS
Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act on Diagnostic Finding
Contributing action or omission: Failing to recognize the intraoperative signs of urologic injury or misinterpreting postoperative symptoms delays treatment and increases morbidity.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve intraoperative injury detection and reduce delays in recognizing postoperative injuries.

• Perform intraoperative cystoscopy when injury risk is elevated

Consider performing a cystoscopy after a hysterectomy, particularly with a laparoscopic approach, concurrent urogynecologic procedures, difficult dissection or suspected injury. Cystoscopy has moderate sensitivity for ureteral injury and improves intraoperative detection.   The AAGL recommends that cystoscopic evaluation be available for all laparoscopic hysterectomies.        

• Confirm ureteral patency with visualization of bilateral ureteral jets

During cystoscopy, verify bilateral ureteral efflux with intravenous dye, such as sodium fluorescein or phenazopyridine or by visualizing clear urine jets. Absence of ureteral efflux requires further evaluation, including retrograde pyelography or ureteral catheterization. Thermal injuries may not be immediately apparent and can result in delayed ureteral necrosis. 

• Recognize that normal intraoperative cystoscopy does not exclude delayed injury

Remain vigilant for postoperative symptoms. Thermal injuries from electrosurgery can cause delayed ureteral necrosis that may not be detected during surgery. In one study, 60% of ureteral injuries were recognized postoperatively despite intraoperative assessment. Document that cystoscopy was performed with normal findings and counsel patients about symptoms requiring evaluation.            

• Educate patients about warning signs of delayed urologic injury

Before discharge, explain to patients symptoms requiring immediate evaluation, including flank pain, fever, decreased urine output, abdominal distension, urinary leakage or hematuria. Pain is the most common symptom of unrecognized ureteral injury (77%), followed by urinary leakage (13%) and fever (12%). Effective communication can encourage patients to seek follow-up care earlier; documenting that education demonstrates proper postoperative care.
CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL
Technique-Related Error 
Contributing action or omission: Errors in surgical technique, such as inadequate ureteral identification, improper use of electrosurgery and failure to recognize distorted anatomy, are primary contributors to urologic injury.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce the likelihood of intraoperative injury through systematic surgical technique and appropriate escalation.

• Consider prophylactic ureteral stenting selectively in high-risk cases
Explore prophylactic ureteral stenting for patients with the highest injury risk, particularly cesarean hysterectomy for placenta accreta spectrum, where stenting was associated with significantly lower genitourinary injury. Individualize the decision for other complex procedures based on anticipated anatomic distortion, weighing potential benefit against added procedural time and complication risk. Routine use during gynecologic surgery is not supported, and stenting may increase urinary tract infection risk.  Document the rationale for or against stenting in the operative plan.
• Locate the ureter before ligating vascular pedicles

Visualize the ureter at key points during a hysterectomy, including at the pelvic brim, along the pelvic sidewall and at the uterine artery crossing. In cases of distorted anatomy from endometriosis, large fibroids or prior surgery, consider ureterolysis to mobilize the ureter away from the operative field before ligating the uterine artery. Use cold dissection techniques during ureterolysis to preserve ureteral vascularity and avoid thermal injury. Visualizing the ureter at the pelvic brim and sidewall without retroperitoneal dissection may be inadequate, as the segment between the uterine artery crossing and the bladder is not visible in a transperitoneal view. The ureter can be safely dissected up to 15 cm without compromising viability. However, ureterolysis itself carries risk and should be performed only when necessary, based on intraoperative findings. Documentation of ureteral identification is present in only 30% of cases resulting in compensation claims. 

• Use electrosurgery judiciously near the ureter

Maintain adequate distance between energy devices and the ureter. Use the lowest effective power settings and avoid prolonged activation near ureteral tissue. Thermal spread from bipolar and ultrasonic devices can lead to delayed ureteral necrosis that is not detectable during surgery. Consider cold dissection or suture ligation when working close to the ureter. Electrosurgery causes 33% of ureteral injuries during laparoscopic gynecologic surgery, so using indicated techniques is key to avoiding adverse outcomes. 

• Reduce bladder injury risk during cesarean delivery

When adhesions are present, dissect under direct visualization and identify the bladder margins before making the hysterotomy. In cases with dense adhesions obscuring the vesicouterine plane, retrograde bladder filling (cystoinflation) with 200–300 mL saline can delineate bladder boundaries and has been shown in an RCT to significantly reduce bladder injury (2.8% vs 20.6%, p<0.0001) in patients with adhesive disease from prior cesarean deliveries. Avoid excessive lateral extension of the uterine incision, which is an independent risk factor for injury. Bladder injury during cesarean delivery occurs at a rate of approximately 0.28%–0.3% overall and is significantly more common with repeat procedures, adhesive disease, emergency cesarean and failed VBAC. Most injuries occur during uterine incision extension (60%) and peritoneal entry (22%) rather than during bladder mobilization alone.  

• Recognize when to convert or seek assistance

Convert from a minimally invasive to an open approach or seek assistance from a more experienced colleague or subspecialist if anatomy is severely distorted, bleeding obscures visualization or the procedure exceeds the surgeon's experience. Surgeon inexperience is the main risk factor for urologic injury. Converting or consulting during surgery demonstrates sound judgment and may prevent injury.

• Repair recognized injuries immediately or involve necessary expertise

Perform a two-layer closure with absorbable suture and maintain catheter drainage for 7–14 days if a bladder injury is recognized intraoperatively. Consult urology for ureteral injury repair. Immediate recognition and repair of ureteral injury is associated with lower complication management failure rates compared to delayed recognition. Intraoperative repair of bladder injury has a 98.4% long-term success rate. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Communication failures, such as inadequate intraoperative team coordination, incomplete handoffs and delayed consultation, increase the risk of unrecognized injury and delayed treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communications and support coordinated team responses.

• Communicate intraoperative concerns and findings to the surgical team

Verbalize key issues to the surgical team using closed-loop communication when difficult dissection, bleeding or potential injury occurs. Document intraoperative events, including difficult dissection, bleeding requiring additional hemostasis and any concerns for injury. Clear communication helps teams make proper intraoperative decisions and creates a contemporaneous record.

• Establish low thresholds for urology consultation

Consult urology promptly when a urologic injury is suspected intra- or postoperatively. Early subspecialty involvement improves outcomes for complex injuries, such as ureteral transection, injuries near the ureterovesical junction and injuries in patients with prior pelvic radiation. Document consultation requests and recommendations.
• Implement postoperative handoff protocols

Use frameworks such as SBAR when transferring care from the operating room to the recovery unit and from recovery to the floor. Communicate surgical complexity, intraoperative concerns, cystoscopy findings (if one was performed) and symptoms requiring evaluation. Document handoff information in the medical record.
• Communicate with patients about postoperative concerns

Promptly triage and arrange evaluation for patients who contact the office with symptoms consistent with urologic injury, such as flank pain, fever and decreased urine output. Document reported symptoms, clinical advice and follow-up plans. Delayed responses to patient-reported symptoms are commonly associated with adverse outcomes.

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider
Contributing action or omission: Failing to adequately inform patients about urologic injury risks during surgery or postoperative warning signs compromises informed consent and delays injury recognition.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients understand surgical risks and can make informed decisions, which improves defensibility in the case of adverse outcomes.

• Include urologic injury in preoperative informed consent discussions

Discuss the risk of bladder and ureteral injuries during informed consent conversations for hysterectomy, cesarean delivery and other pelvic surgeries. Share procedure- and patient-specific estimates of risk, such as those related to prior cesarean sections, endometriosis or laparoscopic approaches. Document the discussion and answers to patient questions. 
Sample consent documentation: "Discussed risks of surgery, including bladder injury (approximately 1%) and ureteral injury (approximately 0.3%), with higher risk given patient's history of [prior cesarean/endometriosis/etc.]. Patient verbalized understanding and consented to proceed."

• Provide written discharge instructions about warning signs

Before the patient is discharged, give them written guidance on symptoms that require immediate evaluation, such as flank pain, fever, decreased urine output, abdominal distension, urinary leakage and blood in urine. Include contact information for the surgical team and after-hours instructions. Document the provided instructions, confirming both receipt and patient understanding. 
• Communicate immediately after recognizing an injury
Speak honestly with the patient and family when a urologic injury is recognized intra- or postoperatively. Explain what happened, describe the steps taken to address the injury and outline necessary follow-up. Document the disclosure conversation. Transparent communication strengthens the patient relationship and demonstrates professional accountability.
ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR
Failure to Recognize Responsibility
Contributing action or omission: Failing to acknowledge surgical complications, dismissing patient symptoms or attributing symptoms to other causes delays diagnosis and erodes patient trust.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate accountability and timely response to potential complications.

• Maintain a high index of suspicion for urologic injury in symptomatic postoperative patients

Consider urologic injury in the differential diagnosis when patients present with flank pain, fever, ileus, decreased urine output or abdominal distension after pelvic surgery. Avoid prematurely attributing symptoms to other causes, such as constipation, incisional pain or urinary tract infection, without proper evaluation. Unrecognized ureteral injury increases mortality risk.

• Take ownership of postoperative complications

After suspecting or diagnosing a urologic injury, assume responsibility for coordinating care, involving necessary consultants and ensuring timely treatment. Avoid defensive responses that delay evaluation or treatment. Document clinical reasoning and actions. Professional accountability improves patient safety and defensibility.

• Participate in quality improvement and case review

Engage in departmental case reviews and quality improvement processes when urologic injuries occur. Identify contributing factors and implement changes to prevent recurrence. Documenting quality improvement activities demonstrates commitment to patient safety.

ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Insufficient documentation of surgical technique, intraoperative findings, cystoscopy results and postoperative evaluation compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document ureteral identification and surgical technique

Note ureteral identification in the operative report and specify the technique used, such as visual identification, ureterolysis or palpation. Include details about the approach to vascular pedicle ligation and any areas of difficult dissection. Documentation of ureteral identification is present in only 30% of cases resulting in compensation claims. Clear documentation demonstrates adherence to standard surgical technique.

Sample operative note language: "The ureters were identified bilaterally at the pelvic brim and traced to the level of the uterine artery. Ureterolysis was performed on the left, given adhesions from endometriosis. The uterine arteries were ligated with [technique] with the ureters under direct visualization."

• Document cystoscopy findings completely

When performing cystoscopy, record the indication, technique, visualization of bilateral ureteral jets (including timing and any dye used), bladder mucosal appearance and abnormalities. If cystoscopy was not performed, record the clinical reasoning. Complete documentation supports clinical decision-making and defensibility.             

Sample cystoscopy documentation: "Cystoscopy performed at conclusion of hysterectomy. Bladder mucosa intact without injury. Bilateral ureteral jets visualized within 3 minutes of IV sodium fluorescein administration. No evidence of bladder or ureteral injury."

• Document postoperative evaluation and clinical reasoning

Record the history, physical examination findings, differential diagnosis, diagnostic workup and clinical reasoning when evaluating patients with symptoms consistent with urologic injury. Include review and clinical interpretation of imaging results. Capturing these details demonstrates appropriate evaluation and supports defensibility.
• Document patient education and discharge instructions

Record that the patient received instructions about warning signs of urologic injury and seeking care. Include the patient's stated understanding. This record demonstrates proper postoperative care and engagement.
