TITLE: Cataract surgery
RISK_BRIEF:
Cataract Surgery

SPECIALTY: Ophthalmology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Cataract, Visual Impairment

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Endophthalmitis, Corneal Edema, Retinal Detachment, Wrong-Site/Wrong-IOL Surgery, Refractive Surprise, Posterior Capsular Rupture, Vision Loss 

Complications from cataract surgery are significant drivers of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in ophthalmology.
Mitigating Your Risk
With standardized verification protocols, meticulous surgical technique, comprehensive informed consent and clear patient communication, ophthalmologists can mitigate the risks associated with cataract surgery and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims from cataract surgery and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving cataract surgery typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 67% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 33%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Cataract Surgery Risk [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failure to implement evidence-based infection prophylaxis, inadequate management of intraoperative complications or delayed recognition and treatment of postoperative complications increase the risk of permanent vision loss.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate infection prophylaxis, optimal surgical technique and timely recognition of complications.

• Consider isolating eyelashes from the operative field during draping
Consider using adhesive drapes, Steri-Strips or equivalent techniques to tape or drape eyelashes away from the surgical field before incision. The periocular flora, including organisms harbored on eyelashes, is the primary source of microbes responsible for most cases of sporadic postoperative endophthalmitis. Preventing eyelash contact with the incision site and intraocular instruments reduces the risk of intraoperative contamination.
• Apply povidone-iodine antisepsis to the periocular skin and conjunctival fornices immediately before surgery

Before incision, apply 5% povidone-iodine to the eyelids and conjunctival cul-de-sac with adequate contact time. This intervention reduces postoperative endophthalmitis rates. 

• Administer antibiotic prophylaxis at the conclusion of surgery

Consider intracameral injection of cefuroxime (1 mg/0.1 mL) or moxifloxacin at the end of the procedure. High-certainty evidence demonstrates that intracameral antibiotics reduce endophthalmitis risk by approximately 80%. However, this practice is not universally adopted; no FDA-approved intracameral antibiotic formulation is currently available in the United States and these agents must be obtained from compounding pharmacies.  
When intracameral antibiotics are not used, administer topical antibiotic drops (fluoroquinolone) at the conclusion of surgery and continue postoperatively. Topical antibiotics alone are less effective than intracameral injection for endophthalmitis prevention but remain a widely used prophylactic measure. When intracameral antibiotics are administered, the incremental benefit of adding topical antibiotics is uncertain.
Subconjunctival antibiotic injection is an alternative route used by some surgeons, but evidence indicates

If a posterior capsular tear occurs, perform anterior vitrectomy to remove vitreous from the anterior chamber and wound. Capsular rupture increases endophthalmitis risk up to 10-fold and is associated with higher rates of CME and retinal detachment. Administer intracameral antibiotic prophylaxis (cefuroxime or moxifloxacin) if not already given, as meta-analysis evidence demonstrates a significantly lower incidence of endophthalmitis in patients with posterior capsular rupture who receive intracameral antibiotics. Subconjunctival antibiotic injection may be considered as an adjunct, particularly when intracameral delivery is not feasible, though it achieves lower anterior chamber drug concentrations. Document the complication, management steps.
• Manage dropped lens fragments with timely referral
If lens fragments dislocate into the posterior segment, remove accessible material from the anterior approach, perform anterior vitrectomy to clear vitreous from the anterior segment and place an IOL. Refer promptly to a vitreoretinal specialist for pars plana vitrectomy. Delaying vitrectomy beyond 30 days is associated with poorer outcomes. Monitor carefully for elevated IOP, inflammation and corneal edema in the interim





CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Technical errors during phacoemulsification, inadequate management of challenging anatomy or failure to adapt technique to high-risk features increase the risk of intraoperative complications and poor visual outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize surgical technique and reduce intraoperative complications.

• Identify high-risk ocular features during preoperative evaluation

Assess for pseudoexfoliation syndrome, zonular laxity, shallow anterior chamber, dense cataracts, small pupils and prior vitreoretinal surgery. Those factors increase the risk of posterior capsular rupture (up to 9% in eyes with prior pars plana vitrectomy). Plan for adjunctive devices (capsular tension rings, iris hooks) when indicated. 

• Recognize and prepare for intraoperative floppy iris syndrome

Document current or prior alpha-1 antagonist use (tamsulosin, silodosin) during preoperative history. Consider temporarily suspending alpha-blockers preoperatively based on clinical judgment, though the benefit of discontinuation is uncertain. Regardless of medication status, prepare for iris billowing and prolapse with appropriate surgical modifications, including pharmacologic pupil management, high-viscosity ophthalmic viscosurgical devices and mechanical iris-dilating devices as needed.
• Optimize surgical parameters to minimize endothelial trauma

In patients with corneal endotheliopathy or Fuchs dystrophy, use dispersive ophthalmic viscosurgical devices and optimize machine parameters to reduce cumulative ultrasound time and fluid movement. If corneal decompensation is likely in the near future or a combined endothelial keratoplasty procedure is planned, consider targeting a more myopic refraction to compensate for the potential hyperopic shift induced by endothelial keratoplasty (approximately +0.4 to +0.8 depending on the procedure). 


CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL 
Wrong Patient, Site, Procedure or Implant

Contributing action or omission: Failure to implement verification protocols, inadequate site marking or breakdown in communication during surgical time-out increase the risk of wrong-eye surgery or wrong-IOL implantation.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to prevent wrong-site and wrong-IOL surgical errors through redundant verification at multiple points.

• Verify operative eye and IOL selection at multiple points before surgery day

Document the operative eye, IOL model and power and refractive target in the written surgery order. Measure and compare axial length for both eyes, even if surgery is not planned for the fellow eye. As an additional safety cross check, consider performing IOL power calculations for both eyes to help verify the IOL power of the surgical eye. Communicate key information to surgery staff via written or electronic documentation rather than verbally to minimize transcription errors. 

• Mark the operative eye before the patient enters the procedure room

With the patient involved, mark the operative site before the procedure. Use an unambiguous marking method that remains visible after skin preparation and draping. The mark should be made by the surgeon or a qualified delegate who is familiar with the patient and will be present during the procedure. If the surgeon does not personally place the mark, they should verify that the correct eye is marked during preoperative preparation.

• Conduct a comprehensive time-out immediately before incision

Involve the entire surgical team in verifying patient identity with two identifiers, operative eye, procedure and IOL model and power. Resolve any discrepancies with a hard stop before proceeding. Begin only when all team members agree. 

• Display the surgical plan in the operating room

Keep the patient’s name, eye laterality and IOL power visible on a whiteboard or printed plan near the microscope for final verification before IOL implantation.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate handoffs between clinic and surgical facility, unclear communication of IOL calculations or failure to communicate high-risk features increase the likelihood of surgical errors and complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and ensure accurate information sharing across care settings.

• Ensure seamless information exchange between clinic and surgical sites

When clinic and surgical facility use separate record systems, communicate key information via written or electronic documentation. Include operative eye, IOL model and power, refractive target, astigmatism management plan and any high-risk features. Implement checks and balances to minimize transcription errors. 

• Double-check IOL power calculations before surgery

Establish a process in which all IOL powers are verified in advance of surgery. When calculations appear inaccurate, repeat testing using the same modality and consider verification with another device. Flag eyes with prior refractive surgery, extreme axial lengths or other factors that increase the uncertainty of calculations. 

• Communicate ocular comorbidities that affect visual prognosis

Document and communicate conditions that may limit visual improvement (macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy, glaucoma or other optic neuropathy, corneal disease). This information is essential for setting appropriate patient expectations and for postoperative providers to understand anticipated outcomes. 

• Establish clear protocols for postoperative care transfer
Ensure that co-management or care transfer includes a written agreement, patient consent and a documented care plan. In co-management arrangements, the operating surgeon remains responsible until postoperative recovery is complete. When a formal transfer of care occurs, responsibility shifts to the receiving qualified health care provider functioning within their scope of practice. In either arrangement, patients should always have access to an ophthalmologist for appropriate care if serious problems arise.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION 
Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate informed consent, failure to set realistic expectations or insufficient education about postoperative warning signs increase the risk of patient dissatisfaction and delayed recognition of complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients understand the cataract procedure, have realistic expectations of the outcome and know when to seek postoperative care.

• Obtain informed consent after discussing risks, benefits and expected outcomes

Discuss the anticipated refractive outcome, potential postoperative need for glasses, risks of surgery (including endophthalmitis, posterior capsular rupture, CME, retinal detachment, refractive surprise and corneal edema) and alternatives to surgery. Document the discussion and the patient's questions and answers to them. 

• Establish criteria for successful outcome with the patient before surgery

Discuss working distance preferences, potential limitations due to ocular comorbidities and the possibility that glasses may still be needed. Ensure that patients choosing premium IOLs understand potential visual phenomena (halos, glare) and that outcomes aren't guaranteed. 

• Offer educational materials to enhance understanding
Provide concise, easy-to-read consent information and video presentations to improve patient comprehension of risks, benefits and alternatives. Clear understanding may reduce indemnity claims from inadequate consent. 

• Provide postoperative instructions including warning signs

Before discharge, ensure patients understand medication instructions, activity restrictions, required follow-up visits and symptoms requiring immediate evaluation (pain, decreased vision, redness, new floaters). Provide written instructions and confirm patient understanding. Assess barriers to communication including language or hearing impairment.
 ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of preoperative evaluation, informed consent discussions, intraoperative events or postoperative care compromises continuity and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document the preoperative evaluation, including indications and risk factors

Ensure that documentation accurately reflects symptoms, findings and indications for treatment. Record ocular comorbidities that may affect visual outcome, high-risk surgical features and relevant systemic conditions (alpha-1 antagonist use, diabetes, anticoagulation). Document biometry results and IOL selection rationale. 

• Conduct and record informed consent discussions

Record discussion of risks, benefits, alternatives and expected outcomes. Document the anticipated refractive target and patient understanding that glasses may still be needed. For patients with ocular comorbidities, document discussion of potential limitations in visual improvement. 

Sample documentation language: "Discussed risks of cataract surgery including infection, bleeding, retinal detachment, need for additional surgery, and possibility of refractive surprise requiring glasses or additional procedures. Patient understands that macular degeneration may limit visual improvement. Target refraction -0.50 D OD discussed. Patient's questions answered; informed consent obtained."

• Document completion of surgical safety verification steps

Record completion of pre-procedure verification, site marking and time-out. Note that the surgical team confirmed patient identity, operative eye and IOL selection. Documentation demonstrates adherence to safety protocols. 

• Record intraoperative events and complications

Document surgical technique, complications encountered, management steps and final IOL placement. Document IOL model, power and orientation (for toric lenses). If complications occur, document their recognition, management and factors that may affect postoperative course or prognosis. 

Sample documentation language: "Uncomplicated phacoemulsification OD with posterior chamber IOL implantation. [Model/Power] placed in capsular bag. Intracameral moxifloxacin administered. No posterior capsular rupture. Wound secure without leak."


ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUES

Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure

Contributing action or omission: Lack of protocols for surgical verification, inconsistent time-out procedures or inadequate systems for IOL verification undermine consistent care delivery and increases the risk of preventable errors.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve readiness, standardize response and prevent errors.

• Implement a structured verification protocol

Adopt and administer a verification protocol, such as the AAO Wrong-Site Wrong-IOL Surgery Checklist or an equivalent institutional protocol, at three critical junctures: before anesthesia, before incision and before the patient leaves the operating room. Adherence to presurgical protocols results in fewer surgical errors. Adapt the checklist to fit the practice setting while maintaining core safety elements including patient identification, operative eye confirmation and IOL verification. 

• Develop standard processes for IOL ordering and verification

Create systems that require independent verification of IOL model and power before the lens is opened. Implement barcode scanning or other technology-assisted verification when available. Ensure that the IOL label is checked against the operative plan before implantation. 

• Establish policies for managing discrepancies discovered during verification

Define protocols for hard stops and resolution of discrepancies. Empower all team members to halt a procedure if concerns arise. Document resolution of discrepancies before proceeding. 

• Conduct regular team training and simulation exercises

Train all surgical team members on verification protocols, time-out procedures and closed-loop communication. Use simulation to practice response to discrepancies and complications. Track compliance with protocols and address deviations through quality improvement processes. 

• Ensure adherence to instrument cleaning and sterilization protocols to prevent toxic anterior segment syndrome (TASS)
Establish and maintain facility protocols for cleaning and sterilizing intraocular surgical instruments to prevent toxic anterior segment syndrome (TASS) caused by contaminants introduced into the anterior chamber during surgery. TASS occurs in approximately 0.22% of cases and can cause permanent corneal decompensation and secondary glaucoma. The most common causes are inadequate flushing of phacoemulsification and irrigation/aspiration handpieces, use of enzymatic detergents and use of ultrasonic baths. Follow the AAO/ASCRS/OOSS Guidelines for Cleaning and Sterilization of Intraocular Surgical Instruments. Avoid agents not specifically formulated for intraocular use. If a cluster of postoperative inflammation cases occurs, investigate for TASS and report to the ASCRS TASS registry.
OVERVIEW:
Cataract Surgery

SPECIALTY: Ophthalmology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Cataract, Visual Impairment

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Endophthalmitis, Corneal Edema, Retinal Detachment, Wrong-Site/Wrong-IOL Surgery, Refractive Surprise, Posterior Capsular Rupture, Vision Loss 

Complications from cataract surgery are significant drivers of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in ophthalmology.
Mitigating Your Risk
With standardized verification protocols, meticulous surgical technique, comprehensive informed consent and clear patient communication, ophthalmologists can mitigate the risks associate
PRESENTING_CONDITIONS: Cataract, Visual Impairment
ADVERSE_OUTCOMES: Endophthalmitis, Corneal Edema, Retinal Detachment, Wrong-Site/Wrong-IOL Surgery, Refractive Surprise, Posterior Capsular Rupture, Vision Loss

TITLE: Lasik surgery
RISK_BRIEF:
LASIK Surgery

SPECIALTY: Ophthalmology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Refractive Error (Myopia, Hyperopia, Astigmatism)

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Corneal Ectasia, Loss of Best-Corrected Visual Acuity, Persistent Dry Eye, Visual Disturbances

LASIK surgery is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in ophthalmology.
Mitigating Your Risk
With rigorous patient selection, comprehensive informed consent and structured postoperative surveillance, ophthalmologists can mitigate the risks associated with LASIK surgery and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for LASIK surgery and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving LASIK surgery typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 85% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 15%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving LASIK Surgery Risk [insert chart] 

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify contraindications, risk factors for ectasia or conditions that increase complication risk leads to inappropriate patient selection and adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure comprehensive preoperative evaluation and identification of patients at elevated risk for complications.

• Obtain comprehensive ocular and medical history before surgery

During the initial consultation, systematically screen for absolute contraindications (unstable refraction, keratoconus or ectasia, insufficient corneal thickness, visually significant cataract, uncontrolled glaucoma, uncontrolled external disease, uncontrolled autoimmune disease, unrealistic expectations) and relative contraindications (history of HSV/VZV keratitis, inadequately controlled dry eye, diabetes, pregnancy/lactation, abnormal topography suggesting subclinical keratoconus, certain systemic medications). Document refractive stability (minimum 1–2 years), contact lens wear history and the clinical rationale for proceeding or recommending against surgery.

• Perform corneal topography/tomography on all candidates

Obtain computerized corneal topography and tomography to evaluate for irregular astigmatism, corneal warpage or signs of keratoconus or other ectatic conditions. Use validated indices such as the Belin-Ambrosio enhanced ectasia display (BAD-D) to identify subclinical disease. Abnormal topography is the most significant risk factor for postoperative ectasia. Document topographic findings and interpretation in the medical record.

• Measure central corneal thickness and calculate residual stromal bed

Obtain pachymetry during preoperative evaluation to identify thin corneas and estimate residual stromal bed thickness. A minimum of 250 μm residual stromal bed has been suggested as a safety threshold, though no absolute value guarantees ectasia will not occur. Calculate the percentage of tissue altered (PTA); values ≥40% are associated with higher ectasia risk. Document calculations and the clinical decision-making process.
• Evaluate tear film and ocular surface

Assess for dry eye disease using history, slit-lamp examination and objective testing (Schirmer's test, tear break-up time). Uncontrolled external disease, including blepharitis, dry eye syndrome, and atopy/allergy, is an absolute contraindication to LASIK per the AAO PPP.  Inadequately controlled dry eye is a relative contraindication. Pre-existing dry eye can be significantly aggravated by surgery. Document baseline dry eye status and any treatment initiated before surgery.

• Assess psychological readiness and expectations

Screen for unrealistic expectations, uncontrolled anxiety or depression and history of chronic pain syndromes. Depressive symptoms have been associated with decreased patient satisfaction with visual quality after LASIK. Uncontrolled mental illness is listed as a contraindication in AAO guidelines. Document the assessment of patient expectations and psychological readiness.


CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failure to appropriately manage postoperative complications such as diffuse lamellar keratitis, flap displacement or infection increases the risk of permanent visual loss.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely recognition and appropriate management of postoperative complications.

• Perform postoperative examination within 36  of surgery

Conduct slit-lamp examination documenting visual acuity, flap position, epithelial integrity, interface clarity and presence of any infiltrates or inflammation. Evaluate for epithelial irregularity, interface debris, corneal edema, diffuse or focal infiltrates and microstriae or macrostriae. Early detection of complications enables prompt intervention before permanent damage occurs.

• Recognize and treat diffuse lamellar keratitis promptly

Treat mild cases with increased frequency of topical corticosteroids. For severe or rapidly progressing DLK with central involvement, consider flap lift with interface irrigation to minimize stromal loss and refractive changes. Note: The AAO PPP (2023) states that data are not available to make an evidence-based treatment recommendation for DLK; current management is guided by expert consensus and clinical experience[7] 

• Differentiate DLK from microbial keratitis

Microbial keratitis presents with pain, redness, photophobia and focal infiltrates extending beyond the interface, often with anterior chamber reaction. Obtain scrapings for culture if infection is suspected. Initiate broad-spectrum topical antibiotics immediately, as delayed treatment of infection can result in severe visual loss. Document clinical findings that support the diagnosis and treatment rationale.

• Immediately address flap complications

If visually significant striae are present, promptly refloat and reposition the flap. Flap dislocation is most common within 24 hours but can occur months to years later following trauma. For epithelial ingrowth that is progressive, causing irregular astigmatism or threatening flap integrity, lift the flap and debride the interface. Document the complication, intervention and outcome.

• Monitor for and manage corneal ectasia

Obtain corneal topography/tomography if ectasia is suspected. Ectasia can present months to years after surgery with progressive myopia, irregular astigmatism and decreased BCVA. Management options include contact lenses, corneal cross-linking (FDA-approved for post-refractive ectasia, though generally less effective in post-refractive ectasia than in primary keratoconus per the AAO Corneal Ectasia PPP, 2024) and intrastromal corneal ring segments. Document progression and treatment decisions.



CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Technical errors during flap creation or laser ablation increase the risk of irregular astigmatism, decentered ablation and loss of BCVA.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure safe and accurate surgical technique.

• Verify patient identity, correct operative eye and treatment parameters

Before initiating a procedure, confirm patient identity and operative eye (correct laterality). In addition, ensure that treatment parameters are correctly entered into the laser computer. Use a standardized surgical safety checklist. This verification reduces the risk of wrong-eye surgery and incorrect treatment. 

• Ensure proper instrument calibration and maintenance

As intraoperative equipment malfunction can result in incomplete or irregular flaps, check and calibrate all instrumentation before the procedure. Verify femtosecond laser or microkeratome settings for intended flap thickness and diameter. Use sterile instruments for each patient. Document equipment checks in the operative record. 

• Maintain torsional alignment for astigmatic corrections

In cases of significant astigmatism or wavefront-guided treatment, take appropriate steps to ensure torsional alignment. Axis misalignment is a common cause of residual astigmatism and patient dissatisfaction. Mark the cornea at the slit lamp before surgery if needed. Document alignment verification. 

• Mark the cornea to ensure flap alignment

Perform corneal marking before flap creation to aid in accurate repositioning, particularly in cases of a free cap. When using a mechanical microkeratome, corneal marking must be performed before flap creation to facilitate flap reorientation in the event of a free cap, per the AAO PPP (2023). When using a femtosecond laser, corneal marking may also be performed to facilitate flap alignment, though free caps are rare with this technology. Document marking and alignment verification. 

• Inspect the flap before proceeding with ablation

When using femtosecond lasers, inspect the flap for imperfections before lifting. If significant flap irregularities are present (buttonhole, incomplete cut, free cap), abort the procedure and document the findings. Proceeding with ablation on a compromised flap increases the risk of irregular astigmatism and loss of BCVA.

ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of preoperative evaluation, informed consent, surgical technique and postoperative findings compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Thoroughly document preoperative evaluation

Record all elements of the refractive surgery examination: visual acuity with and without correction, manifest and cycloplegic refraction, corneal topography/tomography findings and interpretation, pachymetry measurements, tear film assessment, ocular motility and alignment and any identified risk factors or contraindications. Document the clinical reasoning for proceeding with surgery or recommending against it. 

Sample documentation language: "Corneal topography demonstrates regular astigmatism with no evidence of keratoconus or forme fruste keratoconus. BAD-D value 1.2 (normal). Central corneal thickness 545 μm by ultrasound pachymetry. Calculated residual stromal bed 298 μm with planned 100 μm flap and 147 μm ablation. PTA 45%—exceeds the 40% threshold associated with elevated ectasia risk. Given elevated PTA, discussed alternative procedures including PRK/surface ablation. Patient counseled that surface ablation eliminates flap-related ectasia risk contribution. Patient elected PRK."

• Document informed consent discussion

Record that the patient was informed of risks, benefits and alternatives. Specifically document discussion of: potential for residual refractive error which may necessitate glasses or contacts, loss of BCVA, night vision symptoms (glare, halos), infection, corneal ectasia, dry eye, effect on future IOL calculations and the possibility of needing enhancement procedures. Record patient questions and responses provided. Note that the patient had opportunity to have all questions answered before surgery. 

• Maintain detailed operative records

Document patient identification verification, operative eye confirmation, laser platform and settings used, flap creation parameters (thickness, diameter, hinge position), intraoperative complications and their management and flap repositioning. Include laser treatment printouts in the medical record. 

• Provide patients with preoperative and postoperative records

Per AAO recommendations, provide patients with a record listing diagnosis, preoperative keratometry readings and refraction as well as stable postoperative refraction. This information is essential for future eye care, particularly cataract surgery IOL calculations. Document that records were provided to the patient. 

• Document postoperative findings at each visit

Record visual acuity, slit-lamp findings (flap position, interface clarity, epithelial integrity), complications and treatment. Document patient-reported symptoms including dry eye, visual disturbances and satisfaction with the outcome. Continue documentation until refractive stability is achieved and complications have resolved.


ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUE

Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure

Contributing action or omission: Lack of standardized protocols for patient selection, informed consent and postoperative monitoring undermines consistent care delivery and increases liability exposure.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize processes and support consistent, defensible care.

• Develop patient selection protocols

Create evidence-based protocols incorporating AAO contraindications and relative contraindications. Include checklists for required preoperative testing (topography/tomography, pachymetry, refraction, dry eye assessment). Protocols reduce the chance of overlooking disqualifying conditions and demonstrate adherence to the standard of care. 

• Implement structured informed consent processes

Develop comprehensive informed consent documents that address all elements recommended by AAO guidelines: range of expected outcomes, possibility of residual refractive error, loss of BCVA, night vision symptoms, infection, corneal ectasia, dry eye, effect on future cataract surgery calculations and postoperative care arrangements. Ensure that patients have adequate time to review materials and ask questions before surgery day. Document obtaining informed consent and answering patient questions. 

• Establish protocols for contact lens discontinuation

Create and communicate clear guidelines for contact lens discontinuation before preoperative evaluation and surgery, as contact lens-induced corneal warpage can lead to inaccurate measurements and poor outcomes. Spherical soft lenses should be discontinued for at least 3 days to 2 weeks; toric and rigid lenses require longer discontinuation. Document compliance with discontinuation requirements. 

• Maintain equipment maintenance and calibration logs

Establish regular maintenance schedules for excimer lasers, femtosecond lasers and microkeratomes. Document all calibration checks, maintenance activities and equipment issues. Equipment logs demonstrate due diligence and can be critical evidence in litigation. 

• Create systems for tracking postoperative follow-up

Implement systems to ensure that patients complete required postoperative visits. Track patients who miss appointments and document outreach efforts. Patients lost to follow-up may develop unrecognized complications. Documentation of follow-up attempts demonstrates appropriate care coordination.

With rigorous patient selection, comprehensive informed consent and structured postoperative surveillance, ophthalmologists can mitigate the risks associated with LASIK surgery and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurri

TITLE: Neurologic injury following spine surgery
RISK_BRIEF:
Neurologic Injury Following Spine Surgery

SPECIALTY: Orthopedic Surgery

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Degenerative Spine Disease, Spinal Deformity, Spinal Stenosis, Cervical Myelopathy

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Paralysis, Paraplegia, Quadriplegia, New Neurological Deficit

Neurologic complications following spine surgery are a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in orthopedic surgery.

Mitigating Your Risk

With rigorous preoperative evaluation, disciplined intraoperative safety practices, vigilant postoperative monitoring and clear communication and documentation, orthopedic surgeons can mitigate the risks associated with neurological injury from spine surgery and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for neurological complications following spine surgery and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving neurologic injury from spine surgery typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 87% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 13%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Neurologic Injury Following Spinal Surgery [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Incomplete preoperative neurological assessment and failure to identify patient-specific risk factors increase the likelihood of unrecognized intraoperative injury and delayed diagnosis of postoperative deficits.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure comprehensive preoperative risk stratification and establish baseline neurological function for postoperative comparison.

• Document baseline neurological examination before surgery

Perform and record a detailed motor examination using Manual Muscle Testing (MMT) for all relevant myotomes before any spine procedure. Include sensory examination, deep tendon reflexes and gait assessment when applicable. This baseline examination is essential for detecting postoperative changes and documenting preoperative status.
• Identify high-risk features on preoperative imaging

Review MRI and CT imaging to identify ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament (OPLL), canal-occupying ratio, multilevel T2 hyperintensity and spinal cord atrophy. OPLL is an independent risk factor for upper limb palsy, neurological deficit, dural tear and dural leakage. A spinal canal occupancy ratio >70% is independently associated with postoperative neurological decline. Document these findings and discuss their implications for surgical planning and patient counseling.

• Screen for modifiable risk factors that may affect neurological outcomes

Assess bone health with DEXA scan, CT scan or serum vitamin D3 level in patients with suspected osteoporosis undergoing spinal instrumentation. Undiagnosed osteoporosis increases the risk of instrumentation failure, screw loosening, cage subsidence and proximal junctional failure, which may result in neurological injury. Counsel patients with confirmed osteoporosis regarding increased postoperative risk and consider preoperative treatment optimization.
• Assess functional capacity and comorbidities affecting anesthetic management

Assess cardiac and pulmonary risk factors through focused history, including known cardiovascular disease, exercise tolerance and active cardiac symptoms. Refer patients with active cardiac conditions, poor functional capacity or multiple cardiac risk factors for preoperative medical evaluation per the 2024 AHA/ACC Perioperative Cardiovascular Management Guidelines before proceeding with elective surgery. Unrecognized cardiac disease increases the risk of intraoperative hypotension, a modifiable risk factor for spinal cord hypoperfusion and neurological injury. 
Communicate hemodynamic concerns and MAP goals with the anesthesia team, particularly for cases involving cord compression, deformity correction or anticipated significant blood loss.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain appropriate preoperative imaging or to plan for intraoperative neuromonitoring increases the risk of unrecognized neural compromise and delayed intervention.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that preoperative diagnostic evaluation is complete and intraoperative monitoring is in place before surgery.

• Obtain MRI or CT myelography for preoperative assessment

Use MRI or CT myelography to confirm clinical radiculopathy or myelopathy and guide surgical planning. Multilevel T2 hyperintensity, T1 focal hypointensity combined with T2 focal hyperintensity and spinal cord atrophy each indicate a poor prognosis. Use these findings to counsel patients on realistic recovery expectations and to inform surgical timing decisions, as advanced cord changes suggest limited potential for neurological improvement even with successful decompression. Document imaging findings and patient counseling.

• Evaluate baseline monitoring feasibility in patients with preoperative deficits

Recognize that patients with severe preoperative motor deficits often have reduced waveform derivation rates and less reliable monitoring specificity. Distal muscles (particularly the abductor hallucis) tend to yield higher derivation rates even in patients with severe preoperative deficit. Discuss monitoring limitations with the patient and document alternative strategies, such as wake-up testing, if monitoring is unreliable.
• Implement multimodal intraoperative neuromonitoring when the spinal cord or nerve roots are at risk

Use somatosensory evoked potentials (SSEPs) and transcranial motor evoked potentials (MEPs) for procedures in which the spinal cord or nerve roots are deemed to be at risk, including deformity correction, procedures requiring instrumentation and cases involving cord compression. IONM is particularly important in high-risk cases including OPLL, thoracic cord pathology, revision surgery, osteotomy/vertebral column resection and adult deformity correction, where the incidence of neuromonitoring alerts and neurological complications is substantially higher. Multimodal monitoring combining SSEPs, MEPs and electromyography maximizes sensitivity and specificity. 
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Wrong-level surgery, failure to respond appropriately to neuromonitoring alerts, inadequate intraoperative hemodynamic management and delayed recognition of postoperative neurological deterioration increase the risk of permanent paralysis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support safe intraoperative technique, appropriate response to monitoring changes and early detection of postoperative complications.

• Verify correct surgical level before proceeding with decompression, instrumentation or fusion

Confirm correct surgical level with intraoperative imaging (fluoroscopy or radiograph) before decompression, instrumentation or fusion. Wrong-level surgery occurs in approximately 0.12% of spine procedures and is a significant source of malpractice claims. The thoracic spine carries the highest per-procedure risk (0.42%) due to limited anatomical landmarks, while the lumbar spine accounts for the largest share of wrong-level cases by volume with intraoperative disorientation at L4/L5 and L5/S1 accounting for 81% of wrong-level errors. A systematic localization technique using fluoroscopic confirmation at two stages (before incision and after facet exposure) eliminated wrong-level surgery across nearly 2,000 consecutive posterior lumbar procedures. Document the method of level confirmation and the confirmed level in the operative report.
• Respond promptly to significant intraoperative neuromonitoring changes

When monitoring changes occur, alert the surgical team and intervene to try to reduce the risk of adverse neurologic outcomes. Interventions may include reducing the degree of distraction, adjusting retractors, removing or adjusting grafts or hardware, checking a wake-up test or modifying remaining surgery. In Class I studies, 16%–40% of patients with evoked potential changes developed postoperative paraparesis, paraplegia or quadriplegia. 
• Maintain adequate mean arterial pressure during surgery

Coordinate with anesthesiology to maintain hemodynamic stability and avoid intraoperative hypotension, which is associated with postoperative neurological deterioration. If an intraoperative spinal cord injury is recognized, the 2024 AO Spine/AANS/CNS guideline suggests augmenting MAP to at least 75–80 mmHg but not higher than 90–95 mmHg for 3–7 days.

• Perform immediate postoperative neurological examination

Assess motor function as soon as the patient emerges from anesthesia. The "knee-up test" provides a simple method to detect postoperative motor deficits before extubation, with high specificity and good sensitivity. This test should be used as an adjunct to, not a replacement for, a comprehensive postoperative neurological examination. A formal motor examination using MMT should be performed as soon as the patient can cooperate. Compare to the preoperative baseline and immediately evaluate any new deficit.

• Closely watch the patient for delayed postoperative neurological deficits

Establish protocols for serial neurological assessments in the immediate postoperative period. Delayed postoperative neurological deficit (DPND) is rare but most cases develop within the first 48 hours. The most common causes are ischemic injury and cord compression. Patients with compression-related DPND have significantly greater likelihood of neurologic recovery than ischemia-related DPND. Pay particularly close attention to patients with preoperative severe motor deficit (MMT ≤3) to identify any worsening of already existing weakness. Preoperative severe motor deficit means intraoperative monitoring can be less accurate; physicians cannot rely solely on equipment to determine whether surgery was safe.

• Identify and urgently treat postoperative epidural hematoma

Order emergent imaging (typically MRI) if epidural hematoma is suspected. Symptomatic postoperative epidural hematoma is uncommon but is one of the most frequent causes of postoperative spinal cord injury. Patients typically present with severe axial pain, followed by radicular symptoms, motor weakness and sphincter dysfunction. If a hematoma is confirmed, perform surgical evacuation as quickly as possible and without delay. Some data suggest a trend toward better outcomes with evacuation within 6 hours, though this has not reached statistical significance. Earlier intervention is consistently associated with better neurological recovery.



ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Communication failures during handoffs, unclear escalation pathways and inadequate coordination between surgical, anesthesia and monitoring teams increase the risk of delayed recognition and treatment of neurological complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and enable coordinated team response throughout the perioperative continuum.

• Conduct structured preoperative briefing with the surgical team

During the preoperative sign-in, review the following topics with participating surgeons, the anesthesiologist and the neuromonitoring team: Monitoring modalities, anesthetic protocols (avoiding agents that suppress evoked potentials), warning criteria for possible neurological injury and planned responses to positive alarms. 
• Standardize postoperative handoff protocols

Use evidence-based handoff frameworks such as I-PASS for transitions from operating room to recovery. Standardized handoffs improve communication of neurological findings, intraoperative events and vital sign goals. 
• Define escalation triggers for neurological changes

Establish protocols for escalating care in cases of new motor weakness, sensory changes or bowel/bladder dysfunction, including who is responsible for evaluation and intervention. Ensure that nursing staff promptly reports any neurological changes, as delayed recognition of postoperative deficits reduces the opportunity for intervention.

• Educate patients and families about warning signs

Before patient discharge, offer clear written and verbal instructions on symptoms requiring immediate evaluation, including new weakness, numbness, difficulty walking or bowel/bladder changes. Provide contact information for on-call providers. Document patient education and stated understanding. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR

Failure to Recognize Responsibility

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate informed consent, failure to document risk discussions and incomplete documentation of intraoperative events and postoperative assessments compromise patient understanding and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate informed consent, comprehensive documentation and clear accountability throughout the surgical episode.

• Obtain and document comprehensive informed consent

Initiate the informed consent discussion during the preoperative visit to allow time for patient questions. Explain the diagnosis, disease progression, proposed procedure, risks (including paralysis, nerve injury, sphincter disturbance), benefits and alternatives, including no treatment. Use plain language and present information patients can easily understand. Document the content of the informed consent discussion, including patient questions and their understanding of risks, benefits and alternatives. 
Sample documentation language: "Discussed with patient the diagnosis of cervical stenosis with myelopathy and proposed C4-C6 laminoplasty. Explained risks including infection, bleeding, nerve injury, spinal cord injury with potential for weakness or paralysis (approximately 1 in 100 cases), dural tear and need for additional surgery. Discussed alternatives including continued conservative management, anterior approach and no surgery. Patient verbalized understanding and asked about recovery timeline. Questions answered. Patient wishes to proceed.”
• Document intraoperative safety measures and neuromonitoring events 

Document the key safety actions taken during the surgery, including the method of level confirmation, neural monitoring status and any steps taken to protect neural structures during decompression or instrumentation. Record baseline monitoring status, any significant changes during the procedure, interventions and subsequent monitoring response. If a wake-up test was performed, document the indication, findings and any modifications to surgical plan. This documentation demonstrates appropriate vigilance and response to potential neural compromise.
Sample documentation language: “Correct surgical level confirmed with lateral fluoroscopy prior to incision and again after facet exposure; images saved. Multimodal IONM with SSEPs, MEPs, and EMG was utilized throughout the procedure. Baselines obtained and stable. During rod reduction on the left side, a transient >50% MEP amplitude decrease was noted in the left tibialis anterior. Surgical team was immediately notified. Set screw torque was reduced and rod was repositioned. MEPs returned to baseline within 4 minutes. No further monitoring changes. Postoperative neurological examination was performed in the OR prior to extubation — see postoperative note.”
• Document serial postoperative neurological examinations

Record neurological examination findings when the patient emerges from anesthesia, at regular intervals during the period immediately after surgery and any time a change is suspected. Compare findings to the documented preoperative baseline. Document the time of examination, findings and actions taken in response to changes.
Sample documentation language: "Postoperative neurological examination performed at [time], approximately [X hours] after completion of surgery. Patient alert and cooperative. Motor examination: bilateral upper and lower extremity strength 5/5 in all myotomes tested (deltoid, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, grip, hip flexors, quadriceps, tibialis anterior, EHL, gastrocnemius). Sensation intact to light touch in all dermatomes. No bowel or bladder complaints. Findings compared to preoperative baseline examination dated [date]—no new deficits identified. Will continue serial neurological checks per protocol."

• Ensure timely recognition and documentation of complications

If a neurological deficit is identified, document the time of recognition, clinical findings, diagnostic workup, consultations and interventions. Document communication with the patient and family regarding the complication, its potential causes and the treatment plan. Timely, thorough documentation supports patient care and defensibility.

Mitigating Your Risk

With rigorous preoperative evaluation, disciplined intraoperative safety practices, vigilant postoperative monitoring and clear communication and documentation, orth

TITLE: Compartment syndrome
RISK_BRIEF:
Compartment Syndrome


SPECIALTY: Orthopedic Surgery

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Extremity Fractures, High-Energy Trauma, Crush Injuries, Vascular Injuries

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Compartment Syndrome

Compartment syndrome is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in orthopedic surgery.

Mitigating Your Risk

With vigilant monitoring, timely recognition and coordinated surgical response, orthopedic surgeons can mitigate the risks associated with compartment syndrome and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for compartment syndrome and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving compartment syndrome typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 75% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 25%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Compartment Syndrome Risk [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failure to obtain intracompartmental pressure measurements in patients with inconclusive clinical findings or unreliable examinations increases the risk of missed or delayed diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate use of objective diagnostic testing when clinical assessment is limited or inconclusive.

• Acknowledge the limits of clinical examination alone

Recognize that individual clinical signs (pain, pain on passive stretch, paresthesias, paresis) have low sensitivity but high specificity for ACS. However, when more than two clinical findings are present, sensitivity and positive predictive value improve. By the time all symptoms have developed, irreversible damage may have occurred. Avoid relying solely on clinical exams in high-risk patients—particularly those with tibial shaft fractures, high-energy fractures or dislocations, diaphyseal forearm fractures, distal radius fractures, femoral fractures, crush injuries, soft-tissue injuries without fracture, or vascular injuries requiring revascularization. Use objective pressure measurements when clinical findings are unclear.
• Measure intracompartmental pressure in patients unable to provide clinical information

Use a validated device to measure intracompartmental pressure in patients who cannot offer reliable clinical information, such as those who are unconscious, sedated or pediatric. Rely on delta pressure (ΔP) rather than absolute pressure; fasciotomy is indicated when ΔP is less than 30 mmHg. This method minimizes missed compartment syndrome and supplements equivocal clinical exams. 

• Consider continuous pressure monitoring for high-risk patients where available

Implement continuous compartmental pressure monitoring, where available, to track pressure trends and detect rising pressures before acute compartment syndrome (ACS) develops. A sustained ΔP below 30 mmHg for two hours is highly sensitive for ACS. This approach is especially useful for obtunded patients or those under regional anesthesia. However, continuous monitoring is not universally available and is not a substitute for serial clinical examination.

• Obtain serial creatine kinase measurements in patients with impaired consciousness

Monitor serial serum creatine kinase levels in patients at high risk for compartment syndrome who cannot reliably report symptoms. CK elevation may provide supplementary evidence of muscle injury; however, CK is not specific for skeletal muscle injury and is a late indicator of muscle damage. CK should not be used as a primary diagnostic tool for ACS and does not replace clinical examination or intracompartmental pressure measurement. 
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC
Failure to Recognize, Interpret or Act On Diagnostic Finding

Contributing action or omission: Failing to recognize early signs of compartment syndrome, misinterpretation of examination findings or over-reliance on late findings delays diagnosis and increases the risk of irreversible tissue damage.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve recognition of evolving compartment syndrome and ensure appropriate escalation of care.

• Recognize patients and injuries at elevated risk for compartment syndrome
Maintain heightened suspicion for ACS in patients with the following risk factors: tibial shaft fractures (associated with up to 36% of ACS cases), high-energy tibial plateau fractures and fracture-dislocations of the knee, diaphyseal forearm fractures, distal radius fractures, femoral fractures, crush injuries, soft-tissue injuries without fracture (up to 30% of ACS cases) and limb ischemia with revascularization. Patient-level risk modifiers include younger age with high-energy mechanism, anticoagulant use, prolonged limb compression or immobility, associated vascular injury and altered mental status, sedation or regional anesthesia that limits clinical examination.
• Treat disproportionate pain as a critical warning sign
Be alert to pain out of proportion to injury, as it is the earliest and most reliable sign of compartment syndrome. Pain that is well controlled at first and then becomes refractory or requires escalating analgesics and pain with passive stretch of the musculature within the involved compartment also are highly concerning. Treat increased pain or analgesic requirements as a red flag requiring immediate evaluation. 

• Abandon reliance on the "5 Ps" mnemonic

Focus on early indicators like pain, pain with passive stretch and paresthesias instead of pallor, pulselessness and paralysis, which are late signs more indicative of arterial ischemia. Understand that distal pulses are typically preserved until pressures exceed systolic blood pressure, at which point irreversible damage may occur. 

• Identify atypical presentations in special populations

Monitor children for agitation, anxiety and increasing analgesic needs ("three As"), as classic symptoms of compartment syndrome may not be present. In patients with regional anesthesia, patient-controlled analgesia or epidural catheters, evaluate breakthrough pain as a potential sign of compartment syndrome.

• Monitor for ACS in open injuries

Be vigilant in monitoring patients with open fractures for acute compartment syndrome, as an open injury does not exclude its development.
• Maintain suspicion despite negative initial examination

In patients with high-risk injuries, perform serial evaluations at regular intervals—typically every 1-2 hours—for a period of time guided by the patient’s clinical trajectory, generally up to 24-48 hours or as clinically warranted. Compartment syndrome can develop rapidly—clinical symptoms may appear within hours of injury—but onset may also be delayed; the exact time of onset is often uncertain and difficult to establish clinically. A single normal assessment is unreliable, and ACS can develop from relatively minor injuries even in the absence of fracture. 

CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL
Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate fasciotomy technique—including incomplete release of all compartments, insufficient incision length or premature wound closure—increases the risk of recurrent compartment syndrome and poor functional outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure complete surgical decompression and appropriate postoperative wound management.

• Perform fasciotomy emergently when compartment syndrome is suspected

When suspicion of ACS is high, do not delay surgical intervention for additional diagnostic testing. Irreversible tissue damage can occur within six hours of impaired perfusion, and fasciotomy should be performed as soon as reasonably possible after diagnosis. Orthopedic Trauma Association (OTA) expert consensus supports a target of within two hours. While evidence suggests outcomes are improved when fasciotomy occurs within 12–24 hours of ACS onset compared with later intervention, this represents the outer boundary beyond which outcomes significantly worsen, not an acceptable target window. The potential to salvage viable muscle outweighs the risks of fasciotomy in most cases.
• Completely release all compartments in the affected limb

When compartment syndrome is suspected, perform emergency fasciotomy using long, generous skin and fascial incisions to release all compartments of the involved limb. For the lower leg, release all four compartments (anterior, lateral, superficial posterior and deep posterior) typically through two incisions: one centered over the lateral intermuscular septum and one 2 cm posterior to the subcutaneous border of the tibia. Wide release is crucial regardless of technique.
• Leave fasciotomy wounds open at the conclusion of the procedure

Avoid primary closure of fasciotomy wounds, which is associated with recurrence of ACS. Approximate skin edges with elastic bands or vessel loops in a crisscross fashion and cover with moist dressings or vacuum-assisted wound closure. Plan for repeat debridement at 48–72 hours and delayed primary closure or split-thickness skin grafting as needed.
• Presume compartment syndrome in patients with vascular injuries

In patients with injuries undergoing revascularization, development of compartment syndrome should be presumed until proven otherwise. Consider prophylactic fasciotomy at the time of vascular repair in high-risk cases. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION
Communication Failure Between Providers
Contributing action or omission: Poor communication between providers—including inadequate handoffs, failure to communicate concerning findings and unclear escalation pathways—increases the likelihood of delayed diagnosis and treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support coordinated team response.

• Implement structured handoff protocols for patients at risk of compartment syndrome

Use standardized handoff frameworks such as SBAR when transferring patients with high-risk injuries between providers, shifts or care settings. Structured handoffs can help ensure the relaying of critical information including the patient’s risk status, current examination findings, pain trajectory, analgesic requirements and monitoring plan. Document handoff details in the medical record. 

• Consider establishing clear escalation triggers and notification protocols

Consider defining protocols for escalating care (such as increasing pain scores, increasing analgesic requirements, new neurological findings, concerning pressure measurements) and notifying providers. Tailor protocols to the institution’s clinical environment and staffing model. Ensure that nurses can reach the orthopedic team immediately. 

• Communicate regional anesthesia decisions and monitoring implications

When regional anesthesia is used in patients at risk for compartment syndrome, ensure clear communication between anesthesia and orthopedic teams regarding the type of block, expected duration and implications for clinical monitoring. Document the shared decision-making process and the plan for alternative monitoring strategies.

ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION
Documentation Failure
Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of serial examinations, clinical findings, pressure measurements and clinical decision-making compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document serial neurovascular examinations with specific findings

Record each compartment check with details: time of examination, pain level and character, response to passive stretch, sensory examination findings, motor function and compartment tension assessment. Document the examiner's name and any changes from prior examinations. Serial documentation demonstrates appropriate monitoring frequency and clinical vigilance. 

Sample documentation language: "Compartment check 0200 by [Name]: Patient reports 7/10 pain in left lower leg, improved from 8/10 at 2300 with current analgesia. No pain with passive toe extension or ankle plantar/dorsiflexion. Sensation intact to light touch in superficial peroneal, deep peroneal and tibial nerve distributions. EHL 5/5, FHL 5/5. Anterior, lateral, superficial and deep posterior compartments soft and compressible. No change from prior examination. Will continue q2h checks per protocol."

• Document clinical reasoning when compartment syndrome is suspected or ruled out

When concerning findings are present, document the clinical reasoning, differential diagnosis, objective measurements and the plan for continued monitoring or intervention. After ruling out compartment syndrome, document support for that clinical determination. 

• Document pressure measurements with technique and interpretation

When measuring intracompartmental pressure, record the device used, compartment(s) measured, absolute pressure values, concurrent diastolic blood pressure and calculated delta pressure. Document the clinical interpretation and resulting management decision. 

Sample documentation language: "ICP measurement performed using Stryker device. Anterior compartment: 38 mmHg. DBP: 72 mmHg. ΔP = 34 mmHg (>30 mmHg threshold). Clinical exam reassuring. Will continue serial monitoring q1h with repeat ICP if clinical change."


• Document patient education regarding warning signs

Provide written instructions and document the education and the patient’s stated understanding of warning signs requiring immediate return (severe pain, numbness, weakness, swelling). 


ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS
Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failing to address barriers to patient recognition of warning signs and return for additional care increases the risk of delayed compartment syndrome diagnosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and ensure timely recognition of concerning symptoms.

• Provide clear written and verbal discharge instructions

Ensure that patients with high-risk injuries receive instructions about warning signs of compartment syndrome: severe or worsening pain not relieved by prescribed medications, numbness or tingling, weakness and significant swelling. Provide instructions in the patient's primary language and at an appropriate literacy level. Use interpreter services when necessary. 

• Ensure that patients understand the time-sensitive nature of compartment syndrome

Explain that compartment syndrome can cause permanent damage within hours and that warning signs require immediate evaluation. Document this discussion. 

• Address barriers to emergency care access

Identify patients who may have difficulty accessing emergency care (transportation limitations, rural location, lack of phone access) and consider extended observation or admission for high-risk patients. 

• Document patient non-adherence and continued outreach

If a patient misses follow-up appointments or declines recommended observation, document the conversation, recommendations, risks explained and the patient's stated reasons for declining. Continue outreach by phone, patient portal or mail.
