TITLE: Malunion following ORIF
RISK_BRIEF:
Malunion After Open Reduction Internal Fixation (ORIF)

SPECIALTY: Orthopedic Surgery

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Fractures requiring open reduction and internal fixation

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Malunion


Malunion after open reduction internal fixation are a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in orthopedic surgery.

Mitigating Your Risk

With meticulous surgical technique, structured postoperative surveillance and clear patient communication, orthopedic surgeons can mitigate the risks associated with malunion after open reduction internal fixation and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for malunion after open reduction internal fixation and provides strategies to help prevent the

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns
Malpractice claims involving malunion after open reduction internal fixation typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 64% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 36%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.

Actions and Omissions Driving Malunion After Open Reduction Internal Fixation Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to
them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most
improve patient care and reduce potential liability.

CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failure to obtain appropriate postoperative imaging to assess reduction quality and monitor for secondary displacement increases the risk of unrecognized malalignment progressing to malunion.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely identification of inadequate reduction and early detection of secondary displacement before malunion occurs.

• Obtain immediate postoperative radiographs to confirm reduction quality and establish a baseline

Following ORIF, obtain orthogonal radiographs before the patient leaves the operating room or within the immediate postoperative period to document fracture reduction and implant position. While immediate postoperative radiographs rarely lead to acute management changes, their primary value is establishing a baseline reference against which subsequent displacement can be measured. This practice is widely adopted and supports ongoing surveillance throughout the healing period.

• Establish fracture-specific radiographic follow-up intervals

For extra-articular fractures, obtain radiographs at 6 weeks and 3 months postoperatively; for intra-articular fractures, extend surveillance to 6–12 months. The period from consideration of weight-bearing advancement through fracture union is when 98% of radiographically driven management changes occur. Follow-up intervals aligned with fracture type ensure detection of secondary displacement or hardware failure before malunion becomes established.

• Utilize advanced imaging when standard radiographs are inadequate

When plain radiographs are limited by hardware artifact or complex anatomy, consider CT imaging to assess articular reduction and alignment. CT provides superior visualization of articular step-off and rotational alignment that may not be apparent on plain films. For wrist fractures, digital tomosynthesis offers improved detection of cortical fracture lines compared with radiography with lower radiation than CT. 


CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Insufficient postoperative clinical and radiographic monitoring allows secondary displacement and hardware failure to progress unrecognized, resulting in established malunion requiring complex revision surgery.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support early identification of healing complications and prompt intervention before malunion becomes established.

• Implement structured clinical follow-up with defined assessment parameters

Schedule clinical evaluations at intervals appropriate to fracture type and complexity. A common approach includes visits at 2 weeks (primarily for wound assessment), 6 weeks, 3 months and 6 months for extra-articular fractures, with extended follow-up to 12 months for intra-articular fractures. Radiographic surveillance is most critical during the period from weight-bearing advancement through fracture union, when nearly all radiographically driven management changes occur. Follow-up intervals should be individualized based on fracture stability, patient risk factors and clinical progress.
• Monitor high-risk patients with increased surveillance frequency

Closely watch patients with identified risk factors (tobacco use, diabetes, osteoporosis, comminuted fractures) warrant closer monitoring. Smokers have 2-fold higher risk of nonunion after ORIF. Consider more frequent radiographic surveillance and earlier intervention thresholds for these patients. Document the rationale for modified surveillance protocols.
• Correlate radiographic findings with clinical examination

When radiographic healing appears adequate but clinical symptoms persist, consider advanced imaging to evaluate for occult malalignment or hardware complications. Patients with adverse radiographic outcomes (delayed union, malunion) demonstrate significantly worse functional scores and higher incidence of post-traumatic osteoarthritis.

• Establish clear criteria for intervention when secondary displacement is identified

Define protocols specifying thresholds for acceptable alignment by fracture location. For tibial fractures, accepted malalignment is shortening <10 mm, varus/valgus <5 degrees and sagittal deformity <10 degrees. When displacement exceeds acceptable parameters, early revision before callus consolidation is generally preferred, as it is technically easier and may reduce the overall period of disability compared with delayed correction of established malunion.

CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Failure to achieve and maintain anatomic reduction during fracture fixation is the primary technical factor leading to malunion.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize intraoperative reduction quality and construct stability to prevent malunion.

• Apply systematic reduction principles appropriate to fracture pattern

Utilize established reduction frameworks: direct reduction for tension-sided failures and indirect reduction through ligamentotaxis for compression-sided failures with comminution. For metaphyseal and intra-articular fractures, achieve anatomic reduction of articular surfaces; for diaphyseal fractures, restore length, alignment and rotation. Reduction quality significantly influences outcomes across fracture types. For example, anatomic reduction of proximal humeral type-C fractures resulted in lower complication rates than malreduction (20% vs. 41%, p = 0.027), and articular incongruity ≥2 mm after tibial plateau fixation is associated with worse functional outcomes. 

• Utilize intraoperative imaging to verify reduction before final fixation

Obtain orthogonal fluoroscopic views and, when available, intraoperative 3D imaging for complex periarticular fractures. Proximal tibia fractures treated with intramedullary nailing carry historically high malalignment rates, but modern techniques, including semiextended/suprapatellar approaches, blocking screws and percutaneous clamps have reduced malreduction rates to under 10%. Verify rotational alignment by comparison to the contralateral extremity when possible, as clinically significant rotational malalignment remains common even with contemporary methods.
• Select implant configuration appropriate to fracture pattern and bone quality

Match implant length, screw number and construct stiffness to the specific fracture pattern. Inappropriate device configuration, including inadequate plate length, insufficient screw number and construct stiffness mismatch, is a common contributor to locked plating failure. In osteoporotic bone, consider longer plates with fewer screws to improve mechanical leverage while minimizing bone damage. Avoid reliance on laterally based implants alone when medial comminution is present. 

• Address medial cortical support in peritrochanteric and metaphyseal fractures

When medial comminution prevents cortical contact, consider augmentation strategies or accept that the construct must function as a bridge plate with appropriate implant selection. Positive support of the medial cortex provides the best mechanical stability for intertrochanteric fractures; varus fixation demonstrates the poorest stability and should be avoided. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of reduction quality, postoperative imaging interpretation and clinical decision-making compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document intraoperative challenges and technical decisions

Record any intraoperative difficulties encountered and the rationale for implant selection. Include fluoroscopic images in the operative record when possible. Documenting these details demonstrates thoughtful surgical decision-making and provides defensible evidence of technique.

Sample documentation language: "Final fluoroscopic images demonstrated near-anatomic reduction of the articular surface with less than 2 mm step-off. Medial cortical contact was achieved with positive support. Rotational alignment was confirmed by comparison to contralateral femoral anteversion on lateral imaging."
• Document quantitative reduction quality with specific measurements on postoperative imaging

Record angular alignment in coronal and sagittal planes (degrees), articular step-off and gap (millimeters) and rotational alignment. Compare measurements to established acceptable parameters for the specific fracture type. Quantitative documentation demonstrates that reduction quality was assessed against objective standards, supports clinical decision-making regarding potential revision surgery and provides defensible evidence of reduction assessment.

• Document interpretation of postoperative radiographs

At each follow-up visit, record findings on radiographic review, including alignment, evidence of healing (callus formation, bridging), hardware position and any interval change from prior imaging. Avoid generic statements such as "healing well" without supporting observations. This level of documentation supports clinical decision-making and demonstrates ongoing surveillance.

Sample documentation language: "AP and lateral radiographs of the tibia demonstrate maintained alignment with valgus 3 degrees, no sagittal angulation. Early bridging callus is present at the medial cortex. Hardware remains in satisfactory position without evidence of loosening. Compared to 6-week films, progressive healing is evident."

• Document clinical reasoning when observation is chosen over intervention

When radiographs demonstrate suboptimal alignment but revision is not pursued, record the rationale, including patient factors, functional status and shared decision-making discussions. Capturing these details demonstrates thoughtful clinical judgment rather than failure to recognize the finding.

• Document patient education on activity restrictions and warning signs

Record that the patient received and understood instructions regarding weight-bearing restrictions, activity limitations and symptoms requiring urgent evaluation. Patients correctly answer only 64% of recall-oriented questions about their injury and treatment; 42% of patients nonadherent with weight-bearing restrictions could not remember their restrictions. Documentation of education provided supports defensibility when patients do not follow recommendations.
ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUE

Failure or Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure

Contributing action or omission: Lack of protocols for postoperative surveillance and follow-up tracking allows patients to be lost to follow-up before healing is confirmed, preventing early detection of malunion.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve systematic tracking of postoperative patients and ensure consistent application of surveillance protocols.

• Develop postoperative follow-up protocols by fracture type

Create institutional standards specifying radiographic and clinical follow-up intervals based on fracture location and complexity. For extra-articular fractures, standard radiographic follow-up of 3–6 months is considered appropriate; for intra-articular fractures, 12-month follow-up is recommended. Guidelines reduce practice variation and ensure that all patients receive necessary surveillance.

• Implement systems to track and recall patients who miss appointments

Establish processes to identify patients who fail to attend scheduled follow-ups and initiate outreach. Post-discharge phone calls significantly improve appointment attendance (70% for patients reached by conversation and 51% for patients by voicemail vs. 34% for no contact). Patients with economic barriers, tobacco use and lack of insurance are at highest risk for poor follow-up adherence and should be prioritized for outreach efforts.

• Create EHR-based alerts for overdue follow-up imaging

Implement electronic health record alerts that flag patients who have not completed expected postoperative imaging within defined timeframes. Implementing systematic tracking of overdue follow-up, whether through EHR alerts, scheduling dashboards or administrative workflows, represents a reasonable systems-improvement strategy. 
PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failure to address risk factors that impair healing and to address barriers to follow-up attendance, weight-bearing compliance and rehabilitation increases 




The strategies below work together to optimize


Counsel patients on smoking cessation as it is a modifiable risk factor for impaired healing. Smoking significantly increases the risk of fracture nonunion. 
• Implement structured postoperative education using teach-back methodology

Before discharge, provide education about the injury, treatment plan, weight-bearing restrictions and follow-up schedule. Use teach-back methodology where patients repeat information back until correct. This approach improves patient recall from 64% to 89% and increases adherence to DVT prophylaxis from 84% to 99%. Improved recall is the only modifiable factor associated with better adherence.

• Provide clear written instructions in the patient's primary language

Explicitly state weight-bearing status, activity restrictions, medication regimens and symptoms requiring urgent evaluation in discharge guidance. Provide guidance at an appropriate literacy level and in the patient's primary language. Patients with lower education levels demonstrate lower rehabilitation adherence. 
• Identify and address barriers to follow-up attendance

Screen for barriers including transportation, insurance status, employment constraints and substance use. Insured patients attend follow-up at significantly higher rates (71% vs. 46%); current tobacco users attend at only 30% compared with 56% for nonsmokers. Consider telehealth options, flexible scheduling and coordination with social services for patients with identified barriers.

• Document patient non-adherence and continued outreach efforts

When patients miss appointments or report non-adherence with restrictions, note the occurrence, education provided about risks and continued outreach efforts. If a patient declines recommended follow-up despite counseling, record the conversation, recommendations and patient's reasons for refusal. Continue outreach by phone, patient portal or mail.

TITLE: Neurological injury
RISK_BRIEF:
Neurologic Injury Following Spine Surgery: A High-Impact Malpractice Risk

SPECIALTY: Orthopedic Surgery

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Degenerative Spine Disease, Spinal Deformity, Spinal Stenosis, Cervical Myelopathy

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Paralysis, Paraplegia, Quadriplegia, New Neurological Deficit

Neurologic injury following spine surgery is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in orthopedic surgery.

Mitigating Your Risk

With thorough preoperative evaluation, appropriate intraoperative monitoring and vigilant postoperative surveillance, orthopedic surgeons can mitigate the risk of  neurological injury from spine surgery and reduce malpractice risk. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures associated with malpractice claims for orthopedic spinal cord injury and provides strategies to help prevent or mitigate them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving neurologic injury from spine surgery typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for [xx%] of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining [xx%]. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Neurologic Injury Following Spinal Surgery Risk [insert chart]


CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing Action or Omission: Incomplete preoperative neurological assessment and failure to identify patient-specific risk factors increase the likelihood of unrecognized intraoperative injury and delayed diagnosis of postoperative deficits.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure comprehensive preoperative risk stratification and establish baseline neurological function for postoperative comparison.

• Document baseline neurological examination before surgery

Perform and record a detailed motor examination using Manual Muscle Testing (MMT) for all relevant myotomes before any spine procedure. Include sensory examination, deep tendon reflexes and gait assessment when applicable. This baseline examination is essential for detecting postoperative changes and documenting preoperative status.
• Identify high-risk features on preoperative imaging

Review MRI and CT imaging to identify ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament (OPLL), canal-occupying ratio, multilevel T2 hyperintensity and spinal cord atrophy. OPLL is an independent risk factor for upper limb palsy, neurological deficit, dural tear and dural leakage. A spinal canal occupancy ratio >70% is independently associated with postoperative neurological decline. 
Document these findings and discuss their implications for surgical planning and patient counseling.

• Screen for modifiable risk factors that may affect neurological outcomes

Assess bone health with DEXA scan, CT scan or serum vitamin D3 level in patients with suspected osteoporosis undergoing spinal instrumentation. Undiagnosed osteoporosis increases the risk of instrumentation failure, screw loosening, cage subsidence, and proximal junctional failure, which may result in neurological injury. 
Counsel patients with confirmed osteoporosis regarding increased postoperative risk and consider preoperative treatment optimization.
• Assess functional capacity and comorbidities affecting anesthetic management

Assess cardiac and pulmonary risk factors through focused history, including known cardiovascular disease, exercise tolerance, and active cardiac symptoms. Refer patients with active cardiac conditions, poor functional capacity, or multiple cardiac risk factors for preoperative medical evaluation per the 2024 AHA/ACC Perioperative Cardiovascular Management Guidelines before proceeding with elective surgery. Unrecognized cardiac disease increases the risk of intraoperative hypotension, a modifiable risk factor for spinal cord hypoperfusion and neurological injury. 
Communicate hemodynamic concerns and MAP goals with the anesthesia team, particularly for cases involving cord compression, deformity correction, or anticipated significant blood loss.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing Action or Omission: Failing to obtain appropriate preoperative imaging or to plan for intraoperative neuromonitoring increases the risk of unrecognized neural compromise and delayed intervention.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that preoperative diagnostic evaluation is complete and intraoperative monitoring is in place before surgery.

• Obtain MRI or CT myelography for preoperative assessment

Use MRI or CT myelography to confirm clinical radiculopathy or myelopathy and guide surgical planning.[12] Multilevel T2 hyperintensity, T1 focal hypointensity combined with T2 focal hyperintensity and spinal cord atrophy each indicate a poor prognosis. Use these findings to counsel patients on realistic recovery expectations and to inform surgical timing decisions, as advanced cord changes suggest limited potential for neurological improvement even with successful decompression.
Document imaging findings and patient counseling.

• Evaluate baseline monitoring feasibility in patients with preoperative deficits

Recognize that patients with severe preoperative motor deficits often have reduced waveform derivation rates and less reliable monitoring specificity. Distal muscles (particularly the abductor hallucis) tend to yield higher derivation rates even in patients with severe preoperative deficit.
Discuss monitoring limitations with the patient and document alternative strategies, such as wake-up testing, if monitoring is unreliable
• Implement multimodal intraoperative neuromonitoring when the spinal cord or nerve roots are at risk

Use somatosensory evoked potentials (SSEPs) and transcranial motor evoked potentials (MEPs) for procedures in which the spinal cord or nerve roots are deemed to be at risk, including deformity correction, procedures requiring instrumentation, and cases involving cord compression. Multimodal monitoring combining SSEPs, MEPs and electromyography maximizes sensitivity and specificity. 
Alert the surgical team to significant monitoring changes, as recommended by the American Academy of Neurology and American Clinical Neurophysiology Society. 

• Coordinate a preoperative discussion with the surgical team

During the preoperative sign-in, discuss monitoring goals, anesthetic protocols, warning criteria for possible neurological injury and planned responses to positive alarms with other participating surgeons, the anesthesiologist and the neuromonitoring team.

CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Treatment Other Than Medication

Contributing Action or Omission: Intraoperative technical errors, failure to respond appropriately to neuromonitoring alerts and delayed recognition of postoperative neurological deterioration increase the risk of permanent paralysis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support safe intraoperative technique, appropriate response to monitoring changes and early detection of postoperative complications.

• Respond promptly to significant intraoperative neuromonitoring changes

When monitoring changes occur, intervene to try to reduce the risk of adverse neurologic outcomes. Interventions may include reducing the degree of distraction, adjusting retractors, removing or adjusting grafts or hardware, checking a wake-up test or modifying remaining surgery. In Class I studies, 16%–40% of patients with evoked potential changes developed postoperative paraparesis, paraplegia or quadriplegia. 
• Maintain adequate mean arterial pressure during surgery

Coordinate with anesthesiology to maintain hemodynamic stability and avoid intraoperative hypotension, which is associated with postoperative neurological deterioration. If an intraoperative spinal cord injury is recognized, the 2024 AO Spine/AANS/CNS guideline suggests augmenting MAP to at least 75–80 mmHg but not higher than 90–95 mmHg for 3–7.

• Perform immediate postoperative neurological examination

Assess motor function as soon as the patient emerges from anesthesia. The "knee-up test" provides a simple method to detect postoperative motor deficits before extubation, with high specificity and good sensitivity. This test should be used as an adjunct to—not a replacement for—a comprehensive postoperative neurological examination. A formal motor examination using MMT should be performed as soon as the patient can cooperate.
Compare to the preoperative baseline and immediately evaluate any new deficit.

• Closely watch the patient for delayed postoperative neurological deficits

Establish protocols for serial neurological assessments in the immediate postoperative period. Delayed postoperative neurological deficit (DPND) is rare but the majority of cases develop within the first 48 hours. The most common causes are ischemic injury and cord compression. Patients with compression-related DPND have significantly greater likelihood of neurologic recovery than ischemia-related DPND.
Pay particularly close attention to patients with preoperative severe motor deficit (MMT ≤3) to identify any worsening of already existing weakness. Preoperative severe motor deficit means intraoperative monitoring can be less accurate; physicians cannot rely solely on equipment to determine whether surgery was safe.

• Identify and urgently treat postoperative epidural hematoma

Order emergent imaging - typically MRI - if epidural hematoma is suspected. Symptomatic postoperative epidural hematoma is uncommon but is one of the most frequent causes of postoperative spinal cord injury. Patients typically present with severe axial pain, followed by radicular symptoms, motor weakness and sphincter dysfunction. 
If a hematoma is confirmed, perform surgical evacuation as quickly as possible and without delay. Some data suggest a trend toward better outcomes with evacuation within 6 hours, though this has not reached statistical significance. Earlier intervention is consistently associated with better neurological recovery. 



ADMINISTRATION: COMMUNICATION

Insufficient Communication Between Providers

Contributing Action or Omission: Communication failures during handoffs, unclear escalation pathways and inadequate coordination between surgical, anesthesia and monitoring teams increase the risk of delayed recognition and treatment of neurological complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and enable coordinated team response throughout the perioperative continuum.

• Conduct structured preoperative briefing with the surgical team

During the preoperative sign-in, review the following topics with participating surgeons, the anesthesiologist and the neuromonitoring team: Monitoring modalities, anesthetic protocols (avoiding agents that suppress evoked potentials), warning criteria for possible neurological injury and planned responses to positive alarms. 
• Standardize postoperative handoff protocols

Use evidence-based handoff frameworks, such as Illness severity, Patient summary, Action list, Situation awareness and contingency planning (I-PASS), and Synthesis by receiver, for transitions from operating room to recovery. Standardized handoffs improve communication of neurological findings, intraoperative events and vital sign goals. 
• Define escalation triggers for neurological changes

Establish protocols for escalating care in cases of new motor weakness, sensory changes or bowel/bladder dysfunction, including who is responsible for evaluation and intervention.
Ensure that nursing staff promptly reports any neurological changes, as delayed recognition of postoperative deficits reduces the opportunity for intervention.

• Educate patients and families about warning signs

Before patient discharge, offer clear written and verbal instructions on symptoms requiring immediate evaluation, including new weakness, numbness, difficulty walking or bowel/bladder changes. Provide contact information for on-call providers.
Document patient education and stated understanding. 
ADMINISTRATION: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR

Provider’s Failure to Recognize Responsibility

Contributing Action or Omission: Inadequate informed consent, failure to document risk discussions and incomplete documentation of intraoperative events and postoperative assessments compromise patient understanding and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate informed consent, comprehensive documentation and clear accountability throughout the surgical episode.

• Obtain and document comprehensive informed consent

Initiate the informed consent discussion during the preoperative visit to allow time for patient questions. 
Explain the diagnosis, disease progression, proposed procedure, risks (including paralysis, nerve injury, sphincter disturbance), benefits and alternatives, including no treatment. 
Use plain language and present information patients can easily understand.
Document the content of the informed consent discussion, including patient questions and their understanding of risks, benefits and alternatives. 
Sample documentation language: "Discussed with patient the diagnosis of cervical stenosis with myelopathy and proposed C4-C6 laminoplasty. Explained risks including infection, bleeding, nerve injury, spinal cord injury with potential for weakness or paralysis (approximately 1 in 100 cases), dural tear and need for additional surgery. Discussed alternatives including continued conservative management, anterior approach and no surgery. Patient verbalized understanding and asked about recovery timeline. Questions answered. Patient wishes to proceed."

• Document intraoperative neuromonitoring events and responses

Record baseline monitoring status, any significant changes during the procedure, interventions and subsequent monitoring response. If a wake-up test was performed, document the indication, findings and any modifications to surgical plan. Documentation demonstrates appropriate vigilance and response to potential neural compromise.

• Document serial postoperative neurological examinations

Record neurological examination findings when the patient emerges from anesthesia, at regular intervals during the period immediately after surgery and any time a change is suspected.
Compare findings to the documented preoperative baseline.
Document the time of examination, findings and actions taken in response to changes.

• Ensure timely recognition and documentation of complications

If a neurological deficit is identified, document the time of recognition, clinical findings, diagnostic workup, consultations and interventions. Document communication with the patient and family regarding the complication, its potential causes and the treatment plan. Timely, thorough documentation supports patient care and defensibility.

TITLE: Head and neck cancer
RISK_BRIEF:
Head and Neck Cancers
SPECIALTY: Otolaryngology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Oral Cavity Lesions, Oropharyngeal Mass, Laryngeal Symptoms, Neck Mass

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Delayed Diagnosis, Treatment Complications, Recurrence, Functional Impairment, Death

Head and neck cancers are a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in otolaryngology.
Mitigating Your Risk
With systematic diagnostic evaluation, coordinated multidisciplinary care and structured surveillance protocols, otolaryngologists can mitigate risks associated with head and neck cancers and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for head and neck cancers and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving cancers of the head and neck typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 85% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 15%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Head and Neck Cancer Risk [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain appropriate diagnostic testing—including biopsy, HPV/p16 testing and imaging—delays diagnosis and may result in disease progression from early to advanced stage.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely and complete diagnostic evaluation for patients presenting with concerning signs or symptoms.

• Obtain tissue diagnosis promptly for persistent lesions or unexplained neck masses

At initial evaluation, perform biopsy of primary site lesions or fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of neck masses that persist beyond 2-3 weeks without clear infectious causes. Image-guided (ultrasound or CT) needle biopsy of cystic neck nodes offers better diagnostic yield than FNA by palpation alone. For unresectable or metastatic disease where systemic therapy is planned, obtain core biopsy to allow for biomarker testing. 

• Complete staging workup before initiating treatment

For all head and neck cancers, obtain CT with contrast and/or MRI with and without contrast of the primary site and the neck. Consider FDG-PET/CT for unknown primary tumors, locoregionally advanced disease and evaluation for distant metastases. Perform chest CT to evaluate for pulmonary metastases or synchronous primary. Complete examination under anesthesia (EUA) with endoscopy when indicated to assess tumor extent and exclude synchronous primary tumors. Incomplete staging may result in undertreatment or inappropriate treatment selection. 
• Perform HPV/p16 testing for all oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinomas

Obtain p16 immunohistochemistry for all oropharyngeal cancers, as HPV status fundamentally changes staging, prognosis and treatment planning. In the United States, p16 IHC alone is sufficient when clinical and pathologic features are consistent with HPV-associated disease. Perform HPV-specific confirmatory testing (DNA PCR, RNA ISH) when p16 results are equivocal, discordant with tumor morphology, or involve tumors overlapping the oropharynx from adjacent sites. Additional HPV-specific testing may also be performed at the discretion of the pathologist or treating clinician and is recommended for clinical trial enrollment. 
• Obtain PD-L1 testing to inform immunotherapy eligibility
Perform PD-L1 testing by immunohistochemistry using combined positive score (CPS) on biopsy or surgical specimens for all patients with HNSCC being considered for systemic therapy. CPS ≥1 is an indication for neoadjuvant pembrolizumab in resectable stage III–IVA disease (oral cavity, p16-negative oropharynx, hypopharynx, larynx) when surgery is planned, based on the KEYNOTE-689 trial demonstrating significantly improved event-free survival (HR 0.70; CPS ≥1 population). CPS also guides first-line treatment selection in recurrent or metastatic disease, where pembrolizumab monotherapy is indicated only for CPS ≥1 tumors. Failing to obtain PD-L1 testing before treatment decisions may result in missed eligibility for immunotherapy regimens with demonstrated survival benefit, or inappropriate use of pembrolizumab monotherapy in PD-L1–negative tumors where combination chemotherapy is required. 
• Pursue systematic workup for squamous cell carcinoma of unknown primary presenting as a neck mass
When FNA of a neck mass reveals squamous cell carcinoma and no primary is identified on clinical examination, follow a stepwise workup to localize the primary site. Obtain CT with contrast and/or MRI, followed by FDG-PET/CT—performed before EUA or surgical intervention—to identify potential primary sites. Perform p16 IHC, HPV-specific testing and EBV testing on the FNA or biopsy specimen, as HPV-positive status localizes the likely primary to the oropharynx and EBV positivity suggests nasopharyngeal origin. Importantly, p16 IHC alone is not sufficient to establish HPV positivity in the unknown primary setting—HPV-specific confirmatory testing (e.g., DNA PCR, RNA ISH) is required, as HPV status determines whether the tumor is staged and treated as an oropharyngeal cancer. If imaging does not reveal a primary, proceed with EUA including direct laryngoscopy and ipsilateral palatine tonsillectomy ± lingual tonsillectomy, which identifies the primary in approximately 30%–50% of cases beyond what imaging alone detects. Failure to complete this workup may result in unnecessarily wide radiation fields, increased treatment-related morbidity (xerostomia, dysphagia, laryngeal dysfunction), or missed opportunities for focused surgical or radiation therapy. 
• Obtain adequate tissue for lymphoma diagnosis when suspected in a neck mass
Obtain excisional biopsy (preferred) or core needle biopsy with multiple passes when lymphoma is suspected based on clinical features (painless, rubbery lymphadenopathy, B symptoms, rapid nodal enlargement) or FNA findings suggestive of lymphoid neoplasm. FNA alone is insufficient; intact tissue architecture is required for accurate subtyping by immunohistochemistry, flow cytometry and molecular studies. Core needle biopsy is diagnostic in the majority of cases but remains less conclusive than excisional biopsy; nondiagnostic results must be followed by excisional biopsy. Reliance on FNA alone significantly prolongs time to correct diagnosis when results are incorrectly interpreted as benign. 
• Assess young patients without risk factors for Fanconi anemia

Evaluate adolescents or young adults with head and neck squamous cell carcinoma and no traditional risk factors (tobacco, alcohol) for Fanconi anemia. FA confers a >500-fold increased risk of HNSCC, with a median age of onset in the 30s, and impacts tolerance to platinum-based chemotherapy and radiation. Evaluation may include chromosomal breakage analysis (the diagnostic standard for FA) or targeted genetic testing for FA pathway genes. Missing the diagnosis can lead to severe treatment toxicity. 

CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Inappropriate treatment selection, inadequate surgical margins, failure to involve a multidisciplinary team or delays in initiating treatment increase the risk of recurrence, functional impairment and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate treatment selection and execution aligned with current standards of care.

• Present all head and neck cancer cases at tumor board before treatment initiation

Ensure that multidisciplinary evaluation and treatment planning is coordinated between all disciplines before initiating any treatment. The multidisciplinary team should include head and neck surgery, radiation oncology, medical oncology, pathology, radiology, reconstructive surgery, speech-language pathology, nutrition and dental services. Outcomes are improved when patients are treated at high-volume centers with multidisciplinary expertise. Document tumor board recommendations and the rationale for the selected treatment approach. 

• Engage in shared decision-making regarding treatment 

For early-stage disease where surgery and radiation have similar survival outcomes, discuss the risks, benefits and functional consequences of each approach with the patient. Surgery is generally preferred for oral cavity cancer due to the risk of osteoradionecrosis with radiation. For oropharynx, larynx and hypopharynx cancers, radiation may be preferred to minimize effects on swallowing, speech and voice quality. Document the shared decision-making discussion including treatment alternatives, patient preferences and rationale for final treatment selection. 

• Achieve adequate surgical margins and document margin status

Design surgical procedures to resect all gross tumor with adequate tumor-free margins. Intraoperative frozen section analysis should guide margin assessment. Document final margin status (positive, close, negative) and any adverse pathologic features (extranodal extension, perineural invasion, lymphovascular invasion) that affect adjuvant treatment decisions. Positive margins and extranodal extension are indications for adjuvant chemoradiation. 

• Initiate adjuvant radiation within 6 weeks of surgery when indicated

Coordinate with radiation oncology during surgical planning to ensure timely transition to adjuvant treatment. The preferred interval between resection and initiation of postoperative radiation is 6 weeks or less. Delays in adjuvant therapy are associated with worse outcomes. Document any delays and the clinical rationale for them. 

CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate post-treatment surveillance can lead to missed recurrence, second primary cancers or treatment-related complications at a stage when intervention may still be effective.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure systematic surveillance for recurrence, second primaries and treatment sequelae.

• Follow structured surveillance schedule based on recurrence risk

Take a history and perform a physical examination including a complete head and neck exam with mirror and fiberoptic examination: every 1-3 months in year 1, every 2-6 months in year 2, every 4-8 months in years 3-5 and annually thereafter. Most recurrences occur in the first 2 years and are reported by the patient. Tailor surveillance intensity to tumor type, stage, HPV status and tobacco use history. Document examination findings at each visit. 

• Obtain post-treatment imaging to establish new baseline and assess response

Perform FDG-PET/CT at a minimum of 12 weeks after completing definitive radiation or chemoradiation to assess treatment response. The optimal timing appears to be approximately 3 months (12 weeks). FDG-PET/CT scans performed before 12 weeks are associated with significant false-positive rates due to post-treatment inflammatory changes and should be avoided in the absence of signs of recurrence or progression. For patients with negative 3-month PET/CT, there is limited evidence supporting benefit of further routine imaging in asymptomatic patients with negative examination. Obtain CT or MRI within 3-4 months after surgical treatment for locoregionally advanced disease to establish new baseline. 

• Monitor for treatment-related complications and late effects

Check thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) every 6-12 months if neck was irradiated or partial thyroidectomy performed. Consider venous duplex ultrasound of the neck every 3 years for patients who received neck radiation to screen for carotid stenosis. Perform dental evaluation for patients with oral cavity involvement or significant intraoral radiation exposure. Evaluate for panhypopituitarism annually following radiation to skull base. Screen for depression using validated questionnaires, as suicide rates are significantly elevated in head and neck cancer survivors. 

• Coordinate survivorship care with primary care

Integrate survivorship care plan within 1 year of treatment completion, complementary to ongoing involvement from head and neck oncologist. Communicate with primary care regarding long-term monitoring needs, including annual head and neck examination (with direct or indirect laryngoscopy as clinically indicated) for second primary surveillance, thyroid monitoring and cardiovascular risk assessment. Document the survivorship care plan and ensure patient understanding of follow-up requirements. 


ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate communication regarding diagnosis, treatment options, expected outcomes and warning signs can lead to patient confusion, delayed presentation for complications and allegations of inadequate informed consent.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure clear, documented communication with patients throughout the care continuum.

• Conduct and document comprehensive informed consent discussions

Before treatment, discuss the recommended treatment plan including risks, benefits and potential outcomes. Address treatment alternatives and their respective risks and benefits. For locoregionally advanced disease, discuss the potential for significant functional consequences including effects on speech, swallowing and appearance. Engage the patient in the final decision and document the discussion, their stated understanding and preferences. 

• Communicate clearly about prognosis and goals of care

For patients with advanced or recurrent disease, discuss prognosis honestly and explore goals of care. Involve palliative care early for symptom management and goals-of-care discussions. Document these conversations including patient understanding and preferences regarding treatment intensity. 

• Educate patients about warning signs requiring urgent evaluation

Provide written and verbal instructions about symptoms that should prompt immediate contact, including new or worsening pain, difficulty swallowing or breathing, bleeding, unintentional weight loss, new neck mass or rapid growth of existing mass and neurologic symptoms. For post-treatment patients, emphasize that most recurrences are first noticed by the patient. Provide instructions in the patient's primary language and at the appropriate literacy level. Document patient education and their stated understanding. 

• Screen for distress and address psychosocial needs

Screen all patients for distress using validated tools such as the NCCN Distress Thermometer. Head and neck cancer patients experience elevated rates of depression, anxiety and suicide compared to other cancer types and the general population. Refer to psycho-oncology, psychiatry or addiction services as indicated. Document screening results and referrals made. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of clinical findings, decision-making rationale, patient communication and treatment planning undermines continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Document complete head and neck examination findings

Record systematic examination of all head and neck subsites including oral cavity, oropharynx, hypopharynx, larynx and neck. Document fiberoptic nasopharyngolaryngoscopy findings. Describe lesion characteristics (size, location, mobility, relationship to adjacent structures) and lymph node findings (size, level, mobility, laterality). Include tobacco use history quantified in pack-years and alcohol use history with counseling provided. 

Sample documentation language: "Complete head and neck examination performed including fiberoptic nasopharyngolaryngoscopy. 2.5 cm ulcerated lesion right lateral tongue, crossing midline, with decreased tongue mobility. Right level II lymph node 3 cm, firm, mobile. No other cervical lymphadenopathy. Larynx: true vocal folds mobile bilaterally, no lesions. 45 pack-year smoking history; smoking cessation counseling provided."

• Document clinical staging and rationale for treatment selection

Record AJCC staging (8th edition) including T, N, M classification and overall stage. For oropharyngeal cancer, document p16/HPV status and use appropriate staging system. Document the rationale for treatment selection including tumor board recommendations, patient preferences and factors influencing the decision. If treatment deviates from tumor board recommendation, document the reason. 

• Document informed consent discussions

Record discussion of risks, benefits and alternatives. Document specific risks addressed (effects on speech, swallowing or appearance, risk of recurrence, treatment-related complications). Note patient questions and responses provided. Document the patient's stated understanding and decision. 

• Document surveillance visits 

At each follow-up visit, document interval history including any new symptoms, examination findings and comparison to prior examinations. Personally review and record imaging results and their interpretation. Document patient education regarding warning signs and next scheduled follow-up. Note any referrals made (speech therapy, nutrition, dental, psycho-oncology). 


ADMINISTRATIVE: SYSTEMS ISSUE

Failure of Lack of Clinical Process, Policy or Procedure

Contributing action or omission: Lack of standard referral pathways, adequate tracking systems and timely specialist evaluation contributes to diagnostic delays and fragmented care.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to improve care coordination and reduce system-level failures.

• Establish expedited referral pathways for suspected head and neck cancer

Develop institutional protocols for urgent evaluation of patients with concerning symptoms (persistent neck mass, oral lesion not healing within 2-3 weeks, unexplained hoarseness >3 weeks). Communicate urgency to referring providers and triage staff. Track time from referral to initial evaluation and biopsy. AAO-HNS guidelines recommend against antibiotic prescribing for adults with neck mass without infectious symptoms. Consider disseminating this guidance to primary care and urgent care providers. 

• Implement closed-loop referral communication

Establish systems to confirm that patients referred to other providers are scheduled and seen. Communicate specialist findings and recommendations back to referring providers. Studies show that 25%-50% of referring providers are unaware whether patients see specialists, and about 45% of referrals involve no communication from specialists. Poor  communication between clinicians is associated with delayed care and worse outcomes. 

• Establish regular multidisciplinary tumor board meetings

Include participants from head and neck surgery, radiation oncology, medical oncology, pathology, radiology and other relevant disciplines. Present all new head and neck cancer cases before treatment initiation. Document tumor board recommendations in the medical record. For complex or rare tumors, consider referral to high-volume multidisciplinary cancer centers. 

• Develop systems for tracking pathology results and imaging

Implement reliable systems to ensure review of and action on biopsy results and imaging studies. Establish protocols for communicating results to patients and documenting that communication. Track pending results and ensure follow-up of indeterminate or non-diagnostic biopsies.

Mitigating Your Risk
With systematic diagnostic evaluation, coordinated multidisciplinary care and structured surveillance protocols, otolaryngologists can mitigate risks associated with head and neck cancers and reduce malpractic

TITLE: Meningitis
RISK_BRIEF:
Pediatric Meningitis

SPECIALTY: Pediatrics

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Fever, Altered Mental Status, Meningeal Signs

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Delayed Diagnosis, Neurological Sequelae, Death

Meningitis is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in pediatrics.
Mitigating Your Risk
With early recognition, timely treatment and thorough documentation, pediatricians can mitigate the risks associated with meningitis in the pediatric population and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for pediatric meningitis and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving pediatric meningitis typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 75% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 25%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Pediatric Meningitis Risk [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Incomplete history-taking or physical examination delays recognition of meningitis, particularly in infants with nonspecific presentations.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure systematic clinical assessment and early recognition of meningitis across all pediatric age groups.

• Obtain age-appropriate history focusing on meningitis risk factors

Systematically inquire about fever duration and pattern, headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, vomiting, irritability, lethargy, seizures, recent infections (otitis, sinusitis, respiratory illness), immunization status and sick contacts. In neonates, ask about poor feeding, respiratory distress and temperature instability. This approach identifies both classic and atypical presentations. 

• Perform complete neurological examination including meningeal signs

Evaluate for nuchal rigidity, Kernig sign, Brudzinski sign, bulging fontanelle (in infants), altered consciousness, focal neurological deficits and cranial nerve palsies. Record the presence or absence of each finding. Bulging fontanelle, neck stiffness or seizures outside the febrile convulsion age range significantly raises concern for meningitis. 

• Recognize atypical presentations in neonates and young infants

Identify nonspecific signs in neonates, such as poor feeding, irritability, abnormal tone, respiratory distress, hypothermia or seizures, as classic meningeal signs are often absent. Maintain a high index of suspicion in febrile infants younger than 60 days, as the prevalence of bacterial meningitis in this age group is 0.5%–1.3%.

• Assess for petechial or purpuric rash

Examine the skin for petechiae or purpura, which occur in 63%–80% of meningococcal meningitis cases with 83%–92% specificity for meningococcal disease. Rapidly progressing hemorrhagic eruptions with shock-like symptoms strongly suggest meningococcemia (Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome). 

CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failure to perform lumbar puncture or obtain appropriate laboratory studies delays diagnosis and initiation of treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely and appropriate diagnostic evaluation when meningitis is suspected.

• Promptly perform lumbar puncture when suspecting meningitis

Obtain CSF for cell count with differential, protein, glucose, Gram stain and bacterial culture in all suspected cases. CSF examination is essential for diagnosis. Do not delay LP for neuroimaging unless specific indications exist. If LP is postponed, immediately obtain blood cultures and initiate empiric antibiotics. 

• Identify indications for CT before lumbar puncture

Consider obtaining cranial imaging prior to LP only in patients with new-onset seizures, focal neurological deficits, immunocompromised state or moderate-to-severe impairment of consciousness. Isolated cranial nerve VI or VII palsy does not warrant delaying LP. When these features are absent, the negative predictive value for intracranial abnormality is 97%. If imaging is needed, administer empiric antibiotics first to avoid treatment delays.

• Obtain blood cultures before antibiotic administration

Draw blood cultures in all cases of suspected bacterial meningitis before starting antibiotics. Blood cultures are positive in a substantial proportion of patients and may provide the only microbiological diagnosis if CSF cultures are negative due to prior antibiotic exposure. 

• Conduct multiplex PCR testing when available

Order multiplex PCR testing of CSF when available. PCR testing is particularly valuable when CSF Gram stain is negative (true in about 95% of adults and a substantial proportion of children with meningitis) or when patients received antibiotics before LP. 

• Obtain inflammatory markers and apply validated clinical prediction rules

Measure procalcitonin, CRP and CBC with differential. These markers, combined with CSF findings, can be integrated into two validated pediatric prediction rules for children ≥29 days old with CSF pleocytosis to help distinguish bacterial from viral meningitis. These scores are the Bacterial Meningitis Score (BMS) or the Meningitis Score for Emergencies (MSE). Neither score is validated in neonates <29 days or critically ill or pretreated children, and neither replaces clinical judgment.


CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Consult or Transfer Care

Contributing action or omission: Delay in initiating appropriate antimicrobial therapy when indicated or escalating care for deteriorating patients increases the risk of neurological sequelae and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely start of treatment, appropriate adjunctive therapy and escalation of care when needed.

• Initiate empiric antibiotics within 1 hour of presentation

Start empiric antimicrobial therapy as soon as bacterial meningitis is suspected—ideally within 1 hour of presentation. If LP is delayed, administer empiric antibiotics after obtaining blood cultures. Select empiric regimens based on age: per the AAP 2021 guideline, neonates require ampicillin plus ceftazidime (or gentamicin); infants and children more than 1 month old require vancomycin plus ceftriaxone. 

• Administer adjunctive dexamethasone when indicated
Consider adjunctive dexamethasone for children older than 2 months with suspected bacterial meningitis, administered with or before the first dose of antibiotics. Meta-analyses demonstrate reduced hearing loss in children with H. influenzae meningitis and reduced neurological sequelae in high-income settings. Dexamethasone is not recommended for neonates and should be discontinued if Listeria monocytogenes is identified. Document the rationale for administering or withholding dexamethasone.

• Engage pediatric critical care when indicated

Consult with pediatric critical care for patients with hemodynamic instability, respiratory compromise, altered consciousness (GCS ≤12), status epilepticus, signs of increased intracranial pressure (Cushing's triad) or rapidly progressive disease. Perform brain imaging in patients with declining consciousness or no improvement within 48 hours of beginning treatment to detect hydrocephalus, infarction, empyema or abscess. 

• Arrange transfer to a higher level of care when resources are insufficient

Transfer patients to a facility with pediatric intensive care capabilities if the presenting institution cannot provide appropriate monitoring, subspecialty consultation (pediatric infectious disease, neurology, neurosurgery) or interventions such as intracranial pressure monitoring or emergent neurosurgical procedures. Do not delay antibiotic administration pending transfer.

• Ensure infectious disease consultation for complex cases

Obtain pediatric infectious disease consultation for patients with unusual pathogens, antibiotic allergies requiring alternative regimens, treatment failure, immunocompromised hosts or recurrent meningitis. Consultation aids in appropriate antibiotic selection, duration and de-escalation based on culture and susceptibility results. 



ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of clinical findings, decision-making rationale and interventions compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that demonstrates adherence to the standard of care and supports continuity across care transitions.

• Document the clinical assessment with specific findings

Record the presence or absence of each meningeal sign (nuchal rigidity, Kernig, Brudzinski, bulging fontanelle), level of consciousness, vital signs and any focal neurological findings. Document skin examination results, including the presence or absence of petechiae or purpura. Specify the patient's immunization status and relevant risk factors. Documentation demonstrates systematic evaluation and supports clinical decision-making. 

Sample documentation language: "3-month-old male with fever to 39.2°C, irritability, poor feeding x12 hours. Fontanelle soft and flat. No nuchal rigidity. Kernig and Brudzinski signs negative. No petechiae or purpura. Immunizations up to date. No sick contacts."

• Document timing of key interventions

Record the time of first clinical suspicion of meningitis, blood culture collection, LP performance, CSF results and antibiotic administration. When giving antibiotics before LP, document the clinical rationale. This documentation demonstrates adherence to the 1-hour treatment goal and provides a defensible record if LP is delayed. 
Sample documentation language:  "Empiric ceftriaxone and vancomycin administered at 14:32 prior to LP due to clinical instability; blood cultures obtained at 14:28.” 

• Document clinical reasoning for management decisions

When LP is deferred or delayed, document the reason (hemodynamic instability, concern for mass lesion, coagulopathy) and the plan for subsequent evaluation. When initiating empiric antibiotics without LP, document the rationale and plan for ongoing assessment. If administering or withholding dexamethasone, document the reasoning. 

Sample documentation language: "LP deferred due to concern for increased ICP (GCS 10, papilledema on fundoscopic exam). Blood cultures obtained. Empiric ceftriaxone, vancomycin and dexamethasone administered at 15:45. CT head ordered; LP to be performed after imaging if no contraindication."

• Document communication with consultants and accepting providers

Record consultations with pediatric infectious disease, neurology or critical care, including recommendations received and actions taken. For transferred patients, document the information communicated to the accepting team, including current clinical status, interventions performed, pending results and outstanding concerns. 

• Document discharge instructions and follow-up plans

For patients discharged after evaluation for possible meningitis, document education, including signs and symptoms requiring immediate re-evaluation, and the follow-up plan. For patients recovering from bacterial meningitis, document the plan for audiological evaluation (recommended for all) and neurodevelopmental follow-up.

TITLE: Pneumonia
RISK_BRIEF:
Pediatric Pneumonia

SPECIALTY: Pediatrics

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Respiratory Illness, Fever, Cough

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Respiratory Failure, Sepsis, Empyema, Death

Pneumonia is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in pediatrics.
Mitigating Your Risk
With thorough clinical assessment, timely escalation of care and reliable communication across care transitions, pediatricians can mitigate the risks associated with pneumonia among pediatric patients and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for pediatric pneumonia and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving pediatric pneumonia typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 73% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 27%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Pediatric Pneumonia Risk [insert chart] 

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Obtain Relevant Medical History or Perform Pertinent Physical Exam

Contributing action or omission: Incomplete assessment of respiratory status, vaccination historyor risk factors for severe disease increases the likelihood of missed or delayed diagnosis of pneumonia and its complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure systematic evaluation of children with respiratory illness and early identification of those at risk for severe disease.

• Assess respiratory status using age-appropriate vital sign thresholds

At initial presentation, measure respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, heart rate and temperature. Use age-specific tachypnea thresholds: >60 breaths/min for newborns to 2 months, >50 for 2–12 months, >40 for 1–5 years.[1] Oxygen saturation ≤96% and increased work of breathing (grunting, flaring or retractions) are the findings most strongly associated with radiographic pneumonia and should prompt further evaluation. Hypoxemia—defined as SpO₂ <90% by WHO criteria—is an independent indicator of disease severity and should prompt assessment for hospitalization.

• Document vaccination status for pneumococcal and H. influenzae type b vaccines

During history-taking, confirm immunization status against S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae type b. Incomplete vaccination affects empiric antibiotic selection and increases risk of invasive bacterial disease. Documenting vaccination status demonstrates adherence to guideline-based care and supports defensible antibiotic choices.

• Identify comorbidities that increase risk for severe pneumonia

Screen for underlying conditions that increase pneumonia complication risk, including congenital heart disease, chronic lung disease, neurocognitive disorders, immunocompromise, prematurity, malnutrition and sickle cell disease. Previous viral illness, particularly influenza and RSV, increases risk for secondary bacterial pneumonia; influenza is especially associated with necrotizing pneumonia. Children with comorbidities are more likely to require hospitalization and intensive care. Early identification allows appropriate triage and monitoring intensity.

• Evaluate for signs of complicated pneumonia

Assess for clinical features suggesting complications of pneumonia, such as parapneumonic effusion, necrotizing pneumonia, lung abscess or empyema, persistent fever or lack of clinical improvement despite 48–72 hours of appropriate antibiotics, dullness to percussion, decreased breath sounds and pleuritic chest pain. Early recognition of complicated pneumonia prompts escalation of care, including timely imaging, antibiotic choice and specialist consultation.


CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain appropriate imaging or laboratory evaluation when clinically indicated delays diagnosis of pneumonia and its complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate diagnostic testing while avoiding unnecessary imaging in patients at low risk for pneumonia.

• Obtain chest radiography for patients with hypoxemia, significant respiratory distress or treatment failure
Order chest x-ray when suspected or documented hypoxemia is present, when significant respiratory distress is observed (grunting, retractions, nasal flaring, tachypnea) or when patients fail to improve within 48–72 hours of antibiotic initiation. Chest radiography is not routinely required to confirm suspected pneumonia in outpatients who appear well and have no hypoxemia or significant respiratory distress. Targeted imaging supports timely diagnosis of complications while avoiding unnecessary radiation exposure.

• Use chest ultrasound to characterize parapneumonic effusions

When chest radiography suggests moderate to large parapneumonic effusion, obtain chest ultrasound rather than CT to characterize size and complexity. Ultrasound imaging avoids radiation exposure and provides adequate information for management decisions. Reserve CT for cases in which ultrasound is unavailable or findings are inconclusive.
• Obtain blood cultures in hospitalized patients before beginning antibiotics

Order blood cultures in children requiring hospitalization, though pathogen yield is low (2%–7%). When a pathogen is identified, sensitivities guide targeted therapy. Document that cultures were obtained prior to antibiotics when possible.


CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Consult or Transfer Care

Contributing action or omission: Delays in escalating care, consulting specialists or transferring patients with severe or complicated pneumonia increase the risk of respiratory failure, sepsis and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely escalation of care and appropriate specialist involvement.

• Apply IDSA criteria for hospitalization at initial evaluation and reassessment

Hospitalize children with hypoxemia (SpO2 < 90% on room air at sea level; some clinicians hospitalize at SpO2 < 92-93%), significant respiratory distress (grunting, retractions, nasal flaring), altered mental status, inability to tolerate oral intake, suspected virulent organism (CA-MRSA, group A Streptococcus) or significant underlying comorbidities. Reassess disposition if clinical status changes. Adherence to established hospitalization criteria supports an appropriate level of care and defensible decision-making.

• Recognize indications for ICU admission or continuous monitoring

Admit to ICU or a unit with continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring when a child requires invasive or noninvasive positive pressure ventilation, has impending respiratory failure, sustained tachycardia or hypotension requiring pharmacologic support, oxygen saturation ≤92% on FiO2 ≥0.50 or altered mental status due to hypoxemia or hypercarbia. Early ICU involvement reduces risk of unrecognized deterioration.

• Reassess patients who do not respond to initial therapy

Repeat imaging and re-evaluate patients who fail to improve after 48–72 hours of appropriate antibiotics. Consider the possibility of complicated pneumonia. Persistent fever or worsening respiratory status should prompt evaluation for parapneumonic effusion, empyema or alternative diagnoses. Document the reassessment and clinical reasoning.

• Consult pediatric surgery or interventional radiology for complicated effusions 

When pleural drainage is indicated for moderate-to-large or purulent effusions, involve appropriate specialists (pediatric surgery or interventional radiology) to ensure effective drainage. IDSA/PIDS guidelines suggest chest tube drainage with fibrinolytics as first-line therapy over VATS in most cases, but approach should be guided by local resources and expertise. VATS may be reserved for patients with extensive loculation or refractory disease.

• Arrange transfer to a higher level of care when local resources are insufficient

When a child requires subspecialty care, advanced respiratory support or surgical intervention not available at the current facility, promptly initiate transfer. Document the clinical indication for transfer and communication with the receiving facility.



ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Communication failures during handoffs, shift changes and care transitions increase the risk of missed clinical deterioration and delayed intervention.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and enable coordinated team response.

• Implement structured handoff protocols for patients with respiratory illness

Use standardized handoff frameworks such as I-PASS or SBAR when transferring care between providers, from ED to inpatient units and across shift changes. Communicate current respiratory status, oxygen requirements, antibiotic regimen, pending studies and monitoring parameters. Document handoff details in the medical record.

• Identify high-risk patients requiring enhanced monitoring and communication

Flag patients with uncertain diagnosis, unstable respiratory status, pending imaging or laboratory results, deviation from typical treatment response or prolonged ED stay for enhanced surveillance. Monitor these patients closely for clinical change.

• Use closed-loop communication during acute clinical events

During respiratory deterioration or escalation of care, verbalize instructions and confirm receipt to minimize errors. Designate a team leader during resuscitation or rapid response events. Document team communication and interventions in real time.

• Clearly communicate discharge instructions to families and outpatient providers

Before patient discharge, provide written and verbal instructions, including expected clinical course, warning signs requiring return (increased efforts to breathe, persistent fever, poor oral intake, lethargy), medication instructions and follow-up appointment. Confirm family understanding. Communicate with the primary care provider regarding the diagnosis, treatment and follow-up plan. Document the communication.


ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR

Failure to Recognize Responsibility

Contributing action or omission: Failure to take ownership of clinical findings, pending results or follow-up increases the risk of missed diagnoses and delayed treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure accountability for clinical findings and follow-up.

• Establish clear ownership of pending test results

Assign responsibility for reviewing and acting on pending laboratory results, imaging studies and culture data. Implement systems to track outstanding results and ensure that a responsible clinician reviews them. Document the review and resulting clinical actions.

• Acknowledge and address diagnostic uncertainty

When the diagnosis is uncertain, document the differential diagnosis, planned reassessment interval and return precautions provided to the family. Communicate uncertainty to the receiving provider during handoffs. Acknowledging uncertainty encourages systematic follow-up and reduces the risk of anchoring bias.

• Document clinical reasoning for management decisions

Record the rationale for disposition decisions (outpatient vs. inpatient), antibiotic selection, decision to obtain or defer imaging and escalation or de-escalation of care. Documenting clinical reasoning demonstrates thoughtful decision-making and supports defensibility.

• Ensure timely follow-up of discharged patients

Arrange outpatient follow-up within a timeframe appropriate to the child’s clinical status and risk factors; for most children, follow-up within 48-72 hours is a reasonable practice, as this aligns with the expected timeline for clinical response to antibiotic therapy. Clearly document the plan and share it with the family and primary care provider. If the patient does not attend the follow-up, activate outreach protocols.

Mitigating Your Risk
With thorough clinical assessment, timely escalation of care and reliable communication across care transitions, pediatricians can mitigate the risks associated with pneumonia among pediatric patients and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that ca
