ADVERSE_OUTCOMES: Respiratory Failure, Sepsis, Empyema, Death

TITLE: Breast reconstruction and necrosis
RISK_BRIEF:
Necrosis Injury Following Breast Reconstruction
SPECIALTY: Plastic Surgery

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Breast Reconstruction (Implant-Based and Autologous)

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Necrosis Injury (Mastectomy Skin Flap, Nipple-Areolar Complex and/or Flap Necrosis)

Necrosis injury following breast reconstruction is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in plastic surgery.
Mitigating Your Risk
With careful surgical planning, standardized postoperative surveillance and transparent patient communication, plastic surgeons can mitigate the risks associated with necrosis and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for necrosis injury following breast reconstruction and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving necrosis injury in breast reconstruction typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 83% of the risk, which administrative contributors account for the remaining 17%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.

Actions and Omissions Driving Necrosis Injury Risk [insert chart] 

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Errors in treatment approach—including inappropriate patient selection, failure to optimize modifiable risk factors and suboptimal surgical planning—increase the likelihood of necrosis and subsequent adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce the likelihood of necrosis by ensuring appropriate patient selection, preoperative optimization and evidence-based intraoperative decision-making.

● Complete structured preoperative risk stratification for every reconstruction candidate

Before committing to a reconstructive approach, assess and document all patient-specific risk factors for necrosis: smoking status (including pack-year history and date of last use), BMI, diabetes control (HbA1c), prior breast radiation, breast size/ptosis and history of prior breast surgery. Published risk calculators such as the Breast Reconstruction Risk Assessment (BRA) Score provide individualized complication estimates to guide shared decision-making. This assessment should occur at the initial reconstructive consultation and be revisited at the preoperative visit. 

● Counsel patients and offer evidence-based treatment for smoking cessation before elective reconstruction

At the initial consultation, counsel all tobacco users that smoking is independently associated with mastectomy flap necrosis, NAC necrosis, infection and implant failure. Start evidence-based smoking cessation treatment (combination NRT or varenicline) as early as possible. The NCCN Smoking Cessation Guidelines recommend that patients stop smoking at least 4 weeks before surgery when feasible, though this should not delay cancer resection. For patients who cannot stop smoking, consider delayed reconstruction or submuscular expander placement at least 7 days after mastectomy. Postoperatively, counsel patients to remain abstinent for at least 4–6 weeks (if permanent cessation is not achieved), as the inflammatory and proliferative wound-healing responses require at least 4 weeks to partially recover after cessation. Pharmacotherapy and behavioral therapy should continue postoperatively, framing the perioperative period as a bridge to permanent cessation. Document the cessation discussion, the patient's response and any pharmacotherapy prescribed. Demonstrating discussion of cessation and adaptation of the surgical plan to the patient's smoking status is a key element of defensibility.
● Consider intraoperative perfusion assessment to guide tissue management

Consider using indocyanine green (ICG) angiography or fluorescein dye imaging after mastectomy and before final reconstruction to objectively assess mastectomy skin flap viability. ICG angiography has been shown to significantly reduce mastectomy skin flap necrosis, reoperation and infection rates compared to clinical assessment alone. Use ICG findings in conjunction with clinical assessment to guide tissue management decisions; excise areas of insufficient perfusion identified by imaging rather than relying solely on clinical judgment. ICG is particularly valuable in higher-risk patients (smokers, prior radiation, large breast volume) and when clinical assessment is equivocal, though routine use is supported by the available evidence. Document the perfusion assessment findings and the clinical decision made in response. 

● Apply non-operative adjuncts to reduce necrosis in high-risk patients

For patients with elevated necrosis risk, consider topical nitroglycerin ointment (2% NTG, 45 mg total dose) applied to mastectomy skin flaps at the time of surgical dressing. Closed-incision negative-pressure wound therapy (ciNPWT) is another option supported by observational data and meta-analysis. These adjuncts are low-cost, low-risk interventions that can be applied intraoperatively or immediately postoperatively.



CLINICAL: TREATMENT
Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Insufficient postoperative monitoring delays recognition of evolving necrosis, vascular compromise or infection — all of which can convert a manageable complication into a catastrophic outcome including total flap loss, implant extrusion or delayed adjuvant therapy.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support earlier identification of tissue compromise, prompt escalation and sustained surveillance through the critical postoperative window.

● Establish and follow a structured postoperative flap monitoring protocol

For autologous free flap reconstruction, perform hourly clinical assessments (flap color, capillary refill, temperature, turgor) for the first 24 hours, then every 2–4 hours through postoperative day 3, which is the window in which 89% of complications requiring revision occur. For implant-based reconstruction, which is increasingly performed on an outpatient or same-day discharge basis, schedule next-day postoperative follow-up to assess mastectomy skin flap viability, noting color changes, ecchymosis, firmness and early epidermolysis. For inpatients, assess mastectomy skin flaps at least daily. Monitoring frequency may be adapted per institutional policies and patient risk factors. A structured protocol increases the detection of deterioration before it becomes irreversible. Document findings of each assessment.
● Incorporate adjunctive monitoring technology for free flap reconstruction

Use continuous or near-continuous tissue oximetry (near-infrared spectroscopy) or implantable Doppler monitoring as an adjunct to clinical examination during the early postoperative period. Apply these devices intraoperatively and maintain them for at least 24–48 hours postoperatively. Train nursing staff to interpret device readings and define clear escalation criteria (such as sustained tissue oxygen saturation drop >20% from baseline, which should trigger immediate notification of responsible providers). Document device readings and clinical responses. Adjunctive monitoring provides an objective, continuous safety net that complements intermittent clinical checks and creates a contemporaneous record of flap status.

● Define clear escalation criteria and response pathways for evolving necrosis

Create written criteria establishing when evolving skin flap changes require escalation from observation to intervention. Minor superficial necrosis without evidence of infection, drainage or implant/expander exposure may be managed with local wound care and close outpatient follow-up, while deeper or more extensive necrosis or those with implant/expander exposure typically require operative debridement. The decision to intervene should account for both the depth and surface area of necrosis as well as clinical signs of infection. The median time to treatment for mastectomy skin necrosis is approximately 15 days postoperatively; timely and appropriate debridement has been shown to prevent delays in adjuvant therapy. For free flaps, any clinical or device-detected sign of vascular compromise should trigger immediate surgical team evaluation and consideration for operative re-exploration. Clear escalation pathways prevent the “watchful waiting” drift that is a common failure mode in necrosis-related claims.
● Maintain close outpatient surveillance through the wound-healing period

Schedule follow-up at 1 week, 2 weeks and 4–6 weeks postoperatively. At each visit, assess wound healing, photograph areas of concern and evaluate for infection or progressive necrosis. Patients with risk factors (smoking, diabetes, prior radiation, obesity) warrant more frequent visits. Provide clear instructions for contacting the surgical team between visits if skin color changes, increasing pain, drainage or wound breakdown develop. Document this education. Consistent outpatient surveillance closes the gap between discharge and full wound maturation, when delayed necrosis can evolve undetected.





CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Failure to coordinate with the oncologic surgeon on mastectomy technique, along with reconstruction-specific errors, increases the risk of tissue ischemia and necrosis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce the incidence of necrosis through meticulous surgical technique, evidence-based intraoperative decision-making and coordinated multidisciplinary execution.

● Advocate for mastectomy skin flap vascularity through preoperative coordination with the oncologic surgeon 

Before surgery, discuss with the oncologic surgeon the importance of maintaining an appropriate plane of dissection that preserves the subdermal vascular plexus. Prospective data has identified preservation of the second intercostal perforator vessel as a modifiable intraoperative factor that significantly decreases the risk of necrosis after nipple-sparing mastectomy. Patients with thicker subcutaneous tissue (>17 mm on preoperative mammography) have been associated with higher ischemic complication rates. Coordinate with the oncologic surgeon preoperatively to discuss incision placement, dissection planes and the importance of flap thickness preservation. Because plastic surgeons are frequently named in malpractice claims arising from mastectomy flap necrosis even when the mastectomy is performed by the oncologic surgeon, proactive communication about flap quality is a key element of defensibility.
● Tailor the reconstructive approach to the patient's risk profile

Take the patient’s individual risk factors and comorbidities into account when planning the reconstruction method and timing. For patients with multiple risk factors (active smoking, BMI >30, prior radiation), consider delayed reconstruction, submuscular expander placement or autologous tissue options that may better tolerate compromised skin flaps. In implant-based reconstruction, avoid overfilling tissue expanders at the initial operation. For patients with macromastia or significant ptosis, discuss reduction-pattern mastectomy techniques with the oncologic surgeon. Document the rationale for the chosen approach, including why alternatives were considered and rejected.

● Coordinate intraoperatively with the oncologic surgeon to optimize flap quality

Establish a preoperative plan and intraoperative communication protocol between the oncologic and reconstructive surgeons. Key coordination points include: incision design that balances oncologic adequacy with reconstructive blood supply; real-time communication about flap thickness and quality during dissection; and joint assessment of flap perfusion before proceeding with reconstruction. NCCN guidelines state that skin- and nipple-sparing mastectomy should be performed by an experienced breast surgery team that works in a coordinated, multidisciplinary fashion to guide proper patient selection, determine optimal sequencing and perform a resection that achieves appropriate surgical margins. Use a surgical pause or time-out after mastectomy completion and before reconstruction to assess flap viability (clinically and/or with ICG angiography) and to confirm the reconstructive plan. Document the joint assessment and any intraoperative plan modifications.

● Ensure meticulous hemostasis and appropriate drain management

Achieve thorough hemostasis before closure and place drains according to institutional protocol. Hematoma formation beneath mastectomy skin flaps increases pressure on already compromised vasculature and can precipitate necrosis. Ensure that drain output is monitored and documented at defined intervals and that criteria for drain removal are standardized. Excessive compression dressings should be avoided as they may further compromise flap perfusion.



ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Communication failures—including inadequate informed consent, failure to set realistic expectations about complication risk and insufficient postoperative patient education—are independently associated with paid malpractice claims in plastic surgery.
Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that providers fully inform patients about necrosis risk, set appropriate expectations and communicate regularly throughout the perioperative course.

● Conduct and document a comprehensive informed consent discussion specific to necrosis risk

At the preoperative consultation, discuss mastectomy skin flap necrosis, NAC necrosis and autologous flap necrosis as complications, with incidence rates tailored to the patient's risk profile. Cover the potential consequences: additional operations, wound care, aesthetic compromise, delayed adjuvant therapy and, for nipple-sparing procedures, loss of pigmentation, sensation, projection or the NAC itself. Use individualized risk estimates (BRA Score) when available. Supplement verbal discussion with multimedia educational tools, which improve patient comprehension compared to standard consent processes. Document the specific risks discussed, patient questions and their acknowledgment of understanding. A well-documented, patient-specific consent discussion is one of the most important defenses against a necrosis-related claim.
● Set clear expectations about the possibility of revision surgery and wound management

Before reconstruction, inform patients that revisional surgery may be necessary and that wound-healing complications, including necrosis requiring debridement, are a recognized possibility even with optimal surgical technique. The MROC study demonstrated that 2-year overall complication rates ranged from 26.6% for expander-implant to 73.9% for SIEA flap reconstruction, and reoperative complication rates ranged from 15.5% to 30.8%. Patients who understand these rates preoperatively are less likely to perceive a complication as a deviation from the standard of care. Document the discussion.

● Provide clear postoperative instructions with warning signs for necrosis

At patient discharge, provide written and verbal instructions that include signs and symptoms requiring that they contact their provider immediately: skin color changes (darkening, mottling or blanching), increasing firmness or swelling, new or worsening pain, wound breakdown, drainage or fever. Include a 24/7 contact number for the surgical team. Educate patients with free flap reconstruction on the importance of avoiding compression, maintaining warmth and reporting any sudden changes in flap appearance. Document providing education and confirming the patient’s (or caregiver’s) understanding. Clear discharge communication closes the gap between inpatient monitoring and outpatient follow-up and demonstrates that the patient was informed about seeking timely care.

● Maintain proactive communication when complications arise

When necrosis develops, communicate promptly and transparently with the patient about the findings, likely cause, management plan and expected timeline. Avoid minimizing the complication or delaying the conversation. Explain the range of management options (observation, local wound care, operative debridement, possible implant removal or flap revision) and involve the patient in shared decision-making. Document each communication, including the patient's response and the agreed-upon plan. Proactive, empathetic communication after a complication is strongly associated with reduced litigation risk. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR

Failure to Recognize Responsibility

Contributing action or omission: Failure to take ownership of evolving complications—including delayed follow-up, inadequate response to patient concerns, failure to coordinate with co-managing surgeons and reluctance to intervene when necrosis is progressing—increases both patient harm and medicolegal exposure.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that the reconstructive surgeon maintains clear ownership of the patient's postoperative course and responds decisively to complications.

● Establish and communicate clear lines of responsibility between oncologic and reconstructive surgeons

When planning surgery, define and document which surgeon is responsible for postoperative wound monitoring, management of skin flap complications and coordination of adjuvant therapy timing. Confusion about who is responsible for the skin flap after surgery is a known cause of problems in multidisciplinary breast cancer care. Ensure that the reconstructive surgeon's role in postoperative wound surveillance is explicitly communicated to the patient, the oncologic surgeon and the nursing team. Document the division of responsibilities in the operative note and the postoperative care plan.

● Respond promptly and decisively to signs of evolving necrosis

When skin flap changes are identified, the reconstructive surgeon should evaluate the patient in a timely manner and document the assessment. Avoid a pattern of serial observation without a clear decision point. Establish internal benchmarks: for example, if necrosis is progressing at the 1-week visit, define whether the next step is operative debridement, local wound care with close follow-up or referral. The median time to treatment for mastectomy skin necrosis is approximately 15 days; timely debridement has been shown to prevent delays in adjuvant therapy. Document the clinical rationale for the chosen management approach at each encounter. Decisive action, even if the decision is to observe, demonstrates clinical engagement and reduces the perception of neglect.

● Coordinate with the oncology team when necrosis threatens adjuvant therapy timelines

When necrosis or wound complications could delay chemotherapy or radiation, proactively communicate with the medical and radiation oncologists. Document the communication, the clinical status and the agreed-upon plan for managing the wound while preserving oncologic treatment timelines. NCCN guidelines state that the process of breast reconstruction should not govern the timing or the scope of appropriate surgical treatment. Demonstrating that the reconstructive surgeon actively coordinated with the oncology team to minimize treatment delays is an important element of defensibility.

● Conduct a structured debrief and quality review after significant necrosis events

When a patient experiences significant necrosis (full-thickness MSFN, NAC loss, partial or total flap loss), conduct a structured case review with the surgical team. Identify contributing factors and determine if process improvements are warranted. This demonstrates a commitment to patient safety and continuous learning.

TITLE: Burn injury
RISK_BRIEF:
Burn Injuries as a Complication of Plastic Surgery 

SPECIALTY: Plastic Surgery

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Aesthetic and Reconstructive Procedures

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Thermal Injury, Operating Room Fire, Tissue Necrosis

Burn injuries as a complication of plastic surgery are a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in plastic surgery.

Mitigating Your Risk

With standardized fire prevention protocols, vigilant postoperative thermal injury surveillance and thorough patient education, plastic surgeons can mitigate the risks associated with burn injuries as a complication of plastic surgery and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for burn injuries in plastic surgery and provides strategies to help prevent or mitigate them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving burn injuries in plastic surgery stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 73% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 27%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.

Actions and Omissions Driving Burn Injury Risk in Plastic Surgery [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Improper use of electrosurgical devices, failure to manage the fire triangle and inadequate safety protocols during high-risk procedures increase the risk of intraoperative burns and fires.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce the likelihood of intraoperative thermal injuries through standardized safety protocols and proper equipment management.

• Conduct fire risk assessment before every procedure

During the preoperative briefing, assess whether the procedure involves an ignition source (electrocautery, laser) near an oxidizer-enriched atmosphere. For high-risk face, neck or upper chest procedures, communicate the fire risk to the surgical team and confirm preventive measures. This assessment aligns with the American Society of Anesthesiologists Practice Advisory and shows safety standard adherence. Ensure documentation of the fire risk classification (high vs. low), the specific ignition source and confirmation of preventive measures with the team.
• Verify proper function and handling of electrosurgical equipment

Confirm electrosurgical devices function properly before use. Place the electrosurgical pencil in a holster when not in use to prevent accidental activation. Verify that return electrode pads are properly positioned with adequate skin contact. Mishandling cautery devices accounts for 13% of burn-related litigation in plastic surgery. 

• Ensure adequate drying time for alcohol-based skin preparations

Allow flammable skin-prepping solutions to dry completely before draping. Follow the manufacturer’s instructions for use (IFU), as minimum drying times vary by product. Confirm that the prep appears completely dry with no pooling under the patient, in body folds or beneath drapes before activating any ignition source. Document the prep agent used and confirmation of dryness before draping. 
• Minimize oxidizer-enriched atmospheres near the surgical site

Reduce oxygen concentration to the minimum required to maintain adequate oxygenation during procedures involving electrocautery or lasers near the face or airway. Arrange surgical drapes to prevent oxygen buildup under or around the surgical site. Avoid nitrous oxide for high-risk procedures. Coordinate with anesthesia to reduce FiO2 before activating ignition sources and monitor oxygenation using pulse oximetry. 

CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Failure to Monitor Patient Following Treatment or Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate postoperative monitoring for thermal injuries in patients with sensory deficits following breast reconstruction or flap procedures increases the risk of delayed burn recognition and tissue loss.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure early detection of postoperative thermal injuries and appropriate wound management.

• Conduct postoperative assessments for high-risk patients

Assess the reconstructed area and donor site during each postoperative visit for patients undergoing breast reconstruction or flap procedures. Check for signs of thermal injury, such as erythema, blistering or tissue changes. Document the sensory status of the reconstructed area. Early detection enables timely wound care and may prevent progression to full-thickness injury. 

• Establish follow-up protocols for patients reporting heat exposure

Instruct patients to contact the office immediately if they experience heat exposure to the reconstructed area. Offer same-day or next-day evaluations for reported incidents. Document the cause of injury, tissue involvement and treatment plan.

CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Failure to follow protocols for electrosurgery, laser use and management of heated instruments increases the risk of intraoperative thermal injury.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize safe surgical technique and reduce thermal injury risk from improper equipment use.

• Coordinate with anesthesia before activating ignition sources during facial procedures

Notify the anesthesiologist before activating the ignition source for facial aesthetic procedures using electrocautery (blepharoplasty, brow lift, facelift, rhinoplasty) or laser resurfacing (CO2, erbium:YAG). Ensure that the anesthesiologist reduces or temporarily discontinues supplemental oxygen and discontinues nitrous oxide, allowing adequate time for the oxidizer-enriched atmosphere to dissipate before proceeding. The required wait time varies based on drape configuration, oxygen flow rate and room ventilation. Effective surgeon-anesthesiologist coordination is essential to prevent ignition during facial plastic surgery. 

• Use moistened sponges and gauze near ignition sources

Avoid using common fuel sources in operating room fires like dry sponges and gauze. Moisten sponges, cottonoids and packing material when used near electrocautery or lasers, especially in or near the airway. Studies show flammability is significantly reduced when materials are wet. 
• Ensure proper ocular protection during periorbital laser procedures

Use metal corneal shields instead of plastic ones during periorbital laser resurfacing (CO2, erbium:YAG), as plastic can melt and generate significant heat during CO2 laser exposure. Avoid placing gauze under eye shields, as dry gauze ignites rapidly when exposed to ablative lasers. Limit direct laser impacts on metal shields, as consecutive pulses can heat the shield enough to cause corneal injury. Apply ophthalmic lubricant to the cornea before placing the shield. 
• Verify the temperature of heated surgical instruments

Check that heated instruments are within safe temperature limits before applying them to tissue. Overheated instruments contribute to 13% of burn-related litigation in plastic surgery. Use built-in safeguards, such as mandatory cool-down periods, temperature indicators or equipment that prevents activation above a set threshold, to avoid applying excessively heated instruments to tissue. 
• Conduct team training and simulation for fire response

Engage in regular multidisciplinary simulation exercises for operating room fire response, such as the SAGES Fundamental Use of Surgical Energy (FUSE) program, which provides a structured curriculum on energy device safety and fire prevention. Define roles for removing burning materials, stopping oxygen flow, irrigating and evacuating patients. Conduct formal debriefings to identify areas for improvement. Team training improves response time and coordination during fires. 
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Failure to educate patients about postoperative thermal injury risks and include this information in informed consent discussions increases the risk of preventable burns after surgery.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients understand and can act to prevent postoperative burns.

• Include thermal injury risk in informed consent for breast reconstruction

Document thermal injury risk discussions during informed consent for all breast reconstruction procedures. Explain to patients that reconstructed breasts and donor sites will have reduced or absent sensation, preventing them from detecting harmful heat exposure. This information has often been missing from surgery-related consent documents despite being a recognized complication. 

Sample consent language: "The reconstructed breast [and abdominal donor site] will have reduced or absent sensation for months to years following surgery. This means you may not feel heat or pain that would normally warn you of injury. You must take precautions to avoid heat exposure to these areas."
• Provide specific written instructions on heat avoidance

Give patients discharge guidance in their primary language, listing heat sources to avoid, including hot tubs, saunas, heating pads, hot water bottles, direct sunlight and dark-colored bathing suits. For autologous flap reconstruction (DIEP, TRAM, latissimus dorsi), recommend that patients follow thermal precautions until protective sensation is confirmed by clinical assessment. For implant-based reconstruction, advise patients that thermal precautions should be followed indefinitely, as the pattern and timeline of sensory recovery differ from autologous reconstruction and may be permanently altered, particularly in patients who have received radiation therapy. Reinforce that irradiated skin overlying implants is at increased risk for full-thickness burns and implant exposure. Document patient education and confirmation of the patient's understanding. 

• Reinforce thermal precautions postoperatively

Review heat avoidance safeguards at each postoperative visit during the first year. Ask patients about any heat exposures since their last visit. Document the discussion and the patient's reported adherence. Emphasize these measures before summer or travel to warm climates. 

• Consider communicating with the referring physicians about thermal injury risk

Consider informing referring physicians and oncologists about thermal injury risks and necessary precautions in correspondence. This communication with any providers who care for breast reconstructions patients enhances continuity of care while reinforcing patient education.
ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR

Failure to Recognize Responsibility

Contributing action or omission: Failure to acknowledge the surgeon's role in preventing intraoperative burns and fires or attributing responsibility solely to other team members undermines safety culture and increases liability exposure.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to establish clear accountability and promote a culture of shared responsibility for burn prevention.

• Participate in fire risk assessment during the preoperative briefing

Take an active role in the fire risk assessment during the surgical pause. The surgeon controls the ignition source and shares responsibility for identifying and mitigating fire risk. Confirm preventive measures with the team and verify that the assessment is documented in the operative record. Plastic surgeons have been defendants in 87% of burn-related malpractice cases, indicating that courts hold surgeons accountable regardless of team dynamics. 

• Actively coordinate oxygen management with anesthesia before using ignition source

Acknowledge that preventing operating room fires requires collaboration among surgeons, anesthesiologists and nursing staff. Surgeons control the ignition source and must coordinate with anesthesia on oxygen concentration before activating electrocautery or lasers. 

• Participate in adverse event disclosure

Engage in timely and transparent communication with patients following burn injuries. Share factual details about the event, express concern for their wellbeing and explain steps to investigate and prevent a recurrence. National guidelines emphasize full disclosure of adverse events, which fosters trust and benefits patients and providers. 

• Engage in quality improvement following burn events

Participate in root cause analysis and quality improvement activities following a burn injury. Identify contributing factors, implement system changes to prevent recurrence and document involvement in those activities. This demonstrates a commitment to patient safety and adherence to the standard of care. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failing to identify and address barriers to patient adherence with thermal precautions increases the likelihood of preventable postoperative burns.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient engagement and address barriers to adherence with thermal injury prevention.

• Provide comprehensive patient education

Combine verbal instructions with written materials and consider incorporating video or smartphone-based education to strengthen understanding of thermal injury prevention. Smartphone follow-up improves the patient's experience and enables early detection of complications. Using multiple communication channels reinforces precautions. 
• Address seasonal and environmental risk factors

Recognize that thermal injuries are more common during summer and in warm climates. Proactively address seasonal risks during visits before summer. Provide guidance on sun exposure and hot tub avoidance to patients traveling to warm destinations,. Document seasonal counseling. 

• Assess patient understanding and barriers at discharge

Use teach-back methodology to evaluate the patient's understanding of thermal precautions before discharge. Identify potential barriers to adherence, such as occupation, living situation and planned activities. Address scenarios relevant to the patient's lifestyle. Document the assessment and any adjustments to the discharge plan. 

• Document patient non-adherence and continued outreach

Record instances of heat exposure, reinforcing precautions and assessing for potential injury. Document missed follow-ups, outreach efforts and instances in which the patient failed to follow precautions. Include discussion details, recommendations, risks and the patient's reasons for non-adherence to safety recommendations.

Mitigating Your Risk

TITLE: Intra or postop death
RISK_BRIEF:
Intra/Postoperative Death
SPECIALTY: Plastic Surgery

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Elective Aesthetic and Reconstructive Procedures

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Intra/Postoperative Death

Intra/postoperative death is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk in plastic surgery.

Mitigating Your Risk

With systematic VTE risk identification and prophylaxis, evidence-based patient selection and procedural planning and rigorous documentation and patient communication, plastic surgeons can mitigate the risk of intra- or postoperative death and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for intra- or postoperative death and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving intra- or postoperative death related to plastic surgery stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 70% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 30%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to patient death and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.

Actions and Omissions Driving Intra/Postoperative Death Risk [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failure to implement appropriate VTE prophylaxis, establish perioperative monitoring or promptly recognize clinical deterioration increases the risk of preventable death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure systematic risk assessment, appropriate prophylaxis and early recognition of life-threatening complications.

• Perform VTE risk stratification using the Caprini score preoperatively

Calculate the patient's Caprini Risk Assessment score before every surgical procedure to determine VTE risk category and guide prophylaxis decisions. The 2005 Caprini score, validated in plastic surgery populations, predicts a 15- to 20-fold variation in VTE risk. Patients with scores of 7–8 have a 2.7% VTE risk, while those scoring >8 have an 11.3% risk. Document the score and prophylaxis plan in the preoperative record to align with ASPS evidence-based recommendations and support defensibility. 

• Implement risk-stratified VTE prophylaxis protocols

Use mechanical prophylaxis with intermittent pneumatic compression for patients at moderate risk (Caprini 3–6). For patients with Caprini scores ≥7, combine mechanical and pharmacological prophylaxis (low-molecular-weight heparin or unfractionated heparin) unless contraindicated by bleeding risk. Abdominoplasty carries the highest VTE risk among aesthetic procedures. The decision to use chemoprophylaxis should be individualized but it should be strongly considered in patients with Caprini scores ≥ 7. Emerging data on apixaban suggest it may offer VTE prophylaxis without increased hematoma risk, though evidence remains limited. Document the risk-benefit discussion and rationale for the chosen prophylaxis approach.

• Define criteria for postoperative monitoring and observation

Identify patients requiring extended postoperative monitoring or overnight observation based on the following risk factors:
BMI >30 kg/m²

Operative time >3 hours

Lipoaspirate volume >3 liters

Combined procedures (particularly involving abdominoplasty)

Caprini score ≥7. 
Develop protocols for monitoring parameters (vital signs, oxygen saturation, mental status and surgical site assessment) and escalation triggers. Document the monitoring plan and deviations.

• Recognize and respond to early signs of clinical deterioration

Train perioperative staff to identify VTE (tachycardia, dyspnea, hypoxia, leg swelling), hemorrhage (tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, excessive drain output) and sepsis (fever, tachycardia, altered mental status). Establish escalation pathways and ensure immediate access to emergency equipment and transfers. Early intervention before hemodynamic collapse improves survival and supports defensible clinical decision-making. 


CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Technical errors during surgery, including inadequate hemostasis, excessive tissue trauma and failure to recognize anatomical variations, increase the risk of hemorrhage, fat embolism and other fatal complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure safe surgical technique and minimize the risk of intraoperative catastrophe.

• Use anesthesia depth and monitoring tailored to the procedure and setting

Select patients appropriate for the office-based setting based on ASA classification (see ASA Physical Status Classification System,), procedure complexity and facility capabilities. ASA class I–II patients are generally preferred for office-based procedures. Stable ASA class III patients may be considered on a case-by-case basis in appropriately accredited facilities (AAAASF, AAAHC, Joint Commission, or state-licensed ASCs) with a qualified anesthesia provider present, consistent with facility accreditation standards and applicable state regulations. ASA class IV patients are not appropriate candidates for office-based surgery. Document the patient selection rationale, including ASA classification and any comorbidity considerations.
• Avoid combining high-risk procedures in a single session

When determining whether to combine procedures, carefully weigh cumulative risks and consider staging in high-risk patients Combined procedures are the strongest predictive factor for VTE and unplanned hospital admissions. Document the risk-benefit discussion with the patient and the clinical rationale for the operative plan. 

• Adhere to evidence-based limits on liposuction volume and operative duration

Perform large-volume liposuction (LVL), the removal of ≥5L of total aspirate (fat plus wetting solution) in a single procedure, only in facilities with inpatient-level monitoring and fluid resuscitation resources. Limit total operative time, particularly when combining procedures, as duration >3 hours is an independent risk factor for complications. Consider staging procedures for patients requiring LVL or treatment of multiple body areas. Complying with evidence-based thresholds for lipoaspirate volume and operative duration reduces the risk of fluid shifts, fat embolism and hemodynamic instability. Document the planned approach and rationale for any deviation from standard limits.
• Maintain meticulous hemostasis throughout the procedure

Control bleeding before closure to reduce the risk of hematoma, the most common major complication in abdominoplasty (31.5% of complications). Use appropriate electrocautery settings and consider tumescent technique for liposuction. Systematically inspect surgical sites before closing. Document estimated blood loss and hemostatic measures employed. 


CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Retained Foreign Body

Contributing action or omission: Failure to account for all surgical instruments, sponges and needles increases the risk of retained foreign bodies, a surgical “never event” that can cause sepsis, bowel perforation and death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to prevent retained surgical items through systematic counting and verification.

• Conduct systematic wound exploration before closure

Perform a methodical visual and manual inspection of wounds before closing surgical sites. This is particularly important in procedures involving deep tissue planes or multiple operative sites. Document the inspection in the operative note.
• Perform surgical counts at defined intervals

Use only radiopaque sponges and count all sponges, sharps and instruments before incision, before cavity closure and at skin closure. Consider using laparotomy sponges rather than smaller gauze sponges (Raytec/4×4s) to reduce the risk of sponges being lost in the operative field, particularly in procedures involving deep tissue planes or larger wounds. Record counts in the operative record, including the names of personnel performing counts. Note that counts do not guarantee absence of retained items, as 88% of retained foreign body cases had falsely correct final counts.
• Resolve count discrepancies before closure of the body cavity
If counts are incorrect or discrepant, obtain radiographic imaging and resolve the discrepancy before cavity closure. An unaccounted-for item present at the time of cavity closure meets the definition of a retained surgical item: a "never event" under the National Quality Forum classification, with significant reporting, reimbursement and liability consequences. Retrieval after closure requires reopening and adds operative time and risk. For high-risk categories, such as emergencies, unexpected procedural changes, obese patients or procedures involving open cavities, consider routine intraoperative radiographic screening before the patient leaves the operating room, even when counts are documented as correct. Document imaging results and any actions taken.
• Use adjunct technology for sponge detection in high-risk cases

Consider radiofrequency identification (RFID) or barcode-tagged sponge systems, particularly for procedures involving body cavities, emergency cases or cases with intraoperative changes in plan. Facilities using RF technology demonstrated a 93% reduction in retained sponge rates. While not universally required, these technologies add an additional safety layer.

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate informed consent, communication of risks and postoperative instructions increase the risk of patient harm and malpractice liability.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients understand procedural risks and their role in postoperative safety.

• Conduct informed consent discussions on procedure-specific mortality risks

Explain procedure risks to patients, including VTE, hemorrhage, infection and anesthesia complications. Highlight elevated mortality risks for abdominoplasty and combined procedures compared with other aesthetic surgeries, using visual aids and written materials to improve understanding. Record the discussion, questions and responses and the patient's stated understanding.
Sample documentation language: "Discussed risks of abdominoplasty including bleeding, infection, wound healing problems, DVT/PE (which can be fatal), and anesthesia risks. Patient asked about blood clot prevention; explained Caprini score of 5 and plan for compression devices plus postoperative anticoagulation. Patient verbalized understanding and agreed to proceed."

• Provide written postoperative instructions that include warning signs

Before discharge, provide clear written instructions specifying warning signs requiring immediate evaluation: chest pain, shortness of breath, leg swelling or pain, excessive bleeding, fever and altered mental status. Include contact information and instructions for after-hours emergencies. Ensure that instructions are provided in the patient's primary language and at an appropriate literacy level. Document education and the patient's stated understanding.

• Establish and communicate postoperative follow-up expectations

Schedule postoperative follow-up appointments before discharge. Clearly communicate the importance of attending follow-up visits and the surgeon's ongoing responsibility for postoperative care. Per ASPS position statements, surgeons must maintain responsibility for continuity of care and should not delegate postoperative management to unqualified providers.
ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR

Failure to Recognize Responsibility

Contributing action or omission: Failure to maintain appropriate oversight of patient care, inadequate credentialing of surgical facilities and delegation of care to unqualified providers increase the risk of preventable death.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate professional accountability and facility standards.

• Perform surgery only in appropriately accredited facilities

Conduct procedures in facilities accredited by recognized organizations (AAAASF, AAAHC, Joint Commission or state-licensed ambulatory surgery centers). Accreditation requires adherence to safety standards including emergency equipment, transfer agreements and quality reporting. Accreditation agencies provide transparency and enable identification of safety trends. 

• Ensure appropriate credentialing and privileges for all procedures performed

Maintain current board certification and hospital privileges for procedures performed in office-based settings. Participate in continuing medical education specific to patient safety and procedural techniques. Document credentials and training for any new procedures before offering them to patients. 

• Maintain personal involvement in all phases of patient care

Per ASPS ethical standards, surgeons must be appropriately involved in preoperative diagnosis and planning, informed consent, performance of the procedure and postoperative care, including management of complications. Do not delegate critical aspects of care to providers who are not qualified to manage potential complications. If care must be transferred, ensure that the receiving provider is appropriately qualified and document the transfer.

• Implement quality assessment and outcomes tracking systems

Establish systems to track complications, adverse events and near-misses. Participate in national registries (such as TOPS) to benchmark outcomes against peers. Conduct regular case reviews and implement process improvements based on findings. Quality assessment systems demonstrate commitment to patient safety and support continuous improvement. 

PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify and address barriers to preoperative optimization and postoperative compliance increases the risk of preventable complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to optimize patient engagement and address modifiable risk factors.

• Screen for and address modifiable risk factors preoperatively

Identify patients using estrogen-containing medications (combined oral contraceptives, hormone replacement therapy) and address the associated VTE risk. Two approaches are supported in the literature:

1) Discontinue combined hormonal contraceptives 4–6 weeks before major surgery when prolonged immobilization is anticipated, per ACOG guidance. Prothrombotic clotting factor changes persist for 4–6 weeks after cessation. 

2) Continue hormone therapy perioperatively and ensure appropriate thromboprophylaxis based on the Caprini score, which already incorporates hormone use as a risk factor. This approach avoids the practical downsides of cessation, including unintended pregnancy, menstrual bleeding and resumption of menopausal symptoms. 

No randomized trials demonstrate that preoperative discontinuation of hormone therapy reduces postsurgical VTE. The decision should be individualized based on the patient's overall Caprini score, procedure type and anticipated immobilization. Document the discussion and rationale for the chosen approach.

• Assess patient reliability and support systems

Evaluate the patient's ability to comply with postoperative instructions, including activity restrictions, medication regimens and follow-up appointments. Assess availability of responsible adult supervision during the immediate postoperative period. Consider whether the patient's home environment supports safe recovery. Document concerns and any accommodations made. 

• Provide multimodal patient education

Use multiple educational methods (verbal discussion, written materials, videos) to reinforce key safety information. Confirm patient understanding using teach-back techniques. Address language barriers with interpreter services and translated materials. Document educational interventions and patient comprehension. 

• Document non-adherence and continued outreach

When patients miss follow-up appointments, fail to comply with preoperative instructions or decline recommended interventions, document the non-adherence, risks explained and continued outreach attempts. If a patient declines recommended VTE prophylaxis or other safety measures despite being informed of risks, document the discussion.

Mitigating Your Risk

With systematic VTE risk identification and prophylaxis, evidence-based patient selection and procedural planning and rigorous documentation and patient communication, plastic surgeons can mitigate the risk of intra- or postoperative death and reduce malpractice exposure. TITLE: Cancer
RISK_BRIEF:
Bladder Cancer

SPECIALTY: Urology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Hematuria, Bladder Tumor, Urinary Tract Malignancy

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Delayed Diagnosis, Understaging, Surgical Complications, Disease Progression, Patient Death

Bladder cancer is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in urology.
Mitigating Your Risk
With systematic hematuria evaluation, guideline-adherent staging and treatment and thorough documentation and communication, urologists can mitigate the risks associated with bladder cancer and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for bladder cancer and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving bladder cancer typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 73% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 27%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Bladder Cancer Risk [insert chart]

While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failure to perform guideline-recommended hematuria evaluation—including cystoscopy, upper tract imaging and appropriate laboratory testing—delays bladder cancer diagnosis and increases the risk of disease progression and adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely identification of bladder cancer through systematic, risk-stratified evaluation of hematuria.

• Follow AUA risk-stratified hematuria evaluation guidelines

At initial presentation of microscopic hematuria, classify patients into low-, intermediate- or high-risk categories based on age, sex, smoking history and degree of hematuria per the AUA/SUFU Microhematuria Guideline (2020; amended 2025). For intermediate-risk patients, recommend cystoscopy and renal ultrasound. For high-risk patients and all patients with gross hematuria, perform cystoscopy and cross-sectional urography (CT urogram). Risk stratification reduces both missed diagnoses and unnecessary procedures. The risk of bladder cancer is approximately 4% in patients with microscopic hematuria and 16.5% in those with gross hematuria.

• Perform urine cytology in appropriate clinical contexts

Use clinical judgment informed by guidelines rather than routinely ordering urine cytology as part of the initial hematuria evaluation. Per the AUA/SUFU Guideline (2025), cytology may be considered for high-risk patients when cystoscopic findings are unclear or when persistent microhematuria with irritative voiding symptoms raises clinical suspicion for CIS after a negative initial workup. For intermediate-risk patients, cystoscopy and renal ultrasound remain the standard recommendation; however, cytology or validated urine-based tumor markers may be offered to appropriately counseled patients who wish to avoid cystoscopy, provided renal and bladder ultrasound is performed and urinalysis repeated within 12 months. Abnormal cytology with a negative cystoscopy should prompt further evaluation, including enhanced cystoscopy or upper tract assessment.
• Obtain upper tract imaging before or concurrent with TURBT

Complete abdomen/pelvis imaging that includes the upper urinary tract collecting system before transurethral resection of bladder tumor. Approximately 2%–4% of patients with bladder cancer develop upper tract urothelial carcinoma, and 40% of patients with UTUC develop bladder cancer. Failure to image the upper tracts before definitive endoscopic management risks missing synchronous disease.

• Do not dismiss hematuria in women as presumptively benign

Maintain a high index of suspicion for bladder cancer in women presenting with hematuria. Hematuria in women is frequently attributed to urinary tract infection or gynecologic causes, resulting in diagnostic delays and worse cancer-specific outcomes. Persistent or recurrent hematuria after treatment of infection warrants urological evaluation regardless of gender.



CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failure to perform repeat TURBT, omission of perioperative intravesical chemotherapy, delays in escalating from intravesical therapy to cystectomy and failure to offer neoadjuvant chemotherapy for muscle-invasive disease increase the risk of understaging, disease progression and preventable mortality.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate and timely treatment across the bladder cancer disease spectrum.

• Perform repeat TURBT for high-grade T1 and select high-grade Ta tumors

Perform a repeat resection within 4–6 weeks for all high-grade T1 tumors and for high-grade Ta tumors when the initial specimen lacks muscularis propria or when resection was visually incomplete. Residual or understaged disease is found in 20%–71% of T1 tumors and 17%–67% of high-grade Ta tumors at repeat resection. For T1 tumors, upstaging to muscle-invasive disease changes the management plan from intravesical therapy to curative-intent cystectomy or trimodal therapy. For high-grade Ta tumors, repeat TURBT is recommended when the initial specimen lacks muscularis propria, when resection was visually incomplete, or when tumors are large (≥3 cm) or multifocal. Repeat TURBT is essential for accurate staging and directly affects treatment decisions.
• Administer single-dose intravesical chemotherapy within 24 hours of TURBT when appropriate

For patients with suspected low-grade, low-volume Ta urothelial cancer, administer a single perioperative dose of intravesical gemcitabine (preferred) or mitomycin within 24 hours of TURBT. This reduces recurrence rates by about 35%. Do not administer if there is concern for bladder perforation.

• Refer to medical oncology for perioperative systemic therapy evaluation in muscle-invasive disease

For patients with cT2–T4a N0 M0 bladder cancer, estimate GFR during the staging workup to assess cisplatin eligibility and refer to medical oncology early in the treatment planning process for evaluation of perioperative systemic therapy. For cisplatin-eligible patients, discuss both neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy alone and perioperative immunotherapy with neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy as Category 1 options. Failure to refer for consideration of perioperative systemic therapy may delay guideline-recommended treatment.


• Escalate treatment promptly for BCG-unresponsive NMIBC

For patients meeting the definition of BCG-unresponsive disease, recommend radical cystectomy as the preferred option for medically fit patients. When cystectomy is declined or contraindicated, discuss FDA-approved alternatives (e.g., nadofaragene firadenovec, nogapendekin alfa inbakicept + BCG, pembrolizumab, gemcitabine intravesical system) and clinical trials. Continued BCG in unresponsive patients delays definitive treatment and risks progression to incurable disease.

• Present all curative-intent treatment options for MIBC using a multidisciplinary approach

For patients with newly diagnosed muscle-invasive bladder cancer, discuss both radical cystectomy with urinary diversion and trimodal therapy (maximal TURBT + concurrent chemoradiation) as curative-intent options. Use a multidisciplinary team approach. Counsel patients regarding complications and quality-of-life implications of each option, including impact on continence, sexual function, fertility and bowel function. Failure to present bladder-preserving options to eligible patients may constitute inadequate informed consent.



CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Incomplete tumor resection, failure to obtain muscularis propria in the TURBT specimen, bladder perforation during TURBT and surgical complications during radical cystectomy increase the risk of understaging, disease recurrence and severe patient harm.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce procedural complications, ensure adequate staging and support optimal surgical outcomes.

• Perform bimanual examination under anesthesia at the time of TURBT

Conduct a bimanual examination under anesthesia before and after resection to assess for extravesical extension. This clinical staging step informs treatment planning and is recommended by both AUA/ASCO/SUO and NCCN guidelines. Document findings in the operative report.
• Use enhanced cystoscopy techniques when available

Consider narrow-band imaging or blue light (photodynamic) cystoscopy during diagnostic cystoscopy and TURBT. Narrow-band imaging improves detection rates by about 10%-20% on a per-patient and per-lesion basis and decreases recurrence at 3 and 12 months. Blue light cystoscopy detects up to 14% of papillary Ta/T1 lesions and 40% of CIS lesions missed on conventional white light cystoscopy. Enhanced visualization reduces the risk of incomplete resection and missed lesions.
• Ensure muscularis propria is present in the TURBT specimen

Resect deeply enough to include muscularis propria (detrusor muscle) in the pathology specimen. Absence of muscle in the specimen prevents accurate staging and may necessitate repeat resection, delaying definitive treatment. Document the presence or absence of muscularis propria in the operative and pathology reports. This is a fundamental quality metric for TURBT adequacy.

• Avoid intravesical chemotherapy instillation when suspecting bladder perforation

After TURBT, assess for signs of bladder perforation before administering perioperative intravesical chemotherapy. Instillation into a perforated bladder can cause chemical peritonitis and severe patient harm. When perforation is suspected, document the clinical reasoning for withholding instillation.
• Optimize radical cystectomy outcomes through volume and protocol adherence

Consider referral to experienced centers for complex cases, as institutional and surgeon volume have been associated with improved radical cystectomy outcomes in observational studies. Ensure adequate pelvic lymph node dissection as part of the procedure. Consider implementing Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) protocols which are associated with reduced length of stay and faster return of bowel function in systematic reviews and meta-analyses, with some evidence of reduced overall complication rates. Document the extent of lymphadenectomy and diversion type.


ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Providers

Contributing action or omission: Communication failures between urologists, pathologists, oncologists, radiation oncologists and primary care providers—including inadequate handoffs, unclear referral pathways and lack of multidisciplinary coordination—increase the risk of delayed or inappropriate treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support coordinated, multidisciplinary bladder cancer care.

• Establish structured pathology review and communication protocols

Ensure that pathology reports for TURBT specimens include tumor grade, stage (depth of invasion), presence or absence of muscularis propria, presence of CIS, lymphovascular invasion and variant histology. When variant histology is suspected or muscle invasion is equivocal, obtain review by an experienced genitourinary pathologist. Promptly communicate critical pathology findings (unexpected muscle invasion, aggressive subtypes) directly to the treating urologist.

• Use multidisciplinary tumor boards for complex treatment decisions

Consider presenting patients with high-risk NMIBC, MIBC and BCG-unresponsive disease at a multidisciplinary tumor board that includes urology, medical oncology, radiation oncology and pathology. Document tumor board recommendations and communicate them to the patient. Multidisciplinary evaluation facilitates consideration of all curative-intent options and supports defensible treatment decisions.

• Implement structured handoff protocols for care transitions

Use standardized handoff frameworks such as SBAR when transferring care between providers, including transitions from the operating room to the inpatient unit, from inpatient to outpatient follow-up and between referring and treating urologists. Communicate key details, including tumor stage, pathology results, treatment plan and surveillance schedule. Document handoff information in the medical record.

• Communicate surveillance plans to primary care providers

Notify primary care providers of the bladder cancer diagnosis, treatment rendered, ongoing surveillance requirements and signs of recurrence. Bladder cancer requires long-term cystoscopic surveillance, and lapses in follow-up, particularly when patients transition between providers, represent a common failure point. Ensure that the surveillance schedule is documented and communicated to all involved providers.

ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider
Contributing action or omission: Inadequate patient counseling regarding diagnosis, treatment options, expected outcomes, surveillance requirements and follow-up increases the risk of non-adherence, delayed treatment and claims alleging lack of informed consent.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients are well-informed participants in their bladder cancer care.

• Conduct thorough informed consent discussions before TURBT and cystectomy

Before TURBT, discuss the diagnostic and therapeutic purpose of the procedure, the possibility of repeat resection, risks (bleeding, perforation, infection, obturator nerve reflex) and the potential for findings that change the treatment plan. Before radical cystectomy, counsel patients on complications, urinary diversion options, impact on continence, sexual function, fertility and bowel function. Document the informed consent discussion in the medical record, including specific risks discussed and patient questions.

• Discuss all guideline-recommended treatment options using shared decision-making

For patients with MIBC, present both radical cystectomy and trimodal therapy as curative-intent options when clinically appropriate. For patients with BCG-unresponsive NMIBC, discuss cystectomy, FDA-approved alternatives and clinical trials. Use shared decision-making to align treatment with patient values and preferences. Document the options discussed, the patient's preferences and the rationale for the chosen treatment plan.

• Provide education on the importance of surveillance

Explain that bladder cancer has a high recurrence rate and that long-term cystoscopic surveillance is essential. Provide written instructions specifying the surveillance schedule, what to expect during cystoscopy and symptoms that should prompt earlier evaluation (such as hematuria or irritative voiding symptoms). Emphasize that missed surveillance appointments increase the risk of undetected recurrence and disease progression.
ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Incomplete documentation of hematuria evaluation, TURBT findings, staging, clinical decision-making and surveillance undermines continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Thoroughly document the hematuria evaluation

Record the type of hematuria (gross vs. microscopic), degree (RBC/HPF), risk stratification category per AUA guidelines, evaluation (cystoscopy findings, imaging results, cytology results) and clinical reasoning for the assessment plan. If evaluation is deferred or modified based on risk stratification, document the rationale. This demonstrates adherence to guideline-based practice and supports defensibility if cancer is later diagnosed.

Sample documentation language: "Microscopic hematuria, 15 RBC/HPF. AUA intermediate risk (age 58, male, 10 pack-year smoking history). Cystoscopy and renal ultrasound recommended per AUA/SUFU 2025 guidelines. Risks and benefits of evaluation discussed with patient."

• Document TURBT operative findings in detail

Record tumor location, size, number, appearance (papillary vs. flat), completeness of resection (visually complete vs. incomplete), bimanual examination findings before and after resection, presence or absence of muscularis propria in the specimen and whether perioperative intravesical chemotherapy was administered (or the reason it was withheld). This operative detail is essential for accurate staging and treatment planning and is the primary defensible record of surgical adequacy.

• Document clinical reasoning for treatment decisions

Record the factors influencing treatment selection, including tumor stage and grade, risk stratification, patient comorbidities, cisplatin eligibility, patient preferences and tumor board recommendations. When expectant management or surveillance is chosen over more aggressive treatment, document the rationale and alternatives presented to the patient. This demonstrates thoughtful clinical judgment.

• Maintain a documented surveillance schedule and track adherence

Record the recommended surveillance protocol (cystoscopy intervals, imaging schedule, laboratory monitoring) in the medical record and communicate it to the patient. Consider using a standardized, guideline-based surveillance reference at the point of care to ensure accurate documentation of risk-stratified surveillance intervals. Document each surveillance encounter, including cystoscopy findings, cytology results and any imaging performed. When patients miss surveillance appointments, document outreach attempts and the patient's response.


PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence to Recommended Care

Contributing action or omission: Failure to identify and address barriers to surveillance adherence, treatment completion and follow-up increases the likelihood of undetected recurrence and disease progression.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and address barriers to timely evaluation and treatment.

• Provide written surveillance instructions at each visit

Give patients a written schedule of upcoming surveillance cystoscopies, imaging and laboratory tests. Include dates or timeframes and contact information for scheduling. Explain in plain language why each component of surveillance is necessary and what happens if surveillance is missed. Provide instructions in the patient's primary language and at an appropriate literacy level.

• Implement a tracking system for surveillance compliance

Use EHR-based recall systems or patient registries to track scheduled surveillance appointments and flag missed visits. Bladder cancer surveillance spans years to decades, and system-level tracking reduces the risk of patients being lost to follow-up. Assign responsibility for monitoring compliance to a specific member of the care team.

• Address barriers to follow-up

Identify barriers to surveillance adherence, including transportation, financial constraints, work obligations, anxiety about cystoscopy and competing medical conditions. Consider offering flexible scheduling, telehealth for non-procedural visits and coordination with primary care for patients who live far from the practice. For patients who decline recommended treatment or surveillance, document the discussion, recommendations, risks explained and the patient's stated reasons for declining.

• Document non-adherence and continued outreach

When patients miss surveillance appointments or decline recommended evaluation, document outreach attempts (phone calls, letters, patient portal messages) and the patient's response. If a patient declines cystoscopy or other recommended testing despite counseling, document the conversation, the risks communicated (including risk of undetected recurrence and progression) and the patient's stated understanding. Continue outreach by phone, patient portal or mail.
