TITLE: Infection
RISK_BRIEF:
Infectious Complications of Urologic Procedures


SPECIALTY: Urology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Urologic Procedures, Instrumentation, Catheterization

ADVERSE OUTCOME: Urinary Tract Infection, Surgical Site Infection, Urosepsis

Infections following urologic procedures are significant drivers of malpractice risk and severe patient harm in urology.
Mitigating Your Risk
With appropriate antibiotic selection, thorough preoperative risk stratification and timely recognition and escalation of complications, urologists can mitigate the risks of infectious post-procedure complications and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures that can lead to malpractice claims for infectious complications of urologic procedures and provides strategies to help prevent them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving infectious complications of urologic procedures typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for 71% of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining 29%. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Infectious Complications of Urologic Procedures Risk [insert chart]
While multiple actions and omissions drive risk, our advice discusses the top contributors to them in each specialty. This helps physicians focus on the mitigation strategies that can most improve patient care and reduce potential liability.
CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing action or omission: Failing to obtain preoperative urine evaluation or to identify patients at elevated risk for infectious complications increases the likelihood of post-procedural UTI and urosepsis.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate preoperative risk stratification and identification of patients requiring targeted prophylaxis or procedure deferral.

• Identify patient-specific risk factors during preoperative assessment

Systematically assess for risk factors that increase infectious complications: diabetes mellitus, immunosuppression, prior UTI within 12 months, recent urologic instrumentation, indwelling catheter, urinary tract obstruction and recent antibiotic use. Document those risk factors and adjust prophylaxis strategy accordingly. Patients with multiple risk factors may benefit from augmented prophylaxis or targeted therapy based on prior culture data. 
• Obtain urine evaluation prior to urologic procedures based on risk stratification

Before a urologic procedure, evaluate the patient for urinary tract symptoms suggestive of infection. For low-risk procedures (simple cystoscopy, urodynamics in healthy adults), a urine dipstick may suffice. For intermediate- and high-risk procedures (TURP, TURBT, ureteroscopy, prostate biopsy, prosthetic implantation), obtain urine microscopy and culture with sensitivities. Complete this evaluation with enough lead time for culture results to guide prophylaxis selection. Targeted antimicrobial therapy based on preoperative culture reduces post-procedural infection rates compared to empiric therapy. 

• Screen for and treat asymptomatic bacteriuria prior to mucosal-breaching procedures

For asymptomatic patients undergoing endoscopic urologic procedures associated with mucosal trauma (TURP, TURBT, ureteroscopy, percutaneous stone surgery), obtain a preoperative urine culture with adequate lead time for results. If bacteriuria is identified, administer targeted antimicrobial therapy based on culture sensitivities rather than empiric prophylaxis. The IDSA recommends a short course (1 or 2 doses) initiated 30–60 minutes before the procedure rather than prolonged therapy. Positive preoperative urine culture is independently associated with postoperative infection. This practice aligns with IDSA guidelines and reduces the risk of post-procedural sepsis. 

• Defer elective procedures in the presence of active infection

Postpone elective urologic procedures until UTI symptoms resolve and treatment is complete. Performing procedures during active infection increases the risk of post-procedural UTI and surgical site infection. For urgent cases, obtain urine microscopy and cultures to guide antibiotic selection.
CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Non-Medication Therapeutic Intervention

Contributing action or omission: Failure to administer appropriate antimicrobial prophylaxis, improper timing or selection of prophylactic agents, or delayed recognition and treatment of post-procedural infection increase the risk of serious infectious complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate prophylaxis administration and timely recognition and treatment of infectious complications.

• Administer procedure-appropriate antimicrobial prophylaxis

For Class II/clean-contaminated procedures (TURP, TURBT, ureteroscopy, percutaneous nephrolithotomy, prostate biopsy, prosthetic implantation), administer single-dose periprocedural antimicrobial prophylaxis. Prophylaxis is not routinely recommended for simple outpatient cystoscopy or urodynamics in healthy adults without risk factors. For patients with risk factors (neurogenic bladder, immunosuppression, recent instrumentation, recent antibiotic use), administer prophylaxis even for lower-risk procedures. This risk-stratified approach aligns with AUA guidelines and reduces unnecessary antibiotic exposure while protecting high-risk patients. 

• Select antimicrobial agents based on local resistance patterns and patient factors

Choose prophylactic agents based on likely organisms, local antibiograms, patient allergy history and prior culture data when available. Per the AUA Best Practice Statement (2020), trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is one of the primary recommended agents for procedures requiring prophylaxis, with first- or second-generation cephalosporins, amoxicillin/clavulanate or an aminoglycoside ± ampicillin as alternatives. Fluoroquinolones are no longer considered first-line for most urologic procedures due to rising resistance rates. For transrectal prostate biopsy, employ targeted prophylaxis based on rectal swab cultures or augmented prophylaxis (fluoroquinolone plus aminoglycoside or non-fluoroquinolone alternatives such as fosfomycin or ceftriaxone) in high-risk patients. Consult local infectious disease experts for patients with multi-drug-resistant organism history. 

• Time prophylaxis administration to achieve adequate tissue concentrations

Administer oral prophylaxis within one hour before a procedure, accounting for the agent's oral pharmacokinetics. Administer intravenous prophylaxis within 60 minutes of incision to ensure adequate tissue concentrations at the time of instrumentation. Re-dose during prolonged procedures according to the agent's half-life. Limit prophylaxis to the duration of the procedure with no postoperative continuation unless active infection is present. Prolonged prophylaxis (>48 hours) increases antibiotic resistance without reducing surgical site infection rates.
Exception for transrectal prostate biopsy: Meta-analytic evidence from 59 RCTs indicates that for transrectal prostate biopsy, a minimum of a full 1-day course of prophylaxis is superior to single-dose prophylaxis in reducing infectious complications. Targeted prophylaxis based on rectal swab cultures is superior to empiric prophylaxis. For transperineal prostate biopsy, the infection risk is substantially lower, and emerging evidence suggests that prophylaxis may not be required in all cases, though practice is evolving.

• Recognize and treat urosepsis as a medical emergency

Initiate empiric broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics within one hour of recognition, targeting likely gram-negative organisms including ESBL-producing bacteria. Observational data consistently demonstrate that delays in antibiotic administration are associated with increased mortality, with the strongest association in patients with septic shock. At the same time, pursue source control through drainage of obstructed systems (nephrostomy, ureteral stent) and provide supportive care, including fluid resuscitation. Gram-negative uropathogens in urosepsis demonstrate the highest susceptibility rates to Carbapenems.
CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Technique-Related Error

Contributing action or omission: Failure to prepare skin properly, adhere to sterile technique or follow appropriate intraoperative practices increase the risk of surgical site infection and post-procedural UTI.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to minimize bacterial contamination during urologic procedures and reduce surgical site infection risk.

• Implement evidence-based skin antisepsis protocols

For non-mucosal surgical sites, use chlorhexidine gluconate plus alcohol-based skin preparation. For transrectal prostate biopsy, perform intrarectal povidone-iodine cleansing. Avoid alcohol-based products on genital mucosa. Standardize skin preparation protocols across all procedures. 

• Minimize modifiable intraoperative risk factors

Maintain normothermia using active warming measures (warmed IV fluids, forced air warming); guideline-recommended thresholds range from >35.5°C to >36°C. Implement perioperative glycemic control targeting blood glucose <200 mg/dL (CDC, Category IA); tighter targets (100-180 mg/dL per ADA) may further reduce SSI rates but must be balanced against hypoglycemia risk. Minimize operative time when clinically appropriate. Re-dose antimicrobial prophylaxis during prolonged procedures according to the agent's half-life per AUA recommendations. Avoid unnecessary blood transfusion when possible. 

• Consider transperineal approach for prostate biopsy in high-risk patients

For patients who have experienced infectious complications with previous transrectal biopsy, are at elevated risk for biopsy-related infection or have anterior lesions, consider transperineal biopsy. Meta-analyses suggest that transperineal approach can lower infection risk compared to transrectal approach, though prospective randomized data are limited. When using transrectal approach, employ adjunctive measures including rectal swab cultures and augmented antibiotic administration. 

• Manage catheters and surgical drains appropriately

To reduce catheter-associated UTI and surgical site infection risk, avoid breaking a closed urinary catheter drainage system. Keep catheter bags below bladder level to prevent urinary reflux. Bring surgical drains through separate stab wounds rather than the primary incision.
ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Communication Failure Between Patient and Provider

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate patient education regarding infection warning signs, failure to provide clear post-procedure instructions or insufficient communication about when to seek care increase the risk of delayed recognition of serious infectious complications.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients can recognize and appropriately respond to signs of post-procedural infection.

• Inform patients with risk factors about their elevated chance of infection

Advise patients with diabetes, immunosuppression, prior infections or other risk factors about their higher risk of post-procedural infection. Emphasize completing prescribed antibiotics, monitoring for symptoms and seeking prompt care if symptoms develop. Document this counseling. 
• Provide explicit written and verbal discharge instructions regarding infection warning signs

Before discharging patients after urologic procedures, educate them about symptoms requiring immediate evaluation: fever (≥38°C/100.4°F), chills, rigors, flank or abdominal pain, dysuria with systemic symptoms, cloudy or foul-smelling urine or inability to urinate. Provide written instructions in the patient's primary language and at an appropriate literacy level. Document this education and stated patient understanding. 

• Give patients clear instructions on seeking urgent evaluation

Provide patients with contact information for the urology practice and clear instructions for after-hours concerns. Specify when to call the office and when to go directly to the emergency department. Patients with fever, rigors or signs of sepsis following urologic procedures should be instructed to seek emergency evaluation immediately. Document the communication plan. 

ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Documentation Failure

Contributing action or omission: Inadequate documentation of preoperative risk assessment, antibiotic administration, intraoperative events and post-procedural instructions compromises continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that demonstrates adherence to standard of care and continuity of care.

• Document preoperative infection risk assessment and prophylaxis plan

Record the preoperative urine evaluation results (dipstick, microscopy, culture), identified patient risk factors and the rationale for prophylaxis selection. When prophylaxis is withheld for low-risk procedures, document the reasons for this decision to demonstrate thoughtful clinical judgment aligned with guidelines. 

Sample documentation language: "Preoperative UA negative for nitrites/leukocyte esterase, microscopy with <5 WBC/hpf. No risk factors for post-procedural UTI identified (no diabetes, no recent instrumentation, no indwelling catheter). Per AUA guidelines, antimicrobial prophylaxis not indicated for diagnostic cystoscopy in this low-risk patient."

• Document prophylaxis administration with timing and agent details

Record the antimicrobial agent, dose, route and time of administration relative to procedure start. For procedures requiring redosing, document each dose. When targeted prophylaxis is used based on prior culture data, reference the culture results guiding selection. Documentation demonstrates adherence to timing and selection recommendations. 

Sample documentation language: "Cefazolin 2g IV administered at 0745, 55 minutes prior to incision at 0840. Single-dose prophylaxis per AUA Best Practice Statement for clean-contaminated urologic procedure."

• Document patient education and discharge instructions

Record patient education and stated understanding about post-procedural infection warning signs and symptoms for which to seek care. Provide and document contact information for urgent concerns. For high-risk patients, document specific counseling about their elevated risk. Documentation supports defensibility if delayed presentation occurs. 

Sample documentation language: "Discussed post-procedure infection warning signs with patient including fever >100.4°F, chills, flank pain, and inability to urinate. Patient instructed to seek emergency evaluation for fever with chills or rigors. Written instructions provided in English. Patient verbalized understanding and confirmed ability to reach office or ED if symptoms develop." 


ADMINISTRATIVE: PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIOR

Failure to Recognize Responsibility

Contributing action or omission: Failure to establish clear responsibility for post-procedural monitoring, follow-up on culture results or coordination of care increases the risk of delayed treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure accountability for infection prevention and timely response to complications.

• Establish systems for tracking pending culture results

Implement reliable processes to ensure that preoperative and postoperative urine culture results are reviewed and acted on. Assign clear responsibility for follow-up on culture results. When cultures reveal resistant organisms or unexpected pathogens, contact patients promptly to adjust therapy. Document culture review and resulting clinical actions. 

• Develop processes for responding to post-procedural infection

Establish protocols specifying when and how to escalate care for patients with suspected post-procedural infection. Urosepsis requires interdisciplinary management involving urology, infectious disease and critical care. Ensure that on-call coverage understands expectations for responding to patients with post-procedural fever or sepsis. Time from symptom onset to effective treatment is critical for survival. 

• Conduct post-event review of infectious complications

When serious infectious complications occur, perform a structured review to identify contributing factors and opportunities for improvement. Track infection rates by procedure type and provider. Use findings to refine antibiotic prophylaxis protocols, patient selection criteria and perioperative processes.
OVERVIEW:
Infectious Complications of Urologic Procedures

Infections following urologic procedures are significant drivers of malpractice risk and severe patient harm in urology.
Mitigating Your Risk
With appropriate antibiotic selection, thorough preoperative risk stratification and timely recognition and escalation of complications, urologists can mitigate the risks of infectious post-procedure complications and reduce malpr

ADVERSE_OUTCOMES: Urinary Tract Infection, Surgical Site Infection, Urosepsis

TITLE: Bladder Cancer
RISK_BRIEF:
Bladder Cancer: A High-Impact Malpractice Risk

SPECIALTY: Urology

PRESENTING CONDITION(S): Hematuria, Bladder Tumor, Urinary Tract Malignancy

ADVERSE OUTCOME(S): Delayed Diagnosis, Understaging, Surgical Complications, Disease Progression, Patient Death

Bladder cancer is a significant driver of medical malpractice risk and severe patient harm in urology.
Mitigating Your Risk
With urologists can mitigate  bladder cancer and reduce malpractice exposure. This report examines recurring clinical and administrative failures associated with malpractice claims for bladder cancer and provides strategies to help prevent or mitigate them.

Clinical and Administrative Breakdowns

Malpractice claims involving bladder cancer typically stem from a recurring set of clinical and administrative failures. Clinical contributors account for [xx%] of the risk, while administrative contributors account for the remaining [xx%]. Any one of these factors—or a combination of them—can lead to severe patient harm and a malpractice claim, even if the other aspects of care were appropriate.
Actions and Omissions Driving Bladder Cancer Risk [insert chart]



CLINICAL: DIAGNOSTIC

Failure to Order Indicated Testing

Contributing Action or Omission: Failure to perform guideline-recommended hematuria evaluation—including cystoscopy, upper tract imaging and appropriate laboratory testing—delays bladder cancer diagnosis and increases the risk of disease progression and adverse outcomes.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure timely identification of bladder cancer through systematic, risk-stratified evaluation of hematuria.

• Follow AUA risk-stratified hematuria evaluation guidelines

At initial presentation of microscopic hematuria, classify patients into low-, intermediate- or high-risk categories based on age, sex, smoking history and degree of hematuria per the AUA/SUFU Microhematuria Guideline (2020; amended 2025). For intermediate-risk patients, recommend cystoscopy and renal ultrasound. For high-risk patients and all patients with gross hematuria, perform cystoscopy and cross-sectional urography (CT urogram). Risk stratification reduces both missed diagnoses and unnecessary procedures. The risk of bladder cancer is approximately 4% in patients with microscopic hematuria and 16.5% in those with gross hematuria.

• Perform urine cytology in appropriate clinical contexts

Use clinical judgment informed by guidelines rather than routinely ordering urine cytology as part of the initial hematuria evaluation. he AUA/SUFU Guideline (2025)for high-risk patientscystoscopic findings are equivocal or when persistent microhematuria with irritative voiding symptoms raises clinical suspicion for CIS after a negative initial workup. For intermediate-risk patients, cytology may be offered Abnormal cytology with a negative cystoscopy should prompt further evaluation, including enhanced cystoscopy or upper tract assessment.
• Obtain upper tract imaging before or concurrent with TURBT

Complete abdomen/pelvis imaging that includes the upper urinary tract collecting system before transurethral resection of bladder tumor. Approximately 2%–4% of patients with bladder cancer develop upper tract urothelial carcinoma, and 40% of patients with UTUC develop bladder cancer. Failure to image the upper tracts before definitive endoscopic management risks missing synchronous disease.

• Do not dismiss hematuria in women as presumptively benign

Maintain a high index of suspicion for bladder cancer in women presenting with hematuria. Hematuria in women is frequently attributed to urinary tract infection or gynecologic causes, resulting in diagnostic delays and worse cancer-specific outcomes. Persistent or recurrent hematuria after treatment of infection warrants urological evaluation regardless of gender.

Evidence for this section: AUA/SUFU Microhematuria Guideline, 2020/2025; NCCN Bladder Cancer Guidelines, v1.2026; Lenis et al., JAMA 2020; Compérat et al., Lancet 2022.


CLINICAL: TREATMENT

Error in Treatment Other Than Medication

Contributing Action or Omission: Failure to perform repeat TURBT, omission of perioperative intravesical chemotherapy, delays in escalating from intravesical therapy to cystectomy and failure to offer neoadjuvant chemotherapy for muscle-invasive disease increase the risk of understaging, disease progression and preventable mortality.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure appropriate and timely treatment across the bladder cancer disease spectrum.

• Perform repeat TURBT for high-grade T1 and select high-grade Ta tumors

Perform a repeat resection within 4–6 weeks for all high-grade T1 tumors and for high-grade Ta tumors when the initial specimen lacks muscularis propria or when resection was visually incomplete. Residual or understaged disease is found in 20%–71% of T1 tumors and 17%–67% of high-grade Ta tumors at repeat resection. For T1 tumors, upstaging to muscle-invasive disease changes the management plan from intravesical therapy to curative-intent cystectomy or trimodal therapy. For high-grade Ta tumors, repeat TURBT is recommended when the initial specimen lacks muscularis propria, when resection was visually incomplete, or when tumors are large (≥3 cm) or multifocal. Repeat TURBT is essential for accurate staging and directly affects treatment decisions
• Administer single-dose intravesical chemotherapy within 24 hours of TURBT when appropriate

For patients with suspected low-grade, low-volume Ta urothelial cancer, administer a single perioperative dose of intravesical gemcitabine (preferred) or mitomycin within 24 hours of TURBT. This reduces recurrence rates by about 35%. Do not administer if there is concern for bladder perforation.

• 

For patients with cT2–T4a N0 M0 bladder cancer cisplatin-eligible, both neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy alone and perioperative immunotherapy with neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy Failure to perioperative may .

• Escalate treatment promptly for BCG-unresponsive NMIBC

For patients meeting the definition of BCG-unresponsive disease, recommend radical cystectomy as the preferred option for medically fit patients. When cystectomy is declined or contraindicated, discuss FDA-approved alternatives (e.g., nadofaragene firadenovec, nogapendekin alfa inbakicept + BCG, pembrolizumab, gemcitabine intravesical system) and clinical trials. Continued BCG in unresponsive patients delays definitive treatment and risks progression to incurable disease.

• Present all curative-intent treatment options for MIBC using a multidisciplinary approach

For patients with newly diagnosed muscle-invasive bladder cancer, discuss both radical cystectomy with urinary diversion and trimodal therapy (maximal TURBT + concurrent chemoradiation) as curative-intent options. Use a multidisciplinary team approach. Counsel patients regarding complications and quality-of-life implications of each option, including impact on continence, sexual function, fertility and bowel function. Failure to present bladder-preserving options to eligible patients may constitute inadequate informed consent.

Evidence for this section: AUA/ASCO/SUO MIBC Guideline, 2017/2024; NCCN Bladder Cancer Guidelines, v1.2026; Lenis et al., JAMA 2020; Compérat et al., Lancet 2022; Zlotta et al., Lancet Oncol 2023; Powles et al. (NIAGARA), NEJM 2024; Vulsteke et al. (KEYNOTE-905), NEJM 2026.


CLINICAL: PROCEDURAL/SURGICAL

Improper Technique

Contributing Action or Omission: Incomplete tumor resection, failure to obtain muscularis propria in the TURBT specimen, bladder perforation during TURBT and surgical complications during radical cystectomy increase the risk of understaging, disease recurrence and severe patient harm.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to reduce procedural complications, ensure adequate staging and support optimal surgical outcomes.


• Ensure muscularis propria is present in the TURBT specimen

Resect deeply enough to include muscularis propria (detrusor muscle) in the pathology specimen. Absence of muscle in the specimen prevents accurate staging and may necessitate repeat resection, delaying definitive treatment. Document the presence or absence of muscularis propria in the operative and pathology reports. This is a fundamental quality metric for TURBT adequacy.


• Optimize radical cystectomy outcomes through volume and protocol adherence

Consider referral to experienced centers for complex cases, as institutional and surgeon volume have been associated with improved radical cystectomy outcomes in observational studies. Ensure adequate pelvic lymph node dissection as part of the procedure. Consider implementing Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) protocols which are associated with reduced length of stay and faster return of bowel function in systematic reviews and meta-analyses, with some evidence of reduced overall complication rates. Document the extent of lymphadenectomy and diversion type.



Evidence for this section: NCCN Bladder Cancer Guidelines, v1.2026; AUA/ASCO/SUO MIBC Guideline, 2017/2024; Lenis et al., JAMA 2020; Pereira et al., Urology 2019; Maibom et al., BMJ Open 2021; Lai et al., Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2022.


ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Insufficient Communication Between Providers

Contributing Action or Omission: Communication failures between urologists, pathologists, oncologists, radiation oncologists and primary care providers—including inadequate handoffs, unclear referral pathways and lack of multidisciplinary coordination—increase the risk of delayed or inappropriate treatment.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to standardize communication processes and support coordinated, multidisciplinary bladder cancer care.

• Establish structured pathology review and communication protocols

Ensure that pathology reports for TURBT specimens include tumor grade, stage (depth of invasion), presence or absence of muscularis propria, presence of CIS, lymphovascular invasion and variant histology. When variant histology is suspected or muscle invasion is equivocal, obtain review by an experienced genitourinary pathologist. Promptly communicate critical pathology findings (e.g., unexpected muscle invasion, aggressive subtypes) directly to the treating urologist.

• Use multidisciplinary tumor boards for complex treatment decisions

Consider presenting patients with high-risk NMIBC, MIBC and BCG-unresponsive disease at a multidisciplinary tumor board that includes urology, medical oncology, radiation oncology and pathology. Document tumor board recommendations and communicate them to the patient. Multidisciplinary evaluation facilitates consideration of all curative-intent options and supports defensible treatment decisions.

• Implement structured handoff protocols for care transitions

Use standardized handoff frameworks, such as SBAR, when transferring care between providers, including transitions from the operating room to the inpatient unit, from inpatient to outpatient follow-up and between referring and treating urologists. Communicate key details, including tumor stage, pathology results, treatment plan and surveillance schedule. Document handoff information in the medical record.

• Communicate surveillance plans to primary care providers

Notify primary care providers of the bladder cancer diagnosis, treatment rendered, ongoing surveillance requirements and signs of recurrence. Bladder cancer requires long-term cystoscopic surveillance, and lapses in follow-up, particularly when patients transition between providers, represent a common failure point. Ensure that the surveillance schedule is documented and communicated to all involved providers.

Evidence for this section: AUA/ASCO/SUO MIBC Guideline, 2017/2024; NCCN Bladder Cancer Guidelines, v1.2026; Aydogdu et al., Ann Surg Oncol 2026.


ADMINISTRATIVE: COMMUNICATION

Insufficient Communication Between Provider and Patient

Contributing Action or Omission: Inadequate patient counseling regarding diagnosis, treatment options, expected outcomes, surveillance requirements and the importance of follow-up increases the risk of non-adherence, delayed treatment and claims alleging lack of informed consent.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to ensure that patients are well-informed participants in their bladder cancer care.

• Conduct thorough informed consent discussions before TURBT and cystectomy

Before TURBT, discuss the diagnostic and therapeutic purpose of the procedure, the possibility of repeat resection, risks (bleeding, perforation, infection, obturator nerve reflex) and the potential for findings that change the treatment plan. Before radical cystectomy, counsel patients on complications, urinary diversion options, impact on continence, sexual function, fertility and bowel function. Document the informed consent discussion in the medical record, including specific risks discussed and patient questions.

• Discuss all guideline-recommended treatment options using shared decision-making

For patients with MIBC, present both radical cystectomy and trimodal therapy as curative-intent options when clinically appropriate. For patients with BCG-unresponsive NMIBC, discuss cystectomy, FDA-approved alternatives and clinical trials. Use shared decision-making to align treatment with patient values and preferences. Document the options discussed, the patient's preferences and the rationale for the chosen treatment plan.

• Provide education on the importance of surveillance

Explain that bladder cancer has a high recurrence rate and that long-term cystoscopic surveillance is essential. Provide written instructions specifying the surveillance schedule, what to expect during cystoscopy and symptoms that should prompt earlier evaluation (such as hematuria or irritative voiding symptoms). Emphasize that missed surveillance appointments increase the risk of undetected recurrence and disease progression.

Evidence for this section: AUA/ASCO/SUO MIBC Guideline, 2017/2024; Bangs et al., Clin Oncol 2022; Ossin et al., Nat Rev Urol 2022; Smith et al., Urology 2026.


ADMINISTRATIVE: DOCUMENTATION

Insufficient Documentation

Contributing Action or Omission: Incomplete documentation of hematuria evaluation, TURBT findings, staging, clinical decision-making and surveillance undermines continuity of care and defensibility.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to support comprehensive documentation that reinforces continuity of care and provides a defensible record.

• Thoroughly document the hematuria evaluation

Record the type of hematuria (gross vs. microscopic), degree (RBC/HPF), risk stratification category per AUA guidelines, evaluation (cystoscopy findings, imaging results, cytology results) and clinical reasoning for the assessment plan. If evaluation is deferred or modified based on risk stratification, document the rationale. This demonstrates adherence to guideline-based practice and supports defensibility if cancer is later diagnosed.

Sample documentation language: "Microscopic hematuria, 15 RBC/HPF. AUA intermediate risk (age 58, male, 10 pack-year smoking history). Cystoscopy and renal ultrasound recommended per AUA/SUFU 2025 guidelines. Risks and benefits of evaluation discussed with patient."

• Document TURBT operative findings in detail

Record tumor location, size, number, appearance (papillary vs. flat), completeness of resection (visually complete vs. incomplete), bimanual examination findings before and after resection, presence or absence of muscularis propria in the specimen and whether perioperative intravesical chemotherapy was administered (or the reason it was withheld). This operative detail is essential for accurate staging and treatment planning and is the primary defensible record of surgical adequacy.

• Document clinical reasoning for treatment decisions

Record the factors influencing treatment selection, including tumor stage and grade, risk stratification, patient comorbidities, cisplatin eligibility, patient preferences and tumor board recommendations. When expectant management or surveillance is chosen over more aggressive treatment, document the rationale and alternatives presented to the patient. This demonstrates thoughtful clinical judgment.

• Maintain a documented surveillance schedule and track adherence

Record the recommended surveillance protocol (cystoscopy intervals, imaging schedule, laboratory monitoring) in the medical record and communicate it to the patient. Document each surveillance encounter, including cystoscopy findings, cytology results and any imaging performed. When patients miss surveillance appointments, document outreach attempts and the patient's response.

Evidence for this section: AUA/ASCO/SUO MIBC Guideline, 2017/2024; NCCN Bladder Cancer Guidelines, v1.2026; Lenis et al., JAMA 2020.


PATIENT FACTORS

Patient Non-Adherence

Contributing Action or Omission: Failure to identify and address barriers to surveillance adherence, treatment completion and follow-up increases the likelihood of undetected recurrence and disease progression.

Mitigation Strategies

The strategies below work together to maximize patient engagement and address barriers to timely evaluation and treatment.

• Provide written surveillance instructions at each visit

Give patients a written schedule of upcoming surveillance cystoscopies, imaging and laboratory tests. Include dates or timeframes and contact information for scheduling. Explain in plain language why each component of surveillance is necessary and what happens if surveillance is missed. Provide instructions in the patient's primary language and at an appropriate literacy level.

• Implement a tracking system for surveillance compliance

Use EHR-based recall systems or patient registries to track scheduled surveillance appointments and flag missed visits. Bladder cancer surveillance spans years to decades, and system-level tracking reduces the risk of patients being lost to follow-up. Assign responsibility for monitoring compliance to a specific member of the care team.

• Address barriers to follow-up

Identify barriers to surveillance adherence, including transportation, financial constraints, work obligations, anxiety about cystoscopy and competing medical conditions. Consider offering flexible scheduling, telehealth for non-procedural visits and coordination with primary care for patients who live far from the practice. For patients who decline recommended treatment or surveillance, document the discussion, recommendations, risks explained and the patient's stated reasons for declining.

• Document non-adherence and continued outreach

When patients miss surveillance appointments or decline recommended evaluation, document outreach attempts (phone calls, letters, patient portal messages) and the patient's response. If a patient declines cystoscopy or other recommended testing despite counseling, document the conversation, the risks communicated (including risk of undetected recurrence and progression) and the patient's stated understanding. Continue outreach by phone, patient portal or mail.

Evidence for this section: NCCN Bladder Cancer Guidelines, v1.2026; Lenis et al., JAMA 2020; Woldu et al., BJU Int 2017.
